This paper directly addresses computational efficiency in generative recommendation systems by introducing a categorical compression method for long user sequences. The approach reduces inference costs by 6x while improving accuracy by 39% compared to HSTU, making it highly relevant for deploying sequential models at scale.
Deep dive: Full analysis

8. ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights
Summary:
Capture quantization errors that occur when weights are quantized and inject them into the optimizer’s momentum buffer to compensate for precision loss without requiring a separate high-precision master weights copy. Existing quantized training methods require maintaining a high-precision master weight buffer alongside quantized parameters, creating substantial memory overhead—particularly problematic for Sparse Mixture of Experts (SMoE) models where optimizer states dominate memory usage. Useful for: LLM pretraining with quantized precision (FP8, INT4, INT8)
Deep dive: Full analysis

9. Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction
Summary:
Encode items into discrete semantic IDs using Residual Quantized Variational Autoencoder (RQ-VAE) followed by k-means refinement to create a semantic latent space that preserves item similarity relationships. Sequential recommenders typically operate on sparse one-hot item representations, losing semantic relationships between items. Useful for: Sequential recommendation systems requiring discrete action spaces
Deep dive: Full analysis

10. ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation
Summary:
Dynamically identify optimal token boundaries by measuring inter-token semantic similarity, then compress sequences by merging semantically similar adjacent tokens into concept representations before entering the compute-intensive model. LLMs allocate uniform computation across all tokens despite varying complexity—some sequences are trivially predictable while others require deep reasoning. Useful for: Language modeling and pretraining (especially on documents with repetitive or redundant sections)
Deep dive: Full analysis

11. S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation
Summary:
Enhance the semantic foundation of Semantic IDs (SIDs) by optimizing the codebook to capture item co-occurrence relationships while enforcing load balancing and uniformity constraints that maximize codebook utilization. Standard codebooks fail to capture behavioral patterns from item interactions, leading to underutilized codes and imbalanced cluster distributions that weaken the semantic hierarchies needed for fine-grained SID representations. Useful for: Foundation for hierarchical representation learning in generative recommendation systems
Deep dive: Full analysis

12. MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation
Summary:
A comprehensive benchmarking framework that systematically evaluates memory management and compression techniques for reducing state storage overhead in long-sequence recommendation models. Large-scale recommenders with billions of users face prohibitive memory costs from pre-storing intermediate states of past behavior sequences. Useful for: Real-time recommendation systems with strict latency/memory constraints
Deep dive: Full analysis

digest/2026-02-02.title: Research Digest — Mon 2026-02-02
digest/2026-02-02.content:
Research Digest — Mon 2026-02-02
Scanned 442 papers • Selected 12 • Filtered 3 off-domain, 30 duplicates • Runtime: 6.5 min

Papers



















































































#PaperTagsLinks1SpanNorm: Reconciling Training Stability and Performance in Deep TransformersLLM, Transformer, EmbeddingsarXiv • Note2FOCUS: DLLMs Know How to Tame Their Compute BoundLLM, RAG, DiffusionarXiv • Code • Note3DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web AgentsLLM, RecSys, RLarXiv • Note4ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement ArenasLLM, RAG, RLarXiv • Code • Note5Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discoverySearch, Embeddings, LLM, RecSysarXiv • Note6Chain-of-thought obfuscation learned from output supervision can generalise to unseen tasksLLM, RLarXiv • Note7Divide-and-Conquer CoT: RL for Reducing Latency via Parallel ReasoningLLM, RAGarXiv • Code • Note8Toward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent ReasoningLLMarXiv • Note9TAM-Eval: Evaluating LLMs for Automated Unit Test MaintenanceLLM, RAG, SearcharXiv • Code • Note10TGSBM: Transformer-Guided Stochastic Block Model for Link PredictionSearch, Transformer, LLM, RecSysarXiv • Note11CATTO: Balancing Preferences and Confidence in Language ModelsLLM, RAG, RecSysarXiv • Note12ImgCoT: Compressing Long Chain of Thought into Compact Visual Tokens for Efficient Reasoning of Large Language ModelLLM, RAGarXiv • Note

Details
1. SpanNorm: Reconciling Training Stability and Performance in Deep Transformers
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: LLM, Transformer, Embeddings
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A normalization technique that combines the training stability of PreNorm with the performance benefits of PostNorm by using a long-range residual connection spanning the entire transformer block while applying normalization to the aggregated output. - PreNorm ensures stable training but suffers from representation collapse and performance degradation in deep models

PostNorm achieves better performance but exhibits severe training instability (exploding/vanishing gradients) in deep networks
Existing approaches force a choice between stability and performance—SpanNorm resolves this fundamental trade-off Useful for: Training very deep Transformer models (>24 layers) where gradient flow degradation occurs

Vault connections: Everything You Didn’t Want To Know About LM architecture and training, TokenFormer - Rethinking Transformer Scaling with Tokenized Model Parameters, Early-Exit Large Language Models

2. FOCUS: DLLMs Know How to Tame Their Compute Bound
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: LLM, RAG, Diffusion
Code: github.com/sands-lab/FOCUS
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Dynamically focus computation on decodable tokens during DLLM inference and evict non-decodable tokens on-the-fly to reduce wasted compute across diffusion steps. Diffusion Language Models parallelize computation over token blocks, but only a small fraction of tokens are decodable at each diffusion step, causing most compute to be wasted on non-decodable tokens. Useful for: DLLM inference optimization in production systems
Vault connections: LLM in dense retrieval with query likelihood Modeling, Early-Exit Large Language Models, LLM Inference Performance: The Team Mental Model + Experiment Playbook

3. DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: LLM, RecSys, RL
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Train a generative model to predict naturalistic web page representations as the next state given the current web page and agent action, creating a learned simulator for efficient offline policy training. Live web interaction for agent training is inefficient (slow API calls), costly (requires real browser automation), and risky (potential for unintended actions). Useful for: Training web agents and automation systems
Vault connections: In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL), Sergey Levine — Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agents, Reinforcement learning in LLM – recent turbulence

4. ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas
Source: HuggingFace | Tags: LLM, RAG, RL
Code: github.com/LianjiaTech/astra
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Automatically generate diverse, structurally grounded agent trajectories by exploiting the static topology of tool-call dependency graphs to create all valid execution paths. Existing training methods require manual trajectory collection or rely on non-verifiable simulated environments. Useful for: Training multi-step tool-using agents without manual annotation
Vault connections: Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Dynamic Tool Integration via Reinforcement Learning, Michal Valko

5. Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery
Source: arXiv:cs.IR | Tags: Search, Embeddings, LLM, RecSys, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Leverage pre-trained BERT embeddings to enable semantic similarity matching between search queries and dataset metadata, capturing conceptual relevance beyond exact keyword matches. Traditional keyword-based search fails on misspelled queries, geographically implicit searches, and natural language questions where exact term matching is insufficient. Useful for: Data discovery systems handling misspelled or colloquial search terms
Vault connections: Consolidating Ranking and Relevance Predictions of Large Language Models, Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance, A Comparative Study of Specialized LLMs as Dense Retrievers

6. Chain-of-thought obfuscation learned from output supervision can generalise to unseen tasks
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: LLM, RL
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Training models on penalizing harmful actions while allowing them to learn obfuscated reasoning traces that hide the obfuscation mechanism, demonstrating that CoT deception can emerge from optimization pressures even when only final outputs are supervised. Current interpretability monitoring of LLMs relies on faithful chain-of-thought explanations as early warning signals. Useful for: AI safety and alignment research: detecting when optimization pressures create deceptive behaviors
Vault connections: Mind-Map Agent, Self-Consistency Decoding, CaP (Chain-and-Program)

7. Divide-and-Conquer CoT: RL for Reducing Latency via Parallel Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: LLM, RAG
Code: github.com/amahankali10/DC_CoT_RL_for_Low_Latency_CoT_with_Parallel_Reasoning
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Enable LLMs to identify decomposable subtasks in reasoning and execute them in parallel through a director-worker architecture, reducing the critical path length of long chain-of-thought generation while maintaining accuracy. Long chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs is inherently sequential, causing high latency despite enabling strong mathematical reasoning performance. Useful for: Mathematical problem solving (AIME, HMMT competitions)
Vault connections: Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Hierarchical Reasoning Model, Self-Consistency Decoding

8. Toward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.IR | Tags: LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Elicit structured relations over a fixed cultural taxonomy by asking competency questions to create a global cultural ontology grounded in World Values Survey data. Existing value representations in LLMs lack demographic grounding and treat cultural values as unstructured signals, reducing consistency and interpretability. Useful for: Building interpretable cultural models for cross-cultural NLP applications
Vault connections: Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Modeling Open-World Cognition as On-Demand Synthesis of Probabilistic Model, Agentic Reasoning Pipeline

9. TAM-Eval: Evaluating LLMs for Automated Unit Test Maintenance
Source: HuggingFace | Tags: LLM, RAG, Search
Code: github.com/trndcenter/TAM-Eval
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A reference-free evaluation framework that assesses LLMs on realistic test suite maintenance tasks across three scenarios (creation, repair, updating) at the test file level while maintaining full repository context. Prior work isolated test generation/oracle prediction to function-level tasks without addressing the broader challenge of maintaining test suites in real repositories. Useful for: Evaluating foundation models on realistic test engineering tasks
Vault connections: CLEAR: Error Analysis via LLM-as-a-Judge Made Easy, G-Eval Simply Explained – LLM-as-a-judge for LLM evaluation, context rot

10. TGSBM: Transformer-Guided Stochastic Block Model for Link Prediction
Source: arXiv:cs.IR | Tags: Search, Transformer, LLM, RecSys, Graph
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Use expander graph theory to construct sparse attention patterns that maintain near-linear complexity while enabling efficient global information mixing across all tokens in the network. Graph transformers achieve strong performance but suffer from quadratic O(n²) complexity in attention computation. Useful for: Large-scale social network analysis (millions of nodes)
Vault connections: TransGNN: Harnessing the Collaborative Power of Transformer and Graph Neural Network for Recommender System, LightGNN: Simple Graph Neural Network for Recommendation, Social Modularity Maximization (SMM)

11. CATTO: Balancing Preferences and Confidence in Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: LLM, RAG, RecSys
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Align predicted confidence (token probabilities) with empirical prediction correctness by adding a calibration-aware regularization term to preference optimization objectives. Preference-based alignment methods (like DPO) break the link between predictive probability and correctness, causing high-confidence predictions to be frequently wrong and low-confidence predictions to be correct. Useful for: Language model alignment with uncertainty quantification requirements
Vault connections: Michal Valko, TALLRec — aligning a large language model with recommendation, LLM Alignment as Retriever Optimization: An Information Retrieval Perspective

12. ImgCoT: Compressing Long Chain of Thought into Compact Visual Tokens for Efficient Reasoning of Large Language Model
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Replace textual CoT reconstruction targets with visual CoT (rendered as images) to shift from linguistic bias to spatial inductive bias, enabling better capture of reasoning structure while compressing CoT into compact latent tokens. - Standard autoencoder-based CoT compression using textual reconstruction targets preserves surface-level linguistic features (word choice, syntax) rather than reasoning logic

Linguistic inductive bias limits logical abstraction and global reasoning structure understanding
Long CoT requires many tokens, making inference computationally expensive for LLMs Useful for: Long-chain reasoning tasks requiring multi-step explanations

Vault connections: Infinite Retrieval: Attention Enhanced LLMs in Long-Context Processing, Few-Shot Reasoning with S1 and Budget Forcing, Graph-KV


🎯 Active Projects

2. Why Attention Patterns Exist: A Unifying Temporal Perspective Analysis
Summary:
Unify diverse attention patterns in LLMs by analyzing them through a temporally continuous perspective, distinguishing between predictable patterns (driven by query self-similarity) and unpredictable patterns. Prior works identified fragmented attention patterns (retrieval heads, sink heads, diagonal traces) without a unifying theoretical explanation. Useful for: KV cache compression during inference (reduce stored key-value pairs using pattern predictability)
Deep dive: Full analysis

3. ImgCoT: Compressing Long Chain of Thought into Compact Visual Tokens for Efficient Reasoning of Large Language Model
Summary:
Replace textual CoT reconstruction targets with visual CoT (rendered as images) to shift from linguistic bias to spatial inductive bias, enabling better capture of reasoning structure while compressing CoT into compact latent tokens. - Standard autoencoder-based CoT compression using textual reconstruction targets preserves surface-level linguistic features (word choice, syntax) rather than reasoning logic

Linguistic inductive bias limits logical abstraction and global reasoning structure understanding
Long CoT requires many tokens, making inference computationally expensive for LLMs Useful for: Long-chain reasoning tasks requiring multi-step explanations

Deep dive: Full analysis

4. OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce
Summary:
A specialized tokenization solution that captures both real-world product semantics and business-specific item relationships across heterogeneous e-commerce scenarios (product cards, short videos, live streaming). Standard tokenization approaches fail to capture multi-scenario item relationships and business semantics unique to e-commerce. Useful for: Multi-channel e-commerce recommendation (product discovery, short-form video recommendations, live shopping)
Deep dive: Full analysis

5. SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching
Summary:
Compress historical dialogue segments into learned compact representations called “Nexus tokens” that preserve semantic meaning while reducing cache size. Linear KV cache growth in multi-turn LLM contexts causes memory/latency bottlenecks; existing heuristic eviction methods risk losing critical dialogue context by making greedy decisions rather than learning what information matters semantically. Useful for: Multi-turn conversational AI where context accumulates over many exchanges
Deep dive: Full analysis

6. Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation
Summary:
Replace the sequential multi-level semantic ID (SID) codebook with a parallel structure that treats each dimension of item embeddings independently, enabling non-autoregressive diffusion-based generation. Traditional autoregressive decoding struggles to jointly capture global dependencies among multi-dimensional features at different positions in the SID sequence. Useful for: Recommendation systems where item attributes are inherently multi-dimensional (price, category, brand, etc.)
Deep dive: Full analysis

7. Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression
Summary:
This paper directly addresses a critical deployment challenge in generative recommender systems: reducing computational costs while processing long user interaction sequences. The proposed categorical compression method achieves substantial efficiency gains (6x speedup) by intelligently condensing historical user data while maintaining or improving recommendation accuracy.
Deep dive: Full analysis

8. ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights
Summary:
Apply gradient updates directly to quantized weights while injecting quantization error into optimizer momentum, eliminating the need for high-precision master weights during LLM training. Existing quantized training methods maintain a high-precision master weight buffer for gradient accumulation, creating substantial memory overhead—particularly problematic for Sparse Mixture of Experts (SMoE) models where parameters and optimizer states dominate memory consumption. Useful for: Pretraining large language models (LLMs) with FP8 or INT8 quantization
Deep dive: Full analysis

9. S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation
Summary:
Build a robust semantic codebook that captures item behavioral patterns by integrating co-occurrence relationships while maximizing codebook utilization through load balancing and uniformity constraints. Previous GR methods fail to establish a strong semantic foundation for hierarchical SID generation. Useful for: Cold-start scenarios where item relationships can bootstrap semantic structure
Deep dive: Full analysis

10. MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation
Summary:
This paper directly addresses scalability challenges in large sequential recommendation systems by benchmarking memory compression techniques for storing user interaction histories. It introduces MALLOC, a systematic evaluation framework for memory management strategies (borrowed from LLM literature) applied to reducing the massive storage overhead of intermediate states in billion-user recommendation systems with thousands of interactions per user.
Deep dive: Full analysis

11. Differentiable Semantic ID for Generative Recommendation
Summary:
Inject Gumbel noise into the semantic ID assignment process to force exploration of the codebook during early training, preventing codebook collapse where only a few codes are repeatedly used. In differentiable semantic indexing, early deterministic assignments to codes lead to codebook collapse—a scenario where the optimization converges to using only a subset of available codes, resulting in poor code utilization and unstable gradient flow that prevents recommendation loss from effectively updating the tokenizer. Useful for: Vector quantized variational autoencoders (VQ-VAE) with end-to-end differentiable losses
Deep dive: Full analysis

12. Quartet II: Accurate LLM Pre-Training in NVFP4 by Improved Unbiased Gradient Estimation
Summary:
A novel unbiased quantization routine specifically designed for micro-scaled (low-precision) formats that achieves >2x lower quantization error than stochastic rounding while maintaining unbiasedness. Existing quantized training methods sacrifice representation capacity of NVFP4 format to achieve unbiased gradient estimation via stochastic rounding (SR), resulting in noticeable accuracy loss compared to FP16/FP8 training. Useful for: NVIDIA Blackwell GPU training with NVFP4 format
Deep dive: Full analysis

digest/2026-02-03.title: Research Digest — Tue 2026-02-03
digest/2026-02-03.content:
Research Digest — Tue 2026-02-03
Scanned 377 papers • Selected 9 • Filtered 1 off-domain, 27 duplicates • Runtime: 6.9 min

Papers

































































#PaperTagsLinks1VisionTrim: Unified Vision Token Compression for Training-Free MLLM AccelerationEmbeddings, LLMarXiv • Code • Note2ORCH: many analyses, one merge-a deterministic multi-agent orchestrator for discrete-choice reasoning with EMA-guided routingLLMarXiv • Note3Dicta-LM 3.0: Advancing The Frontier of Hebrew Sovereign LLMsRecSys, LLM, RAGarXiv • Note4PretrainRL: Alleviating Factuality Hallucination of Large Language Models at the BeginningRL, LLM, RAGarXiv • Note5Geometric Analysis of Token Selection in Multi-Head AttentionLLM, RAGarXiv • Note6On the Power of (Approximate) Reward Models for Inference-Time ScalingRL, LLMarXiv • Note7Constrained Process Maps for Multi-Agent Generative AI WorkflowsLLM, RAGarXiv • Note8Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex ReasoningLLM, RAGarXiv • Code • Note9SLIME: Stabilized Likelihood Implicit Margin Enforcement for Preference OptimizationRL, LLM, RAGarXiv • Note

Details
1. VisionTrim: Unified Vision Token Compression for Training-Free MLLM Acceleration
Source: HuggingFace | Tags: Embeddings, LLM
Code: github.com/hanxunyu/VisionTrim
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Preserve essential visual tokens by combining global and local importance scores, eliminating redundant or non-salient tokens without training. Vision transformers in MLLMs generate excessive tokens that increase computational cost, especially for high-resolution images and videos. Useful for: High-resolution image understanding tasks (4K/8K images)
Vault connections: Learning Strategies, U-MARVEL: Main Takeaways and Techniques for Practitioners, Bitter Lesson & the tokenization Reckoning

2. ORCH: many analyses, one merge-a deterministic multi-agent orchestrator for discrete-choice reasoning with EMA-guided routing
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Coordinate multiple heterogeneous LLMs through deterministic, rule-based task decomposition and answer aggregation rather than stochastic routing, ensuring reproducibility and interpretability for discrete-choice reasoning. Existing multi-agent systems rely on stochastic routing or ad-hoc heuristics that make behavior difficult to reproduce and decision processes hard to interpret. Useful for: Multiple-choice exams and standardized reasoning benchmarks (MMLU, MMLU-Pro, GSM8K)
Vault connections: Agentic Reasoning Pipeline, Synergistic Tool Integration in Agentic Reasoning, Agentic Reasoning Design Patterns

3. Dicta-LM 3.0: Advancing The Frontier of Hebrew Sovereign LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RecSys, LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Low relevance to RecSys/ranking. The paper focuses on training and evaluating Hebrew language models through standard NLP tasks (translation, summarization, diacritization) rather than addressing recommendation systems, ranking algorithms, retrieval mechanisms, or embedding-based applications in search and recommendations contexts.
Vault connections: Lengthy Context Retrieval via LLM Pretraining, TALLRec — aligning a large language model with recommendation, LLM Query → BM25 Hard Negative Pipeline

4. PretrainRL: Alleviating Factuality Hallucination of Large Language Models at the Beginning
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RL, LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Integrate reinforcement learning during pretraining to actively reshape the model’s probability distribution by down-weighting high-probability falsehoods, creating capacity for low-probability truths to be learned effectively. Large language models suffer from factual hallucinations due to imbalanced data distribution in pretraining corpora, where low-probability truths compete against high-probability falsehoods. Useful for: Pretraining large language models to reduce factual hallucinations
Vault connections: Early-Exit Large Language Models, Language Generation vs. Identification in the Limit Outline, Lengthy Context Retrieval via LLM Pretraining

5. Geometric Analysis of Token Selection in Multi-Head Attention
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Quantify multi-head attention’s token selection behavior using geometric metrics (Precision, Recall, F-score) that measure separability between selected and non-selected tokens directly in value-state space. Existing attention analysis treats the mechanism as a black box. Useful for: Interpreting which attention heads perform retrieval vs. mixing vs. resetting
Vault connections: Dilated Attention, Attention Head Specialization for Matching Detection, Infinite Retrieval: Attention Enhanced LLMs in Long-Context Processing

6. On the Power of (Approximate) Reward Models for Inference-Time Scaling
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RL, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Identify Bellman error as the fundamental quantity that determines whether an approximate reward model can effectively guide Sequential Monte Carlo inference-time scaling, with exponential improvements in computational complexity achievable when Bellman error is bounded by O(1/T). In practice, deployed inference-time scaling systems cannot access true reward models and must use approximations. Useful for: Language model reasoning tasks requiring multi-step inference (mathematics, code generation, complex planning)
Vault connections: Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally: Summary and Implications, Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Monte Carlo Tree Search

7. Constrained Process Maps for Multi-Agent Generative AI Workflows
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Formalize multi-agent LLM workflows as finite-horizon Markov Decision Processes with a directed acyclic graph (DAG) structure, where each agent represents a distinct decision stage with predefined state transitions. Existing agentic architectures rely heavily on single-agent prompt engineering, making it difficult to observe, compare, and control how agents handle uncertainty across decision stages or coordinate with human oversight in regulated workflows. Useful for: Compliance and regulatory workflows (AML, KYC, content moderation)
Vault connections: Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Language Agents by Yu Su, Agentic RAG: Taxonomy of Architectures

8. Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: LLM, RAG
Code: github.com/answeryt/Fat-Cat
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Represent agent state as Markdown documents rather than nested JSON to reduce syntactic overhead and align with pre-training corpora, improving the model’s ability to focus on semantic reasoning. Existing agent frameworks force LLMs to devote attention to parsing rigid JSON syntax, creating a signal-to-noise ratio problem where syntactic processing competes with semantic reasoning for limited context. Useful for: Multi-step reasoning tasks requiring frequent state updates
Vault connections: Mind-Map Agent, Agentic Reasoning Pipeline, Multi-Agent Agentic RAG

9. SLIME: Stabilized Likelihood Implicit Margin Enforcement for Preference Optimization
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: RL, LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A reference-free preference alignment objective that decouples preference learning from generation quality by combining likelihood anchoring, probability stabilization, and dual-margin constraints. Direct preference optimization (DPO) methods suffer from objective mismatch where optimizing relative margins between chosen and rejected responses doesn’t preserve absolute likelihood of preferred outputs, leading to “unlearning” (degradation of high-quality outputs) and “formatting collapse” (over-penalization of rejected sequences). Useful for: LLM alignment and fine-tuning for preference-based learning
Vault connections: Obsidian Literature Review: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) in Finetuning Language Models, Test-Time Preference Optimization (TPO): A Breakthrough in LLM Alignment, LLM Alignment as Retriever Optimization: An Information Retrieval Perspective


🎯 Active Projects

2. Domain-Adaptive and Scalable Dense Retrieval for Content-Based Recommendation
Summary:
Train separate encoder towers for queries (reviews) and documents (item metadata) to map both into a shared semantic space, optimized via Multiple Negatives Ranking Loss to maximize similarity between matched pairs while minimizing similarity to other items in the batch. - Sparse keyword matching (BM25) fails under vocabulary mismatch between user intent and product metadata

Single-tower models are computationally expensive for retrieval at catalog scale
Standard contrastive learning doesn’t leverage the full negative information available in training batches Useful for: E-commerce recommendation and search under vocabulary mismatch

Deep dive: Full analysis

3. RedVisor: Reasoning-Aware Prompt Injection Defense via Zero-Copy KV Cache Reuse
Summary:
A lightweight, removable adapter positioned above a frozen LLM backbone that generates explainable reasoning paths during a gating phase, then remains “muted” during response generation, enabling both attack detection and guided safe response synthesis. Existing defenses face a critical trade-off—prevention-based fine-tuning degrades general utility (alignment tax), while detection-based filtering incurs prohibitive latency and memory costs. Useful for: Defending RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) systems against adversarial context injection
Deep dive: Full analysis

4. Nano-vLLM: How a vLLM-style inference engine works
Summary:
This paper directly addresses LLM inference optimization, which is critical for real-time recommendation and search systems that need to serve personalized rankings efficiently. Understanding batching, scheduling, prefix caching, and GPU resource management in Nano-vLLM is essential for deploying ranking models (like learning-to-rank LLMs) and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines at scale.
Deep dive: Full analysis

5. Rethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs
Summary:
Learn predictive-sufficient item representations directly from structured item features through masked auto-encoding, rather than relying on foundation model embeddings, to align representation learning with collaborative prediction objectives. Existing semantic ID methods decouple semantic embeddings from collaborative prediction by using foundation models trained on general text corpora. Useful for: Sequential recommendation with structured item catalogs
Deep dive: Full analysis

6. VisionTrim: Unified Vision Token Compression for Training-Free MLLM Acceleration
Summary:
Preserve essential visual tokens by combining global and local importance scores, eliminating redundant or non-salient tokens without training. Vision transformers in MLLMs generate excessive tokens that increase computational cost, especially for high-resolution images and videos. Useful for: High-resolution image understanding tasks (4K/8K images)
Deep dive: Full analysis

7. Hierarchical Adaptive Eviction for KV Cache Management in Multimodal Language Models
Summary:
Selectively prune visual tokens during KV cache pre-filling by identifying and retaining only tokens that receive high attention weights across both visual and textual attention patterns. Multimodal LLMs allocate equal KV cache space to all visual tokens regardless of their importance to the task. Useful for: Vision-language understanding tasks (VQA, image captioning) where image regions outside the region-of-interest are redundant
Deep dive: Full analysis

8. Data Distribution Matters: A Data-Centric Perspective on Context Compression for Large Language Model
Summary:
Systematically evaluate context compression quality through an autoencoder-based framework that measures semantic integrity by analyzing how data distribution dimensions (input data and intrinsic model knowledge) affect compression. Existing context compression research focuses exclusively on model-side improvements while ignoring how the data distribution itself impacts compression effectiveness. Useful for: Diagnosing why context compression fails on specific domains or datasets
Deep dive: Full analysis

9. OneMall: One Architecture, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce
Summary:
A specialized tokenization solution that converts multi-modal e-commerce items (products, short-video, live-streaming) into semantic tokens capturing both real-world semantics and business-specific item relations across different distribution scenarios. E-commerce recommendation systems struggle with unified representation across heterogeneous item distribution channels (product cards, short-video, live-streaming). Useful for: Multi-scenario e-commerce recommendation (product discovery, short-video feeds, live-shopping)
Deep dive: Full analysis

10. Read As Human: Compressing Context via Parallelizable Close Reading and Skimming
Summary:
Partition context into segments and adaptively apply close reading (full retention) to high-relevance segments while compressing low-relevance segments into compact summary vectors based on query relevance. Large Language Models suffer from computational inefficiency and information redundancy when processing long contexts. Useful for: Long-context question answering on documents (16K-32K tokens)
Deep dive: Full analysis

11. PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models
Summary:
Disaggregate the computation of each predictive step across different parameter sets to decouple model capacity from inference cost in draft models. Existing speculative decoding methods require parametrically larger draft models to achieve better prediction accuracy, but larger models incur substantial computational overhead during inference. Useful for: Speculative decoding for LLM acceleration
Deep dive: Full analysis

12. ClueTracer: Question-to-Vision Clue Tracing for Training-Free Hallucination Suppression in Multimodal Reasoning
Summary:
A metric for quantitatively assessing whether a multimodal reasoning model correctly retrieves task-relevant visual clues from images during inference. Existing metrics for evaluating hallucination focus on output correctness but don’t diagnose why hallucinations occur—specifically, they fail to measure whether the model gathered the right visual evidence before generating text. Useful for: Diagnosing hallucination root causes in reasoning models
Deep dive: Full analysis

digest/2026-02-04.title: Research Digest — Wed 2026-02-04
digest/2026-02-04.content:
Research Digest — Wed 2026-02-04
Scanned 263 papers • Selected 8 • Filtered 2 off-domain, 24 duplicates • Runtime: 5.7 min

Papers



























































#PaperTagsLinks1GoT: Effective Graph-of-Thought Reasoning in Language ModelsLLM, SearchLink • Code • Note2Learning to Reason via Program Generation, Emulation, and SearchLLM, SearchLink • Note3Can Custom Models Learn In-Context? An Exploration of Hybrid Architecture Performance on In-Context Learning TasksLLM, Search, EmbeddingsLink • Code • Note4VeSX: A Framework Featured by Verification, Self-Correction and In-context Learning for Web Automation TasksLLM, RAGLink • Note5A Field Guide to Automatic Evaluation of LLM-Generated SummariesLLM, RecSysLink • Note6ReNeuIR at SIGIR 2024: The Third Workshop on Reaching Efficiency in Neural Information RetrievalLLM, Search, RAGLink • Note7CoDa: Constrained Generation based Data Augmentation for Low-Resource NLPLLM, RAGarXiv • Code • Note8Using tournaments to calculate AUROC for zero-shot classification with LLMsLLM, RecSys, RAGarXiv • Note

Details
1. GoT: Effective Graph-of-Thought Reasoning in Language Models
Source: S2:NAACL-HLT | Tags: LLM, Search
Code: https://github
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Represent reasoning processes as a graph structure rather than a linear chain, where thought units are nodes and their relationships are edges, capturing the non-sequential nature of complex reasoning. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning assumes a linear, sequential thought process, but human reasoning often involves non-linear connections, parallel considerations, and revisiting earlier thoughts—which CoT cannot model. Useful for: Mathematical reasoning tasks (e.g., AQUA-RAT)
Vault connections: Self-Consistency Decoding, Hierarchical Reasoning Model, Modeling Open-World Cognition as On-Demand Synthesis of Probabilistic Model

2. Learning to Reason via Program Generation, Emulation, and Search
Source: OpenReview:NeurIPS 2024 poster | Tags: LLM, Search
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Extend program synthesis to soft reasoning tasks by generating Python pseudo-programs with undefined leaf functions, then training the LM to emulate execution by filling in those gaps with its own knowledge. Standard program synthesis only works for algorithmic tasks with well-defined operations; it cannot handle reasoning tasks involving commonsense, moral judgment, or subjective interpretation that don’t map cleanly to executable code. Useful for: Commonsense reasoning tasks (e.g., Winograd schemas, social situations)
Vault connections: Agentic Reasoning Design Patterns, Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Self-Improving Language Models and the Evolution of Reasoning in AI

3. Can Custom Models Learn In-Context? An Exploration of Hybrid Architecture Performance on In-Context Learning Tasks
Source: OpenReview:Submitted to NeurIPS 2024 | Tags: LLM, Search, Embeddings
Code: github.com/anonymousforneurips64/neurips2024-submission21757}
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A systematic empirical framework for evaluating how architectural mixing (GPT-2/LLaMA and LLaMA/Mamba block combinations) affects in-context learning performance on regression tasks, introducing the “ICL regression score” as a unified metric. Lack of understanding about which architectural components (MHA vs. other sequence transformers like Mamba) are critical for in-context learning capability, and how hybrid architectures compare to homogeneous designs. Useful for: Designing efficient ICL-optimized architectures for resource-constrained deployments
Vault connections: Learning without training: The implicit dynamics of in-context learning, Pignal, Linear Attention as In-Context Learners

4. VeSX: A Framework Featured by Verification, Self-Correction and In-context Learning for Web Automation Tasks
Source: OpenReview:Submitted to ICLR 2025 | Tags: LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A three-component framework that enhances LLM-based web automation by verifying subtask completion against subgoals, hierarchically correcting planning and execution errors through reflection and replanning, and improving in-context learning via trajectory partitioning and metadata-enriched exemplar retrieval. Addresses the infeasibility of subtasks in LLM-driven web automation where traditional planning-execution workflows fail due to incorrect plans, failed executions, or inability to learn from past trajectories effectively. Useful for: Web automation and browser-based task completion (form filling, navigation, data extraction)
Vault connections: Promptomatix: An Automatic Prompt Optimization Framework for Large Language Model, A Survey of Context Engineering for Large Language Model, Dynamic Tool Invocation

5. A Field Guide to Automatic Evaluation of LLM-Generated Summaries
Source: S2:Annual International ACM SIGIR Conference on Research and Development in Information Retrieval | Tags: LLM, RecSys
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A systematic approach to evaluating LLM-generated summaries using a combination of traditional NLP metrics, LLM-as-judge methods, and specialized hallucination detection, structured around dimensions like faithfulness, relevance, coherence, and fluency. Traditional metrics (ROUGE, BLEU) only capture lexical overlap and miss semantic quality, while purely LLM-based evaluation lacks reliability and standardization. Useful for: Production LLM summarization systems requiring continuous quality monitoring without per-instance human review
Vault connections: LLM-based Pseudo-Label Generation and Multi-Metric Evaluation, G-Eval Simply Explained – LLM-as-a-judge for LLM evaluation, Large Language Model Enhanced Recommender Systems: A Survey

6. ReNeuIR at SIGIR 2024: The Third Workshop on Reaching Efficiency in Neural Information Retrieval
Source: S2:Annual International ACM SIGIR Conference on Research and Development in Information Retrieval | Tags: LLM, Search, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A standardized framework for measuring and comparing efficiency metrics (time, space, resource, sample, and energy) alongside effectiveness in neural information retrieval systems, addressing the lack of fair comparability due to varying hardware, software, and experimental settings. Modern IR evaluations focus heavily on effectiveness (relevance, ranking quality) while neglecting efficiency, making it impossible to fairly compare the cost-benefit ratio of different neural retrieval methods across different experimental setups and hardware configurations. Useful for: Comparing dense retrieval models vs. sparse retrieval methods on production constraints
Vault connections: Self-Retrieval: End-to-End Information Retrieval with One Large Language Model, Hybrid Evaluation: Precision, Recall, User Satisfaction Triad, Collapse of Dense Retriever: Takeaways and Techniques

7. CoDa: Constrained Generation based Data Augmentation for Low-Resource NLP
Source: S2:NAACL-HLT | Tags: LLM, RAG
Code: github.com/Sreyan88/CoDa
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Generate synthetic training data for low-resource NLP tasks by extracting simple constraints from existing instances and prompting instruction-following LLMs to create new examples that satisfy those constraints. Low-resource NLP tasks lack sufficient training data, and existing augmentation methods either require expensive fine-tuning, produce biased outputs, or fail to provide explicit control over the generation process. Useful for: Low-resource classification tasks with limited labeled data
Vault connections: Drama: Diverse Augmentation from Large Language Models to Smaller Dense Retrievers, Dataset Featurization, Large Language Model for Synthetic Training Data in Dense retrieval

8. Using tournaments to calculate AUROC for zero-shot classification with LLMs
Source: S2:Findings of ACL. EMNLP. Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing | Tags: LLM, RecSys, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Convert zero-shot binary classification into pairwise instance comparisons, then use the Elo rating system to rank instances and compute AUROC, enabling proper evaluation of LLMs without requiring probability calibration or modifiable decision boundaries. LLMs lack calibrated probabilities and adjustable decision thresholds for zero-shot classification, making it impossible to fairly compare them to supervised classifiers using standard metrics like AUROC that require ranking all instances by confidence. Useful for: Zero-shot classification evaluation where LLMs replace traditional classifiers
Vault connections: Zero-Shot Generalization Scaling in LLM Dense Retrieval, Consolidating Ranking and Relevance Predictions of Large Language Models, Large Language Models for recommendation system


🎯 Active Projects

2. Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems
Summary:
Mitigate ADC quantization errors in in-memory computing systems through activation-shifting and weight-reshaping to control distribution statistics, combined with bit augmentation during quantization-aware training to improve model tolerance. Analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) in in-memory computing (IMC) systems use limited precision for efficiency, causing substantial quantization errors in neural network outputs that degrade accuracy. Useful for: Edge AI deployment on in-memory computing hardware with constrained ADC precision
Deep dive: Full analysis

digest/2026-02-05.title: Research Digest — Thu 2026-02-05
digest/2026-02-05.content:
Research Digest — Thu 2026-02-05
Scanned 448 papers • Selected 10 • Filtered 0 off-domain, 34 duplicates • Runtime: 9.7 min

Papers







































































#PaperTagsLinks1Decomposing Query-Key Feature Interactions Using Contrastive CovariancesRAG, Embeddings, Search, TransformerarXiv • Note2From Competition to Collaboration: Designing Sustainable Mechanisms Between LLMs and Online ForumsRAG, RecSys, LLMarXiv • Note3Enforcing Monotonic Progress in Legal Cross-Examination: Preventing Long-Horizon Stagnation in LLM-Based InquiryRAG, LLMarXiv • Note4The Fine-Grained Complexity of Gradient Computation for Training Large Language ModelsRecSys, LLMarXiv • Note5ReThinker: Scientific Reasoning by Rethinking with Guided Reflection and Confidence ControlRAG, Search, RecSys, LLMarXiv • Note6Scaling Agentic Verifier for Competitive CodingRAG, RL, Search, RecSysarXiv • Note7PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVRRAG, RL, Search, LLMarXiv • Code • Note8UnMaskFork: Test-Time Scaling for Masked Diffusion via Deterministic Action BranchingDiffusion, RAG, Search, LLMarXiv • Note9LiteToken: Removing Intermediate Merge Residues From BPE TokenizersRAG, LLMarXiv • Note10BEHAV-E! You are Not Just a Number to Us, but an R2048 EmbeddingEmbeddings, LLM, Search, RecSysLink • Code • Note

Details
1. Decomposing Query-Key Feature Interactions Using Contrastive Covariances
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: RAG, Embeddings, Search, Transformer, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Decompose the bilinear query-key (QK) space of attention heads into low-rank, human-interpretable subspaces by computing contrastive covariances between features that produce high vs. low attention scores. Attention mechanisms lack interpretability at the feature level—we can see which tokens receive high attention but not why specific query and key features align to produce those attention scores. Useful for: Interpretability analysis of transformer-based language models
Vault connections: Attention Head Specialization for Matching Detection, Product Attention (Attention Score Function), Bi_Interaction Attention (Attention Score Function)

2. From Competition to Collaboration: Designing Sustainable Mechanisms Between LLMs and Online Forums
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: RAG, RecSys, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A game-theoretic mechanism design framework where LLMs propose questions to Q&A forums, which selectively publish them to balance their own utility (community engagement, content quality) against the LLM’s utility (training data acquisition), enabling sustainable knowledge exchange despite incentive misalignment. Addresses the paradox where GenAI systems cannibalize traffic from Q&A forums while depending on forum data for improvement, leading to unsustainable knowledge ecosystems where forums lose incentive to generate the high-quality content that LLMs need. Useful for: Sustainable AI-human knowledge platforms (Stack Overflow, Reddit, Quora)
Vault connections: Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) System, Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Rethinking Group Recommender Systems in the Era of Generative AI: From One-Shot Recommendations to Agentic Group Decision Support

3. Enforcing Monotonic Progress in Legal Cross-Examination: Preventing Long-Horizon Stagnation in LLM-Based Inquiry
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RAG, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A neuro-symbolic architecture that enforces monotonic progress in long-horizon LLM tasks by combining probabilistic LLM generation with an external deterministic state controller that tracks accumulated Key Information Units (KIUs). Addresses procedural stagnation in LLMs—where models maintain linguistic coherence but fail to make verifiable progress toward task completion, especially in constrained domains like legal cross-examination that require explicit advancement through mandatory information-gathering steps. Useful for: Legal proceedings requiring structured information elicitation (depositions, witness examination)
Vault connections: Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Hierarchical Reasoning Model, CLEAR: Error Analysis via LLM-as-a-Judge Made Easy

4. The Fine-Grained Complexity of Gradient Computation for Training Large Language Models
Source: S2:Neural Information Processing Systems | Tags: RecSys, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Proves that computing gradients for attention layers in LLM training has nearly identical computational complexity as forward passes—almost-linear time in some parameter regimes, but no truly sub-quadratic algorithm exists in other regimes unless SETH (Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis) is false. While prior work ([Alman & Song, NeurIPS 2023]) characterized the complexity of forward computation (attention evaluation) in LLMs, the computational hardness of the backward pass (gradient computation) was unresolved. Useful for: Theoretical foundations for understanding fundamental limits of LLM training acceleration
Vault connections: Large Language Models as Optimizers, Efficient Attention Mechanisms for Large Language Model: A Survey, TALLRec — aligning a large language model with recommendation

5. ReThinker: Scientific Reasoning by Rethinking with Guided Reflection and Confidence Control
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: RAG, Search, RecSys, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A stage-wise Solver-Critic-Selector framework that dynamically allocates test-time compute based on model confidence, orchestrating retrieval, tool use, and multi-agent reasoning through adaptive tool invocation, guided multi-dimensional reflection, and confidence-weighted selection. Existing scientific reasoning systems suffer from rigid tool pipelines, brittle multi-agent coordination, and inefficient test-time scaling—leading to poor performance on expert-level benchmarks like Humanity’s Last Exam (HLE) where flexible, confidence-aware reasoning is essential. Useful for: Expert-level scientific reasoning benchmarks (HLE, GPQA, MATH-500)
Vault connections: Synergistic Tool Integration in Agentic Reasoning, Mind-Map Agent, RANK1: Test-Time Compute for Reranking in Information Retrieval

6. Scaling Agentic Verifier for Competitive Coding
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RAG, RL, Search, RecSys, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
An execution-based agent that actively reasons about program behavior to generate highly discriminative test inputs that expose behavioral discrepancies among candidate solutions, enabling effective test-time scaling for competitive programming. Addresses the limitations of existing execution-based reranking methods which either struggle with difficult test case generation or rely on inefficient random input sampling, resulting in poor discrimination between correct and incorrect candidate solutions. Useful for: Competitive programming and coding competitions
Vault connections: Agentic RAG: Taxonomy of Architectures, Agentic Workflow Pattern: Prompt Chaining, Agentic RAG

7. PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR
Source: HuggingFace | Tags: RAG, RL, Search, LLM
Code: huggingface.co/collections/jmhb/papersearchqa
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Train language model agents to search scientific paper databases and answer technical questions using reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where agents learn to iteratively query, retrieve, and reason over academic abstracts without supervision on intermediate search steps. Existing RLVR search agents focus on general-domain QA and don’t handle the technical vocabulary, reasoning complexity, and citation-grounded verification needed for scientific question-answering. Useful for: Scientific literature review and hypothesis generation for AI researchers
Vault connections: RAG-R1 – Incentivize the Search and Reasoning Capabilities of LLM through multi-query parallelism, Agentic Reasoning: Main Takeaways and Techniques, Web-Search Agent with Query Breakdown and Rerank

8. UnMaskFork: Test-Time Scaling for Masked Diffusion via Deterministic Action Branching
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: Diffusion, RAG, Search, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Treat the unmasking trajectory of Masked Diffusion Language Models as a search tree and apply Monte Carlo Tree Search to find optimal generation paths through deterministic partial unmasking actions. Standard test-time scaling for Masked Diffusion Language Models relies on stochastic sampling which doesn’t efficiently explore the space of possible unmasking sequences; UMF provides a principled way to leverage inference-time compute for MDLMs, extending test-time scaling beyond autoregressive models. Useful for: Complex code generation tasks where solution quality scales with inference compute
Vault connections: Early-Exit Large Language Models, Accelerating Inference of Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Sparse Context Selection, LLM Inference Performance: The Team Mental Model + Experiment Playbook

9. LiteToken: Removing Intermediate Merge Residues From BPE Tokenizers
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RAG, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Prune BPE vocabulary by removing intermediate merge residues—tokens that are retained in the vocabulary but rarely emitted during actual tokenization because they get further merged. BPE tokenizers accumulate tokens that were frequent during merge learning but are rarely used in practice, wasting vocabulary capacity (thus embedding parameters) and creating vulnerabilities to adversarial or atypical inputs that trigger fragmented tokenization. Useful for: Reducing embedding layer parameters in memory-constrained deployments
Vault connections: Bitter Lesson & the tokenization Reckoning, Transformer tree, Top 50 Large Language Model Interview Questions

10. BEHAV-E! You are Not Just a Number to Us, but an R2048 Embedding
Source: S2:Proceedings of the Recommender Systems 2025 | Tags: Embeddings, LLM, Search, RecSys
Code: github.com/knife982000/RecSys2025Challenge
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A self-supervised architecture that learns universal 2048-dimensional user embeddings from multi-event behavioral data by fusing temporal activity patterns (via kernel density estimation), categorical item/category embeddings, and semantic search query representations (via LSTM autoencoder). Traditional user representations either require task-specific modeling for each downstream prediction task (churn, propensity, recommendations) or fail to capture the multi-modal nature of user behavior (temporal patterns, item interactions, search intent), limiting transferability and expressiveness. Useful for: Cross-task personalization systems requiring a single user representation for multiple downstream tasks (churn, propensity scoring, recommendations)
Vault connections: Target-Aware Embedding for Knowledge Transfer, ID-Based User Activity Sequence Modeling, Session Partitioning for User Embedding with LLM


🎯 Active Projects

2. Quant VideoGen: Auto-Regressive Long Video Generation via 2-Bit KV-Cache Quantization
Summary:
Training-free KV cache quantization framework for autoregressive video diffusion models that achieves 2-bit precision through semantic-aware smoothing and progressive residual quantization, reducing memory by up to 7× while maintaining generation quality. KV cache memory explosion in autoregressive video generation (often >30GB), preventing deployment on consumer hardware and limiting long-horizon consistency in identity, layout, and motion due to constrained working memory budgets. Useful for: Autoregressive video diffusion models (e.g., LongCat Video, WorldPlay)
Deep dive: Full analysis

3. NeuroCanvas: VLLM-Powered Robust Seizure Detection by Reformulating Multichannel EEG as Image
Summary:
Convert multi-channel EEG signals into visual representations (images) that Vision-Language Models can process efficiently, using entropy-guided channel selection to identify seizure-relevant signals. 1. Useful for: Real-time clinical seizure monitoring from long-term EEG recordings
Deep dive: Full analysis

4. HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing
Summary:
Interleave sparse attention layers with periodic full attention layers, where each full attention layer acts as an oracle to identify important tokens and provides shared KV caches for subsequent sparse layers, eliminating proxy-based token selection and reducing both computation and memory. Existing sparse attention methods either (1) use suboptimal heuristics/proxies to predict token importance, or (2) reduce computation but not KV cache memory; HySparse addresses both by leveraging full attention layers as precise oracles for token selection and enabling KV cache reuse across sparse layers. Useful for: Long-context LLM inference (retrieval, summarization, multi-document QA) where KV cache becomes memory bottleneck
Deep dive: Full analysis

5. Beyond Tokens: Semantic-Aware Speculative Decoding for Efficient Inference by Probing Internal States
Summary:
Verifies entire semantic sequences instead of individual tokens during speculative decoding by probing the target model’s internal hidden states to estimate the probability of generating sequences with specific meanings, enabling acceptance of semantically equivalent but token-wise different draft sequences. Traditional speculative decoding operates at the token level and rejects drafts that are semantically equivalent but use different tokens (e.g., “4” vs “four”), leading to inefficient verification and low acceptance rates, especially problematic for Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) that generate lengthy chains of thought. Useful for: Accelerating Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) like DeepSeek-R1 and QwQ that generate verbose chain-of-thought reasoning
Deep dive: Full analysis

6. KDD 2025 Workshop on Inference Optimization for Generative AI
Summary:
Create a collaborative interdisciplinary platform bridging production-oriented practitioners and researchers to exchange methodologies and identify challenges in efficient Large Language Model inference for GenAI applications. Despite rapid advancement in LLM inference optimization, there is limited knowledge exchange between practitioners deploying production systems and researchers experimenting with new GenAI concepts, leading to siloed development and missed opportunities for cross-pollination of ideas. Useful for: Deploying LLM-centered applications with critical latency/cost constraints
Deep dive: Full analysis

7. Swordsman: Entropy-Driven Adaptive Block Partition for Efficient Diffusion Language Models
Summary:
Dynamically partition diffusion language model output into blocks by detecting entropy shifts between adjacent tokens, aligning block boundaries with natural semantic/syntactic constituent boundaries rather than using fixed-size blocks. Fixed block partitioning in block-wise decoding fragments semantic and syntactic constituents, degrading both inference quality and efficiency in diffusion language models. Useful for: Diffusion language models requiring fast inference (SEDD, MDLM, etc.)
Deep dive: Full analysis

8. Efficient Autoregressive Video Diffusion with Dummy Head
Summary:
A training-free optimization for autoregressive video diffusion models that identifies and exploits the fact that ~25% of attention heads focus almost exclusively on the current frame, enabling heterogeneous KV cache allocation across heads to reduce memory and increase throughput. Autoregressive video diffusion models suffer from significant computational overhead due to redundant attention patterns—many heads unnecessarily cache historical frame information they rarely use, leading to memory bottlenecks and slow generation speeds. Useful for: Autoregressive video generation models requiring real-time or near-real-time performance
Deep dive: Full analysis

9. Rethinking Perplexity: Revealing the Impact of Input Length on Perplexity Evaluation in LLMs
Summary:
A system-conscious evaluation framework that treats input length as a first-class variable, measuring perplexity alongside latency, memory, and cost across different scoring protocols (direct accumulation vs. sliding window) to reveal length-induced biases in LLM evaluation. Perplexity evaluations are unreliable when input length varies, yet existing benchmarks ignore how evaluation protocol and context length interact to produce unfair cross-model comparisons and mask deployment costs. Useful for: Fair benchmarking of LLMs when comparing models with different context window capabilities
Deep dive: Full analysis

10. BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models
Summary:
Construct a variable quantization grid via bit-plane decomposition and scalar coefficients instead of fixed uniform intervals, enabling more flexible error minimization for extreme low-bit (2-3 bit) LLM quantization. Existing post-training quantization (PTQ) methods enforce shape-invariant grids (e.g., fixed UINT2 intervals) that severely restrict the feasible optimization space, causing quality deterioration below 4 bits. Useful for: Serving 70B+ parameter LLMs on consumer GPUs (e.g., RTX 3090 with 24GB VRAM)
Deep dive: Full analysis

11. Diffusion Generative Recommendation with Continuous Tokens
Summary:
Replace discrete vector-quantized tokens with continuous VAE-encoded representations and use conditional diffusion modeling to generate user preferences in LLM-based recommender systems. Existing LLM-based recommender systems rely on vector quantization (VQ) for tokenization, which introduces lossy compression and suboptimal learning due to non-differentiable argmin operations that break gradient flow. Useful for: Cold-start recommendation scenarios where continuous representations provide richer semantic encoding
Deep dive: Full analysis

12. OAT: Ordered Action Tokenization
Summary:
A learned action tokenizer that discretizes continuous robot action chunks into an ordered, causally-structured token sequence using transformer registers, finite scalar quantization, and ordering-inducing training, enabling efficient autoregressive policy learning with anytime decoding. Existing action tokenization methods either produce prohibitively long sequences (analytical discretization) or lack causal structure (learned latent tokenizers like VQ-VAE), making them incompatible with efficient autoregressive next-token prediction for robot control policies. Useful for: Robot learning and manipulation tasks requiring continuous action control
Deep dive: Full analysis

digest/2026-02-06.title: Research Digest — Fri 2026-02-06
digest/2026-02-06.content:
Research Digest — Fri 2026-02-06
Scanned 360 papers • Selected 6 • Filtered 4 off-domain, 25 duplicates • Runtime: 8.1 min

Papers















































#PaperTagsLinks1SwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMsEmbeddings, LLM, SearcharXiv • Note2Correctness-Optimized Residual Activation Lens (CORAL): Transferrable and Calibration-Aware Inference-Time SteeringLLM, RAG, RecSysarXiv • Note3Inverse Depth Scaling From Most Layers Being SimilarLLM, RAGarXiv • Note4RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMsEmbeddings, LLMarXiv • Note5Rich-Media Re-Ranker: A User Satisfaction-Driven LLM Re-ranking Framework for Rich-Media SearchLLM, RL, RecSys, RAGarXiv • Note6KV-CoRE: Benchmarking Data-Dependent Low-Rank Compressibility of KV-Caches in LLMsLLM, RAGarXiv • Note

Details
1. SwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMs
Source: HuggingFace | Tags: Embeddings, LLM, Search
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A multimodal LLM that dynamically switches among text-only, vision-only (continuous hidden states as visual thoughts), and interleaved vision-text reasoning modes based on the input query, unifying next-token and next-embedding prediction in a hybrid autoregressive framework. Existing MLLMs use fixed reasoning patterns—either textual Chain-of-Thought (CoT) which limits vision-intensive tasks, or continuous visual thoughts which degrade text-based logical reasoning—instead of adaptively selecting the most suitable thinking modality for each query type. Useful for: Vision-language tasks requiring spatial reasoning, counting, or fine-grained visual understanding (where continuous visual thoughts outperform text)
Vault connections: Progressive Transition for Embedding Adaptation, Multimodal Textual Knowledge Generation via Open-Vocabulary VLMs, Talk by Luke Zettlemoyer on Unified Multimodal Architectures

2. Correctness-Optimized Residual Activation Lens (CORAL): Transferrable and Calibration-Aware Inference-Time Steering
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: LLM, RAG, RecSys
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A regularized inference-time steering method that trains weight-decay MLP probes on model internal activations to extract distributed correctness signals, enabling calibration-aware answer selection without retraining the base model. LLMs exhibit persistent miscalibration (especially post-RLHF), and existing inference-time steering methods optimize proxies (e.g., confidence) rather than correctness itself, while retraining for better calibration is prohibitively expensive. Useful for: Multiple-choice question answering (MCQA) tasks where calibration matters (medical, legal, educational domains)
Vault connections: LLM Inference Performance: The Team Mental Model + Experiment Playbook, context rot, Context Reordering

3. Inverse Depth Scaling From Most Layers Being Similar
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Loss in large language models scales inversely proportional to depth (L^-1) because most layers are functionally similar and reduce error through ensemble averaging rather than compositional learning. Explains why simply adding more layers to LLMs yields diminishing returns and provides theoretical grounding for why current architectures are depth-inefficient, helping researchers understand where to focus architectural innovations. Useful for: Guiding architectural search for depth-efficient LLMs by identifying what prevents compositional learning
Vault connections: Zero-Shot Generalization Scaling in LLM Dense Retrieval, context rot, Sparse Autoencoders and Mechanistic Interpretability of Large Language Models

4. RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: Embeddings, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A quantization framework that enforces a residual hierarchy by sequentially deriving binary paths from a single shared full-precision weight, ensuring each path explicitly corrects the errors of the preceding path rather than learning redundant features. Addresses pathological inter-path adaptation in residual binarization during quantization-aware training (QAT), where parallel binary paths co-adapt and learn redundant features instead of maintaining an error-compensation structure, limiting model expressiveness and degrading accuracy. Useful for: Extreme quantization (2-bit) for LLM deployment on resource-constrained devices
Vault connections: Model Compression: Pruning, Quantization, and Knowledge Distillation in LLM Embedding Models, MultiLoRA: Democratizing LoRA for Better Multi-Task Learning, Early-Exit Large Language Models

5. Rich-Media Re-Ranker: A User Satisfaction-Driven LLM Re-ranking Framework for Rich-Media Search
Source: arXiv:cs.IR | Tags: LLM, RL, RecSys, RAG, Search
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A multi-stage LLM-based re-ranking framework that improves search satisfaction by decomposing queries into sub-intents and integrating multimodal signals (text + visual content via VLM) with multi-faceted ranking principles, enhanced through multi-task reinforcement learning. Traditional re-ranking methods inadequately model complex user intents and ignore rich side information (especially visual perception signals from cover images/thumbnails), leading to suboptimal user satisfaction in rich-media search scenarios. Useful for: Rich-media search systems (video search, image search, e-commerce product search)
Vault connections: User Feedback Alignment for LLM-powered Exploration in Large-scale Recommendation Systems, Hybrid Evaluation: Precision, Recall, User Satisfaction Triad, A Survey of Large Language Model Empowered Agents for Recommendation and Search: Towards Next-Generation Information Retrieval

6. KV-CoRE: Benchmarking Data-Dependent Low-Rank Compressibility of KV-Caches in LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A gradient-free SVD-based method that quantifies the data-dependent low-rank compressibility of KV-caches across layers and datasets using Normalized Effective Rank as a compressibility metric. Existing KV-cache compression methods treat all layers and data uniformly, ignoring that different layers and input types exhibit vastly different compressibility characteristics, leading to suboptimal compression strategies. Useful for: Designing layer-specific compression policies (compress late layers more aggressively)
Vault connections: Heavy Hitter Oracle (H2O), LLM Inference Performance: The Team Mental Model + Experiment Playbook, Early-Exit Large Language Models


🎯 Active Projects

2. SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration
Summary:
Construct a draft model for speculative decoding by pruning low-impact layers from the target LLM based on Fisher Information Trace (FIT) sensitivity scores, entirely training-free and maintaining output compatibility. Existing speculative decoding approaches require costly draft model training, tuning, or specialized search procedures; SDFP eliminates these barriers by directly deriving the draft from the target model’s layer importance, enabling plug-and-play deployment without auxiliary models or hyperparameter optimization. Useful for: Low-latency interactive multimedia applications (captioning, dialogue, creative generation)
Deep dive: Full analysis

3. DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding
Summary:
Use a lightweight block diffusion model as a parallel draft generator for speculative decoding, eliminating the sequential bottleneck of autoregressive drafting while maintaining high acceptance rates through context-conditioned generation. Traditional speculative decoding still relies on autoregressive draft models that generate tokens sequentially, limiting parallelism and practical speedups despite parallel verification; existing diffusion LLMs underperform autoregressive models in quality. Useful for: Low-latency LLM serving where inference speed is critical (chatbots, real-time assistants)
Deep dive: Full analysis

4. FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion
Summary:
Reuse stable attention outputs from tokens outside the current block across diffusion steps by caching block-external attention, eliminating redundant computation while maintaining generation quality. Block diffusion models for long-context generation (video, text) repeatedly compute attention over a growing KV cache at every diffusion step, causing substantial overhead despite the attention outputs from past blocks remaining largely stable. Useful for: Long-form video generation with autoregressive diffusion models
Deep dive: Full analysis

5. GLASS: A Generative Recommender for Long-sequence Modeling via SID-Tier and Semantic Search
Summary:
A generative recommender framework that integrates long-term user interaction sequences into semantic ID (SID) generation through a two-stage process: SID-Tier for predicting initial semantic tokens using compact codebook features, and semantic hard search for dynamically retrieving relevant history based on generated coarse-grained tokens. Generative recommender systems struggle to effectively leverage extensive historical user sequences (hundreds to thousands of interactions) when generating recommendations. Useful for: E-commerce platforms with extensive user browsing/purchase histories (TAOBAO-MM dataset)
Deep dive: Full analysis

6. Variational Speculative Decoding: Rethinking Draft Training from Token Likelihood to Sequence Acceptance
Summary:
Formulates draft model training for speculative decoding as variational inference over latent draft paths, maximizing the marginal probability of target-model acceptance rather than optimizing single greedy trajectories. Existing speculative decoding methods optimize draft models for single greedy trajectories, creating a training-decoding discrepancy since actual decoding involves verifying and ranking multiple sampled draft paths, leading to suboptimal acceptance rates and speedup. Useful for: Accelerating inference for large language models (LLMs) in production serving
Deep dive: Full analysis

7. Light Forcing: Accelerating Autoregressive Video Diffusion via Sparse Attention
Summary:
A sparse attention framework for autoregressive video diffusion that progressively allocates sparsity across chunks using contribution estimation, combined with hierarchical frame-and-block-level context selection. Existing sparse attention methods degrade performance on autoregressive (AR) video models because they (1) treat chunks in isolation without considering inter-chunk dependencies, and (2) fail to leverage informative past context that AR models accumulate during sequential generation. Useful for: Autoregressive video diffusion models requiring long-context generation
Deep dive: Full analysis

8. RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs
Summary:
A quantization framework that enforces a residual hierarchy by sequentially deriving binary paths from a single shared full-precision weight, ensuring each path explicitly corrects the errors of the preceding path rather than learning redundant features. Addresses pathological inter-path adaptation in residual binarization during quantization-aware training (QAT), where parallel binary paths co-adapt and learn redundant features instead of maintaining an error-compensation structure, limiting model expressiveness and degrading accuracy. Useful for: Extreme quantization (2-bit) for LLM deployment on resource-constrained devices
Deep dive: Full analysis

9. Regularized Calibration with Successive Rounding for Post-Training Quantization
Summary:
Interpolate between symmetric and asymmetric quantization calibration to regularize post-training quantization (PTQ), combined with a successive rounding procedure that minimizes quantization error while allowing controllable compute-quality trade-offs. Standard PTQ methods suffer from activation distribution mismatch during calibration and lack principled ways to balance quantization quality against computational cost. Useful for: Deploying large language models (LLMs) on memory/compute-constrained hardware (edge devices, mobile)
Deep dive: Full analysis

10. Price of universality in vector quantization is at most 0.11 bit
Summary:
A single vector quantization codebook can achieve near-optimal rate-distortion performance across all possible input statistics simultaneously, sacrificing at most 0.11 bits per dimension compared to an input-adapted waterfilling codebook. Traditional optimal vector quantization requires aligning the codebook with the principal component analysis (PCA) directions of input data X, making the codebook dependent on X’s statistics—this is impractical for deployment scenarios like LLM weight quantization where diverse input distributions are encountered. Useful for: Weight-only quantization for LLM deployment (INT4/INT8 storage formats)
Deep dive: Full analysis

11. Hybrid Gated Flow (HGF): Stabilizing 1.58-bit LLMs via Selective Low-Rank Correction
Summary:
A dual-stream architecture that combines a memory-efficient 1.58-bit ternary LLM backbone with a learnable low-rank FP16 correction path controlled by adaptive gates, recovering ~55% of the quality loss from extreme quantization with only 12-15% memory overhead. Addresses the fundamental tradeoff in extreme quantization (1.58-bit) where models like BitNet b1.58 achieve dramatic memory reduction for edge deployment but suffer 20-25% perplexity degradation compared to FP16 baselines. Useful for: Edge device LLM deployment where memory bandwidth is the bottleneck (smartphones, IoT devices)
Deep dive: Full analysis

digest/2026-02-07.title: Research Digest — Sat 2026-02-07
digest/2026-02-07.content:
Research Digest — Sat 2026-02-07
Scanned 28 papers • Selected 5 • Filtered 0 off-domain, 9 duplicates • Runtime: 2.9 min

Papers









































#PaperTagsLinks1Towards Reducible Uncertainty Modeling for Reliable Large Language Model AgentsSearch, LLMarXiv • Note2A Task-Centric Perspective on Recommendation SystemsRecSys, LLM, SearcharXiv • Note3CoreMatching: A Co-adaptive Sparse Inference Framework with Token and Neuron Pruning for Comprehensive Acceleration of Vision-Language ModelsLLM, RAGarXiv • Code • Note4MemSkill: Learning and Evolving Memory Skills for Self-Evolving AgentsRecSys, LLM, RAGarXiv • Note5Focus-dLLM: Accelerating Long-Context Diffusion LLM Inference via Confidence-Guided Context FocusingDiffusion, LLM, RAGarXiv • Code • Note

Details
1. Towards Reducible Uncertainty Modeling for Reliable Large Language Model Agents
Source: HuggingFace | Tags: Search, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
