A principled framework that models agent uncertainty quantification (UQ) as a dynamic reduction process where uncertainty decreases through interactive actions, rather than as passive accumulation across agent trajectory steps. Existing UQ methods designed for single-turn QA fail in multi-step agent scenarios because they treat uncertainty as accumulating over time—an approach that breaks down when agents can actively reduce uncertainty through interactive tool use, feedback gathering, and environmental queries in open-world settings. Useful for: Multi-step reasoning agents that use external tools (code execution, search, APIs)
Vault connections: Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Language Agents by Yu Su, Mind-Map Agent

2. A Task-Centric Perspective on Recommendation Systems
Source: S2:RecSys | Tags: RecSys, LLM, Search
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A structured analytical framework that decomposes recommender system problems into explicit task components (input-output structure, temporal dynamics, candidate selection, interaction complexity) to enable fair evaluation and domain-appropriate model design. Addresses the mismatch between abstract “recommend items to users” problem definitions and the domain-specific realities of deployed systems, which leads to unfair benchmarking, inappropriate model choices, and evaluation metrics that don’t reflect real-world performance. Useful for: Cross-domain RecSys research where comparing methods across different platforms (e.g., e-commerce vs. streaming vs. social feeds)
Vault connections: Multi-Task Learning in Recommender Systems, User Feedback Alignment for LLM-powered Exploration in Large-scale Recommendation Systems, Mini-Lesson: LLMs in Recommender Systems

3. CoreMatching: A Co-adaptive Sparse Inference Framework with Token and Neuron Pruning for Comprehensive Acceleration of Vision-Language Models
Source: S2:International Conference on Machine Learning | Tags: LLM, RAG
Code: github.com/wangqinsi1/2025-ICML-CoreMatching/tree/main
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A sparse inference framework that exploits the mutual reinforcement between important neurons (Core Neurons) and important tokens (Core Tokens) in Vision-Language Models, pruning both dimensions adaptively to achieve multiplicative efficiency gains. Existing VLM acceleration methods treat token sparsity and neuron sparsity as independent optimizations, missing synergistic opportunities; combining them naively yields suboptimal speedups because key neurons and tokens co-depend during inference. Useful for: Real-time multimodal inference (image captioning, VQA, visual reasoning) on edge devices
Vault connections: Native Sparse Attention (NSA): Summary, Block Sparse Attention, Routing-based Block-Sparse Attention

4. MemSkill: Learning and Evolving Memory Skills for Self-Evolving Agents
Source: HuggingFace | Tags: RecSys, LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Reframes static memory operations (extract, consolidate, prune) as learnable and evolvable memory skills, using a controller to select relevant skills and a designer to periodically refine the skill set based on hard cases. Fixed memory extraction procedures hard-code human priors about what to store and how to revise memory, making them rigid under diverse interaction patterns and inefficient on long agent interaction histories. Useful for: Long-horizon LLM agent tasks requiring adaptive memory management
Vault connections: MemoryBank, Hierarchical Agent Memory for Contextual Recommendation, Agentic RAG: Memory Management

5. Focus-dLLM: Accelerating Long-Context Diffusion LLM Inference via Confidence-Guided Context Focusing
Source: HuggingFace | Tags: Diffusion, LLM, RAG
Code: github.com/Longxmas/Focus-dLLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A training-free attention sparsification framework that accelerates long-context inference in diffusion language models by predicting which tokens will be unmasked using past confidence scores, then pruning redundant attention computation while preserving attention sinks. Diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) can process long contexts non-autoregressively but suffer from quadratic computational cost due to bidirectional full attention. Useful for: Long-document understanding and generation (32K+ token contexts)
Vault connections: Lengthy Context Retrieval via LLM Pretraining, Infinite Retrieval: Attention Enhanced LLMs in Long-Context Processing, LLM in dense retrieval with query likelihood Modeling


🎯 Active Projects

digest/2026-02-08.title: Research Digest — Sun 2026-02-08
digest/2026-02-08.content:
Research Digest — Sun 2026-02-08
Scanned 14 papers • Selected 3 • Filtered 0 off-domain, 7 duplicates • Runtime: 2.7 min

Papers





























#PaperTagsLinks1LLMs as the new high level languageLLMLink • Note2Haskell for all: Beyond agentic codingSearch, RAGLink • Note3Software factories and the agentic momentLLMLink • Note

Details
1. LLMs as the new high level language
Source: HackerNews | Tags: LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Multiple autonomous LLM agents working in parallel represent a new abstraction layer in programming, enabling developers to achieve 10x productivity gains similar to historical shifts from assembly → C → Java → Python. Current programming languages still require developers to write detailed implementation code, limiting the scale and scope of what one person can build. Useful for: Full-stack application development where multiple components need simultaneous work
Vault connections: Language Agents by Yu Su, LongAgent, Agentic Reasoning Design Patterns

2. Haskell for all: Beyond agentic coding
Source: HackerNews | Tags: Search, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Instead of delegating entire coding tasks to AI agents, provide developers with AI-powered contextual cues and suggestions that preserve developer agency and codebase familiarity while still leveraging AI capabilities. Current agentic coding tools reduce developer productivity and codebase understanding because they prioritize code velocity over correctness, comprehension, and quality—leading to worse outcomes in controlled studies and real-world scenarios. Useful for: Professional software development where code quality and maintainability matter
Vault connections: Best practices for agentic codebases, Agentic Tools vs API Calls for External Reasoning, The Codebase Singularity: When Agents Run Your Codebase Better Than You

3. Software factories and the agentic moment
Source: HackerNews | Tags: LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A development paradigm where specifications and test scenarios drive autonomous agents to write, test, and iterate on code until convergence, eliminating human code writing and review from the critical path. Traditional human-in-the-loop development creates bottlenecks in iteration speed and limits the scale of parallel work. Useful for: High-velocity product development teams where iteration speed > code review latency
Vault connections: Barry – Building Effective agents, Best practices for agentic codebases, Six Principles for Building Reliable Agentic AI Systems


🎯 Active Projects

digest/2026-02-09.title: Research Digest — Mon 2026-02-09
digest/2026-02-09.content:
Research Digest — Mon 2026-02-09
Scanned 314 papers • Selected 8 • Filtered 2 off-domain, 23 duplicates • Runtime: 8.3 min

Papers



























































#PaperTagsLinks1LogicSkills: A Structured Benchmark for Formal Reasoning in Large Language ModelsRAG, LLMarXiv • Note2Is my model “mind blurting”? Interpreting the dynamics of reasoning tokens with Recurrence Quantification Analysis (RQA)Embeddings, RecSys, RAGarXiv • Note3JADE: Expert-Grounded Dynamic Evaluation for Open-Ended Professional TasksLLMarXiv • Code • Note4R2LED: Equipping Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs for CTR PredictionEmbeddings, RAG, LLMarXiv • Code • Note5AgentStepper: Interactive Debugging of Software Development AgentsRAG, LLMarXiv • Note6RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMsEmbeddings, LLM, RAGarXiv • Note7Multimodal Generative Retrieval Model with Staged Pretraining for Food Delivery on MeituanEmbeddings, RAGarXiv • Note8MSign: An Optimizer Preventing Training Instability in Large Language Models via Stable Rank RestorationRecSys, LLMarXiv • Note

Details
1. LogicSkills: A Structured Benchmark for Formal Reasoning in Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RAG, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A structured benchmark that decomposes formal reasoning into three isolated, verifiable skills—symbolization, countermodel construction, and validity assessment—to diagnose which logical capabilities LLMs truly possess versus superficial pattern matching. Existing logical reasoning benchmarks provide only end-to-end accuracy scores, making it impossible to determine whether models succeed through genuine symbolic reasoning or by exploiting surface-level linguistic patterns. Useful for: Diagnosing specific weaknesses in LLM reasoning pipelines (e.g., symbolization failure vs. inference failure)
Vault connections: Formal Semantics of Programming Languages (book), Compositional Reasoning Retrieval Evaluation, Large Language Models Can Solve Real-World Planning Rigorously with Formal Verification Tools

2. Is my model “mind blurting”? Interpreting the dynamics of reasoning tokens with Recurrence Quantification Analysis (RQA)
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: Embeddings, RecSys, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Analyze the latent dynamics of reasoning token generation in LLMs by treating it as a dynamical system and applying RQA to hidden embedding trajectories to quantify patterns of repetition, stalling, and determinism. Response length is a crude proxy for reasoning effort that fails to capture the quality, dynamics, or effectiveness of chain-of-thought reasoning. Useful for: Diagnosing reasoning quality in test-time compute systems without parsing generated text
Vault connections: Self-Consistency Decoding, RANK1: Test-Time Compute for Reranking in Information Retrieval, Modeling Open-World Cognition as On-Demand Synthesis of Probabilistic Model

3. JADE: Expert-Grounded Dynamic Evaluation for Open-Ended Professional Tasks
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: LLM
Code: github.com/smiling-world/JADE
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A two-layer evaluation framework that combines predefined expert-grounded evaluation skills (Layer 1) with dynamic claim-level assessment (Layer 2) to rigorously yet flexibly evaluate diverse agent strategies on open-ended professional tasks. Static rubrics fail to accommodate valid diverse response strategies in open-ended tasks, while LLM-as-a-judge approaches suffer from instability and bias. Useful for: Evaluating business analysis agents (BizBench domain: strategy consulting, market research)
Vault connections: Agent-as-a-Judge Evaluation, Synergistic Tool Integration in Agentic Reasoning, Taxonomy-Guided Benchmarking for Agentic Evaluation

4. R2LED: Equipping Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs for CTR Prediction
Source: arXiv:cs.IR | Tags: Embeddings, RAG, LLM
Code: github.com/abananbao/R2LED
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Use multi-granularity semantic IDs to enhance both the retrieval and refinement stages of lifelong user modeling for CTR prediction, enabling mixed collaborative-semantic retrieval and bi-level fusion. - Noisy retrieval in lifelong user modeling caused by skewed data distribution in long behavior sequences

Lack of semantic understanding in refinement stage where collaborative filtering alone misses content meaning
Impractical inference costs of LLM-based semantic enhancement at scale
Insufficient granularity of simple semantic embeddings for capturing multi-level user interests Useful for: Large-scale recommendation systems with long user behavior histories (e-commerce, video platforms)

Vault connections: Semantic IDs for prompt compression in retrieval, Semantic IDs: design decisions and optimization alternatives, Others

5. AgentStepper: Interactive Debugging of Software Development Agents
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: RAG, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
An interactive debugger for LLM-based software development agents that represents agent execution as structured conversations among the LLM, agent program, and tools, enabling stepwise execution, breakpoints, and live editing of prompts and tool invocations. Understanding and debugging software development agents is challenging because developers must reason about complex trajectories of LLM queries, tool calls, and code modifications, but current techniques provide little visibility into this intermediate process. Useful for: Debugging software development agents (e.g., SWE-Agent, ExecutionAgent, RepairAgent)
Vault connections: Language Agents by Yu Su, Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?, Coding Agent for Tool-Augmented Reasoning

6. RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs
Source: HuggingFace | Tags: Embeddings, LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A quantization framework that achieves 2-bit LLM inference by sequentially deriving binary (±1) residual paths from a single shared full-precision weight, ensuring each path corrects the error of the preceding one rather than learning redundant features. Addresses inter-path adaptation in residual binarization—a pathological failure mode where parallel binary paths learn redundant features during quantization-aware training (QAT), degrading error-compensation structure and limiting model expressiveness. Useful for: Edge deployment of large language models where memory and compute are severely constrained
Vault connections: Model Compression: Pruning, Quantization, and Knowledge Distillation in LLM Embedding Models, Native Sparse Attention (NSA): Summary, MultiLoRA: Democratizing LoRA for Better Multi-Task Learning

7. Multimodal Generative Retrieval Model with Staged Pretraining for Food Delivery on Meituan
Source: arXiv:cs.IR | Tags: Embeddings, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Decouples multimodal training into sequential stages where each stage focuses on specialized tasks (e.g., single-modality encoding, then cross-modal alignment, then semantic ID generation), preventing modality dominance and the one-epoch problem in multimodal retrieval systems. Joint optimization of multimodal features causes certain modalities (typically text) to dominate training while others are neglected, and inconsistent convergence rates across modalities lead to the one-epoch problem where some modalities overfit before others have properly learned. Useful for: E-commerce and food delivery platforms with rich multimodal item catalogs (images, text descriptions, structured attributes)
Vault connections: Adaptive Multimodal Search Planning, Multimodal Data Augmentation for Retrieval, Alignment-Guided Representation Learning

8. MSign: An Optimizer Preventing Training Instability in Large Language Models via Stable Rank Restoration
Source: HuggingFace | Tags: RecSys, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A gradient optimizer that periodically applies matrix sign decomposition to weight matrices during training to restore their stable rank and prevent the gradient explosion instability that causes LLM training collapse. Training instability in large language models, where sudden gradient explosions occur due to the joint effect of declining weight matrix stable rank (ratio of squared Frobenius to spectral norm) and increasing alignment between adjacent layer Jacobians, leading to exponential gradient growth with depth. Useful for: Pretraining large language models (5M to 3B+ parameters) where gradient explosions waste compute
Vault connections: Everything You Didn’t Want To Know About LM architecture and training, Early-Exit Large Language Models, Michal Valko


🎯 Active Projects

2. R2LED: Equipping Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs for CTR Prediction
Summary:
Use multi-granularity semantic IDs to enhance both the retrieval and refinement stages of lifelong user modeling for CTR prediction, enabling mixed collaborative-semantic retrieval and bi-level fusion. - Noisy retrieval in lifelong user modeling caused by skewed data distribution in long behavior sequences

Lack of semantic understanding in refinement stage where collaborative filtering alone misses content meaning
Impractical inference costs of LLM-based semantic enhancement at scale
Insufficient granularity of simple semantic embeddings for capturing multi-level user interests Useful for: Large-scale recommendation systems with long user behavior histories (e-commerce, video platforms)

Deep dive: Full analysis

3. Stop the Flip-Flop: Context-Preserving Verification for Fast Revocable Diffusion Decoding
Summary:
Performs leave-one-out verification and parallel drafting in a single forward pass by constructing dual attention views via KV cache override—masking selected seeds for verification while injecting their cached states for all other positions to preserve context. Existing revocable diffusion decoding schemes cause flip-flop oscillations where tokens are repeatedly remasked and later restored unchanged, wasting both the revision budget and degrading conditioning context for parallel token generation. Useful for: Accelerating diffusion-based language model inference where quality-speed tradeoffs matter
Deep dive: Full analysis

4. Multimodal Generative Retrieval Model with Staged Pretraining for Food Delivery on Meituan
Summary:
Decouples multimodal training into sequential stages where each stage focuses on specialized tasks (e.g., single-modality encoding, then cross-modal alignment, then semantic ID generation), preventing modality dominance and the one-epoch problem in multimodal retrieval systems. Joint optimization of multimodal features causes certain modalities (typically text) to dominate training while others are neglected, and inconsistent convergence rates across modalities lead to the one-epoch problem where some modalities overfit before others have properly learned. Useful for: E-commerce and food delivery platforms with rich multimodal item catalogs (images, text descriptions, structured attributes)
Deep dive: Full analysis

5. RelayGen: Intra-Generation Model Switching for Efficient Reasoning
Summary:
Training-free segment-level model switching that dynamically delegates low-difficulty portions of long reasoning trajectories to smaller models while keeping high-difficulty reasoning on large models, guided by offline-analyzed token probability margins. Large reasoning models (LRMs) generate expensive long-form reasoning chains where generation difficulty varies significantly within a single output, but existing efficiency methods either ignore this intra-generation variation or require complex supervised token-level routing. Useful for: Long-form reasoning tasks (math, code generation, multi-step problem solving)
Deep dive: Full analysis

6. SPARC: Separating Perception And Reasoning Circuits for Test-time Scaling of VLMs
Summary:
Decouple visual perception from reasoning in VLMs by explicitly separating inference into two stages—visual search to localize relevant regions, then reasoning conditioned only on those regions—enabling independent test-time scaling and more robust error correction. Traditional VLMs entangle perception and reasoning in unstructured chains-of-thought, causing small perceptual errors to cascade into wrong answers, requiring expensive RL with hand-crafted rewards, and making test-time compute allocation brittle across distribution shifts. Useful for: Visual question answering with complex spatial reasoning (e.g., V* VQA benchmark)
Deep dive: Full analysis

7. PANC: Prior-Aware Normalized Cut for Object Segmentation
Summary:
A weakly supervised spectral segmentation method that augments token-token affinity graphs with a minimal set of annotated visual tokens as anchor nodes, biasing the spectral eigenspace toward partitions consistent with user annotations while preserving global grouping from self-supervised features. Fully unsupervised segmentation methods like TokenCut produce non-deterministic, initialization-sensitive partitions that lack user control and reproducibility. Useful for: Domains with costly dense labels (medical imaging, infrastructure defect detection)
Deep dive: Full analysis

8. RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs
Summary:
A quantization framework that achieves 2-bit LLM inference by sequentially deriving binary (±1) residual paths from a single shared full-precision weight, ensuring each path corrects the error of the preceding one rather than learning redundant features. Addresses inter-path adaptation in residual binarization—a pathological failure mode where parallel binary paths learn redundant features during quantization-aware training (QAT), degrading error-compensation structure and limiting model expressiveness. Useful for: Edge deployment of large language models where memory and compute are severely constrained
Deep dive: Full analysis

9. QuantLRM: Quantization of Large Reasoning Models via Fine-Tuning Signals
Summary:
Use the magnitude of weight updates during reasoning-incentivized fine-tuning as signals to identify which weights are important for quantization, protecting weights with both the smallest and largest update magnitudes. Traditional weight-only quantization methods for LLMs don’t account for the unique properties of Large Reasoning Models (LRMs), where reasoning capabilities developed during fine-tuning may be lost when using generic importance metrics like activation statistics or second-order Hessian information. Useful for: Compressing reasoning models fine-tuned with RL (e.g., models trained with process reward models)
Deep dive: Full analysis

10. Uncertainty Drives Social Bias Changes in Quantized Large Language Models
Summary:
Post-training quantization causes up to 21% of individual LLM responses to flip between biased and unbiased states without changing aggregate bias scores, driven by model uncertainty and creating asymmetric demographic impacts. Aggregate bias metrics (e.g., average bias scores) mask critical instance-level changes after quantization, failing to detect that compression fundamentally alters which specific responses exhibit bias and which demographic groups are affected. Useful for: Model compression auditing: Ensuring quantized models deployed in production don’t introduce hidden demographic harms
Deep dive: Full analysis

11. NanoQuant: Efficient Sub-1-Bit Quantization of Large Language Models
Summary:
A post-training quantization method that compresses LLM weights to binary and sub-1-bit levels by formulating quantization as a low-rank binary factorization problem, using ADMM initialization followed by block-wise reconstruction. Existing weight-only quantization methods cannot efficiently compress LLMs to binary (1-bit) or sub-1-bit levels without requiring massive amounts of calibration data, extensive compute resources, or incurring additional storage overhead for codebooks and auxiliary structures. Useful for: Deploying 70B+ parameter models on consumer GPUs (e.g., Llama2-70B on 8GB GPU)
Deep dive: Full analysis

12. ProtoQuant: Quantization of Prototypical Parts For General and Fine-Grained Image Classification
Summary:
Constrain learned prototypes to a discrete codebook in latent space via vector quantization, preventing prototype drift and enabling interpretable “this looks like that” classification without backbone finetuning. Traditional prototypical parts methods suffer from prototype drift (prototypes lack grounding in training data and change under small perturbations), struggle to scale to ImageNet-level datasets, and require expensive backbone finetuning to achieve competitive accuracy. Useful for: Large-scale interpretable image classification (ImageNet-scale)
Deep dive: Full analysis

Cross-Paper Insights
• Papers #4, #5 share technique: based on the paper text provided

digest/2026-02-10.title: Research Digest — Tue 2026-02-10
digest/2026-02-10.content:
Research Digest — Tue 2026-02-10
Scanned 415 papers • Selected 5 • Filtered 5 off-domain, 36 duplicates • Runtime: 8.3 min

Papers









































#PaperTagsLinks1LLaDA2.1: Speeding Up Text Diffusion via Token EditingRL, Diffusion, LLMarXiv • Note2OSCAR: Optimization-Steered Agentic Planning for Composed Image RetrievalSearch, Embeddings, RAGarXiv • Note3Do MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMsRL, LLM, RAGarXiv • Note4Improving Data and Reward Design for Scientific Reasoning in Large Language ModelsRL, RAG, LLMarXiv • Note5LIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge GraphsRecSys, Graph, RAG, LLMarXiv • Note

Details
1. LLaDA2.1: Speeding Up Text Diffusion via Token Editing
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: RL, Diffusion, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Combine Mask-to-Token (M2T) prediction with Token-to-Token (T2T) editing in a configurable threshold-based decoding scheme to enable speed-quality tradeoffs in diffusion language models. Traditional diffusion language models face a rigid tradeoff between decoding speed and generation quality. Useful for: Code generation tasks requiring high throughput (achieves 663-892 TPS on coding benchmarks)
Vault connections: Batch Verification in Speculative Decoding, LLM Inference Performance: The Team Mental Model + Experiment Playbook, Bitter Lesson & the tokenization Reckoning

2. OSCAR: Optimization-Steered Agentic Planning for Composed Image Retrieval
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: Search, Embeddings, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Reformulates agentic composed image retrieval from heuristic trial-and-error into a principled trajectory optimization problem, using offline mixed-integer programming to derive optimal retrieval trajectories that are stored as in-context demonstrations for online VLM planner steering. Addresses the limitations of both unified embedding retrieval (single-model myopia) and heuristic agentic retrieval (suboptimal trial-and-error orchestration) by replacing ad-hoc exploration with mathematically optimal planning trajectories. Useful for: Composed image retrieval tasks requiring multi-constraint reasoning (visual + textual modifications)
Vault connections: HM-RAG: Hierarchical Multi-Agent Multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation, Modular Plug-and-Play Multi-Source Retrieval Agents, Multi-Agent Agentic RAG

3. Do MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: RL, LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A reinforcement learning framework that trains multimodal LLMs with explicit region-level visual attention rewards to learn stable visual grounding policies during chain-of-thought reasoning. Current MLLMs exhibit weak visual attention during reasoning—early-stage visual misalignment propagates through reasoning chains without correction because training lacks proper credit assignment for where the model looks, leading to failed inferences even with correct reasoning structure. Useful for: Complex visual reasoning tasks requiring multi-hop inference over image regions
Vault connections: Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Multimodal Textual Knowledge Generation via Open-Vocabulary VLMs, Multimodal Knowledge Graph Construction with Contextualized Visual Entities

4. Improving Data and Reward Design for Scientific Reasoning in Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RL, RAG, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A three-stage scientific reasoning training pipeline that combines exploration-expanding supervised fine-tuning, dynamic difficulty curriculum, and rubric-guided reinforcement learning to enable stable RL on open-ended scientific questions. Standard SFT → RL workflows fail on scientific reasoning due to: (1) narrow reasoning pattern coverage before RL, (2) static training data mismatched to evolving model capability, and (3) inability to provide stable reward signals for open-ended scientific answers without verifiable ground truth. Useful for: Training models for scientific question answering (chemistry, physics, biology, mathematics)
Vault connections: Reasoning in Language Models - Hanna Hajishirzi, Mathematical Problem-Solving Synthetic Query Generation, Beyond Chunks and Graphs: Retrieval-Augmented Generation through Triplet-Driven Thinking

5. LIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs
Source: arXiv:cs.IR | Tags: RecSys, Graph, RAG, LLM, Embeddings
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A domain-specific knowledge graph recommendation system that compares shallow (DeepWalk, Biased Random Walk) and deep (R-GCN) graph embeddings to balance structural connectivity and semantic relevance for pedagogically aligned literature recommendations. Addresses curriculum stagnation in English education by scaffolding teachers to discover diverse, pedagogically appropriate literature beyond commonly taught texts, while navigating the trade-off between graph structure fidelity and domain-specific semantic quality in recommendations. Useful for: Educational content recommendation: Curriculum design, course material selection, adaptive learning paths
Vault connections: Instance-Aware Knowledge Distillation from LLMs to Sequential Models (DLLM2Rec), Knowledge Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation for LLM-based Recommendation, A novel recommender system using light graph convolutional network and personalized knowledge-aware attention sub-network


🎯 Active Projects

2. FlashVID: Efficient Video Large Language Models via Training-free Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging
Summary:
Training-free inference acceleration for Video Large Language Models through spatiotemporal token compression that preserves video relationships by first selecting representative tokens, then merging spatiotemporally correlated features across frames. Existing VLLMs require processing massive volumes of visual tokens, causing computational inefficiency. Useful for: Long-form video understanding where computational budget is limited
Deep dive: Full analysis

3. Rethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs
Summary:
A generative recommendation framework that learns collaborative-predictive item representations and quantizes them into semantic IDs optimized for sequential predictability, without relying on pre-trained language models. Existing semantic ID methods use foundation model embeddings weakly coupled with collaborative signals and generic quantization schemes (like RQ-VAE) that don’t minimize sequential uncertainty for autoregressive generation, leading to suboptimal recommendation quality and inefficient tokenization. Useful for: Large-scale sequential recommendation systems (e-commerce, video, music streaming)
Deep dive: Full analysis

4. ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention
Summary:
A sparse multimodal architecture where visual tokens bypass all self-attention and feed-forward layers in the language model, interacting with text only through cross-attention at a small subset of strategically selected layers. Existing multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) process visual tokens through every Transformer layer alongside text tokens, causing massive computational overhead despite visual embeddings already being well-aligned with the language space after projection. Useful for: Vision-language tasks requiring efficient inference: VQA, image captioning, visual reasoning
Deep dive: Full analysis

5. Predicting Future Utility: Global Combinatorial Optimization for Task-Agnostic KV Cache Eviction
Summary:
Optimize KV cache eviction by allocating per-head budgets based on marginal utility for preserving long-term semantic information, rather than treating all attention heads uniformly with instantaneous heuristic metrics. Existing KV cache eviction methods assume score magnitudes are consistent proxies for importance across all attention heads, ignoring that different heads have heterogeneous predictive fidelity—some prioritize instantaneous token contribution while others capture long-horizon utility. Useful for: Long-context LLM inference (document QA, summarization, multi-turn dialogue)
Deep dive: Full analysis

6. ParisKV: Fast and Drift-Robust KV-Cache Retrieval for Long-Context LLMs
Summary:
A drift-robust, GPU-native KV-cache retrieval framework that uses collision-based candidate selection followed by quantized inner-product reranking to enable efficient top-k attention on long contexts, with CPU-offloading support via Unified Virtual Addressing for million-token scale. Existing KV-cache retrieval methods for long-context LLMs suffer from distribution drift between prefill and decode phases, high retrieval latency at scale, and inability to handle million-token contexts due to memory constraints. Useful for: Long-document understanding and retrieval (RAG over 100k+ token contexts)
Deep dive: Full analysis

7. Attn-GS: Attention-Guided Context Compression for Efficient Personalized LLMs
Summary:
Use attention patterns from a fine-tuned marking model to identify and preserve important personalization signals, then guide a compression model to generate condensed user contexts that retain task-relevant information. Existing context compression methods use heuristic selection (e.g., recency) or blind summarization that treats user profiles as monolithic text, failing to leverage how LLMs internally prioritize information during personalization tasks, resulting in poor quality-compression tradeoffs. Useful for: Personalized chatbots with extensive user interaction histories
Deep dive: Full analysis

8. Semantic Search At LinkedIn
Summary:
A co-designed inference system that treats ranking as a prefill-dominated workload, using context compression and hybrid text-embedding interactions to enable 75x throughput improvement for production LLM-based semantic search. Traditional LLM ranking suffers from poor throughput (10-100x slower than classical models) due to sequential token generation and large context windows when scoring multiple candidates, making it infeasible for high-QPS production retrieval systems. Useful for: Production semantic search with strict latency SLAs (job search, people search, product retrieval)
Deep dive: Full analysis

9. ManifoldKV: Training-Free KV Cache Compression via Euclidean Outlier Detection
Summary:
Score KV cache tokens for eviction using Euclidean distance to the key centroid rather than cosine similarity, preserving both angular direction and magnitude information to identify semantically salient outliers. Cosine-similarity-based eviction methods are scale-invariant and discard magnitude cues, causing directional collisions in multi-key retrieval and centroid dilution at extreme context lengths (64K+), leading to catastrophic performance collapse. Useful for: Long-context LLM inference (4K-128K tokens) with memory constraints
Deep dive: Full analysis

10. DynamiQ: Accelerating Gradient Synchronization using Compressed Multi-hop All-reduce
Summary:
A gradient quantization framework optimized for multi-hop all-reduce topologies that dynamically re-quantizes partial sums at intermediate aggregation hops using adaptive scaling and range tracking, paired with a fused decompress-accumulate-recompress kernel. Existing gradient quantization methods (e.g., THC, Omni-Reduce, MXFP) are designed for single-hop aggregation and suffer significant accuracy degradation in multi-hop topologies because they don’t account for partial sums accumulating quantization errors across multiple hops. Useful for: Large-scale distributed training (1000+ GPUs) with multi-tier network topologies (rack → pod → cluster aggregation)
Deep dive: Full analysis

11. Generative Reasoning Re-ranker
Summary:
A three-stage LLM training framework (semantic ID mid-training → reasoning trace distillation → RL with verifiable rewards) that teaches models to rerank recommendations through explicit chain-of-thought reasoning rather than implicit scoring. - Existing LLM-based recommenders neglect the critical reranking phase (focusing only on retrieval/ranking)

Standard LLM approaches underutilize reasoning capabilities enhanced through RL and high-quality reasoning data
Non-semantic item IDs create severe scalability issues in industrial systems with billions of items
Models exploit reward hacking by preserving input order rather than truly optimizing reranking quality Useful for: Industrial recommendation reranking with massive item catalogs (billions of items)

Deep dive: Full analysis

12. OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization
Summary:
A layer-wise post-training quantization method that jointly optimizes over activations and weights by formulating each layer’s quantization as a box-constrained integer least squares problem, then solving it approximately using extended Babai and Klein algorithms with K-best sampling. Existing weight-only post-training quantization methods rely on heuristic objectives and greedy rounding, causing significant accuracy degradation at low bit-widths (3-4 bits). Useful for: Post-training quantization of large language models for deployment on resource-constrained devices
Deep dive: Full analysis

digest/2026-02-11.title: Research Digest — Wed 2026-02-11
digest/2026-02-11.content:
Research Digest — Wed 2026-02-11
Scanned 344 papers • Selected 7 • Filtered 3 off-domain, 25 duplicates • Runtime: 7.6 min

Papers





















































#PaperTagsLinks1One Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral ProfilesEmbeddings, RecSys, LLM, GraphLink • Note2PABU: Progress-Aware Belief Update for Efficient LLM AgentsLLM, RAGarXiv • Note3Decomposing Reasoning Efficiency in Large Language ModelsRecSys, LLMarXiv • Note4Long Chain-of-Thought Compression via Fine-Grained Group Policy OptimizationLLM, RLarXiv • Note5Answer First, Reason Later: Aligning Search Relevance via Mode-Balanced Reinforcement LearningRAG, LLM, RL, SearcharXiv • Note6When Less is More: The LLM Scaling Paradox in Context CompressionEmbeddings, LLMarXiv • Note7Generalizing GNNs with Tokenized Mixture of ExpertsEmbeddings, Graph, RAGarXiv • Note

Details
1. One Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral Profiles
Source: S2:Proceedings of the Recommender Systems 2025 | Tags: Embeddings, RecSys, LLM, Graph
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Extend the LightGCN graph neural network architecture with relation-aware message passing to learn unified user embeddings that generalize across multiple prediction tasks (recommendation, churn, search) by modeling multi-faceted user journeys as heterogeneous graphs. Traditional recommender systems model different prediction tasks (e.g., product recommendation vs. churn prediction) separately, resulting in inconsistent and task-specific user representations. Useful for: Multi-task learning scenarios where a single user representation must support multiple downstream objectives
Vault connections: Multi-Behavior Data Augmentation Framework, Session Partitioning for User Embedding with LLM, Actions Speak Louder than Words: Trillion-Parameter Sequential Transducers for Generative Recommendations

2. PABU: Progress-Aware Belief Update for Efficient LLM Agents
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A belief-state framework for LLM agents that compresses action-observation histories by explicitly tracking task progress and selectively retaining only relevant past interactions, rather than conditioning on full histories. Full action-observation histories introduce task-irrelevant information that leads to redundant actions, higher inference costs, and cognitive overload—particularly in multi-step agent environments where context windows become bloated with unnecessary details. Useful for: Long-horizon agent tasks (web navigation, tool use, game playing)
Vault connections: Mind-Map Agent, Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Hierarchical Reasoning Model

3. Decomposing Reasoning Efficiency in Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RecSys, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A systematic framework that decomposes LLM reasoning efficiency into interpretable factors (completion rate, conditional correctness, verbosity, and optional trace quality) to reveal where inference tokens are spent or wasted, enabling targeted optimization interventions. Standard reasoning benchmarks report only final accuracy, obscuring critical efficiency bottlenecks—models may fail by running out of tokens, making reasoning errors, being unnecessarily verbose, or generating degenerate outputs. Useful for: Reasoning model development: Diagnosing whether o1/R1-style models waste tokens on verbosity vs. poor reasoning vs. budget mismanagement
Vault connections: Few-Shot Reasoning with S1 and Budget Forcing, context rot, 100M Token Context Windows: Expanding AI’s Memory and Capabilities

4. Long Chain-of-Thought Compression via Fine-Grained Group Policy Optimization
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: LLM, RL
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
An RL algorithm that compresses verbose Chain-of-Thought reasoning by subdividing group responses into fine-grained segments and assigning length/entropy-based weights to prioritize concise, informative reasoning paths. - LLMs generate unnecessarily verbose CoT reasoning that increases computational costs and latency without performance gains

Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) suffers from inefficient data utilization and entropy collapse during training Useful for: Mathematical reasoning tasks (MATH500, AIME24, AMC23)

Vault connections: Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO): Main Takeaways for Practitioners, Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Few-Shot Reasoning with S1 and Budget Forcing

5. Answer First, Reason Later: Aligning Search Relevance via Mode-Balanced Reinforcement Learning
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: RAG, LLM, RL, Search
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A search relevance paradigm that outputs the definitive relevance score in the first token followed by reasoning traces, trained with a hybrid RL+SFT approach that balances mode-seeking (reward maximization) and mode-covering (expert knowledge retention) to prevent mode collapse. Traditional search relevance models face a latency-performance tradeoff: small models are fast but lack reasoning capability, while LLM-based models provide interpretable reasoning but are too slow for millisecond-level requirements. Useful for: Search relevance scoring systems requiring both sub-millisecond latency and interpretability
Vault connections: RAG-R1 – Incentivize the Search and Reasoning Capabilities of LLM through multi-query parallelism, Unveiling the Potential of Multimodal Retrieval Augmented Generation with Planning, RaDeR: Reasoning-aware Dense Retrieval Model

6. When Less is More: The LLM Scaling Paradox in Context Compression
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: Embeddings, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
In compressor-decoder setups for context compression, increasing the compressor’s model size counter-intuitively reduces reconstruction faithfulness despite lower training loss, due to knowledge overwriting and semantic drift. Challenges the assumption that larger models always produce better outputs; reveals that scaling up compressor models in context compression systems leads to unfaithful reconstructions where the decoder replaces source facts with prior knowledge or paraphrases content incorrectly. Useful for: Context compression systems for long-document processing
Vault connections: context rot, Context Compression, Model Compression: Pruning, Quantization, and Knowledge Distillation in LLM Embedding Models

7. Generalizing GNNs with Tokenized Mixture of Experts
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: Embeddings, Graph, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A pretrain-then-finetune framework that uses mixture-of-experts (MoE) routing for diverse computation paths, vector quantization to stabilize encoder-to-head signals, and Lipschitz regularization to bound output amplification, achieving simultaneous robustness to distribution shifts and input perturbations without sacrificing clean-data performance. Static GNN inference faces a fundamental tradeoff where improving stability to perturbations requires reducing reliance on shift-sensitive features, creating an irreducible generalization floor under distribution shifts; instance-conditional routing can break this ceiling but is fragile because shifts mislead routing and perturbations cause routing fluctuation. Useful for: Node classification under degree or homophily distribution shifts (e.g., deploying models trained on one social network to another with different connectivity patterns)
Vault connections: Learnable GNN Pruning, Alternating Transformer-GNN Layering, Adaptive Uniformity Constraint in GNN Pruning


🎯 Active Projects

2. When Less is More: The LLM Scaling Paradox in Context Compression
Summary:
In compressor-decoder setups for context compression, increasing the compressor’s model size counter-intuitively reduces reconstruction faithfulness despite lower training loss, due to knowledge overwriting and semantic drift. Challenges the assumption that larger models always produce better outputs; reveals that scaling up compressor models in context compression systems leads to unfaithful reconstructions where the decoder replaces source facts with prior knowledge or paraphrases content incorrectly. Useful for: Context compression systems for long-document processing
Deep dive: Full analysis

3. WildCat: Near-Linear Attention in Theory and Practice
Summary:
Approximates full attention by attending only over a small weighted coreset selected via randomly pivoted Cholesky decomposition, achieving super-polynomial error decay with near-linear runtime complexity. Existing attention approximations either lack provable error guarantees or require quadratic runtime to achieve high fidelity; WildCat provides both theoretical guarantees (O(n^{-\sqrt{\log(\log(n))}}) error) and practical efficiency (O(n^{1+o(1)}) time). Useful for: Long-context language modeling with KV cache compression for reduced memory footprint during inference
Deep dive: Full analysis

4. Benchmarking the Energy Savings with Speculative Decoding Strategies
Summary:
Systematic empirical framework for measuring and comparing the energy consumption of speculative decoding strategies across model families, draft lengths, and datasets, revealing that energy savings don’t always align with latency improvements. Prior work on speculative decoding focused exclusively on latency/throughput metrics, ignoring energy consumption—a critical factor for deployment costs, carbon footprint, and edge/mobile inference where power budgets are constrained. Useful for: Production LLM deployments where electricity costs dominate TCO (hyperscaler inference)
Deep dive: Full analysis

5. Generative Reasoning Re-ranker
Summary:
A three-stage LLM training pipeline for recommendation reranking that combines semantic ID encoding, reasoning trace generation via supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards to optimize final ranking quality. Existing LLM-based recommendation systems focus on retrieval/ranking while neglecting reranking, fail to leverage reasoning abilities enhanced through RL, suffer from scalability issues with non-semantic IDs in billion-scale industrial systems, and are vulnerable to reward hacking where models preserve input order instead of truly reranking. Useful for: Industrial-scale recommendation systems requiring final-stage reranking with billions of items
Deep dive: Full analysis

6. A Behavioral Fingerprint for Large Language Models: Provenance Tracking via Refusal Vectors
Summary:
Extract behavioral fingerprints from LLMs by computing directional vectors in activation space that represent the difference between processing harmful versus harmless prompts, leveraging safety alignment artifacts as a unique model signature. Protects intellectual property of proprietary LLMs against unauthorized derivative models (finetuned, merged, or quantized versions) by providing a robust, alignment-based identification method that doesn’t require watermarking or explicit model access. Useful for: Detecting unauthorized model derivatives in commercial LLM ecosystems
Deep dive: Full analysis

7. AfriNLLB: Efficient Translation Models for African Languages
Summary:
Compress large multilingual translation models by iteratively removing transformer layers while fine-tuning with knowledge distillation from a larger teacher model to maintain translation quality. Large multilingual translation models (like NLLB-200) are too computationally expensive and memory-intensive to deploy in resource-constrained settings, particularly for serving African languages where infrastructure may be limited. Useful for: Deploying multilingual NMT systems in low-resource settings with limited compute/memory
Deep dive: Full analysis

digest/2026-02-12.title: Research Digest — Thu 2026-02-12
digest/2026-02-12.content:
Research Digest — Thu 2026-02-12
Scanned 333 papers • Selected 8 • Filtered 2 off-domain, 16 duplicates • Runtime: 8.4 min

Papers



























































#PaperTagsLinks1LHAW: Controllable Underspecification for Long-Horizon TasksRAG, LLMarXiv • Note2Search or Accelerate: Confidence-Switched Position Beam Search for Diffusion Language ModelsSearch, RAG, Diffusion, LLMarXiv • Note3When to Memorize and When to Stop: Gated Recurrent Memory for Long-Context ReasoningRAG, RL, LLMarXiv • Note4Exploring the impact of adaptive rewiring in Graph Neural NetworksSearch, RAG, GrapharXiv • Note5Kalman Linear Attention: Parallel Bayesian Filtering For Efficient Language Modelling and State TrackingTransformer, LLMarXiv • Note6TVCACHE: A Stateful Tool-Value Cache for Post-Training LLM AgentsRAG, RL, LLMarXiv • Note7Asymmetric Prompt Weighting for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable RewardsRL, LLMarXiv • Note8GraphSeek: Next-Generation Graph Analytics with LLMsSearch, RAG, LLMarXiv • Note

Details
1. LHAW: Controllable Underspecification for Long-Horizon Tasks
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RAG, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A synthetic pipeline that systematically transforms well-specified tasks into controllable underspecified variants by removing information across four dimensions (Goals, Constraints, Inputs, Context) at configurable severity levels, then validates ambiguity impact through empirical agent trials. Existing frameworks lack scalable, task-agnostic methods to curate and measure how ambiguity affects long-horizon agent performance—current approaches rely on unreliable LLM predictions of ambiguity rather than empirical validation of whether underspecification actually causes divergent outcomes. Useful for: Evaluating clarification-seeking behavior in autonomous agents operating over extended workflows
Vault connections: Agentic Workflow Pattern: Prompt Chaining, Agentic Reasoning Pipeline, Agentic Workflow Pattern: Orchestrator-Workers

2. Search or Accelerate: Confidence-Switched Position Beam Search for Diffusion Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: Search, RAG, Diffusion, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A training-free decoding algorithm for Diffusion Language Models that dynamically switches between beam search over unmasking orders when confidence is low and parallel greedy decoding when confidence is high, balancing generation quality and inference speed. Standard greedy position selection in DLMs commits to unmasking the most confident tokens at each step, which can lock the model into suboptimal unmasking trajectories—especially on reasoning-heavy tasks where early greedy choices propagate errors through subsequent denoising iterations. Useful for: Mathematical reasoning tasks (GSM8K) where multi-step logic requires careful unmasking order
Vault connections: Early-Exit Large Language Models, LLM in dense retrieval with query likelihood Modeling, Accelerating Inference of Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Sparse Context Selection

3. When to Memorize and When to Stop: Gated Recurrent Memory for Long-Context Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RAG, RL, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A gated recurrent memory system for long-context LLM reasoning that uses text-controlled update and exit gates to selectively update memory on informative chunks and terminate processing early when sufficient evidence is collected. Naive recurrent memory approaches like MemAgent suffer from memory explosion (updating on irrelevant chunks) and computational waste (continuing to process after gathering sufficient evidence), leading to degraded performance and inefficiency on long-context reasoning tasks. Useful for: Long-document question answering where evidence is sparse
Vault connections: Data-Dependent Decay Linear Attention, Data-Independent Decay Linear Attention, context rot

4. Exploring the impact of adaptive rewiring in Graph Neural Networks
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: Search, RAG, Graph
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Apply probabilistic edge pruning based on Erdős-Rényi random graph theory during GNN training, allowing the model to dynamically adjust its connectivity structure to balance memory efficiency with learning capacity for critical pattern recognition tasks. GNNs on large-scale graphs (e.g., electrical grids) face prohibitive memory and computational costs; naive sparsification can destroy critical structural patterns needed for tasks like N-1 contingency assessment, while dense graphs are infeasible at scale. Useful for: Critical infrastructure reliability analysis (power grid N-1 contingency assessment)
Vault connections: Alternating Transformer-GNN Layering, Sampling-Based Global Transformer (SGT), Adaptive Uniformity Constraint in GNN Pruning

5. Kalman Linear Attention: Parallel Bayesian Filtering For Efficient Language Modelling and State Tracking
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: Transformer, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A neural sequence-modelling layer that reformulates the Kalman filter in information form to enable parallel training via associative scan, providing principled probabilistic state tracking with explicit uncertainty while maintaining linear complexity. State-space models like Mamba and gated linear attention (GLA) achieve efficient linear complexity but lack the expressivity and robust uncertainty-aware state tracking needed for complex reasoning tasks, while traditional Kalman filters are inherently sequential and cannot be parallelized for training. Useful for: Language modelling tasks requiring state tracking (variable binding, counting, copying)
Vault connections: Efficient Attention Mechanisms for Large Language Model: A Survey, Hybrid Efficient Attention Models, Chunkwise Recurrent Linear Attention

6. TVCACHE: A Stateful Tool-Value Cache for Post-Training LLM Agents
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: RAG, RL, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A stateful caching system for LLM agent post-training that uses longest-prefix matching on tool-call sequences to safely reuse tool outputs only when the environment state is guaranteed to be identical. During RL post-training of LLM agents, external tool calls (taking seconds to minutes) leave GPUs idle, but naively caching tool outputs is incorrect because tool results depend on accumulated environment state from prior agent interactions. Useful for: RL post-training of LLM agents with expensive external tools (databases, APIs, simulators)
Vault connections: LLM-dCache — Reimplementation Guide, LLM Inference Performance: The Team Mental Model + Experiment Playbook, Dynamic Tool Integration via Reinforcement Learning

7. Asymmetric Prompt Weighting for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: RL, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Weight policy gradient updates asymmetrically by assigning higher importance to prompts with low or zero empirical success probability, accelerating learning in low-success regimes where informative responses are rare. Existing RL algorithms (GRPO, DAPO, RLOO) focus on ambiguous prompts with intermediate success probability, downweighting very hard prompts. Useful for: From-scratch RL training (R1-Zero style) where model traverses wide accuracy ranges from near-zero to high performance
Vault connections: GEPA Explained!, GRPO outline, Reinforcement learning in LLM – recent turbulence

8. GraphSeek: Next-Generation Graph Analytics with LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.IR | Tags: Search, RAG, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Separates LLM planning from query execution using a Semantic Catalog abstraction—LLMs reason over schema and operation descriptions to generate execution plans, while a separate deterministic layer executes queries over the full property graph. LLMs struggle with large-scale, heterogeneous property graphs due to token inefficiency, inability to process full datasets, and unreliable direct query generation from natural language; existing approaches fail on complex multi-query analytics workflows. Useful for: Enterprise knowledge graphs with heterogeneous schemas and millions of nodes
Vault connections: GraphToken, Graph-Enhanced RAG, Graph RAG


🎯 Active Projects

2. End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation
Summary:
End-to-end joint optimization of item embeddings and discrete Semantic IDs directly from raw advertising data, eliminating the two-stage compression paradigm of traditional RQ-VAE approaches to prevent semantic degradation and error accumulation. Addresses two fundamental limitations of existing Residual Quantization (RQ)-based Semantic ID generation: (1) objective misalignment and semantic loss from cascading item embedding learning and quantization as separate stages, and (2) error propagation across RQ layers where early-layer quantization mistakes compound in later layers. Useful for: Large-scale advertisement recommendation systems requiring semantic item representation
Deep dive: Full analysis

3. Learning to Evict from Key-Value Cache
Summary:
Train lightweight per-head RL agents to learn eviction policies that rank KV cache tokens by predicted future utility, using only key and value vectors from pre-computed generation traces. Existing KV cache eviction methods rely on heuristic proxies (recency, past attention scores) that don’t directly predict which tokens will be useful for future decoding, leading to suboptimal memory-accuracy tradeoffs. Useful for: Long-context LLM inference where KV cache memory is the primary bottleneck
Deep dive: Full analysis

4. MoToRec: Sparse-Regularized Multimodal Tokenization for Cold-Start Recommendation
Summary:
Transform multimodal recommendation into discrete semantic tokenization using a sparsely-regularized Residual Quantized Variational Autoencoder (RQ-VAE) that generates compositional codes of interpretable tokens for disentangled item representations. Addresses the item cold-start problem in recommendation systems where new items with limited or no interaction history suffer from suboptimal representations due to noise and entanglement in sparse multimodal data, limiting GNN-based recommender performance. Useful for: Cold-start recommendation scenarios with new items lacking interaction history
Deep dive: Full analysis

5. Flow caching for autoregressive video generation
Summary:
A caching framework for autoregressive video generation that maintains independent caching policies per video chunk, allowing fine-grained control over which chunks require recomputation at each denoising timestep, rather than assuming uniform denoising across all frames. Existing video diffusion caching methods assume uniform denoising across frames, but autoregressive video models generate content in sequential chunks with varying similarity patterns at identical timesteps, making traditional caching strategies ineffective and leaving autoregressive generation prohibitively slow. Useful for: Autoregressive video generation models (e.g., MAGI-1, SkyReels-V2) requiring real-time synthesis
Deep dive: Full analysis

6. AugVLA-3D: Depth-Driven Feature Augmentation for Vision-Language-Action Models
Summary:
Augment 2D vision-language-action models with depth-estimated 3D features using a geometry-aware depth estimator (VGGT) and an action assistant module to improve spatial understanding and robotic control in 3D environments. Existing VLA models rely primarily on 2D visual representations from vision-language models, limiting their spatial understanding and action grounding in complex 3D environments where geometric reasoning is critical for robotic manipulation tasks. Useful for: Robotic manipulation in geometrically ambiguous scenarios (occlusions, texture-less surfaces, similar-looking objects at different depths)
Deep dive: Full analysis

7. C^2ROPE: Causal Continuous Rotary Positional Encoding for 3D Large Multimodal-Models Reasoning
Summary:
A spatially-aware extension of RoPE that preserves 2D visual continuity and models causal relationships via Chebyshev distance, preventing locality loss and long-term attention decay in vision-language models. Standard RoPE applies 1D temporal indices to 2D image patches, breaking spatial continuity across columns and causing progressive neglect of earlier visual tokens due to temporal-only causal assumptions; this harms 3D scene reasoning where spatial relationships are critical. Useful for: 3D visual question answering and scene reasoning tasks requiring spatial understanding
Deep dive: Full analysis

8. When Fusion Helps and When It Breaks: View-Aligned Robustness in Same-Source Financial Imaging
Summary:
Constructs multiple aligned views (price charts and technical indicators) from the same rolling window of financial time series, then systematically evaluates early vs. late fusion architectures under a leakage-resistant train-test regime and ex-post minimum-movement label filtering to isolate predictive signal from near-zero-return noise. Financial direction prediction suffers from (1) label ambiguity when next-day moves are near zero, (2) train-test leakage in time series, and (3) unclear guidance on when multi-view fusion helps versus harms performance. Useful for: Financial time-series direction/return prediction with multiple representations (technical, fundamental, sentiment)
Deep dive: Full analysis

9. Compute Only Once: UG-Separation for Efficient Large Recommendation Models
Summary:
A masking framework that disentangles user-side and item-side computation in dense feature interaction models, enabling user representations to be computed once and reused across multiple candidate items during inference. Dense feature interaction architectures (e.g., RankMixer) entangle user and item features across all layers, preventing the computation reuse benefits available in sequence models with KV caching; this leads to redundant user-side computation for every user-item pair during inference. Useful for: Large-scale recommendation systems with dense feature interactions (feed ranking, ads ranking)
Deep dive: Full analysis

10. Enhancing Predictability of Multi-Tenant DNN Inference for Autonomous Vehicles’ Perception
Summary:
Dynamically select critical frames and regions of interest (ROIs) in multi-tenant DNN inference for autonomous vehicles to reduce computation while maintaining accuracy and achieving predictable real-time performance. Closes the gap between high computation requirements of multi-tenant DNN inference in AVs and limited onboard resources, while addressing unpredictable inference times that hinder real-time perception and sensor fusion. Useful for: Real-time autonomous vehicle perception pipelines requiring multi-model inference (object detection, segmentation, depth estimation)
Deep dive: Full analysis

digest/2026-02-13.title: Research Digest — Fri 2026-02-13
digest/2026-02-13.content:
Research Digest — Fri 2026-02-13
Scanned 362 papers • Selected 10 • Filtered 0 off-domain, 20 duplicates • Runtime: 9.6 min

Papers







































































#PaperTagsLinks1TSR: Trajectory-Search Rollouts for Multi-Turn RL of LLM AgentsSearch, RL, LLMarXiv • Note2DMAP: A Distribution Map for TextRAG, Search, LLM, RecSysarXiv • Note3Learning to Configure Agentic AI SystemsSearch, RL, LLM, RAGarXiv • Note4Think like a Scientist: Physics-guided LLM Agent for Equation DiscoveryLLM, RAGarXiv • Note5Scene-Aware Memory Discrimination: Deciding Which Personal Knowledge StaysRAG, Search, LLM, RecSysarXiv • Note6Extending Puzzle for Mixture-of-Experts Reasoning Models with Application to GPT-OSS AccelerationSearch, RL, LLM, RAGarXiv • Note7Temperature as a Meta-Policy: Adaptive Temperature in LLM Reinforcement LearningRL, LLM, RAGarXiv • Note8PrefillShare: A Shared Prefill Module for KV Reuse in Multi-LLM Disaggregated ServingLLM, RAGarXiv • Note9Towards On-Policy SFT: Distribution Discriminant Theory and its Applications in LLM TrainingRL, LLM, RAGarXiv • Code • Note10Improving Neural Retrieval with Attribution-Guided Query RewritingRAG, Search, LLM, RecSysarXiv • Note

Details
1. TSR: Trajectory-Search Rollouts for Multi-Turn RL of LLM Agents
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: Search, RL, LLM
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Move test-time search techniques (beam search, lookahead) into the training-time rollout generation phase to construct higher-quality trajectories for multi-turn RL of LLM agents. Multi-turn RL suffers from sparse/delayed rewards and stochastic environments, where naive trajectory sampling leads to poor exploitation, mode collapse, and unstable learning. Useful for: Multi-turn agent tasks with sparse rewards (planning, navigation, puzzle-solving)
Vault connections: Reinforcement learning in LLM – recent turbulence, Sergey Levine — Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning for LLM Agents, Proximal policy optimization (PPO)

2. DMAP: A Distribution Map for Text
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RAG, Search, LLM, RecSys
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Maps text through a language model into a set of samples in the unit interval [0,1] that jointly encode both rank and probability information from next-token distributions, enabling model-agnostic statistical analysis. Traditional metrics like perplexity fail to account for context—interpreting a next-token probability requires understanding the shape of the conditional distribution (i.e., how many reasonable choices exist). Useful for: Generation parameter validation: Detecting contaminated or improperly generated training data
Vault connections: LLM Query → BM25 Hard Negative Pipeline, Consolidating Ranking and Relevance Predictions of Large Language Models, LLM in dense retrieval with query likelihood Modeling

3. Learning to Configure Agentic AI Systems
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: Search, RL, LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Learn a hierarchical reinforcement learning policy that dynamically configures LLM agent systems on a per-query basis—selecting workflows, tools, token budgets, and prompts—rather than applying fixed “one-size-fits-all” configurations. Static agent configurations waste compute on simple queries and underperform on complex ones; hand-tuned heuristics are brittle across diverse inputs. Useful for: Multi-turn reasoning systems with variable query complexity (e.g., SWE-bench, HotpotQA, tool-augmented QA)
Vault connections: Synergistic Tool Integration in Agentic Reasoning, Automated Multi-Agent Architecture Search (ADAS, AFlow, EvoFlow), Agentic Reasoning Pipeline

4. Think like a Scientist: Physics-guided LLM Agent for Equation Discovery
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
An agentic framework that mimics scientific reasoning by first extracting physical properties (symmetries, units, conservation laws) from data using physics-based tools, then using these properties as structured priors to guide symbolic regression engines toward interpretable equations. Existing LLM-based equation discovery systems try to guess formulas directly from data without modeling the multi-step reasoning process scientists use, leading to poor symbolic accuracy and brittleness to noise. Useful for: Scientific discovery from experimental physics data (mechanics, electromagnetism, fluid dynamics)
Vault connections: Language Agents by Yu Su, Coding Agent for Tool-Augmented Reasoning, Pocket Flow

5. Scene-Aware Memory Discrimination: Deciding Which Personal Knowledge Stays
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: RAG, Search, LLM, RecSys
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A two-stage method that filters non-memorable user interactions via a lightweight gating unit and establishes adaptive memory standards through clustered prompting, enabling efficient personal knowledge organization at scale. Addresses the dual challenge of filtering irrelevant information from large-scale user interactions and managing rising computational costs in LLM-based memory systems, while adapting to diverse memory standards across different application contexts. Useful for: Personal assistant systems that accumulate user interaction history over time
Vault connections: Hierarchical Agent Memory for Contextual Recommendation, MemoryBank, Augmented Multimodal Memory

6. Extending Puzzle for Mixture-of-Experts Reasoning Models with Application to GPT-OSS Acceleration
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: Search, RL, LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Apply post-training neural architecture search to reasoning-focused LLMs by combining heterogeneous MoE expert pruning, selective window attention replacement, FP8 KV-cache quantization, and reinforcement learning recovery to reduce inference cost while maintaining accuracy across varying reasoning efforts. Reasoning models generate long traces that increase serving cost dramatically; traditional per-token throughput metrics are misleading because reasoning token counts vary with model architecture changes, making it unclear whether optimizations actually improve end-to-end efficiency. Useful for: Deploying reasoning-focused LLMs (o1-style models) in production where long traces dominate cost
Vault connections: Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Hierarchical Reasoning Model, RaDeR: Reasoning-aware Dense Retrieval Model

7. Temperature as a Meta-Policy: Adaptive Temperature in LLM Reinforcement Learning
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: RL, LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Treat temperature selection as a learnable meta-policy that adaptively chooses exploration/exploitation trade-offs during LLM reinforcement learning by rewarding temperatures that maximize the likelihood of high-advantage trajectories. Static or heuristic temperature schedules fail to adapt to the dynamic exploration needs throughout RL training—high temperatures generate noisy exploration when exploitation is needed, while low temperatures cause premature convergence when exploration is required, limiting policy improvement. Useful for: Mathematical reasoning tasks requiring adaptive exploration (GSM8K, MATH, ARC)
Vault connections: Learnable Temperature Parameter in InfoNCE Contrastive Learning, Pignal, Population-Based Evolution of LLMs (GENOME)

8. PrefillShare: A Shared Prefill Module for KV Reuse in Multi-LLM Disaggregated Serving
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Share a frozen prefill module and its generated KV cache across multiple specialized models in disaggregated serving, eliminating redundant computation when multiple LLMs process the same prompt prefix. Multi-agent systems that invoke multiple specialized models over a shared context suffer from redundant prefill computation and KV cache storage for identical prompts, leading to high aggregate prefill load, worse tail latency due to prefill-decode interference, and wasted GPU memory—even in disaggregated architectures. Useful for: Multi-agent orchestration systems (e.g., ReAct, AutoGPT) where specialized models collaborate on shared context
Vault connections: LLM Inference Performance: The Team Mental Model + Experiment Playbook, Chunked prefill in LLMs, SGLang Architecture - PM Perspective

9. Towards On-Policy SFT: Distribution Discriminant Theory and its Applications in LLM Training
Source: arXiv:cs.AI | Tags: RL, LLM, RAG
Code: github.com/zhangmiaosen2000/Towards-On-Policy-SFT
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Enable supervised fine-tuning to achieve RL-level generalization by quantifying and correcting the mismatch between training data distribution and the model’s own output distribution, without requiring expensive online sampling. SFT is efficient but generalizes poorly because it trains on off-policy data (static datasets that don’t reflect what the model actually generates). Useful for: Domains where RL is infeasible (limited compute, reward modeling is expensive, unstable training)
Vault connections: GRPO outline, Policy Gradients, Reinforcement learning in LLM – recent turbulence

10. Improving Neural Retrieval with Attribution-Guided Query Rewriting
Source: arXiv:cs.IR | Tags: RAG, Search, LLM, RecSys
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Use gradient-based token attributions from a neural retriever to identify weak or misleading query terms, then feed those attribution scores as soft guidance into an LLM prompt that rewrites the query to improve retrieval effectiveness while preserving user intent. Neural retrievers are brittle to underspecified or ambiguous queries, and existing solutions operate in isolation—LLMs rewrite queries without retriever feedback, while explainability methods (gradient attributions, attention) are used only for post-hoc analysis rather than actively improving retrieval. Useful for: Open-domain search with implicit or ambiguous information needs (BEIR benchmarks)
Vault connections: Leveraging No-Retrieval as a Routing Option, Multi-step Agentic Retrieval and Rewriting for Massive GraphRAG, RANK1: Test-Time Compute for Reranking in Information Retrieval


🎯 Active Projects

2. SToRM: Supervised Token Reduction for Multi-modal LLMs toward efficient end-to-end autonomous driving
Summary:
A supervised framework that learns to identify and remove redundant visual tokens from multi-modal LLMs while maintaining end-task performance through pseudo-supervision from an all-token reference pass. Multi-modal LLMs for autonomous driving require processing thousands of visual tokens from camera inputs, creating computational bottlenecks unsuitable for resource-constrained vehicle platforms. Useful for: End-to-end autonomous driving systems requiring multi-modal understanding (vision + language instructions)
Deep dive: Full analysis

3. PrefillShare: A Shared Prefill Module for KV Reuse in Multi-LLM Disaggregated Serving
Summary:
Share a frozen prefill module and its generated KV cache across multiple specialized models in disaggregated serving, eliminating redundant computation when multiple LLMs process the same prompt prefix. Multi-agent systems that invoke multiple specialized models over a shared context suffer from redundant prefill computation and KV cache storage for identical prompts, leading to high aggregate prefill load, worse tail latency due to prefill-decode interference, and wasted GPU memory—even in disaggregated architectures. Useful for: Multi-agent orchestration systems (e.g., ReAct, AutoGPT) where specialized models collaborate on shared context
Deep dive: Full analysis

4. End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation
Summary:
End-to-end learnable Semantic ID generation that jointly optimizes item embeddings and discrete SIDs directly from raw advertising data, eliminating the two-stage compression bottleneck of traditional Residual Quantization-based approaches. Addresses objective misalignment, semantic degradation, and error accumulation inherent in cascading two-stage SID generation (embedding learning → quantization) used by existing Generative Recommendation systems. Useful for: Large-scale advertisement recommendation systems requiring semantic item tokenization
Deep dive: Full analysis

5. Hi-SAM: A Hierarchical Structure-Aware Multi-modal Framework for Large-Scale Recommendation
Summary:
A multi-modal recommendation framework that disentangles cross-modal semantics from modality-specific details during tokenization, then preserves user interaction hierarchy through memory-compressed attention over semantic ID sequences. Existing semantic ID approaches suffer from (1) tokenization redundancy/collapse due to lack of disentanglement between shared and modality-specific information, and (2) flattened attention that ignores the hierarchical structure of user sequences (user → items → tokens), causing bias toward local token details over holistic item semantics. Useful for: Large-scale multi-modal recommendation systems with text, image, and video content
Deep dive: Full analysis

6. LASER: An Efficient Target-Aware Segmented Attention Framework for End-to-End Long Sequence Modeling
Summary:
A full-stack framework combining hybrid DRAM-SSD storage infrastructure (SeqVault) with a sparse attention mechanism (Segmented Target Attention) to enable efficient modeling of ultra-long user behavior sequences in real-time recommendation systems. Deploying long-sequence models in production faces a dual “Latency Wall”: (1) high I/O latency retrieving massive user histories from storage, and (2) quadratic computational complexity of attention mechanisms over sequences with millions of interactions. Useful for: Large-scale recommendation systems requiring lifelong user modeling (e-commerce, social media, streaming)
Deep dive: Full analysis

7. Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation
Summary:
Compresses long user interaction sequences into compact learnable memory tokens via self-referential teacher-forcing, enabling parallel training of recurrent models while maintaining O(1) inference memory. Full-attention generative recommendation models face quadratic computational cost and cannot scale to lifelong user histories; traditional recurrent models require serial training and accumulate noise from stochastic interactions. Useful for: Sequential recommendation with extremely long user histories (thousands+ interactions)
Deep dive: Full analysis

8. Thinking with Drafting: Optical Decompression via Logical Reconstruction
Summary:
Reconceptualizes visual reasoning as optical decompression by forcing models to draft their mental model into executable Domain-Specific Language (DSL) code, which then generates deterministic visual proofs for self-verification rather than directly hallucinating answers. Addresses the precision paradox in multimodal LLMs where optical perception transcribes symbols without capturing logical topology, and pixel-based generation produces visual artifacts lacking mathematical exactness—resulting in reasoning failures on complex visual tasks requiring precise logical structure. Useful for: Visual algebra and symbolic mathematics problems requiring exact symbolic manipulation
Deep dive: Full analysis

9. Deep Kernel Fusion for Transformers
Summary:
