#PaperProject ScoreLinks11-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization0arXiv • Note2COMPOT: Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers Compression0arXiv • Note3Enabling Low-Latency Machine learning on Radiation-Hard FPGAs with hls4ml0arXiv • Note4Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs0arXiv • Note5The Equalizer: Introducing Shape-Gain Decomposition in Neural Audio Codecs0arXiv • Note6The Vision Wormhole: Latent-Space Communication in Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Systems0arXiv • Note7jina-embeddings-v5-text: Task-Targeted Embedding Distillation0arXiv • Note
1. 1-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization
Summary:
Replace uniform integer quantization grids with k-means-learned codebooks during quantization-aware training, then show that 1-bit k-means quantization maximizes downstream generative task performance under a fixed inference memory budget. Standard QAT uses integer (uniform) quantization formats whose fixed grid spacing is suboptimal for the non-uniform distributions of neural network weights; additionally, the QAT design space (format × bit-width) is underexplored, and evaluations rely on perplexity rather than generative task performance, obscuring the true quality/memory trade-off. Useful for: Deploying large language models on memory-constrained hardware (edge devices, consumer GPUs) where integer quantization leaves accuracy on the table
Deep dive: Full analysis

2. COMPOT: Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers Compression
Summary:
Replace truncated SVD with sparse dictionary learning using orthogonal dictionaries that admit closed-form Procrustes solutions and single-step sparse coding, enabling training-free Transformer weight compression without iterative optimization. Standard low-rank SVD compression forces all weight structure into a single shared subspace, degrading accuracy at moderate compression ratios. Useful for: Compressing large language models (LLMs) for inference on memory-constrained hardware without fine-tuning
Deep dive: Full analysis

3. Enabling Low-Latency Machine learning on Radiation-Hard FPGAs with hls4ml
Summary:
A new synthesis backend for the hls4ml library that automatically translates trained neural network models into High-Level Synthesis (HLS) projects targeting Microchip PolarFire radiation-hard FPGAs, enabling on-detector machine learning inference in high-radiation environments. The hls4ml library — the standard tool in high-energy physics (HEP) for deploying ML on FPGAs — had no support for radiation-hard FPGAs (e.g., PolarFire), blocking adoption of on-detector ML in experiments where commercial Xilinx/Intel FPGAs would fail due to radiation-induced bit flips and latch-up events. Useful for: High-energy physics trigger systems requiring sub-100 ns inference latency at LHC detector frontends (e.g., LHCb, ATLAS, CMS upgrade detectors)
Deep dive: Full analysis

4. Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs
Summary:
A speculative decoding framework for Video LLMs that offloads visual computation entirely to the target model by reusing cached text hidden states in a sliding window attention mechanism, while training the draft model on semantically-enriched intermediate layer states instead of raw visual tokens. Standard speculative decoding collapses on Video LLMs because the draft model suffers from (1) attention dilution — visual KV-cache grows proportionally with video length, drowning out relevant signal — and (2) negative visual gain — processing raw visual tokens in the draft model adds noise rather than useful signal, since by deep inference layers the visual semantics have already been absorbed into text hidden states. Useful for: Real-time long-video understanding (25k+ visual tokens) where standard speculative decoding degrades
Deep dive: Full analysis

5. The Equalizer: Introducing Shape-Gain Decomposition in Neural Audio Codecs
Summary:
Decompose audio signals into a normalized shape vector and a scalar gain before encoding, process them through separate quantization paths, and reconstruct the output signal post-decoding — preventing gain variation from polluting the NAC latent space. Neural Audio Codecs (NACs) encode gain (short-term energy) and shape (normalized structure) jointly in the same latent space, causing global amplitude variations to strongly perturb encoder embeddings and their vector quantization, wasting codebook capacity on level-dependent redundancy and degrading bitrate-distortion performance. Useful for: Neural speech/audio compression at low-to-mid bitrates where codebook efficiency is critical
Deep dive: Full analysis

6. The Vision Wormhole: Latent-Space Communication in Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Systems
Summary:
Repurpose the visual input pathway of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as a universal inter-agent communication channel by encoding heterogeneous reasoning traces into synthetic visual tokens, enabling text-free, model-agnostic latent state transfer between agents of different architectures. Standard multi-agent LLM systems communicate via serialized text, incurring tokenization overhead, context-length bottlenecks, and lossy information quantization. Useful for: Heterogeneous multi-agent pipelines where different tasks are routed to specialized model families (e.g., Qwen-VL for vision-heavy subtasks, Gemma for text reasoning)
Deep dive: Full analysis

7. jina-embeddings-v5-text: Task-Targeted Embedding Distillation
Summary:
Combine knowledge distillation from a large teacher embedding model with task-specific contrastive loss during student training, jointly optimizing for both teacher alignment and discriminative task performance. Small embedding models trained purely with contrastive loss lack the representational richness of larger models, while pure distillation ignores task-specific discriminative structure — TTED addresses the quality/size tradeoff by leveraging both signals simultaneously. Useful for: Information retrieval / dense passage retrieval where compact models are needed at query-time
Deep dive: Full analysis

digest/2026-02-22.title: Research Digest — Sun 2026-02-22
digest/2026-02-22.content:
Research Digest — Sun 2026-02-22
Scanned 428 papers • Selected 7 • Filtered 3 off-domain, 24 duplicates • Runtime: 35.8 min

Papers





















































#PaperTagsLinks1Fail-Closed Alignment for Large Language ModelsLLM, RecSysarXiv • Note2Hybrid-Gym: Training Coding Agents to Generalize Across TasksLLM, SearcharXiv • Code • Note3Quecto-V1: Empirical Analysis of 8-bit Quantized Small Language Models for On-Device Legal RetrievalLLM, RAGarXiv • Note4A Picture of Agentic SearchSearch, RAGarXiv • Note5ArXiv-to-Model: A Practical Study of Scientific LM TrainingLLM, Transformer, RAG, SearcharXiv • Note6How I use Claude Code: Separation of planning and executionSearchLink • Note7Pailitao-VL: Unified Embedding and Reranker for Real-Time Multi-Modal Industrial SearchLLM, RecSys, Embeddings, SearcharXiv • Note

Details
1. Fail-Closed Alignment for Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.LG | Tags: LLM, RecSys
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Iteratively identify and ablate learned refusal directions during safety fine-tuning, forcing the model to reconstruct alignment along orthogonal, causally independent subspaces, so that no single prompt-based suppression can collapse refusal behavior. Current refusal mechanisms are fail-open: a single dominant latent feature encodes most refusal behavior, so suppressing it via prompt-based jailbreaks (e.g., role-play, prefix injection, adversarial suffixes) causes full alignment collapse. Useful for: Hardening production LLMs against prompt-based jailbreaks (role-play, prefix attacks, adversarial suffixes, many-shot prompting)
Vault connections: Long-Context Repeated Sequence Replication, Early-Exit Large Language Models, TALLRec — aligning a large language model with recommendation

2. Hybrid-Gym: Training Coding Agents to Generalize Across Tasks
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: LLM, Search
Code: github.com/yiqingxyq/Hybrid-Gym
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A synthetic training environment composed of fine-grained, scalable auxiliary tasks derived from decomposed agent trajectories, designed to teach transferable coding-agent skills that generalize to unseen real-world software engineering benchmarks. Predominant benchmarks (SWE-Bench) train and evaluate agents on single-issue GitHub tasks, leaving agents brittle to the broader skill set required in practice—codebase exploration, dependency analysis, test design, and architectural reasoning. Useful for: Training coding agents that must operate across heterogeneous software repositories without task-specific fine-tuning
Vault connections: Self-Challenging Agent (SCA), Agent Symbolic Learning Framework, Best practices for agentic codebases

3. Quecto-V1: Empirical Analysis of 8-bit Quantized Small Language Models for On-Device Legal Retrieval
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: LLM, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Train a compact language model exclusively on a narrow legal corpus, then apply post-training 8-bit quantization to produce a sub-150MB, fully offline, privacy-preserving retrieval system that outperforms generalist models on domain-specific exact-match tasks. State-of-the-art legal NLP systems require 7B+ parameter cloud-hosted models, creating a resource divide that excludes practitioners with constrained hardware, introduces data sovereignty risks, and prevents fully offline deployment. Useful for: On-device legal retrieval in low-bandwidth or air-gapped environments (field lawyers, rural courts)
Vault connections: Lengthy Context Retrieval via LLM Pretraining, LLM-based Fine-grained Retrieval (in Ensemble Framework), Infinite Retrieval: Attention Enhanced LLMs in Long-Context Processing

4. A Picture of Agentic Search
Source: arXiv:cs.IR | Tags: Search, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A systematic data collection methodology that intercepts and logs all intermediate artifacts—queries, retrieved documents, and chain-of-thought reasoning traces—produced by agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems during query answering, yielding a benchmark dataset for studying non-human search behavior. IR evaluation infrastructure (metrics, datasets, user models, caching strategies, query pre-processors) is built around human querying behavior; as automated agents increasingly issue search queries, these assumptions break down, yet no datasets exist to characterize the distinct behavioral signature of agentic search. Useful for: Benchmarking IR systems under realistic agentic workloads (burst query patterns, reformulated sub-queries)
Vault connections: User-Centric Reinforcement Feedback Loop in IR, Hybrid Evaluation: Precision, Recall, User Satisfaction Triad, Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) System

5. ArXiv-to-Model: A Practical Study of Scientific LM Training
Source: arXiv:cs.CL | Tags: LLM, Transformer, RAG, Search
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
A complete, engineering-grounded pipeline for training domain-specialized scientific language models directly from raw arXiv LaTeX sources under constrained compute, covering every stage from metadata filtering through dense transformer pretraining. The practical process of building domain-specialized scientific LMs is under-documented — researchers lack transparent, reproducible accounts of how preprocessing decisions, tokenization choices, and I/O constraints affect usable token yield and training stability at moderate compute budgets. Useful for: Training specialized LMs for scientific domains (math, physics, CS) without frontier compute
Vault connections: CS 336 Language Models From Scratch - Lecture 1, Reasoning Beyond Limits: Advances and Open Problems for LLMs, Reasoning in Language Models - Hanna Hajishirzi

6. How I use Claude Code: Separation of planning and execution
Source: HackerNews | Tags: Search
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Enforce strict phase separation between research, planning, and code generation — Claude never writes code until a human-reviewed written plan exists. LLM coding assistants produce architectural drift, wasted tokens, and hard-to-reverse decisions when prompted to generate code directly, especially on non-trivial tasks where early structural choices cascade through the implementation. Useful for: Any non-trivial feature implementation spanning multiple files or subsystems
Vault connections: Completing unfinished projects with Claude code, Maximizing Claude Code with reusable workflows, Boris Cherny’s Claude Code Setup (2026)

7. Pailitao-VL: Unified Embedding and Reranker for Real-Time Multi-Modal Industrial Search
Source: arXiv:cs.IR | Tags: LLM, RecSys, Embeddings, Search, RAG
Deep dive: Full analysis
Summary:
Replace contrastive embedding with prototype-anchored absolute ID-recognition and replace pointwise reranking with a chunk-based listwise compare-and-calibrate policy, jointly eliminating granularity collapse in retrieval and latency-accuracy tradeoffs in reranking for large-scale multimodal industrial search. Three compounding failures in production multimodal retrieval: (1) contrastive learning produces stochastic, coarse-grained embeddings that fail to discriminate visually similar products; (2) image embeddings are brittle to environmental noise (background clutter, lighting variation); (3) listwise rerankers achieve high accuracy but are too slow for real-time serving, while pointwise rerankers are fast but lack comparative resolution. Useful for: Large-scale e-commerce product search where fine-grained visual discrimination matters (color, texture, brand logo)
Vault connections: MixLM: High-Throughput and Effective LLM Ranking via Text-Embedding Mix-Interaction, Contrastive Learning for Dense Retrieval with Specialized LLMs, Main Takeaways and Techniques: On the Theoretical Limitations of Embedding-Based Retrieval


🎯 Active Projects

2. Why Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models
Summary:
A training-free inference framework that suppresses textual inertia in Chain-of-Thought (CoT)-augmented recommendation foundation models by compressing reasoning chains and applying bias-subtracted contrastive decoding to re-anchor outputs toward Semantic ID tokens. When CoT reasoning is integrated into Semantic ID-based recommendation foundation models (e.g., OpenOneRec), verbose reasoning text dominates the model’s output distribution at inference time—a phenomenon called textual inertia—causing the model to drift away from grounded Semantic IDs and paradoxically degrading recommendation accuracy. Useful for: Semantic ID-based recommendation foundation models where CoT is appended to improve explainability but hurts ranking metrics
Deep dive: Full analysis

3. CLAA: Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation for Accelerating LLM Prefill
Summary:
Aggregate token importance scores across multiple transformer layers rather than relying on any single layer’s attention pattern, closing the gap to an oracle upper bound for selective token processing during LLM prefill. Existing token-ranking heuristics for accelerating LLM prefill suffer from high per-layer variance — rankings can degrade sharply at specific layers, causing important tokens to be dropped, a failure mode invisible to end-to-end benchmarks. Useful for: Long-context LLM inference where prefill is the dominant latency bottleneck (RAG pipelines, document Q&A, long-form summarization)
Deep dive: Full analysis

4. Dynamic Delayed Tree Expansion For Improved Multi-Path Speculative Decoding
Summary:
Rather than branching a draft tree immediately at the root via i.i.d. rollouts, DDTE delays the branching point by first drafting a single linear path, then applies a learned neural selector to decide context-dependently where along that path to begin i.i.d. expansion — enabling optimal-transport (OT)-based verification methods to reclaim their theoretical advantage. OT-based multi-path speculative decoding methods (e.g., SpecInfer) achieve high multi-token acceptance near the root but waste their budget where it matters least; the largest acceptance gains occur deeper in the draft tree where draft and target distributions diverge, yet root-branching i.i.d. rollouts never reach those depths efficiently. Useful for: Any autoregressive LLM inference pipeline using multi-path speculative decoding with a cheap draft model
Deep dive: Full analysis

5. The Case for Multi-Vector Contrastive Pre-Training, Eliminating Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search in Large-Scale Recommendation, and More!
Summary:
Insert a supervised contrastive pre-training stage in the late-interaction (ColBERT) multi-vector setting before knowledge distillation fine-tuning, achieving parity with full unsupervised contrastive pre-training at ~10x lower compute cost. Full unsupervised contrastive pre-training for ColBERT-style multi-vector retrievers is computationally prohibitive; the common shortcut of skipping pre-training entirely (KD-only on a frozen dense backbone) yields suboptimal multi-vector representations because the backbone was never trained to produce useful per-token late-interaction embeddings. Useful for: Large-scale document retrieval requiring multi-vector late-interaction (ColBERT, PLAID)
Deep dive: Full analysis

6. Greedy Multi-Path Block Verification for Faster Decoding in Speculative Sampling
Summary:
A tractable multi-path speculative decoding verification algorithm that greedily maximizes block efficiency by jointly verifying multiple draft token sequences using an information-agnostic linear program framework. Standard block verification (BV) is optimal for single-path speculative decoding but cannot exploit multiple draft candidates; naive multi-path extensions require solving an intractable optimization over all possible verification algorithms, leaving significant throughput gains on the table. Useful for: LLM inference serving where latency reduction is critical (e.g., Llama-3 70B serving)
Deep dive: Full analysis

7. 2Mamba2Furious: Linear in Complexity, Competitive in Accuracy
Summary:
A simplified and enhanced linear attention architecture derived from Mamba-2 that improves the A-mask and increases hidden state order to close the accuracy gap with softmax attention while retaining linear complexity. Linear attention variants like Mamba-2 are memory-efficient but trail softmax attention in accuracy due to limited expressivity in their state-transition masks and low-order hidden states; 2Mamba targets this accuracy gap without sacrificing linear-time scaling. Useful for: Long-context language modeling where quadratic softmax attention is memory-prohibitive
Deep dive: Full analysis

8. Conditional Flow Matching for Continuous Anomaly Detection in Autonomous Driving on a Manifold-Aware Spectral Space
Summary:
Model the continuous probability density of expert driving behavior by training a flow-matching generative model constrained to a low-rank PCA spectral manifold, then use exact log-likelihood scores as anomaly signals for safety-critical event detection. Traditional rule-based heuristics cannot scale to rare long-tail driving scenarios; standard generative models operating in raw high-dimensional coordinate space suffer from kinematic instability, intractable Jacobian computation, and multi-modal ambiguity at complex junctions. Useful for: Safety validation and statistical gating for Level 4 autonomous vehicle fleets
Deep dive: Full analysis

9. Variable-Length Semantic IDs for Recommender Systems
Summary:
A discrete variational autoencoder (dVAE) with Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization that learns item representations as variable-length token sequences, assigning shorter codes to frequent items and longer codes to rare ones, guided by information-theoretic principles. Fixed-length semantic IDs allocate equal description length to all items regardless of frequency, wasting capacity on popular items and under-representing rare long-tail items — misaligned with both natural language structure and the skewed frequency distributions of real-world catalogs. Useful for: Generative recommender systems using autoregressive LLMs to predict item IDs as token sequences
Deep dive: Full analysis

10. Quecto-V1: Empirical Analysis of 8-bit Quantized Small Language Models for On-Device Legal Retrieval
Summary:
Train a compact language model exclusively on a narrow legal corpus, then apply post-training 8-bit quantization to produce a sub-150MB, fully offline, privacy-preserving retrieval system that outperforms generalist models on domain-specific exact-match tasks. State-of-the-art legal NLP systems require 7B+ parameter cloud-hosted models, creating a resource divide that excludes practitioners with constrained hardware, introduces data sovereignty risks, and prevents fully offline deployment. Useful for: On-device legal retrieval in low-bandwidth or air-gapped environments (field lawyers, rural courts)
Deep dive: Full analysis

11. Mine and Refine: Optimizing Graded Relevance in E-commerce Search Retrieval
Summary:
A two-stage embedding training framework that first builds a global semantic space with label-aware contrastive learning, then iteratively mines hard boundary samples via Approximate Nearest Neighbor search and sharpens inter-relevance-level similarity margins using a multi-class extension of circle loss. Standard bi-encoder training collapses graded relevance signals (exact match, substitute, complement) into an undifferentiated similarity score, making downstream hybrid score blending and relevance thresholding unstable and requiring embeddings that generalize to long-tail, noisy e-commerce queries at scale. Useful for: Large-scale e-commerce semantic retrieval where relevance is graded (exact, substitute, complement)
Deep dive: Full analysis

12. Rethinking ANN-based Retrieval: Multifaceted Learnable Index for Large-scale Recommendation System
Summary:
Co-train multifaceted item embeddings with a hierarchical residual quantization codebook to produce discrete indices that directly identify relevant items at serving time, eliminating ANN search entirely. ANN-based retrieval suffers from a training-indexing disconnect (embeddings and indices optimized separately), poor cold-start performance for new items, and unavoidable per-request ANN computation cost at billion-user scale. Useful for: Large-scale recommendation retrieval stages (e.g., short-video, e-commerce) operating at billions of users and millions of QPS
Deep dive: Full analysis

literature/2026-01-23-Beyond-Marginals--.filePath: literature/2026-01-23 Beyond Marginals - Evaluating Demographic-Aligned LLMs.md
literature/2026-01-23-Beyond-Marginals--.title: Beyond Marginal Distributions: Evaluating Demographic-Aligned LLMs
literature/2026-01-23-Beyond-Marginals--.content:
Beyond Marginal Distributions: Evaluating Demographic-Aligned LLMs
Source: arXiv:2601.15755 (cs.CL)
Date: 2026-01-22
Score: 2.46 (max_sim=0.754, fresh evaluation angle)
The Problem
LLMs are increasingly used to represent human opinions, values, and beliefs. Current evaluation of “aligned” models focuses on marginal response distributions — does the model’s answer distribution match the target population’s for each question independently?
But this misses deeper latent structures that characterize real populations:

Correlations between values
Cultural value theories
Multivariate patterns

Key Insight
Matching marginals ≠ matching the underlying structure. A model could perfectly match individual response distributions while completely failing to capture how those responses relate to each other.
The Evaluation Framework
Beyond Marginals
Propose evaluating aligned models through:

Marginal response distributions (existing)
Multivariate correlation patterns (new)

Comparison: Two Steering Techniques




















TechniqueMarginalsCorrelationsPersona PromptingModeratePoorDemographic Fine-tuningBetterStill fails
Using World Values Survey as ground truth.
Key Finding

Demographically fine-tuned models better approximate marginal distributions, but both techniques fail to capture gold standard correlation patterns.

Implications
For Value Alignment Research

Representativeness is distinct from value alignment
Evaluation focused on marginals gives overly optimistic conclusions
Need multivariate metrics to detect structural failures

For LLM-as-Population-Proxy Use Cases
If you’re using LLMs to:

Simulate user responses
Predict population-level preferences
Generate synthetic survey data

…you may be getting individual answers right but missing systematic relationships between them.
Connections to Vault

























Related NoteConnectionPersona VectorRelated work on controlling character traitsRevisiting Prompt Engineering for LLM RecEvaluation of LLM steering for personalizationLLM Alignment as Retriever OptimizationAlternative framing of alignmentIterative Multi-Turn LLM User ProfilingUser modeling context
Why This Matters for Recommendations
User Preference Modeling
If we use LLMs to model user preferences:

Getting individual preferences right isn’t enough
Need to capture preference correlations (people who like X tend to like Y)
Marginal accuracy could mask missing collaborative signal

Synthetic User Generation
For simulation/testing:

Individual response accuracy ≠ realistic user behavior
Correlation structure matters for evaluating diversity, coverage

Fairness/Representation

A model that matches marginal demographics but misses correlation structure could:

Flatten cultural differences
Miss intersectional effects
Produce unrealistic demographic representations



Key Technique to Extract
Multivariate correlation pattern matching as an evaluation metric for aligned/steered LLMs. Compare full correlation matrix, not just marginal distributions.
Questions

What specific correlation metrics work best? (Pearson? Rank? Structural?)
Could this apply to evaluating user simulators in RecSys?
Is the correlation failure due to training data or architecture limitations?

literature/2026-01-23-Dualformer---Time-.filePath: literature/2026-01-23 Dualformer - Time-Frequency Dual Domain Learning for LTSF.md
literature/2026-01-23-Dualformer---Time-.title: Dualformer: Time-Frequency Dual Domain Learning for Long-term Time Series Forecasting
literature/2026-01-23-Dualformer---Time-.content:
Dualformer: Time-Frequency Dual Domain Learning for Long-term Time Series Forecasting
Source: arXiv:2601.15669 (cs.LG)
Date: 2026-01-22
Score: 2.64 (max_sim=0.736, novel territory)
The Problem
Transformer-based LTSF models suffer from an inherent low-pass filtering effect — high-frequency information gets progressively attenuated across layers because frequency components propagate indiscriminately. This loses fine-grained temporal variations crucial for accurate forecasting.
Key Insight
Don’t treat all frequencies the same across layers. Instead:

Preserve high-frequency details in lower/earlier layers
Model low-frequency trends in deeper layers

This is a layer-wise perspective on frequency modeling that existing work ignores.
The Dualformer Architecture
Three components:
1. Dual-Branch Architecture
Concurrently models patterns in both time and frequency domains:

Time branch: captures local temporal dependencies
Frequency branch: captures global periodic patterns

2. Hierarchical Frequency Sampling
Different layers handle different frequency bands:

Lower layers → high-frequency bands (fine details)
Deeper layers → low-frequency bands (trends)

This prevents the low-pass filtering effect by giving high frequencies their own dedicated processing path.
3. Periodicity-Aware Weighting
Dynamically balances contributions from dual branches based on harmonic energy ratio of inputs:

High periodicity → weight frequency branch more
Low periodicity → weight time branch more

Theoretically grounded with a derived lower bound.
Results
Tested on 8 benchmarks. Particularly strong on:

Heterogeneous data (mixed patterns)
Weakly periodic data (where frequency-only approaches struggle)

Connections to Vault





















Related NoteConnectionTransformer Models for Time SeriesPrior work on factorized attention for time seriesSampling-Based Global Transformer (SGT)Different approach to handling long sequencesEnergy-based TransformerAlternative transformer scaling philosophy
Key Technique to Extract
Hierarchical frequency band allocation across transformer layers — assign different frequency ranges to different depth levels rather than processing all frequencies uniformly.
Questions

How does this compare to explicit Fourier features in the input?
Could this apply to transformer-based ranking where temporal signals matter?
What’s the computational overhead of the dual-branch design?

literature/2026-01-23-GOSV---Safety-Vect.filePath: literature/2026-01-23 GOSV - Safety Vectors in LLMs via Global Optimization.md
literature/2026-01-23-GOSV---Safety-Vect.title: GOSV: Global Optimization for Safety Vector Extraction in LLMs
literature/2026-01-23-GOSV---Safety-Vect.content:
GOSV: Global Optimization for Safety Vector Extraction in LLMs
Source: arXiv:2601.15801 (cs.LG)
Date: 2026-01-22
Score: 2.56 (max_sim=0.744, novel interpretability angle)
The Problem
LLM safety guardrails are fragile against jailbreaks. Existing interpretability methods use local, greedy attribution — they assume components (attention heads) contribute independently. But safety is cooperative: multiple heads work together.
Key Insight
Safety behavior isn’t controlled by individual heads — it emerges from global coordination across many heads. To find the true safety-critical components, you need to optimize over all heads simultaneously.
The GOSV Framework
Global Optimization Approach
Instead of testing heads one-by-one (greedy), GOSV finds the optimal set of heads to modify using global optimization.
Two Complementary Strategies
1. Harmful Patching

Inject activations from harmful prompt processing
Identifies “Malicious Injection Vectors” — heads that, when activated, enable harmful outputs

2. Zero Ablation

Zero out head activations
Identifies “Safety Suppression Vectors” — heads that, when disabled, break safety

Key Finding: Two Distinct Pathways
The two strategies find spatially distinct head sets with low overlap. This means:

Aligned LLMs maintain separate functional pathways for safety — one for detecting harmful content, another for suppressing harmful outputs.

Quantitative Results

~30% of total heads need repatching for complete safety breakdown
Consistent across multiple model architectures
Enables a novel white-box jailbreak that outperforms existing attacks

Connections to Vault





















Related NoteConnectionMaking LLM ReliablePractical framework context for safetyExplainability and User ControlInterpretability for trustworthy systemsRule BeatingAdversarial behavior in recommendation context
Why This Matters
For LLM Safety Research

Provides mechanistic understanding of how safety works
Identifies specific components to monitor/protect
Dual-pathway finding suggests redundancy in safety design

For Adversarial ML

New attack surface: activation patching is more effective than prompt-based jailbreaks
White-box attacks remain a threat even for aligned models

Broader Implications
If safety is distributed across ~30% of heads, then:

Pruning/quantization must be careful about which heads to remove
Fine-tuning can inadvertently break safety by modifying these heads
Safety monitoring could track activations in critical heads

Key Technique to Extract
Global optimization over component sets — instead of greedy/local attribution, search the joint space of component combinations. More expensive but finds cooperative effects.
Questions

Do these safety vectors transfer across model families?
Could similar analysis find “recommendation safety” pathways (e.g., preventing filter bubbles)?
What’s the relationship between safety vectors and RLHF training dynamics?

literature/2026-01-25-ANN-v3-100B-Vector.title: ANN v3: 200ms p99 query latency over 100 billion vectors
literature/2026-01-25-ANN-v3-100B-Vector.content:
ANN v3: 200ms p99 query latency over 100 billion vectors
Summary
Turbopuffer rebuilt their vector search to handle 100 billion vectors at 200ms p99 latency and >1k QPS. This is a significant engineering achievement—100B vectors at 1024 dimensions in f16 is 200 TiB of data. The key insight is that vector search is bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound, so optimization must focus on memory hierarchy utilization rather than faster distance calculations.
The Core Insight: Arithmetic Intensity
Vector search has low arithmetic intensity. In a dot product, each data element is used exactly once—you multiply d[i] * q[i] and move on. Compare this to matrix multiplication where each element participates in N operations.
Low arithmetic intensity means: you’re limited by how fast you can feed data to the CPU, not how fast the CPU can process it.
This is why naive optimizations (faster SIMD instructions, GPU kernels) don’t help much—you’re already waiting on memory. The solution is to either:

Reduce how much data you need to load (approximation)
Keep frequently-accessed data in faster memory tiers (caching hierarchy)

Turbopuffer does both.
Technique 1: Hierarchical Clustering (SPFresh)
Instead of searching all 100B vectors, cluster them hierarchically:
Root centroid
├── Centroid C1
│   ├── Data vectors v1, v2, ...
│   └── ...
├── Centroid C2
│   └── ...
└── Centroid C3
    └── ...

At query time:

Compare query to root centroids → identify promising subtrees
Descend into those subtrees, compare to child centroids
Only scan data vectors in the final selected clusters

With a 100x branching factor at each level, a 100B vector index has ~4 levels. You scan maybe 500 clusters × 100 vectors = 50k vectors instead of 100B. That’s a 2-million-fold reduction in data touched.
Why 100x Branching?
This is clever: the branching factor roughly matches the DRAM-to-SSD size ratio (10-50x). If all data vectors fit on SSD, all centroid vectors fit in DRAM. Upper tree levels stay hot in fast memory; data vectors come from slower storage only when needed.
Spatial and Temporal Locality
Clustering provides:

Spatial locality: Vectors close in embedding space are stored contiguously → efficient sequential reads
Temporal locality: Upper tree levels accessed on every query → stay resident in cache

Technique 2: Binary Quantization (RaBitQ)
Even with clustering, you still need to scan ~50k vectors per query at ~2KB each = 100MB of bandwidth per tree level. At SSD speeds (~10 GB/s), that limits you to ~100 QPS.
Solution: compress the vectors. Binary quantization converts f16 (2 bytes/dim) to 1 bit/dim—a 16-32x compression.
[0.94, -0.01, 0.39, -0.72, ...] → [1, 0, 1, 0, ...]

Now those 50k vectors fit in ~6MB instead of 100MB. They can live higher in the memory hierarchy.
Why Doesn’t This Kill Accuracy?
RaBitQ exploits concentration of measure in high dimensions. In high-D space, vector components become more uniformly distributed, so quantization errors spread evenly rather than concentrating. The result: 1-bit quantization with surprisingly high recall.
The system stores both quantized vectors (for fast approximate search) and full-precision vectors (for final re-ranking of top candidates). Approximation + refinement.
The Memory Hierarchy
Tier              Size        Bandwidth
─────────────────────────────────────────
CPU Registers     <1KB        >10 TB/s
L1/L2/L3 Cache    KB-MB       1-10s TB/s
DRAM              GB-TB       100-500 GB/s
NVMe SSD          1-10s TB    1-30 GB/s
Object Storage    PB-EB       1-10 GB/s

The optimization goal: don’t let any single tier become the bottleneck. If you max out SSD bandwidth while DRAM sits idle, you’ve left performance on the table.
Turbopuffer’s architecture:

Stateless query tier over S3
NVMe as a cache layer
Quantized vectors sized to fit in DRAM
Centroids sized to fit in L3

Connections

embeddings — Production-scale embedding infrastructure considerations
vector search — State of the art for ANN at scale
vault-query — Scaling considerations if vault grows (currently tiny, but principles apply)
memory hierarchy — Classic systems concept, well-applied here
quantization — RaBitQ is a specific technique worth understanding

Key Numbers













































MetricValueVectors100 billionDimensions1024Precisionf16 (2 bytes/dim)Raw data size200 TiBQuery latency200ms p99Throughput>1k QPSClusters scanned~500Vectors per cluster~100Quantization ratio16-32x
Practical Takeaways

Think about bandwidth, not compute for vector workloads
Hierarchical clustering reduces search space by orders of magnitude
Binary quantization (especially RaBitQ) is surprisingly effective
Match data structure sizes to memory tier sizes for balanced utilization
The “approximation + refinement” pattern applies broadly

Questions for Review

 Is RaBitQ available in any open-source libraries?
 What’s our current vector count in any embedding use case? Growth trajectory?
 Could quantized embeddings reduce storage for hashcards?

Citation
VanBenschoten, Nathan. "ANN v3: 200ms p99 query latency over 100 billion vectors." 
Turbopuffer Blog, January 21, 2026.
turbopuffer.com/blog/ann-v3

literature/2026-01-25-Agentic-R-Learning.title: Agentic-R: Learning to Retrieve for Agentic Search
literature/2026-01-25-Agentic-R-Learning.content:
Agentic-R: Learning to Retrieve for Agentic Search
Summary
This paper addresses a fundamental problem in agentic search systems: standard retrievers don’t optimize for what actually matters—whether a passage helps produce correct final answers in multi-turn reasoning. Existing retrievers are trained for semantic similarity or single-turn RAG, but agentic systems iterate across multiple retrieval-reasoning steps. A passage that seems relevant might not actually help the agent reach the correct answer.
Agentic-R is a retriever training framework specifically designed for this setting.
The Core Problem
In agentic search:

Agent decomposes complex question into sub-queries
Agent retrieves passages for each sub-query
Agent reasons over passages, potentially generating follow-up queries
Process repeats until final answer

Standard retrievers optimize: “Is this passage semantically similar to the query?”
But the real question is: “Does using this passage lead to the correct final answer?”
These are very different objectives. A passage can be relevant but unhelpful (contains noise that confuses reasoning), or seemingly less relevant but highly useful (provides a crucial bridging fact).
The Solution: Two-Signal Training
Agentic-R trains retrievers using two complementary signals:
1. Local Relevance (Sub-query Level)
Uses LLM-based listwise scoring to assess how well a passage answers the current sub-query. This is a more nuanced relevance signal than embedding similarity—it captures whether the passage actually contains the information needed.
2. Global Answer Correctness (Final Answer Level)
Critically: measures whether using that passage leads the agent to the correct final answer. This propagates supervision from the outcome back to individual retrieval decisions.
The combination is powerful: local relevance prevents retrieving garbage, global correctness ensures retrieval actually helps reasoning.
Bidirectional Iterative Optimization
This is the key architectural insight:
┌─────────────┐      better passages      ┌─────────────┐
│  Retriever  │ ────────────────────────► │   Search    │
│             │                           │   Agent     │
└─────────────┘ ◄──────────────────────── └─────────────┘
                   better queries


Optimized retriever → Better passages for agent RL training

Agent learns from higher-quality retrieved context


Improved agent → Higher-quality queries for retriever training

Better queries provide cleaner supervision signal



This creates a virtuous cycle where retriever and agent improve each other.
Why This Matters for Autonomous Research
For any agentic system that does multi-step retrieval (including autonomous research), the retriever is a critical bottleneck. If early retrieval steps return unhelpful passages, errors compound through the reasoning chain.
Agentic-R’s insight applies directly:

Don’t just retrieve “relevant” papers—retrieve papers that help reach correct conclusions
The quality signal should come from downstream success, not just similarity scores
Retriever and reasoning components should co-evolve

Results
Outperforms both:

General-purpose retrievers (E5, BGE)
RAG-specific methods (REPLUG, SCARLet)

Particularly strong on multi-hop questions where the retriever-agent interplay matters most.
Connections

autonomous research — Directly applicable to multi-step research retrieval
RAG — Evolution beyond single-turn RAG toward agentic RAG
retrieval — New paradigm: retrieval quality measured by reasoning outcomes
self-improving agents — Bidirectional optimization is a form of co-evolution

Technical Details

Training uses RL to optimize retriever based on agent performance
Listwise scoring via LLM provides soft relevance labels
Global correctness computed by running full agent pipeline and checking final answer
Supports iterative refinement across training rounds

Practical Takeaways

Train retrieval for your actual use case — Generic retrievers optimize for the wrong objective
Propagate outcome supervision — Final answer correctness is the true quality signal
Co-optimize retriever + agent — They’re not independent components
Multi-hop benefits most — Single-hop questions don’t expose retriever limitations as clearly

Questions for Review

 Could we apply this to vault-query? What’s our “final answer correctness” signal?
 Is the bidirectional optimization tractable for our scale?
 What’s the computational cost of agent-in-the-loop retriever training?

Citation
@article{liu2026agenticr,
  title={Agentic-R: Learning to Retrieve for Agentic Search},
  author={Liu et al.},
  journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2601.11888},
  year={2026}
}

literature/2026-01-25-RAG-Chunking-Reran.title: Chunking, Retrieval, and Re-ranking: An Empirical Evaluation of RAG Architectures
literature/2026-01-25-RAG-Chunking-Reran.content:
Chunking, Retrieval, and Re-ranking: An Empirical Evaluation of RAG Architectures
Summary
This paper provides an empirical comparison of three RAG architecture tiers on CDC policy documents, measuring how each affects hallucination rates in high-stakes QA. The key finding is that two-stage retrieval with cross-encoder re-ranking is essential for professional-grade accuracy—basic RAG alone leaves significant misgrounding.
The Three Architectures
1. Vanilla LLM (Baseline)
Just prompting the model directly with no retrieval. Achieves only 0.347 faithfulness—essentially unusable for policy/legal work where every claim must be grounded.
2. Basic RAG
Standard retrieve-then-generate: embed the query, find similar chunks via vector search, stuff them into context. Uses all-MiniLM-L6-v2 for embeddings. Improves faithfulness to 0.621—a 79% improvement, but still leaves notable errors.
3. Advanced RAG (with Re-ranking)
Adds a cross-encoder re-ranking stage after initial retrieval. Cross-encoders score query-document pairs jointly (rather than encoding them separately), which captures finer semantic relationships. This pushes faithfulness to 0.797—a 2.3x improvement over vanilla.
The intuition: bi-encoders (used in basic RAG) encode query and document independently, then compare via cosine similarity. This is fast but loses cross-attention signal. Cross-encoders are slower but more accurate because they see query and document together.
Chunking Strategies Compared
The paper tests two approaches:


Recursive character-based splitting — Split on character boundaries, recursively subdivide large chunks. Simple but can break mid-sentence.


Token-based semantic splitting — Split on token boundaries with awareness of semantic units (sentences, paragraphs). Preserves meaning better but requires more preprocessing.


Finding: Semantic chunking helps, but the re-ranking stage matters more. A good re-ranker can partially compensate for suboptimal chunking.
Why This Matters
The “notable misgrounding” in basic RAG is the key insight. When you retrieve 5 chunks and stuff them in context, the model might:

Hallucinate connections between unrelated chunks
Misattribute claims to the wrong source
Fill gaps with plausible-sounding fabrications

The re-ranker acts as a precision filter—it sees query + each chunk together and can catch mismatches that embedding similarity misses.
Connections

RAG — This is the canonical architecture comparison paper for RAG tiers
hallucination — Concrete numbers on how retrieval architecture affects fabrication rates
vault-query — Worth testing cross-encoder re-ranking on vault search results

Practical Takeaways

If you care about accuracy, you need re-ranking. Period.
Chunking strategy matters less than re-ranking quality
For high-stakes domains, 0.621 faithfulness (basic RAG) is still too low
Cross-encoders are the key—consider cross-encoder models like ms-marco-MiniLM

Questions for Review

 What re-ranker should we test in vault-query? (ms-marco variants?)
 Is our current chunking strategy semantic or character-based?
 Can we measure our current faithfulness score?

Citation
@article{yadav2026chunking,
  title={Chunking, Retrieval, and Re-ranking: An Empirical Evaluation of RAG Architectures for Policy Document Question Answering},
  author={Yadav, Umesh et al.},
  journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2601.15457},
  year={2026}
}

literature/2026-01-25-RecSys-Substack-Di.title: RecSys Substack Digest: Retriever Gap in Agentic Search + 9 More
literature/2026-01-25-RecSys-Substack-Di.content:
RecSys Substack Digest: 10 Papers on RAG, Retrieval, and RecSys
This digest covers 10 recent papers. I’ve synthesized the most relevant ones below with brief notes on the others.

Highly Relevant (Full Notes Available)
1. Agentic-R: Learning to Retrieve for Agentic Search
→ See separate note: 2026-01-25-Agentic-R-Learning-to-Retrieve
TL;DR: Train retrievers for multi-turn agentic search by optimizing for final answer correctness, not just passage relevance. Bidirectional co-optimization of retriever + agent.

Relevant (Summarized)
2. Deep GraphRAG (Ant Group)
📚 arxiv.org/abs/2601.11144
Problem: Global graph search captures broad context but is slow; local search is fast but misses cross-community relationships.
Solution: Three-stage hierarchical retrieval:

Inter-community filtering
Community-level refinement
Entity-level search

Uses beam search (k=3) over a weighted Louvain clustering hierarchy.
Key technique: DW-GRPO (Dynamic Weighting Reward GRPO) — RL approach that adaptively rebalances reward weights for relevance, faithfulness, and conciseness during training. Counteracts the “seesaw effect” where models over-optimize easy metrics.
Result: 1.5B model achieves 94% of 72B model’s performance on Natural Questions.
Relevance: Graph-based RAG for knowledge-intensive domains. Connects to knowledge graphs, RAG.

3. PruneRAG: Confidence-Guided Query Decomposition Trees
📚 arxiv.org/abs/2601.11024
👨‍💻 github.com/Fdioa/PruneRAG
Problem: Multi-hop RAG suffers from “evidence forgetting” (retrieved docs not used) and inefficiency from uncontrolled query expansion.
Solution: Structured query decomposition tree with:

Adaptive node expansion — Decides: answer directly, decompose into sub-queries, or abstract into entities
Confidence-guided pruning — Token-level log probability scoring, only accepts answers above threshold (τ=0.95)
Fine-grained retrieval — Extracts key entities as retrieval anchors

New metric: Evidence Forgetting Rate (EFR) — proportion of cases where all golden docs were retrieved but answer still wrong.
Result: Highest F1 on HotpotQA/2WikiMultihop/MusiQue, 4.9× faster than RAG-Star and Search-o1.
Relevance: Efficient multi-hop reasoning. Connects to RAG, hallucination (confidence thresholds).

4. Rank4Gen: Generator-Aware Document Ranking
📚 arxiv.org/abs/2601.11273
👨‍💻 github.com/JOHNNY-fans/Rank4Gen
Problem: Traditional rankers optimize for query-document relevance, but relevance ≠ generation quality. What helps Qwen3-8B may not help Llama-3.1-8B.
Solution: Train ranker conditioned on generator-specific information:

Generator unique IDs
Textual descriptions of each generator’s characteristics
Optimize for downstream response quality, not relevance scores

Key insight: Optimal document configurations vary by generator. A single ranker can learn generator-specific preferences.
Connects to ranking systems, RAG.

5. RT-RAG: Tree-Structured Reasoning for Multi-Hop QA
📚 arxiv.org/abs/2601.11255
👨‍💻 github.com/sakura20221/RT-RAG
Solution: Decompose questions into explicit tree structures via entity analysis. Traverse bottom-up (resolve leaf nodes before parents). Includes rejection sampling, query rewriting for failed retrievals, adaptive restructuring.
Relevance: Structured decomposition for complex questions. Connects to RAG, multi-hop reasoning.

RecSys-Focused (Brief Notes)
6. CoNRec: Negative Feedback Modeling (Alibaba)
📚 arxiv.org/abs/2601.15721
Models what users dislike rather than just what they like. Uses hierarchical semantic IDs via RQ-VAE and Progressive GRPO training. 24.9% improvement on long-tail items.
Relevance: Negative signal modeling for recommenders. Connects to recommender systems.

7. HyFormer: Long-Sequence CTR Prediction (ByteDance)
📚 arxiv.org/abs/2601.12681
Hybrid transformer for industrial CTR. Unifies long sequence modeling + feature interaction via Global Tokens. Better than Full Transformer + RankMixer with fewer FLOPs.
Relevance: Industrial-scale CTR.

8. Sparse Autoencoders for Collaborative Filtering
📚 arxiv.org/abs/2601.11182
Insert SAEs into CFAE to get interpretable, steerable recommendations. Individual neurons correspond to concepts (“Quentin Tarantino”, “love story”). Can boost/suppress neurons to steer recommendations.
Relevance: Interpretability + controllability in RecSys. Connects to recommender systems, interpretability.

9. PopSteer: Steering Popularity Bias (TU Delft)
📚 arxiv.org/abs/2601.15122
Uses SAEs to identify and suppress popularity-aligned neurons in recommender embeddings. Achieves fairness improvements while maintaining accuracy.
Relevance: Bias mitigation in RecSys. Connects to recommender systems, fairness.

10. Predicting RAG Utility and Answer Quality (Glasgow)
📚 arxiv.org/abs/2601.14546
Formalizes Retrieval Performance Prediction (RPP) and Generation Performance Prediction (GPP). Combines QPP methods + perplexity-based measures + document quality metrics.
Relevance: Meta-prediction for RAG systems. Connects to RAG, evaluation.

Cross-Cutting Themes

Multi-hop reasoning is the frontier — single-turn RAG is increasingly solved
Confidence/quality signals are critical for pruning and reliability
Generator-aware retrieval — what the model can use matters more than relevance
Graph structure helps for knowledge-intensive domains
Sparse autoencoders emerging as interpretability tool for embeddings/recommendations


Follow-Up Actions

 Deep dive on Agentic-R (done: separate note)
 Consider PruneRAG’s confidence thresholds for our synthesis pipeline
 Rank4Gen’s generator-awareness might apply to vault-query

literature/2026-01-25-Reliability-by-Des.title: Reliability by Design: Quantifying and Eliminating Fabrication Risk in LLMs
literature/2026-01-25-Reliability-by-Des.content:
Reliability by Design: Quantifying and Eliminating Fabrication Risk in LLMs
Summary
This paper tackles a fundamental question: how do we make LLMs trustworthy for high-stakes work? The authors introduce two new metrics for measuring fabrication, evaluate 12 LLMs across 75 legal tasks with expert review, and show that advanced RAG architectures can reduce fabrication to negligible levels (<0.2%).
The core insight is that “consultative AI” (retrieval-based, verification-focused) is fundamentally different from “generative AI” (creative, unconstrained)—and high-stakes domains need the former.
The Two Metrics
False Citation Rate (FCR)
Percentage of citations that are:

Nonexistent (made-up case law, fake papers)
Misattributed (real source, wrong claim)
Irrelevant (real source, doesn’t support the point)

Standalone LLMs show >30% FCR—nearly 1 in 3 citations is wrong. This is disqualifying for legal work.
Fabricated Fact Rate (FFR)
Percentage of factual assertions that are invented rather than grounded in source material. Measures the “confident bullshit” problem where models state things authoritatively that have no basis.
These metrics separate two failure modes: bad sourcing (FCR) vs bad claims (FFR). A system could cite real sources but make wrong claims, or vice versa.
The Three Paradigms
The paper frames three approaches as characters:
1. “Creative Oracle” (Standalone LLM)
No retrieval, pure generation. FCR >30%, unsuitable for professional use. The model just makes things up when it doesn’t know—and it doesn’t know it doesn’t know.
2. “Expert Archivist” (Basic RAG)
Retrieves relevant documents, generates grounded responses. Dramatically better, but still has 10-15% error rates. The problem: retrieval errors propagate to generation, and the model still fills gaps creatively.
3. “Rigorous Archivist” (Advanced RAG)
The full stack: embedding fine-tuning + re-ranking + self-correction loops. Achieves <0.2% fabrication.
The Self-Correction Loop
This is the key technique beyond standard RAG:

Generate initial response with citations
Verify each citation — Does it actually exist? Does it support the claim?
Check for gaps — Are there unsourced claims?
Regenerate with corrections

This is expensive (multiple LLM calls per response) but necessary for high-stakes use. The system essentially reviews its own work before submitting.
The verification step is critical: the model checks “did I actually get this from the retrieved documents, or did I make it up?” This catches the subtle hallucinations that slip through basic RAG.
Why 30% FCR is So Bad
Consider: if 1 in 3 citations is wrong, and a legal brief has 30 citations, you’d expect ~10 fabricated references. Any opposing counsel who checks will destroy your credibility. This is why the paper concludes standalone LLMs are “unsuitable for professional use” in legal contexts—not just suboptimal, but dangerous.
Connections

RAG — Complements the chunking paper; both show advanced RAG >> basic RAG
hallucination — FCR and FFR are concrete, measurable definitions of hallucination
autonomous research — For any system generating literature notes with citations, FCR is the metric to track
self-improving agents — The self-correction loop is a form of self-critique, related to constitutional AI and self-refinement patterns

Practical Takeaways

Measure fabrication explicitly — FCR and FFR should be standard metrics for any RAG system
Self-correction is expensive but necessary for high-stakes domains
Embedding fine-tuning helps — Domain-specific embeddings catch more relevant chunks
The gap between “impressive demo” and “professional-grade” is 30x (30% → <1% error)

Application to Our Work
For the autonomous research system, we should:

Verify every [wikilink](/refs/wikilink/) actually exists in the vault before approving
Track approval/rejection rates as a proxy for synthesis quality
Consider a self-correction pass that checks “does this claim appear in the source material?”

Questions for Review

 Should we implement FCR tracking for synthesized literature notes?
 What would a self-correction loop look like in our pipeline?
 Is 0.2% fabrication rate achievable with current models + good RAG?

Citation
@article{dantart2026reliability,
  title={Reliability by design: quantifying and eliminating fabrication risk in LLMs},
  author={Dantart, Alex},
  journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2601.15476},
  year={2026}
}

literature/2026-01-26-A-Feature-Extracti.filePath: literature/2026-01-26 A Feature Extraction Pipeline for Enhancing Lightweight Neur.md
literature/2026-01-26-A-Feature-Extracti.title: A Feature Extraction Pipeline for Enhancing Lightweight Neural Networks in sEMG-based Joint Torque Estimation
literature/2026-01-26-A-Feature-Extracti.content:
A Feature Extraction Pipeline for Enhancing Lightweight Neural Networks in sEMG-based Joint Torque Estimation
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-23
Rank: #9 (score: 2.62, max_sim: 0.74)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Graph
Abstract
Robot-assisted rehabilitation offers an effective approach, wherein exoskeletons adapt to users’ needs and provide personalized assistance. However, to deliver such assistance, accurate prediction of the user’s joint torques is essential. In this work, we propose a feature extraction pipeline using 8-channel surface electromyography (sEMG) signals to predict elbow and shoulder joint torques. For preliminary evaluation, this pipeline was integrated into two neural network models: the Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) and the Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN). Data were collected from a single subject performing elbow and shoulder movements under three load conditions (0 kg, 1.10 kg, and 1.85 kg) using three motion-capture cameras. Reference torques were estimated from center-of-mass kinematics under the assumption of static equilibrium. Our offline analyses showed that, with our feature extraction pipeline, MLP model achieved mean RMSE of 0.963 N m, 1.403 N m, and 1.434 N m (over five seeds) for elbow, front-shoulder, and side-shoulder joints, respectively, which were comparable to the TCN performance. These results demonstrate that the proposed feature extraction pipeline enables a simple MLP to achieve performance comparable to that of a network designed explicitly for temporal dependencies. This finding is particularly relevant for applications with limited training data, a common scenario patient care.
Key Techniques

Based on the abstract
here are the 3 key techniques:

**sEMG signal processing

Feature extraction pipeline
Neural network models (MLP and TCN)**

literature/2026-01-26-AI-Co-Scientist-fo.filePath: literature/2026-01-26 AI Co-Scientist for Knowledge Synthesis in Medical Contexts.md
literature/2026-01-26-AI-Co-Scientist-fo.title: AI Co-Scientist for Knowledge Synthesis in Medical Contexts: A Proof of Concept
literature/2026-01-26-AI-Co-Scientist-fo.content:
AI Co-Scientist for Knowledge Synthesis in Medical Contexts: A Proof of Concept
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-16
Rank: #17 (score: 5.20, max_sim: 0.78)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Graph, Transformer
Abstract
Research waste in biomedical science is driven by redundant studies, incomplete reporting, and the limited scalability of traditional evidence synthesis workflows. We present an AI co-scientist for scalable and transparent knowledge synthesis based on explicit formalization of Population, Intervention, Comparator, Outcome, and Study design (PICOS). The platform integrates relational storage, vector-based semantic retrieval, and a Neo4j knowledge graph. Evaluation was conducted on dementia-sport and non-communicable disease corpora. Automated PICOS compliance and study design classification from titles and abstracts were performed using a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory baseline and a transformer-based multi-task classifier fine-tuned from PubMedBERT. Full-text synthesis employed retrieval-augmented generation with hybrid vector and graph retrieval, while BERTopic was used to identify thematic structure, redundancy, and evidence gaps. The transformer model achieved 95.7% accuracy for study design classification with strong agreement against expert annotations, while the Bi-LSTM achieved 87% accuracy for PICOS compliance detection. Retrieval-augmented generation outperformed non-retrieval generation for queries requiring structured constraints, cross-study integration, and graph-based reasoning, whereas non-retrieval approaches remained competitive for high-level summaries. Topic modeling revealed substantial thematic redundancy and identified underexplored research areas. These results demonstrate that PICOS-aware and explainable natural language processing can improve the scalability, transparency, and efficiency of evidence synthesis. The proposed architecture is domain-agnostic and offers a practical framework for reducing research waste across biomedical disciplines.
Key Techniques

PICOS formalization
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
BERTopic topic modeling

Related Litnotes

literature/2026-01-26-Artificial-Rigidit.title: Artificial Rigidities vs. Biological Noise: A Comparative Analysis of Multisensory Integration in AV-HuBERT and Human Observers
literature/2026-01-26-Artificial-Rigidit.content:
Artificial Rigidities vs. Biological Noise: A Comparative Analysis of Multisensory Integration in AV-HuBERT and Human Observers
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-22
Rank: #11 (score: 5.67, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
This study evaluates AV-HuBERT’s perceptual bio-fidelity by benchmarking its response to incongruent audiovisual stimuli (McGurk effect) against human observers (N=44). Results reveal a striking quantitative isomorphism: AI and humans exhibited nearly identical auditory dominance rates (32.0% vs. 31.8%), suggesting the model captures biological thresholds for auditory resistance. However, AV-HuBERT showed a deterministic bias toward phonetic fusion (68.0%), significantly exceeding human rates (47.7%). While humans displayed perceptual stochasticity and diverse error profiles, the model remained strictly categorical. Findings suggest that current self-supervised architectures mimic multisensory outcomes but lack the neural variability inherent to human speech perception.
Key Techniques

Bayesian multisensory integration models
causal inference models
self-supervised audiovisual learning (AV-HuBERT)

literature/2026-01-26-Balancing-Fairness.filePath: literature/2026-01-26 Balancing Fairness and High Match Rates in Reciprocal Recomm.md
literature/2026-01-26-Balancing-Fairness.title: Balancing Fairness and High Match Rates in Reciprocal Recommender Systems: A Nash Social Welfare Approach
literature/2026-01-26-Balancing-Fairness.content:
Balancing Fairness and High Match Rates in Reciprocal Recommender Systems: A Nash Social Welfare Approach
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-20
Rank: #18 (score: 5.17, max_sim: 0.78)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RecSys
Code: github.com/CyberAgentAILab/FairReciprocalRecommendation
Abstract
Matching platforms, such as online dating services and job recommendations, have become increasingly prevalent. For the success of these platforms, it is crucial to design reciprocal recommender systems (RRSs) that not only increase the total number of matches but also avoid creating unfairness among users. In this paper, we investigate the fairness of RRSs on matching platforms. From the perspective of fair division, we define the users’ opportunities to be recommended and establish the fairness concept of envy-freeness in the allocation of these opportunities. We first introduce the Social Welfare (SW) method, which approximately maximizes the number of matches, and show that it leads to significant unfairness in recommendation opportunities, illustrating the trade-off between fairness and match rates. To address this challenge, we propose the Nash Social Welfare (NSW) method, which alternately optimizes two NSW functions and achieves nearly envy-free recommendations. We further generalize the SW and NSW method to the α-SW method, which balances the trade-off between fairness and high match rates. Additionally, we develop a computationally efficient approximation algorithm for the SW/NSW/α-SW methods based on the Sinkhorn algorithm. Through extensive experiments on both synthetic datasets and two real-world datasets, we demonstrate the practical effectiveness of our approach.
Key Techniques

Nash Social Welfare optimization
Sinkhorn algorithm approximation
envy-freeness allocation

Related Litnotes

literature/2026-01-26-Bayesian-Experimen.filePath: literature/2026-01-26 Bayesian Experimental Design for Model Discrepancy Calibrati.md
literature/2026-01-26-Bayesian-Experimen.title: Bayesian Experimental Design for Model Discrepancy Calibration: A Rivalry between Kullback--Leibler Divergence and Wasserstein Distance
literature/2026-01-26-Bayesian-Experimen.content:
Bayesian Experimental Design for Model Discrepancy Calibration: A Rivalry between Kullback—Leibler Divergence and Wasserstein Distance
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-23
Rank: #10 (score: 5.69, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Designing experiments that systematically gather data from complex physical systems is central to accelerating scientific discovery. While Bayesian experimental design (BED) provides a principled, information-based framework that integrates experimental planning with probabilistic inference, the selection of utility functions in BED is a long-standing and active topic, where different criteria emphasize different notions of information. Although Kullback—Leibler (KL) divergence has been one of the most common choices, recent studies have proposed Wasserstein distance as an alternative. In this work, we first employ a toy example to illustrate an issue of Wasserstein distance - the value of Wasserstein distance of a fixed-shape posterior depends on the relative position of its main mass within the support and can exhibit false rewards unrelated to information gain, especially with a non-informative prior (e.g., uniform distribution). We then further provide a systematic comparison between these two criteria through a classical source inversion problem in the BED literature, revealing that the KL divergence tends to lead to faster convergence in the absence of model discrepancy, while Wasserstein metrics provide more robust sequential BED results if model discrepancy is non-negligible. These findings clarify the trade-offs between KL divergence and Wasserstein metrics for the utility function and provide guidelines for selecting suitable criteria in practical BED applications.
Key Techniques

Kullback-Leibler divergence
Wasserstein distance
Sequential Bayesian experimental design

Related Litnotes

literature/2026-01-26-BioPulse-QA-A-Dyna.filePath: literature/2026-01-26 BioPulse-QA A Dynamic Biomedical Question-Answering Benchmar.md
literature/2026-01-26-BioPulse-QA-A-Dyna.title: BioPulse-QA: A Dynamic Biomedical Question-Answering Benchmark for Evaluating Factuality, Robustness, and Bias in Large Language Models
literature/2026-01-26-BioPulse-QA-A-Dyna.content:
BioPulse-QA: A Dynamic Biomedical Question-Answering Benchmark for Evaluating Factuality, Robustness, and Bias in Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-19
Rank: #21 (score: 4.85, max_sim: 0.78)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Graph, LLM, RAG
Abstract
Objective: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in biomedical settings, and existing benchmark datasets have played an important role in supporting model development and evaluation. However, these benchmarks often have limitations. Many rely on static or outdated datasets that fail to capture the dynamic, context-rich, and high-stakes nature of biomedical knowledge. They also carry increasing risk of data leakage due to overlap with model pretraining corpora and often overlook critical dimensions such as robustness to linguistic variation and potential demographic biases.   Materials and Methods: To address these gaps, we introduce BioPulse-QA, a benchmark that evaluates LLMs on answering questions from newly published biomedical documents including drug labels, trial protocols, and clinical guidelines. BioPulse-QA includes 2,280 expert-verified question answering (QA) pairs and perturbed variants, covering both extractive and abstractive formats. We evaluate four LLMs - GPT-4o, GPT-o1, Gemini-2.0-Flash, and LLaMA-3.1 8B Instruct - released prior to the publication dates of the benchmark documents.   Results: GPT-o1 achieves the highest relaxed F1 score (0.92), followed by Gemini-2.0-Flash (0.90) on drug labels. Clinical trials are the most challenging source, with extractive F1 scores as low as 0.36.   Discussion and Conclusion: Performance differences are larger for paraphrasing than for typographical errors, while bias testing shows negligible differences. BioPulse-QA provides a scalable and clinically relevant framework for evaluating biomedical LLMs.
Key Techniques

Dynamic biomedical QA benchmark
Expert-verified QA pairs with perturbation variants
Multi-source evaluation (drug labels/clinical trials/guidelines)

literature/2026-01-26-Blockchain-Based-S.title: Blockchain-Based Spectrum Resource Securitization via Semi-Fungible Token-Lock
literature/2026-01-26-Blockchain-Based-S.content:
Blockchain-Based Spectrum Resource Securitization via Semi-Fungible Token-Lock
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-22
Rank: #8 (score: 2.62, max_sim: 0.74)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
As 6G networks evolve, spectrum assets require flexible, dynamic, and efficient utilization, motivating blockchain based spectrum securitization. Existing approaches based on ERC404 style hybrid token models rely on frequent minting and burning during asset transfers, which disrupt token identity continuity and increase on chain overhead. This paper proposes the Semi Fungible Token Lock (SFT Lock) method, a lock/unlock based mechanism that preserves NFT identity and historical traceability while enabling fractional ownership and transferability. By replacing mint/burn operations with deterministic state transitions, SFT Lock ensures consistent lifecycle representation of spectrum assets and significantly reduces on chain operations. Based on this mechanism, a modular smart contract architecture is designed to support spectrum authorization, securitization, and sharing, and a staking mechanism is introduced to enhance asset liquidity. Experimental results on a private Ethereum network demonstrate that, compared with ERC404 style hybrid token models, the proposed method achieves substantial gas savings while maintaining functional correctness and traceability.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper abstract
here are the key techniques:

Semi Fungible Token Lock (SFT Lock) mechanism

Deterministic state transitions with lock/unlock operations
Modular smart contract architecture for spectrum management

literature/2026-01-26-Calibrated-Probabi.filePath: literature/2026-01-26 Calibrated Probabilistic Interpolation for GEDI Biomass.md
literature/2026-01-26-Calibrated-Probabi.title: Calibrated Probabilistic Interpolation for GEDI Biomass
literature/2026-01-26-Calibrated-Probabi.content:
Calibrated Probabilistic Interpolation for GEDI Biomass
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-23
Rank: #5 (score: 2.67, max_sim: 0.73)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Reliable wall-to-wall biomass mapping from NASA’s GEDI mission requires interpolating sparse LiDAR observations across heterogeneous landscapes. While machine learning approaches like Random Forest and XGBoost are standard for this task, they treat spatial predictions of GEDI observations from multispectral or SAR remote sensing data as independent without adapting to the varying difficulty of heterogeneous landscapes. We demonstrate these approaches generally fail to produce calibrated prediction intervals. We identify that this stems from conflating ensemble variance with aleatoric uncertainty and ignoring local spatial context.   To resolve this, we introduce Attentive Neural Processes (ANPs), a probabilistic meta-learning framework that explicitly conditions predictions on local observation sets and geospatial foundation model embeddings. Unlike static ensembles, ANPs learn a flexible spatial covariance function, allowing uncertainty estimates to expand in complex landscapes and contract in homogeneous areas. We validate this approach across five distinct biomes ranging from Tropical Amazonian forests to Boreal and Alpine ecosystems, demonstrating that ANPs achieve competitive accuracy while maintaining near-ideal uncertainty calibration. We demonstrate the operational utility of the method through few-shot adaptation, where the model recovers most of the performance gap in cross-region transfer using minimal local data. This work provides a scalable, theoretically rigorous alternative to ensemble variance for continental scale earth observation.
Key Techniques

Based on the abstract
here are the key techniques/methods mentioned:

**Attentive Neural Processes (ANPs)

Probabilistic meta-learning
probabilistic meta-learning as the foundational approach

literature/2026-01-26-ChartVerse-Scaling.filePath: literature/2026-01-26 ChartVerse Scaling Chart Reasoning via Reliable Programmatic.md
literature/2026-01-26-ChartVerse-Scaling.title: ChartVerse: Scaling Chart Reasoning via Reliable Programmatic Synthesis from Scratch
literature/2026-01-26-ChartVerse-Scaling.content:
ChartVerse: Scaling Chart Reasoning via Reliable Programmatic Synthesis from Scratch
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-20
Rank: #22 (score: 4.57, max_sim: 0.79)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM
Abstract
Chart reasoning is a critical capability for Vision Language Models (VLMs). However, the development of open-source models is severely hindered by the lack of high-quality training data. Existing datasets suffer from a dual challenge: synthetic charts are often simplistic and repetitive, while the associated QA pairs are prone to hallucinations and lack the reasoning depth required for complex tasks. To bridge this gap, we propose ChartVerse, a scalable framework designed to synthesize complex charts and reliable reasoning data from scratch. (1) To address the bottleneck of simple patterns, we first introduce Rollout Posterior Entropy (RPE), a novel metric that quantifies chart complexity. Guided by RPE, we develop complexity-aware chart coder to autonomously synthesize diverse, high-complexity charts via executable programs. (2) To guarantee reasoning rigor, we develop truth-anchored inverse QA synthesis. Diverging from standard generation, we adopt an answer-first paradigm: we extract deterministic answers directly from the source code, generate questions conditional on these anchors, and enforce strict consistency verification. To further elevate difficulty and reasoning depth, we filter samples based on model fail-rate and distill high-quality Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. We curate ChartVerse-SFT-600K and ChartVerse-RL-40K using Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Thinking as the teacher. Experimental results demonstrate that ChartVerse-8B achieves state-of-the-art performance, notably surpassing its teacher and rivaling the stronger Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking.
Key Techniques

Rollout Posterior Entropy (RPE)
truth-anchored inverse QA synthesis
Chain-of-Thought (CoT) distillation

literature/2026-01-26-Curated-endoscopic.title: Curated endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography images dataset
literature/2026-01-26-Curated-endoscopic.content:
Curated endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography images dataset
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-23
Rank: #1 (score: 3.37, max_sim: 0.66)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Graph
Abstract
Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) is a key procedure in the diagnosis and treatment of biliary and pancreatic diseases. Artificial intelligence has been pointed as one solution to automatize diagnosis. However, public ERCP datasets are scarce, which limits the use of such approach. Therefore, this study aims to help fill this gap by providing a large and curated dataset. The collection is composed of 19.018 raw images and 19.317 processed from 1.602 patients. 5.519 images are labeled, which provides a ready to use dataset. All images were manually inspected and annotated by two gastroenterologist with more than 5 years of experience and reviewed by another gastroenterologist with more than 20 years of experience, all with more than 400 ERCP procedures annually. The utility and validity of the dataset is proven by a classification experiment. This collection aims to provide or contribute for a benchmark in automatic ERCP analysis and diagnosis of biliary and pancreatic diseases.

literature/2026-01-26-Domain-Incremental.title: Domain-Incremental Continual Learning for Robust and Efficient Keyword Spotting in Resource Constrained Systems
literature/2026-01-26-Domain-Incremental.content:
Domain-Incremental Continual Learning for Robust and Efficient Keyword Spotting in Resource Constrained Systems
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-22
Rank: #3 (score: 6.02, max_sim: 0.76)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG
Abstract
Keyword Spotting (KWS) systems with small footprint models deployed on edge devices face significant accuracy and robustness challenges due to domain shifts caused by varying noise and recording conditions. To address this, we propose a comprehensive framework for continual learning designed to adapt to new domains while maintaining computational efficiency. The proposed pipeline integrates a dual-input Convolutional Neural Network, utilizing both Mel Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC) and Mel-spectrogram features, supported by a multi-stage denoising process, involving discrete wavelet transform and spectral subtraction techniques, plus model and prototype update blocks. Unlike prior methods that restrict updates to specific layers, our approach updates the complete quantized model, made possible due to compact model architecture. A subset of input samples are selected during runtime using class prototypes and confidence-driven filtering, which are then pseudo-labeled and combined with rehearsal buffer for incremental model retraining. Experimental results on noisy test dataset demonstrate the framework’s effectiveness, achieving 99.63% accuracy on clean data and maintaining robust performance (exceeding 94% accuracy) across diverse noisy environments, even at -10 dB Signal-to-Noise Ratio. The proposed framework work confirms that integrating efficient denoising with prototype-based continual learning enables KWS models to operate autonomously and robustly in resource-constrained, dynamic environments.
Key Techniques

Dual-input CNN with MFCC and Mel-spectrogram features
Multi-stage denoising with discrete wavelet transform and spectral subtraction
Prototype-based continual learning with rehearsal buffer and confidence-driven sample selection
