literature/2026-01-27-LLM-powered-Real-t.filePath: literature/2026-01-27 LLM-powered Real-time Patent Citation Recommendation for Fin.md
literature/2026-01-27-LLM-powered-Real-t.title: LLM-powered Real-time Patent Citation Recommendation for Financial Technologies
literature/2026-01-27-LLM-powered-Real-t.content:
LLM-powered Real-time Patent Citation Recommendation for Financial Technologies
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-23
Rank: #5 (score: 5.95, max_sim: 0.76)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RecSys, LLM, RAG, Graph
Abstract
Rapid financial innovation has been accompanied by a sharp increase in patenting activity, making timely and comprehensive prior-art discovery more difficult. This problem is especially evident in financial technologies, where innovations develop quickly, patent collections grow continuously, and citation recommendation systems must be updated as new applications arrive. Existing patent retrieval and citation recommendation methods typically rely on static indexes or periodic retraining, which limits their ability to operate effectively in such dynamic settings. In this study, we propose a real-time patent citation recommendation framework designed for large and fast-changing financial patent corpora. Using a dataset of 428,843 financial patents granted by the China National Intellectual Property Administration (CNIPA) between 2000 and 2024, we build a three-stage recommendation pipeline. The pipeline uses large language model (LLM) embeddings to represent the semantic content of patent abstracts, applies efficient approximate nearest-neighbor search to construct a manageable candidate set, and ranks candidates by semantic similarity to produce top-k citation recommendations. In addition to improving recommendation accuracy, the proposed framework directly addresses the dynamic nature of patent systems. By using an incremental indexing strategy based on hierarchical navigable small-world (HNSW) graphs, newly issued patents can be added without rebuilding the entire index. A rolling day-by-day update experiment shows that incremental updating improves recall while substantially reducing computational cost compared with rebuild-based indexing. The proposed method also consistently outperforms traditional text-based baselines and alternative nearest-neighbor retrieval approaches.
Key Techniques

LLM embeddings
approximate nearest-neighbor search
hierarchical navigable small-world (HNSW) graphs

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLLM-powered Real-time Patent Citation Recommendation for Financial Technologies0.89LLM-powered Real-time Patent Citation Recommendation for Financial Technologies0.88Domain-Adaptive and Scalable Dense Retrieval for Content-Based Recommendation0.24Rethinking ANN-based Retrieval: Multifaceted Learnable Index for Large-scale Recommendation System0.14Rethinking ANN-based Retrieval: Multifaceted Learnable Index for Large-scale Recommendation System0.14
Analysis: Patent Citation as Specialized Citation Recommendation
This patent citation paper directly instantiates the “Traditional Retrieval Techniques” framework but in a domain-specific, real-time setting. Rather than static recommender matrices, this work applies LLM embeddings as the foundational semantic representation layer, then layers on efficient approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search via HNSW graphs to handle the “scale and latency constraints” that the traditional notes identify as critical evolution points. The paper’s three-stage pipeline—embedding → candidate retrieval via ANN → semantic similarity ranking—mirrors the progression described in the vault notes: starting simple (deterministic retrieval), adding latent representation (LLM embeddings as learned latent space), and finally composing mechanisms for dynamic indexing. This directly addresses the “What changes at scale” question from the notes, specifically tackling the “indexing and latency constraints” through incremental HNSW insertion rather than periodic retraining.
The connection to “Generative Large Recommendation Model” is equally significant. While that note explores LLM-enhanced recommendation through reasoning and content generation, this patent paper uses LLM embeddings as a retrieval primitive—treating LLM representations as pre-computed, dense features for semantic matching. This is a complementary approach: rather than using LLM reasoning for ranking, the paper leverages LLM-generated embeddings for first-stage retrieval efficiency. Both share the insight that LLMs provide richer semantic understanding than traditional lexical or collaborative methods, but this work emphasizes the retrieval-efficiency side (finding candidates fast in dynamic corpora) rather than the ranking-reasoning side.
A novel perspective this paper adds is the financial patent domain instantiation. Unlike general recommender systems addressing information overload or traditional academic citation graphs, patent citation has unique constraints: rapid patent arrival, domain-specific semantic drift (financial tech innovations), and the need to avoid stale indexes. The incremental HNSW strategy is particularly salient here—it solves the “freshness” problem mentioned in the traditional notes by enabling online graph updates without full recomputation. This extends recommender system thinking into highly dynamic, regulated corpora where new items arrive continuously and retrieval must remain fast and current.

literature/2026-01-27-Learned-harmonic-m.title: Learned harmonic mean estimation of the marginal likelihood for multimodal posteriors with flow matching
literature/2026-01-27-Learned-harmonic-m.content:
Learned harmonic mean estimation of the marginal likelihood for multimodal posteriors with flow matching
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #10 (score: 5.44, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The marginal likelihood, or Bayesian evidence, is a crucial quantity for Bayesian model comparison but its computation can be challenging for complex models, even in parameters space of moderate dimension. The learned harmonic mean estimator has been shown to provide accurate and robust estimates of the marginal likelihood simply using posterior samples. It is agnostic to the sampling strategy, meaning that the samples can be obtained using any method. This enables marginal likelihood calculation and model comparison with whatever sampling is most suitable for the task. However, the internal density estimators considered previously for the learned harmonic mean can struggle with highly multimodal posteriors. In this work we introduce flow matching-based continuous normalizing flows as a powerful architecture for the internal density estimation of the learned harmonic mean. We demonstrate the ability to handle challenging multimodal posteriors, including an example in 20 parameter dimensions, showcasing the method’s ability to handle complex posteriors without the need for fine-tuning or heuristic modifications to the base distribution.
Key Techniques

Learned harmonic mean estimator
Flow matching-based continuous normalizing flows
Posterior density estimation

literature/2026-01-27-MalURLBench-A-Benc.title: MalURLBench: A Benchmark Evaluating Agents' Vulnerabilities When Processing Web URLs
literature/2026-01-27-MalURLBench-A-Benc.content:
MalURLBench: A Benchmark Evaluating Agents’ Vulnerabilities When Processing Web URLs
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #13 (score: 5.52, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM
Code: github.com/JiangYingEr/MalURLBench
Abstract
LLM-based web agents have become increasingly popular for their utility in daily life and work. However, they exhibit critical vulnerabilities when processing malicious URLs: accepting a disguised malicious URL enables subsequent access to unsafe webpages, which can cause severe damage to service providers and users. Despite this risk, no benchmark currently targets this emerging threat. To address this gap, we propose MalURLBench, the first benchmark for evaluating LLMs’ vulnerabilities to malicious URLs. MalURLBench contains 61,845 attack instances spanning 10 real-world scenarios and 7 categories of real malicious websites. Experiments with 12 popular LLMs reveal that existing models struggle to detect elaborately disguised malicious URLs. We further identify and analyze key factors that impact attack success rates and propose URLGuard, a lightweight defense module. We believe this work will provide a foundational resource for advancing the security of web agents. Our code is available at github.com/JiangYingEr/MalURLBench.
Key Techniques

literature/2026-01-27-Pisets-A-Robust-Sp.filePath: literature/2026-01-27 Pisets A Robust Speech Recognition System for Lectures and I.md
literature/2026-01-27-Pisets-A-Robust-Sp.title: Pisets: A Robust Speech Recognition System for Lectures and Interviews
literature/2026-01-27-Pisets-A-Robust-Sp.content:
Pisets: A Robust Speech Recognition System for Lectures and Interviews
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #3 (score: 6.07, max_sim: 0.76)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, RAG, Transformer
Code: github.com/bond005/pisets
Abstract
This work presents a speech-to-text system “Pisets” for scientists and journalists which is based on a three-component architecture aimed at improving speech recognition accuracy while minimizing errors and hallucinations associated with the Whisper model. The architecture comprises primary recognition using Wav2Vec2, false positive filtering via the Audio Spectrogram Transformer (AST), and final speech recognition through Whisper. The implementation of curriculum learning methods and the utilization of diverse Russian-language speech corpora significantly enhanced the system’s effectiveness. Additionally, advanced uncertainty modeling techniques were introduced, contributing to further improvements in transcription quality. The proposed approaches ensure robust transcribing of long audio data across various acoustic conditions compared to WhisperX and the usual Whisper model. The source code of “Pisets” system is publicly available at GitHub: github.com/bond005/pisets.
Key Techniques

Wav2Vec2 primary recognition
Audio Spectrogram Transformer filtering
curriculum learning with Russian-language corpora

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityUnheard in the Digital Age: Rethinking AI Bias and Speech Diversity0.25The Equalizer: Introducing Shape-Gain Decomposition in Neural Audio Codecs0.21End-to-End Joint ASR and Speaker Role Diarization with Child-Adult Interactions0.14Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.13Temp-R1: A Unified Autonomous Agent for Complex Temporal KGQA via Reverse Curriculum Reinforcement Learning0.12
Analysis: Pisets and Its Relationship to Prior Work
The Pisets system represents a practical application of ensemble and filtering techniques that directly addresses a central concern in the LLM compression and robustness literature—reducing hallucinations and errors in neural models. While the “Compressing LLMs” note focuses on model size reduction through quantization and distillation, Pisets takes a complementary approach by maintaining multiple specialized models (Wav2Vec2, AST, Whisper) rather than compressing a single model. However, both approaches share the underlying principle that redundancy in neural systems can be exploited: compression literature shows that models contain redundant parameters that can be pruned or quantized, while Pisets demonstrates that redundant processing pathways (multiple recognition stages) can filter out false positives. This represents an architectural trade-off—trading computational overhead for accuracy rather than accepting the accuracy loss inherent in aggressive compression.
The connection to the “Retrieval-Augmented Generation” survey is particularly illuminating. Just as RAG addresses hallucinations in LLMs by integrating external, non-parametric retrieval systems, Pisets combats Whisper’s hallucination tendencies through a multi-stage filtering pipeline where the Audio Spectrogram Transformer acts as a validation layer. The RAG framework identifies hallucination, factual inconsistency, and poor domain adaptation as key drawbacks of parametric-only models—precisely the problems Pisets targets in speech recognition. By introducing curriculum learning and diverse Russian-language corpora (domain-specific training data), Pisets implements a form of domain adaptation similar to RAG’s approach of grounding outputs in external knowledge, except applied to the speech modality rather than text generation.
The Transformer tree architecture survey reveals how specialized encoder-only models like BERT have driven advances in natural language understanding through focused pre-training objectives. Pisets mirrors this specialization strategy: rather than relying on Whisper alone, it employs Wav2Vec2 (a speech-specific encoder) for primary recognition and AST (an audio-specialized transformer) for validation. This ensemble of task-specialized models echoes the principle demonstrated by models like DistilBERT, which achieves both efficiency and performance retention through architectural innovation. Pisets thus contributes a novel perspective to the knowledge base: that for robust end-to-end speech recognition, particularly in challenging domains like lectures and interviews, architectural diversity and staged filtering may be more effective than pursuing a single, increasingly complex unified model. The system’s public availability also emphasizes the importance of reproducibility in addressing model robustness—a practical commitment that bridges the theoretical robustness concerns outlined in the RAG survey.

literature/2026-01-27-Real-Time-Trend-Pr.filePath: literature/2026-01-27 Real-Time Trend Prediction via Continually-Aligned LLM Query.md
literature/2026-01-27-Real-Time-Trend-Pr.title: Real-Time Trend Prediction via Continually-Aligned LLM Query Generation
literature/2026-01-27-Real-Time-Trend-Pr.content:
Real-Time Trend Prediction via Continually-Aligned LLM Query Generation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-24
Rank: #15 (score: 5.41, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RecSys, LLM, RAG
Abstract
Trending news detection in low-traffic search environments faces a fundamental cold-start problem, where a lack of query volume prevents systems from identifying emerging or long-tail trends. Existing methods relying on keyword frequency or query spikes are inherently slow and ineffective in these sparse settings, lagging behind real-world shifts in attention. We introduce RTTP, a novel Real-Time Trending Prediction framework that generates search queries directly from news content instead of waiting for users to issue them. RTTP leverages a continual learning LLM (CL-LLM) that converts posts into search-style queries and scores them using engagement strength + creator authority, enabling early trend surfacing before search volume forms. To ensure adaptation without degrading reasoning, we propose Mix-Policy DPO, a new preference-based continual learning approach that combines on-policy stability with off-policy novelty to mitigate catastrophic forgetting during model upgrades. Deployed at production scale on Facebook and Meta AI products, RTTP delivers +91.4% improvement in tail-trend detection precision@500 and +19% query generation accuracy over industry baselines, while sustaining stable performance after multi-week online training. This work demonstrates that LLM-generated synthetic search signals, when aligned and continually updated, unlock timely trend understanding in low-traffic search environments.
Key Techniques

Continual learning LLM query generation
Mix-Policy DPO
Engagement strength + creator authority scoring

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySelf-Distillation Enables Continual Learning0.26Semantic Search At LinkedIn0.19Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.19Rich-Media Re-Ranker: A User Satisfaction-Driven LLM Re-ranking Framework for Rich-Media Search0.17A Picture of Agentic Search0.17
Analysis: RTTP’s Innovations Within the Broader LLM-for-Search Landscape
Bridging Synthetic Signal Generation and LLM-Based Ranking
The RTTP framework advances beyond traditional LLM ranking approaches documented in “Consolidating Ranking and Relevance Predictions of Large Language Models” by inverting the signal generation pipeline. Rather than using LLMs as pseudo-raters or rankers to evaluate existing user queries (as in the matched notes’ pseudo-rater and pairwise ranking paradigms), RTTP generates synthetic search queries from news content itself. This represents a fundamental shift: LLMs move from being evaluators of relevance to being generative sources of search demand signals. Where prior work applies LLMs to score query-document relevance, RTTP uses LLMs to hallucinate the queries that users would eventually search for, bootstrapping signal in cold-start environments where traditional frequency-based methods fail. The engagement strength + creator authority scoring mechanism then replaces the pseudo-rater’s human-like relevance judgment with authority-weighted synthetic demand, creating a fundamentally different optimization target.
Continual Learning as a Bridge to Persistent LLM Adaptation
RTTP’s Mix-Policy DPO contribution directly addresses a gap in existing literature on LLM adaptation for information retrieval. While “RecPrompt” demonstrates self-tuning for news recommendation through iterative prompt refinement and “Time-Aware Query Processing and Retrieval” handles dynamic constraint evolution, neither addresses the specific challenge of continual learning without catastrophic forgetting in production LLM systems. Mix-Policy DPO—combining on-policy stability with off-policy novelty during preference-based learning—extends continual learning theory into the domain of LLM-based query generation. This is particularly significant because it acknowledges a tradeoff absent from the matched notes: how to upgrade models with new trends while preserving reasoning stability across multi-week deployment cycles. The paper implicitly challenges the assumption in prior work that static or manually-tuned LLM systems suffice for information retrieval, introducing instead the concept of continuously-aligned LLMs that co-evolve with content distributions.
Reframing “Search” as a Derived Signal Rather Than Primary Input
Conceptually, RTTP inverts the dependency structure assumed across all matched notes. RecPrompt, ranking consolidation work, and time-aware retrieval all treat search queries as given inputs to which systems adapt. RTTP treats search queries as outputs to be synthesized, fundamentally restructuring how trends surface in low-traffic environments. This perspective addition suggests that for sparse or emerging phenomena, LLM-generated synthetic queries may be more timely than waiting for aggregated user behavior—a claim that challenges the implicit assumption that query frequency is a reliable demand signal. The +91.4% improvement in tail-trend detection precision@500 over baselines validates this inversion: by generating queries before users issue them, RTTP achieves earlier trend capture than systems relying on passive signal collection. This frames a new frontier for information retrieval: proactive rather than reactive search signal generation.

literature/2026-01-27-Shortcut-Learning-.filePath: literature/2026-01-27 Shortcut Learning in Binary Classifier Black Boxes Applicati.md
literature/2026-01-27-Shortcut-Learning-.title: Shortcut Learning in Binary Classifier Black Boxes: Applications to Voice Anti-Spoofing and Biometrics
literature/2026-01-27-Shortcut-Learning-.content:
Shortcut Learning in Binary Classifier Black Boxes: Applications to Voice Anti-Spoofing and Biometrics
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-25
Rank: #12 (score: 5.58, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings
Abstract
The widespread adoption of deep-learning models in data-driven applications has drawn attention to the potential risks associated with biased datasets and models. Neglected or hidden biases within datasets and models can lead to unexpected results. This study addresses the challenges of dataset bias and explores shortcut learning'' or Clever Hans effect” in binary classifiers. We propose a novel framework for analyzing the black-box classifiers and for examining the impact of both training and test data on classifier scores. Our framework incorporates intervention and observational perspectives, employing a linear mixed-effects model for post-hoc analysis. By evaluating classifier performance beyond error rates, we aim to provide insights into biased datasets and offer a comprehensive understanding of their influence on classifier behavior. The effectiveness of our approach is demonstrated through experiments on audio anti-spoofing and speaker verification tasks using both statistical models and deep neural networks. The insights gained from this study have broader implications for tackling biases in other domains and advancing the field of explainable artificial intelligence.
Key Techniques

Black-box classifier analysis framework
Linear mixed-effects model for post-hoc analysis
Intervention and observational perspectives

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMitigating Bias in Automated Grading Systems for ESL Learners: A Contrastive Learning Approach0.15BitDance: Scaling Autoregressive Generative Models with Binary Tokens0.12Unheard in the Digital Age: Rethinking AI Bias and Speech Diversity0.11PretrainRL: Alleviating Factuality Hallucination of Large Language Models at the Beginning0.10Reasoning-Enhanced Rare-Event Prediction with Balanced Outcome Correction0.10
Analysis
This paper on shortcut learning in binary classifiers addresses a complementary challenge to the concerns raised in Disentangled Representation Learning (DRL). While DRL focuses on learning interpretable, separable representations of underlying factors of variation, this work tackles the inverse problem: understanding why models fail to learn these proper disentangled representations and instead exploit spurious correlations or “shortcuts” in the data. The paper’s framework for analyzing black-box classifier bias aligns with DRL’s goal of achieving interpretable and generalizable models, but shifts the lens toward post-hoc diagnosis rather than architectural solutions. By using linear mixed-effects models to decompose the contributions of training and test data on classifier scores, the authors provide an observational methodology for detecting when models have learned confounded shortcuts rather than robust features—a critical concern that DRL seeks to prevent through disentanglement from the outset.
The connection to Cross-Dataset Fine-Tuning and Evaluation for Negation Generalization reveals a methodological parallel in addressing generalization failures. Just as that work exposes overfitting to single-source data by evaluating across different datasets, this paper’s intervention-observational framework similarly exposes how classifiers rely on dataset-specific biases rather than learning generalizable patterns. Both approaches recognize that inflated performance metrics on benchmark data can mask brittle solutions; this paper extends that insight by proposing explicit analytical tools to decompose where biases originate (training data characteristics vs. test data characteristics). The audio anti-spoofing and speaker verification experiments serve as concrete instantiations of this cross-dataset generalization problem in the voice domain.
The paper also contributes a new diagnostic perspective to Negative Sampling for Contrastive Representation Learning, particularly regarding how models distinguish between genuine signals and spurious correlations. While contrastive learning aims to improve representations through careful negative sampling, this work highlights that classifiers can perversely exploit the structure of their training data and negative examples to learn shortcuts rather than robust discriminative features. The framework’s ability to isolate which factors in the dataset drive classifier decisions provides actionable insights for how practitioners might design better negative samples or identify problematic data distributions before model deployment—representing a preventative approach to the shortcut learning problem that complements contrastive learning’s representational strategies.

literature/2026-01-27-Smooth-Sparse-and-.filePath: literature/2026-01-27 Smooth Sparse and Stable Finite-Time Exact Skeleton Recovery.md
literature/2026-01-27-Smooth-Sparse-and-.title: Smooth, Sparse, and Stable: Finite-Time Exact Skeleton Recovery via Smoothed Proximal Gradients
literature/2026-01-27-Smooth-Sparse-and-.content:
Smooth, Sparse, and Stable: Finite-Time Exact Skeleton Recovery via Smoothed Proximal Gradients
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #9 (score: 5.87, max_sim: 0.76)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Graph
Abstract
Continuous optimization has significantly advanced causal discovery, yet existing methods (e.g., NOTEARS) generally guarantee only asymptotic convergence to a stationary point. This often yields dense weighted matrices that require arbitrary post-hoc thresholding to recover a DAG. This gap between continuous optimization and discrete graph structures remains a fundamental challenge. In this paper, we bridge this gap by proposing the Hybrid-Order Acyclicity Constraint (AHOC) and optimizing it via the Smoothed Proximal Gradient (SPG-AHOC). Leveraging the Manifold Identification Property of proximal algorithms, we provide a rigorous theoretical guarantee: the Finite-Time Oracle Property. We prove that under standard identifiability assumptions, SPG-AHOC recovers the exact DAG support (structure) in finite iterations, even when optimizing a smoothed approximation. This result eliminates structural ambiguity, as our algorithm returns graphs with exact zero entries without heuristic truncation. Empirically, SPG-AHOC achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and strongly corroborates the finite-time identification theory.
Key Techniques

Hybrid-Order Acyclicity Constraint (AHOC)
Smoothed Proximal Gradient (SPG-AHOC)
Manifold Identification Property

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBeyond Message Passing: A Symbolic Alternative for Expressive and Interpretable Graph Learning0.11Diffusion Generative Recommendation with Continuous Tokens0.10Conditional Flow Matching for Continuous Anomaly Detection in Autonomous Driving on a Manifold-Aware Spectral Space0.10Algorithmic Prompt-Augmentation for Efficient LLM-Based Heuristic Design for A* Search0.10OAT: Ordered Action Tokenization0.10
Analysis
The SPG-AHOC paper addresses a fundamental tension in optimization-based causal discovery: the gap between continuous relaxations and discrete graph structures. This challenge parallels problems tackled in Soft-Mask Graph Substructure Extraction, which also grapples with extracting discrete structural information (sparse subgraphs) from continuous, differentiable mechanisms. Both papers leverage continuous optimization to discover underlying discrete structures, though in different domains—SPG-AHOC targets causal DAG recovery while soft-mask methods target task-relevant subgraph identification. The key methodological parallel is the use of continuous relaxations (smoothed approximations in SPG-AHOC, soft masks in [0,1] in substructure extraction) to enable gradient-based learning while preserving the ability to identify exact discrete structures. However, SPG-AHOC goes further by providing finite-time guarantees that the algorithm recovers exact zeros in the adjacency matrix, whereas soft-mask methods typically require post-hoc extraction thresholds.
The Sparse Matrix Format Selection note, while superficially distant, touches on a related computational principle: the importance of structural properties (sparsity patterns) in determining algorithmic efficiency. Just as sparse matrix formats must be chosen to harmonize with hardware execution models, SPG-AHOC’s theoretical contribution—the Finite-Time Oracle Property—harmonizes the structure of the optimization problem (acyclicity constraints via AHOC) with the algorithmic solver (smoothed proximal gradients) to guarantee exact structural recovery. Both works recognize that how you represent and optimize a problem fundamentally constrains what you can guarantee about the solution. SPG-AHOC’s elimination of post-hoc thresholding mirrors the principle that choosing the right representation (sparse format) avoids downstream complications.
The matched notes do not directly address causal inference or the NOTEARS-style methods that SPG-AHOC improves upon. The novel contribution here is the Manifold Identification Property applied to causal discovery—a rigorous framework proving that optimization on a smoothed manifold can still identify the exact combinatorial structure (the DAG skeleton) in finite time. This is absent from the vault notes and represents a specific theoretical innovation: bridging continuous optimization theory with discrete structural recovery in the causal discovery setting.

literature/2026-01-27-Unheard-in-the-Dig.title: Unheard in the Digital Age: Rethinking AI Bias and Speech Diversity
literature/2026-01-27-Unheard-in-the-Dig.content:
Unheard in the Digital Age: Rethinking AI Bias and Speech Diversity
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #14 (score: 5.33, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM
Abstract
Speech remains one of the most visible yet overlooked vectors of inclusion and exclusion in contemporary society. While fluency is often equated with credibility and competence, individuals with atypical speech patterns are routinely marginalized. Given the current state of the debate, this article focuses on the structural biases that shape perceptions of atypical speech and are now being encoded into artificial intelligence. Automated speech recognition (ASR) systems and voice interfaces, trained predominantly on standardized speech, routinely fail to recognize or respond to diverse voices, compounding digital exclusion. As AI technologies increasingly mediate access to opportunity, the study calls for inclusive technological design, anti-bias training to minimize the impact of discriminatory algorithmic decisions, and enforceable policy reform that explicitly recognize speech diversity as a matter of equity, not merely accessibility. Drawing on interdisciplinary research, the article advocates for a cultural and institutional shift in how we value voice, urging co-created solutions that elevate the rights, representation, and realities of atypical speakers in the digital age. Ultimately, the article reframes speech inclusion as a matter of equity (not accommodation) and advocates for co-created AI systems that reflect the full spectrum of human voices.
Key Techniques

Inclusive technological design
anti-bias training
enforceable policy reform

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPisets: A Robust Speech Recognition System for Lectures and Interviews0.25Dissecting Subjectivity and the”Ground Truth”Illusion in Data Annotation0.13The Equalizer: Introducing Shape-Gain Decomposition in Neural Audio Codecs0.12Artificial Rigidities vs. Biological Noise: A Comparative Analysis of Multisensory Integration in AV-HuBERT and Human Observers0.12Shortcut Learning in Binary Classifier Black Boxes: Applications to Voice Anti-Spoofing and Biometrics0.11
Analysis: “Unheard in the Digital Age” and Your Knowledge Base
Extending the Bitter Lesson through Bias Recognition: This paper fundamentally challenges one of the central premises in “The Bitter Lesson” by showing that raw computational power and scale alone don’t guarantee inclusive AI systems. While the Bitter Lesson emphasizes that search and learning beat human-designed knowledge, this speech diversity paper reveals that scale-dependent training (on “standardized speech”) actually amplifies existing biases rather than eliminating them. The paper extends this insight by arguing that technical optimization isn’t sufficient—we need conscious intervention in what data we optimize over and explicit design decisions that value diverse training corpora.
Bridging AI Psychology with Equity Frameworks: The connection to “AI psychology and Evaluating cognitive ability in large language model” is particularly strong here. While that note likely examines how LLMs model or fail to model human cognition, this paper argues that ASR and voice systems encode not just cognitive biases but social and institutional biases rooted in historical marginalization. The paper reframes “atypical speech” not as a cognitive or technical problem requiring individual accommodation, but as a systems-level equity issue. This shifts the framing from “how do we make AI understand diverse voices?” to “whose voices did we choose to exclude in training, and why?” It bridges individual cognition assessment to structural power analysis.
Co-Creation as Collective Intelligence: The paper’s advocacy for “co-created solutions that elevate the rights, representation, and realities of atypical speakers” directly operationalizes principles likely discussed in your “Literature Review: Understanding Artificial Intelligence through Collective Intelligence.” Rather than treating voice diversity as a technical specification, the paper models inclusion as a deliberative, participatory process where affected communities shape system design. This connects to “I Learned a System for Speaking Articulately”—moving beyond individual articulation toward recognizing that speech value is culturally constructed and enforced through systems, making collective redesign of those systems essential to genuine equity.

literature/2026-01-27-textscNaVIDA-Visio.filePath: literature/2026-01-27 textscNaVIDA Vision-Language Navigation with Inverse Dynamic.md
literature/2026-01-27-textscNaVIDA-Visio.title: extsc{NaVIDA}: Vision-Language Navigation with Inverse Dynamics Augmentation
literature/2026-01-27-textscNaVIDA-Visio.content:
\textsc{NaVIDA}: Vision-Language Navigation with Inverse Dynamics Augmentation
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #9 (score: 5.48, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires agents to interpret natural language instructions and act coherently in visually rich environments. However, most existing methods rely on reactive state-action mappings without explicitly modeling how actions causally transform subsequent visual observations. Lacking such vision-action causality, agents cannot anticipate the visual changes induced by its own actions, leading to unstable behaviors, weak generalization, and cumulative error along trajectory. To address these issues, we introduce \textsc{NaVIDA} (\textbf{Nav}igation with \textbf{I}nverse \textbf{D}ynamics \textbf{A}ugmentation), a unified VLN framework that couples policy learning with action-grounded visual dynamics and adaptive execution. \textsc{NaVIDA} augments training with chunk-based inverse-dynamics supervision to learn causal relationship between visual changes and corresponding actions. To structure this supervision and extend the effective planning range, \textsc{NaVIDA} employs hierarchical probabilistic action chunking (HPAC), which organizes trajectories into multi-step chunks and provides discriminative, longer-range visual-change cues. To further curb error accumulation and stabilize behavior at inference, an entropy-guided mechanism adaptively sets the execution horizon of action chunks. Extensive experiments show that \textsc{NaVIDA} achieves superior navigation performance compared to state-of-the-art methods with fewer parameters (3B vs. 8B). Real-world robot evaluations further validate the practical feasibility and effectiveness of our approach. Code and data will be available upon acceptance.
Key Techniques

Inverse dynamics supervision
Hierarchical probabilistic action chunking (HPAC)
Entropy-guided adaptive execution

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityYoutu-VL: Unleashing Visual Potential via Unified Vision-Language Supervision0.28Do MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.27OAT: Ordered Action Tokenization0.23Thinking with Drafting: Optical Decompression via Logical Reconstruction0.21AugVLA-3D: Depth-Driven Feature Augmentation for Vision-Language-Action Models0.20
Analysis of NaVIDA’s Connections to Existing Knowledge
Extending Inverse Dynamics and Causal Learning: NaVIDA directly addresses a core weakness that connects to both Learning Strategies and the Agent Symbolic Learning Framework—the gap between reactive policies and causal understanding. Where traditional VLN agents learn state-action mappings reactively, NaVIDA introduces chunk-based inverse-dynamics supervision to explicitly model how actions transform visual observations. This extends the principle of symbolic learning by grounding it in vision-action causality: the agent learns not just what to do, but why actions produce specific visual changes. The hierarchical probabilistic action chunking (HPAC) further enriches this by organizing trajectories into multi-step chunks, enabling the agent to reason about longer-range causal sequences—a technique that bridges reactive execution with the kind of deliberative planning central to symbolic agents.
Integration with Policy Optimization and Reinforcement Learning: The paper’s architecture naturally complements Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) frameworks. Rather than applying PPO to a black-box policy that treats observations as inputs and actions as outputs, NaVIDA augments the learning signal with structured inverse-dynamics supervision during training. This is conceptually aligned with how Reflexion (Verbal Reinforcement Learning) uses additional supervision signals to guide learning—here, the “supervision” comes from visual dynamics rather than verbal feedback. The entropy-guided mechanism for adaptive execution horizons echoes reward-shaping principles used in PPO variants, optimizing not just action selection but also the temporal scope of action commitment. The paper’s efficiency gains (3B vs. 8B parameters while matching performance) suggest that this causal grounding provides a more sample-efficient learning signal than purely reactive approaches.
Multimodal Architecture and Unified Frameworks: NaVIDA’s approach aligns with the emerging paradigm discussed in Luke Zettlemoyer’s talk on Unified Multimodal Architectures—the integration of vision and language through a coherent framework. Where Zettlemoyer’s work emphasizes unified architectural principles, NaVIDA demonstrates a practical instantiation by making vision-language understanding causally grounded. The inverse-dynamics augmentation acts as a unifying principle that ties together language interpretation (understanding instructions), visual perception (observing environments), and action execution (moving through space) through explicit action-outcome relationships. This extends the unified architecture concept beyond simple multi-input processing to semantic coherence: all modalities are related through their causal effects on visual state transitions.

literature/2026-01-28-A-Generative-AI-Dr.filePath: literature/2026-01-28 A Generative AI-Driven Reliability Layer for Action-Oriented.md
literature/2026-01-28-A-Generative-AI-Dr.title: A Generative AI-Driven Reliability Layer for Action-Oriented Disaster Resilience
literature/2026-01-28-A-Generative-AI-Dr.content:
A Generative AI-Driven Reliability Layer for Action-Oriented Disaster Resilience
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #1 (score: 1.49, max_sim: 0.73)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, RecSys
Abstract
As climate-related hazards intensify, conventional early warning systems (EWS) disseminate alerts rapidly but often fail to trigger timely protective actions, leading to preventable losses and inequities. We introduce Climate RADAR (Risk-Aware, Dynamic, and Action Recommendation system), a generative AI-based reliability layer that reframes disaster communication from alerts delivered to actions executed. It integrates meteorological, hydrological, vulnerability, and social data into a composite risk index and employs guardrail-embedded large language models (LLMs) to deliver personalized recommendations across citizen, volunteer, and municipal interfaces. Evaluation through simulations, user studies, and a municipal pilot shows improved outcomes, including higher protective action execution, reduced response latency, and increased usability and trust. By combining predictive analytics, behavioral science, and responsible AI, Climate RADAR advances people-centered, transparent, and equitable early warning systems, offering practical pathways toward compliance-ready disaster resilience infrastructures.
Key Techniques

Composite risk index integration
guardrail-embedded large language models
personalized multi-interface recommendations

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySTATe-of-Thoughts: Structured Action Templates for Tree-of-Thoughts0.14	extsc{NaVIDA}: Vision-Language Navigation with Inverse Dynamics Augmentation0.13OAT: Ordered Action Tokenization0.12Temp-R1: A Unified Autonomous Agent for Complex Temporal KGQA via Reverse Curriculum Reinforcement Learning0.12Constrained Process Maps for Multi-Agent Generative AI Workflows0.12
Analysis: Climate RADAR and Its Theoretical Foundations
Climate RADAR’s architecture fundamentally reframes the early warning problem through a reinforcement of human agency rather than passive alert reception. This directly engages with concepts from your Reinforcement learning based recommender system note, particularly in moving from “one-shot classification/prediction to sequential decision modeling.” Where traditional EWS treat warnings as discrete events, Climate RADAR treats protective actions as sequential decisions requiring personalized recommendation—users progress through stages (awareness → evaluation → action execution), mirroring the Markov decision process framing that RL-based systems employ. However, this paper inverts the typical RL paradigm: rather than learning user preferences through interaction, it combines predictive analytics (meteorological/hydrological data) with behavioral science to proactively scaffold decisions, using LLMs as a reasoning layer that translates composite risk indices into actionable recommendations. This is less about traditional collaborative filtering and more about bounded rationality—recognizing that users facing crisis information suffer from cognitive overload and need decision scaffolding.
The integration of “guardrail-embedded LLMs” introduces a crucial safety layer absent from the RL-recommender discussion. This suggests the paper is grappling with responsible AI and preventing harmful escalation—concerns implicit in your Escalation note about “mutually reinforcing feedback loops between competing actors.” In disaster contexts, without guardrails, an AI system might generate self-fulfilling prophecies (e.g., recommending evacuation to some but not others could trigger panic cascades) or optimize for system efficiency at the cost of equity. Climate RADAR’s emphasis on “transparent and equitable” recommendations positions guardrails not just as safety mechanisms but as fairness interventions.
The novel contribution here is the concept of a “reliability layer”—treating AI not as a replacement for EWS but as middleware that bridges the gap between risk detection and behavioral action. This extends beyond the Message-as-a-Model paradigm’s vision of embedding intelligence in messages; instead, it embeds trust and clarity, ensuring that intelligent recommendations carry sufficient transparency and context-awareness that vulnerable populations (volunteers, citizens) can make informed decisions. Where previous work optimizes information flow or decision sequencing, Climate RADAR optimizes for action execution under uncertainty, a distinctly people-centered reframing of what algorithmic mediation should accomplish in critical infrastructure.

literature/2026-01-28-AI-Agent-for-Rever.title: AI Agent for Reverse-Engineering Legacy Finite-Difference Code and Translating to Devito
literature/2026-01-28-AI-Agent-for-Rever.content:
AI Agent for Reverse-Engineering Legacy Finite-Difference Code and Translating to Devito
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #8 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, RL, RAG, Graph, LLM
Abstract
To facilitate the transformation of legacy finite difference implementations into the Devito environment, this study develops an integrated AI agent framework. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and open-source Large Language Models are combined through multi-stage iterative workflows in the system’s hybrid LangGraph architecture. The agent constructs an extensive Devito knowledge graph through document parsing, structure-aware segmentation, extraction of entity relationships, and Leiden-based community detection. GraphRAG optimisation enhances query performance across semantic communities that include seismic wave simulation, computational fluid dynamics, and performance tuning libraries. A reverse engineering component derives three-level query strategies for RAG retrieval through static analysis of Fortran source code. To deliver precise contextual information for language model guidance, the multi-stage retrieval pipeline performs parallel searching, concept expansion, community-scale retrieval, and semantic similarity analysis. Code synthesis is governed by Pydantic-based constraints to guarantee structured outputs and reliability. A comprehensive validation framework integrates conventional static analysis with the G-Eval approach, covering execution correctness, structural soundness, mathematical consistency, and API compliance. The overall agent workflow is implemented on the LangGraph framework and adopts concurrent processing to support quality-based iterative refinement and state-aware dynamic routing. The principal contribution lies in the incorporation of feedback mechanisms motivated by reinforcement learning, enabling a transition from static code translation toward dynamic and adaptive analytical behavior.
Key Techniques

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
GraphRAG optimisation
Pydantic-based constraints
G-Eval validation framework

literature/2026-01-28-Algorithmic-Prompt.filePath: literature/2026-01-28 Algorithmic Prompt-Augmentation for Efficient LLM-Based Heur.md
literature/2026-01-28-Algorithmic-Prompt.title: Algorithmic Prompt-Augmentation for Efficient LLM-Based Heuristic Design for A* Search
literature/2026-01-28-Algorithmic-Prompt.content:
Algorithmic Prompt-Augmentation for Efficient LLM-Based Heuristic Design for A* Search
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #7 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Search, RAG, LLM
Abstract
Heuristic functions are essential to the performance of tree search algorithms such as A*, where their accuracy and efficiency directly impact search outcomes. Traditionally, such heuristics are handcrafted, requiring significant expertise. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) and evolutionary frameworks have opened the door to automating heuristic design. In this paper, we extend the Evolution of Heuristics (EoH) framework to investigate the automated generation of guiding heuristics for A* search. We introduce a novel domain-agnostic prompt augmentation strategy that includes the A* code into the prompt to leverage in-context learning, named Algorithmic - Contextual EoH (A-CEoH). To evaluate the effectiveness of A-CeoH, we study two problem domains: the Unit-Load Pre-Marshalling Problem (UPMP), a niche problem from warehouse logistics, and the classical sliding puzzle problem (SPP). Our computational experiments show that A-CEoH can significantly improve the quality of the generated heuristics and even outperform expert-designed heuristics.
Key Techniques

Evolution of Heuristics (EoH) framework
prompt augmentation strategy with A* code inclusion
in-context learning for heuristic generation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLearning to Reason via Program Generation, Emulation, and Search0.15Asymmetric Prompt Weighting for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards0.14Algorithmic Simplification of Neural Networks with Mosaic-of-Motifs0.14CoDa: Constrained Generation based Data Augmentation for Low-Resource NLP0.14MergeMix: Optimizing Mid-Training Data Mixtures via Learnable Model Merging0.14
Analysis: Algorithmic Prompt-Augmentation for A* Heuristic Design
Extending LLM-as-Optimizer Frameworks
This paper directly extends the paradigm established in “Large Language Models as Optimizers,” which demonstrated that LLMs can act as derivative-free optimizers through natural language prompting. Where OPRO applies this concept to mathematical optimization problems like linear regression and TSP, A-CEoH applies it to the specialized domain of search heuristic design. The key innovation—incorporating the A* algorithm code directly into the prompt as “contextual knowledge”—represents a domain-specific refinement of OPRO’s in-context learning approach. Rather than having the LLM optimize prompts in isolation, A-CEoH grounds the optimization process in the algorithmic context of the actual search mechanism being optimized, leveraging the LLM’s ability to understand and reason about code structures.
Algorithmic Contextualization as Prompt Evolution Strategy
The paper’s prompt augmentation strategy aligns with the “Reflective Prompt Evolution” concepts discussed in GEPA Explained, where iterative refinement and evolution of prompts themselves become the optimization target. A-CEoH implements this through the Evolution of Heuristics framework, systematically evolving heuristic functions rather than static prompts. However, it introduces a crucial innovation beyond standard APE: by embedding algorithmic context (the A* implementation), it enables what could be termed “algorithm-aware prompt optimization.” This bridges the gap between generic prompt optimization and domain-specific algorithm design—the LLM isn’t just generating better prompts or heuristics, but doing so with explicit understanding of the computational structure that will evaluate them.
Novel Contribution: Context-Grounded Evolutionary Optimization
Where prior work in Automatic Prompt Optimization focused on optimizing prompts for downstream NLP tasks, A-CEoH demonstrates that this approach can be applied to generate algorithmic components themselves. The domain-agnostic nature of the prompt augmentation strategy—validated on both warehouse logistics (UPMP) and classical puzzles (SPP)—suggests that providing algorithmic context is a generalizable technique for improving LLM-based optimization across search domains. The finding that A-CEoH can outperform expert-designed heuristics represents a significant extension of the OPRO thesis: not only can LLMs optimize through prompting, but they can do so to match or exceed human expertise in specialized algorithmic domains when given proper contextual grounding.

literature/2026-01-28-Beyond-Retention-O.filePath: literature/2026-01-28 Beyond Retention Orchestrating Structural Safety and Plastic.md
literature/2026-01-28-Beyond-Retention-O.title: Beyond Retention: Orchestrating Structural Safety and Plasticity in Continual Learning for LLMs
literature/2026-01-28-Beyond-Retention-O.content:
Beyond Retention: Orchestrating Structural Safety and Plasticity in Continual Learning for LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RecSys, RAG, LLM
Abstract
Continual learning in Large Language Models (LLMs) faces the critical challenge of balancing stability (retaining old knowledge) and plasticity (learning new tasks). While Experience Replay (ER) is a standard countermeasure against catastrophic forgetting, its impact across diverse capabilities remains underexplored. In this work, we uncover a critical dichotomy in ER’s behavior: while it induces positive backward transfer on robust, unstructured tasks (e.g., boosting performance on previous NLP classification tasks through repeated rehearsal), it causes severe negative transfer on fragile, structured domains like code generation (e.g., a significant relative drop in coding accuracy). This reveals that ER trades structural integrity for broad consolidation. To address this dilemma, we propose \textbf{Orthogonal Subspace Wake-up (OSW)}. OSW identifies essential parameter subspaces of previous tasks via a brief “wake-up” phase and enforces orthogonal updates for new tasks, providing a mathematically grounded “safety guarantee” for established knowledge structures. Empirical results across a diverse four-task sequence demonstrate that OSW uniquely succeeds in preserving fragile coding abilities where Replay fails, while simultaneously maintaining high plasticity for novel tasks. Our findings emphasize the necessity of evaluating structural safety alongside average retention in LLM continual learning.
Key Techniques

Experience Replay
Orthogonal Subspace Wake-up
Parameter Subspace Identification
Orthogonal Update Enforcement

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySelf-Distillation Enables Continual Learning0.23Real-Time Trend Prediction via Continually-Aligned LLM Query Generation0.17Quantized Evolution Strategies: High-precision Fine-tuning of Quantized LLMs at Low-precision Cost0.17Domain-Incremental Continual Learning for Robust and Efficient Keyword Spotting in Resource Constrained Systems0.16The Side Effects of Being Smart: Safety Risks in MLLMs’ Multi-Image Reasoning0.13
Connection to Prior Continual Learning Work
The OSW paper directly addresses a limitation acknowledged but not deeply explored in the “Continual Learning for Sequential Models” note: the non-uniform impact of standard replay mechanisms across different task types. While that work focuses on integrating experience replay into sequential architectures, the OSW paper reveals a critical blind spot—that ER’s effectiveness depends heavily on task structure rather than just model architecture. The paper’s dichotomy between robust, unstructured tasks (where ER succeeds via consolidation) and fragile, structured domains like code generation (where ER causes negative transfer) challenges the assumption that replay alone provides universal forgetting mitigation. This is a crucial refinement: continual learning isn’t just about what you replay, but whether replay is semantically compatible with a task’s structural constraints.
The Safety-Plasticity Trade-off and Orthogonal Constraints
OSW introduces a mathematically grounded alternative to the broad consolidation approach of Experience Replay. Rather than relying on episodic memory sampling (as explored in MemoryBank’s use of forgetting curves to prioritize salient information), OSW takes a parameter-space approach: it explicitly maps the “essential subspaces” of previous tasks and enforces orthogonal updates for new learning. This bridges to the HippoRAG 2 concept of leveraging human memory principles, but inverts the strategy—instead of using associative retrieval to ground new knowledge in past context (as HippoRAG does through graph-based retrieval), OSW uses geometric constraints in parameter space to prevent interference. The “wake-up” phase parallels HippoRAG’s sense-making process, but operates at the weight level rather than the semantic level.
Novel Contribution: Structural Integrity as a First-Class Concern
The paper’s most significant addition to the knowledge base is reframing the stability-plasticity problem from a retention perspective to a structural safety perspective. Prior work (including the matched notes) treats catastrophic forgetting as a uniform threat to be mitigated through replay, regularization, or memory augmentation. OSW reveals that some knowledge domains require protection of internal structure itself—not just parameter values. For coding tasks, the learned relationships between concepts may be as important as the weights themselves, and naive replay can degrade these relationships. This shifts the evaluation paradigm: the paper argues for measuring “structural safety” alongside traditional accuracy metrics, and proposes orthogonality as a mechanism for separating task subspaces in a way that’s provably non-interfering. This is a departure from the implicit assumption in prior continual learning work that more plasticity and consolidation are always better—OSW shows that some domains demand guardrails, not just flexibility.

literature/2026-01-28-Beyond-the-Rabbit-.filePath: literature/2026-01-28 Beyond the Rabbit Hole Mapping the Relational Harms of QAnon.md
literature/2026-01-28-Beyond-the-Rabbit-.title: Beyond the Rabbit Hole: Mapping the Relational Harms of QAnon Radicalization
literature/2026-01-28-Beyond-the-Rabbit-.content:
Beyond the Rabbit Hole: Mapping the Relational Harms of QAnon Radicalization
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-25
Rank: #7 (score: 1.25, max_sim: 0.76)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search, RAG
Abstract
The rise of conspiracy theories has created far-reaching societal harm in the public discourse by eroding trust and fueling polarization. Beyond this public impact lies a deeply personal toll on the friends and families of conspiracy believers, a dimension often overlooked in large-scale computational research. This study fills this gap by systematically mapping radicalization journeys and quantifying the associated emotional toll inflicted on loved ones. We use the prominent case of QAnon as a case study, analyzing 12747 narratives from the r/QAnonCasualties support community through a novel mixed-methods approach. First, we use topic modeling (BERTopic) to map the radicalization trajectories, identifying key pre-existing conditions, triggers, and post-radicalization characteristics. From this, we apply an LDA-based graphical model to uncover six recurring archetypes of QAnon adherents, which we term “radicalization personas.” Finally, using LLM-assisted emotion detection and regression modeling, we link these personas to the specific emotional toll reported by narrators. Our findings reveal that these personas are not just descriptive; they are powerful predictors of the specific emotional harms experienced by narrators. Radicalization perceived as a deliberate ideological choice is associated with narrator anger and disgust, while those marked by personal and cognitive collapse are linked to fear and sadness. This work provides the first empirical framework for understanding radicalization as a relational phenomenon, offering a vital roadmap for researchers and practitioners to navigate its interpersonal fallout.
Key Techniques

Topic modeling (BERTopic)
LDA-based graphical modeling
LLM-assisted emotion detection

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityAI Co-Scientist for Knowledge Synthesis in Medical Contexts: A Proof of Concept0.11TGSBM: Transformer-Guided Stochastic Block Model for Link Prediction0.10Facial Emotion Recognition on FER-2013 using an EfficientNetB2-Based Approach0.09MeepleLM: A Virtual Playtester Simulating Diverse Subjective Experiences0.09Conformal Prediction Algorithms for Time Series Forecasting: Methods and Benchmark0.07
Extending the Spiral Framework
This paper operationalizes the theoretical spiral mechanisms discussed in “Why We Spiral” by mapping how conspiracy radicalization functions as a documented feedback loop with measurable emotional consequences. While Walton’s framework identifies the general psychological architecture of spirals—core questions about identity and belonging, construal patterns, and calcification—the QAnon study empirically demonstrates these dynamics in a specific high-stakes context. The six radicalization personas effectively represent different entry points into Walton’s spiral: some individuals spiral through identity-based appeals (“Who am I?”), others through belonging (“Do I belong to a community?”), and still others through existential questions that calcify into worldviews. The paper’s finding that personas rooted in “personal and cognitive collapse” trigger fear and sadness aligns with Walton’s observation that uncertain or challenging situations activate defensive psychological patterns. Crucially, the paper extends the theory by quantifying the relational toll—showing that these individual spirals have documented emotional impacts on social networks, which Walton’s framework doesn’t explicitly address.
Detecting Engineered Narratives Through Persona Mapping
The matched note on PSYOPs and engineered reality provides a complementary lens for interpreting the radicalization personas. Chase Hughes’ FATE model (Focus, Authority, Tribe, Emotion) maps manipulative communication tactics, while this paper’s persona-based approach identifies who becomes susceptible and why. The personas marked by ideological choice-based radicalization—associated with narrator anger and disgust—may represent individuals particularly responsive to the “Focus” and “Authority” components of the FATE model, while those experiencing cognitive collapse may be more vulnerable to “Tribe” and “Emotion” appeals. The paper’s LLM-assisted emotion detection methodology offers a scalable approach to identifying PSYOP impacts that Hughes emphasizes: by mapping specific emotional outcomes to radicalization patterns, the study reveals the emotional weaponization mechanisms that PSYOPs exploit. This bridges computational narrative analysis with behavioral psychology’s understanding of manipulation tactics.
New Methodological Contribution: Persona-Based Harm Quantification
The paper’s distinctive contribution is developing “radicalization personas” as a predictive framework for relational harm—a technique not directly addressed in the matched notes. By combining BERTopic for trajectory mapping, LDA graphical models for persona identification, and LLM-assisted emotion detection with regression analysis, the study creates a novel pipeline for connecting who people become (personas) to what emotional impacts they inflict on networks. This methodology extends the comment culture analysis from the third matched note by showing how online communities like r/QAnonCasualties generate structured data about harm that can be computationally analyzed. Where the comments paper examines platform affordances and community culture, this study demonstrates how support communities create a unique research corpus—narratives of radicalization from the perspective of affected loved ones—that enables harm quantification rather than just discourse analysis.

literature/2026-01-28-Breaking-Flat-A-Ge.filePath: literature/2026-01-28 Breaking Flat A Generalised Query Performance Prediction Eva.md
literature/2026-01-28-Breaking-Flat-A-Ge.title: Breaking Flat: A Generalised Query Performance Prediction Evaluation Framework
literature/2026-01-28-Breaking-Flat-A-Ge.content:
Breaking Flat: A Generalised Query Performance Prediction Evaluation Framework
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-24
Rank: #3 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Search, RAG, RecSys
Abstract
The traditional use-case of query performance prediction (QPP) is to identify which queries perform well and which perform poorly for a given ranking model. A more fine-grained and arguably more challenging extension of this task is to determine which ranking models are most effective for a given query. In this work, we generalize the QPP task and its evaluation into three settings: (i) SingleRanker MultiQuery (SRMQ-PP), corresponding to the standard use case; (ii) MultiRanker SingleQuery (MRSQ-PP), which evaluates a QPP model’s ability to select the most effective ranker for a query; and (iii) MultiRanker MultiQuery (MRMQ-PP), which considers predictions jointly across all query ranker pairs. Our results show that (a) the relative effectiveness of QPP models varies substantially across tasks (SRMQ-PP vs. MRSQ-PP), and (b) predicting the best ranker for a query is considerably more difficult than predicting the relative difficulty of queries for a given ranker.
Key Techniques

Query performance prediction for single ranker
Query performance prediction for multiple rankers
Ranking model selection for queries
Multi-task evaluation framework

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBreaking Flat: A Generalised Query Performance Prediction Evaluation Framework0.76Beyond Correlations: A Downstream Evaluation Framework for Query Performance Prediction0.30Improving Neural Retrieval with Attribution-Guided Query Rewriting0.18Real-Time Trend Prediction via Continually-Aligned LLM Query Generation0.16RecSys Substack Digest: 10 Papers on RAG, Retrieval, and RecSys0.15
Analysis
Extending Ranking Beyond Single-Stage Retrieval
The matched notes focus primarily on improving ranking within a fixed retrieval context—whether through selection-then-rank pipelines, reranking with query context, or stochastic diversification. “Breaking Flat” fundamentally challenges this single-ranker assumption by introducing MultiRanker scenarios (MRSQ-PP and MRMQ-PP). Where Selection-Then-Rank for Mathematical Retrieval assumes a dense retriever feeds into a fixed re-ranker, this paper asks a more foundational question: which ranking model should we use in the first place? This represents a shift from optimizing within a ranking architecture to selecting between ranking architectures—a meta-ranking problem that none of the matched notes address.
Prediction Complexity and the Ranker Selection Gap
A critical finding—that predicting the best ranker for a query (MRSQ-PP) is substantially harder than predicting query difficulty under a fixed ranker (SRMQ-PP)—reveals a blind spot in existing retrieval frameworks. The matched notes assume ranking models are interchangeable or that once selected, their performance remains stable across queries. This paper exposes that ranker effectiveness varies dramatically by query characteristics, suggesting that approaches like Reranking with Original Query Context may oversimplify by treating all queries similarly with respect to reranker selection. The paper implicitly argues that query-specific ranker adaptation—knowing when a BGE-style reranker is better than alternatives—requires new prediction signals beyond traditional QPP features.
New Directions in Query-Aware Model Selection
This work introduces a hierarchical prediction problem absent from prior notes: predicting not just document relevance but model suitability. This complements Stochastic Reranking’s focus on diversity by suggesting that even probabilistic ranking models benefit from upstream knowledge about which model family suits a given query. The MRMQ-PP setting—jointly predicting across all query-ranker pairs—hints at potential solutions: learning cross-domain features that characterize when retrieval/ranking architectures succeed or fail, potentially informed by query representation and ranker properties rather than document pools alone.

literature/2026-01-28-Closing-the-Modali.filePath: literature/2026-01-28 Closing the Modality Gap Aligns Group-Wise Semantics.md
literature/2026-01-28-Closing-the-Modali.title: Closing the Modality Gap Aligns Group-Wise Semantics
literature/2026-01-28-Closing-the-Modali.content:
Closing the Modality Gap Aligns Group-Wise Semantics
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #12 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, Embeddings
Abstract
In multimodal learning, CLIP has been recognized as the \textit{de facto} method for learning a shared latent space across multiple modalities, placing similar representations close to each other and moving them away from dissimilar ones. Although CLIP-based losses effectively align modalities at the semantic level, the resulting latent spaces often remain only partially shared, revealing a structural mismatch known as the modality gap. While the necessity of addressing this phenomenon remains debated, particularly given its limited impact on instance-wise tasks (e.g., retrieval), we prove that its influence is instead strongly pronounced in group-level tasks (e.g., clustering). To support this claim, we introduce a novel method designed to consistently reduce this discrepancy in two-modal settings, with a straightforward extension to the general n-modal case. Through our extensive evaluation, we demonstrate our novel insight: while reducing the gap provides only marginal or inconsistent improvements in traditional instance-wise tasks, it significantly enhances group-wise tasks. These findings may reshape our understanding of the modality gap, highlighting its key role in improving performance on tasks requiring semantic grouping.
Key Techniques

CLIP-based contrastive learning
modality gap reduction
instance-wise vs group-level task optimization

Related Litnotes

literature/2026-01-28-Conformal-Predicti.filePath: literature/2026-01-28 Conformal Prediction Algorithms for Time Series Forecasting.md
literature/2026-01-28-Conformal-Predicti.title: Conformal Prediction Algorithms for Time Series Forecasting: Methods and Benchmark
literature/2026-01-28-Conformal-Predicti.content:
Conformal Prediction Algorithms for Time Series Forecasting: Methods and Benchmark
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #5 (score: 1.35, max_sim: 0.75)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG
Abstract
Reliable uncertainty quantification is of critical importance in time series forecasting, yet traditional methods often rely on restrictive distributional assumptions. Conformal prediction (CP) has emerged as a promising distribution-free framework for generating prediction intervals with rigorous theoretical guarantees. However, applying CP to sequential data presents a primary challenge: the temporal dependencies inherent in time series fundamentally violate the core assumption of data exchangeability, upon which standard CP guarantees are built. This review critically examines the main categories of algorithmic solutions designed to address this conflict. We survey and benchmark methods that relax the exchangeability assumption, those that redefine the data unit to be a collection of independent time series, approaches that explicitly model the dynamics of the prediction residuals, and online learning algorithms that adapt to distribution shifts to maintain long-run coverage. By synthesizing these approaches, we highlight computational efficiency and practical performance on real-world data.
Key Techniques

Conformal prediction
Exchangeability relaxation
Residual dynamics modeling
Online learning with distribution shift adaptation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityFrom Passive Metric to Active Signal: The Evolving Role of Uncertainty Quantification in Large Language Models0.31Towards Reducible Uncertainty Modeling for Reliable Large Language Model Agents0.28Constrained Process Maps for Multi-Agent Generative AI Workflows0.23Closing the Loop: A Control-Theoretic Framework for Provably Stable Time Series Forecasting with LLMs0.20Dual-Prototype Disentanglement: A Context-Aware Enhancement Framework for Time Series Forecasting0.17
Bridging Distribution Shift and Uncertainty Quantification
The conformal prediction paper directly addresses a complementary problem to the test-time adaptation (TTA) survey by “Beyond Model Adaptation at Test Time.” While the TTA survey examines how models themselves can be adjusted during inference to handle distribution shifts, the CP paper tackles the equally critical challenge of quantifying uncertainty reliably when distribution shifts occur in sequential data. Both works recognize that real-world deployment requires handling non-stationary conditions—TTA focuses on model parameter/inference adjustments, while CP focuses on generating valid prediction intervals that maintain coverage guarantees despite distributional changes. The online learning algorithms discussed in the CP paper that “adapt to distribution shifts to maintain long-run coverage” represent a direct operational parallel to TTA’s adaptation mechanisms, but with explicit theoretical guarantees on uncertainty bounds rather than implicit adaptation through model adjustment.
Exchangeability as the Core Technical Bridge
The fundamental contribution of the conformal prediction paper—addressing the violation of exchangeability assumptions in sequential data—reveals a deeper structural insight absent from the matched notes. Where the TTA survey addresses adaptation strategies generically and “Uncertain⟨T⟩ Summary” treats uncertainty as a type system abstraction, the CP paper explicitly formalizes why standard uncertainty quantification fails for time series: the temporal dependencies inherent in sequential data violate the exchangeability condition required for rigorous CP guarantees. This represents a critical refinement to how uncertainty should be modeled in adaptive systems. The paper’s categorization of solutions—relaxing exchangeability, redefining data units as independent time series collections, modeling residual dynamics, and online adaptation—provides a structured taxonomy that could inform how TTA methods should be evaluated when applied to temporal problems.
Novel Contribution: Synthesis of Temporal Structure and Uncertainty
The conformal prediction paper introduces a perspective largely absent from the matched notes: the intersection of temporal modeling and distribution-free uncertainty quantification. While “Transformer Models for Time Series” demonstrates how architectural innovations can capture temporal dependencies through attention mechanisms, the CP paper asks a complementary question: given these temporal representations, how do we rigorously quantify prediction uncertainty without distributional assumptions? By explicitly modeling residual dynamics and employing online learning for adaptive coverage, the CP framework offers a principled way to pair modern time series architectures (like the transformers discussed in the matched notes) with theoretically grounded uncertainty bounds—addressing a practical gap in how practitioners should deploy time series models in safety-critical applications where both accuracy and reliable uncertainty estimates are essential.
