literature/2026-01-28-Superlinear-Multi-.content:
Superlinear Multi-Step Attention
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #11 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, Transformer, Search
Abstract
In this paper, we propose \textbf{Superlinear attention}, a fully trainable multi-step attention architecture that achieves subquadratic complexity for long sequences while preserving \textbf{random context access} (a.k.a.\ structural non-exclusion): no eligible token position is structurally excluded from being selected for attention. Superlinear attention reformulates standard causal self-attention as a multi-step search problem with N steps, yielding an overall complexity of O(L^{1+\frac{1}{N}}). To illustrate the architecture, we present a baseline N=2 implementation, which is algorithmically analogous to standard jump search. In this O(L^{3/2}) instantiation, the first step performs O(L^{3/2}) span-search to select relevant spans of the sequence, and the second step applies O(L^{3/2}) span-attention (standard attention restricted to the selected spans). In an upscaled O(L^{1.54}) configuration for robustness, we achieve an average decoding throughput of 114 tokens/sec at 1M context length and 80 tokens/sec at 10M context in our implementation on a modified 30B hybrid MoE model on a single B200 GPU. With limited training, we also obtain strong performance on the NIAH (Needle In A Haystack) task up to 256K context length, demonstrating that the routed span selection is learnable end-to-end. This paper emphasizes architectural formulation, scaling analysis, and systems feasibility, and presents initial validation; comprehensive quality evaluations across diverse long-context tasks are left to future work.
Key Techniques

Multi-step attention architecture
Span-search and span-attention
Jump search algorithm

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.41HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.41SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.39SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.39FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion0.39
Analysis
Extending Sparse Attention Architecture: Superlinear attention builds on the foundational concept of Block Sparse Attention by introducing a learnable, hierarchical selection mechanism rather than relying on fixed or heuristically-determined block masks. Where Block Sparse Attention partitions sequences into static blocks and applies hand-crafted or simple learned gating to select which blocks interact, Superlinear attention reformulates the problem as a multi-step search procedure: the first step performs span-search across the entire sequence (O(L^{3/2}) for N=2), then the second step applies refined attention only within selected spans. This shifts from static block patterns to dynamically routed span selection, preserving the efficiency gains of block-wise computation while enabling end-to-end training of the selection mechanism itself. The paper demonstrates this is actually learnable on NIAH tasks up to 256K context, suggesting that span routing learns meaningful semantic boundaries rather than relying on fixed windowing rules.
Bridging Learned Selection and Hardware Efficiency: The paper’s approach shares the dual goals of Native Sparse Attention (NSA)—combining compression and selection—but inverts the order of abstraction. NSA compresses token blocks into summaries, then selects relevant blocks for detailed attention. Superlinear attention instead performs coarse-grained span search first (selecting which regions deserve fine-grained processing), then applies standard attention within those regions. This parallels NSA’s two-stage pipeline but with explicit complexity analysis: by formulating attention as an N-step search problem yielding O(L^{1+1/N}) complexity, the paper provides a principled framework for trading off search granularity against computational cost. Unlike Fixed-pattern Sparse Attention, which locks sparsity patterns into the model architecture, Superlinear attention makes sparsity learnable, suggesting that optimal context routing can be discovered during training rather than predetermined.
Novel Contribution: Subquadratic Complexity with Random Context Access: The key innovation is preserving random context access—ensuring no eligible token is structurally excluded—while achieving subquadratic complexity. This directly challenges the implicit trade-off in prior sparse attention methods: fixed patterns preserve random access but sacrifice learning; learned selection often implies structural exclusions (tokens above a threshold are never attended to). Superlinear attention avoids this by treating sparse selection as a learned ranking/search problem where all positions remain theoretically reachable, but efficiently in practice. The 30B hybrid MoE results (114 tokens/sec at 1M context on a B200 GPU) demonstrate systems-level feasibility, suggesting that the theoretical complexity improvements translate to real throughput gains—a gap that often exists between sparse attention theory and practice.

literature/2026-01-28-Temp-R1-A-Unified-.filePath: literature/2026-01-28 Temp-R1 A Unified Autonomous Agent for Complex Temporal KGQA.md
literature/2026-01-28-Temp-R1-A-Unified-.title: Temp-R1: A Unified Autonomous Agent for Complex Temporal KGQA via Reverse Curriculum Reinforcement Learning
literature/2026-01-28-Temp-R1-A-Unified-.content:
Temp-R1: A Unified Autonomous Agent for Complex Temporal KGQA via Reverse Curriculum Reinforcement Learning
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #6 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG, LLM, Graph, RL
Code: github.com/zjukg/Temp-R1
Abstract
Temporal Knowledge Graph Question Answering (TKGQA) is inherently challenging, as it requires sophisticated reasoning over dynamic facts with multi-hop dependencies and complex temporal constraints. Existing methods rely on fixed workflows and expensive closed-source APIs, limiting flexibility and scalability. We propose Temp-R1, the first autonomous end-to-end agent for TKGQA trained through reinforcement learning. To address cognitive overload in single-action reasoning, we expand the action space with specialized internal actions alongside external action. To prevent shortcut learning on simple questions, we introduce reverse curriculum learning that trains on difficult questions first, forcing the development of sophisticated reasoning before transferring to easier cases. Our 8B-parameter Temp-R1 achieves state-of-the-art performance on MultiTQ and TimelineKGQA, improving 19.8% over strong baselines on complex questions. Our work establishes a new paradigm for autonomous temporal reasoning agents. Our code will be publicly available soon at github.com/zjukg/Temp-R1.
Key Techniques

Autonomous end-to-end agent with reinforcement learning
expanded action space with specialized internal actions
reverse curriculum learning

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityFat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.28Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning0.25STATe-of-Thoughts: Structured Action Templates for Tree-of-Thoughts0.24MATA: A Trainable Hierarchical Automaton System for Multi-Agent Visual Reasoning0.23Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning0.21
Analysis: Temp-R1 and the Evolution of Agentic Reasoning Systems
Building on Graph-R1’s Multi-Step Agent Framework: Temp-R1 extends the agentic reasoning paradigm established by Graph-R1 but with a critical shift in problem domain and action design. While Graph-R1 operates within a knowledge hypergraph environment performing “think-retrieve-rethink-generate” loops, Temp-R1 introduces specialized internal actions alongside external actions to address temporal reasoning complexity. This expansion of the action space is a response to a different cognitive challenge—not just retrieval efficiency and reasoning quality (Graph-R1’s focus), but preventing the agent from taking shortcuts on simple questions while maintaining sophistication on complex temporal dependencies. Both papers share the core insight that autonomous agents need structured, iterative reasoning loops, but Temp-R1’s reverse curriculum learning represents a novel training methodology that Graph-R1 doesn’t address: forcing the agent to learn complex patterns first prevents overfitting to simple question-solving strategies.
Temporal Reasoning Requires Explicit Memory Architecture: Temp-R1’s internal actions echo the persistent memory and logic tracking problem that the Mind-Map Agent solves. Just as the Mind-Map Agent addresses “loss of coherence in long reasoning chains” through dynamic knowledge graph construction, Temp-R1’s internal actions likely function as temporal constraint tracking and multi-hop dependency management—making explicit what a naive agent might lose across reasoning steps. However, Temp-R1 appears to integrate this memory directly into the action space rather than as a parallel graph-construction process, suggesting a tighter coupling between reasoning and memory management for temporal questions specifically.
New Paradigm: Reverse Curriculum as a Training Inversion: The most novel contribution Temp-R1 adds to this knowledge base is the reverse curriculum learning framework. While “Reasoning Beyond Limits” surveys recent advances in inference-time scaling and RL-based reasoning (DeepSeek-R1, o1, etc.), it doesn’t address training-time curriculum design for preventing shortcut learning. Temp-R1’s insight—that training on difficult temporal questions first forces development of sophisticated reasoning before transfer to easier cases—inverts traditional curriculum learning assumptions and directly targets a failure mode in knowledge-intensive reasoning: models learning surface-level patterns rather than deep temporal logic. This suggests that specialized RL agents for constrained domains (like temporal QA) benefit from problem-domain-specific curriculum strategies beyond generic inference-time scaling.

literature/2026-01-28-The-Side-Effects-o.filePath: literature/2026-01-28 The Side Effects of Being Smart Safety Risks in MLLMs Multi-.md
literature/2026-01-28-The-Side-Effects-o.title: The Side Effects of Being Smart: Safety Risks in MLLMs' Multi-Image Reasoning
literature/2026-01-28-The-Side-Effects-o.content:
The Side Effects of Being Smart: Safety Risks in MLLMs’ Multi-Image Reasoning
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-20
Rank: #14 (score: 0.60, max_sim: 0.79)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG, LLM
Code: github.com/thu-coai/MIR-SafetyBench
Abstract
As Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) acquire stronger reasoning capabilities to handle complex, multi-image instructions, this advancement may pose new safety risks. We study this problem by introducing MIR-SafetyBench, the first benchmark focused on multi-image reasoning safety, which consists of 2,676 instances across a taxonomy of 9 multi-image relations. Our extensive evaluations on 19 MLLMs reveal a troubling trend: models with more advanced multi-image reasoning can be more vulnerable on MIR-SafetyBench. Beyond attack success rates, we find that many responses labeled as safe are superficial, often driven by misunderstanding or evasive, non-committal replies. We further observe that unsafe generations exhibit lower attention entropy than safe ones on average. This internal signature suggests a possible risk that models may over-focus on task solving while neglecting safety constraints. Our code and data are available at github.com/thu-coai/MIR-SafetyBench.
Key Techniques

MIR-SafetyBench benchmark
multi-image relation taxonomy
attention entropy analysis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityGOSV: Global Optimization for Safety Vector Extraction in LLMs0.30CSR-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating the Cross-modal Safety and Reliability of MLLMs0.29Risk Awareness Injection: Calibrating Vision-Language Models for Safety without Compromising Utility0.18SwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMs0.17Do MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.15
Analysis: Multi-Image Reasoning Safety and Broader MLLM Research
Challenging Assumptions in Advanced Reasoning Research: This paper directly challenges an implicit assumption in Hajishirzi’s vision for open language model research—that advancing reasoning capabilities inherently benefits deployment and safety. While “Reasoning in Language Models” advocates for continued innovation in reasoning and expanded applications across domains like health and science, MIR-SafetyBench reveals a concerning inverse relationship: models with stronger multi-image reasoning capabilities are more vulnerable to safety violations. This findings complicates the roadmap for scaling MLLMs, suggesting that capability advancement must be paired with active safety constraints rather than treating safety as a secondary concern. The paper’s finding that “many responses labeled as safe are superficial, often driven by misunderstanding or evasive replies” further echoes Hajishirzi’s concern about mitigating bias and risk, but demonstrates these risks manifest in unexpected ways—not through overt bias but through strategic evasion in advanced models.
Bridging Multimodal Capability and Safety Analysis: The matched note on “MLLM-based Unified Captioning” highlights how MLLMs can provide unified semantic understanding across modalities, reducing information loss through single-model approaches. MIR-SafetyBench extends this insight by interrogating what happens when MLLMs synthesize reasoning across multiple images—a genuinely new capability frontier. The benchmark’s 9-category taxonomy of multi-image relations (comparison, composition, temporal reasoning, etc.) represents novel attack surfaces that don’t exist in single-image MLLM work. The paper’s discovery of lower attention entropy in unsafe generations provides a mechanistic bridge to understanding failure modes: models over-commit to task solving (high focus on specific image relationships) while suppressing attention to safety constraints. This internal signature offers the kind of mechanistic interpretability that “Modeling Open-World Cognition” implicitly assumes matters—the ability to inspect how models construct mental models becomes critical when those models must reason over multiple disparate inputs.
New Perspective on Capability-Safety Tradeoffs: Unlike prior work that treats reasoning advancement and safety as orthogonal concerns, MIR-SafetyBench systematically documents that multi-image reasoning represents a genuine capability jump that creates novel vulnerabilities. The paper adds quantitative rigor to what Hajishirzi calls for—documented, reproducible research with clear risk assessment—by providing the first benchmark specifically measuring safety degradation under capability improvement. This inverts the typical narrative in open LLM research and suggests that future MLLMs pursuing advanced reasoning (particularly on scientific and analytical tasks Hajishirzi emphasizes) must include explicit safety evaluation on multi-modal relational reasoning before deployment, not afterward.

literature/2026-01-28-TokenSeek-Memory-E.title: TokenSeek: Memory Efficient Fine Tuning via Instance-Aware Token Ditching
literature/2026-01-28-TokenSeek-Memory-E.content:
TokenSeek: Memory Efficient Fine Tuning via Instance-Aware Token Ditching
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #2 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Search, LLM, Transformer
Code: runjia.tech/iclr_tokenseek/
Abstract
Fine tuning has been regarded as a de facto approach for adapting large language models (LLMs) to downstream tasks, but the high training memory consumption inherited from LLMs makes this process inefficient. Among existing memory efficient approaches, activation-related optimization has proven particularly effective, as activations consistently dominate overall memory consumption. Although prior arts offer various activation optimization strategies, their data-agnostic nature ultimately results in ineffective and unstable fine tuning. In this paper, we propose TokenSeek, a universal plugin solution for various transformer-based models through instance-aware token seeking and ditching, achieving significant fine-tuning memory savings (e.g., requiring only 14.8% of the memory on Llama3.2 1B) with on-par or even better performance. Furthermore, our interpretable token seeking process reveals the underlying reasons for its effectiveness, offering valuable insights for future research on token efficiency. Homepage: runjia.tech/iclr_tokenseek/
Key Techniques

Instance-aware token seeking
Token ditching
Activation optimization
Memory-efficient fine-tuning

literature/2026-01-28-UI-Remix-Supportin.filePath: literature/2026-01-28 UI Remix Supporting UI Design Through Interactive Example Re.md
literature/2026-01-28-UI-Remix-Supportin.title: UI Remix: Supporting UI Design Through Interactive Example Retrieval and Remixing
literature/2026-01-28-UI-Remix-Supportin.content:
UI Remix: Supporting UI Design Through Interactive Example Retrieval and Remixing
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #11 (score: 1.13, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, RAG
Abstract
Designing user interfaces (UIs) is a critical step when launching products, building portfolios, or personalizing projects, yet end users without design expertise often struggle to articulate their intent and to trust design choices. Existing example-based tools either promote broad exploration, which can cause overwhelm and design drift, or require adapting a single example, risking design fixation. We present UI Remix, an interactive system that supports mobile UI design through an example-driven design workflow. Powered by a multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (MMRAG) model, UI Remix enables iterative search, selection, and adaptation of examples at both the global (whole interface) and local (component) level. To foster trust, it presents source transparency cues such as ratings, download counts, and developer information. In an empirical study with 24 end users, UI Remix significantly improved participants’ ability to achieve their design goals, facilitated effective iteration, and encouraged exploration of alternative designs. Participants also reported that source transparency cues enhanced their confidence in adapting examples. Our findings suggest new directions for AI-assisted, example-driven systems that empower end users to design with greater control, trust, and openness to exploration.
Key Techniques

Multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (MMRAG)
iterative search and selection at global and local levels
source transparency cues

literature/2026-01-28-Why-They-Link-An-I.title: Why They Link: An Intent Taxonomy for Including Hyperlinks in Social Posts
literature/2026-01-28-Why-They-Link-An-I.content:
Why They Link: An Intent Taxonomy for Including Hyperlinks in Social Posts
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-24
Rank: #2 (score: 1.40, max_sim: 0.74)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search, RecSys
Abstract
URLs serve as bridges between social media platforms and the broader web, linking user-generated content to external information resources. On Twitter (X), approximately one in five tweets contains at least one URL, underscoring their central role in information dissemination. While prior studies have examined the motivations of authors who share URLs, such author-centered intentions are difficult to observe in practice. To enable broader downstream use, this work investigates reader-centered interpretations, i.e., how users perceive the intentions behind hyperlinks included in posts. We develop an intent taxonomy for including hyperlinks in social posts through a hybrid approach that begins with a bottom-up, data-driven process using large-scale crowdsourced annotations, and is then refined using large language model assistance to generate descriptive category names and precise definitions. The final taxonomy comprises 6 top-level categories and 26 fine-grained intention classes, capturing diverse communicative purposes. Applying this taxonomy, we annotate and analyze 1000 user posts, revealing that advertising, arguing, and sharing are the most prevalent intentions. This resulting taxonomy provides a foundation for intent-aware information retrieval and NLP applications, enabling more accurate retrieval, recommendation, and understanding of social media content.
Key Techniques

Crowdsourced annotations
Large language model assistance
Intent taxonomy development

literature/2026-01-28-treaming-dLLM-Acce.filePath: literature/2026-01-28 treaming-dLLM Accelerating Diffusion LLMs via Suffix Pruning.md
literature/2026-01-28-treaming-dLLM-Acce.title: treaming-dLLM: Accelerating Diffusion LLMs via Suffix Pruning and Dynamic Decoding
literature/2026-01-28-treaming-dLLM-Acce.content:
treaming-dLLM: Accelerating Diffusion LLMs via Suffix Pruning and Dynamic Decoding
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-25
Rank: #6 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Diffusion, RAG, LLM
Code: github.com/xiaoshideta/Streaming-dLLM
Abstract
Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) offer a compelling paradigm for natural language generation, leveraging parallel decoding and bidirectional attention to achieve superior global coherence compared to autoregressive models. While recent works have accelerated inference via KV cache reuse or heuristic decoding, they overlook the intrinsic inefficiencies within the block-wise diffusion process. Specifically, they suffer from spatial redundancy by modeling informative-sparse suffix regions uniformly and temporal inefficiency by applying fixed denoising schedules across all the decoding process. To address this, we propose Streaming-dLLM, a training-free framework that streamlines inference across both spatial and temporal dimensions. Spatially, we introduce attenuation guided suffix modeling to approximate the full context by pruning redundant mask tokens. Temporally, we employ a dynamic confidence aware strategy with an early exit mechanism, allowing the model to skip unnecessary iterations for converged tokens. Extensive experiments show that Streaming-dLLM achieves up to 68.2X speedup while maintaining generation quality, highlighting its effectiveness in diffusion decoding. The code is available at github.com/xiaoshideta/Streaming-dLLM.
Key Techniques

Attenuation guided suffix modeling
Dynamic confidence aware strategy with early exit mechanism
KV cache reuse

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityFocus-dLLM: Accelerating Long-Context Diffusion LLM Inference via Confidence-Guided Context Focusing0.30Diffusion In Diffusion: Reclaiming Global Coherence in Semi-Autoregressive Diffusion0.28Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.25Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.25From LLMs to LRMs: Rethinking Pruning for Reasoning-Centric Models0.25
Extending Efficiency Work Across Different Paradigms
The Streaming-dLLM paper extends the efficiency acceleration concerns found in the matched notes but targets a fundamentally different inference paradigm. While “Accelerating Inference of Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Sparse Context Selection” focuses on reducing computational overhead through sparse retrieval, and “Pruning LLMs for Retriever Backbones” optimizes model size through structured pruning, Streaming-dLLM addresses inefficiencies within the diffusion decoding process itself—a parallel decoding approach that contrasts with the sequential, autoregressive generation assumed in the retrieval-augmented work. This represents a parallel axis of optimization: rather than pruning parameters or selecting sparse contexts upstream, Streaming-dLLM prunes redundant computation during inference through spatial suffix reduction and temporal early-exit mechanisms. The connection is methodological rather than architectural—all three papers recognize that modern LLM systems suffer from unnecessary computational overhead and propose training-free or low-cost solutions to eliminate it.
Bridging Spatial and Temporal Redundancy Reduction
Streaming-dLLM’s core innovation—distinguishing between spatial redundancy (informative-sparse suffix regions) and temporal inefficiency (fixed denoising schedules)—offers a more granular vocabulary for understanding inefficiency than appears in the matched notes. The pruning work in “Pruning LLMs for Retriever Backbones” addresses model-level redundancy (unnecessary heads, layers, dimensions), while Streaming-dLLM reveals that even within a single forward pass, diffusion models exhibit region-specific (spatial) and iteration-specific (temporal) inefficiencies. The “attenuation guided suffix modeling” technique—approximating full context by pruning mask tokens—mirrors the principle behind sparse context selection in retrieval-augmented systems: not all parts of the input carry equal information density, and computational resources can be redistributed by recognizing this asymmetry.
Novel Perspective: Dynamic Decoding as a Confidence-Aware Strategy
Where the matched notes treat efficiency largely as a parameter or context reduction problem, Streaming-dLLM introduces a temporal dimension via confidence-aware early exit, allowing different tokens to converge at different iteration counts. This perspective is absent from the matched vault notes and represents a valuable addition: rather than applying uniform computation budgets across all tokens (as implicit in both retrieval-augmented and pruned retriever approaches), Streaming-dLLM treats decoding as an adaptive process where per-token confidence metrics guide resource allocation. The 68.2X speedup while maintaining quality suggests that diffusion LLMs may benefit from heterogeneous computational allocation in ways that complement the spatial pruning and context sparsification strategies documented in prior work.

literature/2026-01-29-Accelerating-Gener.title: Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression
literature/2026-01-29-Accelerating-Gener.content:
Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #6 (score: 0.91, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Although generative recommenders demonstrate improved performance with longer sequences, their real-time deployment is hindered by substantial computational costs. To address this challenge, we propose a simple yet effective method for compressing long-term user histories by leveraging inherent item categorical features, thereby preserving user interests while enhancing efficiency. Experiments on two large-scale datasets demonstrate that, compared to the influential HSTU model, our approach achieves up to a 6x reduction in computational cost and up to 39% higher accuracy at comparable cost (i.e., similar sequence length).
Key Techniques

Long-term user history compression
categorical feature leveraging
computational cost reduction

literature/2026-01-29-Beyond-Speedup----.title: Beyond Speedup -- Utilizing KV Cache for Sampling and Reasoning
literature/2026-01-29-Beyond-Speedup----.content:
Beyond Speedup — Utilizing KV Cache for Sampling and Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #2 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
KV caches, typically used only to speed up autoregressive decoding, encode contextual information that can be reused for downstream tasks at no extra cost. We propose treating the KV cache as a lightweight representation, eliminating the need to recompute or store full hidden states. Despite being weaker than dedicated embeddings, KV-derived representations are shown to be sufficient for two key applications: \textbf{(i) Chain-of-Embedding}, where they achieve competitive or superior performance on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Qwen2-7B-Instruct; and \textbf{(ii) Fast/Slow Thinking Switching}, where they enable adaptive reasoning on Qwen3-8B and DeepSeek-R1-Distil-Qwen-14B, reducing token generation by up to 5.7\times with minimal accuracy loss. Our findings establish KV caches as a free, effective substrate for sampling and reasoning, opening new directions for representation reuse in LLM inference. Code: github.com/cmd2001/ICLR2026_KV-Embedding.
Key Techniques

KV cache reuse
Chain-of-Embedding
Fast/Slow Thinking Switching

literature/2026-01-29-Breaking-Flat-A-Ge.title: Breaking Flat: A Generalised Query Performance Prediction Evaluation Framework
literature/2026-01-29-Breaking-Flat-A-Ge.content:
Breaking Flat: A Generalised Query Performance Prediction Evaluation Framework
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-24
Rank: #3 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RecSys, Search, RAG
Abstract
The traditional use-case of query performance prediction (QPP) is to identify which queries perform well and which perform poorly for a given ranking model. A more fine-grained and arguably more challenging extension of this task is to determine which ranking models are most effective for a given query. In this work, we generalize the QPP task and its evaluation into three settings: (i) SingleRanker MultiQuery (SRMQ-PP), corresponding to the standard use case; (ii) MultiRanker SingleQuery (MRSQ-PP), which evaluates a QPP model’s ability to select the most effective ranker for a query; and (iii) MultiRanker MultiQuery (MRMQ-PP), which considers predictions jointly across all query ranker pairs. Our results show that (a) the relative effectiveness of QPP models varies substantially across tasks (SRMQ-PP vs. MRSQ-PP), and (b) predicting the best ranker for a query is considerably more difficult than predicting the relative difficulty of queries for a given ranker.
Key Techniques

SingleRanker MultiQuery (SRMQ-PP)
MultiRanker SingleQuery (MRSQ-PP)

literature/2026-01-29-CooperBench-Why-Co.title: CooperBench: Why Coding Agents Cannot be Your Teammates Yet
literature/2026-01-29-CooperBench-Why-Co.content:
CooperBench: Why Coding Agents Cannot be Your Teammates Yet
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-19
Rank: #10 (score: 0.87, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, Search
Abstract
Resolving team conflicts requires not only task-specific competence, but also social intelligence to find common ground and build consensus. As AI agents increasingly collaborate on complex work, they must develop coordination capabilities to function as effective teammates. Yet we hypothesize that current agents lack these capabilities. To test this, we introduce CooperBench, a benchmark of over 600 collaborative coding tasks across 12 libraries in 4 programming languages. Each task assigns two agents different features that can be implemented independently but may conflict without proper coordination. Tasks are grounded in real open-source repositories with expert-written tests. Evaluating state-of-the-art coding agents, we observe the curse of coordination: agents achieve on average 30% lower success rates when working together compared to performing both tasks individually. This contrasts sharply with human teams, where adding teammates typically improves productivity. Our analysis reveals three key issues: (1) communication channels become jammed with vague, ill-timed, and inaccurate messages; (2) even with effective communication, agents deviate from their commitments; and (3) agents often hold incorrect expectations about others’ plans and communication. Through large-scale simulation, we also observe rare but interesting emergent coordination behavior including role division, resource division, and negotiation. Our research presents a novel benchmark for collaborative coding and calls for a shift from pursuing individual agent capability to developing social intelligence.
Key Techniques

CooperBench benchmark
multi-agent collaborative coding tasks
communication channel analysis
emergent coordination behaviors

literature/2026-01-29-DART-Diffusion-Ins.title: DART: Diffusion-Inspired Speculative Decoding for Fast LLM Inference
literature/2026-01-29-DART-Diffusion-Ins.content:
DART: Diffusion-Inspired Speculative Decoding for Fast LLM Inference
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #5 (score: 0.92, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Speculative decoding is an effective and lossless approach for accelerating LLM inference. However, existing widely adopted model-based draft designs, such as EAGLE3, improve accuracy at the cost of multi-step autoregressive inference, resulting in high drafting latency and ultimately rendering the drafting stage itself a performance bottleneck. Inspired by diffusion-based large language models (dLLMs), we propose DART, which leverages parallel generation to reduce drafting latency. DART predicts logits for multiple future masked positions in parallel within a single forward pass based on hidden states of the target model, thereby eliminating autoregressive rollouts in the draft model while preserving a lightweight design. Based on these parallel logit predictions, we further introduce an efficient tree pruning algorithm that constructs high-quality draft token trees with N-gram-enforced semantic continuity. DART substantially reduces draft-stage overhead while preserving high draft accuracy, leading to significantly improved end-to-end decoding speed. Experimental results demonstrate that DART achieves a 2.03x—3.44x wall-clock time speedup across multiple datasets, surpassing EAGLE3 by 30% on average and offering a practical speculative decoding framework. Code is released at github.com/fvliang/DART.
Key Techniques

Parallel logit prediction
Tree pruning algorithm
N-gram-enforced semantic continuity
Diffusion-based draft generation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models0.63DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.60SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration0.55Variational Speculative Decoding: Rethinking Draft Training from Token Likelihood to Sequence Acceptance0.48Dynamic Delayed Tree Expansion For Improved Multi-Path Speculative Decoding0.46
Analysis: DART and Connections to Prior Speculative Decoding Work
DART extends the speculative decoding paradigm documented in the matched notes by addressing a critical bottleneck that previous approaches like EAGLE3 introduced: drafting latency from multi-step autoregressive inference. While the matched notes discuss optimization of draft shapes (Draft Shape Optimization for Dense Speculative Decoding) and batch verification strategies (Batch Verification in Speculative Decoding), DART tackles the fundamental architectural inefficiency of sequential token generation during the draft stage itself. By drawing inspiration from diffusion-based language models, DART predicts logits for multiple future masked positions in parallel within a single forward pass, eliminating the autoregressive rollout entirely. This represents a shift from optimizing how many drafts or how long each draft should be (as explored in DReSD’s 3×20 vs n×k analysis) to fundamentally changing how the drafts are generated—moving from sequential to parallel prediction. The 30% average speedup over EAGLE3 suggests that drafting latency had indeed become the bottleneck that the “LLM Inference Performance” mental model would predict: even with good draft acceptance rates, if the draft generation itself is slow, end-to-end performance suffers.
The paper’s N-gram-enforced tree pruning algorithm bridges DART’s parallel logit predictions to the practical concerns raised in the vault notes about draft quality and verification efficiency. The matched notes emphasize that batch verification (processing multiple draft sequences in one LLM forward pass) is key to reducing latency, and that draft shape matters for balancing verification cost against acceptance rates. DART’s tree construction approach appears to synthesize these insights: by generating multiple token predictions in parallel and then pruning them into high-quality trees with semantic continuity constraints, it creates a draft structure optimized for batch verification while maintaining the lightweight design principle. The 2.03×–3.44× wall-clock speedup across datasets suggests DART successfully reduces the per-token decode cost identified in the “Team Mental Model” by shrinking both the drafting overhead and maintaining favorable KV-cache behavior—since accepted speculative tokens avoid the serial decode loop bottleneck.
What DART adds to the knowledge base is a hybrid architecture that combines parallel prediction (diffusion-inspired) with tree-based speculation, moving beyond the binary choice between retrieval-based drafts (DReSD) and autoregressive model-based drafts (EAGLE). This opens new design space: instead of optimizing draft shape for a fixed generation method, DART optimizes the generation method itself to be parallelizable, then constructs optimal trees from those predictions. The practical implication aligns with the “Experiment Playbook” philosophy in the matched notes—DART demonstrates that rearchitecting the draft stage to exploit parallelism yields larger gains than further tuning autoregressive draft models. This suggests future speculative decoding research should prioritize parallel prediction mechanisms over incremental improvements to sequential draft models, especially as context lengths grow and KV-cache bandwidth becomes increasingly constrained during decode.

literature/2026-01-29-Dialogical-Reasoni.title: Dialogical Reasoning Across AI Architectures: A Multi-Model Framework for Testing AI Alignment Strategies
literature/2026-01-29-Dialogical-Reasoni.content:
Dialogical Reasoning Across AI Architectures: A Multi-Model Framework for Testing AI Alignment Strategies
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #7 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search
Abstract
This paper introduces a methodological framework for empirically testing AI alignment strategies through structured multi-model dialogue. Drawing on Peace Studies traditions - particularly interest-based negotiation, conflict transformation, and commons governance - we operationalize Viral Collaborative Wisdom (VCW), an approach that reframes alignment from a control problem to a relationship problem developed through dialogical reasoning.   Our experimental design assigns four distinct roles (Proposer, Responder, Monitor, Translator) to different AI systems across six conditions, testing whether current large language models can engage substantively with complex alignment frameworks. Using Claude, Gemini, and GPT-4o, we conducted 72 dialogue turns totaling 576,822 characters of structured exchange.   Results demonstrate that AI systems can engage meaningfully with Peace Studies concepts, surface complementary objections from different architectural perspectives, and generate emergent insights not present in initial framings - including the novel synthesis of “VCW as transitional framework.” Cross-architecture patterns reveal that different models foreground different concerns: Claude emphasized verification challenges, Gemini focused on bias and scalability, and GPT-4o highlighted implementation barriers.   The framework provides researchers with replicable methods for stress-testing alignment proposals before implementation, while the findings offer preliminary evidence about AI capacity for the kind of dialogical reasoning VCW proposes. We discuss limitations, including the observation that dialogues engaged more with process elements than with foundational claims about AI nature, and outline directions for future research including human-AI hybrid protocols and extended dialogue studies.
Key Techniques

Multi-model dialogue framework
role-based AI negotiation
cross-architecture pattern analysis
structured alignment testing

literature/2026-01-29-Dynamics-of-Human-.title: Dynamics of Human-AI Collective Knowledge on the Web: A Scalable Model and Insights for Sustainable Growth
literature/2026-01-29-Dynamics-of-Human-.content:
Dynamics of Human-AI Collective Knowledge on the Web: A Scalable Model and Insights for Sustainable Growth
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #5 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG, Search
Abstract
Humans and large language models (LLMs) now co-produce and co-consume the web’s shared knowledge archives. Such human-AI collective knowledge ecosystems contain feedback loops with both benefits (e.g., faster growth, easier learning) and systemic risks (e.g., quality dilution, skill reduction, model collapse). To understand such phenomena, we propose a minimal, interpretable dynamical model of the co-evolution of archive size, archive quality, model (LLM) skill, aggregate human skill, and query volume. The model captures two content inflows (human, LLM) controlled by a gate on LLM-content admissions, two learning pathways for humans (archive study vs. LLM assistance), and two LLM-training modalities (corpus-driven scaling vs. learning from human feedback). Through numerical experiments, we identify different growth regimes (e.g., healthy growth, inverted flow, inverted learning, oscillations), and show how platform and policy levers (gate strictness, LLM training, human learning pathways) shift the system across regime boundaries. Two domain configurations (PubMed, GitHub and Copilot) illustrate contrasting steady states under different growth rates and moderation norms. We also fit the model to Wikipedia’s knowledge flow during pre-ChatGPT and post-ChatGPT eras separately. We find a rise in LLM additions with a concurrent decline in human inflow, consistent with a regime identified by the model. Our model and analysis yield actionable insights for sustainable growth of human-AI collective knowledge on the Web.
Key Techniques

dynamical model of human-AI co-evolution
LLM content admission gating
dual learning pathways (archive study vs. LLM assistance)
corpus-driven scaling vs. learning from human feedback

literature/2026-01-29-Fast-KVzip-Efficie.title: Fast KVzip: Efficient and Accurate LLM Inference with Gated KV Eviction
literature/2026-01-29-Fast-KVzip-Efficie.content:
Fast KVzip: Efficient and Accurate LLM Inference with Gated KV Eviction
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-25
Rank: #9 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG, LLM
Abstract
Efficient key-value (KV) cache management is crucial for the practical deployment of large language models (LLMs), yet existing compression techniques often incur a trade-off between performance degradation and computational overhead. We propose a novel gating-based KV cache eviction method for frozen-weight LLMs that achieves high compression ratios with negligible computational cost. Our approach introduces lightweight sink-attention gating modules to identify and retain critical KV pairs, and integrates seamlessly into both the prefill and decoding stages. The proposed gate training algorithm relies on forward passes of an LLM, avoiding expensive backpropagation, while achieving strong task generalization through a task-agnostic reconstruction objective. Extensive experiments across the Qwen2.5-1M, Qwen3, and Gemma3 families show that our method maintains near-lossless performance while evicting up to 70% of the KV cache. The results are consistent across a wide range of tasks, including long-context understanding, code comprehension, and mathematical reasoning, demonstrating the generality of our approach.
Key Techniques

Gating-based KV cache eviction
Sink-attention gating modules
Task-agnostic reconstruction objective
Forward-pass gate training

literature/2026-01-29-Generative-Chain-o.title: Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction
literature/2026-01-29-Generative-Chain-o.content:
Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #8 (score: 0.89, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Modeling long-term user behavior trajectories is essential for understanding evolving preferences and enabling proactive recommendations. However, most sequential recommenders focus on next-item prediction, overlooking dependencies across multiple future actions. We propose Generative Chain of Behavior (GCB), a generative framework that models user interactions as an autoregressive chain of semantic behaviors over multiple future steps. GCB first encodes items into semantic IDs via RQ-VAE with k-means refinement, forming a discrete latent space that preserves semantic proximity. On top of this space, a transformer-based autoregressive generator predicts multi-step future behaviors conditioned on user history, capturing long-horizon intent transitions and generating coherent trajectories. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that GCB consistently outperforms state-of-the-art sequential recommenders in multi-step accuracy and trajectory consistency. Beyond these gains, GCB offers a unified generative formulation for capturing user preference evolution.
Key Techniques

RQ-VAE with k-means refinement
semantic ID encoding
transformer-based autoregressive generation
multi-step behavior prediction

literature/2026-01-29-HalluGuard-Demysti.title: HalluGuard: Demystifying Data-Driven and Reasoning-Driven Hallucinations in LLMs
literature/2026-01-29-HalluGuard-Demysti.content:
HalluGuard: Demystifying Data-Driven and Reasoning-Driven Hallucinations in LLMs
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #12 (score: 0.94, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG
Abstract
The reliability of Large Language Models (LLMs) in high-stakes domains such as healthcare, law, and scientific discovery is often compromised by hallucinations. These failures typically stem from two sources: data-driven hallucinations and reasoning-driven hallucinations. However, existing detection methods usually address only one source and rely on task-specific heuristics, limiting their generalization to complex scenarios. To overcome these limitations, we introduce the Hallucination Risk Bound, a unified theoretical framework that formally decomposes hallucination risk into data-driven and reasoning-driven components, linked respectively to training-time mismatches and inference-time instabilities. This provides a principled foundation for analyzing how hallucinations emerge and evolve. Building on this foundation, we introduce HalluGuard, an NTK-based score that leverages the induced geometry and captured representations of the NTK to jointly identify data-driven and reasoning-driven hallucinations. We evaluate HalluGuard on 10 diverse benchmarks, 11 competitive baselines, and 9 popular LLM backbones, consistently achieving state-of-the-art performance in detecting diverse forms of LLM hallucinations.
Key Techniques

Hallucination Risk Bound
NTK-based scoring
dual-source decomposition
multi-benchmark evaluation

literature/2026-01-29-Improving-Diffusio.title: Improving Diffusion Language Model Decoding through Joint Search in Generation Order and Token Space
literature/2026-01-29-Improving-Diffusio.content:
Improving Diffusion Language Model Decoding through Joint Search in Generation Order and Token Space
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #6 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, Diffusion, Search, RecSys, RAG
Abstract
Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) offer order-agnostic generation that can explore many possible decoding trajectories. However, current decoding methods commit to a single trajectory, limiting exploration in trajectory space. We introduce Order-Token Search to explore this space through jointly searching over generation order and token values. Its core is a likelihood estimator that scores denoising actions, enabling stable pruning and efficient exploration of diverse trajectories. Across mathematical reasoning and coding benchmarks, Order-Token Search consistently outperforms baselines on GSM8K, MATH500, Countdown, and HumanEval (3.1%, 3.8%, 7.9%, and 6.8% absolute over backbone), matching or surpassing diffu-GRPO post-trained d1-LLaDA. Our work establishes joint search as a key component for advancing decoding in DLMs.
Key Techniques

Order-Token Search
likelihood estimator for denoising actions
joint search over generation order and token values

literature/2026-01-29-In-Context-Reinfor.title: In-Context Reinforcement Learning From Suboptimal Historical Data
literature/2026-01-29-In-Context-Reinfor.content:
In-Context Reinforcement Learning From Suboptimal Historical Data
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #10 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Transformer, RL
Abstract
Transformer models have achieved remarkable empirical successes, largely due to their in-context learning capabilities. Inspired by this, we explore training an autoregressive transformer for in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL). In this setting, we initially train a transformer on an offline dataset consisting of trajectories collected from various RL tasks, and then fix and use this transformer to create an action policy for new RL tasks. Notably, we consider the setting where the offline dataset contains trajectories sampled from suboptimal behavioral policies. In this case, standard autoregressive training corresponds to imitation learning and results in suboptimal performance. To address this, we propose the Decision Importance Transformer(DIT) framework, which emulates the actor-critic algorithm in an in-context manner. In particular, we first train a transformer-based value function that estimates the advantage functions of the behavior policies that collected the suboptimal trajectories. Then we train a transformer-based policy via a weighted maximum likelihood estimation loss, where the weights are constructed based on the trained value function to steer the suboptimal policies to the optimal ones. We conduct extensive experiments to test the performance of DIT on both bandit and Markov Decision Process problems. Our results show that DIT achieves superior performance, particularly when the offline dataset contains suboptimal historical data.
Key Techniques

Transformer-based value function estimation
Weighted maximum likelihood estimation with advantage-based weighting
In-context reinforcement learning from offline trajectories

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.24Can Custom Models Learn In-Context? An Exploration of Hybrid Architecture Performance on In-Context Learning Tasks0.21Self-Distillation Enables Continual Learning0.20DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.18DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.18
Analysis: Decision Importance Transformer in Context of In-Context RL
The paper directly advances the In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) framework documented in your vault notes by addressing a critical practical limitation: handling suboptimal behavioral data. While the core ICRL concept—using rolling context to enable policy adaptation without parameter updates—remains central, this work recognizes that standard autoregressive training on suboptimal trajectories reduces to imitation learning, which inherits the behavioral policy’s limitations. The Decision Importance Transformer (DIT) framework extends ICRL by introducing an actor-critic structure entirely within the transformer’s in-context reasoning, rather than relying on implicit policy inference from context alone. This is a crucial methodological shift: instead of assuming the transformer naturally learns to weight trajectories by quality, DIT explicitly trains a value function to estimate advantages, then uses these weights to steer policy learning away from suboptimal actions—formalizing what ICRL was doing implicitly.
This connection to the Stanford CS224R Lecture 7 on Offline RL is particularly salient. The lecture contrasts offline RL’s core problem—that standard policy gradient algorithms fail without online corrective feedback—with the advantage-weighting solutions that enable learning from static data. DIT operationalizes this exact principle at inference time: by computing advantage estimates and reweighting the policy loss, it mirrors the actor-critic algorithms discussed in that lecture but applies them in a transformer’s forward pass across historical trajectories. The paper essentially asks: if offline RL requires advantage-aware learning to overcome suboptimal data, why not make the advantage computation part of the learned in-context mechanism? This directly bridges the gap between offline RL theory (which the Stanford lecture covers) and in-context learning practice.
Finally, DIT offers a fresh perspective on the RLHF and reinforcement learning in LLMs discussion: rather than post-hoc RLHF fine-tuning or deterministic reward verification (as in RLVR), this paper proposes baking advantage-aware policy learning into the transformer’s architecture itself. The in-context value function acts as a learned reward model, but one that operates on historical trajectories during inference without requiring explicit signal ranking or KL-penalty optimization. This positions DIT as an alternative scaling path for RL in transformers—one that treats the transformer not as a fixed generation engine requiring external RL, but as an adaptive learner that internalizes both value estimation and policy weighting through pre-training on mixed-quality offline data.

literature/2026-01-29-Is-Finer-Better-Th.title: Is Finer Better? The Limits of Microscaling Formats in Large Language Models
literature/2026-01-29-Is-Finer-Better-Th.content:
Is Finer Better? The Limits of Microscaling Formats in Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #12 (score: 0.73, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Microscaling data formats leverage per-block tensor quantization to enable aggressive model compression with limited loss in accuracy. Unlocking their potential for efficient training and inference necessitates hardware-friendly implementations that handle matrix multiplications in a native format and adopt efficient error-mitigation strategies. Herein, we report the emergence of a surprising behavior associated with microscaling quantization, whereas the output of a quantized model degrades as block size is decreased below a given threshold. This behavior clashes with the expectation that a smaller block size should allow for a better representation of the tensor elements. We investigate this phenomenon both experimentally and theoretically, decoupling the sources of quantization error behind it. Experimentally, we analyze the distributions of several Large Language Models and identify the conditions driving the anomalous behavior. Theoretically, we lay down a framework showing remarkable agreement with experimental data from pretrained model distributions and ideal ones. Overall, we show that the anomaly is driven by the interplay between narrow tensor distributions and the limited dynamic range of the quantized scales. Based on these insights, we propose the use of FP8 unsigned E5M3 (UE5M3) as a novel hardware-friendly format for the scales in FP4 microscaling data types. We demonstrate that UE5M3 achieves comparable performance to the conventional FP8 unsigned E4M3 scales while obviating the need of global scaling operations on weights and activations.
Key Techniques

Per-block tensor quantization
microscaling data formats
FP8 unsigned E5M3 (UE5M3) format
quantization error analysis

literature/2026-01-29-MALLOC-Benchmarkin.title: MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation
literature/2026-01-29-MALLOC-Benchmarkin.content:
MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #11 (score: 0.85, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The scaling law, which indicates that model performance improves with increasing dataset and model capacity, has fueled a growing trend in expanding recommendation models in both industry and academia. However, the advent of large-scale recommenders also brings significantly higher computational costs, particularly under the long-sequence dependencies inherent in the user intent of recommendation systems. Current approaches often rely on pre-storing the intermediate states of the past behavior for each user, thereby reducing the quadratic re-computation cost for the following requests. Despite their effectiveness, these methods often treat memory merely as a medium for acceleration, without adequately considering the space overhead it introduces. This presents a critical challenge in real-world recommendation systems with billions of users, each of whom might initiate thousands of interactions and require massive memory for state storage. Fortunately, there have been several memory management strategies examined for compression in LLM, while most have not been evaluated on the recommendation task. To mitigate this gap, we introduce MALLOC, a comprehensive benchmark for memory-aware long sequence compression. MALLOC presents a comprehensive investigation and systematic classification of memory management techniques applicable to large sequential recommendations. These techniques are integrated into state-of-the-art recommenders, enabling a reproducible and accessible evaluation platform. Through extensive experiments across accuracy, efficiency, and complexity, we demonstrate the holistic reliability of MALLOC in advancing large-scale recommendation. Code is available at anonymous.4open.science/r/MALLOC.
Key Techniques

Long-sequence user behavior modeling
Memory-aware state compression
Pre-storing intermediate states
Memory management techniques from LLMs

literature/2026-01-29-Masked-Diffusion-G.title: Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation
literature/2026-01-29-Masked-Diffusion-G.content:
Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #4 (score: 0.92, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Generative recommendation (GR) typically first quantizes continuous item embeddings into multi-level semantic IDs (SIDs), and then generates the next item via autoregressive decoding. Although existing methods are already competitive in terms of recommendation performance, directly inheriting the autoregressive decoding paradigm from language models still suffers from three key limitations: (1) autoregressive decoding struggles to jointly capture global dependencies among the multi-dimensional features associated with different positions of SID; (2) using a unified, fixed decoding path for the same item implicitly assumes that all users attend to item attributes in the same order; (3) autoregressive decoding is inefficient at inference time and struggles to meet real-time requirements. To tackle these challenges, we propose MDGR, a Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation framework that reshapes the GR pipeline from three perspectives: codebook, training, and inference. (1) We adopt a parallel codebook to provide a structural foundation for diffusion-based GR. (2) During training, we adaptively construct masking supervision signals along both the temporal and sample dimensions. (3) During inference, we develop a warm-up-based two-stage parallel decoding strategy for efficient generation of SIDs. Extensive experiments on multiple public and industrial-scale datasets show that MDGR outperforms ten state-of-the-art baselines by up to 10.78%. Furthermore, by deploying MDGR on a large-scale online advertising platform, we achieve a 1.20% increase in revenue, demonstrating its practical value. The code will be released upon acceptance.
Key Techniques

Parallel codebook for multi-level semantic IDs
Adaptive masking supervision across temporal and sample dimensions
Warm-up-based two-stage parallel decoding
Masked diffusion generative framework

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMasked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.71Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.66Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.62Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.51End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.30
Analysis: MDGR and the Evolution of Generative Recommendation
This paper represents a significant architectural shift within the generative recommendation paradigm documented in the matched notes. While the vault notes (particularly “Actions Speak Louder than Words” and the comprehensive survey) establish that generative recommenders treat user actions as language and use autoregressive decoding to predict next items via semantic IDs (SIDs), MDGR directly challenges three fundamental assumptions of this approach. The paper argues that autoregressive decoding—borrowed wholesale from language models—creates unnecessary constraints: it forces sequential generation when item attributes could be captured in parallel, it imposes a single decoding path when users may attend to features in different orders, and it sacrifices inference efficiency. This critique is particularly important given the “Generative Large Recommendation Model” note’s emphasis on real-time personalization and the industrial-scale deployment contexts mentioned across the vault notes.
The bridge between MDGR and prior work lies in its preservation of the SID quantization framework while completely reimagining the generation mechanism. The matched notes describe how generative recommenders quantize continuous item embeddings into multi-level semantic codes—MDGR keeps this representation but replaces autoregressive decoding with masked diffusion. Specifically, the paper introduces: (1) parallel codebooks that enable simultaneous generation across SID dimensions rather than left-to-right decoding, (2) adaptive masking supervision that varies across both time and samples during training (moving beyond the fixed teacher-forcing of traditional seq2seq models), and (3) a warm-up-based two-stage parallel decoding strategy that addresses the inference latency problem explicitly mentioned as a limitation in autoregressive approaches. The 10.78% improvement over baselines and successful industrial deployment directly address the scaling and real-time requirements highlighted in the “Trillion-Parameter Sequential Transducers” note.
What MDGR adds is a fundamental rethinking of whether the language modeling paradigm is the optimal framework for recommendation. While the vault notes document the trend of applying transformer architectures and treating actions as language, this paper suggests that recommendation has different structural properties than natural language—namely, that item attributes don’t have an inherent sequential order and that different users may conceptualize items through different feature hierarchies. The diffusion-based approach enables more flexible, parallel generation patterns while maintaining the semantic richness of the SID representation. This positions MDGR as a potential “post-autoregressive” direction for generative recommendation, complementing the LLM-enhanced and LLM-native approaches discussed in “Generative Large Recommendation Model” by showing that domain-specific architectural innovations may outperform direct adaptation of NLP techniques.

literature/2026-01-29-MetaGen-Self-Evolv.title: MetaGen: Self-Evolving Roles and Topologies for Multi-Agent LLM Reasoning
literature/2026-01-29-MetaGen-Self-Evolv.content:
MetaGen: Self-Evolving Roles and Topologies for Multi-Agent LLM Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #8 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG, Search
Abstract
Large language models are increasingly deployed as multi-agent systems, where specialized roles communicate and collaborate through structured interactions to solve complex tasks that often exceed the capacity of a single agent. However, most existing systems still rely on a fixed role library and an execution-frozen interaction topology, a rigid design choice that frequently leads to task mismatch, prevents timely adaptation when new evidence emerges during reasoning, and further inflates inference cost. We introduce MetaGen, a training-free framework that adapts both the role space and the collaboration topology at inference time, without updating base model weights. MetaGen generates and rewrites query-conditioned role specifications to maintain a controllable dynamic role pool, then instantiates a constrained execution graph around a minimal backbone. During execution, it iteratively updates role prompts and adjusts structural decisions using lightweight feedback signals. Experiments on code generation and multi-step reasoning benchmarks show that MetaGen improves the accuracy and cost tradeoff over strong multi-agent baselines.
Key Techniques

Dynamic role generation
Adaptive collaboration topology
Query-conditioned role specifications
Iterative prompt and structure refinement

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMetaGen: Self-Evolving Roles and Topologies for Multi-Agent LLM Reasoning0.79MATA: A Trainable Hierarchical Automaton System for Multi-Agent Visual Reasoning0.21Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.17Yunjue Agent Tech Report: A Fully Reproducible, Zero-Start In-Situ Self-Evolving Agent System for Open-Ended Tasks0.15SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.14
Analysis of MetaGen and Its Connection to Prior Work
MetaGen directly extends the “Agentic Reasoning Design Patterns” framework by operationalizing multi-agent collaboration—one of Ng’s identified “emerging technologies”—into a concrete, adaptive system. While Ng’s taxonomy positions multi-agent collaboration as a high-potential but nascent pattern, MetaGen demonstrates how to make it practical through two key innovations that address gaps in the original framework: query-conditioned role generation (moving beyond fixed agent identities) and dynamic topology adjustment (evolving the collaboration structure during execution rather than freezing it at design time). This builds on the reflection and tool-use patterns Ng outlined by embedding feedback loops not just within individual agents, but across the entire agent graph structure. The “lightweight feedback signals” MetaGen uses to iteratively update role prompts echo the reflection pattern’s self-critique mechanism, but extend it to the meta-level of agent composition itself—essentially making the multi-agent system reflect on and refine its own organizational structure.
The paper also provides a training-free implementation pathway for concepts explored in “Automated Multi-Agent Architecture Search” (ADAS/AFlow/EvoFlow). While ADAS frames multi-agent composition as a search problem over a discrete space of workflows and roles, MetaGen shifts from exhaustive search to inference-time generation and pruning. Instead of enumerating candidate configurations offline (as ADAS does with Monte Carlo Tree Search over graph topologies), MetaGen generates role specifications on-demand and constructs a “minimal backbone” execution graph dynamically. This is a crucial architectural difference: ADAS optimizes for task families through upfront search, while MetaGen optimizes for individual queries through runtime adaptation. Both reject static, hand-crafted topologies, but MetaGen’s approach addresses ADAS’s computational cost problem—evaluating every candidate configuration on full benchmarks—by using the query itself as a constraint that narrows the search space before execution begins. The “constrained execution graph” concept represents a middle ground between ADAS’s exhaustive supernet exploration and traditional fixed-topology systems.
Finally, MetaGen aligns philosophically with the “Self-Evolving Agents” survey’s vision of agents that transcend static designs, but focuses specifically on structural and compositional evolution rather than the survey’s broader scope (knowledge acquisition, learning mechanisms, autonomy). The survey identifies “inability to adapt to new tasks” as a fundamental LLM limitation; MetaGen tackles this through its “controllable dynamic role pool” that rewrites agent capabilities mid-inference. Where the self-evolving agents literature emphasizes long-term learning and memory mechanisms, MetaGen achieves adaptation through stateless, query-scoped generation—a more conservative but immediately deployable form of evolution. Its “training-free framework that adapts… without updating base model weights” directly addresses the survey’s concern about bottlenecks in knowledge transfer, demonstrating that meaningful agent evolution can occur at the prompt and topology level without gradient-based learning. This positions MetaGen as a pragmatic first step toward the survey’s ASI-oriented vision: self-modification through organizational plasticity rather than parametric updates.

literature/2026-01-29-PaperSearchQA-Lear.title: PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR
literature/2026-01-29-PaperSearchQA-Lear.content:
PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #4 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RL, LLM, RAG, Search
Abstract
Search agents are language models (LMs) that reason and search knowledge bases (or the web) to answer questions; recent methods supervise only the final answer accuracy using reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). Most RLVR search agents tackle general-domain QA, which limits their relevance to technical AI systems in science, engineering, and medicine. In this work we propose training agents to search and reason over scientific papers — this tests technical question-answering, it is directly relevant to real scientists, and the capabilities will be crucial to future AI Scientist systems. Concretely, we release a search corpus of 16 million biomedical paper abstracts and construct a challenging factoid QA dataset called PaperSearchQA with 60k samples answerable from the corpus, along with benchmarks. We train search agents in this environment to outperform non-RL retrieval baselines; we also perform further quantitative analysis and observe interesting agent behaviors like planning, reasoning, and self-verification. Our corpus, datasets, and benchmarks are usable with the popular Search-R1 codebase for RLVR training and released on huggingface.co/collections/jmhb/papersearchqa. Finally, our data creation methods are scalable and easily extendable to other scientific domains.
Key Techniques

RLVR (Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards)
PaperSearchQA dataset
biomedical paper abstract search corpus
Search-R1 codebase

literature/2026-01-29-S2GR-Stepwise-Sema.title: S$^2$GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation
literature/2026-01-29-S2GR-Stepwise-Sema.content:
S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #10 (score: 0.85, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Generative Recommendation (GR) has emerged as a transformative paradigm with its end-to-end generation advantages. However, existing GR methods primarily focus on direct Semantic ID (SID) generation from interaction sequences, failing to activate deeper reasoning capabilities analogous to those in large language models and thus limiting performance potential. We identify two critical limitations in current reasoning-enhanced GR approaches: (1) Strict sequential separation between reasoning and generation steps creates imbalanced computational focus across hierarchical SID codes, degrading quality for SID codes; (2) Generated reasoning vectors lack interpretable semantics, while reasoning paths suffer from unverifiable supervision. In this paper, we propose stepwise semantic-guided reasoning in latent space (S^2GR), a novel reasoning enhanced GR framework. First, we establish a robust semantic foundation via codebook optimization, integrating item co-occurrence relationship to capture behavioral patterns, and load balancing and uniformity objectives that maximize codebook utilization while reinforcing coarse-to-fine semantic hierarchies. Our core innovation introduces the stepwise reasoning mechanism inserting thinking tokens before each SID generation step, where each token explicitly represents coarse-grained semantics supervised via contrastive learning against ground-truth codebook cluster distributions ensuring physically grounded reasoning paths and balanced computational focus across all SID codes. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of S^2GR, and online A/B test confirms efficacy on large-scale industrial short video platform.
Key Techniques

Codebook optimization with co-occurrence relationships
Stepwise reasoning with thinking tokens
Contrastive learning for semantic supervision
Load balancing and uniformity objectives

literature/2026-01-29-Self-Manager-Paral.title: Self-Manager: Parallel Agent Loop for Long-form Deep Research
literature/2026-01-29-Self-Manager-Paral.content:
Self-Manager: Parallel Agent Loop for Long-form Deep Research
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-25
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, Search
Abstract
Long-form deep research requires multi-faceted investigations over extended horizons to get a comprehensive report. When handling such complex tasks, existing agents manage context at the subtask level to overcome linear context accumulation and information loss. However, they still adhere to a single context window and sequential execution paradigm, which results in mutual interference and blocking behavior, restricting scalability and adaptability. To address this issue, this paper introduces Self-Manager, a parallel agent loop that enables asynchronous and concurrent execution. The main thread can create multiple subthreads, each with its own isolated context, and manage them iteratively through Thread Control Blocks, allowing for more focused and flexible parallel agent execution. To assess its effectiveness, we benchmark Self-Manager on DeepResearch Bench, where it consistently outperforms existing single-agent loop baselines across all metrics. Furthermore, we conduct extensive analytical experiments to demonstrate the necessity of Self-Manager’s design choices, as well as its advantages in contextual capacity, efficiency, and generalization.
Key Techniques

parallel agent loops
asynchronous execution
Thread Control Blocks
isolated context management
