literature/2026-01-29-SuperInfer-SLO-Awa.title: SuperInfer: SLO-Aware Rotary Scheduling and Memory Management for LLM Inference on Superchips
literature/2026-01-29-SuperInfer-SLO-Awa.content:
SuperInfer: SLO-Aware Rotary Scheduling and Memory Management for LLM Inference on Superchips
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #3 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Large Language Model (LLM) serving faces a fundamental tension between stringent latency Service Level Objectives (SLOs) and limited GPU memory capacity. When high request rates exhaust the KV cache budget, existing LLM inference systems often suffer severe head-of-line (HOL) blocking. While prior work explored PCIe-based offloading, these approaches cannot sustain responsiveness under high request rates, often failing to meet tight Time-To-First-Token (TTFT) and Time-Between-Tokens (TBT) SLOs. We present SuperInfer, a high-performance LLM inference system designed for emerging Superchips (e.g., NVIDIA GH200) with tightly coupled GPU-CPU architecture via NVLink-C2C. SuperInfer introduces RotaSched, the first proactive, SLO-aware rotary scheduler that rotates requests to maintain responsiveness on Superchips, and DuplexKV, an optimized rotation engine that enables full-duplex transfer over NVLink-C2C. Evaluations on GH200 using various models and datasets show that SuperInfer improves TTFT SLO attainment rates by up to 74.7% while maintaining comparable TBT and throughput compared to state-of-the-art systems, demonstrating that SLO-aware scheduling and memory co-design unlocks the full potential of Superchips for responsive LLM serving.
Key Techniques

RotaSched
DuplexKV
NVLink-C2C offloading
SLO-aware scheduling

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityOut of the Memory Barrier: A Highly Memory Efficient Training System for LLMs with Million-Token Contexts0.26Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.23Towards Resiliency in Large Language Model Serving with KevlarFlow0.23Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.22Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.21
Analysis: SuperInfer’s Contribution to LLM Inference Understanding
SuperInfer directly addresses the TTFT (Time-To-First-Token) vs. TPOT (Time-Per-Output-Token) trade-off highlighted in “LLM Inference Performance: The Team Mental Model + Experiment Playbook.” While the playbook identifies these as distinct optimization targets for prefill and decode phases, SuperInfer introduces RotaSched, a scheduler that actively manages both SLOs simultaneously by rotating requests between GPU and CPU memory. This is a significant advancement over the note’s observation that systems typically optimize one phase at the expense of the other. The paper validates the playbook’s core insight that “KV-cache reads” dominate decode-phase performance—SuperInfer’s entire architecture (DuplexKV’s full-duplex NVLink-C2C transfers) is designed to minimize the latency penalty of these reads when offloading to CPU memory.
The connection to “SGLang Architecture - PM Perspective” is particularly revealing. SGLang’s RadixAttention (mentioned in the buzzwords section as reusing KV cache across requests) operates entirely within GPU memory, optimizing for cache reuse. SuperInfer complements this with proactive cache rotation: when GPU memory is exhausted, it doesn’t block new requests (HOL blocking) but instead rotates cold KV caches to CPU via NVLink-C2C. This represents a fundamental architectural shift—SGLang assumes GPU memory is the constraint and optimizes around it, while SuperInfer exploits Superchip hardware heterogeneity (tightly coupled GPU-CPU via NVLink) to dynamically expand the effective KV cache pool. For product planning, this means Superchips enable a new class of SLO guarantees that weren’t economically viable on PCIe-based systems.
The paper introduces SLO-aware proactive scheduling as a new dimension beyond the memory/compute trade-offs in existing notes. While “Organizing risks by inference phase” would categorize KV cache exhaustion as a memory risk, SuperInfer shows that the scheduling policy (when to rotate, which requests to prioritize) is equally critical. RotaSched’s proactive rotation—moving KV cache before it’s needed for generation—prevents the cascading TTFT violations that plague reactive systems under load. This adds a crucial perspective missing from the existing knowledge base: hardware-software co-design (exploiting NVLink bandwidth + intelligent scheduling) can achieve SLO attainment rates impossible with either hardware or algorithmic improvements alone. The 74.7% TTFT SLO improvement demonstrates that Superchip-native designs unlock fundamentally different operating points than retrofitting PCIe-based offloading strategies.

literature/2026-01-29-TABED-Test-Time-Ad.title: TABED: Test-Time Adaptive Ensemble Drafting for Robust Speculative Decoding in LVLMs
literature/2026-01-29-TABED-Test-Time-Ad.content:
TABED: Test-Time Adaptive Ensemble Drafting for Robust Speculative Decoding in LVLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #7 (score: 0.90, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Speculative decoding (SD) has proven effective for accelerating LLM inference by quickly generating draft tokens and verifying them in parallel. However, SD remains largely unexplored for Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), which extend LLMs to process both image and text prompts. To address this gap, we benchmark existing inference methods with small draft models on 11 datasets across diverse input scenarios and observe scenario-specific performance fluctuations. Motivated by these findings, we propose Test-time Adaptive Batched Ensemble Drafting (TABED), which dynamically ensembles multiple drafts obtained via batch inference by leveraging deviations from past ground truths available in the SD setting. The dynamic ensemble method achieves an average robust walltime speedup of 1.74x over autoregressive decoding and a 5% improvement over single drafting methods, while remaining training-free and keeping ensembling costs negligible through parameter sharing. With its plug-and-play compatibility, we further enhance TABED by integrating advanced verification and alternative drafting methods. Code and custom-trained models are available at github.com/furiosa-ai/TABED.
Key Techniques

Speculative decoding
Test-time Adaptive Batched Ensemble Drafting (TABED)
dynamic ensemble method
batch inference

literature/2026-01-29-TokenSeek-Memory-E.title: TokenSeek: Memory Efficient Fine Tuning via Instance-Aware Token Ditching
literature/2026-01-29-TokenSeek-Memory-E.content:
TokenSeek: Memory Efficient Fine Tuning via Instance-Aware Token Ditching
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, Search, Transformer
Code: runjia.tech/iclr_tokenseek/
Abstract
Fine tuning has been regarded as a de facto approach for adapting large language models (LLMs) to downstream tasks, but the high training memory consumption inherited from LLMs makes this process inefficient. Among existing memory efficient approaches, activation-related optimization has proven particularly effective, as activations consistently dominate overall memory consumption. Although prior arts offer various activation optimization strategies, their data-agnostic nature ultimately results in ineffective and unstable fine tuning. In this paper, we propose TokenSeek, a universal plugin solution for various transformer-based models through instance-aware token seeking and ditching, achieving significant fine-tuning memory savings (e.g., requiring only 14.8% of the memory on Llama3.2 1B) with on-par or even better performance. Furthermore, our interpretable token seeking process reveals the underlying reasons for its effectiveness, offering valuable insights for future research on token efficiency. Homepage: runjia.tech/iclr_tokenseek/
Key Techniques

instance-aware token seeking
activation optimization
memory-efficient fine-tuning

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityTokenSeek: Memory Efficient Fine Tuning via Instance-Aware Token Ditching0.80Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.12Out of the Memory Barrier: A Highly Memory Efficient Training System for LLMs with Million-Token Contexts0.12FASA: Frequency-aware Sparse Attention0.12Language Model Memory and Memory Models for Language0.12
Analysis
TokenSeek addresses a critical bottleneck that complements the memory-reduction strategies discussed in “Practical Tips for Finetuning LLM Using LoRA.” While LoRA focuses on reducing parameter memory by training low-rank adapter matrices, TokenSeek tackles activation memory—which Sebastian Raschka’s article identifies as a major constraint when finetuning 7B models on single GPUs. The key innovation is TokenSeek’s instance-aware approach: rather than applying uniform optimizations across all training examples (what the paper calls “data-agnostic”), it dynamically identifies and discards uninformative tokens during forward passes. This creates a complementary memory reduction that could stack with LoRA’s parameter efficiency, potentially enabling even larger models or batch sizes on constrained hardware. The 14.8% memory requirement on Llama3.2 1B suggests TokenSeek could push the boundaries of what “Practical Tips” considered feasible for single-GPU finetuning.
The paper’s focus on activation memory optimization directly addresses gaps in existing finetuning guides. “When Fine-Tuning Actually Makes Sense” emphasizes reducing inference cost and latency through finetuning, but TokenSeek extends this logic to the training phase itself—making finetuning more accessible by reducing memory overhead without sacrificing quality. The instance-aware token ditching mechanism also bridges to “LLM Inference Performance,” which discusses KV-cache efficiency during decode. TokenSeek’s interpretable token-seeking process (revealing which tokens are redundant during training) offers insights that could inform inference optimizations: if certain tokens contribute minimally during gradient computation, similar patterns might exist during generation, suggesting new approaches to dynamic KV-cache pruning or speculative decoding strategies.
What distinguishes TokenSeek is its universal plugin architecture and interpretability. Unlike LoRA, which requires architectural modifications (adding adapter layers), or QLoRA’s quantization trade-offs (mentioned in Raschka’s article as causing consistency issues), TokenSeek operates at the token level and claims to work across transformer variants. The interpretable seeking process—showing why certain tokens can be safely discarded—adds a research dimension absent from purely engineering-focused efficiency techniques. This interpretability could inform future work on understanding what linguistic patterns LLMs actually need during training, potentially leading to better data curation strategies that preemptively filter low-signal tokens before training begins, addressing the “multi-epoch overfitting on static datasets” problem Raschka identified.

literature/2026-01-29-Youtu-VL-Unleashin.title: Youtu-VL: Unleashing Visual Potential via Unified Vision-Language Supervision
literature/2026-01-29-Youtu-VL-Unleashin.content:
Youtu-VL: Unleashing Visual Potential via Unified Vision-Language Supervision
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #9 (score: 0.88, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, LLM, RAG
Abstract
Despite the significant advancements represented by Vision-Language Models (VLMs), current architectures often exhibit limitations in retaining fine-grained visual information, leading to coarse-grained multimodal comprehension. We attribute this deficiency to a suboptimal training paradigm inherent in prevailing VLMs, which exhibits a text-dominant optimization bias by conceptualizing visual signals merely as passive conditional inputs rather than supervisory targets. To mitigate this, we introduce Youtu-VL, a framework leveraging the Vision-Language Unified Autoregressive Supervision (VLUAS) paradigm, which fundamentally shifts the optimization objective from vision-as-input'' to vision-as-target.” By integrating visual tokens directly into the prediction stream, Youtu-VL applies unified autoregressive supervision to both visual details and linguistic content. Furthermore, we extend this paradigm to encompass vision-centric tasks, enabling a standard VLM to perform vision-centric tasks without task-specific additions. Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that Youtu-VL achieves competitive performance on both general multimodal tasks and vision-centric tasks, establishing a robust foundation for the development of comprehensive generalist visual agents.
Key Techniques

Vision-Language Unified Autoregressive Supervision (VLUAS)
vision-as-target optimization
unified autoregressive supervision for visual and linguistic tokens

literature/2026-01-29-Yunjue-Agent-Tech-.title: Yunjue Agent Tech Report: A Fully Reproducible, Zero-Start In-Situ Self-Evolving Agent System for Open-Ended Tasks
literature/2026-01-29-Yunjue-Agent-Tech-.content:
Yunjue Agent Tech Report: A Fully Reproducible, Zero-Start In-Situ Self-Evolving Agent System for Open-Ended Tasks
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #10 (score: 0.94, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search
Abstract
Conventional agent systems often struggle in open-ended environments where task distributions continuously drift and external supervision is scarce. Their reliance on static toolsets or offline training lags behind these dynamics, leaving the system’s capability boundaries rigid and unknown. To address this, we propose the In-Situ Self-Evolving paradigm. This approach treats sequential task interactions as a continuous stream of experience, enabling the system to distill short-term execution feedback into long-term, reusable capabilities without access to ground-truth labels. Within this framework, we identify tool evolution as the critical pathway for capability expansion, which provides verifiable, binary feedback signals. Within this framework, we develop Yunjue Agent, a system that iteratively synthesizes, optimizes, and reuses tools to navigate emerging challenges. To optimize evolutionary efficiency, we further introduce a Parallel Batch Evolution strategy. Empirical evaluations across five diverse benchmarks under a zero-start setting demonstrate significant performance gains over proprietary baselines. Additionally, complementary warm-start evaluations confirm that the accumulated general knowledge can be seamlessly transferred to novel domains. Finally, we propose a novel metric to monitor evolution convergence, serving as a function analogous to training loss in conventional optimization. We open-source our codebase, system traces, and evolved tools to facilitate future research in resilient, self-evolving intelligence.
Key Techniques

In-Situ Self-Evolving paradigm
tool evolution
Parallel Batch Evolution strategy
zero-start setting

literature/2026-01-30-ARGORA-Orchestrate.title: ARGORA: Orchestrated Argumentation for Causally Grounded LLM Reasoning and Decision Making
literature/2026-01-30-ARGORA-Orchestrate.content:
ARGORA: Orchestrated Argumentation for Causally Grounded LLM Reasoning and Decision Making
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #7 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, RecSys
Abstract
Existing multi-expert LLM systems gather diverse perspectives but combine them through simple aggregation, obscuring which arguments drove the final decision. We introduce ARGORA, a framework that organizes multi-expert discussions into explicit argumentation graphs showing which arguments support or attack each other. By casting these graphs as causal models, ARGORA can systematically remove individual arguments and recompute outcomes, identifying which reasoning chains were necessary and whether decisions would change under targeted modifications. We further introduce a correction mechanism that aligns internal reasoning with external judgments when they disagree. Across diverse benchmarks and an open-ended use case, ARGORA achieves competitive accuracy and demonstrates corrective behavior: when experts initially disagree, the framework resolves disputes toward correct answers more often than it introduces new errors, while providing causal diagnostics of decisive arguments.
Key Techniques

Argumentation graphs
Causal modeling of arguments
Counterfactual argument removal
Correction mechanism for reasoning alignment

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityARGORA: Orchestrated Argumentation for Causally Grounded LLM Reasoning and Decision Making0.82ARGORA: Orchestrated Argumentation for Causally Grounded LLM Reasoning and Decision Making0.75To Search or Not to Search: Aligning the Decision Boundary of Deep Search Agents via Causal Intervention0.18Unified Multi-Domain Graph Pre-training for Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Graphs via Domain-Specific Expert Encoding0.12STATe-of-Thoughts: Structured Action Templates for Tree-of-Thoughts0.12
Causal Argument Removal & Necessity Analysis
Core Idea: Cast argumentation graphs as causal models and systematically remove individual argument nodes to compute decision stability—identifying which arguments are necessary to maintain the final outcome.
Problem It Solves: Simple confidence scores or attention weights don’t reveal whether a specific argument was necessary for the decision; removing one argument might have no effect while removing another changes the outcome. Standard feature importance methods don’t apply to discrete logical argumentation.
How It Works:

Define a decision function D(G) that maps an argumentation graph G to an output decision (e.g., final answer, confidence)
For each argument node argᵢ in graph G:

Compute baseline decision: d₀ = D(G)
Compute counterfactual decision: d’ = D(G \ {argᵢ}) (remove node and incident edges)
Compute necessity score: necessity(argᵢ) = |d₀ - d’| or binary indicator (did removal change outcome?)


Rank arguments by necessity scores
Identify “critical argument chains”—paths through the graph where all intermediate nodes must be present for the decision to hold

Where It’s Useful:

Model debugging: pinpoint which reasoning steps a system actually depends on
Robustness testing: identify fragile arguments that single-handedly support a conclusion
Decision explanation: generate human-readable summaries of “why” the system chose the answer
Adversarial design: discover which specific arguments could be attacked to flip decisions

Limitations / Caveats:

Computational cost scales with graph size (O(n) graph removals for n arguments)
Binary removal is coarse-grained; cannot capture soft influence or partial contributions
Assumes the decision function D is well-defined and differentiable or at least recomputable
Interdependent arguments may show false negatives (neither is individually necessary but both together are)

Internal Use Cases:

In ranking, identify which features or content signals are “necessary” to rank item A above item B; removes can reveal if ranking is brittle
In personalization, determine which user-history signals are essential to a recommendation vs. redundant signals


Expert Disagreement Correction
Core Idea: When multiple experts produce conflicting outputs, apply a learned or heuristic correction mechanism that aligns the system’s internal reasoning (argumentation) toward the externally correct answer, rather than simply breaking ties.
Problem It Solves: Multi-expert systems often leave disputes unresolved (voting deadlocks) or resolve them arbitrarily. This leaves systematic biases in place and provides no mechanism to learn from cases where the initial expert consensus was wrong.
How It Works:

Identify disagreement cases: instances where expert outputs produce different final decisions
Obtain ground truth label or external feedback for the disputed case
Compare internal argumentation graphs from disagreeing experts:

Extract arguments supporting each outcome
Identify conflicting vs. complementary arguments


Apply correction:

If true answer matches one expert’s conclusion: reweight or amplify supporting arguments from the correct expert
If true answer differs from all experts: identify which argument in the incorrect reasoning chains should be removed or weakened


Update decision aggregation (e.g., reweight expert contributions) or argumentation graph weights to resolve toward correct answer on similar future cases

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Disagreeing<br/>Expert Outputs"] --> B["Extract Argument<br/>Graphs"]
    B --> C["Compare Against<br/>Ground Truth"]
    C --> D["Identify Incorrect<br/>Arguments"]
    D --> E["Adjust Graph Weights<br/>or Expert Weights"]
    E --> F["Corrected<br/>Decision"]

Where It’s Useful:

Learning from failure cases in adversarial or high-stakes domains
Online learning systems where expert feedback is collected gradually
Calibration: reducing systematic overconfidence in expert consensus
Bias mitigation: identifying which argument patterns lead to consistently wrong decisions

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires ground truth labels, which may be expensive or unavailable for open-ended tasks
Correction is local to the disputed case; unclear how well it generalizes to similar but distinct problems
Risk of overcorrection if disagreement is due to legitimate ambiguity rather than error
Assumes a single correct answer exists; breaks down for subjective or multi-valid-answer tasks

Internal Use Cases:

In ranking: when ranker experts disagree, use feedback to correct systematic biases (e.g., consistently overranking certain item types)
In recommendation: when multiple CF or content models conflict, learn which argument patterns predict user satisfaction better


Causal Decision Attribution
Core Idea: Formalize argumentation graphs as causal models to enable structured inference about which argument chains were sufficient for the final decision and what decision would result under targeted argument modifications.
Problem It Solves: Black-box decision models offer no mechanistic insight into causal pathways; even explicit graphs don’t inherently support counterfactual reasoning (“what if this argument were stronger?” or “which combination of arguments alone is sufficient?”).
How It Works:

Translate argumentation DAG into a causal DAG (cDAG):

Each argument node → causal variable with support/attack edges as causal relationships
Reframe edge types: support(A→B) = “A increases likelihood of B,” attack(A→B) = “A decreases likelihood of B”


Define structural causal model (SCM): For each argument node, specify functional form

E.g., argⱼ = f(arg₁, arg₂, …, argₖ, noise) where f aggregates incoming edges


For counterfactual queries, condition on observed argumentation state and simulate:

Do(argᵢ = strengthened): recompute decision D(G with argᵢ amplified)
Do(argᵢ = removed): recompute decision D(G \ {argᵢ})
Do(argᵢ = flipped): invert support/attack of argᵢ’s conclusions


Generate decision counterfactuals: “System would choose X instead of Y if argument Z were absent or reversed”

Where It’s Useful:

Post-hoc explainability: “Your loan was denied because argument A1 (low credit score) attacked the approval chain”
Robustness certification: prove which argument combinations are sufficient to ensure a decision
Policy analysis: “Decision changes under modifications to argument K” → identify policy leverage points
Fairness auditing: identify arguments that are causally responsible for disparity between groups

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires strong assumptions about functional forms of argument aggregation (often unspecified in practice)
Counterfactuals are model-dependent; different SCM specifications yield different counterfactuals
Causal identification is only possible with additional assumptions (no hidden confounders, etc.)
Computational complexity grows with graph complexity; scale to very large argument graphs is unclear

Internal Use Cases:

In ranking: compute counterfactual rankings (“rank would change to X if feature F were strengthened”) for fairness/diversity debugging
In recommendation: causal attribution of which user features or item features drove the recommendation decision

literature/2026-01-30-ASTRA-Automated-Sy.title: ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas
literature/2026-01-30-ASTRA-Automated-Sy.content:
ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #2 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RL, RAG
Code: github.com/LianjiaTech/astra
Abstract
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as tool-augmented agents for multi-step decision making, yet training robust tool-using agents remains challenging. Existing methods still require manual intervention, depend on non-verifiable simulated environments, rely exclusively on either supervised fine-tuning (SFT) or reinforcement learning (RL), and struggle with stable long-horizon, multi-turn learning. To address these challenges, we introduce ASTRA, a fully automated end-to-end framework for training tool-augmented language model agents via scalable data synthesis and verifiable reinforcement learning. ASTRA integrates two complementary components. First, a pipeline that leverages the static topology of tool-call graphs synthesizes diverse, structurally grounded trajectories, instilling broad and transferable tool-use competence. Second, an environment synthesis framework that captures the rich, compositional topology of human semantic reasoning converts decomposed question-answer traces into independent, code-executable, and rule-verifiable environments, enabling deterministic multi-turn RL. Based on this method, we develop a unified training methodology that integrates SFT with online RL using trajectory-level rewards to balance task completion and interaction efficiency. Experiments on multiple agentic tool-use benchmarks demonstrate that ASTRA-trained models achieve state-of-the-art performance at comparable scales, approaching closed-source systems while preserving core reasoning ability. We release the full pipelines, environments, and trained models at github.com/LianjiaTech/astra.
Key Techniques

Tool-call graph synthesis
environment synthesis from QA traces
SFT with online RL integration
trajectory-level reward optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.83ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.83ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.79SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.25Answer First, Reason Later: Aligning Search Relevance via Mode-Balanced Reinforcement Learning0.23
Environment Synthesis via Question-Answer Decomposition
Core Idea: Convert human semantic reasoning patterns from question-answer traces into independent, code-executable, rule-verifiable environments by capturing the compositional structure of multi-turn reasoning.
Problem It Solves: Existing RL methods rely on simulated environments that are non-verifiable or manually crafted, limiting scalability and reproducibility. This provides a bridge from human reasoning traces to deterministic, automated environments suitable for RL.
How It Works:

Decompose human question-answer reasoning traces into atomic reasoning units (each representing a single step, tool invocation, or decision point)
Extract the compositional dependencies between units—which outputs become inputs to subsequent steps, what constraints apply
Convert each decomposed unit into executable code (e.g., Python functions) with embedded verification rules that check correctness against ground truth
Assemble these code-executable units into independent, rule-verifiable environments where deterministic reward signals can be automatically computed

Architecture (if applicable):
flowchart LR
    A["Q&A Reasoning<br/>Traces"] --> B["Decompose into<br/>Atomic Units"]
    B --> C["Extract<br/>Compositional<br/>Dependencies"]
    C --> D["Convert to<br/>Executable Code"]
    D --> E["Embed Verification<br/>Rules"]
    E --> F["Rule-Verifiable<br/>Environments"]

Where It’s Useful:

Training agents with deterministic, reproducible reward signals
Creating verifiable multi-turn dialogue or interaction datasets
Enabling RL on complex, multi-step reasoning tasks with ground truth verification
Building compositional task environments from human demonstrations

Limitations / Caveats:

Decomposition quality depends on clarity of reasoning traces and manual annotation effort
Verification rules are only as accurate as the ground truth provided
Scaling to very long reasoning traces with complex interdependencies can be challenging
Requires domain-specific knowledge to define meaningful verification rules

Internal Use Cases:

Decomposing multi-turn ranking feedback into executable environments for RL-based ranker training
Creating verifiable environments for conversational recommendation interactions with multi-step constraints


Integrated SFT + Online RL with Trajectory-Level Rewards
Core Idea: Unify supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning by using trajectory-level reward signals that balance task completion and interaction efficiency, enabling stable long-horizon multi-turn learning.
Problem It Solves: Existing approaches rely exclusively on either SFT or RL, struggle with stable multi-turn learning, and don’t balance competing objectives (accuracy vs. efficiency). This provides a principled framework that leverages both inductive biases and online optimization.
How It Works:

Warm-start the agent with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on synthesized trajectories to instill broad tool-use competence and stable baseline behavior
Define trajectory-level reward function R(τ) that evaluates complete multi-turn trajectories on both task success metrics and interaction efficiency (e.g., R(τ) = α·task_completion(τ) + β·efficiency(τ))
Collect trajectories during online interaction with the verifiable environments
Apply RL updates (e.g., policy gradient, DPO variants) using the trajectory-level rewards to optimize the agent’s multi-turn behavior
Iterate between online collection and RL updates to refine the policy while maintaining stability through warm-start initialization

Where It’s Useful:

Training multi-turn dialogue and interaction agents
Optimizing agents that must balance competing objectives (accuracy, cost, latency)
Long-horizon decision-making with verifiable outcomes
Transferable agent training across diverse tool-use tasks

Limitations / Caveats:

Warm-start SFT data quality directly impacts RL stability (poor initialization can lead to divergence)
Trajectory-level reward design requires careful tuning of balance weights (α, β)
Exploration-exploitation tradeoff in multi-turn settings can be data-inefficient
Scalability to very long trajectories increases computational cost and variance in reward signals

Internal Use Cases:

Training ranking rankers with both accuracy (NDCG) and efficiency (latency) objectives via trajectory-level rewards
Optimizing multi-turn recommendation policies that balance recommendation diversity and user satisfaction

Discussions & References

huggingface.co
huggingface.co

literature/2026-01-30-Accelerating-Gener.title: Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression
literature/2026-01-30-Accelerating-Gener.content:
Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #7 (score: 0.91, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Although generative recommenders demonstrate improved performance with longer sequences, their real-time deployment is hindered by substantial computational costs. To address this challenge, we propose a simple yet effective method for compressing long-term user histories by leveraging inherent item categorical features, thereby preserving user interests while enhancing efficiency. Experiments on two large-scale datasets demonstrate that, compared to the influential HSTU model, our approach achieves up to a 6x reduction in computational cost and up to 39% higher accuracy at comparable cost (i.e., similar sequence length).
Key Techniques

Long-term user history compression
Categorical feature leveraging
Sequence length reduction
Computational cost optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityAccelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression1.00Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression1.00Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression0.99Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression0.97MixFormer: Co-Scaling Up Dense and Sequence in Industrial Recommenders0.22

literature/2026-01-30-Adaptive-Confidenc.title: Adaptive Confidence Gating in Multi-Agent Collaboration for Efficient and Optimized Code Generation
literature/2026-01-30-Adaptive-Confidenc.content:
Adaptive Confidence Gating in Multi-Agent Collaboration for Efficient and Optimized Code Generation
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #12 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM
Abstract
While Large Language Models (LLMs) have catalyzed breakthroughs in automated code generation, Small Language Models (SLMs) often encounter reasoning bottlenecks and failure loops when addressing complex logical requirements. To overcome these challenges, we propose DebateCoder, a multi-agent collaborative framework designed to improve the reasoning ability of SLMs (e.g., Pangu-1B) in resource-constrained environments. DebateCoder uses a structured role-playing protocol with three agents: User Agent (A_UA), Technical Agent (A_TA), and Quality Assurance Agent (A_QA). It also includes an Adaptive Confidence Gating mechanism with a 95% threshold to balance accuracy and inference efficiency. In addition, we introduce a multi-turn deliberation module and a reviewer-guided analytical debugging loop for orthogonal pre-generation debate and post-generation refinement. Experiments on HumanEval and MBPP show that DebateCoder achieves 70.12% Pass@1 on HumanEval, outperforming MapCoder while reducing API overhead by about 35%. These results indicate that collaborative protocols can mitigate limitations of small-parameter models and provide a scalable, efficient approach to high-quality automated software engineering.
Key Techniques

Multi-agent collaborative framework
Adaptive Confidence Gating
Multi-turn deliberation module
Reviewer-guided analytical debugging loop

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityAdaptive Confidence Gating in Multi-Agent Collaboration for Efficient and Optimized Code Generation0.68Adaptive Confidence Gating in Multi-Agent Collaboration for Efficient and Optimized Code Generation0.66AgentStepper: Interactive Debugging of Software Development Agents0.23Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.22SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.22
Adaptive Confidence Gating (ACG) with 95% Threshold
Core Idea: A filtering mechanism that gates the output of the debate protocol, only accepting final code solutions when the collective confidence of all agents reaches or exceeds a 95% threshold, balancing quality assurance with inference efficiency.
Problem It Solves: Resource-constrained environments cannot afford unlimited multi-turn debates; ACG determines when sufficient confidence has been achieved to stop deliberation and output code, avoiding redundant refinement passes while maintaining solution quality.
How It Works:

After each agent’s contribution, extract or compute a confidence score c_i ∈ [0, 1] representing the agent’s certainty in the current solution state
Aggregate confidence scores from all three agents: C_agg = mean(c_UA, c_TA, c_QA) or weighted combination
Compare aggregate confidence to the 95% threshold: if C_agg ≥ 0.95, gate the solution as “ready for output”
If C_agg < 0.95, trigger another debate round (return to User Agent) to gather additional perspectives
Once threshold is met or maximum iterations reached, output the gated solution and terminate the debate loop

Where It’s Useful:

Inference optimization in resource-constrained code generation pipelines
Early-stopping mechanisms for iterative LLM-based problem solving
Quality control gates in automated software engineering workflows
Scenarios where inference cost must be balanced against solution correctness

Limitations / Caveats:

The 95% threshold is a fixed hyperparameter; optimal threshold may vary by task domain or SLM capability
Confidence scoring methodology is not fully specified in abstract—actual computation (e.g., probability calibration, heuristic scoring) impacts effectiveness
Risk of premature termination if confidence estimation is poorly calibrated for the SLM
Does not account for problem difficulty; same threshold applied to simple and complex problems

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking confidence gates: stopping candidate re-ranking when ensemble scores converge above a threshold
Multi-stage retrieval filtering where confidence gates determine whether additional retrieval passes are needed


Multi-Turn Deliberation Module (Orthogonal Pre-Generation Debate)
Core Idea: A pre-generation debate phase where agents iteratively discuss and refine problem understanding and solution strategy before code is generated, separating problem analysis from implementation.
Problem It Solves: SLMs often begin code generation prematurely without thorough problem decomposition, leading to incorrect implementations; orthogonal debate focuses the model’s reasoning on logical structure before committing to code syntax.
How It Works:

Initial Problem Encoding: User Agent frames the coding problem and extracts key requirements (inputs, outputs, constraints)
Technical Scoping: Technical Agent proposes a solution approach, breaking the problem into sub-tasks or algorithmic strategies
QA Pre-Analysis: QA Agent identifies edge cases, boundary conditions, and potential failure modes of the proposed approach
Deliberation Loop: Agents exchange perspectives for multiple turns (T turns), each refining the solution strategy based on prior feedback
Solution Plan Finalization: Once consensus is reached on the algorithmic approach (via confidence gating or max iterations), proceed to actual code generation with a clear, agreed-upon blueprint

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Coding Problem"] --> B["Turn 1: Problem<br/>Decomposition"]
    B --> C["Turn 2: Strategy<br/>Proposal & Critique"]
    C --> D["Turn 3: Edge Case<br/>Analysis"]
    D --> E{"Consensus &<br/>Confidence OK?"}
    E -->|No| B
    E -->|Yes| F["Code Generation<br/>with Plan"]

Where It’s Useful:

Complex algorithmic problems requiring upfront design before implementation
Scenarios where reasoning transparency is valuable for debugging
Educational contexts where problem decomposition is as important as final code
Multi-step reasoning tasks where intermediate agreement reduces solution space

Limitations / Caveats:

Pre-generation debate adds latency; benefit diminishes on simple problems with obvious solutions
Requires SLMs to articulate intermediate reasoning, which may not align with their pre-training
Quality depends on diversity of agent prompts; identical or similar prompts reduce deliberation value
Pre-generated plans may become outdated if implementation reveals missing constraints

Internal Use Cases:

Multi-stage ranking where initial deliberation aligns on ranking criteria before candidate scoring
Recommendation explanation generation with pre-debate on explanation strategy before text synthesis


Reviewer-Guided Analytical Debugging Loop (Post-Generation Refinement)
Core Idea: A post-code-generation phase where the QA Agent acts as a structured reviewer, identifying bugs and logical errors, and iteratively refining the generated code through targeted debugging guided by analytical error classification.
Problem It Solves: Generated code often contains logical errors, off-by-one mistakes, or incorrect API usage; post-generation debugging loops allow targeted correction without discarding and regenerating the entire solution.
How It Works:

Code Generation: Technical Agent produces initial code implementation based on pre-deliberation plan
QA Review & Error Classification: QA Agent analyzes the code and categorizes identified issues:

Logical errors (algorithm incorrectness)
Runtime errors (exceptions, invalid operations)
Edge case handling (boundary condition failures)


Error Reporting: QA Agent communicates specific failure modes with traced examples
Targeted Refinement: Technical Agent produces a revised code version addressing classified errors
Iterative Verification: QA Agent re-reviews refined code; if issues remain, loop back to step 3
Termination: When code passes QA critique (or max iterations reached), finalize and output

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Initial Code<br/>Generation"] --> B["QA Review<br/>& Classify Bugs"]
    B --> C["Error Report<br/>+ Examples"]
    C --> D["Targeted Code<br/>Refinement"]
    D --> E{"All Issues<br/>Resolved?"}
    E -->|No| B
    E -->|Yes| F["Final Code<br/>Output"]

Where It’s Useful:

Automated code generation pipelines where quality gates require high correctness rates
Iterative debugging in educational programming assistants
Software engineering tasks where partial solutions can be incrementally improved
Test-driven scenarios where error messages guide refinement direction

Limitations / Caveats:

QA Agent must accurately identify real errors without false positives; hallucinated bugs lead to unnecessary refinement
Iterative refinement can degrade code quality if subsequent passes introduce new errors
Effectiveness depends on bug classification accuracy; vague or incorrect error descriptions limit targeted fixes
May enter infinite loops if the same errors recur across iterations (requires detection and escalation)

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking quality assurance where iterative feedback from relevance judges refines candidate scoring models
Recommendation explanation refinement where reviewer feedback guides iterative explanation text improvement

literature/2026-01-30-AgentLongBench-A-C.title: AgentLongBench: A Controllable Long Benchmark For Long-Contexts Agents via Environment Rollouts
literature/2026-01-30-AgentLongBench-A-C.content:
AgentLongBench: A Controllable Long Benchmark For Long-Contexts Agents via Environment Rollouts
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #13 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, RAG, Search
Abstract
The evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) into autonomous agents necessitates the management of extensive, dynamic contexts. Current benchmarks, however, remain largely static, relying on passive retrieval tasks that fail to simulate the complexities of agent-environment interaction, such as non-linear reasoning and iterative feedback. To address this, we introduce AgentLongBench, which evaluates agents through simulated environment rollouts based on Lateral Thinking Puzzles. This framework generates rigorous interaction trajectories across knowledge-intensive and knowledge-free scenarios. Experiments with state-of-the-art models and memory systems (32K to 4M tokens) expose a critical weakness: while adept at static retrieval, agents struggle with the dynamic information synthesis essential for workflows. Our analysis indicates that this degradation is driven by the minimum number of tokens required to resolve a query. This factor explains why the high information density inherent in massive tool responses poses a significantly greater challenge than the memory fragmentation typical of long-turn dialogues.
Key Techniques

Lateral Thinking Puzzles for evaluation
simulated environment rollouts
dynamic information synthesis
token density analysis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityAgentLongBench: A Controllable Long Benchmark For Long-Contexts Agents via Environment Rollouts0.78AgentLongBench: A Controllable Long Benchmark For Long-Contexts Agents via Environment Rollouts0.76ATACompressor: Adaptive Task-Aware Compression for Efficient Long-Context Processing in LLMs0.18MemFly: On-the-Fly Memory Optimization via Information Bottleneck0.17FASA: Frequency-aware Sparse Attention0.17
Token Requirement Analysis (Minimum Resolution Tokens - MRT)
Core Idea: A diagnostic metric that identifies the minimum number of tokens required to resolve a query as the primary driver of agent performance degradation in long-context scenarios.
Problem It Solves: Previous analysis attributed long-context performance degradation to memory fragmentation from long-turn dialogues, but this metric reveals the real bottleneck: high information density in tool responses requiring more tokens to process for task resolution than dialogue length itself.
How It Works:

Query Analysis: For each task instance, extract the minimal token count needed to contain sufficient information for task completion
Comparison: Map this MRT against actual context length used by the agent and performance (success/failure)
Degradation Correlation: Correlate performance drops with queries requiring higher MRT relative to context length
Pattern Classification: Distinguish between failures due to insufficient tokens vs. failures from fragmentation/noise in long-turn dialogues

Where It’s Useful:

Diagnosing why agents fail in long-context scenarios (token density vs. sequence length)
Prioritizing information compression strategies vs. memory architecture improvements
Tuning attention mechanisms to focus on high-density information chunks
Designing context windows that account for information density rather than raw token count

Limitations / Caveats:

MRT is task-specific and may not transfer across puzzle domains
Doesn’t account for task-specific reasoning complexity independent of token density
Assumes information density is measurable/calculable without additional metadata

Internal Use Cases:

Optimizing ranking models for dense information retrieval (e.g., entity-heavy queries)
Designing LLM prompts and system messages to reduce MRT through information prioritization
Context window design for recommendation systems with high-cardinality features

literature/2026-01-30-Bridging-Graph-Str.title: Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning
literature/2026-01-30-Bridging-Graph-Str.content:
Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #6 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Graph, RAG
Abstract
Temporal knowledge graph reasoning (TKGR) aims to predict future events by inferring missing entities with dynamic knowledge structures. Existing LLM-based reasoning methods prioritize contextual over structural relations, struggling to extract relevant subgraphs from dynamic graphs. This limits structural information understanding, leading to unstructured, hallucination-prone inferences especially with temporal inconsistencies. To address this problem, we propose IGETR (Integration of Graph and Editing-enhanced Temporal Reasoning), a hybrid reasoning framework that combines the structured temporal modeling capabilities of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with the contextual understanding of LLMs. IGETR operates through a three-stage pipeline. The first stage aims to ground the reasoning process in the actual data by identifying structurally and temporally coherent candidate paths through a temporal GNN, ensuring that inference starts from reliable graph-based evidence. The second stage introduces LLM-guided path editing to address logical and semantic inconsistencies, leveraging external knowledge to refine and enhance the initial paths. The final stage focuses on integrating the refined reasoning paths to produce predictions that are both accurate and interpretable. Experiments on standard TKG benchmarks show that IGETR achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong baselines with relative improvements of up to 5.6% on Hits@1 and 8.1% on Hits@3 on the challenging ICEWS datasets. Additionally, we execute ablation studies and additional analyses confirm the effectiveness of each component.
Key Techniques

Temporal GNN-based path extraction
LLM-guided path editing
Hybrid graph-language reasoning

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning0.84Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning0.67NeuroSymActive: Differentiable Neural-Symbolic Reasoning with Active Exploration for Knowledge Graph Question Answering0.30LogitsCoder: Towards Efficient Chain-of-Thought Path Search via Logits Preference Decoding for Code Generation0.28RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs0.21
LLM-Guided Path Editing and Refinement
Core Idea: Use LLMs to refine graph-extracted candidate paths by detecting and correcting logical and semantic inconsistencies while leveraging external knowledge to enhance reasoning quality.
Problem It Solves: GNN-extracted paths may contain semantically illogical sequences (e.g., “person graduated university” → “person won Nobel prize” → “person attended high school”) or miss context crucial for prediction. Path editing bridges the gap between structured graph reasoning and semantic understanding.
How It Works:

Input candidate path from GNN stage: [entity_1 —r_1⇒ entity_2 —r_2⇒ … —r_n⇒ entity_n] with context window of temporal facts
Prompt LLM with path, query, and external knowledge (e.g., ontology, commonsense facts) to identify:

Temporal contradictions (e.g., event happens before necessary prerequisite)
Semantic inconsistencies (relations do not naturally follow each other)
Missing intermediate entities or relations needed for logical coherence


LLM generates correction suggestions and rationale explaining why edits improve the path
Apply LLM-suggested edits to produce refined path: remove contradictory segments, reorder relation sequences, or insert missing intermediate entities
Validate edited path against KG structure to ensure consistency

How It Works (Algorithm Detail):

Input: Path P = [(e_1, r_1, e_2), (e_2, r_2, e_3), …, (e_n, r_n, e_{n+1})], query Q = (s, r_query, ?, τ_query)
LLM Call: Encode P and Q into natural language prompt with instruction to identify logical gaps and temporal issues
Output: Sequence of edit operations {insert, remove, reorder, substitute} applied to path with confidence scores
Refinement: Apply edits in order of confidence, re-validating graph consistency after each operation

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Candidate Path<br/>from GNN"] --> B["LLM Inspection<br/>with External<br/>Knowledge"]
    B --> C["Identify<br/>Inconsistencies"]
    C --> D["Generate<br/>Edit Operations"]
    D --> E["Apply Refinements<br/>& Validate"]
    E --> F["Refined Path<br/>(logically coherent)"]

Where It’s Useful:

Correcting multi-hop reasoning paths in QA systems with knowledge graphs
Refining retrieved evidence passages before using them for LLM-based prediction
Improving reasoning chains in complex event understanding with temporal constraints
Semantic validation of entity linking chains in knowledge base construction

Limitations / Caveats:

LLM editing can introduce new hallucinations if external knowledge is incorrect or outdated
Editing decisions are non-transparent; difficult to trace which knowledge source drove a specific refinement
Computationally expensive (per-path LLM inference); scales poorly with large candidate sets
LLM may over-correct, removing valid structural information in pursuit of semantic coherence
Sensitive to prompt engineering; small wording changes can significantly alter editing behavior

Internal Use Cases:

Refining retrieved ranking features in learning-to-rank models before final scoring
Validating retrieved context passages before feeding to LLM ranker for interpretability
Correcting temporal inconsistencies in user behavior sequences during session-based recommendations


Temporal Reasoning Path Integration and Prediction
Core Idea: Synthesize multiple refined reasoning paths from the GNN + LLM pipeline into a unified, interpretable prediction by aggregating evidence and assessing path consistency.
Problem It Solves: Multiple valid paths may support different entity predictions with varying confidence; naive aggregation loses interpretability. Integration must balance path consensus against diversity of valid reasoning while maintaining traceability.
How It Works:

Collect set of refined paths from stage 2: {P_1, P_2, …, P_K} each supporting potential prediction (s, r_query, e_candidate_i, τ_query)
Score each path on: support strength (GNN score + LLM refinement confidence), semantic plausibility, temporal consistency, and evidence diversity
Aggregate path scores using weighted ensemble: each candidate entity e_i receives score S(e_i) = Σ_j weight(P_j) · match(P_j, e_i) where match returns 1 if path P_j supports e_i
Rank candidates by aggregated score and return top-1 prediction with supporting paths for interpretability
Optionally, use path diversity as confidence calibration: predictions supported by diverse reasoning paths are more reliable

Scoring Detail:

Support Strength: Combined GNN structural score + LLM refinement confidence (1-5 scale) normalized to [0,1]
Temporal Consistency: Binary check if path respects τ_query; discounted if timeline ambiguous
Path Diversity Weight: Boost scores for predictions supported by structurally different paths (measured via path graph edit distance)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Refined Paths<br/>P_1, P_2, ..., P_K"] --> B["Score Each Path<br/>Structural + Semantic<br/>+ Temporal"]
    B --> C["Candidate Entity<br/>Evidence Aggregation"]
    C --> D["Rank Candidates<br/>by Aggregated<br/>Score"]
    D --> E["Return Prediction<br/>+ Supporting Paths"]

Where It’s Useful:

Final ranking stage in temporal event prediction with multiple reasoning chains
Interpretable entity linking by returning top paths that justify disambiguation
Confidence estimation in knowledge graph completion by path diversity metrics
Explainable recommendations by surfacing reasoning paths to users

Limitations / Caveats:

Aggregation weights are manually tuned; optimal weighting may be domain-specific
Path diversity metric (graph edit distance) is expensive to compute for large path sets
Predictions supported by few high-confidence paths may be brittle and domain-specific
Interpretability benefit degrades if paths themselves are complex; user-facing explanations require further summarization

Internal Use Cases:

Aggregating multiple ranking signals (structural, semantic, temporal) into final ranking score
Calibrating confidence scores for ranking predictions based on path diversity (more diverse supporting evidence = higher confidence)
Generating explainable ranking justifications by selecting and presenting top supporting paths to users

literature/2026-01-30-Comparing-how-Larg.title: Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery
literature/2026-01-30-Comparing-how-Larg.content:
Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #5 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search, Embeddings, RecSys, RAG
Abstract
This paper evaluates the performance of a large language model (LLM) based semantic search tool relative to a traditional keyword-based search for data discovery. Using real-world search behaviour, we compare outputs from a bespoke semantic search system applied to UKRI data services with the Consumer Data Research Centre (CDRC) keyword search. Analysis is based on 131 of the most frequently used search terms extracted from CDRC search logs between December 2023 and October 2024. We assess differences in the volume, overlap, ranking, and relevance of returned datasets using descriptive statistics, qualitative inspection, and quantitative similarity measures, including exact dataset overlap, Jaccard similarity, and cosine similarity derived from BERT embeddings. Results show that the semantic search consistently returns a larger number of results than the keyword search and performs particularly well for place based, misspelled, obscure, or complex queries. While the semantic search does not capture all keyword based results, the datasets returned are overwhelmingly semantically similar, with high cosine similarity scores despite lower exact overlap. Rankings of the most relevant results differ substantially between tools, reflecting contrasting prioritisation strategies. Case studies demonstrate that the LLM based tool is robust to spelling errors, interprets geographic and contextual relevance effectively, and supports natural-language queries that keyword search fails to resolve. Overall, the findings suggest that LLM driven semantic search offers a substantial improvement for data discovery, complementing rather than fully replacing traditional keyword-based approaches.
Key Techniques

Semantic search using LLMs
Keyword-based search comparison
BERT embeddings for similarity measurement
Natural language query processing

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityComparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.84Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.79Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.77Semantic Search At LinkedIn0.25Semantic Search At LinkedIn, LLM-Driven Autonomous Optimization for Industrial-Scale Recommendation Systems, and More!0.19
Multi-dimensional Evaluation Framework for Search Tool Comparison
Core Idea: Assess search tool performance across four independent dimensions—result volume, set overlap, ranking quality, and semantic relevance—using complementary quantitative metrics rather than a single evaluation score.
Problem It Solves: Traditional search evaluation (e.g., precision/recall on labeled relevance judgments) cannot capture how substantially different two search systems are when they return non-overlapping results that are semantically coherent. Requires a framework that reveals whether differences are due to volume disparity, ranking priorities, or fundamental disagreement on relevance.
How It Works:

Volume Comparison: Report result counts for each tool on identical queries; compute ratio or difference to quantify whether semantic search returns more/fewer results than keyword search
Set Overlap Analysis: Calculate Jaccard Similarity: J(A, B) = |A ∩ B| / |A ∪ B| where A = keyword results, B = semantic results; indicates proportion of shared results vs. tool-specific results
Ranking Divergence: Compare positions of shared datasets in two ranking lists; qualitatively inspect how results are ordered differently (e.g., most relevant result ranked 1st in semantic but 15th in keyword)
Semantic Coherence: Compute cosine similarity between BERT embeddings of datasets returned by each tool and the query embedding; compare mean/distribution of similarity scores to assess whether results are semantically relevant even if they differ

Where It’s Useful:

Comparing alternative retrieval/ranking systems without gold-standard relevance labels
Evaluating hybrid or multi-stage search pipelines
Assessing whether new ranking algorithms improve relevance (not just overlap)
A/B testing search system changes in production
Validating that semantic search results make sense even when they differ from baseline

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires manual or subjective judgment to assess relevance when tools disagree; descriptive statistics alone cannot declare one approach superior
Jaccard similarity favors tools that return fewer results (can be inflated if one tool is overly restrictive)
Ranking divergence is inherently qualitative; requires careful case study inspection rather than automatic scoring
High cosine similarity between query and results doesn’t guarantee practical utility (could match irrelevant datasets that happen to share terminology)
Real-world query logs may not be representative of all use cases; results generalize only to query distributions similar to observed data

Internal Use Cases:

Comparing ranking models in recommender systems when A/B test setup is unavailable
Validating that new embedding models improve semantic coherence of retrieved results
Assessing diversity of results returned by semantic vs. keyword search (semantic may return more redundant results)

literature/2026-01-30-ConceptMoE-Adaptiv.title: ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation
literature/2026-01-30-ConceptMoE-Adaptiv.content:
ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #10 (score: 0.87, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Large language models allocate uniform computation across all tokens, ignoring that some sequences are trivially predictable while others require deep reasoning. We introduce ConceptMoE, which dynamically merges semantically similar tokens into concept representations, performing implicit token-level compute allocation. A learnable chunk module identifies optimal boundaries by measuring inter-token similarity, compressing sequences by a target ratio R before they enter the compute-intensive concept model. Crucially, the MoE architecture enables controlled evaluation: we reallocate saved computation to match baseline activated FLOPs (excluding attention map computation) and total parameters, isolating genuine architectural benefits. Under these conditions, ConceptMoE consistently outperforms standard MoE across language and vision-language tasks, achieving +0.9 points on language pretraining, +2.3 points on long context understanding, and +0.6 points on multimodal benchmarks. When converting pretrained MoE during continual training with layer looping, gains reach +5.5 points, demonstrating practical applicability. Beyond performance, ConceptMoE reduces attention computation by up to R^2times and KV cache by Rtimes. At R=2, empirical measurements show prefill speedups reaching 175% and decoding speedups up to 117% on long sequences. The minimal architectural modifications enable straightforward integration into existing MoE, demonstrating that adaptive concept-level processing fundamentally improves both effectiveness and efficiency of large language models.
Key Techniques

dynamic token merging
learnable chunk boundaries
compute-controlled MoE evaluation
layer looping conversion

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.77ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.74FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.25FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.23FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.20
Learnable Chunk Module
Core Idea: A trainable module that automatically learns to identify semantically meaningful token boundaries by measuring inter-token similarity, enabling data-driven compression without manual heuristics.
Problem It Solves:
Compression requires knowing where to merge tokens. Fixed heuristics (e.g., merge every k tokens) are inflexible. The learnable chunk module discovers task-specific merging patterns during training, adapting to different domains and token distributions.
How It Works:

Similarity Computation: For each position in the sequence, compute similarity scores between adjacent or nearby tokens (e.g., cosine similarity in embedding space, or learned similarity function)
Threshold Learning: A learnable threshold or gating mechanism determines which token pairs/groups are similar enough to merge
Boundary Assignment: Tokens below the threshold are marked as mergeable; boundaries form at positions where similarity drops above threshold
Token Pooling: Mergeable token groups are pooled (e.g., mean, learned weighting) into single concept vectors while maintaining position encoding information

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Token Embeddings<br/>(N × d)"] --> B["Similarity<br/>Scorer"]
    B --> C["Learned<br/>Threshold"]
    C --> D["Boundary<br/>Detection"]
    D --> E["Pooling<br/>Operation"]
    E --> F["Concept<br/>Vectors"]

Where It’s Useful:

Any task where token redundancy patterns are learnable from data
Domain adaptation—module learns compression patterns specific to a domain
Continual training scenarios where compression strategy can be fine-tuned on new data
Multi-task settings where different tasks have different compression needs

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires access to training data and backpropagation; not applicable to inference-only scenarios
Learnable parameters add overhead; must be amortized over many tokens/sequences to justify cost
Similarity computation itself adds latency; must be efficient (e.g., lightweight compared to expert layers)
May overfit to training distribution; compression boundaries learned on one domain may not transfer to out-of-distribution data
No explicit control over compression ratio during forward pass—ratio emerges from learned boundaries, reducing predictability

Internal Use Cases:

Adaptive ranking: Learn which feature tokens can be merged in ranking systems without affecting prediction quality, reducing compute per ranking call
Cold-start handling: For new users/items with sparse histories, learnable chunks could identify and merge noise vs. signal in behavior tokens before expert routing
A/B testing infrastructure: Easy to iterate on compression strategies by retraining the chunk module with different architectures/thresholds


Controlled MoE Evaluation Framework
Core Idea: When comparing ConceptMoE to baseline MoE, reallocate computation saved from token compression back into the MoE layer (more expert diversity, deeper processing) to match baseline activated FLOPs and total parameters, ensuring fair comparison of architectural benefits vs. mere efficiency gains.
Problem It Solves:
Compression naturally improves efficiency because you’re doing less computation. Without controls, it’s unclear if ConceptMoE wins because of the architectural innovation (concept-level processing) or simply because it computes less. This framework isolates the true architectural contribution.
How It Works:

Baseline Measurement: Compute baseline MoE’s activated FLOPs (per-token expert activation costs, excluding attention map computation) and total parameter count
Compression Savings Quantification: Measure FLOPs saved by compressing N tokens to N/R (lower sequence length → fewer expert routing operations)
Reallocation Strategy: Reinvest saved FLOPs back into ConceptMoE by increasing expert capacity, depth, or expert ensemble size until activated FLOPs match baseline
Fair Comparison: Compare ConceptMoE (with reallocated computation) to baseline MoE on performance metrics; any gains are architectural, not computational-budget artifacts

Where It’s Useful:

Benchmarking new MoE variants to isolate true innovation
Paper validation and reproducibility—ensures claims about “better architecture” aren’t confounded with efficiency gains
Research comparison when multiple compression techniques exist—controls for compute budget to identify best design

Limitations / Caveats:

Labor-intensive; requires careful instrumentation to measure FLOPs accurately and fairly
Reallocation options are discrete (add expert, increase hidden dim, etc.), so perfect parameter/FLOP matching may be impossible
Attention computation excluded from FLOP counting; if compression changes attention complexity (fewer tokens → different attention patterns), this isn’t fully captured
Assumes FLOPs are a good proxy for latency; actual speedup depends on hardware efficiency and memory bottlenecks
Results may not generalize to hardware with different computational/memory characteristics (e.g., GPU vs. TPU optimization profiles differ)

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking system audits: When proposing new ranking architectures (e.g., with token-level selection), enforce similar compute budgets to baseline to prove architectural wins
A/B test design: Ensure experimentation groups have matched compute budgets so performance differences reflect model quality, not resource allocation
Efficiency claims validation: When claiming speedups or quality improvements, control for compute budget to separate genuine innovation from “we spend more compute”

literature/2026-01-30-Dont-be-so-Stief-L.title: Don't be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold
literature/2026-01-30-Dont-be-so-Stief-L.content:
Don’t be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #2 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Key—value (KV) caching enables fast autoregressive decoding but at long contexts becomes a dominant bottleneck in High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) capacity and bandwidth. A common mitigation is to compress cached keys and values by projecting per-head matrixes to a lower rank, storing only the projections in the HBM. However, existing post-training approaches typically fit these projections using SVD-style proxy objectives, which may poorly reflect end-to-end reconstruction after softmax, value mixing, and subsequent decoder-layer transformations.   For these reasons, we introduce StiefAttention, a post-training KV-cache compression method that learns \emph{orthonormal} projection bases by directly minimizing \emph{decoder-layer output reconstruction error}. StiefAttention additionally precomputes, for each layer, an error-rank profile over candidate ranks, enabling flexible layer-wise rank allocation under a user-specified error budget. Noteworthy, on Llama3-8B under the same conditions, StiefAttention outperforms EigenAttention by 11.9 points on C4 perplexity and 5.4\% on 0-shot MMLU accuracy at iso-compression, yielding lower relative error and higher cosine similarity with respect to the original decoder-layer outputs.
Key Techniques

KV-cache compression
orthonormal projection bases
decoder-layer output reconstruction
layer-wise rank allocation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDon’t be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold0.83Don’t be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold0.73SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.18SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.17WildCat: Near-Linear Attention in Theory and Practice0.17
Error-Rank Profile Computation
Core Idea: Precompute a lookup table of reconstruction errors for each layer and rank pair, enabling post-hoc flexible rank allocation without retraining.
Problem It Solves: Standard compression methods require choosing a single rank for all layers, or retraining for each rank configuration. This is inflexible when different layers have different sensitivity to compression. An error-rank profile decouples layer-wise rank selection from end-to-end training, allowing dynamic allocation under a global error budget.
How It Works:


Train projections for multiple ranks: Using StiefAttention, optimize projection bases U_r for several candidate ranks r = {r_1, r_2, …, r_max} (e.g., r ∈ {8, 16, 32, 64}) at each layer L.


Measure layer-wise reconstruction error: For each (layer L, rank r) pair, compute the reconstruction error ε_{L,r} = ||output_L(original) - output_L(compressed with rank r)||.


Store error profile: Build a lookup table mapping (layer_id, rank) → reconstruction error.


Greedy or DP rank allocation: Given a global error budget E_total, select layer-wise ranks to maximize compression (minimize total rank sum) such that Σ_{L} ε_{L,r_L} ≤ E_total. Use dynamic programming or greedy assignment (e.g., compress high-error-per-rank layers more aggressively).


Where It’s Useful:

Adaptive inference systems with varying latency/memory constraints
Serving scenarios where error budgets change per request
Understanding which layers benefit most from compression
Layer-specific pruning strategies in broader model compression pipelines

Limitations / Caveats:

Error additivity assumption (Σ ε_L ≈ total error) may not hold perfectly due to error compounding through layers
Requires storing multiple trained projections (one per rank), increasing post-training storage
Greedy rank selection is suboptimal; true optimal allocation is NP-hard, but profile enables quick approximation

Internal Use Cases:

Ranker budgeting: In listwise ranking, allocate lower ranks to early transformer layers (lower information density) and higher ranks to final layers (decision-critical) under a fixed compression budget
Cascade models: Use profiles to compress early/cheap ranker layers more aggressively than final expensive rankers
A/B testing: Experiment with different error budgets quickly by reusing precomputed profiles without retraining

literature/2026-01-30-DynaWeb-Model-Base.title: DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents
literature/2026-01-30-DynaWeb-Model-Base.content:
DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RL, RecSys
Abstract
The development of autonomous web agents, powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) and reinforcement learning (RL), represents a significant step towards general-purpose AI assistants. However, training these agents is severely hampered by the challenges of interacting with the live internet, which is inefficient, costly, and fraught with risks. Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) offers a promising solution by learning a world model of the environment to enable simulated interaction. This paper introduces DynaWeb, a novel MBRL framework that trains web agents through interacting with a web world model trained to predict naturalistic web page representations given agent actions. This model serves as a synthetic web environment where an agent policy can dream by generating vast quantities of rollout action trajectories for efficient online reinforcement learning. Beyond free policy rollouts, DynaWeb incorporates real expert trajectories from training data, which are randomly interleaved with on-policy rollouts during training to improve stability and sample efficiency. Experiments conducted on the challenging WebArena and WebVoyager benchmarks demonstrate that DynaWeb consistently and significantly improves the performance of state-of-the-art open-source web agent models. Our findings establish the viability of training web agents through imagination, offering a scalable and efficient way to scale up online agentic RL.
Key Techniques

Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL)
web world model
synthetic web environment simulation
expert trajectory interleaving

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.82DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.82DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.73In-Context Reinforcement Learning From Suboptimal Historical Data0.24ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.21
Dreaming with Interleaved On-Policy and Expert Rollouts (Dyna-Style Learning)
Core Idea: Interleave simulated rollouts from the learned world model with real expert trajectories during RL policy training, combining model-based imagination with offline learning to improve sample efficiency and training stability.
Problem It Solves: Pure model-based rollouts suffer from compounding prediction errors; pure offline learning from expert data lacks exploration. Interleaving both provides stability while allowing the agent to explore beyond expert behavior through the learned model.
How It Works:

Initialize replay buffer with expert trajectories from training data (e.g., human demonstrations or logged agent interactions)
During each RL training iteration, alternate between:

Real rollouts: Sample trajectories from the replay buffer (offline expert data)
Imagined rollouts: Use the learned world model to generate synthetic trajectories by rolling out the current policy in imagination


Both real and imagined trajectories are used to compute policy gradients (e.g., via actor-critic, PPO, or DQN variants)
Update the policy to maximize expected returns across both real and simulated environments
Periodically collect new real interactions to refine the world model (though paper emphasizes pre-trained model usage)

Architecture:
flowchart TD
    A["Expert Trajectories<br/>(Offline)"] --> B["Replay Buffer"]
    C["Learned World Model"] --> D["Policy Rollout<br/>in Imagination"]
    B --> E["RL Policy Learner"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Updated Policy"]
    F --> G["Evaluate on<br/>Real Environment"]

Where It’s Useful:

Web agent training where real interactions are expensive
Offline RL scenarios with pre-collected human expert demonstrations
Any domain with a learnable transition model and access to expert data
Training agents for high-risk environments (autonomous driving, manipulation)

Limitations / Caveats:

Expert trajectory quality directly impacts policy learning (garbage in, garbage out)
Requires careful balance between real and imagined rollouts—too much imagination amplifies model errors
The learned world model must be reasonably accurate for this to outperform pure behavioral cloning
Does not fully solve the exploration problem; imagined rollouts are still constrained by world model accuracy

Internal Use Cases:

Could combine logged ranking interactions with learned CTR models to train new rankers offline
Applicable to learning new recommendation policies using historical user behavior + learned engagement model
Offline RL for search ranking without production traffic impact


Online Policy Training in Simulated Environment
Core Idea: Train a web agent policy using standard RL algorithms (e.g., PPO, DQN) entirely within the learned world model, treating it as a fully simulated environment with reward signals derived from task success metrics.
