Problem It Solves: Enables efficient online RL training with dense rollout generation without touching the live web. The policy can explore, learn, and iterate at scale within the simulation before deployment.
How It Works:

Define a reward function for web tasks (e.g., binary success/failure, element-reaching penalties, action count)
Initialize policy πθ with pre-trained or random weights
For each training iteration:

Use the learned world model as the environment: s_{t+1} = WorldModel(s_t, a_t)
Roll out the policy in the simulation to generate trajectories: τ = {(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1})}
Compute advantages and policy gradients using standard RL methods
Update policy parameters: θ ← θ + α∇J(θ)


Optionally interleave with real data collection at test time to refine both model and policy

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Policy πθ"] --> B["Action a_t"]
    C["World Model"] --> D["Next State s_{t+1}"]
    B --> C
    D --> E["Reward Function"]
    E --> F["Gradient Computation"]
    F --> G["Policy Update"]
    G --> A

Where It’s Useful:

Training policies for complex web navigation tasks at scale
Rapid experimentation with different policy architectures
Curriculum learning: gradually increase task difficulty within simulation
Transfer learning: pre-train in simulation, fine-tune on real environment

Limitations / Caveats:

Reality gap: Policies trained purely in simulation may fail on real websites due to distribution shift
Reward function design is critical and non-trivial for complex web tasks
Compounding errors in long-horizon tasks cause cumulative performance degradation
May require fine-tuning on real tasks despite simulation pre-training

Internal Use Cases:

Training ranking policies with simulated user feedback (learned CTR/engagement models)
Offline bandit policy optimization using learned environment dynamics
Testing new recommendation algorithms efficiently without online A/B tests


These three techniques are the core methodological contributions of DynaWeb, working together to enable scalable, safe, and efficient training of web agents through model-based reinforcement learning.
Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-01-30-ECO-Quantized-Trai.title: ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights
literature/2026-01-30-ECO-Quantized-Trai.content:
ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #8 (score: 0.90, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Quantization has significantly improved the compute and memory efficiency of Large Language Model (LLM) training. However, existing approaches still rely on accumulating their updates in high-precision: concretely, gradient updates must be applied to a high-precision weight buffer, known as \textit{master weights}. This buffer introduces substantial memory overhead, particularly for Sparse Mixture of Experts (SMoE) models, where model parameters and optimizer states dominate memory usage. To address this, we introduce the Error-Compensating Optimizer (ECO), which eliminates master weights by applying updates directly to quantized parameters. ECO quantizes weights after each step and carefully injects the resulting quantization error into the optimizer momentum, forming an error-feedback loop with no additional memory. We prove that, under standard assumptions and a decaying learning rate, ECO converges to a constant-radius neighborhood of the optimum, while naive master-weight removal can incur an error that is inversely proportional to the learning rate. We show empirical results for pretraining small Transformers (30-800M), a Gemma-3 1B model, and a 2.1B parameter Sparse MoE model with FP8 quantization, and fine-tuning DeepSeek-MoE-16B in INT4 precision. Throughout, ECO matches baselines with master weights up to near-lossless accuracy, significantly shifting the static memory vs validation loss Pareto frontier.
Key Techniques

Error-Compensating Optimizer (ECO)
FP8/INT4 quantization
error-feedback loop with momentum injection
master weight elimination

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.76ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.75ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.74Quantized Evolution Strategies: High-precision Fine-tuning of Quantized LLMs at Low-precision Cost0.26Quartet II: Accurate LLM Pre-Training in NVFP4 by Improved Unbiased Gradient Estimation0.21
Quantization Error Feedback Mechanism
Core Idea: Capture the rounding error introduced by quantizing parameter updates and systematically feed this error back into the optimizer’s momentum buffer to correct for optimization drift.
Problem It Solves: Quantizing weights introduces systematic rounding errors that accumulate over training steps, causing the optimizer to descend toward a perturbed optimum rather than the true loss surface minimum. Ignoring this error breaks convergence properties; simply reducing learning rate is inefficient.
How It Works:

Ideal vs. Quantized Update: Compute what the parameter should be after update (W_t + \Delta W_t) versus what it actually becomes after quantization (\text{dequant}(\text{quant}(W_t + \Delta W_t))).
Error Signal: Calculate error term \Delta_t = (W_t + \Delta W_t) - \text{dequant}(\text{quant}(W_t + \Delta W_t)).
Momentum Injection: Add error directly to next step’s effective gradient in momentum update: m_{t+1} = \beta m_t + (1-\beta)g_t + \Delta_t.
Error Propagation: The feedback loop ensures uncompensated error from round t influences the update direction in round t+1, preventing error accumulation.

Where It’s Useful:

Any quantized optimizer where per-step weight changes are accumulated (AdamW, SGD with momentum)
Post-training quantization scenarios where weights drift from training distribution
Low-bit quantization (FP8, INT4, INT8) where rounding errors are larger relative to update magnitudes
Distributed training where gradient accumulation across steps must remain stable

Limitations / Caveats:

Error computation itself can introduce numerical instability if quantization scheme is poorly designed
Requires storing error feedback buffer (negligible cost compared to master weights, but non-zero)
Effectiveness depends on quantization granularity—per-channel quantization may require per-channel error tracking
Error feedback must be scaled appropriately; wrong scaling factor can amplify numerical noise

Internal Use Cases:

Compensating for FP8 quantization errors in gradient accumulation steps for large batch training
Maintaining optimizer stability across distributed training steps where quantized parameter updates are synchronized
Improving convergence of sparse model training where weight changes are localized to specific experts

literature/2026-01-30-EWSJF-An-Adaptive-.title: EWSJF: An Adaptive Scheduler with Hybrid Partitioning for Mixed-Workload LLM Inference
literature/2026-01-30-EWSJF-An-Adaptive-.content:
EWSJF: An Adaptive Scheduler with Hybrid Partitioning for Mixed-Workload LLM Inference
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #11 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG
Code: anonymous.4open.science/r/vllm_0110-32D8
Abstract
Serving Large Language Models (LLMs) under mixed workloads—short, latency-sensitive interactive queries alongside long, throughput-oriented batch requests—poses a fundamental scheduling challenge. Standard First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) policies suffer from severe head-of-line blocking, leading to high tail latency and underutilized hardware. We introduce EWSJF (Effective Workload-based Shortest Job First), an adaptive request-level scheduler that learns workload structure in real time to jointly improve fairness and throughput. EWSJF operates upstream of execution-level schedulers and integrates four components: (1) Refine-and-Prune, an unsupervised partitioning algorithm that discovers performance-homogeneous request groups; (2) Dynamic Queue Routing for assigning requests to these groups; (3) Density-Weighted Scoring, a context-aware prioritization function balancing urgency and fairness; and (4) Bayesian Meta-Optimization, which continuously tunes scoring and partitioning parameters based on live performance feedback. Implemented in vLLM, EWSJF improves end-to-end throughput by over 30% and reduces average Time-To-First-Token for short requests by up to 4x compared to FCFS. These results demonstrate that adaptive, learning-based request scheduling is a critical missing layer for efficient and responsive LLM serving. Implementation available at anonymous.4open.science/r/vllm_0110-32D8.
Key Techniques

Refine-and-Prune unsupervised partitioning
Dynamic Queue Routing
Density-Weighted Scoring
Bayesian Meta-Optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityEWSJF: An Adaptive Scheduler with Hybrid Partitioning for Mixed-Workload LLM Inference0.80EWSJF: An Adaptive Scheduler with Hybrid Partitioning for Mixed-Workload LLM Inference0.66Towards Resiliency in Large Language Model Serving with KevlarFlow0.19SuperInfer: SLO-Aware Rotary Scheduling and Memory Management for LLM Inference on Superchips0.17Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.15
Dynamic Queue Routing
Core Idea: A request assignment mechanism that dynamically maps incoming requests to the performance-homogeneous queues discovered by Refine-and-Prune, adapting to partition changes in real time.
Problem It Solves: Once partitions are discovered, requests must be assigned to the correct queue efficiently. Static routing rules become stale as workloads evolve; this technique ensures requests reach the queue matching their performance characteristics without manual re-configuration.
How It Works:

For each incoming request, extract feature vector f (prompt length, batch size, request type, etc.)
Compute similarity score between f and each active partition’s centroid or representative features
Assign request to partition with highest similarity (or confidence above threshold)
As Refine-and-Prune updates partitions, re-evaluate routing assignments for in-flight requests when beneficial
Fall back to default queue if no partition match with sufficient confidence

Where It’s Useful:

Request routing in multi-queue scheduling systems
Online classification of requests into performance-homogeneous buckets
Adaptive load balancing across heterogeneous servers or execution engines

Limitations / Caveats:

Routing quality depends on feature extraction; low-quality features lead to misclassification
Re-routing in-flight requests may incur synchronization overhead
May not handle requests with novel feature combinations well until Refine-and-Prune adapts

Internal Use Cases:

Query routing in recommendation systems (which ranking branch gets this query?)
LLM request classification for specialized serving hardware (GPU vs. CPU inference)


Density-Weighted Scoring
Core Idea: A context-aware prioritization function that balances urgency (short requests needing low latency) and fairness (preventing starvation) by weighting request priority inversely with queue density.
Problem It Solves: Standard prioritization schemes (FCFS, SJF) either starve long requests or block short ones. This technique jointly optimizes for both latency-sensitive and throughput-oriented workloads by modulating priority based on real-time queue congestion.
How It Works:

For request r in partition P, compute base priority score: priority_base(r) = urgency(r) / length(r) (e.g., inverse job length for SJF-like behavior)
Compute partition density: density(P) = |queue(P)| / capacity(P) (ratio of requests to nominal queue capacity)
Apply density weighting: priority_final(r) = priority_base(r) × (1 + α × density(P)) where α ≥ 0 controls sensitivity
When density is high (many pending requests of same type), urgency is amplified; when low, prioritization is relaxed
Rank requests globally by priority_final score; schedule highest-priority request next

Where It’s Useful:

Fair scheduling in overloaded systems with heterogeneous job sizes
Latency-throughput trade-off management in batch + interactive workloads
Priority queue systems where context (current load) should influence individual decisions

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires accurate capacity estimation for each partition
Hyperparameter α must be tuned; poor choices lead to either starvation or underutilization
Does not account for priority inversions in nested queue hierarchies

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking blending: modulate ranker diversity based on query traffic density
LLM serving: prioritize user-facing queries when system is underutilized, balance fairness when saturated


Bayesian Meta-Optimization
Core Idea: A continuous tuning loop that uses Bayesian optimization to adapt EWSJF’s scoring and partitioning parameters (e.g., density weighting coefficient, partition merge thresholds) based on live system performance feedback.
Problem It Solves: Refine-and-Prune, Dynamic Queue Routing, and Density-Weighted Scoring all depend on hyperparameters that are workload- and hardware-dependent. Manual tuning is brittle; this technique automatically discovers near-optimal parameters by observing end-to-end performance metrics.
How It Works:

Define hyperparameter space: θ = {α (density weight), merge_threshold, prune_threshold, split_variance_threshold, …}
Initialize with default values or prior distribution
Execute scheduler with current θ, collect metrics: M = {avg_TTFT (Time-To-First-Token), throughput, tail latency, fairness_score}
Fit Gaussian Process (GP) surrogate model: M̂(θ) ~ GP(μ(θ), σ²(θ)) using historical performance observations
Use acquisition function (e.g., Upper Confidence Bound, Expected Improvement) to select next θ to evaluate
Deploy new θ, observe true performance, update GP model
Repeat steps 3-6 continuously; frequency depends on workload stability (e.g., every 5–30 minutes)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Live Metrics<br/>TTFT, Throughput"] --> B["GP Surrogate<br/>Model"]
    B --> C["Acquisition<br/>Function"]
    C --> D["Propose θ'"]
    D --> E["Deploy θ'<br/>in Scheduler"]
    E --> A

Where It’s Useful:

Hyperparameter tuning for any system with expensive, noisy, high-dimensional reward functions
Online optimization of multi-objective objectives (latency vs. throughput) without manual A/B testing
Systems where optimal parameters drift due to hardware degradation or workload shifts

Limitations / Caveats:

GP modeling assumes smoothness in the parameter-performance landscape; non-smooth objectives may be misfit
Exploration overhead: tuning parameters can temporarily degrade production performance
Does not handle discrete parameter changes efficiently (e.g., switching between partitioning algorithms)
Requires significant historical data to build accurate GP models; cold start can be slow

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking system tuning: automatically optimize diversity, freshness, and CTR trade-off weights
LLM serving: adapt scheduling parameters to gradual hardware changes or new model deployments
RecSys: optimize recommendation blending ratios as user behavior evolves

literature/2026-01-30-FBS-Modeling-Nativ.title: FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer
literature/2026-01-30-FBS-Modeling-Nativ.content:
FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #3 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, Transformer
Abstract
Large language models (LLMs) excel across many tasks, yet inference is still dominated by strictly token-by-token autoregression. Existing acceleration methods largely patch this pipeline and miss core human-reading ingredients: content-adaptive foresight, chunk-structure-aware compute allocation, and train—test consistency for preview/skimming. We propose the \textbf{Fovea-Block-Skip Transformer} (FBS), which injects a causal, trainable loop into Transformers via Parafovea-Attention Window (PAW), Chunk-Head (CH), and Skip-Gate (SG). Across diverse benchmarks, FBS improves the quality-efficiency trade-off without increasing parameters, and ablations show the three modules are complementary.
Key Techniques

Parafovea-Attention Window
Chunk-Head
Skip-Gate
trainable causal loop

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityFBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.67FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.67ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.24Light Forcing: Accelerating Autoregressive Video Diffusion via Sparse Attention0.23ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.23
Chunk-Head (CH)
Core Idea: Organize multiple attention heads into groups that process semantic chunks of the input sequence, enabling hierarchical attention between local intra-chunk and sparse inter-chunk connections.
Problem It Solves: Balances the need for local context modeling within coherent text segments with the computational cost of attending across all tokens; enables chunk-aware compute allocation.
How It Works:

Segment the input sequence into contiguous chunks (e.g., sentences or fixed-length windows) based on content structure
Assign groups of attention heads to process chunks: some heads focus on intra-chunk attention (within a chunk)
Allocate remaining heads to compute sparse inter-chunk attention (between chunk representatives)
Combine intra-chunk and inter-chunk head outputs for the final token representations

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Sequence Divided<br/>into Chunks"] --> B["Intra-Chunk Heads<br/>Local attention"]
    A --> C["Inter-Chunk Heads<br/>Sparse attention"]
    B --> D["Merge Head Outputs"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Hierarchical<br/>Representations"]

Where It’s Useful:

Document-level processing where natural chunks (paragraphs, sections) exist
Multi-hop reasoning tasks where answers span across different semantic segments
Efficient processing of long documents while preserving both local and coarse-grained structure
Training-test consistency when chunk boundaries are known at inference time

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires knowledge of where chunk boundaries occur; performance degrades if chunks don’t align with semantic content
Chunk-based organization may miss dependencies that cross chunk boundaries unpredictably
Adds architectural complexity compared to standard attention; requires careful head allocation strategy
Effectiveness depends on matching chunk size to the task’s semantic structure

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking documents by relevance: use chunks to represent different aspects/sections of documents
Feed ranking: chunk-wise processing allows separate scoring of different types of items in a candidate set


Skip-Gate (SG)
Core Idea: Learn a differentiable gating function that dynamically scores token importance and masks low-importance tokens from attention computation, enabling content-adaptive foresight.
Problem It Solves: Not all tokens contribute equally to understanding; redundant or low-signal tokens waste computation. Existing acceleration methods don’t learn what to skip.
How It Works:

For each token position, compute a gate score g(t) ∈ [0, 1] using a learned function (e.g., small MLP applied to token representations)
During forward pass, apply the gate scores as a mask: tokens with g(t) < threshold are effectively skipped
Train the gate function end-to-end via gradient descent; the gate learns which tokens are most informative for downstream predictions
Skip-Gate reduces the effective sequence length by pruning low-gate-score positions before computing attention

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Token Representations"] --> B["Gate Function<br/>g(t) = MLP(token)"]
    B --> C["Gate Score<br/>per token"]
    C --> D{"Apply<br/>Threshold"}
    D -->|High Score| E["Keep Token"]
    D -->|Low Score| F["Skip Token"]
    E --> G["Reduced Sequence<br/>to Attention"]
    F --> G

Where It’s Useful:

Inference acceleration where tokens can be selectively pruned without sacrificing quality
Modeling content-adaptive processing where different texts require different “reading depth”
Adaptive computation for variable-length inputs with different redundancy levels
Tasks with high-frequency tokens or stop-words that don’t carry semantic weight

Limitations / Caveats:

Gate function adds learnable parameters and forward-pass computation overhead (though typically small)
Differentiable gating (soft masks) must be used during training; discrete hard skipping introduces non-differentiability
May skip important tokens for rare/OOD examples if training distribution doesn’t cover them
Training-test gap: gates trained on full sequences may behave differently when tokens are actually removed at inference

Internal Use Cases:

LLM inference optimization: skip high-frequency or low-information-content tokens (e.g., “the”, “is”) dynamically
Feed ranking: learn to skip feature embeddings that don’t contribute to ranking quality (adaptive feature dropping)

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-01-30-Generative-Chain-o.title: Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction
literature/2026-01-30-Generative-Chain-o.content:
Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #9 (score: 0.89, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Modeling long-term user behavior trajectories is essential for understanding evolving preferences and enabling proactive recommendations. However, most sequential recommenders focus on next-item prediction, overlooking dependencies across multiple future actions. We propose Generative Chain of Behavior (GCB), a generative framework that models user interactions as an autoregressive chain of semantic behaviors over multiple future steps. GCB first encodes items into semantic IDs via RQ-VAE with k-means refinement, forming a discrete latent space that preserves semantic proximity. On top of this space, a transformer-based autoregressive generator predicts multi-step future behaviors conditioned on user history, capturing long-horizon intent transitions and generating coherent trajectories. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that GCB consistently outperforms state-of-the-art sequential recommenders in multi-step accuracy and trajectory consistency. Beyond these gains, GCB offers a unified generative formulation for capturing user preference evolution.
Key Techniques

Semantic ID encoding via RQ-VAE with k-means refinement
Autoregressive chain modeling for multi-step behavior prediction
Transformer-based trajectory generation
Discrete latent space with semantic proximity preservation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityGenerative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.84Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.83Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.83GLASS: A Generative Recommender for Long-sequence Modeling via SID-Tier and Semantic Search0.30DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.26
Autoregressive Chain of Behavior Generation
Core Idea: Use a transformer-based autoregressive decoder to predict multi-step future user behaviors as a sequence of semantic tokens conditioned on user interaction history, enabling coherent long-horizon trajectory generation.
Problem It Solves: Existing sequential recommenders predict only the next item, ignoring dependencies and intent transitions across multiple future steps. This limits the ability to understand evolving user preferences and generate coherent multi-step behavioral trajectories that respect long-term intent.
How It Works:

Encode user history H = (s₁, s₂, …, sₜ) where each sᵢ is a semantic ID obtained from the RQ-VAE quantization
Create positional embeddings and sum with semantic token embeddings: hᵢ = Embed(sᵢ) + PosEmbed(i)
Pass through transformer encoder to build contextual history representation: C = Transformer_Encoder(**h₁, …, hₜ)
Autoregressively generate future behaviors by predicting one semantic token at a time: P(sₜ₊₁, sₜ₊₂, …, sₜ₊ₙ | C) = ∏ᵢ P(sₜ₊ᵢ | C, sₜ₊₁:ₜ₊ᵢ₋₁)
At each step, sample or argmax to obtain the next semantic ID and append to sequence
Convert final semantic IDs back to actual items for the final trajectory prediction

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["User History<br/>(semantic IDs)"] --> B["Embedding +<br/>Positional Encoding"]
    B --> C["Transformer<br/>Encoder"]
    C --> D["Context<br/>Representation"]
    D --> E["Autoregressive<br/>Decoder"]
    E --> F["Predicted<br/>Trajectory<br/>(multi-step)"]

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-step recommendation systems requiring trajectory-level coherence
Long-horizon user intent modeling (e.g., session-based recommendations spanning multiple interactions)
Preference evolution analysis where capturing intent transitions matters
Conversational or sequential recommendation systems generating multi-turn interactions
User journey prediction for marketing and retention applications

Limitations / Caveats:

Autoregressive generation is slow at inference—must generate tokens sequentially rather than in parallel
Error accumulation during multi-step generation (early mistakes propagate to later predictions)
Exposure bias during training—the model sees ground truth at training time but its own predictions at test time
Requires learning long-range dependencies which can be challenging with standard transformers on very long horizons
Quality degrades significantly for very distant future steps (t+n where n is large)

Internal Use Cases:

Predicting user behavior chains in RecSys to enable proactive recommendations before users explicitly request them
Ranking multi-step user intents for display optimization (show items that fit the predicted chain, not just the next step)
Cold-start user modeling by generating realistic trajectory patterns from small interaction histories
A/B testing long-horizon recommendation strategies where multi-step coherence is the evaluation metric

literature/2026-01-30-Heterogeneous-Comp.title: Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference
literature/2026-01-30-Heterogeneous-Comp.content:
Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #1 (score: 0.71, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
AI agent inference is driving an inference heavy datacenter future and exposes bottlenecks beyond compute - especially memory capacity, memory bandwidth and high-speed interconnect. We introduce two metrics - Operational Intensity (OI) and Capacity Footprint (CF) - that jointly explain regimes the classic roofline analysis misses, including the memory capacity wall. Across agentic workflows (chat, coding, web use, computer use) and base model choices (GQA/MLA, MoE, quantization), OI/CF can shift dramatically, with long context KV cache making decode highly memory bound. These observations motivate disaggregated serving and system level heterogeneity: specialized prefill and decode accelerators, broader scale up networking, and decoupled compute-memory enabled by optical I/O. We further hypothesize agent-hardware co design, multiple inference accelerators within one system, and high bandwidth, large capacity memory disaggregation as foundations for adaptation to evolving OI/CF. Together, these directions chart a path to sustain efficiency and capability for large scale agentic AI inference.
Key Techniques

Operational Intensity (OI) and Capacity Footprint (CF) metrics
disaggregated serving architecture
specialized prefill and decode accelerators
agent-hardware co-design

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHeterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.82Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.77PrefillShare: A Shared Prefill Module for KV Reuse in Multi-LLM Disaggregated Serving0.41SuperInfer: SLO-Aware Rotary Scheduling and Memory Management for LLM Inference on Superchips0.22Out of the Memory Barrier: A Highly Memory Efficient Training System for LLMs with Million-Token Contexts0.20
Disaggregated Inference Serving with Specialized Accelerators
Core Idea: Decouple prefill (high OI, compute-bound) and decode (low OI, memory-bound) onto specialized hardware optimized for their distinct computational and memory access patterns.
Problem It Solves: Monolithic inference accelerators designed for balanced compute-memory utilization waste resources—prefill stages underutilize memory bandwidth while decode stages underutilize compute capacity. Long-context agent workloads amplify this mismatch, making hardware-level disaggregation necessary.
How It Works:


Prefill Accelerators: Optimize for high computational throughput and matrix operations

Prioritize compute capacity and instruction-level parallelism
Handle dense matrix multiplications (token embedding → attention → feedforward)
Process batch of tokens → output context vectors efficiently



Decode Accelerators: Optimize for memory bandwidth and low-latency single-token generation

Maximize memory bandwidth-to-compute ratio
Prioritize fast random access to KV cache with low latency
Process single token → output next token with minimal compute



System Integration: Route requests through prefill and decode stages sequentially

Prefill produces KV cache stored in shared memory tier
Decode accesses pre-computed KV cache with low-latency memory interconnect
Optional: Multi-token decode for non-agent workloads to improve compute utilization



Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Input Tokens<br/>(batch)"] --> B["Prefill Accelerator<br/>(High Compute)"]
    B --> C["Shared KV Cache<br/>(Memory Pool)"]
    C --> D["Decode Accelerator<br/>(High Bandwidth)"]
    D --> E["Output Tokens<br/>(streaming)"]

Where It’s Useful:

Datacenter-scale inference serving with agent workloads (long contexts)
Models with extended context windows requiring large KV cache capacity
Mixed-workload serving where prefill and decode can be load-balanced separately
Cost-per-token optimization in production LLM APIs

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires high-speed interconnect between prefill and decode stages to minimize KV cache transfer latency
Adds operational complexity: need separate scaling policies for prefill vs. decode clusters
Not beneficial for short-context workloads with low prefill:decode ratio
Load balancing becomes non-trivial when prefill and decode have asymmetric throughput

Internal Use Cases:

Optimizing inference infrastructure for long-context multi-turn conversations in chat systems
Accelerating computer-use agent workflows where extended context is essential
Designing heterogeneous serving clusters that balance cost and latency for variable-length requests


Compute-Memory Disaggregation via Optical I/O
Core Idea: Physically separate compute and memory into independent scaling domains connected by high-bandwidth optical interconnects, enabling independent optimization of processing nodes (compute-optimized) and memory systems (capacity/bandwidth-optimized).
Problem It Solves: Monolithic system-on-chip designs couple memory capacity and compute, creating inefficient tradeoffs—either memory is wasted when compute is the bottleneck, or memory becomes bottlenecked when compute scales. Agent workloads with variable context lengths expose both extremes within the same inference job.
How It Works:


Compute Nodes: Specialized for FLOPs-per-watt efficiency

Stream-process KV cache and activations from remote memory
Minimal on-chip SRAM (register files + small caches only)
Optimize for low-latency communication to memory



Memory Subsystem: Dedicated fabric for high-capacity, high-bandwidth storage

Pool memory resources (DRAM, HBM, emerging technologies) separately from compute
Serve multiple compute nodes simultaneously with minimal contention
Support low-latency random access patterns (KV cache lookups)



Optical Interconnect: Link compute and memory tiers with microsecond-scale latency, terabit/s bandwidth

Optical links provide future-proofing for bandwidth scaling without electrical constraints
Enable flexible topology (all-to-all, hierarchical) for multi-node memory disaggregation
Support deterministic latency for real-time agent response requirements



Architecture:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Compute["Compute Tier"]
        A["Prefill<br/>Accelerator"]
        B["Decode<br/>Accelerator"]
    end
    subgraph Memory["Memory Tier"]
        C["Capacity Pool<br/>(DRAM/HBM)"]
    end
    A -->|Optical I/O| C
    B -->|Optical I/O| C

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale inference datacenters with heterogeneous workloads
Systems requiring independent scaling of compute and memory (e.g., decoding bottlenecks require more memory capacity without additional compute)
Future-proofing: optical interconnects scale better than electrical chiplets as bandwidth demands increase
Inference systems where memory is shared across multiple accelerators (prefill, decode, reranking modules)

Limitations / Caveats:

Optical I/O latency (microseconds) is higher than on-package electrical links (nanoseconds)—requires algorithm adjustments for memory-latency-sensitive workloads
Increases system complexity: requires sophisticated cache hierarchies and prefetch strategies to hide latency
Power consumption of optical transceivers may offset gains from disaggregated cooling/scaling
Existing software stacks (CUDA, HIP) not optimized for disaggregated memory; requires new memory abstraction layers

Internal Use Cases:

Designing future inference clusters where KV cache memory scales independently of compute capacity
Supporting multiple concurrent agents sharing a memory pool (e.g., embedding caches, shared prompt libraries)
Enabling cost-efficient scale-up: add memory capacity without duplicating expensive accelerators

literature/2026-01-30-Idea2Story-An-Auto.title: Idea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives
literature/2026-01-30-Idea2Story-An-Auto.content:
Idea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #8 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Graph, RAG, Search
Abstract
Autonomous scientific discovery with large language model (LLM)-based agents has recently made substantial progress, demonstrating the ability to automate end-to-end research workflows. However, existing systems largely rely on runtime-centric execution paradigms, repeatedly reading, summarizing, and reasoning over large volumes of scientific literature online. This on-the-spot computation strategy incurs high computational cost, suffers from context window limitations, and often leads to brittle reasoning and hallucination. We propose Idea2Story, a pre-computation-driven framework for autonomous scientific discovery that shifts literature understanding from online reasoning to offline knowledge construction. Idea2Story continuously collects peer-reviewed papers together with their review feedback, extracts core methodological units, composes reusable research patterns, and organizes them into a structured methodological knowledge graph. At runtime, underspecified user research intents are aligned to established research paradigms, enabling efficient retrieval and reuse of high-quality research patterns instead of open-ended generation and trial-and-error. By grounding research planning and execution in a pre-built knowledge graph, Idea2Story alleviates the context window bottleneck of LLMs and substantially reduces repeated runtime reasoning over literature. We conduct qualitative analyses and preliminary empirical studies demonstrating that Idea2Story can generate coherent, methodologically grounded, and novel research patterns, and can produce several high-quality research demonstrations in an end-to-end setting. These results suggest that offline knowledge construction provides a practical and scalable foundation for reliable autonomous scientific discovery.
Key Techniques

Methodological knowledge graph construction
Pre-computation-driven literature understanding
Research pattern extraction and reuse
Intent-to-paradigm alignment

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityIdea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives0.81Idea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives0.76ResearchGym: Evaluating Language Model Agents on Real-World AI Research0.18Towards Personalized Bangla Book Recommendation: A Large-Scale Multi-Entity Book Graph Dataset0.17Improving Data and Reward Design for Scientific Reasoning in Large Language Models0.17
Intent-to-Paradigm Alignment
Core Idea: Map underspecified user research intents to established research paradigms in the knowledge graph, enabling targeted pattern retrieval instead of open-ended generation.
Problem It Solves:

Users provide high-level, incomplete research ideas that are difficult for LLMs to directly ground in specific, reproducible methodologies; typical fallback is open-ended generation leading to trial-and-error and hallucination.

How It Works:

Intent Parsing: Parse user research intent (natural language description of research goal, dataset, or problem) to extract key semantic features (research domain, task type, computational constraints, methodological preferences)
Paradigm Matching: Query the methodological knowledge graph with these features to identify established research paradigms that align with the intent (using semantic similarity or constraint-based matching)
Pattern Retrieval: Retrieve the full set of reusable patterns (methodological components, hyperparameters, validation strategies, architectural templates) associated with the matched paradigm
Instantiation: Ground the user’s intent in the retrieved patterns, substituting domain-specific parameters (dataset names, model architectures, metrics) while preserving the methodological skeleton

Where It’s Useful:

Interactive research planning assistants where users specify rough ideas requiring structured grounding
Reproducible research workflows (patterns ensure consistency with prior methodological choices)
Novice researcher support (patterns provide explicit methodological scaffolding)
Multi-disciplinary research teams needing shared methodological vocabulary

Limitations / Caveats:

Alignment quality depends on semantic similarity matching; ambiguous intents may retrieve misaligned paradigms
Cannot handle intents that require methodological innovation beyond the graph’s paradigm coverage
User intent must be expressible within the feature space used for paradigm matching
Requires explicit feedback loop to improve alignment quality over time

Internal Use Cases:

Recommendation systems: similar to cold-start problem—aligning user preferences to known user/item clusters rather than generating from scratch
LLM prompt optimization: pre-compute template patterns for common use cases instead of generating per-query
Query expansion: intent features serve as expansion terms for better retrieval coverage


Research Pattern Reuse and Composition
Core Idea: Reuse and compose high-quality, pre-vetted research patterns from the knowledge graph as building blocks for end-to-end research execution, replacing trial-and-error generation.
Problem It Solves:

Open-ended LLM generation of research workflows leads to unreliable, non-reproducible, and often methodologically unsound research designs; requires expensive validation and iteration.

How It Works:

Pattern Retrieval: Access pre-extracted patterns from the methodological knowledge graph (each pattern is a self-contained, validated research component)
Compatibility Checking: Verify that selected patterns are compatible in terms of input/output specifications, domain assumptions, and computational requirements
Composition: Chain compatible patterns in sequence to construct a complete research pipeline (e.g., data preprocessing pattern → feature engineering pattern → model training pattern → evaluation pattern)
Domain Instantiation: Bind domain-specific parameters (dataset URIs, model hyperparameters, evaluation metrics) to the composed pattern sequence
Execution: Run the composed pipeline end-to-end with LLM-guided refinement at decision points

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Intent"] --> B["Retrieve<br/>Patterns"]
    B --> C["Compatibility<br/>Check"]
    C --> D["Pattern<br/>Composition"]
    D --> E["Domain<br/>Instantiation"]
    E --> F["Execute<br/>Pipeline"]
    F --> G["Output<br/>Research"]

Where It’s Useful:

Automated research workflow generation with quality guarantees (patterns are peer-reviewed)
Transfer learning across research domains (patterns abstract domain-specific details)
Reproducible research publication (patterns are versioned, immutable references)
Curriculum learning for research skill acquisition (novice researchers learn from pattern templates)

Limitations / Caveats:

Composition assumes pattern modularity and clear interfaces; poorly-designed patterns reduce reusability
May constrain research novelty if composition space is limited or rigid
Requires explicit domain instantiation logic for each new domain/dataset combination
Pattern versioning and updates require careful management to avoid breaking composed workflows

Internal Use Cases:

Recommendation ranking: compose retrieval → filtering → ranking → reranking modules (analogous pattern composition)
ML pipeline generation: pre-built stages (preprocessing, feature extraction, model training, evaluation) similar to research patterns
Workflow automation: pattern library as reusable business process templates

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-01-30-MALLOC-Benchmarkin.title: MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation
literature/2026-01-30-MALLOC-Benchmarkin.content:
MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #12 (score: 0.85, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The scaling law, which indicates that model performance improves with increasing dataset and model capacity, has fueled a growing trend in expanding recommendation models in both industry and academia. However, the advent of large-scale recommenders also brings significantly higher computational costs, particularly under the long-sequence dependencies inherent in the user intent of recommendation systems. Current approaches often rely on pre-storing the intermediate states of the past behavior for each user, thereby reducing the quadratic re-computation cost for the following requests. Despite their effectiveness, these methods often treat memory merely as a medium for acceleration, without adequately considering the space overhead it introduces. This presents a critical challenge in real-world recommendation systems with billions of users, each of whom might initiate thousands of interactions and require massive memory for state storage. Fortunately, there have been several memory management strategies examined for compression in LLM, while most have not been evaluated on the recommendation task. To mitigate this gap, we introduce MALLOC, a comprehensive benchmark for memory-aware long sequence compression. MALLOC presents a comprehensive investigation and systematic classification of memory management techniques applicable to large sequential recommendations. These techniques are integrated into state-of-the-art recommenders, enabling a reproducible and accessible evaluation platform. Through extensive experiments across accuracy, efficiency, and complexity, we demonstrate the holistic reliability of MALLOC in advancing large-scale recommendation. Code is available at anonymous.4open.science/r/MALLOC.
Key Techniques

Long-sequence user behavior modeling
Memory management for state compression
Pre-storing intermediate states
Benchmark evaluation framework

Related Litnotes

literature/2026-01-30-Masked-Diffusion-G.title: Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation
literature/2026-01-30-Masked-Diffusion-G.content:
Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #6 (score: 0.92, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Generative recommendation (GR) typically first quantizes continuous item embeddings into multi-level semantic IDs (SIDs), and then generates the next item via autoregressive decoding. Although existing methods are already competitive in terms of recommendation performance, directly inheriting the autoregressive decoding paradigm from language models still suffers from three key limitations: (1) autoregressive decoding struggles to jointly capture global dependencies among the multi-dimensional features associated with different positions of SID; (2) using a unified, fixed decoding path for the same item implicitly assumes that all users attend to item attributes in the same order; (3) autoregressive decoding is inefficient at inference time and struggles to meet real-time requirements. To tackle these challenges, we propose MDGR, a Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation framework that reshapes the GR pipeline from three perspectives: codebook, training, and inference. (1) We adopt a parallel codebook to provide a structural foundation for diffusion-based GR. (2) During training, we adaptively construct masking supervision signals along both the temporal and sample dimensions. (3) During inference, we develop a warm-up-based two-stage parallel decoding strategy for efficient generation of SIDs. Extensive experiments on multiple public and industrial-scale datasets show that MDGR outperforms ten state-of-the-art baselines by up to 10.78%. Furthermore, by deploying MDGR on a large-scale online advertising platform, we achieve a 1.20% increase in revenue, demonstrating its practical value.
Key Techniques

parallel codebook
masked diffusion training
adaptive masking supervision
warm-up-based two-stage parallel decoding

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMasked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.80Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.72Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.71Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.62DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.31
Adaptive Masking Supervision with Temporal and Sample Dimensions
Core Idea: During training, dynamically construct masking patterns along both temporal (sequential token positions) and sample (batch) dimensions to guide the diffusion model, rather than using fixed random masking.
Problem It Solves:

Standard diffusion models use uniform random masking, which doesn’t reflect the actual generation ordering preferences of different users
Autoregressive training lacks flexibility in learning multiple valid generation paths

How It Works:

Temporal dimension masking: For sequence position i, adaptively determine which positions should be masked at each diffusion timestep based on:

User interaction patterns (users who care about category first vs. price first)
Information-theoretic importance (mask positions that contribute less to overall loss first)
Temporal dependencies between adjacent SID tokens


Sample dimension masking: Across the batch, vary masking patterns per user-sample pair:

Different users get different masking schedules reflecting their attribute attention order
Construct per-sample loss weights to emphasize important attributes for that user


Training objective:
L = Σ(samples) w_sample * Σ(timesteps) L_diffusion(x_t, mask_t(sample))

where mask_t varies both temporally and per-sample

Where It’s Useful:

User-personalized recommendation (different users care about different attributes)
Multi-objective ranking (different business metrics require different attribute priorities)
Cold-start scenarios where user preferences are uncertain

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires learning or inferring good masking patterns; poorly chosen patterns may harm generalization
Adds computational overhead during training to compute adaptive masks
May overfit to training user preferences if mask patterns not regularized

Internal Use Cases:

Ad recommendation where different advertiser verticals (fashion vs. finance) have different attribute importance
Ranking where we want to balance multiple signals (engagement, diversity, quality)—adaptive masking could weight them differently per context


Warm-up-based Two-Stage Parallel Decoding Strategy
Core Idea: During inference, first “warm up” the diffusion model with a partial denoising pass using predicted uncertain positions, then perform parallel decoding of all SID dimensions in a second stage, bypassing the inefficient iterative refinement of autoregressive models.
Problem It Solves:

Autoregressive decoding requires sequential generation of tokens, making latency proportional to sequence length (unacceptable for real-time systems)
Iterative diffusion refinement traditionally requires many denoising steps, compounding latency

How It Works:


Stage 1 - Warm-up phase:

Start with random noise or learnable initialization for all SID dimensions
Run a limited number of diffusion steps (e.g., 5-10 out of total T steps) to establish global structure
Focus on highly uncertain positions (those with highest predicted entropy)
Output: partially denoised embedding with high-confidence regions



Stage 2 - Parallel decoding phase:

Condition all K codebook dimensions jointly on the warm-up output
Perform remaining denoising steps in parallel across dimensions instead of sequentially
Generate all SID tokens simultaneously: [SID₁, SID₂, ..., SIDₖ] ← Denoise_parallel(warm_up_output, steps_remaining)
Output: final item recommendation



Efficiency gain: Total latency = warm_up_steps + parallel_decode_steps (vs. sequential T×K steps in autoregressive)


Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["User Context"] --> B["Random Init<br/>or Learnable Prior"]
    B --> C["Warm-up Phase<br/>5-10 steps"]
    C --> D["Partially Denoised<br/>Embedding"]
    D --> E["Parallel Diffusion<br/>All Dimensions"]
    E --> F["Parallel Decoding<br/>Step 1"]
    E --> G["Parallel Decoding<br/>Step 2"]
    E --> H["Parallel Decoding<br/>Step K"]
    F --> I["Final SID<br/>Recommendation"]
    G --> I
    H --> I

Where It’s Useful:

Real-time serving systems (online ads, e-commerce search)
Mobile recommendation where inference latency directly impacts user experience
Batch serving with strict SLA requirements

Limitations / Caveats:

Warm-up phase requires calibration—too few steps leaves model under-informed, too many wastes efficiency gains
Parallel decoding loses the sequential refinement benefit of autoregressive approaches; may reduce recommendation diversity
Requires careful tuning of the uncertainty threshold for “high-confidence” warm-up positions

Internal Use Cases:

Nextgen ranking/retrieval where we need sub-100ms latencies
A/B testing platform: parallel decoding enables rapid candidate generation for variants
Could speed up LLM-based recommendation rankers that currently use sequential decoding

literature/2026-01-30-OneMall-One-Model-.title: OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios -- End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce
literature/2026-01-30-OneMall-One-Model-.content:
OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #4 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
In the wave of generative recommendation, we present OneMall, an end-to-end generative recommendation framework tailored for e-commerce services at Kuaishou. Our OneMall systematically unifies the e-commerce’s multiple item distribution scenarios, such as Product-card, short-video and live-streaming. Specifically, it comprises three key components, aligning the entire model training pipeline to the LLM’s pre-training/post-training: (1) E-commerce Semantic Tokenizer: we provide a tokenizer solution that captures both real-world semantics and business-specific item relations across different scenarios; (2) Transformer-based Architecture: we largely utilize Transformer as our model backbone, e.g., employing Query-Former for long sequence compression, Cross-Attention for multi-behavior sequence fusion, and Sparse MoE for scalable auto-regressive generation; (3) Reinforcement Learning Pipeline: we further connect retrieval and ranking models via RL, enabling the ranking model to serve as a reward signal for end-to-end policy retrieval model optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OneMall achieves consistent improvements across all e-commerce scenarios: +13.01% GMV in product-card, +15.32% Orders in Short-Video, and +2.78% Orders in Live-Streaming. OneMall has been deployed, serving over 400 million daily active users at Kuaishou.
Key Techniques

E-commerce Semantic Tokenizer
Transformer-based Architecture with Query-Former and Cross-Attention and Sparse MoE
Reinforcement Learning Pipeline for retrieval-ranking connection

Related Litnotes
