literature/2026-01-30-PaperSearchQA-Lear.title: PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR
literature/2026-01-30-PaperSearchQA-Lear.content:
PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #4 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RL, RAG, Search
Abstract
Search agents are language models (LMs) that reason and search knowledge bases (or the web) to answer questions; recent methods supervise only the final answer accuracy using reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). Most RLVR search agents tackle general-domain QA, which limits their relevance to technical AI systems in science, engineering, and medicine. In this work we propose training agents to search and reason over scientific papers — this tests technical question-answering, it is directly relevant to real scientists, and the capabilities will be crucial to future AI Scientist systems. Concretely, we release a search corpus of 16 million biomedical paper abstracts and construct a challenging factoid QA dataset called PaperSearchQA with 60k samples answerable from the corpus, along with benchmarks. We train search agents in this environment to outperform non-RL retrieval baselines; we also perform further quantitative analysis and observe interesting agent behaviors like planning, reasoning, and self-verification. Our corpus, datasets, and benchmarks are usable with the popular Search-R1 codebase for RLVR training and released on huggingface.co/collections/jmhb/papersearchqa. Finally, our data creation methods are scalable and easily extendable to other scientific domains.
Key Techniques

Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR)
search agents with language models
PaperSearchQA dataset construction
retrieval-based question answering over scientific corpora

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.79PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.79PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.77PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.72PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.64
RLVR Search Agent Training over Scientific Papers
Core Idea: Training language models as search agents using reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where agents learn to iteratively search and reason over scientific paper collections to answer complex questions.
Problem It Solves: Search agents trained via RLVR typically optimize only final answer accuracy on general-domain questions. This work extends RLVR to technical, multi-step reasoning tasks where intermediate steps (retrieval decisions, reasoning chains) matter as much as the final answer—crucial for scientific AI systems.
How It Works:

Agent Action Space: Define discrete actions: SEARCH(query) → returns ranked papers, READ(paper_id) → extracts content, REASON(evidence) → generates answer or plan.
Reward Signal: Verifiable reward r(trajectory) checks if final answer matches ground truth AND (optionally) if intermediate retrieval steps cite correct papers.
Policy Optimization: Use policy gradient methods (PPO or A3C) to maximize expected cumulative reward:

J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}[R(\tau)] where \tau is a trajectory of search/read/reason actions


Exploration Strategy: Explore different search query formulations and reasoning paths early in training; converge to high-reward trajectories.
Agent Behaviors Emergence: Through RL, agents learn emergent behaviors like query planning (reformulating questions), multi-step reasoning, and self-verification (checking answers against retrieved evidence).

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    Q["Question (input)"] --> Agent["LM Agent<br/>(policy π)"]
    Agent -->|SEARCH| Retriever["BM25/Dense<br/>Retriever"]
    Retriever --> Papers["Paper<br/>Corpus"]
    Papers --> Agent
    Agent -->|READ| Reader["Context<br/>Extractor"]
    Reader --> Agent
    Agent -->|REASON| Reasoner["Answer<br/>Generator"]
    Reasoner --> A["Answer (output)"]
    A --> Reward["Reward<br/>Verifier"]
    Reward -->|gradient| Agent

Where It’s Useful:

Training agents for multi-source scientific literature synthesis
Building AI systems that can explain answers by citing retrieved papers
Hybrid retrieval-generation systems that need to learn when to search vs. reason from memory
Fact-checking and evidence grounding in technical domains

Limitations / Caveats:

Reward function must be verifiable (binary correctness); harder for open-ended questions (opinion, methodology comparison)
Agent behavior is learned from final answers only; intermediate reasoning chains not explicitly supervised unless added to reward
Computational cost: full RL requires thousands of rollouts per question to estimate policy gradients
Generalization unclear: agents trained on PaperSearchQA biomedical papers may not transfer to other domains
Search action space is large (millions of possible queries); exploration is inefficient without good initialization

Internal Use Cases:

Training LLM-based ranking models that learn to prioritize papers based on query intent (vs. BM25’s lexical matching)
Implementing self-improving search pipelines where user feedback on answer quality updates the reward function
Multi-agent collaboration: teams of search agents that divide QA tasks and verify each other’s findings


Integration with Search-R1 Framework
Core Idea: Leveraging the popular Search-R1 codebase as a standardized training infrastructure for RLVR agents, enabling reproducible experiments and community contributions.
Problem It Solves: Custom implementations of RLVR training are brittle and hard to reproduce. Using Search-R1 as a common foundation reduces implementation variance and lowers the barrier for researchers to benchmark new retrieval corpora and reasoning strategies.
Where It’s Useful:

Rapid prototyping of domain-specific search agents
Community benchmarking (comparing agents trained on different datasets)
Transfer learning experiments (pre-train on general QA, fine-tune on scientific papers)

Limitations / Caveats:

Dependent on Search-R1 API stability; framework updates may break existing experiments
May constrain agent architecture choices (only designs compatible with Search-R1 can be easily evaluated)

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-01-30-S2GR-Stepwise-Sema.title: S$^2$GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation
literature/2026-01-30-S2GR-Stepwise-Sema.content:
S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #11 (score: 0.85, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Generative Recommendation (GR) has emerged as a transformative paradigm with its end-to-end generation advantages. However, existing GR methods primarily focus on direct Semantic ID (SID) generation from interaction sequences, failing to activate deeper reasoning capabilities analogous to those in large language models and thus limiting performance potential. We identify two critical limitations in current reasoning-enhanced GR approaches: (1) Strict sequential separation between reasoning and generation steps creates imbalanced computational focus across hierarchical SID codes, degrading quality for SID codes; (2) Generated reasoning vectors lack interpretable semantics, while reasoning paths suffer from unverifiable supervision. In this paper, we propose stepwise semantic-guided reasoning in latent space (S^2GR), a novel reasoning enhanced GR framework. First, we establish a robust semantic foundation via codebook optimization, integrating item co-occurrence relationship to capture behavioral patterns, and load balancing and uniformity objectives that maximize codebook utilization while reinforcing coarse-to-fine semantic hierarchies. Our core innovation introduces the stepwise reasoning mechanism inserting thinking tokens before each SID generation step, where each token explicitly represents coarse-grained semantics supervised via contrastive learning against ground-truth codebook cluster distributions ensuring physically grounded reasoning paths and balanced computational focus across all SID codes. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of S^2GR, and online A/B test confirms efficacy on large-scale industrial short video platform.
Key Techniques

Codebook optimization with co-occurrence relationships
Stepwise reasoning mechanism with thinking tokens
Contrastive learning for semantic supervision
Load balancing and uniformity objectives

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityS^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation0.83S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation0.82S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation0.78S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation0.74End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.26
Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning with Thinking Tokens (S²GR)
Core Idea: Insert interpretable “thinking tokens” before each Semantic ID generation step to perform explicit coarse-grained semantic reasoning in latent space, with each token supervised via contrastive learning against ground-truth codebook cluster distributions.
Problem It Solves: Previous reasoning-enhanced GR methods suffer from (1) strict sequential separation between reasoning and generation creating imbalanced computational focus (early SID codes get more reasoning, later ones less), and (2) generated reasoning vectors lack interpretability and have no verifiable supervision signal, leading to ungrounded reasoning paths.
How It Works:

For each SID generation step t (coarse-to-fine hierarchy), insert a learnable thinking token \tau_t into the sequence before generating SID code s_t
Each thinking token \tau_t is trained via contrastive learning: push \tau_t closer to the ground-truth codebook cluster distribution p_t^* (from step t labels), and push away from other cluster distributions
Explicitly represent coarse-grained semantics through \tau_t: token embeddings directly correspond to expected semantic clusters at hierarchy level t
Generate SID code s_t using both interaction context and the semantically-grounded thinking token: s_t = \arg\max_k \text{sim}(\text{context}_t + \tau_t, c_k)
Distribute computational focus evenly across all hierarchy levels by ensuring every step has equally-weighted reasoning supervision

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Interaction Seq"] --> B["Step 1: Coarse Level"]
    B --> C["Insert Thinking Token τ₁"]
    C --> D["Generate SID Code s₁"]
    D --> E["Step 2: Medium Level"]
    E --> F["Insert Thinking Token τ₂"]
    F --> G["Generate SID Code s₂"]
    G --> H["Step 3: Fine Level"]
    H --> I["Insert Thinking Token τ₃"]
    I --> J["Generate SID Code s₃"]
    J --> K["Item Recommendation"]
    
    C -.->|contrastive loss| L["Ground-truth Clusters p*₁"]
    F -.->|contrastive loss| M["Ground-truth Clusters p*₂"]
    I -.->|contrastive loss| N["Ground-truth Clusters p*₃"]

Where It’s Useful:

Generative recommendation with hierarchical SID codes (coarse-to-fine item categories)
Any sequential generation task requiring interpretable intermediate reasoning
Recommendation systems needing balanced computational allocation across decoding steps
Short-form video platforms with multi-level item hierarchies (as validated in paper’s A/B test)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires ground-truth codebook cluster labels during training (supervision overhead)
Thinking token insertion adds inference latency (one additional forward pass per SID generation step)
Contrastive learning hyperparameters (temperature, margin) require careful tuning
Assumes codebook cluster distributions are well-defined; fails if semantics are ambiguous

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking systems: thinking tokens could represent intermediate ranking signals (e.g., relevance→diversity→freshness) before final score generation
LLM chains-of-thought: analogous to explicit reasoning steps in language models, applicable to multi-stage retrieval-ranking pipelines
RecSys hierarchies: e-commerce (category→subcategory→SKU) or video platforms (topic→creator→video)


Balanced Hierarchical Supervision via Contrastive Learning
Core Idea: Enforce interpretable semantics in reasoning paths by using contrastive learning to align thinking tokens with ground-truth codebook cluster distributions at each generation step, ensuring physically grounded and verifiable reasoning.
Problem It Solves: Prior reasoning-enhanced GR methods generate reasoning vectors with uninterpretable semantics and no verifiable supervision, making it impossible to validate whether reasoning is meaningful or just memorizing spurious patterns.
How It Works:

For each SID generation step t, obtain ground-truth codebook cluster distribution p_t^* = \{p_t^{(k)}\}_{k=1}^K representing the true semantic cluster assignment for items at hierarchy level t
Define contrastive loss: for thinking token \tau_t, treat its cluster distribution (via softmax of similarities to codebook) as positive anchor
Apply InfoNCE-style loss: \mathcal{L}_t = -\log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(\tau_t, p_t^*) / \tau_{\text{temp}})}{\sum_{j} \exp(\text{sim}(\tau_t, p_j^*) / \tau_{\text{temp}})}
Backpropagate contrastive loss to update thinking token embeddings, explicitly aligning them with ground-truth semantics
Ensures reasoning paths are not just plausible but grounded in verified item-cluster relationships from training data

Where It’s Useful:

Any multi-step generation requiring interpretable intermediate outputs
Supervised learning scenarios with ground-truth hierarchical labels
Recommendation systems with explicit item taxonomy/categorization

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires annotated ground-truth cluster labels (cannot be used in fully unsupervised settings)
Contrastive learning is sensitive to negative sampling and temperature hyperparameters
May overfit to training cluster distributions if taxonomy is biased or incomplete


These three techniques form the integrated S²GR framework: codebook optimization provides semantic grounding, thinking tokens implement stepwise reasoning, and contrastive supervision ensures those tokens are interpretable and verifiable.

literature/2026-01-30-SONIC-Segmented-Op.title: SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching
literature/2026-01-30-SONIC-Segmented-Op.content:
SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #5 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The linear growth of Key-Value (KV) cache remains a bottleneck for multi-turn LLM deployment. Existing KV cache compression methods often fail to account for the structural properties of multi-turn dialogues, relying on heuristic eviction that risks losing critical context. We propose \textbf{SONIC}, a learning-based framework that compresses historical segments into compact and semantically rich \textbf{Nexus} tokens. By integrating dynamic budget training, SONIC allows flexible adaptation to varying memory constraints without retraining. Experiments show that at compression ratios of 80% and 50%, SONIC consistently outperforms baselines such as H2O and StreamingLLM on four diverse multi-turn benchmarks. Specifically, on the widely used MTBench101 benchmark, SONIC achieves an average score improvement of 35.55% over state-of-the-art baselines, validating its effectiveness in sustaining coherent multi-turn dialogues. Furthermore, SONIC enhances deployment efficiency, accelerating the overall inference process by 50.1% compared to full-context generation.
Key Techniques

Nexus tokens
dynamic budget training
learning-based KV cache compression
structural multi-turn dialogue compression

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.67SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.66SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.63Attn-GS: Attention-Guided Context Compression for Efficient Personalized LLMs0.22ATACompressor: Adaptive Task-Aware Compression for Efficient Long-Context Processing in LLMs0.20
Dynamic Budget Training (DBT)
Core Idea: Train the compression model with variable memory budgets during training, enabling flexible compression ratios at inference time without requiring retraining for different memory constraints.
Problem It Solves: Real-world deployments face unpredictable memory constraints. Traditional compression methods are trained for a fixed compression ratio, requiring retraining to adapt to different hardware or operational constraints—this is costly and inflexible.
How It Works:

During training, randomly sample target compression ratios (e.g., compression rates from 50% to 80%) for each training batch
For each sampled ratio, train the compression encoder to produce the appropriate number of Nexus tokens
Use a learnable compression budget parameter β that controls how many Nexus tokens are generated
Train the model to optimize performance across the range of budgets (via curriculum or mixed-budget batches)
At inference time, set β to the desired compression ratio without retraining

Where It’s Useful:

Deployment scenarios with varying hardware (GPUs with different memory)
A/B testing compression ratios in production (latency vs. quality trade-offs)
Systems that need to adapt to runtime memory pressure dynamically
Multi-tenant inference platforms serving users with different SLA requirements

Limitations / Caveats:

Training complexity increases due to need to handle multiple budget targets simultaneously
Performance may be slightly suboptimal at each specific ratio compared to ratio-specific training
Requires careful tuning of the budget parameter range during training to avoid poor generalization at extreme ratios

Internal Use Cases:

Useful for RecSys where recommendation latency budgets vary by user tier or traffic conditions
Could apply to ranking models deployed across different serving infrastructure with varying memory


Nexus Token Integration with Attention (NTA)
Core Idea: Seamlessly integrate learned Nexus tokens into the standard transformer attention mechanism by treating compressed historical segments as part of the KV cache, preserving attention’s ability to weigh all context.
Problem It Solves: Naive compression or truncation breaks attention’s full context access. This method maintains the standard transformer attention API while using compressed representations, avoiding architectural changes.
How It Works:

Maintain current-turn full KV cache (uncompressed): K_current, V_current with shape [seq_len_curr, d]
Maintain compressed historical KV as Nexus tokens: K_nexus, V_nexus with shape [num_nexus, d]
Concatenate along sequence dimension: K_combined = [K_nexus; K_current], V_combined = [V_nexus; V_current]
Compute standard multi-head attention: Attention(Q, K_combined, V_combined) = softmax(Q·K_combined^T / √d)·V_combined
Attention naturally learns to weight both Nexus tokens and current context appropriately

Where It’s Useful:

Minimal-change integration into existing transformer implementations
Systems where attention patterns over historical context matter (e.g., question answering requiring prior context)
Settings where you want to preserve full attention expressiveness despite compression

Limitations / Caveats:

Attention computation still scales with (current_seq_len + num_nexus), so gains are modest if Nexus tokens are numerous
Attention may still overweight recent tokens (current turn) due to recency bias, potentially underutilizing compressed history
Quality depends heavily on Nexus token quality—poor compression leads to poor attention outputs

Internal Use Cases:

For ranking, could compress user history into Nexus tokens while maintaining full attention over user signals and candidates
Applicable to conversational recommendation where historical user preferences are compressed but attend over recommendations

literature/2026-01-30-TBDFiltering-Sampl.title: TBDFiltering: Sample-Efficient Tree-Based Data Filtering
literature/2026-01-30-TBDFiltering-Sampl.content:
TBDFiltering: Sample-Efficient Tree-Based Data Filtering
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #10 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Embeddings, RAG, Search
Abstract
The quality of machine learning models depends heavily on their training data. Selecting high-quality, diverse training sets for large language models (LLMs) is a difficult task, due to the lack of cheap and reliable quality metrics. While querying existing LLMs for document quality is common, this is not scalable to the large number (billions) of documents used in training. Instead, practitioners often use classifiers trained on sparse quality signals. In this paper, we propose a text-embedding-based hierarchical clustering approach that adaptively selects the documents to be evaluated by the LLM to estimate cluster quality. We prove that our method is query efficient: under the assumption that the hierarchical clustering contains a subtree such that each leaf cluster in the tree is pure enough (i.e., it mostly contains either only good or only bad documents), with high probability, the method can correctly predict the quality of each document after querying a small number of documents. The number of such documents is proportional to the size of the smallest subtree with (almost) pure leaves, without the algorithm knowing this subtree in advance. Furthermore, in a comprehensive experimental study, we demonstrate the benefits of our algorithm compared to other classifier-based filtering methods.
Key Techniques

Text-embedding-based hierarchical clustering
Adaptive cluster quality estimation via LLM queries
Query-efficient document selection

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityTBDFiltering: Sample-Efficient Tree-Based Data Filtering0.99TBDFiltering: Sample-Efficient Tree-Based Data Filtering0.78A Picture of Agentic Search0.10RecSys Substack Digest: 10 Papers on RAG, Retrieval, and RecSys0.10S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation0.10

literature/2026-01-30-Toward-Culturally-.title: Toward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning
literature/2026-01-30-Toward-Culturally-.content:
Toward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #9 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly support culturally sensitive decision making, yet often exhibit misalignment due to skewed pretraining data and the absence of structured value representations. Existing methods can steer outputs, but often lack demographic grounding and treat values as independent, unstructured signals, reducing consistency and interpretability. We propose OG-MAR, an Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning framework. OG-MAR summarizes respondent-specific values from the World Values Survey (WVS) and constructs a global cultural ontology by eliciting relations over a fixed taxonomy via competency questions. At inference time, it retrieves ontology-consistent relations and demographically similar profiles to instantiate multiple value-persona agents, whose outputs are synthesized by a judgment agent that enforces ontology consistency and demographic proximity. Experiments on regional social-survey benchmarks across four LLM backbones show that OG-MAR improves cultural alignment and robustness over competitive baselines, while producing more transparent reasoning traces.
Key Techniques

Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning
World Values Survey value summarization
value-persona agent instantiation
judgment agent with ontology consistency enforcement

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityToward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning0.80Toward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning0.78Toward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning0.73Beyond Marginal Distributions: Evaluating Demographic-Aligned LLMs0.17Dissecting Subjectivity and the”Ground Truth”Illusion in Data Annotation0.15
Value-Persona Agent Instantiation
Core Idea: Create distinct reasoning agents that embody specific cultural values and demographic profiles by conditioning prompts with WVS-derived value summaries and ontology-consistent relations.
Problem It Solves: Monolithic LLM reasoning ignores demographic heterogeneity and cultural pluralism. By instantiating multiple agents with different value systems, the method ensures diverse perspectives are considered and no single view dominates.
How It Works:

Summarize respondent-specific values from WVS data (e.g., “prioritizes family over individual achievement”) to create value profiles
At inference time, retrieve k demographically similar profiles based on region, age, education, etc.
For each retrieved profile, construct a prompt that includes:

The query/decision to be made
The value summary for that demographic group
Ontology-consistent relations relevant to the decision (retrieved via similarity to query embeddings)


Run the LLM with each conditioned prompt to generate k distinct outputs representing different cultural perspectives
Pass all k outputs to the judgment agent for synthesis

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-perspective reasoning systems
Culturally-aware chatbots and decision support systems
Fairness-aware LLM outputs that represent diverse viewpoints

Limitations / Caveats:

Demographic similarity metrics are oversimplified; real cultural alignment is more nuanced
WVS data is sparse in many regions, limiting population coverage
Scaling to many agents increases latency and inference cost


Ontology-Consistent Judgment Agent
Core Idea: A meta-agent that synthesizes outputs from multiple value-persona agents by enforcing consistency with the global cultural ontology and verifying demographic proximity.
Problem It Solves: Naive aggregation (voting, averaging) of multi-agent outputs loses the structured reasoning embedded in the ontology. The judgment agent ensures the final decision respects both the ontology’s value relations and the demographic grounding of selected agents.
How It Works:

Receive outputs from k value-persona agents
Check each output O_i against the ontology: verify that implied value priorities and relations in O_i match or align with the ontology
Compute a consistency score for each output based on ontology alignment (e.g., fraction of implied relations that exist in the ontology graph)
Compute a demographic proximity score for each agent’s profile relative to a query-provided demographic (if available)
Combine consistency and proximity scores (weighted aggregation) to select the most reliable output(s), or blend outputs where multiple are equally aligned
Return the synthesized decision along with a reasoning trace explaining which values and relations justified it

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["k Persona Agent<br/>Outputs"] --> B["Ontology<br/>Alignment Check"]
    B --> C["Demographic<br/>Proximity Score"]
    C --> D["Weighted<br/>Aggregation"]
    D --> E["Synthesized<br/>Output + Trace"]

Where It’s Useful:

Final decision-making in multi-agent or multi-expert systems
Explainability in culturally-sensitive LLM outputs
Ranking and re-ranking of candidate responses by cultural consistency

Limitations / Caveats:

Consistency and proximity scores are heuristic; no principled Bayesian or information-theoretic foundation given
The weighting scheme between consistency and proximity is a hyperparameter that must be tuned per domain
Tie-breaking and blending strategies are underspecified in the framework


WVS-Grounded Value Summarization
Core Idea: Extract and summarize respondent-specific cultural values from World Values Survey data into concise natural-language profiles that condition LLM reasoning.
Problem It Solves: Raw WVS survey responses are high-dimensional and noisy. Direct use as prompt conditioning would overwhelm context windows and introduce inconsistency. This technique compresses WVS data into interpretable summaries that efficiently convey demographic value orientations.
How It Works:

Select a target demographic segment (e.g., region R, age group A, education E)
Retrieve all WVS respondents matching that demographic
Aggregate responses to key survey questions (e.g., importance of tradition, family, individual achievement) across respondents
Compute weighted statistics (mean, median, or mode) for each value dimension within the demographic group
Convert high-level statistics into natural-language descriptions (e.g., “Respondents in this group strongly prioritize family values and religious observance”)
Store summaries for retrieval at inference time

Where It’s Useful:

Prompt engineering for demographic-aware systems
Fairness auditing of LLM outputs across populations
Creating synthetic personas for bias testing

Limitations / Caveats:

WVS data is static and cross-sectional; doesn’t capture value drift over time within demographics
Aggregation smooths minority viewpoints within demographics (e.g., secular individuals in religious groups)
Summarization strategy (which values to highlight) is manual and not adaptive

Discussions & References

literature/2026-01-30-Why-Attention-Patt.title: Why Attention Patterns Exist: A Unifying Temporal Perspective Analysis
literature/2026-01-30-Why-Attention-Patt.content:
Why Attention Patterns Exist: A Unifying Temporal Perspective Analysis
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #3 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Attention patterns play a crucial role in both training and inference of large language models (LLMs). Prior works have identified individual patterns such as retrieval heads, sink heads, and diagonal traces, yet these observations remain fragmented and lack a unifying explanation. To bridge this gap, we introduce \textbf{Temporal Attention Pattern Predictability Analysis (TAPPA), a unifying framework that explains diverse attention patterns by analyzing their underlying mathematical formulations} from a temporally continuous perspective. TAPPA both deepens the understanding of attention behavior and guides inference acceleration approaches. Specifically, TAPPA characterizes attention patterns as predictable patterns with clear regularities and unpredictable patterns that appear effectively random. Our analysis further reveals that this distinction can be explained by the degree of query self-similarity along the temporal dimension. Focusing on the predictable patterns, we further provide a detailed mathematical analysis of three representative cases through the joint effect of queries, keys, and Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE). We validate TAPPA by applying its insights to KV cache compression and LLM pruning tasks. Across these tasks, a simple metric motivated by TAPPA consistently improves performance over baseline methods. The code is available at github.com/MIRALab-USTC/LLM-TAPPA.
Key Techniques

Temporal Attention Pattern Predictability Analysis (TAPPA)
query self-similarity analysis
attention pattern mathematical formulation analysis
KV cache compression

Related Litnotes

literature/2026-01-31-ARGORA-Orchestrate.title: ARGORA: Orchestrated Argumentation for Causally Grounded LLM Reasoning and Decision Making
literature/2026-01-31-ARGORA-Orchestrate.content:
ARGORA: Orchestrated Argumentation for Causally Grounded LLM Reasoning and Decision Making
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #7 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, RecSys
Abstract
Existing multi-expert LLM systems gather diverse perspectives but combine them through simple aggregation, obscuring which arguments drove the final decision. We introduce ARGORA, a framework that organizes multi-expert discussions into explicit argumentation graphs showing which arguments support or attack each other. By casting these graphs as causal models, ARGORA can systematically remove individual arguments and recompute outcomes, identifying which reasoning chains were necessary and whether decisions would change under targeted modifications. We further introduce a correction mechanism that aligns internal reasoning with external judgments when they disagree. Across diverse benchmarks and an open-ended use case, ARGORA achieves competitive accuracy and demonstrates corrective behavior: when experts initially disagree, the framework resolves disputes toward correct answers more often than it introduces new errors, while providing causal diagnostics of decisive arguments.
Key Techniques

Argumentation graphs
Causal intervention analysis
Argument-based correction mechanism
Multi-expert deliberation framework

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityARGORA: Orchestrated Argumentation for Causally Grounded LLM Reasoning and Decision Making0.75ARGORA: Orchestrated Argumentation for Causally Grounded LLM Reasoning and Decision Making0.72To Search or Not to Search: Aligning the Decision Boundary of Deep Search Agents via Causal Intervention0.15Unified Multi-Domain Graph Pre-training for Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Graphs via Domain-Specific Expert Encoding0.13Reasoning-Enhanced Rare-Event Prediction with Balanced Outcome Correction0.12
Causal Argument Removal and Sensitivity Analysis
Core Idea: Cast the argumentation graph as a causal model, then systematically remove individual arguments or argument clusters and recompute outcomes to identify which arguments are causally necessary for the final decision.
Problem It Solves: Even with explicit argumentation structure, determining which arguments actually influenced the outcome (vs. being present but irrelevant) requires a principled intervention mechanism. Simple ablation is not principled for argumentation; causal removal ensures we’re measuring true causal necessity.
How It Works:

Given a constructed argumentation graph G with arguments {A₁, A₂, …, Aₘ} and edges representing support/attack relationships
For each argument Aᵢ (or subset of arguments S ⊆ {A₁, …, Aₘ}):

Remove Aᵢ (and its incoming/outgoing edges) from G, creating G₋ᵢ
Recompute the final decision/outcome using the modified graph G₋ᵢ (e.g., via aggregation over remaining arguments)
Compare original outcome with outcome on G₋ᵢ


If outcome changes, Aᵢ is causally necessary; if it remains unchanged, Aᵢ is causally inert for this decision
Rank arguments by impact: those whose removal most drastically changes the outcome are most causal
Identify decisive argument chains: paths in the graph from initial arguments to the final decision that would break if any single argument were removed

Where It’s Useful:

Debugging LLM multi-expert systems: identify which expert contributions actually matter
Regulatory compliance: demonstrate to auditors which reasoning was necessary for a decision
Interactive decision refinement: highlight which arguments should be revisited if the outcome needs to change

Limitations / Caveats:

Assumes clean removal is possible; in practice, arguments may have implicit dependencies not captured in the explicit graph
Requires a deterministic or well-defined way to recompute outcomes after removal; unclear outcomes may persist under different subsets
Computational cost is O(m) or higher (one recomputation per argument); for large graphs this can be expensive
Causal reasoning is only as good as the graph structure—missing or mis-labeled edges will hide true causal relationships

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking system debugging: remove candidate ranking factors one at a time to see which truly impact final ranking
A/B testing alternatives: “if we remove the engagement factor from the ranking, does top-k change?”


Correction Mechanism for Disagreement Resolution
Core Idea: When expert opinions disagree with external ground truth (e.g., held-out labels), apply a correction process that adjusts internal reasoning/argument weights to align with correct answers, then re-aggregate to resolve disputes toward the right conclusion.
Problem It Solves: Multi-expert systems may reach incorrect consensus if all experts are biased in the same direction, or if they agree on premises that are actually false. Simple aggregation cannot self-correct. This mechanism detects when expert consensus diverges from ground truth and applies a targeted correction.
How It Works:

Run the multi-expert argumentation system (with graph construction and aggregation) on a set of instances with known ground truth labels Y_true
Identify discordance cases: instances where the system’s conclusion disagrees with Y_true
For discordance cases, perform causal removal analysis (from technique 2) to identify which arguments/experts contributed most to the wrong conclusion
Apply a correction weight or re-weighting to those arguments/experts based on their role in errors:

Arguments that consistently appear in error chains are down-weighted or adjusted
Arguments that support the correct answer but were overridden are up-weighted


Re-aggregate the argumentation graph with corrected weights to produce a revised decision
(Optional) Fine-tune or prompt-engineer the expert LLMs to reduce bias toward incorrect premises

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Multi-Expert<br/>Argumentation System"] --> B{Prediction =<br/>Ground Truth?}
    B -->|Yes| C["Accept Decision"]
    B -->|No| D["Causal Removal<br/>Analysis on Error"]
    D --> E["Identify Contributing<br/>Arguments"]
    E --> F["Adjust Argument<br/>Weights/Corrections"]
    F --> G["Re-aggregate with<br/>Corrected Weights"]
    G --> H["Revised Decision"]

Where It’s Useful:

Learning from human feedback: when the system disagrees with a human expert, automatically identify which parts of the reasoning were wrong
Active learning: prioritize which expert arguments need improvement based on where corrections are most frequent
Alignment to external evaluators: ensure internal LLM reasoning aligns with external criteria (compliance, domain expertise, fairness)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires ground truth labels to detect and correct discordance; unsuitable for truly open-ended problems without clear correctness
Correction mechanism may overfit to the training set of ground truth labels, reducing generalization
Assumes causal removal accurately identifies error contributors; noisy graphs may misdirect corrections
Introduces additional LLM calls (for causal analysis), increasing computational cost

Internal Use Cases:

Recommendation system calibration: when recommendations disagree with user feedback (“I didn’t like that”), identify which recommendation factors were wrong and down-weight them
Ranking correction: if ranking results diverge from ground-truth relevance (e.g., from human judges), apply correction to the argument factors driving the ranking

literature/2026-01-31-ASTRA-Automated-Sy.title: ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas
literature/2026-01-31-ASTRA-Automated-Sy.content:
ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #2 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, LLM, RL
Code: github.com/LianjiaTech/astra
Abstract
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as tool-augmented agents for multi-step decision making, yet training robust tool-using agents remains challenging. Existing methods still require manual intervention, depend on non-verifiable simulated environments, rely exclusively on either supervised fine-tuning (SFT) or reinforcement learning (RL), and struggle with stable long-horizon, multi-turn learning. To address these challenges, we introduce ASTRA, a fully automated end-to-end framework for training tool-augmented language model agents via scalable data synthesis and verifiable reinforcement learning. ASTRA integrates two complementary components. First, a pipeline that leverages the static topology of tool-call graphs synthesizes diverse, structurally grounded trajectories, instilling broad and transferable tool-use competence. Second, an environment synthesis framework that captures the rich, compositional topology of human semantic reasoning converts decomposed question-answer traces into independent, code-executable, and rule-verifiable environments, enabling deterministic multi-turn RL. Based on this method, we develop a unified training methodology that integrates SFT with online RL using trajectory-level rewards to balance task completion and interaction efficiency. Experiments on multiple agentic tool-use benchmarks demonstrate that ASTRA-trained models achieve state-of-the-art performance at comparable scales, approaching closed-source systems while preserving core reasoning ability. We release the full pipelines, environments, and trained models at github.com/LianjiaTech/astra.
Key Techniques

tool-call graph synthesis
environment synthesis from QA traces
supervised fine-tuning with online RL
trajectory-level reward optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.84ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.83ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.82SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.33Nanbeige4.1-3B: A Small General Model that Reasons, Aligns, and Acts0.26
Environment Synthesis via Question-Answer Trace Decomposition (ASTRA Environment Synthesis)
Core Idea: Convert human question-answer reasoning traces into independent, code-executable, and deterministically verifiable multi-turn environments by capturing and decomposing the compositional structure of semantic reasoning.
Problem It Solves: RL training for multi-turn agent behavior is typically limited to simulated, non-verifiable environments; environment synthesis prevents access to ground truth answers and makes it hard to compute reliable step-level or trajectory-level reward signals, especially for long-horizon tasks.
How It Works:

Collect human reasoning traces consisting of decomposed question-answer pairs (e.g., sub-goals in solving a complex problem)
Extract the semantic dependencies between QA pairs (which sub-answers inform which downstream questions)
Codify each QA pair as an independent, executable code-based environment where the agent must solve the specific sub-problem and receive a verifiable true/false success signal
Compose these independent environments into multi-turn environments where the agent’s outputs from one turn serve as inputs to the next turn, with deterministic rule-based reward functions

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Human Reasoning<br/>Traces"] --> B["Decompose into<br/>QA Pairs"]
    B --> C["Extract Semantic<br/>Dependencies"]
    C --> D["Codify as<br/>Executable Envs"]
    D --> E["Compose into<br/>Multi-Turn Envs"]
    E --> F["Verifiable Reward<br/>Signals"]

Where It’s Useful:

Reinforcement learning training for multi-turn language model agents where trajectory-level and step-level rewards are needed
Creating deterministic evaluation benchmarks where correctness can be verified by code execution and rules
Training agents on complex, compositional reasoning tasks (math, code, logical inference) with reliable feedback
Enabling stable long-horizon RL by providing dense, verifiable reward signals at each interaction step

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires upfront human effort to decompose reasoning traces into interpretable QA pairs with clear semantic dependencies
Generalization is limited by the diversity of human reasoning patterns captured in the trace data
May not capture all valid solution strategies if the decomposition is too prescriptive or rigid
Reward signals are binary/rule-based, which may miss nuanced aspects of solution quality

Internal Use Cases:

Could apply to training rankers or recommendation systems with multi-step decision processes (e.g., filter → rank → personalize) where each step has verifiable intermediate outputs
Useful for offline RL in RecSys where historical user interactions are decomposed into sub-goals (e.g., user searches → clicks → conversions) with ground truth labels at each stage


Integrated SFT + Online RL with Trajectory-Level Rewards (ASTRA Training Methodology)
Core Idea: Unify supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and online reinforcement learning by using trajectory-level rewards that balance task completion success with interaction efficiency, training agents that are both capable and sample-efficient.
Problem It Solves: Existing agent training methods rely exclusively on either SFT (which doesn’t optimize for task-specific efficiency and may overfit to training data) or online RL (which is sample-inefficient and unstable without good initialization), leading to agents that cannot balance correctness with efficiency or that require prohibitive amounts of exploration.
How It Works:

Pretrain the language model agent via supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on synthesized trajectories to establish a strong prior on tool-use patterns and general reasoning
Collect online trajectories by running the pretrained model in the code-executable multi-turn environments generated by environment synthesis
For each collected trajectory τ, compute a trajectory-level reward signal R(τ) that combines: (a) task completion binary reward (1 if final answer correct, 0 otherwise) and (b) efficiency penalty based on interaction count or total length (penalize trajectories with excessive tool calls)
Apply policy gradient or REINFORCE-style RL to optimize the log-probability of high-reward trajectories, refining the agent to balance both objectives
Alternate between environment-guided exploration (sampling from environments) and policy updates (gradient steps on trajectory rewards)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Synthesized<br/>Trajectories"] --> B["SFT Pretraining"]
    B --> C["Pretrained<br/>Agent"]
    C --> D["Online Rollouts<br/>in Environments"]
    D --> E["Compute<br/>Trajectory Rewards"]
    E --> F["Policy Gradient<br/>Update"]
    F --> G["Refined<br/>Agent"]
    G -.->|iterate| D

Where It’s Useful:

Training robust tool-using agents that must make correct decisions while minimizing computational cost (e.g., API calls)
Balancing accuracy and efficiency in multi-step reasoning tasks without manual hyperparameter tuning for each objective
Scaling agent training to multiple benchmark tasks where efficiency constraints vary
Enabling stable long-horizon learning by grounding RL in verifiable, code-executable environments

Limitations / Caveats:

Trajectory-level rewards are sparse, which can lead to high variance in gradient estimates; requires careful reward shaping or variance reduction techniques
The balance between task completion and efficiency is controlled by the reward function design; poor weighting can lead to trivial solutions (always complete the task inefficiently or always fail early)
Requires that the synthesized environments are representative of real task distributions; distribution shift between training and deployment can degrade performance
SFT pretraining quality directly impacts online RL stability; weak pretraining may lead to poor initial exploration and slow convergence

Internal Use Cases:

Could apply to training recommendation agents that must retrieve relevant items while minimizing database queries or API calls
Useful for RecSys ranking agents that learn to balance recommendation quality with latency constraints in production systems

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-01-31-Accelerating-Gener.title: Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression
literature/2026-01-31-Accelerating-Gener.content:
Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #7 (score: 0.91, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Although generative recommenders demonstrate improved performance with longer sequences, their real-time deployment is hindered by substantial computational costs. To address this challenge, we propose a simple yet effective method for compressing long-term user histories by leveraging inherent item categorical features, thereby preserving user interests while enhancing efficiency. Experiments on two large-scale datasets demonstrate that, compared to the influential HSTU model, our approach achieves up to a 6x reduction in computational cost and up to 39% higher accuracy at comparable cost (i.e., similar sequence length).
Key Techniques

sequence compression
categorical feature leveraging
computational cost reduction
long-term user history modeling

Related Litnotes

literature/2026-01-31-Adaptive-Confidenc.title: Adaptive Confidence Gating in Multi-Agent Collaboration for Efficient and Optimized Code Generation
literature/2026-01-31-Adaptive-Confidenc.content:
Adaptive Confidence Gating in Multi-Agent Collaboration for Efficient and Optimized Code Generation
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #12 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM
Abstract
While Large Language Models (LLMs) have catalyzed breakthroughs in automated code generation, Small Language Models (SLMs) often encounter reasoning bottlenecks and failure loops when addressing complex logical requirements. To overcome these challenges, we propose DebateCoder, a multi-agent collaborative framework designed to improve the reasoning ability of SLMs (e.g., Pangu-1B) in resource-constrained environments. DebateCoder uses a structured role-playing protocol with three agents: User Agent (A_UA), Technical Agent (A_TA), and Quality Assurance Agent (A_QA). It also includes an Adaptive Confidence Gating mechanism with a 95% threshold to balance accuracy and inference efficiency. In addition, we introduce a multi-turn deliberation module and a reviewer-guided analytical debugging loop for orthogonal pre-generation debate and post-generation refinement. Experiments on HumanEval and MBPP show that DebateCoder achieves 70.12% Pass@1 on HumanEval, outperforming MapCoder while reducing API overhead by about 35%. These results indicate that collaborative protocols can mitigate limitations of small-parameter models and provide a scalable, efficient approach to high-quality automated software engineering.
Key Techniques

multi-agent collaborative framework
Adaptive Confidence Gating mechanism
multi-turn deliberation module
reviewer-guided analytical debugging loop

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityAdaptive Confidence Gating in Multi-Agent Collaboration for Efficient and Optimized Code Generation0.66Adaptive Confidence Gating in Multi-Agent Collaboration for Efficient and Optimized Code Generation0.62Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.25AgentStepper: Interactive Debugging of Software Development Agents0.24MATA: A Trainable Hierarchical Automaton System for Multi-Agent Visual Reasoning0.22
Multi-Agent Structured Role-Playing Protocol
Core Idea: Decompose code generation into three complementary agent roles—User Agent, Technical Agent, and Quality Assurance Agent—each with distinct responsibilities to enable structured multi-turn deliberation that improves reasoning.
Problem It Solves: SLMs lack the reasoning depth needed for complex logical requirements in code generation. Monolithic inference produces weak outputs. By decomposing the task into specialized roles with explicit turn-taking, the framework creates orthogonal perspectives that collectively mitigate individual model limitations.
How It Works:

User Agent (A_UA): Interprets user intent, clarifies requirements, and proposes initial solution candidates based on the problem specification
Technical Agent (A_TA): Evaluates technical feasibility, suggests optimizations, identifies edge cases, and refines solutions from a correctness perspective
Quality Assurance Agent (A_QA): Reviews proposed solutions against acceptance criteria, identifies bugs, generates test cases, and ensures robustness
Multi-turn deliberation: agents iterate through pre-generation debate phase where each agent provides input before code is generated
Output consensus: final code solution integrates feedback from all three perspectives

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Problem Specification] --> B[A_UA: Propose]
    B --> C[A_TA: Evaluate & Optimize]
    C --> D[A_QA: Review & Test]
    D --> E{Consensus Reached?}
    E -->|No| C
    E -->|Yes| F[Final Code]

Where It’s Useful:

Code generation tasks where logical correctness and edge case handling are critical
Systems requiring diverse viewpoints to improve reasoning (similar to ensemble approaches)
Resource-constrained environments where collaborative inference can boost SLM capability
Complex problem-solving domains where decomposition reduces individual model burden

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires coordination overhead across three agents; total latency may increase vs. single-agent baseline
Agent roles and responsibilities are task-specific; generalization to other domains unclear
No clear mechanism for resolving inter-agent disagreements beyond iteration
Quality depends heavily on prompt engineering and role definition for each agent

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking: ensemble rankers using diverse feature perspectives (A_UA = relevance, A_TA = quality, A_QA = safety)
Recommendation: multi-stage filtering with specialized agents (candidate generation, feature ranking, business rule enforcement)
Search refinement: debate-based query reformulation using specialized semantic and syntactic agents


Reviewer-Guided Analytical Debugging Loop
Core Idea: Apply post-generation refinement through an iterative debugging cycle where a QA reviewer (A_QA) identifies failures and guides the technical agent in targeted analytical fixes, creating orthogonal pre- and post-generation reasoning.
Problem It Solves: SLMs often produce code with subtle bugs that survive initial generation. The debugging loop provides systematic failure analysis and localized correction, avoiding expensive full regeneration while maintaining code quality.
How It Works:

QA Agent (A_QA) executes generated code on test cases and identifies failures (e.g., assertion errors, logic bugs)
A_QA performs analytical debugging: traces failure root cause, pinpoints buggy code section, generates diagnostic information
Provide failure context (error traces, failing test cases, expected vs. actual output) to Technical Agent (A_TA)
A_TA performs targeted refinement: modifies only the problematic code section(s) guided by diagnostic information
Iterate: re-test refined code; if new failures emerge, repeat steps 2-4; stop when all tests pass
Threshold: if confidence score (from A_QA) falls below 95%, trigger this loop; otherwise skip

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Generated Code] --> B[A_QA: Test & Analyze]
    B --> C{Tests Pass?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Accept Code]
    C -->|No| E[Extract Failure Info]
    E --> F[A_TA: Targeted Fix]
    F --> A
    D --> G[Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Code generation with test-driven validation (e.g., HumanEval, MBPP benchmarks)
Scenarios where full regeneration is expensive; incremental debugging is preferable
Systems requiring interpretable error correction (failure traces guide next steps)
Iterative refinement on constrained models where per-iteration cost matters

Limitations / Caveats:

Assumes meaningful test cases or error traces are available to guide debugging; weak test coverage limits effectiveness
Iterative nature increases latency; worst-case loop may require many rounds
Risk of local optima: targeted fixes may not address systemic logic errors requiring broader refactoring
Relies on A_TA’s ability to understand and fix issues from diagnostic information; LLM reasoning failures can perpetuate bugs
No hard stopping criterion beyond test passage; may produce technically correct but suboptimal solutions

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking refinement: iterative feature importance adjustment based on ranking metric failures
RecSys: diagnostic loops for improving click-through or conversion metrics by targeted item set modifications
Information retrieval: iterative query rewriting based on relevance assessment and failure analysis

literature/2026-01-31-AgentLongBench-A-C.title: AgentLongBench: A Controllable Long Benchmark For Long-Contexts Agents via Environment Rollouts
literature/2026-01-31-AgentLongBench-A-C.content:
AgentLongBench: A Controllable Long Benchmark For Long-Contexts Agents via Environment Rollouts
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #13 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Search, RAG, LLM
Abstract
The evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) into autonomous agents necessitates the management of extensive, dynamic contexts. Current benchmarks, however, remain largely static, relying on passive retrieval tasks that fail to simulate the complexities of agent-environment interaction, such as non-linear reasoning and iterative feedback. To address this, we introduce AgentLongBench, which evaluates agents through simulated environment rollouts based on Lateral Thinking Puzzles. This framework generates rigorous interaction trajectories across knowledge-intensive and knowledge-free scenarios. Experiments with state-of-the-art models and memory systems (32K to 4M tokens) expose a critical weakness: while adept at static retrieval, agents struggle with the dynamic information synthesis essential for workflows. Our analysis indicates that this degradation is driven by the minimum number of tokens required to resolve a query. This factor explains why the high information density inherent in massive tool responses poses a significantly greater challenge than the memory fragmentation typical of long-turn dialogues.
Key Techniques

Simulated environment rollouts
Lateral Thinking Puzzles framework
Dynamic information synthesis evaluation
Information density analysis

Related Litnotes
