literature/2026-01-31-Bridging-Graph-Str.title: Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning
literature/2026-01-31-Bridging-Graph-Str.content:
Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #6 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, LLM, Graph
Abstract
Temporal knowledge graph reasoning (TKGR) aims to predict future events by inferring missing entities with dynamic knowledge structures. Existing LLM-based reasoning methods prioritize contextual over structural relations, struggling to extract relevant subgraphs from dynamic graphs. This limits structural information understanding, leading to unstructured, hallucination-prone inferences especially with temporal inconsistencies. To address this problem, we propose IGETR (Integration of Graph and Editing-enhanced Temporal Reasoning), a hybrid reasoning framework that combines the structured temporal modeling capabilities of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with the contextual understanding of LLMs. IGETR operates through a three-stage pipeline. The first stage aims to ground the reasoning process in the actual data by identifying structurally and temporally coherent candidate paths through a temporal GNN, ensuring that inference starts from reliable graph-based evidence. The second stage introduces LLM-guided path editing to address logical and semantic inconsistencies, leveraging external knowledge to refine and enhance the initial paths. The final stage focuses on integrating the refined reasoning paths to produce predictions that are both accurate and interpretable. Experiments on standard TKG benchmarks show that IGETR achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong baselines with relative improvements of up to 5.6% on Hits@1 and 8.1% on Hits@3 on the challenging ICEWS datasets. Additionally, we execute ablation studies and additional analyses confirm the effectiveness of each component.
Key Techniques

Temporal GNN for path identification
LLM-guided path editing
Hybrid GNN-LLM reasoning framework

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning0.84Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning0.70LogitsCoder: Towards Efficient Chain-of-Thought Path Search via Logits Preference Decoding for Code Generation0.30NeuroSymActive: Differentiable Neural-Symbolic Reasoning with Active Exploration for Knowledge Graph Question Answering0.29Temp-R1: A Unified Autonomous Agent for Complex Temporal KGQA via Reverse Curriculum Reinforcement Learning0.21
LLM-Guided Path Editing and Refinement
Core Idea: Use LLM reasoning to detect and correct logical, semantic, and temporal inconsistencies in candidate paths extracted from GNNs, leveraging external knowledge to enhance path validity before final inference.
Problem It Solves: Graph-extracted paths may be structurally valid but logically or semantically flawed (e.g., violating commonsense, containing contradictory relations, or having conceptual misalignment). Raw GNN paths lack contextual reasoning to catch these issues. LLM-guided refinement bridges structural and semantic validity.
How It Works:

Input: Candidate paths from temporal GNN (sequences of entities and relations: e₁ —r₁→ e₂ —r₂→ … —rₙ→ eₙ₊₁)
Prompt LLM with: path sequence, entity/relation definitions, external knowledge context, and query goal
LLM performs path validation: checks for semantic consistency (do relations make logical sense?), temporal consistency (do timestamps align with real-world causality?), and factual accuracy (do facts contradict known knowledge?)
LLM generates refinements: reorder path hops if needed, substitute entities/relations with more semantically appropriate alternatives, insert missing intermediate steps, or prune invalid segments
Output: Edited paths with improved logical coherence, semantic alignment, and temporal validity

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["GNN Candidate<br/>Paths"] --> B["Serialize Paths<br/>+ Context"]
    B --> C["LLM Validation<br/>& Refinement"]
    C --> D["Check: Semantic<br/>Consistency?"]
    D -->|Issues Found| E["Generate<br/>Edits"]
    D -->|Valid| F["Refined Paths"]
    E --> F

Where It’s Useful:

Knowledge graph completion with semantic verification
Event prediction in domains requiring commonsense reasoning
Fact-checking and knowledge base cleaning
Temporal event chains (e.g., detecting anachronisms)

Limitations / Caveats:

LLM refinement adds latency and computational cost per path
LLMs can hallucinate even when “guided”—corrections may introduce new errors
Requires careful prompt engineering to balance over-aggressive editing vs. under-editing
May struggle with domain-specific semantics if not well-represented in training data

Internal Use Cases:

In LLM-based ranking: using path editing to refine retrieved candidate ranking lists by checking semantic coherence with user query intent
For recommendation explanations: editing user-item reasoning paths to ensure they’re both structurally sound (from interaction graphs) and semantically sensible (validated by LLM knowledge)


Path Integration and Ensemble Prediction
Core Idea: Synthesize multiple refined reasoning paths into a unified prediction by aggregating evidence from structurally validated, semantically refined, and temporally consistent paths.
Problem It Solves: Single-path reasoning is brittle and prone to errors. Multiple candidate paths capture different valid reasoning chains; integrating them improves robustness and allows confidence scoring. Naive averaging ignores path quality and relevance differences.
How It Works:

Input: Set of K refined paths from LLM-guided editing, each with confidence scores or quality metrics (path length, consistency score, LLM validation confidence)
Rank paths by quality: combine structural confidence (GNN scores), semantic validity (LLM refinement success), and temporal coherence metrics
Aggregate predictions:

For each refined path, extract predicted tail entity eₙ₊₁ and confidence cᵢ
Compute weighted ensemble: P(e|head, relation, time) = Σᵢ αᵢ · P(eᵢ|pathᵢ) where weights αᵢ reflect path quality
Alternative: use path voting with confidence thresholds


Output: Final predicted entity with confidence score and interpretable reasoning trace (top-k contributing paths)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Refined Paths<br/>with Scores"] --> B["Rank by Quality<br/>GNN + LLM Metrics"]
    B --> C["Extract Predictions<br/>& Confidences"]
    C --> D["Weighted<br/>Aggregation"]
    D --> E["Final Prediction<br/>+ Confidence"]
    E --> F["Interpretable Trace<br/>Top-k Paths"]

Where It’s Useful:

Temporal knowledge graph link prediction (multi-path evidence fusion)
Multi-hop reasoning systems requiring interpretability
Ensemble methods for improving prediction robustness
Confidence calibration in structured reasoning

Limitations / Caveats:

Weight aggregation strategy (how to combine path scores?) requires tuning and may be domain-sensitive
Interpretability benefit decreases if too many low-quality paths are included
Doesn’t account for path correlations—redundant paths may inflate confidence artificially
Ensemble overhead: storing and aggregating multiple paths increases memory and computation

Internal Use Cases:

In ranking systems: combining signals from multiple retrieval paths (e.g., collaborative filtering paths + content-based paths) with learned path quality weights
For LLM-based recommendation: ensemble multiple reasoning chains to ground predictions in diverse evidence while maintaining interpretability via contributing path display

Discussions & References

github.com

literature/2026-01-31-Comparing-how-Larg.title: Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery
literature/2026-01-31-Comparing-how-Larg.content:
Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #5 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, Embeddings, Search, RecSys, LLM
Abstract
This paper evaluates the performance of a large language model (LLM) based semantic search tool relative to a traditional keyword-based search for data discovery. Using real-world search behaviour, we compare outputs from a bespoke semantic search system applied to UKRI data services with the Consumer Data Research Centre (CDRC) keyword search. Analysis is based on 131 of the most frequently used search terms extracted from CDRC search logs between December 2023 and October 2024. We assess differences in the volume, overlap, ranking, and relevance of returned datasets using descriptive statistics, qualitative inspection, and quantitative similarity measures, including exact dataset overlap, Jaccard similarity, and cosine similarity derived from BERT embeddings. Results show that the semantic search consistently returns a larger number of results than the keyword search and performs particularly well for place based, misspelled, obscure, or complex queries. While the semantic search does not capture all keyword based results, the datasets returned are overwhelmingly semantically similar, with high cosine similarity scores despite lower exact overlap. Rankings of the most relevant results differ substantially between tools, reflecting contrasting prioritisation strategies. Case studies demonstrate that the LLM based tool is robust to spelling errors, interprets geographic and contextual relevance effectively, and supports natural-language queries that keyword search fails to resolve. Overall, the findings suggest that LLM driven semantic search offers a substantial improvement for data discovery, complementing rather than fully replacing traditional keyword-based approaches.
Key Techniques

semantic search using LLMs
keyword-based search comparison
BERT embeddings for similarity measurement
qualitative and quantitative evaluation methods

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityComparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.90Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.79Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.77Semantic Search At LinkedIn0.21Semantic Search At LinkedIn, LLM-Driven Autonomous Optimization for Industrial-Scale Recommendation Systems, and More!0.21
BERT Embedding-based Cosine Similarity Assessment
Core Idea: Using pre-trained BERT embeddings to compute cosine similarity between search result sets, measuring semantic coherence of returned datasets independent of exact keyword matching.
Problem It Solves: Exact dataset overlap metrics (Jaccard) miss cases where two systems return semantically equivalent but lexically distinct results; need a continuous similarity measure that captures semantic alignment without requiring identical outputs.
How It Works:

Dataset Representation: Convert each dataset’s metadata (title, description, keywords) into a single vector via BERT embeddings: v_dataset = BERT(concatenated_metadata)
Result Set Embedding: For each search query, represent the full result set as the mean pooled embedding across all returned datasets: v_resultset = (1/|R|) Σᵢ v_datasetᵢ, where R is the result set
Cosine Similarity Computation: Calculate cosine distance between semantic and keyword result set embeddings: sim(S, K) = (v_S · v_K) / (||v_S|| ||v_K||), yielding a score in [0, 1]
Aggregation: Report mean, median, and distribution of cosine similarities across all 131 queries to characterize overall semantic alignment

Where It’s Useful:

Evaluating whether different search strategies capture semantically coherent result sets even with low exact overlap
Information retrieval when ground truth relevance labels are unavailable
Cross-system comparison in federated or multi-model search architectures
Detecting when two systems are “saying different things” despite similar query intent

Limitations / Caveats:

Cosine similarity on mean-pooled embeddings loses fine-grained per-dataset ranking information; averaging flattens ranking quality differences
BERT embeddings are frozen pre-trained vectors; domain-specific datasets may not be optimally represented without fine-tuning on social science data
High cosine similarity between result sets does not imply high individual dataset relevance; both systems could be wrong but wrong in semantically similar ways
Sensitive to metadata quality and completeness; sparse or poor-quality dataset descriptions will produce noisy embeddings

Internal Use Cases:

LLM Embedding Quality: Assessing whether semantic search embeddings truly capture domain semantics or merely surface-level similarity
Search Ranking Evaluation: Complementary metric to normalized discounted cumulative gain (NDCG) when relevance judgments don’t exist

literature/2026-01-31-ConceptMoE-Adaptiv.title: ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation
literature/2026-01-31-ConceptMoE-Adaptiv.content:
ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #10 (score: 0.87, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Large language models allocate uniform computation across all tokens, ignoring that some sequences are trivially predictable while others require deep reasoning. We introduce ConceptMoE, which dynamically merges semantically similar tokens into concept representations, performing implicit token-level compute allocation. A learnable chunk module identifies optimal boundaries by measuring inter-token similarity, compressing sequences by a target ratio R before they enter the compute-intensive concept model. Crucially, the MoE architecture enables controlled evaluation: we reallocate saved computation to match baseline activated FLOPs (excluding attention map computation) and total parameters, isolating genuine architectural benefits. Under these conditions, ConceptMoE consistently outperforms standard MoE across language and vision-language tasks, achieving +0.9 points on language pretraining, +2.3 points on long context understanding, and +0.6 points on multimodal benchmarks. When converting pretrained MoE during continual training with layer looping, gains reach +5.5 points, demonstrating practical applicability. Beyond performance, ConceptMoE reduces attention computation by up to R^2times and KV cache by Rtimes. At R=2, empirical measurements show prefill speedups reaching 175% and decoding speedups up to 117% on long sequences. The minimal architectural modifications enable straightforward integration into existing MoE, demonstrating that adaptive concept-level processing fundamentally improves both effectiveness and efficiency of large language models.
Key Techniques

Dynamic token merging
Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture
Learnable chunk boundaries
Controlled compute reallocation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.77ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.69FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.26FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.24FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.22
Learnable Chunk Module (Boundary Detection)
Core Idea: A learned mechanism that identifies optimal token-merging boundaries by measuring semantic similarity between adjacent or nearby tokens, replacing hand-crafted heuristics with differentiable chunking.
Problem It Solves:
Deciding which tokens to merge is non-trivial—static rules (e.g., “merge every k tokens”) destroy semantic structure. The chunk module replaces fixed chunking with learned, data-dependent boundaries, enabling the model to adapt merging to actual sequence difficulty and semantic coherence.
How It Works:

Similarity Scoring: For each position or sliding window in the sequence, compute inter-token similarity (e.g., s_i = cosine_similarity(emb_i, emb_{i+1}) or learned scoring function f_θ([emb_i, emb_{i+1}])). This produces a similarity profile across the sequence.
Threshold-Based Segmentation: Apply a learned threshold or differentiable filtering (e.g., top-P selection of low-similarity boundaries) to decide chunk boundaries. Boundaries occur at positions where similarity drops below threshold, creating variable-length chunks.
Gradient-Based Optimization: The chunk module is differentiable end-to-end; boundaries adjust during training to maximize downstream task performance while respecting a target compression ratio R. This couples chunking to downstream MoE and task loss.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Token Embeddings<br/>(Length N)"] --> B["Pairwise Similarity<br/>Computation"]
    B --> C["Similarity Profile"]
    C --> D["Learned Threshold<br/>or Selection"]
    D --> E["Chunk Boundary Decisions"]
    E --> F["Variable-Length Chunks<br/>Respecting Ratio R"]

Where It’s Useful:

Adapting chunking to domain-specific patterns (e.g., code has different redundancy than prose)
Tasks where token importance varies (e.g., reasoning steps are dense, narration is sparse)
Online or continual learning where chunk boundaries refine over time
Vision-language scenarios where patch redundancy varies spatially (high redundancy in uniform backgrounds, low in complex regions)

Limitations / Caveats:

Boundary detection adds a learnable module with its own parameters; overfitting risk if chunk module is poorly regularized
Compression ratio R enforcement (e.g., target N/R chunks) may conflict with data-driven boundaries; requires careful loss balancing
Similarity metric choice is crucial—cosine distance may not capture semantic “mergeability” for all modalities
Training cost: computing similarity scores at every forward pass adds overhead that must be amortized by MoE FLOP savings
Inference-time chunking is deterministic but boundaries may be brittle (small embedding perturbations cause boundary flip)

Internal Use Cases:

Query-Document Matching: In retrieval systems, identify “boilerplate” token spans in documents (high inter-token similarity) and compress them into concept tokens before expensive cross-attention with user query, reducing quadratic attention cost
LLM Prompt Compression: Automatically merge redundant prompt tokens (e.g., repeated preambles, system messages) into concepts before expensive decoding, reducing KV cache and improving latency for long-prompt scenarios common in few-shot or RAG systems


Controlled Compute Reallocation for Fair MoE Comparison
Core Idea: Reallocate computation saved by token compression to match baseline MoE’s total activated FLOPs (excluding attention) and parameters, enabling isolation of genuine architectural benefits from compression gains.
Problem It Solves:
Token compression inherently reduces compute. Without careful accounting, it’s unclear whether ConceptMoE outperforms baselines because of better architectural design or simply because fewer tokens were processed. This technique ensures fair comparison by “spending” savings on additional MoE capacity or deeper expert layers.
How It Works:

Measure Baseline FLOPs: Calculate total activated FLOPs for standard MoE baseline (sum over all tokens and experts; excludes attention map computation which is shared).
Calculate Savings: ConceptMoE processes N/R tokens instead of N through experts, saving (1 - 1/R) × baseline FLOPs.
Reallocate Saved Compute: Add capacity to ConceptMoE model to consume the saved FLOPs:

Increase number of experts while keeping tokens-per-expert constant
Increase expert hidden dimensions (wider FFN layers)
Increase number of MoE layers with lower activation ratio
Ensure total parameters and activated parameters match or exceed baseline


Retest: Evaluate ConceptMoE with reallocated capacity against baseline, ensuring both models have equal compute budgets.

Where It’s Useful:

Benchmarking new LLM architectures to avoid conflating compression with actual improvements
Internal ML research where fair compute accounting is critical for publication and reproducibility
Comparing inference-time vs. train-time optimizations (e.g., layer looping benefits isolated from compression)
Any scenario where token count and compute are inversely linked and must be decoupled for honest evaluation

Limitations / Caveats:

Compute reallocation is approximate; attention and other components may have different scaling characteristics, making exact FLOPs matching impossible
Reallocated capacity (e.g., wider experts) may not be equally beneficial as original architecture; compute is not perfectly fungible
Does not account for memory bandwidth or cache effects, which vary with token sequence length—FLOPs alone is incomplete
Requires careful engineering to avoid confounding variables; easy to accidentally introduce unequal architectural changes

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking Model Upgrades: When proposing a new ranking architecture with compression, ensure comparison against baseline with equal activated capacity (e.g., wider hidden layers or more experts) to isolate true ranking quality gains
Production LLM Evaluation: Before deploying efficiency optimizations, enforce that served model improvements are genuine signal, not artifacts of reduced token processing, enabling confident decision-making on compute allocation

literature/2026-01-31-Dont-be-so-Stief-L.title: Don't be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold
literature/2026-01-31-Dont-be-so-Stief-L.content:
Don’t be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #2 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Key—value (KV) caching enables fast autoregressive decoding but at long contexts becomes a dominant bottleneck in High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) capacity and bandwidth. A common mitigation is to compress cached keys and values by projecting per-head matrixes to a lower rank, storing only the projections in the HBM. However, existing post-training approaches typically fit these projections using SVD-style proxy objectives, which may poorly reflect end-to-end reconstruction after softmax, value mixing, and subsequent decoder-layer transformations.   For these reasons, we introduce StiefAttention, a post-training KV-cache compression method that learns \emph{orthonormal} projection bases by directly minimizing \emph{decoder-layer output reconstruction error}. StiefAttention additionally precomputes, for each layer, an error-rank profile over candidate ranks, enabling flexible layer-wise rank allocation under a user-specified error budget. Noteworthy, on Llama3-8B under the same conditions, StiefAttention outperforms EigenAttention by 11.9 points on C4 perplexity and 5.4\% on 0-shot MMLU accuracy at iso-compression, yielding lower relative error and higher cosine similarity with respect to the original decoder-layer outputs.
Key Techniques

KV-cache compression
orthonormal projection learning
decoder-layer output reconstruction
error-rank profiling

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDon’t be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold0.83Don’t be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold0.77SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.21SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.19SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.19
Error-Rank Profile Precomputation (Layer-wise Rank Allocation)
Core Idea: Precompute the relationship between compression rank and decoder-layer output error for each layer independently, enabling efficient allocation of compression ranks across layers under a fixed error budget.
Problem It Solves:
In multi-layer architectures, different layers have different sensitivity to KV compression. A naive uniform rank allocation wastes budget on less-critical layers. Prior work didn’t provide mechanisms to identify which layers can tolerate aggressive compression vs. which need higher rank.
How It Works:

For each layer l, vary the rank r from 1 to d (full rank) and measure the resulting decoder-layer output reconstruction error
Build an error curve: error_l(r) = f(r) showing how layer l’s output error decreases as rank increases
Use these precomputed profiles to solve a constrained rank allocation problem: minimize total error subject to a global compression budget (e.g., total rank sum ≤ B or total HBM savings ≥ target)
Allocate higher ranks to layers with steeper error-vs-rank curves (more sensitive layers) and lower ranks to layers with flat curves (less sensitive)

Where It’s Useful:

Any transformer-based model where layerwise compression budgets must be optimized
Inference serving with variable latency/memory constraints
Comparing compression efficiency across different architectures

Limitations / Caveats:

Precomputation is expensive (requires sweeping ranks and measuring reconstruction for each layer separately)
Error profiles may not transfer across different input distributions or tasks
Assumes additivity of layer errors (may not hold if errors compound non-linearly)

Internal Use Cases:

Adaptive model compression: Allocate more compression to later layers (often less critical in transformers) and preserve early layers
Multi-constraint optimization: Balance latency, memory, and accuracy when deploying to heterogeneous hardware

literature/2026-01-31-DynaWeb-Model-Base.title: DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents
literature/2026-01-31-DynaWeb-Model-Base.content:
DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RecSys, RL
Abstract
The development of autonomous web agents, powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) and reinforcement learning (RL), represents a significant step towards general-purpose AI assistants. However, training these agents is severely hampered by the challenges of interacting with the live internet, which is inefficient, costly, and fraught with risks. Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) offers a promising solution by learning a world model of the environment to enable simulated interaction. This paper introduces DynaWeb, a novel MBRL framework that trains web agents through interacting with a web world model trained to predict naturalistic web page representations given agent actions. This model serves as a synthetic web environment where an agent policy can dream by generating vast quantities of rollout action trajectories for efficient online reinforcement learning. Beyond free policy rollouts, DynaWeb incorporates real expert trajectories from training data, which are randomly interleaved with on-policy rollouts during training to improve stability and sample efficiency. Experiments conducted on the challenging WebArena and WebVoyager benchmarks demonstrate that DynaWeb consistently and significantly improves the performance of state-of-the-art open-source web agent models. Our findings establish the viability of training web agents through imagination, offering a scalable and efficient way to scale up online agentic RL.
Key Techniques

Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL)
web world model prediction
on-policy rollouts with expert trajectory interleaving

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.77DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.73DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.73Hybrid-Gym: Training Coding Agents to Generalize Across Tasks0.25Self-Distillation Enables Continual Learning0.20
Interleaved Real and Synthetic Rollout Training
Core Idea: Randomly interleave real expert trajectories with on-policy synthetic rollouts during RL training to improve stability and sample efficiency.
Problem It Solves: Pure synthetic rollout RL training can suffer from compounding model errors and instability. Pure on-policy real interaction is costly. This hybrid approach balances learning from the synthetic environment while anchoring the policy with real behavioral examples.
How It Works:

Maintain a replay buffer containing both real expert trajectories and synthetic on-policy rollouts generated by the world model
During each training step, randomly sample a batch mixing real and synthetic trajectories with proportion p_real : (1 - p_real)
Train agent policy π on combined batch: L(π) computed over both real and synthetic trajectories
Periodically generate new on-policy synthetic rollouts using current policy π and world model M to keep synthetic data distribution aligned with policy
Continue interleaving until convergence or performance plateau

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Expert<br/>Trajectories"] --> C["Mixed Replay<br/>Buffer"]
    B["World Model M<br/>+ Policy π"] -->|generate| D["Synthetic<br/>Rollouts"]
    D --> C
    C -->|sample batch| E["Policy<br/>Training"]
    E -->|update| B

Where It’s Useful:

RL training with expensive real environments and access to demonstrations
Imitation learning scenarios with limited expert data
Domain adaptation and transfer learning (use expert data from source domain + synthetic data in target domain)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful tuning of the real/synthetic ratio p_real—too much synthetic data propagates model errors, too much real data reduces sample efficiency gains
Effective only if world model M is reasonably accurate; poor model quality can hurt performance
Assumes expert trajectories are available and representative of desired behavior

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys ranking: mix real user interactions with synthetic interactions predicted by user behavior models to augment limited feedback
LLM fine-tuning: combine real human-generated trajectories with model-predicted continuations for dialogue/task completion training

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-01-31-ECO-Quantized-Trai.title: ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights
literature/2026-01-31-ECO-Quantized-Trai.content:
ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #8 (score: 0.90, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Quantization has significantly improved the compute and memory efficiency of Large Language Model (LLM) training. However, existing approaches still rely on accumulating their updates in high-precision: concretely, gradient updates must be applied to a high-precision weight buffer, known as \textit{master weights}. This buffer introduces substantial memory overhead, particularly for Sparse Mixture of Experts (SMoE) models, where model parameters and optimizer states dominate memory usage. To address this, we introduce the Error-Compensating Optimizer (ECO), which eliminates master weights by applying updates directly to quantized parameters. ECO quantizes weights after each step and carefully injects the resulting quantization error into the optimizer momentum, forming an error-feedback loop with no additional memory. We prove that, under standard assumptions and a decaying learning rate, ECO converges to a constant-radius neighborhood of the optimum, while naive master-weight removal can incur an error that is inversely proportional to the learning rate. We show empirical results for pretraining small Transformers (30-800M), a Gemma-3 1B model, and a 2.1B parameter Sparse MoE model with FP8 quantization, and fine-tuning DeepSeek-MoE-16B in INT4 precision. Throughout, ECO matches baselines with master weights up to near-lossless accuracy, significantly shifting the static memory vs validation loss Pareto frontier.
Key Techniques

Error-Compensating Optimizer (ECO)
quantization error injection into optimizer momentum
FP8/INT4 weight quantization
master weight elimination

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.99ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.90ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.76Quantized Evolution Strategies: High-precision Fine-tuning of Quantized LLMs at Low-precision Cost0.21SnapMLA: Efficient Long-Context MLA Decoding via Hardware-Aware FP8 Quantized Pipelining0.15

literature/2026-01-31-EWSJF-An-Adaptive-.title: EWSJF: An Adaptive Scheduler with Hybrid Partitioning for Mixed-Workload LLM Inference
literature/2026-01-31-EWSJF-An-Adaptive-.content:
EWSJF: An Adaptive Scheduler with Hybrid Partitioning for Mixed-Workload LLM Inference
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #11 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, LLM
Code: anonymous.4open.science/r/vllm_0110-32D8
Abstract
Serving Large Language Models (LLMs) under mixed workloads—short, latency-sensitive interactive queries alongside long, throughput-oriented batch requests—poses a fundamental scheduling challenge. Standard First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) policies suffer from severe head-of-line blocking, leading to high tail latency and underutilized hardware. We introduce EWSJF (Effective Workload-based Shortest Job First), an adaptive request-level scheduler that learns workload structure in real time to jointly improve fairness and throughput. EWSJF operates upstream of execution-level schedulers and integrates four components: (1) Refine-and-Prune, an unsupervised partitioning algorithm that discovers performance-homogeneous request groups; (2) Dynamic Queue Routing for assigning requests to these groups; (3) Density-Weighted Scoring, a context-aware prioritization function balancing urgency and fairness; and (4) Bayesian Meta-Optimization, which continuously tunes scoring and partitioning parameters based on live performance feedback. Implemented in vLLM, EWSJF improves end-to-end throughput by over 30% and reduces average Time-To-First-Token for short requests by up to 4x compared to FCFS. These results demonstrate that adaptive, learning-based request scheduling is a critical missing layer for efficient and responsive LLM serving. Implementation available at anonymous.4open.science/r/vllm_0110-32D8.
Key Techniques

Refine-and-Prune unsupervised partitioning
Dynamic Queue Routing
Density-Weighted Scoring
Bayesian Meta-Optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityEWSJF: An Adaptive Scheduler with Hybrid Partitioning for Mixed-Workload LLM Inference0.66EWSJF: An Adaptive Scheduler with Hybrid Partitioning for Mixed-Workload LLM Inference0.64SuperInfer: SLO-Aware Rotary Scheduling and Memory Management for LLM Inference on Superchips0.13RedVisor: Reasoning-Aware Prompt Injection Defense via Zero-Copy KV Cache Reuse0.12Towards Resiliency in Large Language Model Serving with KevlarFlow0.10

literature/2026-01-31-FBS-Modeling-Nativ.title: FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer
literature/2026-01-31-FBS-Modeling-Nativ.content:
FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #3 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Transformer, LLM
Abstract
Large language models (LLMs) excel across many tasks, yet inference is still dominated by strictly token-by-token autoregression. Existing acceleration methods largely patch this pipeline and miss core human-reading ingredients: content-adaptive foresight, chunk-structure-aware compute allocation, and train—test consistency for preview/skimming. We propose the \textbf{Fovea-Block-Skip Transformer} (FBS), which injects a causal, trainable loop into Transformers via Parafovea-Attention Window (PAW), Chunk-Head (CH), and Skip-Gate (SG). Across diverse benchmarks, FBS improves the quality-efficiency trade-off without increasing parameters, and ablations show the three modules are complementary.
Key Techniques

Parafovea-Attention Window
Chunk-Head
Skip-Gate
content-adaptive foresight

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityFBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.67FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.66ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.26ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.25Light Forcing: Accelerating Autoregressive Video Diffusion via Sparse Attention0.24
Chunk-Head (CH)
Core Idea: Groups tokens into fixed-size chunks and applies separate attention heads within each chunk, enabling parallel computation and reducing redundancy by allocating compute proportionally to chunk structure rather than uniformly across all tokens.
Problem It Solves: Standard multi-head attention processes all tokens independently with identical compute. This wastes resources on tokens that could be grouped (punctuation, conjunctions) and misses opportunities to leverage structure. CH allocates compute more efficiently by processing chunks as units, allowing parallel processing and head specialization per chunk.
How It Works:

Partition input sequence into non-overlapping chunks of size c: [tokens 0 to c-1], [tokens c to 2c-1], etc.
For each chunk, apply independent attention computation within that chunk’s tokens
Create “chunk-level” representations by pooling or selecting representative tokens from each chunk (e.g., mean, max, or last token)
Optionally compute limited inter-chunk attention using chunk-level representations before expanding back to token-level

Architecture:
flowchart TD
    A["Input sequence"] --> B["Partition into chunks of size c"]
    B --> C["Intra-chunk attention<br/>in parallel for each chunk"]
    C --> D["Create chunk representations<br/>Pool or select tokens"]
    D --> E["Optional: Inter-chunk<br/>attention on summaries"]
    E --> F["Expand back to token-level"]
    F --> G["Output"]

Where It’s Useful:

Documents with clear structural boundaries (chapters, paragraphs, sentences)
Batch processing with variable-length sequences (chunks reduce padding overhead)
Hierarchical language understanding where local coherence is primary concern
Distributed or accelerator-friendly implementations (chunks map to parallel processors)

Limitations / Caveats:

Hard chunk boundaries may split semantically meaningful units (e.g., multi-line dialogue)
Intra-chunk-only attention means tokens at opposite ends of sequence have no direct attention path
Chunk size is fixed hyperparameter; may be suboptimal for variable-structure text
Information loss from chunk pooling; fine-grained token distinctions within a chunk are compressed

Internal Use Cases:

Candidate ranking where documents are chunked and top chunks are retrieved before full re-ranking
Hierarchical recommendation where user history is chunked by time period or session, with attention within/across temporal chunks
Reducing memory footprint in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) by chunking documents for embedding


Skip-Gate (SG)
Core Idea: A learnable gating mechanism that selectively skips full transformer computation for certain positions (tokens or chunks), routing important positions through full attention while using lightweight approximations (e.g., identity or projection) for less important ones.
Problem It Solves: Not all tokens require equal computational investment; many are redundant or context-filler (filler words, repetitions, obvious continuations). SG achieves content-adaptive computation by learning which positions can be processed more cheaply, reducing FLOPs without uniform token-by-token trade-offs.
How It Works:

For each position (token or chunk), compute a gate score g_i ∈ [0, 1] from previous layer hidden state h_i: g_i = σ(W_gate * h_i + b_gate)
If g_i > threshold τ, process position through full transformer (attention, MLP)
If g_i ≤ τ, skip full computation and apply lightweight fallback: h_i^out = identity(h_i) or α * h_i (residual scaling)
Gate parameters (W_gate, b_gate, threshold τ) are learned jointly with the transformer during training

Architecture:
flowchart TD
    A["Hidden state h_i"] --> B["Compute gate score: σ(W_gate * h_i)"]
    B --> C{"g_i > threshold?"}
    C -->|Yes| D["Full transformer block<br/>attention + MLP"]
    C -->|No| E["Lightweight fallback<br/>identity or projection"]
    D --> F["Output h_i^out"]
    E --> F

Where It’s Useful:

Inference acceleration on latency-critical systems (skip expensive attention for ~40-60% of positions)
Adaptive computation models for heterogeneous workloads (important documents get full processing, summaries get skipped)
Token-level importance prediction with learned gates (interpretable via gate activation patterns)
Variable-cost inference where budget per sequence is fixed (gates automatically balance trade-offs)

Limitations / Caveats:

Gate decisions are based only on local hidden state; global importance signals may be missed
Skipped positions may still be semantically important but appear unimportant locally (requires sufficient context in h_i)
Training may be unstable if gate threshold and network capacity are not balanced; oscillation between always-skip and never-skip
Hard thresholding is non-differentiable; likely uses straight-through estimators or soft gating with learned threshold
Risk of degrading quality if too many critical tokens are skipped; requires careful tuning

Internal Use Cases:

RecommendationSystems ranking where common user profiles or baseline candidates can be processed with cached/lightweight representations
Query-document matching where uninformative query terms (stopwords, low-TF-IDF) skip expensive interaction computations
Multi-stage retrieval pipelines where light ranker uses SG to skip expensive candidate evaluation until final stage


These three modules work together synergistically: PAW reduces attention complexity per position, CH groups computation by structure, and SG adaptively skips entire positions—together achieving “native parallel reading” that mimics how humans efficiently process long texts through selective, structured attention.
Discussions & References

huggingface.co
github.com

literature/2026-01-31-Generative-Chain-o.title: Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction
literature/2026-01-31-Generative-Chain-o.content:
Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #9 (score: 0.89, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Modeling long-term user behavior trajectories is essential for understanding evolving preferences and enabling proactive recommendations. However, most sequential recommenders focus on next-item prediction, overlooking dependencies across multiple future actions. We propose Generative Chain of Behavior (GCB), a generative framework that models user interactions as an autoregressive chain of semantic behaviors over multiple future steps. GCB first encodes items into semantic IDs via RQ-VAE with k-means refinement, forming a discrete latent space that preserves semantic proximity. On top of this space, a transformer-based autoregressive generator predicts multi-step future behaviors conditioned on user history, capturing long-horizon intent transitions and generating coherent trajectories. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that GCB consistently outperforms state-of-the-art sequential recommenders in multi-step accuracy and trajectory consistency. Beyond these gains, GCB offers a unified generative formulation for capturing user preference evolution.
Key Techniques

RQ-VAE with k-means refinement
semantic ID encoding
transformer-based autoregressive generation
multi-step behavior prediction

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityGenerative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.83Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.81Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.80GLASS: A Generative Recommender for Long-sequence Modeling via SID-Tier and Semantic Search0.28Unleash the Potential of Long Semantic IDs for Generative Recommendation0.23
Autoregressive Chain-of-Behavior Generation via Transformer
Core Idea: Use a transformer-based autoregressive decoder to predict multi-step user trajectories by conditioning on user history and generating semantic behaviors token-by-token, capturing long-horizon intent transitions.
Problem It Solves: Most sequential recommenders predict only the next item, ignoring dependencies across multiple future steps and missing long-term preference evolution. This fails to capture trajectory coherence and user intent drift over multiple interactions.
How It Works:

Encode user history (previous items/actions) using sequence encoder: h = Encoder(user_history)
Initialize generation with learned start token or encoded history state
Autoregressive decoding: for each step t ∈ {1, …, T}:

Condition transformer on [h, generated_tokens_{1:t-1}]
Predict next semantic ID token: p(id_t | h, id_{1:t-1})
Sample or beam-search to get id_t
Decode id_t back to item via semantic ID mapping


Output: Multi-step trajectory [item_1, item_2, …, item_T] with learned intent transitions

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    H["User History"] --> Enc["Sequence Encoder"]
    Enc --> S["Start Token"]
    S --> T1["Transformer Decoder<br/>Step 1"]
    T1 --> ID1["Semantic ID Pred"]
    ID1 --> Dec1["ID → Item"]
    ID1 --> T2["Transformer Decoder<br/>Step 2"]
    T2 --> ID2["Semantic ID Pred"]
    ID2 --> Dec2["ID → Item"]
    Dec1 --> Out["Trajectory:<br/>item_1, item_2, ..."]
    Dec2 --> Out

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-step trajectory prediction in recommendation systems
User behavior forecasting for proactive recommendations
Settings requiring coherent, intent-aligned item sequences
Long-horizon planning in conversational or session-based recommenders

Limitations / Caveats:

Autoregressive generation is slow at inference (sequential token sampling) vs. parallel alternatives
Exposure bias: training on ground truth tokens but inference on generated tokens may cause distribution mismatch
Beam search or sampling adds computational overhead; greedy decoding may miss high-quality trajectories
Assumes future user behavior can be modeled as a Markov chain conditioned on history (may fail for sudden preference shifts)

Internal Use Cases:

Could apply to ranking reranking pipelines that need multi-item coherence scoring
Useful for LLM-integrated recommendation where user trajectories inform prompt context
Compatible with reinforcement learning for optimizing long-term user satisfaction rather than greedy CTR

literature/2026-01-31-Heterogeneous-Comp.title: Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference
literature/2026-01-31-Heterogeneous-Comp.content:
Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #1 (score: 0.71, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
AI agent inference is driving an inference heavy datacenter future and exposes bottlenecks beyond compute - especially memory capacity, memory bandwidth and high-speed interconnect. We introduce two metrics - Operational Intensity (OI) and Capacity Footprint (CF) - that jointly explain regimes the classic roofline analysis misses, including the memory capacity wall. Across agentic workflows (chat, coding, web use, computer use) and base model choices (GQA/MLA, MoE, quantization), OI/CF can shift dramatically, with long context KV cache making decode highly memory bound. These observations motivate disaggregated serving and system level heterogeneity: specialized prefill and decode accelerators, broader scale up networking, and decoupled compute-memory enabled by optical I/O. We further hypothesize agent-hardware co design, multiple inference accelerators within one system, and high bandwidth, large capacity memory disaggregation as foundations for adaptation to evolving OI/CF. Together, these directions chart a path to sustain efficiency and capability for large scale agentic AI inference.
Key Techniques

Operational Intensity (OI) and Capacity Footprint (CF) metrics
disaggregated serving architecture
specialized prefill and decode accelerators
agent-hardware co-design

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHeterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.80Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.77PrefillShare: A Shared Prefill Module for KV Reuse in Multi-LLM Disaggregated Serving0.38SuperInfer: SLO-Aware Rotary Scheduling and Memory Management for LLM Inference on Superchips0.21RedVisor: Reasoning-Aware Prompt Injection Defense via Zero-Copy KV Cache Reuse0.20
Disaggregated Serving with Specialized Prefill/Decode Accelerators
Core Idea: Separate inference infrastructure for prefill (processing prompt tokens) and decode (generating output tokens) phases, using hardware specialized for each phase’s distinct OI/CF characteristics.
Problem It Solves: Prefill (high OI, compute-bound) and decode (low OI, memory bandwidth-bound with high CF) have fundamentally different computational characteristics; a single accelerator architecture wastes resources by being over-provisioned for one phase and under-provisioned for the other.
How It Works:

Partition agentic inference workflow into two stages:

Prefill: Process all input prompt tokens in parallel (e.g., N tokens in ~log(N) iterations), high arithmetic intensity
Decode: Generate output tokens sequentially, one token per step, low arithmetic intensity


Route prefill requests to accelerators optimized for high compute density and throughput (e.g., high FLOPs/watt, larger on-chip caches)
Route decode requests to accelerators optimized for memory bandwidth and low-latency response (e.g., wide memory buses, high GB/s per core)
Use request scheduling layer to balance load across prefill and decode clusters based on incoming request characteristics (prompt length, expected output length)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Agentic Request<br/>(prompt + context)"] --> B{Scheduler}
    B -->|Prefill Phase| C["Prefill Cluster<br/>(compute-optimized)"]
    C -->|KV Cache| D["Shared Memory<br/>(optical I/O)"]
    B -->|Decode Phase| E["Decode Cluster<br/>(bandwidth-optimized)"]
    D -->|KV Cache| E
    E -->|Output Tokens| F["Response"]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale inference serving for chat, coding assistants, and web agents with variable input/output lengths
Multi-tenant datacenters where request mixing (short prompts, long outputs vs. long prompts, short outputs) requires flexible resources
Long-context agentic workflows where KV cache becomes memory capacity bottleneck during decode

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful scheduling to minimize latency overhead of request routing between clusters
Prefill cluster idle time when decode-heavy requests dominate (or vice versa) reduces overall utilization
Shared state (KV cache) must be transferred between clusters, creating dependency on interconnect bandwidth
Operational complexity increases compared to monolithic inference cluster

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking systems with variable input feature counts and output ranks per request
LLM-based retrieval where query processing (prefill) and result ranking/re-ranking (decode-like) have different hardware needs


System-Level Heterogeneity with Compute-Memory Disaggregation via Optical I/O
Core Idea: Decouple compute and memory into separate system components connected by high-speed optical interconnects, enabling flexible matching of specialized compute accelerators to memory hierarchies based on workload characteristics.
Problem It Solves: Monolithic accelerators (e.g., single GPU) bundle compute and memory in fixed ratios; agentic inference requires flexible compute-to-memory ratios that vary across prefill/decode phases and model architectures (MoE, quantization, long-context), creating silicon waste and reduced efficiency.
How It Works:

Partition system into compute pools (specialized prefill/decode accelerators) and memory pools (DRAM, high-bandwidth memory hierarchies)
Connect via optical I/O fabric providing high-throughput, low-latency interconnect (overcoming electrical I/O bottlenecks that limit disaggregation viability)
Workload-aware mapping: route prefill requests to compute-heavy accelerators with modest memory attachment; route decode to bandwidth-optimized memory pools
Enable multiple inference accelerators per system to work in concert, each specialized for specific OI/CF regime
Allow dynamic reconfiguration of compute-memory bindings as agentic workflow phases transition

Architecture:
flowchart TB
    A["Request Scheduler"] --> B["Prefill<br/>Accelerator 1"]
    A --> C["Prefill<br/>Accelerator 2"]
    A --> D["Decode<br/>Accelerator 1"]
    A --> E["Decode<br/>Accelerator 2"]
    
    B --> F["Optical I/O<br/>Interconnect<br/>High BW, Low Latency"]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    
    F --> G["Large Capacity<br/>Memory Pool"]
    F --> H["High BW<br/>Memory Pool"]

Where It’s Useful:

Hyperscale inference datacenters serving diverse agentic workloads (chat, coding, web use, computer use)
Scenarios requiring dynamic hardware adaptation as model architectures evolve (GQA → MLA, dense → MoE)
Multi-tenant inference where heterogeneous request patterns justify specialized hardware
Future AI systems with hardware-software co-design constraints

Limitations / Caveats:

Optical I/O technology currently immature; deployment risk and integration complexity
Adds architectural complexity in memory coherence, scheduling, and fault tolerance
Requires new software stacks for workload-aware placement and dynamic reconfiguration
Higher upfront capital costs before achieving efficiency gains at scale
Optical interconnect may have latency overhead for very small (single-token) operations

Internal Use Cases:

Building RecSys inference pipelines where candidate retrieval (compute-heavy) and ranking (memory-heavy with large feature sets) benefit from separate acceleration
Scaling LLM-based agents that alternate between reasoning (compute-bound, small state) and retrieval (memory-bound, large context)

literature/2026-01-31-Idea2Story-An-Auto.title: Idea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives
literature/2026-01-31-Idea2Story-An-Auto.content:
Idea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #8 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, RAG, LLM, Graph
Abstract
Autonomous scientific discovery with large language model (LLM)-based agents has recently made substantial progress, demonstrating the ability to automate end-to-end research workflows. However, existing systems largely rely on runtime-centric execution paradigms, repeatedly reading, summarizing, and reasoning over large volumes of scientific literature online. This on-the-spot computation strategy incurs high computational cost, suffers from context window limitations, and often leads to brittle reasoning and hallucination. We propose Idea2Story, a pre-computation-driven framework for autonomous scientific discovery that shifts literature understanding from online reasoning to offline knowledge construction. Idea2Story continuously collects peer-reviewed papers together with their review feedback, extracts core methodological units, composes reusable research patterns, and organizes them into a structured methodological knowledge graph. At runtime, underspecified user research intents are aligned to established research paradigms, enabling efficient retrieval and reuse of high-quality research patterns instead of open-ended generation and trial-and-error. By grounding research planning and execution in a pre-built knowledge graph, Idea2Story alleviates the context window bottleneck of LLMs and substantially reduces repeated runtime reasoning over literature. We conduct qualitative analyses and preliminary empirical studies demonstrating that Idea2Story can generate coherent, methodologically grounded, and novel research patterns, and can produce several high-quality research demonstrations in an end-to-end setting. These results suggest that offline knowledge construction provides a practical and scalable foundation for reliable autonomous scientific discovery.
Key Techniques

offline knowledge construction
methodological knowledge graph
research pattern extraction
runtime intent alignment

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityIdea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives0.81Idea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives0.76ResearchGym: Evaluating Language Model Agents on Real-World AI Research0.19Towards Personalized Bangla Book Recommendation: A Large-Scale Multi-Entity Book Graph Dataset0.18LIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs0.18
Research Intent Alignment and Pattern Retrieval
Core Idea: Map underspecified user research intents to established research paradigms in the pre-built knowledge graph, enabling efficient retrieval and reuse of validated research patterns instead of open-ended generation.
Problem It Solves: LLM-based systems struggle with underspecified or novel research queries, often falling back to trial-and-error generation that wastes computational resources and produces brittle results. Aligning intents to existing paradigms grounds discovery in established practice.
How It Works:

Intent Parsing: Accept a user research concept or query (potentially underspecified) as input
Intent Embedding: Encode the research intent into a continuous representation (semantic embedding)
Graph Search: Query the pre-built methodological knowledge graph using the intent embedding to find semantically similar research paradigms and patterns
Pattern Ranking: Rank retrieved patterns by relevance, methodological quality (derived from peer review feedback), and applicability to the research intent
Pattern Selection: Return top-k validated research patterns that match the intent rather than generating new approaches
Composition: If needed, compose multiple patterns from the graph to address the full research intent

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["User Research Intent"] --> B["Intent Embedding"]
    B --> C["Knowledge Graph Query"]
    C --> D["Pattern Ranking & Selection"]
    D --> E["Retrieved Research Patterns"]

Where It’s Useful:

Research planning and methodology selection
Exploratory research idea development
Transfer learning across research domains
Reducing hallucination in LLM-based research generation

Limitations / Caveats:

Retrieval quality is bounded by what exists in the knowledge graph; truly novel research approaches not in the graph cannot be discovered
Intent embedding quality depends on the embedding model and training data; domain-specific intents may be misrepresented
Pattern composition for multi-intent queries requires careful handling to avoid incompatible pattern combinations
May bias research toward established paradigms, potentially suppressing genuinely innovative directions

Internal Use Cases:

Retrieval-augmented generation for LLM-based research assistants (replacing generic document retrieval with pattern-level retrieval)
Ranking and filtering research ideas in idea generation systems
Seeding LLM prompts with validated methodological patterns rather than raw literature

Discussions & References

huggingface.co
github.com
github.com

literature/2026-01-31-MALLOC-Benchmarkin.title: MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation
literature/2026-01-31-MALLOC-Benchmarkin.content:
MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #12 (score: 0.85, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The scaling law, which indicates that model performance improves with increasing dataset and model capacity, has fueled a growing trend in expanding recommendation models in both industry and academia. However, the advent of large-scale recommenders also brings significantly higher computational costs, particularly under the long-sequence dependencies inherent in the user intent of recommendation systems. Current approaches often rely on pre-storing the intermediate states of the past behavior for each user, thereby reducing the quadratic re-computation cost for the following requests. Despite their effectiveness, these methods often treat memory merely as a medium for acceleration, without adequately considering the space overhead it introduces. This presents a critical challenge in real-world recommendation systems with billions of users, each of whom might initiate thousands of interactions and require massive memory for state storage. Fortunately, there have been several memory management strategies examined for compression in LLM, while most have not been evaluated on the recommendation task. To mitigate this gap, we introduce MALLOC, a comprehensive benchmark for memory-aware long sequence compression. MALLOC presents a comprehensive investigation and systematic classification of memory management techniques applicable to large sequential recommendations. These techniques are integrated into state-of-the-art recommenders, enabling a reproducible and accessible evaluation platform. Through extensive experiments across accuracy, efficiency, and complexity, we demonstrate the holistic reliability of MALLOC in advancing large-scale recommendation. Code is available at anonymous.4open.science/r/MALLOC.
Key Techniques

Large-scale sequential recommendation models
Memory management for state compression
Pre-storing intermediate user behavior states
Memory-aware long sequence compression benchmark

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation0.99MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation0.99MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation0.98MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation0.96Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression0.17

literature/2026-01-31-Masked-Diffusion-G.title: Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation
literature/2026-01-31-Masked-Diffusion-G.content:
Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #6 (score: 0.92, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Generative recommendation (GR) typically first quantizes continuous item embeddings into multi-level semantic IDs (SIDs), and then generates the next item via autoregressive decoding. Although existing methods are already competitive in terms of recommendation performance, directly inheriting the autoregressive decoding paradigm from language models still suffers from three key limitations: (1) autoregressive decoding struggles to jointly capture global dependencies among the multi-dimensional features associated with different positions of SID; (2) using a unified, fixed decoding path for the same item implicitly assumes that all users attend to item attributes in the same order; (3) autoregressive decoding is inefficient at inference time and struggles to meet real-time requirements. To tackle these challenges, we propose MDGR, a Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation framework that reshapes the GR pipeline from three perspectives: codebook, training, and inference. (1) We adopt a parallel codebook to provide a structural foundation for diffusion-based GR. (2) During training, we adaptively construct masking supervision signals along both the temporal and sample dimensions. (3) During inference, we develop a warm-up-based two-stage parallel decoding strategy for efficient generation of SIDs. Extensive experiments on multiple public and industrial-scale datasets show that MDGR outperforms ten state-of-the-art baselines by up to 10.78%. Furthermore, by deploying MDGR on a large-scale online advertising platform, we achieve a 1.20% increase in revenue, demonstrating its practical value.
Key Techniques

Parallel codebook
Adaptive masking supervision
Warm-up-based two-stage parallel decoding
Masked diffusion generative framework

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMasked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.72Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.72Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.71Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.62End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.29
Parallel Codebook Design
Core Idea: Decompose the traditional single sequential SID into independent parallel SID sequences (one per feature dimension), enabling simultaneous diffusion across multiple item attribute dimensions.
Problem It Solves:

Single sequential SID forces a fixed ordering of feature generation, but different users may attend to different item attributes in different orders
Sequential dependencies in traditional SID prevent efficient parallel generation
One-dimensional SID representation loses structural information about multi-faceted items

How It Works:

Feature Decomposition: Identify and separate key item dimensions (e.g., category ID, brand ID, price-range ID, rating-tier ID) as independent attribute spaces
Per-Dimension Quantization: Apply separate quantization codebooks to each dimension:

Dimension 1: Item category → codebook C₁ of size K₁
Dimension 2: Brand → codebook C₂ of size K₂
… Dimension d: Attribute d → codebook Cₐ of size Kₐ


Parallel SID Representation: For each item, generate SID tuples: (sid₁, sid₂, …, sidₐ) where sidᵢ ∈ Cᵢ, rather than a single concatenated sequence
Embedding Fusion: Combine embeddings from all dimensions before passing to the diffusion model: combined_embedding = FUSION(emb₁, emb₂, …, embₐ)

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-attribute recommendation where items have naturally distinct feature types
Product recommendation (color, size, brand, category are independent)
Real estate recommendation (location, property type, price, amenities)
Any domain with heterogeneous item attributes

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires domain knowledge to identify which attributes should be separate dimensions vs. features within a single dimension
Naive parallelization loses any inherent dependencies between attributes (e.g., certain brands only available in certain price ranges)
Codebook sizes must be chosen carefully; too many dimensions with large vocabularies increases parameter count
Fusion function design is non-trivial and domain-specific

Internal Use Cases:

Recommendation systems: Replace single SID with multi-dimensional representation for richer item descriptions
Language models: Not directly applicable (tokens have intrinsic sequence order), but concept could inform multi-modal token representations


Adaptive Masking Supervision (Temporal + Sample Dimensions)
Core Idea: During diffusion training, dynamically construct different masking patterns across both timesteps (temporal) and training examples (sample), forcing the model to learn multiple valid generation paths instead of a single fixed path.
Problem It Solves:

Standard masked language model training uses fixed masking ratios/patterns, leading to one canonical generation order
Users don’t all attend to item attributes in the same sequence; fixed masking assumes homogeneous attention
Insufficient training diversity limits the model’s ability to handle diverse inference-time generation strategies

How It Works:


Temporal Masking Variation: For each diffusion timestep t ∈ [0, T], randomly select different subsets of SID dimensions to mask:

Timestep t₁: mask dimensions {1, 3, 5} → denoise dimensions {2, 4, 6}
Timestep t₂: mask dimensions {2, 4} → denoise dimensions {1, 3, 5, 6}
Timestep t₃: mask all dimensions randomly
This ensures the model learns to denoise from various partial observation states



Sample Masking Variation: Across different training examples in a batch, apply different masking schedules:

Item A: gradually unmask left-to-right
Item B: gradually unmask right-to-left
Item C: randomly unmask dimensions each step
Training loss: L = Σ ||denoise_prediction(masked_sids) - target_sids||²



Trajectory Diversity: Each training sample follows a unique corruption-to-clean trajectory, preventing overfitting to a single generation path


Where It’s Useful:

Masked language model training for flexible generation
Diffusion model training where multiple valid decoding orders exist
Recommendation systems with heterogeneous user preferences over attribute importance
Any conditional generation task with multiple valid output orderings

Limitations / Caveats:

Significantly increases training complexity and computational cost (must compute denoising losses for many different masking patterns)
Requires careful hyperparameter tuning: masking ratios, temporal schedules, sampling strategies
No guarantee that all learned paths are equally high-quality; some may converge to suboptimal solutions
Difficult to analyze which generation orders the model actually learns (interpretability challenge)

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys: Could train multi-path recommendation models where different user types follow different attribute exploration sequences
LLM training: Could improve generation diversity for tasks where multiple valid orderings exist (e.g., generating sets of related concepts)

Extending the Generative Recommendation Paradigm
This MDGR paper directly extends the generative recommendation framework described in the matched vault notes, particularly addressing limitations of the autoregressive approaches highlighted in “Actions Speak Louder than Words.” While that work established treating user actions as a language and applying Transformer architectures with sequential ID generation, MDGR fundamentally challenges the assumption that autoregressive decoding is optimal for this domain. The paper identifies three critical weaknesses in the language model inheritance pattern: inability to jointly capture global dependencies across multi-dimensional semantic ID positions, the implicit assumption of uniform user attention ordering, and inference latency issues. These observations align with the “scaling limitations” and real-time requirements mentioned in the vault notes, but MDGR proposes a radically different solution—masked diffusion rather than simply scaling autoregressive models.
Bridging Autoregressive and Parallel Generation
The parallel codebook and masked diffusion training strategy in MDGR represents a conceptual bridge between the sequential transducer approach (from the Shaped.ai work) and the broader LLM-enhanced recommendation trends documented in “Generative Large Recommendation Model.” Rather than treating item generation purely as next-token prediction, MDGR adopts a denoising diffusion paradigm that constructs masking supervision adaptively across temporal and sample dimensions. This connects to the “feature interaction” and “user behavior modeling” improvements mentioned in the survey work—MDGR’s diffusion process can be seen as a sophisticated form of feature interaction that doesn’t assume a fixed temporal ordering. The two-stage warm-up decoding strategy also addresses the practical deployment concerns that the vault notes emphasize, achieving the real-time personalization goals while maintaining the generative modeling benefits.
Novel Contributions to the Knowledge Base
MDGR adds three distinctive perspectives absent from the existing notes: (1) the application of diffusion models specifically to recommendation’s semantic ID generation problem, moving beyond both traditional DLRMs and pure autoregressive generative approaches; (2) adaptive masking supervision that respects that different users may attend to item attributes in different orders—a nuanced view of personalization not explicitly captured in the sequential transducer framework; and (3) concrete evidence (10.78% improvement over SOTA) that parallel decoding can outperform autoregressive methods in this domain. This challenges the implicit assumption in the vault notes that “treating actions as language” necessarily means adopting language modeling’s left-to-right generation paradigm, opening a new research direction where diffusion-based generation may be more natural for multi-attribute item representation.

literature/2026-01-31-OneMall-One-Model-.title: OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios -- End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce
literature/2026-01-31-OneMall-One-Model-.content:
OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #4 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
In the wave of generative recommendation, we present OneMall, an end-to-end generative recommendation framework tailored for e-commerce services at Kuaishou. Our OneMall systematically unifies the e-commerce’s multiple item distribution scenarios, such as Product-card, short-video and live-streaming. Specifically, it comprises three key components, aligning the entire model training pipeline to the LLM’s pre-training/post-training: (1) E-commerce Semantic Tokenizer: we provide a tokenizer solution that captures both real-world semantics and business-specific item relations across different scenarios; (2) Transformer-based Architecture: we largely utilize Transformer as our model backbone, e.g., employing Query-Former for long sequence compression, Cross-Attention for multi-behavior sequence fusion, and Sparse MoE for scalable auto-regressive generation; (3) Reinforcement Learning Pipeline: we further connect retrieval and ranking models via RL, enabling the ranking model to serve as a reward signal for end-to-end policy retrieval model optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OneMall achieves consistent improvements across all e-commerce scenarios: +13.01% GMV in product-card, +15.32% Orders in Short-Video, and +2.78% Orders in Live-Streaming. OneMall has been deployed, serving over 400 million daily active users at Kuaishou.
Key Techniques

E-commerce Semantic Tokenizer
Transformer-based Architecture with Query-Former and Sparse MoE
Reinforcement Learning Pipeline for retrieval-ranking optimization

Related Litnotes
