literature/2026-02-01-Comparing-how-Larg.title: Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery
literature/2026-02-01-Comparing-how-Larg.content:
Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #6 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search, Embeddings, RAG, RecSys
Abstract
This paper evaluates the performance of a large language model (LLM) based semantic search tool relative to a traditional keyword-based search for data discovery. Using real-world search behaviour, we compare outputs from a bespoke semantic search system applied to UKRI data services with the Consumer Data Research Centre (CDRC) keyword search. Analysis is based on 131 of the most frequently used search terms extracted from CDRC search logs between December 2023 and October 2024. We assess differences in the volume, overlap, ranking, and relevance of returned datasets using descriptive statistics, qualitative inspection, and quantitative similarity measures, including exact dataset overlap, Jaccard similarity, and cosine similarity derived from BERT embeddings. Results show that the semantic search consistently returns a larger number of results than the keyword search and performs particularly well for place based, misspelled, obscure, or complex queries. While the semantic search does not capture all keyword based results, the datasets returned are overwhelmingly semantically similar, with high cosine similarity scores despite lower exact overlap. Rankings of the most relevant results differ substantially between tools, reflecting contrasting prioritisation strategies. Case studies demonstrate that the LLM based tool is robust to spelling errors, interprets geographic and contextual relevance effectively, and supports natural-language queries that keyword search fails to resolve. Overall, the findings suggest that LLM driven semantic search offers a substantial improvement for data discovery, complementing rather than fully replacing traditional keyword-based approaches.
Key Techniques

Semantic search using LLMs
BERT embeddings for similarity measurement
keyword-based search comparison
cosine similarity analysis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityComparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.90Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.80Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.79Semantic Search At LinkedIn0.22Semantic Search At LinkedIn, LLM-Driven Autonomous Optimization for Industrial-Scale Recommendation Systems, and More!0.21
BERT Embedding-Based Result Similarity Measurement
Core Idea: Applying pre-trained BERT embeddings to compute cosine similarity between datasets returned by different search systems, enabling quantitative assessment of semantic relevance despite low exact overlap.
Problem It Solves: Exact dataset overlap metrics (Jaccard similarity) poorly reflect whether semantically related results are returned; need quantitative measure of result quality when different systems return different but potentially valid datasets.
How It Works:

Embedding Generation: For each dataset in result sets A and B, encode dataset metadata (title, description, keywords) using pre-trained BERT model to obtain fixed-dimensional vector representations: v_i ∈ ℝ
Pairwise Similarity Computation: For each dataset returned by keyword search, compute cosine similarity to each dataset returned by semantic search:

sim(d_i, d_j) = (v_i · v_j) / (||v_i|| ||v_j||) where v_i, v_j are BERT embeddings


Result-Level Scoring: Aggregate pairwise similarities to assess overall semantic coherence of returned sets (mean similarity, maximum similarity, distribution analysis)
Comparative Analysis: Compare cosine similarity distributions between search methods to determine if one system consistently returns more semantically relevant results

Where It’s Useful:

Evaluating search quality when perfect ground truth relevance judgments are unavailable
Assessing semantic consistency of recommendation/retrieval results
Detecting when systems return different but thematically coherent results
Quantifying semantic distance between query interpretation strategies

Limitations / Caveats:

BERT embeddings are task-agnostic; fine-tuning on domain-specific data (social science metadata) would likely improve precision
Cosine similarity assumes relevance is monotonic with vector space proximity; some domain semantics may not map cleanly to embeddings
Metadata quality directly affects embedding quality; sparse or low-quality descriptions reduce discriminative power
High similarity scores don’t guarantee individual result relevance to actual user information need

Internal Use Cases:

Evaluating dense retrieval rerankers against lexical baselines
Assessing embedding-based recommendation systems without explicit click/rating signals
Quantifying result diversity vs. relevance tradeoffs in search result ranking

literature/2026-02-01-ConceptMoE-Adaptiv.title: ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation
literature/2026-02-01-ConceptMoE-Adaptiv.content:
ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #10 (score: 0.87, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Large language models allocate uniform computation across all tokens, ignoring that some sequences are trivially predictable while others require deep reasoning. We introduce ConceptMoE, which dynamically merges semantically similar tokens into concept representations, performing implicit token-level compute allocation. A learnable chunk module identifies optimal boundaries by measuring inter-token similarity, compressing sequences by a target ratio R before they enter the compute-intensive concept model. Crucially, the MoE architecture enables controlled evaluation: we reallocate saved computation to match baseline activated FLOPs (excluding attention map computation) and total parameters, isolating genuine architectural benefits. Under these conditions, ConceptMoE consistently outperforms standard MoE across language and vision-language tasks, achieving +0.9 points on language pretraining, +2.3 points on long context understanding, and +0.6 points on multimodal benchmarks. When converting pretrained MoE during continual training with layer looping, gains reach +5.5 points, demonstrating practical applicability. Beyond performance, ConceptMoE reduces attention computation by up to R^2times and KV cache by Rtimes. At R=2, empirical measurements show prefill speedups reaching 175% and decoding speedups up to 117% on long sequences. The minimal architectural modifications enable straightforward integration into existing MoE, demonstrating that adaptive concept-level processing fundamentally improves both effectiveness and efficiency of large language models.
Key Techniques

Dynamic token merging
learnable chunk boundaries
compute reallocation
layer looping

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.74ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.69HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.19FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.18OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.18
Implicit Compute Reallocation via MoE Integration
Core Idea: Leverage the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture to reallocate computational savings from token merging, redistributing the “freed up” FLOPs and parameters to expert routing decisions, ensuring fair performance comparison by matching total activated parameters and FLOPs to baseline MoE models.
Problem It Solves: Token merging reduces sequence length and thus total computation, making it impossible to isolate whether performance gains come from the merging technique itself or simply from doing less work. This confounds architectural contributions with computational advantages.
How It Works:

Measure FLOPs saved by token merging: if original sequence has N tokens and compressed sequence has N/R tokens, feedforward layer computation is reduced by factor R
Calculate total activated FLOPs for baseline MoE model at full sequence length (excluding attention map computation per the paper’s definition)
Design ConceptMoE’s expert routing and layer depths such that total activated FLOPs match the baseline MoE, reallocating saved computation to additional expert capacity or routing decisions
Match total learnable parameters (excluding attention) between ConceptMoE and baseline MoE by scaling expert width or number proportionally
Compare performance under these controlled conditions to isolate the architectural benefit of adaptive token-level computation from simple reduction in sequence length

Where It’s Useful:

Controlled ablation studies and fair comparison of sequence compression techniques
Research on compute-optimal allocation strategies where confounding variables must be isolated
Hardware-constrained deployments where total FLOPs must remain constant but distribution can change
Benchmarking new architectural modules (e.g., attention variants, pooling strategies) in fair experimental settings

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful instrumentation to measure and match FLOPs and parameters; differences in attention computation or batch processing can invalidate the comparison
The reallocation strategy itself (which saved compute to spend and how) is not specified in detail; suboptimal reallocation may hide true architectural benefits
Constraints (matched FLOPs, matched parameters) may not reflect real-world deployment scenarios where pure throughput or latency gains are the priority
Does not account for memory bandwidth, cache locality, or hardware-specific efficiency differences between dense and sparse/expert computation

Internal Use Cases:

Evaluating new token selection or compression techniques for recommendation systems: ensure gains are from smarter token allocation, not just sequence shortening
Ranking systems: validate that adaptive document sampling outperforms random sampling under equal computational budget
LLM fine-tuning: compare full-sequence training vs. condensed-sequence training while controlling for total gradient updates and parameter counts

literature/2026-02-01-Dont-be-so-Stief-L.title: Don't be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold
literature/2026-02-01-Dont-be-so-Stief-L.content:
Don’t be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #2 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Key—value (KV) caching enables fast autoregressive decoding but at long contexts becomes a dominant bottleneck in High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) capacity and bandwidth. A common mitigation is to compress cached keys and values by projecting per-head matrixes to a lower rank, storing only the projections in the HBM. However, existing post-training approaches typically fit these projections using SVD-style proxy objectives, which may poorly reflect end-to-end reconstruction after softmax, value mixing, and subsequent decoder-layer transformations.   For these reasons, we introduce StiefAttention, a post-training KV-cache compression method that learns \emph{orthonormal} projection bases by directly minimizing \emph{decoder-layer output reconstruction error}. StiefAttention additionally precomputes, for each layer, an error-rank profile over candidate ranks, enabling flexible layer-wise rank allocation under a user-specified error budget. Noteworthy, on Llama3-8B under the same conditions, StiefAttention outperforms EigenAttention by 11.9 points on C4 perplexity and 5.4\% on 0-shot MMLU accuracy at iso-compression, yielding lower relative error and higher cosine similarity with respect to the original decoder-layer outputs.
Key Techniques

KV-cache compression
orthonormal projection learning
decoder-layer output reconstruction
error-rank profiling
