literature/2026-02-01-DynaWeb-Model-Base.title: DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents
literature/2026-02-01-DynaWeb-Model-Base.content:
DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RL, RecSys
Abstract
The development of autonomous web agents, powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) and reinforcement learning (RL), represents a significant step towards general-purpose AI assistants. However, training these agents is severely hampered by the challenges of interacting with the live internet, which is inefficient, costly, and fraught with risks. Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) offers a promising solution by learning a world model of the environment to enable simulated interaction. This paper introduces DynaWeb, a novel MBRL framework that trains web agents through interacting with a web world model trained to predict naturalistic web page representations given agent actions. This model serves as a synthetic web environment where an agent policy can dream by generating vast quantities of rollout action trajectories for efficient online reinforcement learning. Beyond free policy rollouts, DynaWeb incorporates real expert trajectories from training data, which are randomly interleaved with on-policy rollouts during training to improve stability and sample efficiency. Experiments conducted on the challenging WebArena and WebVoyager benchmarks demonstrate that DynaWeb consistently and significantly improves the performance of state-of-the-art open-source web agent models. Our findings establish the viability of training web agents through imagination, offering a scalable and efficient way to scale up online agentic RL.
Key Techniques

Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL)
web world model prediction
policy rollout generation
expert trajectory interleaving

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.87DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.82DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.73AgentLongBench: A Controllable Long Benchmark For Long-Contexts Agents via Environment Rollouts0.20SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.18
Dyna-style Policy Training with Model Rollouts and Expert Mixing
Core Idea: Interleave simulated trajectories from the world model with real expert trajectories during RL training to improve stability, sample efficiency, and final policy performance.
Problem It Solves: Pure model-based rollouts suffer from compounding errors and distribution shift; pure behavioral cloning from experts limits exploration. Mixing both sources balances learning signal quality with coverage and exploration.
How It Works:

Maintain a replay buffer with two sources: (a) real expert trajectories collected from online interaction or demonstrations, (b) simulated rollouts generated from the learned world model by rolling out the current policy π
During each RL training step, sample a minibatch where some trajectories come from the real replay buffer and others from model-generated rollouts (random interleaving or fixed ratio)
Compute policy gradients (e.g., PPO, A3C, DQN) on the mixed batch: ∇_π J(π) ≈ E_{(τ ~ π, world model) ∪ (τ ~ expert)}[∇_π log π(a|s) A(s,a)]
Alternate: collect new real trajectories online, update world model, generate fresh rollouts, and repeat
The ratio of expert to simulated data can be annealed or kept fixed based on convergence

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["World Model"] -->|Generate Rollouts| B["Simulated Trajectories"]
    C["Expert Data"] -->|Store| D["Replay Buffer<br/>(Expert + Simulated)"]
    B --> D
    D -->|Sample Minibatch| E["Policy Gradient Update<br/>via RL Algorithm"]
    E -->|Updated Policy| A
    E -->|Updated Policy| F["New Online Collection"]
    F -->|Real Trajectories| C

Where It’s Useful:

Training agents in costly/risky environments (web automation, robotics, autonomous systems)
Scenarios with limited expert demonstrations; model-based rollouts augment the data
Combining offline (expert) and online (policy) learning signals
Bootstrapping a new policy from weak expert behaviors then improving via exploration

Limitations / Caveats:

Hyperparameter sensitivity: ratio of expert to simulated data, curriculum schedule, and replay buffer management significantly affect results
Expert data quality matters: poor expert trajectories can mislead the policy; bad data mixed in reduces effectiveness
Compounding errors still occur: even with mixing, simulated rollouts accumulate prediction errors; long-horizon tasks degrade faster
Scalability of world model: as environment complexity grows, training and maintaining an accurate world model becomes increasingly expensive

Internal Use Cases:

In recommendation systems: mix historical user interactions (expert) with model-predicted user trajectories (from a learned user behavior model) to train ranking policies
In LLM fine-tuning: combine human demonstrations with model-generated continuations, weighted by confidence or quality signals
Similar to Dyna-Q in tabular RL but extended to function approximation and continuous control domains

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-01-ECO-Quantized-Trai.title: ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights
literature/2026-02-01-ECO-Quantized-Trai.content:
ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #8 (score: 0.90, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Quantization has significantly improved the compute and memory efficiency of Large Language Model (LLM) training. However, existing approaches still rely on accumulating their updates in high-precision: concretely, gradient updates must be applied to a high-precision weight buffer, known as \textit{master weights}. This buffer introduces substantial memory overhead, particularly for Sparse Mixture of Experts (SMoE) models, where model parameters and optimizer states dominate memory usage. To address this, we introduce the Error-Compensating Optimizer (ECO), which eliminates master weights by applying updates directly to quantized parameters. ECO quantizes weights after each step and carefully injects the resulting quantization error into the optimizer momentum, forming an error-feedback loop with no additional memory. We prove that, under standard assumptions and a decaying learning rate, ECO converges to a constant-radius neighborhood of the optimum, while naive master-weight removal can incur an error that is inversely proportional to the learning rate. We show empirical results for pretraining small Transformers (30-800M), a Gemma-3 1B model, and a 2.1B parameter Sparse MoE model with FP8 quantization, and fine-tuning DeepSeek-MoE-16B in INT4 precision. Throughout, ECO matches baselines with master weights up to near-lossless accuracy, significantly shifting the static memory vs validation loss Pareto frontier.
Key Techniques

Error-Compensating Optimizer (ECO)
quantization error injection into optimizer momentum
FP8/INT4 quantization for LLMs
master weight elimination

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.90ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.89ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.74Quantized Evolution Strategies: High-precision Fine-tuning of Quantized LLMs at Low-precision Cost0.30SnapMLA: Efficient Long-Context MLA Decoding via Hardware-Aware FP8 Quantized Pipelining0.23
Master Weight Elimination with Direct Quantized Parameter Updates
Core Idea: Remove the high-precision master weight buffer entirely by applying optimizer updates directly to quantized weights, reducing memory overhead proportional to the number of model parameters.
Problem It Solves: Traditional quantized training maintains two weight representations—quantized weights for computation and high-precision master weights for gradient accumulation—doubling memory cost. This is particularly acute in models with billions of parameters and complex optimizer states.
How It Works:

Initialize weights in target quantization format (e.g., FP8): w_0^q \in \text{FP8}
Cast quantized weights to full-precision only temporarily for the gradient update computation
Apply the optimizer update step directly: w_t^+ = w_t^q + \text{UpdateRule}(g_t, m_t, ...) (in full-precision)
Immediately quantize the updated weights back to target format: w_{t+1}^q = Q(w_t^+)
Discard the full-precision intermediate value; no master weight buffer is retained between steps
The quantization error from step 4 is captured and fed back through the error-feedback mechanism to the next update

Where It’s Useful:

SMoE model training where parameter count × optimizer state memory is prohibitive
Single-GPU fine-tuning of billion-parameter models
Memory-constrained distributed training scenarios
Production training pipelines requiring maximum memory efficiency

Limitations / Caveats:

Without the error-feedback mechanism (ECO), naive master weight removal causes error accumulation inversely proportional to learning rate, degrading convergence
Requires hardware support for efficient FP8/INT4 operations to avoid compute overhead from repeated quantization
May introduce slightly higher numerical instability if quantization precision is too aggressive
Decaying learning rate schedule is necessary for theoretical convergence guarantees

Internal Use Cases:

Training dense ranking models for web search with FP8 quantization, reducing memory cost by ~50%
Fine-tuning embedding models (e.g., 1B-16B parameters) in INT4 to fit on single GPUs
Iterative refinement of recommendation rankers where multiple training runs are needed

literature/2026-02-01-EWSJF-An-Adaptive-.title: EWSJF: An Adaptive Scheduler with Hybrid Partitioning for Mixed-Workload LLM Inference
literature/2026-02-01-EWSJF-An-Adaptive-.content:
EWSJF: An Adaptive Scheduler with Hybrid Partitioning for Mixed-Workload LLM Inference
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #12 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, LLM
Code: anonymous.4open.science/r/vllm_0110-32D8
Abstract
Serving Large Language Models (LLMs) under mixed workloads—short, latency-sensitive interactive queries alongside long, throughput-oriented batch requests—poses a fundamental scheduling challenge. Standard First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) policies suffer from severe head-of-line blocking, leading to high tail latency and underutilized hardware. We introduce EWSJF (Effective Workload-based Shortest Job First), an adaptive request-level scheduler that learns workload structure in real time to jointly improve fairness and throughput. EWSJF operates upstream of execution-level schedulers and integrates four components: (1) Refine-and-Prune, an unsupervised partitioning algorithm that discovers performance-homogeneous request groups; (2) Dynamic Queue Routing for assigning requests to these groups; (3) Density-Weighted Scoring, a context-aware prioritization function balancing urgency and fairness; and (4) Bayesian Meta-Optimization, which continuously tunes scoring and partitioning parameters based on live performance feedback. Implemented in vLLM, EWSJF improves end-to-end throughput by over 30% and reduces average Time-To-First-Token for short requests by up to 4x compared to FCFS. These results demonstrate that adaptive, learning-based request scheduling is a critical missing layer for efficient and responsive LLM serving. Implementation available at anonymous.4open.science/r/vllm_0110-32D8.
Key Techniques

Refine-and-Prune unsupervised partitioning
Dynamic Queue Routing
Density-Weighted Scoring
Bayesian Meta-Optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityEWSJF: An Adaptive Scheduler with Hybrid Partitioning for Mixed-Workload LLM Inference0.80EWSJF: An Adaptive Scheduler with Hybrid Partitioning for Mixed-Workload LLM Inference0.64SuperInfer: SLO-Aware Rotary Scheduling and Memory Management for LLM Inference on Superchips0.19Towards Resiliency in Large Language Model Serving with KevlarFlow0.19Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.18
Dynamic Queue Routing
Core Idea: A real-time request assignment mechanism that routes incoming requests to appropriate partition queues based on learned workload characteristics and current queue state.
Problem It Solves: Once requests are partitioned into groups, the scheduler must decide which queue each new request should join. Naive routing (e.g., round-robin) ignores request-specific properties and queue dynamics, leading to imbalanced queues and suboptimal scheduling.
How It Works:

Feature Extraction: Extract request properties at arrival (prompt length L_p, expected token generation length L_g, arrival time t)
Queue State Assessment: For each partition queue i, compute current queue depth, average wait time, and recent throughput
Assignment Decision: Use a learned model (e.g., nearest-neighbor in feature space, lightweight decision tree, or rule-based heuristic) to select the best-matching partition queue for the new request
Adaptive Updates: Periodically retrain or adjust routing rules based on performance feedback (e.g., which queue assignments led to good/poor latency outcomes)

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-queue scheduling systems where queue selection is not obvious
Online load balancing in heterogeneous server clusters
Real-time request routing in microservice architectures

Limitations / Caveats:

Routing decisions made at arrival time with incomplete information about future workload
Requires maintaining multiple queues, increasing memory and switching overhead
Misrouting a request to the wrong queue persists for the duration of execution (no mid-execution migration)
Performance depends heavily on the quality of learned routing rules

Internal Use Cases:

Search ranking: Routing queries to specialized rankers (neural vs. rule-based) based on query intent
LLM prompt routing: Steering requests to specialized model instances (e.g., smaller models for simpler queries)
Recommendation filtering: Assigning user sessions to different candidate generation strategies based on session state


Density-Weighted Scoring (DWS)
Core Idea: A context-aware prioritization function that balances request urgency and fairness by combining queue density, wait time, and request characteristics into a single score for scheduling decisions.
Problem It Solves: Fixed priority schemes (e.g., always prioritize short requests) are unfair to long requests, while pure FCFS causes head-of-line blocking. DWS adapts priorities dynamically based on current queue state to maximize responsiveness without starving longer requests.
How It Works:

Urgency Metric: Compute urgency for request r as a function of wait time W_r and request length L_r (e.g., W_r / log(L_r) to normalize by length)
Queue Density Factor: For each partition group, compute density ρ = (queue_depth) / (total_system_capacity) to reflect congestion
Weighted Score: Calculate priority score as Score(r) = (Urgency(r)) × (1 + α × ρ) where α is a learned weight parameter
Scheduling Decision: Select the request with highest score across all queues; if tied, use fairness tiebreaker (oldest request wins)
Parameter Learning: Update α and urgency function coefficients via Bayesian optimization using observed latency outcomes

Where It’s Useful:

Request-level scheduling in multi-queue systems
Fair prioritization in resource-constrained environments
Systems combining SLA-sensitive and batch workloads

Limitations / Caveats:

Scoring function is a heuristic; optimal weights depend on workload distribution and hardware characteristics
Urgency metric may not capture all relevant request properties (e.g., memory requirements, GPU affinity)
High contention scenarios may still cause unfairness if density factor is not tuned correctly
Requires careful tuning of α; poor choices lead to starvation or prioritization inversions

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys ranking: Weighting candidate items by freshness urgency and ranking quality fairness constraints
Feed ranking: Balancing engagement prediction urgency against diversity and fair representation of creators
CTR prediction: Adapting model inference priority based on data staleness and batch size pressures


Bayesian Meta-Optimization
Core Idea: A closed-loop learning system that continuously tunes EWSJF’s partitioning and scoring parameters by treating observed performance metrics as feedback signals in a Bayesian optimization framework.
Problem It Solves: EWSJF has multiple hyperparameters (clustering k, urgency weights, density scaling α, partitioning thresholds) that affect performance. Manual tuning is infeasible, and optimal values vary across workloads. Bayesian optimization enables automatic, online parameter tuning without exhaustive grid search.
How It Works:

Observation: Monitor end-to-end performance metrics (average Time-To-First-Token TTFT, throughput, tail latency percentiles) over a sliding window of requests
Objective Function: Define a composite objective J = (1 - λ) × avg_TTFT + λ × throughput, where λ balances latency vs. throughput (λ is also a tuning parameter)
Parameter Hypothesis: Maintain a posterior distribution over promising parameter values using Bayesian inference (e.g., Gaussian Process surrogate model)
Acquisition & Exploration: Select next parameter candidate using an acquisition function (e.g., Expected Improvement) that balances exploiting good parameters and exploring uncertain regions
Feedback Loop: Execute with candidate parameters, collect performance metrics, update posterior belief, and iterate
Online Adaptation: Re-optimize parameters every M requests or when workload characteristics change significantly

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["EWSJF Scheduler\nwith Parameters θ"] --> B["Execute Requests"]
    B --> C["Monitor Performance\nMetrics"]
    C --> D["Bayesian Optimization\nUpdate Posterior P(θ|data)"]
    D --> E["Acquisition Function\nSelect Next θ"]
    E --> F{Check\nConvergence}
    F -->|No| A
    F -->|Yes| G["Use Optimized\nParameters"]

Where It’s Useful:

Adaptive systems that must tune hyperparameters online
Multi-objective optimization in production systems where workload varies
Meta-learning frameworks that auto-tune algorithm behavior

Limitations / Caveats:

Bayesian optimization has computational overhead; if re-optimized too frequently, cost may exceed scheduling savings
Surrogate model (e.g., GP) scales poorly in high-dimensional parameter spaces (curse of dimensionality)
Convergence requires sufficient data; early in a session, parameter estimates are noisy
Assumes performance metrics are stable enough to guide optimization (high variance metrics reduce signal quality)
May get stuck in local optima if objective landscape is multi-modal

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys ranking: Auto-tuning diversity weight, freshness penalty, and position bias correction based on online A/B test feedback
LLM serving: Optimizing batch size, token prediction lookahead, and scheduler thresholds per workload pattern
Search ranking: Adapting neural-to-rule-based ranker balance based on query satisfaction metrics


EWSJF: Integrated Adaptive Request-Level Scheduler
Core Idea: A four-component orchestrated system that combines partitioning discovery, request routing, dynamic prioritization, and online parameter tuning to achieve both fairness and throughput in mixed-workload LLM serving.
Problem It Solves: FCFS scheduling in LLM inference causes severe head-of-line blocking—long batch requests delay short interactive queries, leading to poor tail latency (4x worse) and throughput underutilization. No single fixed policy balances both workload types fairly.
How It Works:

Discover Partitions (Refine-and-Prune): Identify performance-homogeneous request groups from streaming request data
Route Incoming Requests (Dynamic Queue Routing): Assign each new request to the best-matching partition queue
Prioritize Requests (Density-Weighted Scoring): Score all queued requests considering urgency, fairness, and current queue congestion; execute highest-scoring request
Optimize Continuously (Bayesian Meta-Optimization): Monitor performance metrics and auto-tune clustering parameters, urgency weights, and density scaling factors

Architecture:
flowchart TD
    A["Incoming Request Stream"] --> B["Feature Extraction\n(length, tokens, arrival)"]
    B --> C["Refine-and-Prune\nPartitioning"]
    C --> D["Dynamic Queue Routing\nAssign to Queue i"]
    D --> E["Request Queue i"]
    E --> F["Density-Weighted Scoring\nCompute Priority"]
    F --> G["Select Highest-Scoring\nRequest"]
    G --> H["Execute on GPU\nvLLM Engine"]
    H --> I["Monitor Metrics\nTTFT, Throughput, Latency"]
    I --> J["Bayesian Meta-Optimization\nTune Parameters"]
    J --> C

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-tenant LLM inference serving (e.g., shared GPU clusters)
Systems with mixed latency-sensitive and throughput-oriented workloads
Cloud-based language model APIs with heterogeneous user requests
Any request scheduling scenario where workload distribution is unpredictable

Limitations / Caveats:

Operates at request level, before execution-level schedulers; does not directly optimize token-level scheduling
Requires monitoring infrastructure to collect performance feedback for Bayesian optimization
Partition quality depends on request feature selection and clustering algorithm
Parameter tuning introduces non-determinism; exact reproducibility is difficult
Overhead of maintaining multiple queues and computing DWS for all requests must be amortized over throughput gains

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys serving: Multi-request types (rapid contextual rankings vs. bulk offline computations) on shared inference clusters
LLM APIs: Serving multiple client tiers (real-time chat vs. batch document processing) on shared GPUs
Search systems: Blending online ranking (few candidates, low latency) with offline deep learning reranking (many candidates, higher latency)

Discussions & References

huggingface.co
github.com

literature/2026-02-01-FBS-Modeling-Nativ.title: FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer
literature/2026-02-01-FBS-Modeling-Nativ.content:
FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #4 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, Transformer
Abstract
Large language models (LLMs) excel across many tasks, yet inference is still dominated by strictly token-by-token autoregression. Existing acceleration methods largely patch this pipeline and miss core human-reading ingredients: content-adaptive foresight, chunk-structure-aware compute allocation, and train—test consistency for preview/skimming. We propose the \textbf{Fovea-Block-Skip Transformer} (FBS), which injects a causal, trainable loop into Transformers via Parafovea-Attention Window (PAW), Chunk-Head (CH), and Skip-Gate (SG). Across diverse benchmarks, FBS improves the quality-efficiency trade-off without increasing parameters, and ablations show the three modules are complementary.
Key Techniques

Parafovea-Attention Window
Chunk-Head
Skip-Gate
content-adaptive foresight

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityFBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.67FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.66FASA: Frequency-aware Sparse Attention0.22ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.22FOCUS: DLLMs Know How to Tame Their Compute Bound0.21
Chunk-Head (CH)
Core Idea: A specialized attention head that operates on chunk-level representations rather than individual tokens, allocating compute proportionally to chunk importance rather than treating all tokens equally.
Problem It Solves: Token-level attention allocates compute uniformly across all tokens, but human reading chunks information into meaningful units (sentences, paragraphs). CH addresses this by explicitly modeling chunks and focusing computation on important chunks, enabling chunk-structure-aware compute allocation.
How It Works:

Group input tokens into chunks (fixed-size or hierarchical segments, e.g., by sentence/paragraph boundaries)
Compute a chunk-level importance score (via learned projection or aggregation of token representations within chunks)
Use chunk importance to modulate attention weights: tokens in high-importance chunks receive higher attention allocation
This creates a two-level hierarchy: which chunks matter (coarse), and within chunks, how to attend (fine)

Architecture (if applicable):
flowchart LR
    A["Token Sequence"] --> B["Group into<br/>Chunks"]
    B --> C["Compute Chunk<br/>Importance Scores"]
    C --> D["Modulate Token-Level<br/>Attention by Chunk Score"]
    D --> E["Output"]

Where It’s Useful:

Processing long documents where structure (sentences, sections) is meaningful
Hierarchical sequence models (e.g., document + paragraph + sentence levels)
Information retrieval where document structure indicates relevance

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires predefined or learned chunk boundaries; may not align with semantic structure
Chunk importance may not perfectly correlate with token-level importance
Could bottleneck if chunk computation becomes heavy

Internal Use Cases:

Multi-level ranking where documents contain passages, which contain snippets
Hierarchical recommendation systems that operate at user-segment, item-category, and item levels
Feed ranking with story-level vs. post-level importance allocation


Skip-Gate (SG)
Core Idea: A trainable gating mechanism that decides whether to skip processing (or skip-attention) for certain tokens or chunks, enabling dynamic selection of which computations are necessary based on input content.
Problem It Solves: Transformers perform all computations for all tokens regardless of necessity. Skip-Gate allows selective skipping of compute for less important tokens, improving efficiency while maintaining train-test consistency (the same tokens are skipped during training and inference, avoiding train-test mismatch).
How It Works:

For each token (or chunk), compute a skip score using a learned gating function (e.g., a small neural network on hidden states)
The skip score determines whether to skip processing: if skip_score > threshold, bypass expensive operations (attention, FFN) for this token
Tokens marked as skipped still receive outputs from previous layers but do not contribute to downstream computation
The gate is differentiable (using sigmoid or other smooth gates) so gradients flow during training

Architecture (if applicable):
flowchart LR
    A["Token Hidden State"] --> B["Compute Skip Gate<br/>Score"]
    B --> C{Skip?}
    C -->|No| D["Run Attention<br/>& FFN"]
    C -->|Yes| E["Skip to<br/>Next Layer"]
    D --> F["Output"]
    E --> F

Where It’s Useful:

Token-level pruning in long sequence models
Early exit strategies where some tokens/sequences finish inference early
Efficiency-critical applications (mobile inference, streaming)

Limitations / Caveats:

Skip decisions are irreversible; skipped tokens lose processing capacity
Requires careful threshold tuning to balance efficiency and accuracy
May hurt interpretability (which tokens were actually “read”?)
Train-test consistency requires careful handling of randomness during training

Internal Use Cases:

Candidate pruning in ranking: mark obviously irrelevant candidates for skipping
Lazy evaluation in recommendation engines where some user features don’t need full computation
Feed ranking with early-exit for clearly non-relevant items (e.g., already-interacted content)

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-01-Generative-Chain-o.title: Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction
literature/2026-02-01-Generative-Chain-o.content:
Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #9 (score: 0.89, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Modeling long-term user behavior trajectories is essential for understanding evolving preferences and enabling proactive recommendations. However, most sequential recommenders focus on next-item prediction, overlooking dependencies across multiple future actions. We propose Generative Chain of Behavior (GCB), a generative framework that models user interactions as an autoregressive chain of semantic behaviors over multiple future steps. GCB first encodes items into semantic IDs via RQ-VAE with k-means refinement, forming a discrete latent space that preserves semantic proximity. On top of this space, a transformer-based autoregressive generator predicts multi-step future behaviors conditioned on user history, capturing long-horizon intent transitions and generating coherent trajectories. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that GCB consistently outperforms state-of-the-art sequential recommenders in multi-step accuracy and trajectory consistency. Beyond these gains, GCB offers a unified generative formulation for capturing user preference evolution.
Key Techniques

RQ-VAE with k-means refinement
semantic ID encoding
transformer-based autoregressive generation
multi-step behavior prediction

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityGenerative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.84Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.80Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.78GLASS: A Generative Recommender for Long-sequence Modeling via SID-Tier and Semantic Search0.32DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.28
Autoregressive Multi-Step Trajectory Generation with Transformer
Core Idea: Use a transformer-based autoregressive decoder to predict multi-step future user behavior sequences conditioned on historical user interactions, generating coherent long-horizon trajectories one semantic action at a time.
Problem It Solves: Most sequential recommenders predict only the next item, missing dependencies and patterns across multiple future steps. Single-step prediction cannot capture long-term intent evolution or ensure trajectory coherence. Multi-step prediction requires modeling complex temporal dependencies over extended horizons.
How It Works:

Encode user history as a sequence of semantic IDs from step 1 (RQ-VAE encoding), optionally with temporal positional encodings and user/contextual embeddings
Initialize transformer decoder with masked self-attention to prevent attending to future positions
At each generation step t, input the historical sequence plus previously generated actions: [h₁, h₂, …, hₜ₋₁, y₁, y₂, …, yₜ₋₁]
Compute decoder output at position t via multi-head self-attention and feed-forward layers
Apply softmax over semantic ID vocabulary to produce probability distribution p(yₜ | history, y₁:ₜ₋₁)
Sample or greedily select semantic ID ŷₜ and append to sequence
Repeat steps 3-6 for T steps to generate full trajectory [ŷ₁, ŷ₂, …, ŷₜ]
Decode semantic IDs back to actual items for final recommendations

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["User History<br/>(Semantic IDs)"] --> B["Embedding +<br/>Positional Enc"]
    B --> C["Transformer Decoder<br/>(Masked Self-Attn)"]
    C --> D["Output at Step t"]
    D --> E["Softmax over<br/>Semantic Vocab"]
    E --> F["Sample Action ŷₜ"]
    F --> G["Append to Sequence"]
    G --> H{More Steps?}
    H -->|Yes| C
    H -->|No| I["Decoded Trajectory"]

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-step recommendation and trajectory prediction tasks
Modeling long-term user preference evolution and intent transitions
Generating coherent sequences where consistency matters (e.g., playlist generation, content series recommendations)
Settings requiring uncertainty quantification through sampling multiple trajectories

Limitations / Caveats:

Autoregressive generation incurs O(T) computational cost; slow for very long horizons
Error accumulation: mistakes in early steps compound in later predictions (exposure bias during inference)
Requires ground-truth multi-step trajectory labels for training, which may be sparse or noisy
Transformer attention is O(T²) in sequence length; scales poorly to very long user histories
Discrete semantic space may lose fine-grained item distinctions needed for precise multi-step ranking

Internal Use Cases:

Core approach for multi-step ranking in recommendation pipelines
Could extend to conditional trajectory generation (e.g., “generate trajectory favoring diverse items”)
Applicable to LLM-based recommendation where semantic IDs become tokens and trajectories become text sequences
Foundation for uncertainty-aware recommendations by sampling multiple trajectories and ranking by consistency

literature/2026-02-01-Heterogeneous-Comp.title: Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference
literature/2026-02-01-Heterogeneous-Comp.content:
Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #1 (score: 0.71, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
AI agent inference is driving an inference heavy datacenter future and exposes bottlenecks beyond compute - especially memory capacity, memory bandwidth and high-speed interconnect. We introduce two metrics - Operational Intensity (OI) and Capacity Footprint (CF) - that jointly explain regimes the classic roofline analysis misses, including the memory capacity wall. Across agentic workflows (chat, coding, web use, computer use) and base model choices (GQA/MLA, MoE, quantization), OI/CF can shift dramatically, with long context KV cache making decode highly memory bound. These observations motivate disaggregated serving and system level heterogeneity: specialized prefill and decode accelerators, broader scale up networking, and decoupled compute-memory enabled by optical I/O. We further hypothesize agent-hardware co design, multiple inference accelerators within one system, and high bandwidth, large capacity memory disaggregation as foundations for adaptation to evolving OI/CF. Together, these directions chart a path to sustain efficiency and capability for large scale agentic AI inference.
Key Techniques

Operational Intensity (OI) and Capacity Footprint (CF) metrics
disaggregated serving architecture
specialized prefill and decode accelerators
agent-hardware co-design

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHeterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.82Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.80PrefillShare: A Shared Prefill Module for KV Reuse in Multi-LLM Disaggregated Serving0.41SuperInfer: SLO-Aware Rotary Scheduling and Memory Management for LLM Inference on Superchips0.23Language Model Memory and Memory Models for Language0.20
Disaggregated Serving with Specialized Prefill/Decode Accelerators
Core Idea: Decouple prefill and decode inference phases onto heterogeneous accelerators optimized for their distinct compute-memory characteristics, enabling different hardware specialization and scaling curves.
Problem It Solves: Prefill (high OI, compute-bound) and decode (low OI, memory-bound) have fundamentally different hardware requirements; unified accelerators make inefficient tradeoffs, leaving compute or memory underutilized across the workload mix.
How It Works:

Partition inference pipeline: prefill cluster processes prompt tokens (high parallelism, high arithmetic intensity), decode cluster processes autoregressive generation (low parallelism, memory-bound)
Optimize prefill accelerators for high compute throughput (tensor cores, large batch processing), optimized for OI
Optimize decode accelerators for high memory bandwidth and latency hiding (wide memory interface, prefetch mechanisms), optimized for CF
Decouple via request-level scheduling: route requests to prefill cluster until prompt is processed, then transfer KV cache to decode cluster for token generation
Enable independent scaling: add prefill capacity when prompt latency is bottleneck, add decode capacity when generation throughput is bottleneck

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Request<br/>Prompt + Context"] --> B["Prefill<br/>Cluster<br/>(High OI)"]
    B --> C["KV Cache<br/>Transfer"]
    C --> D["Decode<br/>Cluster<br/>(Low OI)"]
    D --> E["Output Tokens"]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale LLM serving with mixed workloads (short chats, long-context document processing, agentic reasoning loops)
Agent inference where prefill latency (prompt encoding) and decode throughput (generating next actions) are distinct bottlenecks
Multi-task serving where some requests are latency-sensitive (short completions) vs. throughput-sensitive (batch reasoning)

Limitations / Caveats:

Introduces network latency for KV cache transfer between clusters; requires high-speed interconnect (optical I/O) to amortize
Adds scheduling complexity: optimal request routing depends on dynamic cluster utilization
Memory isolation and cache coherency overhead when KV state moves between systems
Requires careful tuning of cluster capacity ratio to match workload mix (too much prefill capacity → idle decode)

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking pipeline separation: offline scoring (like prefill) vs. online re-ranking (like decode) can use different hardware
Multi-stage recommendation: candidate retrieval (high compute, like prefill) vs. pointwise scoring (memory-bound, like decode)


System-Level Heterogeneity with Optical I/O for Decoupled Compute-Memory
Core Idea: Enable heterogeneous compute and memory resources to be physically disaggregated and connected via high-bandwidth optical interconnects, allowing independent scaling of compute capacity and memory capacity with fine-grained resource utilization.
Problem It Solves: Monolithic servers couple compute and memory in fixed ratios (e.g., 1:6 cores:GB); long-context inference requires much larger memory relative to compute, wasting expensive compute or overprovisioning memory. Disaggregation decouples the constraint.
How It Works:

Partition datacenter into specialized tiles: compute tiles (high compute density, limited local memory), memory tiles (high capacity, optimized for bandwidth), and I/O tiles (network/storage)
Connect tiles via high-bandwidth optical I/O (100s of Gbps) instead of traditional PCIe or Ethernet to minimize latency and maximize throughput for remote memory access
Implement remote memory access protocol: compute tiles treat memory tiles as extension of address space with optimized prefetch and batching to hide optical latency
Schedule inference workloads onto heterogeneous resource pools: assign long-context prefill to compute+memory bundles, decode to memory-rich compute-light configs
Enable co-location of multiple inference accelerators (e.g., GPU + specialized decode ASIC) sharing disaggregated memory via optical fabric

Architecture:
flowchart TB
    A["Compute<br/>Tile"] <-->|Optical I/O<br/>100s Gbps| B["Memory<br/>Tile"]
    C["Compute<br/>Tile"] <-->|Optical I/O| B
    D["Specialized<br/>Decode ASIC"] <-->|Optical I/O| B
    E["I/O Tile<br/>Network"] <-->|Optical I/O| B

Where It’s Useful:

Serving long-context agents (multi-turn chat, document analysis, code generation) where memory footprint is 10-100x compute footprint
Multi-tenant inference clusters where different requests have vastly different compute:memory ratios
Hardware evolution path: easy to add memory tiers without re-provisioning compute (or vice versa)
Enabling new accelerator types (e.g., specialized decode ASICs) to coexist in same system sharing memory

Limitations / Caveats:

Optical I/O introduces latency (microseconds to nanoseconds) that impacts small, latency-sensitive operations; requires batching/prefetch to amortize
Requires new memory access protocols and potentially compiler/runtime changes to efficiently manage remote memory
Increases system complexity: scheduling, fault tolerance, power management become harder with distributed resources
Initial infrastructure cost is high (optical switches, deployment); benefits amortize over large scale only
Memory coherency and consistency models must be carefully designed to avoid correctness issues

Internal Use Cases:

Disaggregated recommendation serving: candidate retrieval compute can be separate from dense embedding storage
Multi-model inference where one model is compute-heavy (candidate ranking) and another is memory-heavy (retrieval index)

literature/2026-02-01-Idea2Story-An-Auto.title: Idea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives
literature/2026-02-01-Idea2Story-An-Auto.content:
Idea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #9 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Graph, Search, RAG
Abstract
Autonomous scientific discovery with large language model (LLM)-based agents has recently made substantial progress, demonstrating the ability to automate end-to-end research workflows. However, existing systems largely rely on runtime-centric execution paradigms, repeatedly reading, summarizing, and reasoning over large volumes of scientific literature online. This on-the-spot computation strategy incurs high computational cost, suffers from context window limitations, and often leads to brittle reasoning and hallucination. We propose Idea2Story, a pre-computation-driven framework for autonomous scientific discovery that shifts literature understanding from online reasoning to offline knowledge construction. Idea2Story continuously collects peer-reviewed papers together with their review feedback, extracts core methodological units, composes reusable research patterns, and organizes them into a structured methodological knowledge graph. At runtime, underspecified user research intents are aligned to established research paradigms, enabling efficient retrieval and reuse of high-quality research patterns instead of open-ended generation and trial-and-error. By grounding research planning and execution in a pre-built knowledge graph, Idea2Story alleviates the context window bottleneck of LLMs and substantially reduces repeated runtime reasoning over literature. We conduct qualitative analyses and preliminary empirical studies demonstrating that Idea2Story can generate coherent, methodologically grounded, and novel research patterns, and can produce several high-quality research demonstrations in an end-to-end setting. These results suggest that offline knowledge construction provides a practical and scalable foundation for reliable autonomous scientific discovery.
Key Techniques

offline knowledge construction
methodological knowledge graph
pre-computation-driven framework
research pattern extraction

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityIdea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives0.76Idea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives0.76Towards Personalized Bangla Book Recommendation: A Large-Scale Multi-Entity Book Graph Dataset0.19LIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs0.17ResearchGym: Evaluating Language Model Agents on Real-World AI Research0.16
Intent-to-Paradigm Alignment
Core Idea: A runtime mechanism that maps underspecified user research intents to established research paradigms stored in the knowledge graph, enabling pattern matching and retrieval rather than open-ended generation.
Problem It Solves:

Open-ended research planning leads to hallucinated or invalid methodological sequences
Underspecified user queries lack sufficient context for direct execution
LLMs generate arbitrary solutions without grounding in proven research patterns
Context window limitations prevent comprehensive literature search at runtime

How It Works:

Intent Parsing: Process user-provided research intent (which may be incomplete or conceptual)
Semantic Encoding: Encode the intent into a vector representation capturing key research dimensions (domain, objective, constraints)
Paradigm Matching: Query the pre-built knowledge graph to identify compatible research paradigms using similarity matching or graph traversal
Ranking: Rank candidate paradigms by fit quality (e.g., methodological soundness, novelty score, review sentiment from training papers)
Alignment: Select the best-matching paradigm(s) and resolve ambiguities in the user intent through paradigm structure

Where It’s Useful:

Research proposal generation where user provides high-level research questions
Automated literature-informed experimental design
Intelligent research assistant systems
Grant proposal scaffolding with methodological validation

Limitations / Caveats:

Relies on existence of similar paradigms in the knowledge graph; novel research directions may not align well
Quality of alignment depends on quality of semantic encoding and paradigm representation
May under-explore truly innovative methodological combinations by defaulting to nearest established paradigm
Requires effective handling of ambiguous or conflicting intents

Internal Use Cases:

Similar to query-to-document matching in ranking systems but at the methodological pattern level
Could use learned embeddings for intent-paradigm similarity scoring (e.g., as a filtering or re-ranking stage)
Applicable to cold-start problems in recommendation: matching new research ideas to proven patterns is analogous to matching users to similar cohorts


Methodological Pattern Composition & Validation
Core Idea: An extraction and synthesis process that identifies reusable methodological patterns from papers and their peer reviews, ensuring patterns are validated by the research community before inclusion in the knowledge graph.
Problem It Solves:

Raw papers contain methods buried in text and context-dependent descriptions
No explicit ranking or validation of methodological components
Methodological gaps and flawed approaches aren’t automatically filtered out
No structured encoding of what makes a method effective (review feedback signal)

How It Works:

Unit Extraction: Identify methodological components from paper text (e.g., specific algorithms, data preprocessing steps, evaluation protocols)
Context Extraction: Capture conditions under which each component is applied (e.g., dataset characteristics, baseline comparisons)
Review Feedback Integration: Extract relevant critique and validation signals from peer reviews for each methodological unit
Pattern Generalization: Identify recurring methodological sequences across multiple papers
Validation Scoring: Assign quality/reliability scores based on: citation counts of source papers, sentiment from peer reviews, breadth of replication across papers
Graph Integration: Add validated patterns as nodes to the knowledge graph with edges encoding sequencing and compatibility constraints

Where It’s Useful:

Building methodologically validated knowledge bases for specific research domains
Identifying best practices and standard protocols in scientific fields
Creating audit trails for methodological decisions in generated research
Detecting methodological novelty by identifying patterns not yet well-established

Limitations / Caveats:

Extracting methodological units requires domain knowledge or sophisticated NLP; fully automatic extraction may be lossy
Review feedback is heterogeneous in quality and may reflect reviewer bias rather than true methodological validity
Validation signals (citations, review sentiment) are historical and may not predict success of novel combinations
Generalization across papers can obscure important context-dependencies in method application

Internal Use Cases:

For ranking models: methodological validation score could serve as a quality signal in multi-stage ranking pipelines
For RecSys: validation patterns could inform compatibility constraints in composition-based recommendation (e.g., “method A works well with preprocessing B”)
Could enable counterfactual scoring where estimated quality of research patterns informs downstream ranking decisions

literature/2026-02-01-MALLOC-Benchmarkin.title: MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation
literature/2026-02-01-MALLOC-Benchmarkin.content:
MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #12 (score: 0.85, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The scaling law, which indicates that model performance improves with increasing dataset and model capacity, has fueled a growing trend in expanding recommendation models in both industry and academia. However, the advent of large-scale recommenders also brings significantly higher computational costs, particularly under the long-sequence dependencies inherent in the user intent of recommendation systems. Current approaches often rely on pre-storing the intermediate states of the past behavior for each user, thereby reducing the quadratic re-computation cost for the following requests. Despite their effectiveness, these methods often treat memory merely as a medium for acceleration, without adequately considering the space overhead it introduces. This presents a critical challenge in real-world recommendation systems with billions of users, each of whom might initiate thousands of interactions and require massive memory for state storage. Fortunately, there have been several memory management strategies examined for compression in LLM, while most have not been evaluated on the recommendation task. To mitigate this gap, we introduce MALLOC, a comprehensive benchmark for memory-aware long sequence compression. MALLOC presents a comprehensive investigation and systematic classification of memory management techniques applicable to large sequential recommendations. These techniques are integrated into state-of-the-art recommenders, enabling a reproducible and accessible evaluation platform. Through extensive experiments across accuracy, efficiency, and complexity, we demonstrate the holistic reliability of MALLOC in advancing large-scale recommendation. Code is available at anonymous.4open.science/r/MALLOC.
Key Techniques

Scaling law for recommendation models
Pre-storing intermediate states
Memory management strategies
Long sequence compression

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation0.98MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation0.98MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation0.98MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation0.96Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression0.16

literature/2026-02-01-Masked-Diffusion-G.title: Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation
literature/2026-02-01-Masked-Diffusion-G.content:
Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-27
Rank: #6 (score: 0.92, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Generative recommendation (GR) typically first quantizes continuous item embeddings into multi-level semantic IDs (SIDs), and then generates the next item via autoregressive decoding. Although existing methods are already competitive in terms of recommendation performance, directly inheriting the autoregressive decoding paradigm from language models still suffers from three key limitations: (1) autoregressive decoding struggles to jointly capture global dependencies among the multi-dimensional features associated with different positions of SID; (2) using a unified, fixed decoding path for the same item implicitly assumes that all users attend to item attributes in the same order; (3) autoregressive decoding is inefficient at inference time and struggles to meet real-time requirements. To tackle these challenges, we propose MDGR, a Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation framework that reshapes the GR pipeline from three perspectives: codebook, training, and inference. (1) We adopt a parallel codebook to provide a structural foundation for diffusion-based GR. (2) During training, we adaptively construct masking supervision signals along both the temporal and sample dimensions. (3) During inference, we develop a warm-up-based two-stage parallel decoding strategy for efficient generation of SIDs. Extensive experiments on multiple public and industrial-scale datasets show that MDGR outperforms ten state-of-the-art baselines by up to 10.78%. Furthermore, by deploying MDGR on a large-scale online advertising platform, we achieve a 1.20% increase in revenue, demonstrating its practical value.
Key Techniques

Parallel codebook for semantic IDs
Adaptive masking along temporal and sample dimensions
Warm-up-based two-stage parallel decoding
Masked diffusion for generative recommendation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMasked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.80Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.72Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.68Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.66DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.33
Adaptive Masking Supervision (Training Strategy)
Core Idea: Construct masking supervision signals adaptively along both temporal and sample dimensions during training to guide diffusion model learning without enforcing fixed decoding orders.
Problem It Solves: Standard autoregressive training fixes a single decoding path per item, implicitly assuming uniform user attention patterns. This prevents the model from learning flexible, user-specific generation orders.
How It Works:

Temporal masking dimension: Vary which diffusion timesteps mask which SID positions; create multiple masking schedules for the same item
Sample masking dimension: Across the batch, apply different masking patterns to the same item at the same timestep, allowing the model to learn multiple valid generation paths
Generate masked supervision signals M ∈ [0,1]^{k×T} where M[i,t] indicates whether SID position i is masked at diffusion step t
Train the diffusion model to predict unmasked SIDs given masked inputs and noise level, without enforcing a specific unmasking order
Learn that different positions can be revealed in different orders depending on user context

Where It’s Useful:

Training diffusion models for discrete sequence generation
Recommendation systems where user preferences vary in attribute importance
Multi-dimensional code generation with flexible orderings

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful design of masking pattern sampling to ensure coverage of meaningful orderings
Increased training complexity compared to fixed autoregressive supervision
May require more training data to learn all valid decoding paths


Warm-up-Based Two-Stage Parallel Decoding
Core Idea: Use a two-stage inference strategy with warm-up initialization to efficiently generate all SID positions in parallel, eliminating sequential generation latency.
Problem It Solves: Autoregressive decoding requires T sequential forward passes (where T = number of SID positions), creating unacceptable inference latency for real-time recommendation systems. This prevents deployment in latency-sensitive online scenarios.
How It Works:

Warm-up Stage: Initialize all SID positions with a rough estimate (e.g., mask tokens or position embeddings), creating initial noisy codes s⁽ᵀ⁾ = {[MASK], [MASK], ..., [MASK]}
Parallel Decoding Stage: Iteratively refine all k SID positions simultaneously using the diffusion model’s reverse process:

At each diffusion step t: predict all unmasked positions in parallel given current noisy SIDs and timestep
Update all k positions jointly: s⁽ᵗ⁻¹⁾ = denoise(s⁽ᵗ⁾, t, context)
Continue for T_decode steps until convergence


Output final SID vector s = [s₁*, s₂*, ..., sₖ*] ready for item retrieval
Total inference passes: T_decode (typically T_decode ≪ k), vs. k passes in autoregressive approach

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["User Context"] --> B["Warm-up: Init Masked SIDs"]
    B --> C["t=T: Add Noise"]
    C --> D["Stage 1: Denoising Step"]
    D --> E{"t > 0?"}
    E -->|Yes| F["Predict All Positions in Parallel"]
    F --> D
    E -->|No| G["Final SID Vector"]
    G --> H["Item Retrieval"]

Where It’s Useful:

Real-time recommendation systems with latency constraints
Large-scale online advertising platforms (as demonstrated in the paper: 1.20% revenue increase)
Any sequential generation task requiring sub-100ms inference latency

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires pre-trained diffusion model; cannot use in cold-start without warm-up strategy
Parallel generation may produce slightly lower quality than sequential refinement in some domains
Warm-up initialization quality impacts final result; needs careful tuning
T_decode hyperparameter balances quality vs. latency; requires empirical tuning per deployment


Integration: These three techniques work together—the parallel codebook enables multi-position generation, adaptive masking trains the model to handle flexible orderings, and parallel decoding leverages this flexibility for efficient inference.

literature/2026-02-01-OneMall-One-Model-.title: OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios -- End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce
literature/2026-02-01-OneMall-One-Model-.content:
OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #4 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
In the wave of generative recommendation, we present OneMall, an end-to-end generative recommendation framework tailored for e-commerce services at Kuaishou. Our OneMall systematically unifies the e-commerce’s multiple item distribution scenarios, such as Product-card, short-video and live-streaming. Specifically, it comprises three key components, aligning the entire model training pipeline to the LLM’s pre-training/post-training: (1) E-commerce Semantic Tokenizer: we provide a tokenizer solution that captures both real-world semantics and business-specific item relations across different scenarios; (2) Transformer-based Architecture: we largely utilize Transformer as our model backbone, e.g., employing Query-Former for long sequence compression, Cross-Attention for multi-behavior sequence fusion, and Sparse MoE for scalable auto-regressive generation; (3) Reinforcement Learning Pipeline: we further connect retrieval and ranking models via RL, enabling the ranking model to serve as a reward signal for end-to-end policy retrieval model optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OneMall achieves consistent improvements across all e-commerce scenarios: +13.01% GMV in product-card, +15.32% Orders in Short-Video, and +2.78% Orders in Live-Streaming. OneMall has been deployed, serving over 400 million daily active users at Kuaishou.
Key Techniques

E-commerce Semantic Tokenizer
Transformer-based Architecture with Query-Former and Sparse MoE
Reinforcement Learning for end-to-end retrieval-ranking optimization
Multi-behavior sequence fusion with Cross-Attention

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityOneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.77OneMall: One Architecture, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.77OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.56OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.55SCASRec: A Self-Correcting and Auto-Stopping Model for Generative Route List Recommendation0.23
Query-Former for Long Sequence Compression
Core Idea: A Transformer-based module that compresses long user interaction sequences into compact query representations while preserving critical behavioral patterns for downstream ranking.
Problem It Solves: User interaction histories can be extremely long (thousands of clicks/views), making full-sequence attention computationally prohibitive. Query-Former enables efficient Transformer-based generation by reducing sequence length while retaining predictive information.
How It Works:

Input: Long user sequence S = [e₁, e₂, …, eₜ] where t can be very large
Project sequence elements to embeddings and apply positional encodings
Use learnable query tokens (similar to BERT’s [CLS] token) to attend over the entire sequence via multi-head self-attention
Output: Compressed representation (fixed-size query vector) that summarizes the most salient behavioral patterns
Pass compressed query to downstream Transformer layers for ranking/generation

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["User Sequence<br/>[e₁, e₂, ..., eₜ]"] --> B["Embedding +<br/>Positional Encoding"]
    B --> C["Multi-Head<br/>Attention<br/>Query Tokens"]
    C --> D["Compressed Query<br/>Vector"]
    D --> E["Downstream<br/>Ranking/Generation"]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context recommendation (users with extensive interaction history)
Reducing computational cost of attention in generative recommendation
Attention bottleneck in sequential recommendation systems

Limitations / Caveats:

Information loss due to compression; may discard low-frequency but important signals
Fixed compression ratio may not be optimal across different user types
Performance depends on quality of learnable query initialization

Internal Use Cases:

Compressing dense user sequences before ranking model inference
Enabling multi-behavior fusion (see below) without exploding computational cost


Cross-Attention for Multi-Behavior Sequence Fusion
Core Idea: Cross-attention mechanism that dynamically fuses multiple user behavior sequences (clicks, purchases, shares, etc.) by allowing one behavior type to attend to others, creating a unified behavioral representation.
Problem It Solves: Users exhibit multiple types of behaviors (implicit: views/clicks, explicit: purchases/likes, social: shares/follows). Treating these independently loses correlations; simple concatenation is inefficient. Cross-attention learns to weight and integrate heterogeneous behaviors.
How It Works:

Encode each behavior type separately: Click sequence C, Purchase sequence P, Share sequence S, etc., each producing embeddings
For each behavior type, apply cross-attention where:

Query comes from one behavior (e.g., clicks): Q = W_q · C
Key and Value come from another behavior (e.g., purchases): K = W_k · P, V = W_v · P


Compute multi-head attention: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T/√d)V
Concatenate or sum outputs from all cross-attention pairs to create fused representation
Pass fused multi-behavior embedding to ranking/generation modules

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Click<br/>Sequence"] --> Q["Query"]
    B["Purchase<br/>Sequence"] --> KV["Key, Value"]
    C["Share<br/>Sequence"] --> KV2["Key, Value"]
    Q --> CA["Cross-Attention<br/>Modules"]
    KV --> CA
    KV2 --> CA
    CA --> D["Fused Behavioral<br/>Representation"]

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-behavior recommendation systems (clicks + purchases + shares)
Learning inter-behavior dependencies (e.g., how clicks predict purchases)
Implicit-explicit behavior fusion in e-commerce

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful behavior sequence alignment (different timestamps/frequencies)
Quadratic complexity in sequence length; needs compression (Query-Former) to scale
Attention weights may be dominated by high-frequency behaviors if not properly normalized

Internal Use Cases:

Fusing multi-stage conversion behaviors (view → click → purchase → review)
Balancing weak signals (shares) with strong signals (purchases) in unified representation


Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) for Auto-Regressive Generation
Core Idea: A scalable auto-regressive generation architecture using Sparse MoE where a router network dynamically selects a small subset of expert modules for each token prediction, enabling efficient scaling without dense computation.
Problem It Solves: Generative recommendation requires predicting long sequences of item tokens auto-regressively. Naïve dense Transformer scaling is computationally prohibitive at Kuaishou’s scale (400M DAU). Sparse MoE enables parameter-efficient generation by activating only a subset of capacity per step.
How It Works:

Decoder Transformer receives previous predicted tokens: [item₁, item₂, …, itemₙ₋₁]
At each decoding step, embed current context and pass through Transformer layers
For each Transformer layer’s FFN (feed-forward network), replace dense FFN with sparse MoE:

Router network: r = softmax(W_router · h) produces logits over K experts
Top-k selection: Select top-k experts (e.g., k=2) with highest router scores
Expert computation: Only selected experts process the token: output = Σᵢ∈selected rᵢ · Expertᵢ(h)


Aggregate expert outputs and continue to next layer
Final layer outputs logits over item vocabulary; sample or argmax to select next item token
Repeat until end-of-sequence token or maximum length

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Predicted Tokens<br/>[item₁, ..., itemₙ₋₁]"] --> B["Embedding +<br/>Positional Encoding"]
    B --> C["Transformer<br/>Layers"]
    C --> D["Sparse MoE<br/>Router"]
    D --> E["Select Top-k<br/>Experts"]
    E --> F["Expert<br/>Aggregation"]
    F --> C
    C --> G["Output Logits<br/>over Vocabulary"]
    G --> H["Sample/Argmax<br/>Next Token"]
    H --> I["Next Step"]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale auto-regressive recommendation at production scale (400M+ users)
Item recommendation with long recommendation lists
Multi-scenario item prediction (product + video + stream items)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful load balancing to prevent expert imbalance (some experts inactive, others overloaded)
Router training can be unstable; may need auxiliary loss (e.g., load balancing auxiliary loss)
Top-k selection is non-differentiable; requires straight-through estimator or gating tricks
Increased model complexity and training overhead compared to dense models

Internal Use Cases:

Scaling recommendation models to production scale without proportional compute increase
Multi-task generation where different item types route to specialized experts


Reinforcement Learning Pipeline for End-to-End Retrieval-Ranking Optimization
Core Idea: A reinforcement learning framework that treats the ranking model as a reward signal, enabling the retrieval (candidate generation) model to optimize directly for downstream ranking and conversion metrics end-to-end, rather than optimizing retrieval and ranking separately.
Problem It Solves: Traditional recommendation decouples retrieval (candidate generation) from ranking. Retrieval is optimized for recall/coverage, ranking for CTR/conversion, but retrieved candidates may not be optimal for the downstream ranker’s objective. This two-stage separation leaves optimization gaps. RL bridges this by making retrieval learn to fetch candidates that the ranker scores highly.
How It Works:

Policy (Retrieval Model): πθ(a|s) outputs candidate item distributions given user state s, parameterized by Sparse MoE generative model trained as auto-regressive sequence
Reward Signal (Ranking Model): Frozen or slowly-updated ranker R(s, a) scores each retrieved candidate item a, producing reward r = R(s, a) (can be CTR prediction, conversion probability, or GMV proxy)
Trajectory Rollout: Sample K retrieval trajectories (sequences of retrieved items) from policy πθ
Return Computation: Compute discounted cumulative reward: G_t = Σᵢ₌ₜ^T γⁱ⁻ᵗ r(sᵢ, aᵢ) for each step
Policy Gradient Update: ∇J(θ) = E[∇log πθ(a|s) · G_t], updating retrieval model to maximize expected ranking reward
Iteration: Periodically retrain ranking model on new retrieval distribution, creating feedback loop

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["User State<br/>s"] --> B["Retrieval Policy<br/>πθ"]
    B --> C["Sampled Items<br/>{a₁, a₂, ...}"]
    C --> D["Ranking Model<br/>R s,a"]
    D --> E["Reward Signal<br/>r = R s,a"]
    E --> F["Policy Gradient<br/>Update θ"]
    F --> B
    D --> G["Ranking Training<br/>Loop"]
    G --> D

Where It’s Useful:

End-to-end recommendation optimization where retrieval and ranking must align
Multi-objective optimization (GMV, orders, user satisfaction) as reward signals
Production recommendation systems where early-stage decisions (retrieval) impact later stages

Limitations / Caveats:

Reward signal (ranking model) must be well-calibrated and stable, or RL will optimize for its errors
High variance in policy gradient estimates may require large batch sizes or variance reduction techniques (baseline, importance sampling)
Computational cost is significant (requires sampling trajectories, ranking each candidate, backprop through policy)
Training instability if reward and policy diverge too far; requires careful learning rate scheduling

Internal Use Cases:

End-to-end optimization of candidate generation pipelines in RecSys
Aligning multi-stage ranking funnel (retrieval → ranking → re-ranking) via shared objective
Online learning / contextual bandit formulation for adaptive retrieval strategies

literature/2026-02-01-PaperSearchQA-Lear.title: PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR
literature/2026-02-01-PaperSearchQA-Lear.content:
PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #5 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RL, Search, RAG
Abstract
Search agents are language models (LMs) that reason and search knowledge bases (or the web) to answer questions; recent methods supervise only the final answer accuracy using reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). Most RLVR search agents tackle general-domain QA, which limits their relevance to technical AI systems in science, engineering, and medicine. In this work we propose training agents to search and reason over scientific papers — this tests technical question-answering, it is directly relevant to real scientists, and the capabilities will be crucial to future AI Scientist systems. Concretely, we release a search corpus of 16 million biomedical paper abstracts and construct a challenging factoid QA dataset called PaperSearchQA with 60k samples answerable from the corpus, along with benchmarks. We train search agents in this environment to outperform non-RL retrieval baselines; we also perform further quantitative analysis and observe interesting agent behaviors like planning, reasoning, and self-verification. Our corpus, datasets, and benchmarks are usable with the popular Search-R1 codebase for RLVR training and released on huggingface.co/collections/jmhb/papersearchqa. Finally, our data creation methods are scalable and easily extendable to other scientific domains.
Key Techniques

RLVR (Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards)
PaperSearchQA dataset
biomedical paper search corpus
Search-R1 codebase

Related Litnotes
