MCTS traditionally relies on accurate value estimates from simulation or learned models; however, this paper shows that the iterative evaluation, rejection, and resampling cycle inherent to SMC can tolerate substantial approximation errors in the reward model. This is particularly relevant to the “selection, expansion, simulation, and backpropagation phases” of MCTS—the paper’s Bellman error analysis essentially quantifies how much error can propagate through these phases while still achieving exponential speedup. This bridges the gap between the idealized MCTS algorithms (which assume perfect reward signals) and the deployed systems that must use imperfect process reward models or self-evaluation heuristics.
Finally, this work adds a quantitative framework for evaluating reward model quality in inference-time scaling systems. Rather than treating reward model approximation as an engineering inconvenience, the paper establishes that reward models with O(1/T) Bellman error form a theoretically principled sweet spot—they’re loose enough to be practically implementable, yet tight enough to preserve exponential computational benefits. This insight helps contextualize why contemporary approaches like those in DeepSeek-R1 and o1/o3 (mentioned in Reasoning Beyond Limits) succeed despite not having ground-truth reward signals. The paper thus provides both a lower bound on reward model quality (explaining why some approximate models fail) and an upper bound on acceptable error (showing diminishing returns beyond a certain threshold), creating a theoretical roadmap for future research on reward model design and training.

literature/2026-02-03-OneMall-One-Archit.title: OneMall: One Architecture, More Scenarios -- End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce
literature/2026-02-03-OneMall-One-Archit.content:
OneMall: One Architecture, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-01-29
Rank: #9 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2601.21770v2
Abstract
In the wave of generative recommendation, we present OneMall, an end-to-end generative recommendation framework tailored for e-commerce services at Kuaishou. Our OneMall systematically unifies the e-commerce’s multiple item distribution scenarios, such as Product-card, short-video and live-streaming. Specifically, it comprises three key components, aligning the entire model training pipeline to the LLM’s pre-training/post-training: (1) E-commerce Semantic Tokenizer: we provide a tokenizer solution that captures both real-world semantics and business-specific item relations across different scenarios; (2) Transformer-based Architecture: we largely utilize Transformer as our model backbone, e.g., employing Query-Former for long sequence compression, Cross-Attention for multi-behavior sequence fusion, and Sparse MoE for scalable auto-regressive generation; (3) Reinforcement Learning Pipeline: we further connect retrieval and ranking models via RL, enabling the ranking model to serve as a reward signal for end-to-end policy retrieval model optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OneMall achieves consistent improvements across all e-commerce scenarios: +13.01% GMV in product-card, +15.32% Orders in Short-Video, and +2.78% Orders in Live-Streaming. OneMall has been deployed, serving over 400 million daily active users at Kuaishou.
Key Techniques

E-commerce Semantic Tokenizer
Query-Former for sequence compression
Reinforcement Learning Pipeline

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityOneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.77OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.77OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.60OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.59SCASRec: A Self-Correcting and Auto-Stopping Model for Generative Route List Recommendation0.25
Query-Former for Long Sequence Compression
Core Idea: A Transformer-based module that compresses long user behavior sequences into fixed-length query representations by learning to aggregate the most relevant historical interactions into compact learnable tokens.
Problem It Solves: User behavior sequences in e-commerce can span hundreds of interactions, creating computational bottlenecks for Transformer models which have O(n²) complexity. Direct processing of full sequences is infeasible, yet truncation or simple pooling loses important behavioral context critical for accurate recommendations.
How It Works:

Input: Raw user behavior sequence B = [b₁, b₂, …, bₙ] where n is potentially large (hundreds of interactions)
Initialize a fixed set of K learnable query tokens Q = [q₁, q₂, …, qₖ] (typically K << n)
Apply cross-attention from query tokens to the full behavior sequence: Attention(Q, B, B) = softmax(QBᵀ/√d)Bᵥ
Output: Compressed representation C ∈ ℝᵏˣᵈ that captures essential behavioral patterns in fixed size
Pass C to downstream recommendation modules (retrieval/ranking)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Long Behavior Sequence<br/>b₁...bₙ"] --> B["Cross-Attention<br/>from Query Tokens"]
    C["Learnable Queries<br/>q₁...qₖ"] --> B
    B --> D["Compressed Representation<br/>K tokens"]
    D --> E["Downstream<br/>Recommendation"]

Where It’s Useful:

Compressing long user interaction histories in sequential recommendation
Handling users with extensive browsing/purchase records without sequence truncation
Real-time inference on mobile/edge where latency is critical
Scaling Transformer-based rankers to production recommendation pipelines

Limitations / Caveats:

Compression ratio K/n must be carefully tuned; too aggressive compression loses signal
Query initialization and training dynamics affect convergence
Cross-attention is still O(Kn), so gains depend on compression rate relative to full O(n²)
May lose tail interactions that could be important for niche recommendations

Internal Use Cases:

User encoder in two-tower retrieval models handling long-range dependencies
Replacing fixed-size RNN hidden states with interpretable compressed query representations
Pre-processing for MoE routing (next section) to handle variable sequence lengths efficiently


Cross-Attention for Multi-Behavior Sequence Fusion
Core Idea: A cross-attention mechanism that fuses multiple user behavior sequences (e.g., clicks, purchases, shares, likes) into a unified context representation by learning fine-grained interactions between different behavior types.
Problem It Solves: Users exhibit diverse behaviors in e-commerce (clicking, adding to cart, purchasing, sharing) that carry different semantic meanings and predictive signals. Naive concatenation or simple fusion loses the rich signal from cross-behavior dependencies (e.g., a purchased item’s relationship to previously clicked items). Multi-task or multi-head approaches lack explicit interaction modeling.
How It Works:

Input: M behavior sequences S = [S₁, S₂, …, Sₘ] where Sᵢ represents one behavior type (clicks, purchases, etc.)
Embed each sequence: Eᵢ = Embed(Sᵢ) ∈ ℝⁿⁱˣᵈ (varying lengths nᵢ allowed)
Apply cross-attention between pairs of behavior types: For each i,j: CrossAtt(Eᵢ, Eⱼ) = softmax(EᵢWQ·EⱼWKᵀ/√d)EⱼWV
Learn implicit behavior relationships (e.g., which clicks led to purchases, which likes precede clicks)
Output: Fused representation F ∈ ℝᵈ capturing all M behavior signals and their interactions

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Click Sequence"] --> B["Embedding"]
    C["Purchase Sequence"] --> D["Embedding"]
    E["Like Sequence"] --> F["Embedding"]
    B --> G["Cross-Attention<br/>Fusion"]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["Unified Multi-Behavior<br/>Representation"]

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-behavior recommendation (clicks, purchases, shares, comments, etc.)
Capturing behavior hierarchies (e.g., clicks → cart adds → purchases)
Sequential recommendation with heterogeneous feedback signals
Context modeling in ranking systems with diverse user signals

Limitations / Caveats:

Behavior sequences may have highly imbalanced lengths (many clicks vs. few purchases)
Cross-attention learns pairwise interactions; fully modeling M behavior types requires O(M²) attention computations
Interpretation of learned cross-behavior weights can be opaque
Behavior type embeddings must be compatible for meaningful cross-attention

Internal Use Cases:

User context encoder in retrieval models fusing implicit and explicit feedback
Multi-signal ranking features replacing hand-crafted behavior aggregation
Foundation for the RL reward signal (next section) which optimizes over fused behavior predictions


Sparse Mixture of Experts (MoE) for Auto-Regressive Generation
Core Idea: A scalable auto-regressive token generation architecture using Sparse MoE that routes each position’s generation decision to specialized expert sub-networks, enabling efficient scaling without dense computation across all parameters.
Problem It Solves: Generative recommendation requires auto-regressive token generation (producing ranking/retrieval decisions sequentially), but dense Transformer scaling becomes prohibitively expensive. Full Transformer decoding for each token position is O(d²) per step. Simple Transformer baselines cannot handle the parameter scale needed for modeling complex e-commerce scenarios across all 400M+ users.
How It Works:

Input: Compressed user representation U and previous generated tokens [t₁, t₂, …, tᵢ₋₁]
At each generation step i, compute: hᵢ = Attention(U, [t₁, …, tᵢ₋₁])
Compute routing logits: rᵢ = Router(hᵢ) ∈ ℝᴱ (E = number of experts)
Select top-k experts via sparse gating: gᵢ = TopK(softmax(rᵢ)) (typically k=2)
Route hidden state to selected experts: oᵢ = Σⱼ∈TopK gᵢⱼ·Expertⱼ(hᵢ)
Generate next token: P(tᵢ|t₁…tᵢ₋₁,U) = softmax(oᵢWtoken)
Repeat for auto-regressive decoding until stopping token

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["User Representation<br/>+ Previous Tokens"] --> B["Attention Encoder"]
    B --> C["Router Network"]
    C --> D["Sparse Gating<br/>Top-k Selection"]
    E["Expert Pool<br/>E₁...Eₙ"] --> D
    D --> F["Expert Computation<br/>Weighted Sum"]
    F --> G["Token Generation<br/>softmax"]
    G --> H["Next Token"]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale auto-regressive recommendation ranking/retrieval
Generative recommendation systems requiring dense parameter coverage with sparse computation
Scaling recommendation models to millions of items/users without proportional compute cost
Multi-scenario recommendation where different scenarios route through specialized experts

Limitations / Caveats:

Sparse routing can cause load balancing issues (some experts overused, others underutilized)
Training instability: router gradients are discrete (hard routing) or biased (soft routing with gumbel-softmax)
Communication overhead on distributed systems if experts are on different GPUs/TPUs
Top-k selection destroys gradient flow to non-selected experts during training
Expert specialization may not emerge without auxiliary balancing losses

Internal Use Cases:

Auto-regressive ranking model where each step generates next item to rank
Retrieval candidate generation that sequentially produces items
Leveraging domain expertise: different experts for different scenarios (product-card expert, short-video expert, live-streaming expert)
Connection point for RL optimization (next section) where MoE outputs enable discrete policy actions


Reinforcement Learning Pipeline for End-to-End Retrieval-Ranking Optimization
Core Idea: An RL framework that connects retrieval (candidate generation) and ranking models, enabling the ranking model’s predictions to serve as reward signals that directly optimize the retrieval policy end-to-end.
Problem It Solves: Traditional recommendation pipelines (retrieval → ranking) optimize components independently. Retrieval models maximize recall of diverse candidates; ranking models maximize relevance. This decomposition misses the downstream impact: retrieved candidates that ranking subsequently downranks are wasted computation. No mechanism propagates ranking quality back to improve retrieval, leading to retrieval-ranking mismatch and suboptimal GMV/conversion outcomes.
How It Works:

State: User context state S = {user behavior, session context, scenario type}
Retrieval Policy: π_retrieval(a|S) generates candidate set A = {item₁, item₂, …, itemₖ} of size k through sparse MoE auto-regressive generation (previous section)
Ranking Oracle: Score candidates: R = rank_model(A, S), where rank_model outputs relevance scores or predicted CTR/conversion
Reward Signal: Define reward r(a) based on ranking output:

r(a) = predicted_CTR(a) for short-video scenario
r(a) = predicted_conversion_rate(a) for product-card scenario
r(a) = weighted combination for multi-objective (GMV-aware)


RL Objective: Optimize retrieval policy via policy gradient: ∇J = 𝔼[∇log π_retrieval(a|S) · r(a)]
End-to-End Training: Backprop ranking rewards through retrieval via reinforce/actor-critic algorithms

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["User State S"] --> B["Retrieval Policy<br/>Auto-Regressive MoE"]
    B --> C["Candidate Set A"]
    C --> D["Ranking Model<br/>Score Candidates"]
    D --> E["Reward Signal<br/>CTR/Conversion"]
    E --> F["Policy Gradient<br/>Optimization"]
    F -->|Backprop| B

Where It’s Useful:

End-to-end optimization of multi-stage recommendation systems
Learning what types of candidates to retrieve based on actual ranking quality
Handling exploration-exploitation in retrieval (RL naturally balances this)
Multi-objective optimization (GMV, orders, engagement) where ranking encodes objectives
Aligning business metrics (orders, GMV) directly into the retrieval learning signal

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires online interaction data or offline RL formulation; off-policy training is sample-inefficient
Reward signal design is crucial; sparse/delayed rewards (purchase signal) require credit assignment
Policy gradient methods have high variance; requires variance reduction (baseline, importance weighting)
Ranking model must be differentiable or reward must be treated as blackbox (policy gradient only)
Can amplify ranking biases back into retrieval (if ranker has systematic biases, RL learns to exploit them)
Computational cost: must score full candidate set A with ranking model every step

Internal Use Cases:

Learning retrieval strategies that maximize downstream ranking utility (not just diversity/coverage)
Contextual bandit formulation for exploration in retrieval
Connection to business KPIs: reward shaped from GMV/order metrics rather than engagement proxies
Multi-scenario policy learning: single RL framework where reward definition varies by scenario (live-streaming vs. product-card)

Analysis: OneMall’s Position in the Generative Recommender Landscape
OneMall extends the foundational work of OneRec and the broader generative recommendation paradigm by addressing a critical challenge that prior work acknowledged but didn’t fully solve: scenario fragmentation in e-commerce. While “The Rise of Generative Recommenders” highlighted how traditional multi-stage systems suffer from fragmentation across retrieval, ranking, and value modeling stages, OneMall tackles a different but equally important fragmentation—distributing recommendations across heterogeneous e-commerce scenarios (product cards, short-video, live-streaming). The paper bridges this gap by introducing an E-commerce Semantic Tokenizer that captures both semantic content and business-specific item relations across scenarios, effectively creating a unified representation layer. This is a natural evolution of the generative paradigm: if one unified model can replace the traditional three-stage pipeline, then one unified tokenizer can bridge multiple distribution channels—a scaling principle aligned with the 2025 research trends around the “One” series models.
The technical architecture itself represents a sophisticated synthesis of LLM techniques applied to e-commerce constraints. OneMall employs Query-Former for long sequence compression and Sparse MoE for scalable auto-regressive generation—both techniques that mirror advances in LLM efficiency. The Cross-Attention mechanism for multi-behavior sequence fusion suggests a deeper insight: recommendation systems now operate on sequences of user behaviors much like language models operate on token sequences. However, OneMall’s innovation lies in its Reinforcement Learning Pipeline, which connects retrieval and ranking models where the ranking model serves as a reward signal. This represents a methodological advance beyond OneRec’s basic end-to-end training: it creates a closed-loop optimization where downstream ranking performance directly informs upstream retrieval policy—solving a key tension in generative recommendation where you must trade off between diversity (retrieval) and conversion (ranking).
The empirical results validate this architectural philosophy across all scenarios, with particularly strong improvements in structured product recommendations (+13.01% GMV), demonstrating that unified generative models can indeed handle the business-critical metrics that matter in real e-commerce operations. The scale of deployment—400 million daily active users—confirms that generative recommendation has moved from research prototype to production reality, but OneMall’s contribution is specifically showing that one architecture can serve multiple scenarios without degradation, which directly challenges the traditional assumption that different distribution channels require different models.

literature/2026-02-03-Out-of-the-Memory-.title: Out of the Memory Barrier: A Highly Memory Efficient Training System for LLMs with Million-Token Contexts
literature/2026-02-03-Out-of-the-Memory-.content:
Out of the Memory Barrier: A Highly Memory Efficient Training System for LLMs with Million-Token Contexts
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-02
Rank: #1 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.02108v1
Abstract
Training Large Language Models (LLMs) on long contexts is severely constrained by prohibitive GPU memory overhead, not training time. The primary culprits are the activations, whose memory footprints scale linearly with sequence length. We introduce OOMB, a highly memory-efficient training system that directly confronts this barrier. Our approach employs a chunk-recurrent training framework with on-the-fly activation recomputation, which maintains a constant activation memory footprint (O(1)) and shifts the primary bottleneck to the growing KV cache. To manage the KV cache, OOMB integrates a suite of synergistic optimizations: a paged memory manager for both the KV cache and its gradients to eliminate fragmentation, asynchronous CPU offloading to hide data transfer latency, and page-level sparse attention to reduce both computational complexity and communication overhead. The synergy of these techniques yields exceptional efficiency. Our empirical results show that for every additional 10K tokens of context, the end-to-end training memory overhead increases by a mere 10MB for Qwen2.5-7B. This allows training Qwen2.5-7B with a 4M-token context on a single H200 GPU, a feat that would otherwise require a large cluster using context parallelism. This work represents a substantial advance in resource efficiency for long-context LLM training. The source code is available at github.com/wenhaoli-xmu/OOMB.
Key Techniques

Chunk-recurrent training framework
on-the-fly activation recomputation
paged memory manager
asynchronous CPU offloading
page-level sparse attention

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.26SuperInfer: SLO-Aware Rotary Scheduling and Memory Management for LLM Inference on Superchips0.26ParisKV: Fast and Drift-Robust KV-Cache Retrieval for Long-Context LLMs0.24Superlinear Multi-Step Attention0.23HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.23
Paged Memory Manager for KV Cache and Gradients
Core Idea: Organize KV cache and gradient memory into fixed-size pages with dynamic allocation, eliminating fragmentation and enabling efficient offloading to CPU memory.
Problem It Solves: KV cache grows linearly with sequence length and becomes the dominant memory bottleneck after activations are eliminated. Contiguous memory allocation leads to fragmentation and external memory fragmentation, preventing efficient use of available GPU memory and CPU offloading.
How It Works:

Allocate KV cache in fixed-size pages (similar to virtual memory paging in operating systems)
Maintain a page allocation table tracking which pages are in use and their memory locations
For sequences longer than GPU memory allows, allocate overflow pages in CPU RAM
Track page locations (GPU vs CPU) during forward/backward passes
Pages are deallocated and freed for reuse as tokens are processed and context windows shift
Gradient pages follow the same paging mechanism, allowing gradients to also spill to CPU if needed

Architecture:
flowchart TD
    A["KV Cache Request<br/>Size = seq_len * d_model"] --> B{"Page<br/>Available?"}
    B -->|Yes| C["Allocate from GPU<br/>Page Pool"]
    B -->|No| D{"GPU Memory<br/>Full?"}
    D -->|No| C
    D -->|Yes| E["Allocate from CPU<br/>Page Pool"]
    C --> F["Track Page Location<br/>in Allocation Table"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Return Page Handle<br/>to Model"]

Where It’s Useful:

Training on sequences exceeding single GPU memory capacity
Systems with heterogeneous memory (GPU + CPU RAM available)
Long-context inference scenarios where KV cache grows unbounded
Batch training where KV cache varies significantly across examples

Limitations / Caveats:

Page-based allocation introduces indexing overhead during access
CPU offloading introduces PCIe bandwidth bottleneck for data transfer
Page size selection affects fragmentation vs. overhead trade-off
Gradient computation may need to be stalled if gradient pages are on CPU while weights are on GPU

Internal Use Cases:

Managing cache for long user sequences in recommendation systems
Training ranking models with variable-length context windows without manual sequence padding


Asynchronous CPU Offloading with Latency Hiding
Core Idea: Proactively transfer KV cache pages to CPU memory asynchronously during computation phases, overlapping data movement latency with computation to maintain GPU utilization.
Problem It Solves: When KV cache overflows to CPU due to GPU memory constraints, synchronous transfers block computation, creating idle GPU time. Naive offloading strategies result in significant performance degradation as GPUs wait for data transfers.
How It Works:

Predict which KV cache pages will not be needed in the next N computation steps
Initiate asynchronous PCIe transfers to move these pages from GPU to CPU VRAM during forward/backward computation
Overlap transfer latency with GPU computation on independent data
When a page on CPU is needed again, prefetch it back to GPU asynchronously before it’s actually required
Use a scheduling algorithm to coordinate transfers such that data arrives just-in-time without stalling computation
Maintain a pool of available GPU memory pages for rapid re-loading of frequently-accessed offloaded pages

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Computation Queue<br/>Steps t:t+N"] --> B["Identify Unused<br/>Pages in t:t+N"]
    B --> C["Async Transfer:<br/>GPU → CPU"]
    D["GPU Computation<br/>Progress"] --> E{"Transfer<br/>Complete?"}
    E -->|Yes| F["Page in CPU Memory"]
    E -->|No| D
    G["Future Step t+N<br/>Needs Page"] --> H["Async Transfer:<br/>CPU → GPU"]
    H --> I["Page Ready<br/>for Computation"]

Where It’s Useful:

GPU systems with access to large CPU RAM pools
Scenarios where context length grows dynamically during training
Batch inference with variable sequence lengths per example
Multi-GPU training where some GPUs have less memory than others

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires accurate prediction of future page access patterns (mispredictions cause stalls)
PCIe bandwidth limits the rate of offloading (typically 16-32 GB/s, much slower than GPU memory)
Asynchronous operations add complexity and potential synchronization bugs
Effective only if computation can be overlapped; small models may not have enough work to hide latency
Overhead grows with distance between GPU and CPU memory (NUMA effects on larger systems)

Internal Use Cases:

Training recommendation models with long user history where most recent interactions compute while older ones offload
Multi-turn conversational models where early context is offloaded during processing of recent turns


Page-Level Sparse Attention
Core Idea: Apply sparse attention patterns at the granularity of pages (blocks of tokens) rather than individual tokens, reducing both computational complexity and KV cache access overhead.
Problem It Solves: Full attention over millions of tokens requires O(n²) computations and memory access, which remains prohibitively expensive even with KV cache on CPU. Sparse attention reduces complexity, but token-level sparsity introduces irregular memory access patterns and poor hardware utilization.
How It Works:

Organize tokens into fixed-size pages (e.g., 64 or 128 tokens per page)
Define sparse attention pattern at page level: which query pages can attend to which key-value pages
For each query page, compute attention only over a sparse subset of KV cache pages (e.g., local pages + summary pages)
Within selected pages, optionally apply dense attention (all tokens in query page attend to all tokens in selected KV pages)
This two-level hierarchy (sparse at page level, dense within pages) exploits both sequential locality and hardware efficiency
Pattern can be block-diagonal, strided, local-window-plus-summary, or custom based on task requirements

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["KV Cache Pages<br/>P1, P2, P3...Pn"] --> B["Sparse Pattern<br/>at Page Level"]
    C["Query Pages<br/>Q1, Q2, Q3"] --> D["Page Attention<br/>Matrix"]
    B --> D
    D --> E["Select KV Pages<br/>for Each Q Page"]
    E --> F["Dense Attention<br/>Within Selected Pages"]
    F --> G["Output<br/>Attention Scores"]

Where It’s Useful:

Training extremely long-context models (1M-10M tokens) where full attention is infeasible
Retrieval-augmented generation where only relevant context pages matter
Time-series or document analysis where locality is important
Any attention-based model where full O(n²) attention is prohibitive

Limitations / Caveats:

Reduces model expressiveness compared to full attention (some token pairs never interact)
Sparse pattern design is task-dependent; wrong pattern choice hurts performance
Page size selection affects the sparsity-computation trade-off
May require careful tuning to avoid ignoring important long-range dependencies
Implementation complexity increases compared to standard dense attention
Sparse patterns with irregular access can still have poor cache locality if not carefully designed

Internal Use Cases:

Long-context ranking where candidate relevance is determined by recent interactions (local pages) and historical patterns (summary pages)
Multi-document retrieval ranking where attention only focuses on relevant documents (pages) rather than all tokens

Extending Long-Context Training Beyond Efficient Attention
While the matched notes on “Efficient Attention Mechanisms for Large Language Model: A Survey” focus on reducing the computational complexity of attention through linear approximations and architectural innovations (like Mamba and RetNet), OOMB addresses a complementary but distinct bottleneck: the memory overhead of training with long contexts. The survey emphasizes how linear attention variants achieve linear time/space scaling for inference, but OOMB’s insight is that even with efficient attention, the activation memory during training scales linearly with sequence length. By introducing O(1) activation memory through chunk-recurrent training with on-the-fly recomputation, OOMB sidesteps the assumption that longer context windows necessarily require more activation storage—a perspective that complements rather than contradicts efficient attention research. The two approaches are synergistic: efficient attention mechanisms can reduce per-token computation, while OOMB reduces per-token activation memory, together enabling practical long-context training.
Bridging Pretraining Context and Training Feasibility
The “Lengthy Context Retrieval via LLM Pretraining” note describes leveraging LLMs pre-trained on large context windows to enable long-context inference without expensive fine-tuning. OOMB directly addresses the inverse problem: how to make long-context pretraining itself feasible. The note assumes that large-context pretraining is already possible (e.g., 8k+ token windows), but OOMB’s contribution is making this assumption practical for resource-constrained settings—enabling 4M-token context pretraining on a single H200 GPU. This reframes the relationship: OOMB provides the infrastructure that makes the pretraining stage of the pipeline in the matched note economically viable, particularly for organizations without massive compute clusters. The paged KV cache memory manager and asynchronous CPU offloading are specifically engineered solutions to the training-time constraints that Lengthy Context Retrieval takes as solved.
Addressing Context Rot Through Training Infrastructure
The “Long Context Degradation in LLMs” note warns that current benchmarks like Needle in a Haystack obscure real performance degradation on semantically complex tasks with distractors—a phenomenon called context rot. OOMB’s significance here is methodological: by making long-context training dramatically more accessible (eliminating the cluster requirement), it enables broader empirical investigation of context degradation across model scales and training regimes. Previously, studying context rot at million-token scales required institutional-scale compute; OOMB democratizes this investigation. Furthermore, the paper’s chunk-recurrent framework with page-level sparse attention may naturally induce different attention patterns than full-context training, potentially offering a fresh angle on understanding why context degradation occurs and whether certain training approaches mitigate it better than others.

literature/2026-02-03-PRISM-Parametrical.title: PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models
literature/2026-02-03-PRISM-Parametrical.content:
PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-02
Rank: #11 (score: 0.92, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.01762v1
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs), constrained by their auto-regressive nature, suffer from slow decoding. Speculative decoding methods have emerged as a promising solution to accelerate LLM decoding, attracting attention from both systems and AI research communities. Recently, the pursuit of better draft quality has driven a trend toward parametrically larger draft models, which inevitably introduces substantial computational overhead. While existing work attempts to balance the trade-off between prediction accuracy and compute latency, we address this fundamental dilemma through architectural innovation.   We propose PRISM, which disaggregates the computation of each predictive step across different parameter sets, refactoring the computational pathways of draft models to successfully decouple model capacity from inference cost. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that PRISM outperforms all existing draft architectures, achieving exceptional acceptance lengths while maintaining minimal draft latency for superior end-to-end speedup. We also re-examine scaling laws with PRISM, revealing that PRISM scales more effectively with expanding data volumes than other draft architectures. Through rigorous and fair comparison, we show that PRISM boosts the decoding throughput of an already highly optimized inference engine by more than 2.6x.
Key Techniques

Speculative decoding
Architectural disaggregation of computation
Scaling laws analysis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDART: Diffusion-Inspired Speculative Decoding for Fast LLM Inference0.63Variational Speculative Decoding: Rethinking Draft Training from Token Likelihood to Sequence Acceptance0.57SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration0.57DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.53Speculative Decoding with a Speculative Vocabulary0.45
PRISM Architecture (Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling)
Core Idea: An end-to-end architecture that reorganizes draft model computation to achieve exceptional acceptance lengths while maintaining minimal draft latency, enabling superior end-to-end speedup in LLM decoding.
Problem It Solves: Existing speculative decoding architectures cannot simultaneously achieve high acceptance rates (prediction quality) and low per-step latency (inference cost), limiting overall speedup gains in LLM decoding pipelines.
How It Works:

Take an input context/prompt and feed it through the draft model’s parametrically disaggregated computation
At each step, predict the next token by routing through specialized computation pathways optimized for different aspects of prediction
Generate a sequence of draft tokens that balances quality and speed
Present draft tokens to a verification stage where an auxiliary verifier accepts or rejects each draft token
Return the accepted draft tokens for output, with rejected tokens triggering fallback to the target model

Architecture (if applicable):
flowchart LR
    A[Context/Prompt] --> B[Draft Model<br/>with PCD]
    B --> C[Token Prediction<br/>High Quality]
    C --> D[Verifier<br/>Acceptance Check]
    D --> E{Token<br/>Accepted?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Output Token]
    E -->|No| G[Target Model]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Speedup Gains]

Where It’s Useful:

Accelerating autoregressive LLM decoding
Production inference serving with latency constraints
Situations where having a smaller auxiliary verifier is acceptable
Scaling inference throughput on existing inference engines

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires training/tuning of the specialized parameter sets within the disaggregated architecture
Speedup gains are limited by verifier latency and acceptance rates
Requires the target LLM and verifier to be compatible for speculative decoding
Performance gains scale with the quality of draft predictions relative to verification cost

Internal Use Cases:

LLM inference optimization in production systems
Balancing throughput vs. latency in real-time serving pipelines

Analysis: PRISM’s Architectural Innovation in Speculative Decoding
PRISM fundamentally addresses a scaling paradox that prior work in speculative decoding has grappled with but not fully resolved. The matched notes on Batch Verification in Speculative Decoding and Draft Shape Optimization for Dense Speculative Decoding both optimize around the draft model—either through better verification strategies (batching multiple candidates) or through shape tuning (adjusting the number and length of draft sequences). PRISM inverts this approach by refactoring the draft model’s internal computation rather than its external verification pipeline. Where shape optimization seeks the ideal (n, k) parameters for a fixed draft architecture, PRISM disaggregates computation across parameter sets to decouple model capacity from inference cost—effectively asking not “what shape of drafts should we verify?” but “what computational structure should the draft model itself have?”
This architectural innovation directly challenges the implicit assumption in prior work that larger draft models yield better prediction accuracy at the cost of higher compute. The LLM Inference Performance mental model notes that decode-phase performance is fundamentally “KV-bandwidth bound” and sensitive to latency per token. PRISM’s parametric refactoring operates within this constraint by redesigning how draft computation is distributed, reducing the “active-weight compute” term without proportionally sacrificing acceptance lengths. The paper’s demonstration of 2.6x throughput improvement on “already highly optimized inference engines” suggests PRISM moves beyond incremental tuning into the regime where architectural choices—not just batching strategy or shape selection—become the dominant lever.
The paper also contributes a methodological extension by re-examining scaling laws specifically for draft architectures. While prior work optimized for fixed model sizes and shapes, PRISM’s scaling analysis reveals that parametrically disaggregated drafts scale more effectively with data volume, implying a fundamentally different efficiency frontier. This bridges the tension between acceptance quality (which drives speedup) and draft latency (which bounds speedup), repositioning the trade-off itself as architecture-dependent rather than inevitable.

literature/2026-02-03-PretrainRL-Allevia.title: PretrainRL: Alleviating Factuality Hallucination of Large Language Models at the Beginning
literature/2026-02-03-PretrainRL-Allevia.content:
PretrainRL: Alleviating Factuality Hallucination of Large Language Models at the Beginning
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-02
Rank: #4 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RL, LLM, RAG
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.01875v1
Abstract
Large language models (LLMs), despite their powerful capabilities, suffer from factual hallucinations where they generate verifiable falsehoods. We identify a root of this issue: the imbalanced data distribution in the pretraining corpus, which leads to a state of “low-probability truth” and “high-probability falsehood”. Recent approaches, such as teaching models to say “I don’t know” or post-hoc knowledge editing, either evade the problem or face catastrophic forgetting. To address this issue from its root, we propose \textbf{PretrainRL}, a novel framework that integrates reinforcement learning into the pretraining phase to consolidate factual knowledge. The core principle of PretrainRL is “\textbf{debiasing then learning}.” It actively reshapes the model’s probability distribution by down-weighting high-probability falsehoods, thereby making “room” for low-probability truths to be learned effectively. To enable this, we design an efficient negative sampling strategy to discover these high-probability falsehoods and introduce novel metrics to evaluate the model’s probabilistic state concerning factual knowledge. Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks demonstrate that PretrainRL significantly alleviates factual hallucinations and outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
Key Techniques

Debiasing then learning
Efficient negative sampling strategy
Novel metrics for probabilistic state evaluation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDMAP: A Distribution Map for Text0.22CATTO: Balancing Preferences and Confidence in Language Models0.15Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.14Dicta-LM 3.0: Advancing The Frontier of Hebrew Sovereign LLMs0.14A Field Guide to Automatic Evaluation of LLM-Generated Summaries0.13
Efficient Negative Sampling Strategy
Core Idea: A targeted sampling mechanism to discover high-probability falsehoods in the pretraining corpus—statements the model assigns high likelihood but are factually incorrect—enabling efficient RL-based suppression.
Problem It Solves: Naively sampling all false statements is computationally prohibitive. The strategy must efficiently identify the most impactful falsehoods (high model probability + factually wrong) to maximize training signal with limited annotation budget.
How It Works:

Model probability estimation: For candidate statements in the corpus, compute the model’s predicted probability P(statement | context) before intervention.
Factuality filtering: Filter candidates to retain only those with ground-truth label = false (using available factuality annotations, crowdsourcing, or fact-checking databases).
Ranking by impact: Sort filtered falsehoods by their model-assigned probability in descending order, selecting top-k for negative supervision signals during RL.
Dynamic updating: As the model improves through RL iterations, re-sample high-probability falsehoods to maintain training difficulty and relevance.

Where It’s Useful:

Constructing training signals for factuality-focused RL during pretraining
Budget-constrained scenarios where not all data can be annotated
Curriculum learning approaches that gradually increase training difficulty
Identifying model weaknesses for targeted fine-tuning

Limitations / Caveats:

Relies on availability of ground-truth factuality labels, which may be sparse or subjective
High-probability falsehoods may be statistically rare, making efficient sampling challenging
Sampling bias: model-based selection may miss falsehoods the model is confident about for the wrong reasons
Computational cost of probability estimation scales with corpus size

Internal Use Cases:

Negative sampling in recommendation ranking to down-weight low-quality items the ranker incorrectly scores high
Curriculum learning strategies that prioritize hard negatives for collaborative filtering or ranking models


Probabilistic Factuality Metrics
Core Idea: Novel metrics to evaluate a model’s probabilistic state regarding factual knowledge—measuring the distributional alignment between true and false statements to quantify hallucination risk.
Problem It Solves: Existing evaluation focuses on end-to-end accuracy (does the model generate the right answer?) but misses the probabilistic condition underlying hallucinations. Metrics are needed to assess whether the model has resolved the “low-probability truth” / “high-probability falsehood” imbalance.
How It Works:

Compute probability distributions: For each true fact and corresponding false counterstatement, measure the model’s predicted probability: P(true_statement) and P(false_statement).
Probability margin calculation: Calculate the margin as P(true) - P(false) across test cases; positive margins indicate correct ordering.
Aggregate statistics: Compute metrics such as:

Truth probability gap: Mean P(true) - Mean P(false) across all test pairs
Margin consistency: Fraction of test cases where P(true) > P(false)
Entropy/calibration: Measure of how concentrated probability mass is on truths vs. falsehoods


Benchmarking: Use these metrics alongside accuracy to assess whether hallucination risk has been reduced even if downstream task accuracy plateaus.

Where It’s Useful:

Evaluating intermediate states during pretraining without waiting for end-to-end task evaluation
Diagnosing models that generate correct answers by luck but have poor probabilistic calibration
Monitoring fine-tuned models to ensure they retain factual robustness
Comparing different debiasing or knowledge editing interventions

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires paired true/false statements for evaluation—labor-intensive to construct comprehensive test sets
Metrics are sensitive to how “false” counterstatements are chosen (adversarial negatives may not exist)
May not capture all forms of hallucinations (e.g., internally consistent but globally false narratives)
Metrics can be gamed if not carefully designed (model may learn to suppress falsehoods without learning truths)

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking systems could use analogous probability margin metrics to ensure top-k items are not only predicted high but are actually relevant
Item quality prediction models could track “true positive probability” vs. “false positive probability” to monitor calibration

Analysis of PretrainRL in Context of Prior Work
PretrainRL fundamentally challenges a core assumption underlying the matched work on Early-Exit Large Language Models. While early-exit methods optimize efficiency by leveraging confident predictions at intermediate layers, PretrainRL identifies a deeper problem: the model’s probability distributions themselves are miscalibrated regarding factuality. The early-exit work assumes that high-confidence predictions are reliable, but PretrainRL reveals that models can be highly confident about falsehoods due to imbalanced pretraining data. This suggests that early-exit systems built on uncalibrated models may propagate hallucinations efficiently—a critical blind spot in the efficiency-focused approach. PretrainRL’s “debiasing then learning” principle would need to precede early-exit optimization to ensure that intermediate-layer confidence is actually trustworthy.
The connection to Lengthy Context Retrieval via LLM Pretraining is more subtle but important. That work leverages large pre-training context windows to enable better retrieval generalization, implicitly assuming that larger, more diverse pretraining corpora improve downstream performance. PretrainRL complicates this narrative by showing that simply scaling pretraining data without addressing distributional imbalance—where falsehoods may occur more frequently than truths in certain domains—actually worsens factuality. The paper’s negative sampling strategy to identify high-probability falsehoods represents a qualitative shift: rather than passively inheriting whatever distributions exist in raw corpora, PretrainRL actively reshapes the probability landscape during pretraining. This has implications for how we think about pretraining corpus composition and quality, suggesting that future work on context-aware pretraining should incorporate factuality-aware debiasing.
PretrainRL introduces methodological novelty that bridges to both prior works: it reframes pretraining not as a fixed statistical task but as an active reshaping problem. While early-exit methods optimize computation and context-retrieval work optimizes generalization, PretrainRL optimizes the probabilistic calibration of factual knowledge itself. The novel metrics for evaluating the model’s probabilistic state concerning factual knowledge suggest that future pretraining approaches should monitor and correct distributional biases continuously, rather than treating the model’s learned probability distribution as an inevitable byproduct of data. This positions PretrainRL as foundational work that should precede downstream optimizations like early-exiting or context-aware retrieval.

literature/2026-02-03-Read-As-Human-Comp.title: Read As Human: Compressing Context via Parallelizable Close Reading and Skimming
literature/2026-02-03-Read-As-Human-Comp.content:
Read As Human: Compressing Context via Parallelizable Close Reading and Skimming
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-02
Rank: #10 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.01840v1
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate exceptional capability across diverse tasks. However, their deployment in long-context scenarios is hindered by two challenges: computational inefficiency and redundant information. We propose RAM (Read As HuMan), a context compression framework that adopts an adaptive hybrid reading strategy, to address these challenges. Inspired by human reading behavior (i.e., close reading important content while skimming less relevant content), RAM partitions the context into segments and encodes them with the input query in parallel. High-relevance segments are fully retained (close reading), while low-relevance ones are query-guided compressed into compact summary vectors (skimming). Both explicit textual segments and implicit summary vectors are concatenated and fed into decoder to achieve both superior performance and natural language format interpretability. To refine the decision boundary between close reading and skimming, we further introduce a contrastive learning objective based on positive and negative query-segment pairs. Experiments demonstrate that RAM outperforms existing baselines on multiple question answering and summarization benchmarks across two backbones, while delivering up to a 12x end-to-end speedup on long inputs (average length 16K; maximum length 32K).
Key Techniques

Adaptive hybrid reading strategy
Query-guided compression
Contrastive learning objective

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityATACompressor: Adaptive Task-Aware Compression for Efficient Long-Context Processing in LLMs0.27SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.23Mine and Refine: Optimizing Graded Relevance in E-commerce Search Retrieval0.21SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.18Attn-GS: Attention-Guided Context Compression for Efficient Personalized LLMs0.17
Contrastive Learning Objective for Relevance Refinement
Core Idea: Train the relevance scoring function via contrastive learning on positive query-segment pairs (truly relevant) and negative pairs (irrelevant) to sharpen the decision boundary between close reading and skimming.
Problem It Solves: A naive relevance scorer may conflate partial relevance with true relevance, leading to incorrect routing decisions. The contrastive objective ensures the model learns a discriminative boundary that reliably distinguishes segments worth fully retaining from those safe to compress.
How It Works:

Positive Pair Construction: For a given query, identify segments that are semantically aligned or answer-bearing (ground truth relevant)
Negative Pair Construction: Sample segments that are semantically misaligned or irrelevant to the query
Contrastive Loss Formulation: Apply a contrastive loss (e.g., NT-Xent, InfoNCE) to push positive segment encodings close to the query encoding and negative encodings far:

Loss = -log[ exp(sim(q, seg+) / τ) / (exp(sim(q, seg+) / τ) + Σ exp(sim(q, seg−) / τ)) ]
where τ is temperature, sim() is cosine similarity or dot product


Joint Training: Train the segment encoder and relevance scorer end-to-end alongside the main task loss
Threshold Calibration: Use validation data to find the optimal relevance threshold that maximizes task performance

Where It’s Useful:

Refining relevance estimation in any adaptive compression scheme
Improving query-document matching in retrieval systems
Training discriminative encoders for selective processing tasks

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires labeled positive/negative segment pairs during training, increasing annotation overhead
Contrastive loss is sensitive to batch size and negative sampling strategy
The learned boundary may not generalize to out-of-domain queries or document types
Threshold calibration is task-dependent and may require retuning across different domains

Internal Use Cases:

Improving relevance signals in ranking models where some documents should be fully scored and others filtered early
Training efficient encoding models for real-time ranking that compress low-scoring candidates into placeholders

Analysis: RAM in Context of Existing Knowledge
Extending Context Compression Beyond Static Filtering
The RAM paper advances the Context Compression note by introducing adaptive and learned compression strategies rather than static filtering approaches. While the vault note describes compression as applying content filtering and salience scoring, RAM proposes a more sophisticated hybrid model: parallel query-segment encoding where relevance is dynamically determined relative to the input query, not just measured by generic importance. This is a meaningful shift—instead of pre-computing which content is important (keyword extraction, summarization), RAM makes compression decisions contingent on what the user actually asks. The contrastive learning objective the paper introduces (positive/negative query-segment pairs) provides a trainable mechanism to refine the close reading/skimming boundary, moving beyond heuristic filtering toward learned relevance judgment. This directly addresses the information utility problem in the vault note while adding a query-adaptive dimension.
Bridging Long-Context Reliability and Practical Performance
The RAM approach implicitly tackles concerns raised in the Long Context Degradation in LLMs note about non-uniform model performance on complex, distractor-rich inputs. By selectively close-reading high-relevance segments while skimming others, RAM avoids the “Needle in a Haystack” pitfall—the model isn’t just finding needles in uniform hay, it’s actively distinguishing signal from noise before decoding. The 12x speedup on 16K-32K length inputs demonstrates that this compression strategy maintains performance while reducing the computational burden that typically compounds degradation in long contexts. Where the vault note warns against misplaced confidence in long-context reliability, RAM offers a concrete mechanism to improve both speed and semantic fidelity by mimicking human reading behavior.
New Technique: Parallelizable Close Reading as Efficiency Lever
RAM introduces a novel procedural insight: that context compression can be parallelized across segments without sequential processing bottlenecks. The parallel encoding of segments with the query, followed by selective retention versus compression, is computationally different from traditional hierarchical or sequential summarization pipelines. This addresses a gap in the vault notes—they discuss compression mechanisms but don’t explicitly address parallelizability constraints. By keeping high-relevance segments in natural language (not just as latent vectors) while compressing others into summary vectors, RAM also preserves interpretability, a practical advantage not highlighted in the more abstract compression frameworks described in the matched notes. This hybrid explicit/implicit representation bridges theoretical efficiency gains with real-world usability.

literature/2026-02-03-RedVisor-Reasoning.title: RedVisor: Reasoning-Aware Prompt Injection Defense via Zero-Copy KV Cache Reuse
literature/2026-02-03-RedVisor-Reasoning.content:
RedVisor: Reasoning-Aware Prompt Injection Defense via Zero-Copy KV Cache Reuse
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-02
Rank: #3 (score: 0.59, max_sim: 0.79)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.01795v1
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly vulnerable to Prompt Injection (PI) attacks, where adversarial instructions hidden within retrieved contexts hijack the model’s execution flow. Current defenses typically face a critical trade-off: prevention-based fine-tuning often degrades general utility via the “alignment tax”, while detection-based filtering incurs prohibitive latency and memory costs. To bridge this gap, we propose RedVisor, a unified framework that synthesizes the explainability of detection systems with the seamless integration of prevention strategies. To the best of our knowledge, RedVisor is the first approach to leverage fine-grained reasoning paths to simultaneously detect attacks and guide the model’s safe response. We implement this via a lightweight, removable adapter positioned atop the frozen backbone. This adapter serves a dual function: it first generates an explainable analysis that precisely localizes the injection and articulates the threat, which then explicitly conditions the model to reject the malicious command. Uniquely, the adapter is active only during this reasoning phase and is effectively muted during the subsequent response generation. This architecture yields two distinct advantages: (1) it mathematically preserves the backbone’s original utility on benign inputs; and (2) it enables a novel KV Cache Reuse strategy, eliminating the redundant prefill computation inherent to decoupled pipelines. We further pioneer the integration of this defense into the vLLM serving engine with custom kernels. Experiments demonstrate that RedVisor outperforms state-of-the-art defenses in detection accuracy and throughput while incurring negligible utility loss.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques or methods:

Fine-tuned adapter mechanism

Explainable reasoning paths
KV Cache Reuse strategy
Dual-phase architecture (reasoning + response generation)

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPrefillShare: A Shared Prefill Module for KV Reuse in Multi-LLM Disaggregated Serving0.27LRAgent: Efficient KV Cache Sharing for Multi-LoRA LLM Agents0.26Towards Resiliency in Large Language Model Serving with KevlarFlow0.25DeltaKV: Residual-Based KV Cache Compression via Long-Range Similarity0.22SuperInfer: SLO-Aware Rotary Scheduling and Memory Management for LLM Inference on Superchips0.21
Zero-Copy KV Cache Reuse Strategy
Core Idea: Eliminate redundant prefill computation in decoupled reasoning-response pipelines by reusing the Key-Value cache from the reasoning phase during response generation, avoiding duplicate forward passes over the same context.
Problem It Solves: Traditional detection-response pipelines require two separate forward passes—one for analysis and one for response generation—both processing identical context tokens. This doubles memory bandwidth and compute cost. KV cache reuse eliminates this redundancy by keeping attention cache entries valid across phases.
How It Works:

Reasoning Phase: During adapter-guided reasoning, the backbone computes and caches Key-Value (KV) pairs for all input tokens (including context and query), storing them in the vLLM KV cache
Cache Preservation: The cached KV pairs remain valid in GPU memory across the phase boundary because the input context does not change between reasoning and response phases
Response Generation: During response generation, the backbone skips the prefill stage (which would recompute KV for input tokens) and directly begins decoding from the cached KV state, restoring only the output tokens generated during reasoning to the cache and reusing all context KVs

Architecture:
flowchart TD
    A["Input Tokens<br/>(context + query)"] --> B["Prefill + KV Compute<br/>(Reasoning Phase)"]
    B --> C["KV Cache Stored<br/>in GPU Memory"]
    C --> D["Response Decoding Phase"]
    D --> E["Reuse Cached KV<br/>Skip Prefill"]
    E --> F["Output Tokens"]
    style C fill:#99ff99
    style E fill:#99ff99

Where It’s Useful:

Serving inference pipelines where reasoning and response generation are sequential (detection-then-respond patterns)
vLLM and other KV-cache-aware inference engines with custom kernel support
High-throughput LLM serving where latency and memory bandwidth are bottlenecks
Any multi-stage decoding pipeline where intermediate states are cached

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful synchronization between phase boundaries to ensure cache coherence; kernel-level integration is necessary for correctness
Benefit is marginal for very short contexts where prefill is already negligible relative to decoding cost
Compatibility depends on the inference engine’s support for mid-generation cache manipulation (not all inference stacks support this)

Internal Use Cases:

Multi-stage ranking pipelines: Cache context features during initial ranking pass, reuse during scoring/calibration stage in two-stage ranking systems
Streaming RAG systems: Reuse cached embeddings and KV states across retrievals and re-rankings without recomputing context prefills
Interactive recommendation refinement: Store user profile KV cache during initial filtering, reuse during personalization ranking to avoid recomputation


Integration with vLLM Serving Engine
Core Idea: Implement RedVisor’s reasoning adapter and KV cache reuse as custom kernels within the vLLM serving engine, enabling efficient, batched inference with multi-phase request handling.
Problem It Solves: A naive implementation of reasoning-aware defense as a post-hoc wrapper incurs significant overhead due to redundant context loading, unbatched adapter execution, and cache format mismatches. Direct vLLM integration enables (1) seamless request batching across multiple phases, (2) zero-copy cache transfers, and (3) hardware-optimized kernel execution.
How It Works:

Request Queuing: Incoming requests are batched and routed to the reasoning phase; the vLLM scheduler queues adapter inference on the backbone, with requests grouped by context length for efficient computation
Adapter Execution: Custom vLLM kernels execute the adapter module within the same GPU memory space as the backbone, preserving KV cache locality
Phase Transition: After reasoning, the same batched requests are transitioned to response generation; the scheduler maintains KV cache ownership and skips prefill, directly dispatching decoding kernels

Where It’s Useful:

Production LLM serving infrastructure (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang, etc.)
Multi-tenant serving scenarios where request batching across security phases improves hardware utilization
Deployments requiring transparent integration into existing inference pipelines

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires custom kernel development and deep knowledge of the serving engine’s internals; not portable across different inference frameworks without additional engineering
Debugging and profiling multi-phase batched execution is complex and less transparent than simple sequential execution
Vendor lock-in to vLLM ecosystem; changes to vLLM’s cache management or scheduling may require adapter maintenance

Internal Use Cases:

Batch recommendation ranking: Integrate RedVisor into vLLM-based ranking services to defend batch inference on retrieved candidate sets while maintaining high throughput
Collaborative filtering with LLM explanations: Protect explanation generation pipeline in vLLM-served personalized recommendation systems from injection attacks
Large-scale content moderation: Implement reasoning-aware moderation detection at serving scale using vLLM’s batching capabilities

Analysis: RedVisor in Context of Prior Work
RedVisor addresses a fundamental tension in LLM defense that becomes especially critical when viewed through the lens of LLM Inference Performance. The paper’s key innovation—the “zero-copy KV cache reuse” strategy—directly tackles the decode-phase bottleneck identified in the Team Mental Model. Traditional detection-based defenses create two separate inference passes (one for reasoning/detection, one for response generation), which means redundant prefill computation and multiple KV cache instantiations. RedVisor’s adapter-based approach that is “active only during the reasoning phase and muted during response generation” cleverly exploits the inference pipeline structure: by reusing the KV cache from the detection phase, it converts what would be two full prefill operations into a single prefill with a lightweight reasoning pass. This is a concrete instantiation of the principle that “everything works by shrinking one of those terms”—in this case, the attention-on-context and KV-cache-reads terms for the second pass.
The connection to Early-Exit Language Models is more subtle but important. Both papers recognize that not all computation is equally necessary across the inference pipeline. Early-exit systems ask whether we can terminate early on “easy” inputs; RedVisor asks whether we can selectively activate/deactivate reasoning capacity based on threat detection needs. The adapter mechanism mirrors the early-exit pattern of conditional computation: maintaining a frozen backbone while adding lightweight, removable logic that operates conditionally. However, RedVisor’s motivation differs—it’s not about confidence thresholds on normal inputs, but about injecting reasoning paths that explicitly guide safe behavior when adversarial content is detected.
Finally, RedVisor’s approach of decomposing the defense into fine-grained reasoning that localizes attacks echoes the principles in Recursive Language Models, which emphasize that LLMs can “programmatically examine, decompose, and recursively call itself.” While RLMs apply this to handle context-length constraints, RedVisor applies recursive reasoning to handle adversarial constraints—the model must examine the prompt structure, identify injected instructions, and recursively condition its response generation on that analysis. Both papers leverage the insight that LLMs’ reasoning capabilities can be structured and controlled through careful prompt/architecture design. RedVisor’s novel contribution is demonstrating that this structured reasoning can simultaneously serve defense (detecting attacks) and efficiency (avoiding redundant computation through KV reuse) without incurring the “alignment tax” of prevention-based fine-tuning.

literature/2026-02-03-Rethinking-Generat.title: Rethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs
literature/2026-02-03-Rethinking-Generat.content:
Rethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-02
Rank: #5 (score: 0.00, max_sim: 0.90)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.02338v1
Abstract
Semantic ID (SID)-based recommendation is a promising paradigm for scaling sequential recommender systems, but existing methods largely follow a semantic-centric pipeline: item embeddings are learned from foundation models and discretized using generic quantization schemes. This design is misaligned with generative recommendation objectives: semantic embeddings are weakly coupled with collaborative prediction, and generic quantization is inefficient at reducing sequential uncertainty for autoregressive modeling. To address these, we propose ReSID, a recommendation-native, principled SID framework that rethinks representation learning and quantization from the perspective of information preservation and sequential predictability, without relying on LLMs. ReSID consists of two components: (i) Field-Aware Masked Auto-Encoding (FAMAE), which learns predictive-sufficient item representations from structured features, and (ii) Globally Aligned Orthogonal Quantization (GAOQ), which produces compact and predictable SID sequences by jointly reducing semantic ambiguity and prefix-conditional uncertainty. Theoretical analysis and extensive experiments across ten datasets show the effectiveness of ReSID. ReSID consistently outperforms strong sequential and SID-based generative baselines by an average of over 10%, while reducing tokenization cost by up to 122x. Code is available at github.com/FuCongResearchSquad/ReSID.
Key Techniques

Field-Aware Masked Auto-Encoding (FAMAE)
Globally Aligned Orthogonal Quantization (GAOQ)
Semantic ID-based recommendation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityRethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs0.81DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.44End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.32GLASS: A Generative Recommender for Long-sequence Modeling via SID-Tier and Semantic Search0.28Unleash the Potential of Long Semantic IDs for Generative Recommendation0.28
Globally Aligned Orthogonal Quantization (GAOQ)
Core Idea: Quantize continuous item embeddings into discrete semantic IDs by jointly optimizing for semantic alignment and prefix-conditional uncertainty reduction, ensuring both item discrimination and predictable autoregressive sequences.
Problem It Solves: Generic quantization schemes (e.g., standard vector quantization, product quantization) optimize only for reconstruction accuracy, not for sequential predictability. This results in quantization errors that propagate and compound during autoregressive generation. GAOQ jointly minimizes semantic ambiguity (which items map to the same ID) and prefix-conditional entropy (uncertainty given the history), directly targeting the generative recommendation objective.
How It Works:

Initialize K learned codebook vectors {c₁, c₂, …, c_K} where K is the vocabulary size
For each item embedding ϕ(i), assign discrete ID z(i) = argmin_k ||ϕ(i) - c_k||₂ (nearest codebook vector)
Apply orthogonal constraints to codebook to ensure vectors are well-separated and stable
Optimize codebook jointly with two objectives:

Semantic alignment: minimize reconstruction error ||ϕ(i) - c_{z(i)}||² (reduce semantic ambiguity)
Prefix-conditional loss: for sequences, minimize cross-entropy loss L(z_t | z_{<t}) given history, forcing IDs to be predictable from context


Use global alignment loss to ensure codebook vectors are balanced across the embedding space (avoid collapse to few IDs)
Output discrete token sequences where each item i is represented by ID z(i) ∈ {1, …, K}

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Continuous<br/>Embeddings"] --> B["Nearest Neighbor<br/>Assignment"]
    B --> C["Quantized IDs"]
    C --> D["Prefix-Conditional<br/>Loss"]
    D --> E["Codebook<br/>Update"]
    E --> F["Orthogonal<br/>Constraint"]
    F --> G["Updated<br/>Codebook"]
    G --> B
    A --> H["Reconstruction<br/>Loss"]
    H --> E

Where It’s Useful:

Generative sequential recommendation where tokens feed into autoregressive models
Scenarios requiring compact, discrete representations for efficient inference
Systems where both semantic quality and predictability matter (item similarity + sequence coherence)
Tokenization for language-model-based recommenders

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires balancing two potentially competing objectives (semantic vs. predictive); hyperparameter tuning needed
Orthogonal constraints scale poorly as codebook size K increases (expensive for very large vocabularies)
Assumes prefix history is informative for next-item prediction; may perform poorly in sparse/sparse-history regimes
Discrete quantization is non-differentiable; straight-through estimators or other tricks needed during backprop

Internal Use Cases:

Tokenizing recommendation sequences for fine-tuning LLMs or training transformer-based recommenders
Creating “semantic ID” vocabularies that compress item catalogs while maintaining predictive power
Reducing inference cost in production by using small discrete IDs instead of dense embeddings

Analysis: ReSID and Its Place in Semantic ID Research
ReSID fundamentally challenges the LLM-centric assumption that has dominated recent semantic ID work. While papers like the CoST (Contrastive Quantization based Semantic Tokenization) and the broader “Improving Recommendation Systems in the Age of LLM” literature assume foundation models as the starting point for embeddings, ReSID explicitly rejects this dependency. Instead, it proposes recommendation-native learning through Field-Aware Masked Auto-Encoding (FAMAE)—a paradigm shift that asks: why should we inherit semantic representations designed for language when we can learn representations optimized for sequential predictability? This directly extends the original RQ-VAE work (Better Generalization with Semantic IDs) by replacing generic quantization with domain-specific objectives, but goes further by questioning the embedding source itself.
The paper’s core innovation—Globally Aligned Orthogonal Quantization (GAOQ)—builds on insights from CoST’s success with neighborhood preservation during quantization (+43% recall@5 improvement), but reframes the problem. Where CoST focuses on maintaining collaborative relationships through contrastive learning, ReSID tackles prefix-conditional uncertainty and semantic ambiguity jointly. This is a crucial methodological bridge: both papers recognize that naive reconstruction-based quantization (minimizing reconstruction error) is misaligned with recommendation objectives, but ReSID goes deeper by treating quantization as an information-theoretic problem about reducing sequential predictability uncertainty rather than just preserving neighborhood structure. The 122x reduction in tokenization cost while achieving 10% average improvements suggests that FAMAE’s structured feature learning is more sample-efficient than relying on expensive foundation model embeddings.
What ReSID adds to the knowledge base is a post-LLM perspective on semantic tokenization. The matched notes show a clear trend toward integrating LLMs (Generative Explore-Exploit using LLM optimizers, semantic augmentation architectures), but ReSID’s 10% gains without LLMs suggest that this trend may be over-correcting. Its success indicates that for recommendation systems, task-specific inductive biases (structured fields, sequential prediction as the primary objective) may provide more value than general-purpose semantic knowledge. This doesn’t invalidate LLM augmentation but repositions it as complementary rather than foundational, opening a new research direction: hybrid approaches that selectively integrate LLM-derived signals only where collaborative filtering alone is weak (e.g., cold-start, cross-domain transfer).

literature/2026-02-03-SLIME-Stabilized-L.title: SLIME: Stabilized Likelihood Implicit Margin Enforcement for Preference Optimization
literature/2026-02-03-SLIME-Stabilized-L.content:
SLIME: Stabilized Likelihood Implicit Margin Enforcement for Preference Optimization
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-02
Rank: #9 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RL, LLM, RAG
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.02383v1
Abstract
Direct preference optimization methods have emerged as a computationally efficient alternative to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs). Latest approaches have streamlined the alignment process by deriving implicit reward functions, yet they often suffer from a critical objective mismatch: optimizing the relative margin between chosen and rejected responses does not guarantee the preservation of the chosen response’s absolute likelihood. This can lead to unlearning'', where the model degrades the probability of high-quality outputs to satisfy margin constraints, and formatting collapse” caused by the over-penalization of rejected sequences. In this work, we introduce SLIME (Stabilized Likelihood Implicit Margin Enforcement), a reference-free alignment objective designed to decouple preference learning from generation quality. SLIME incorporates a three-pronged objective: (1) an anchoring term to maximize the likelihood of preferred responses; (2) a stabilizing penalty that prevents the probabilities of rejected tokens from collapsing to zero; and (3) a dual-margin mechanism that combines hard and soft constraints for precise boundary shaping. Our results demonstrate that SLIME achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines while maintaining higher generation stability.
Key Techniques

Anchoring term
Stabilizing penalty
Dual-margin mechanism

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityCold-Start Personalization via Training-Free Priors from Structured World Models0.22To Search or Not to Search: Aligning the Decision Boundary of Deep Search Agents via Causal Intervention0.21The Trajectory Alignment Coefficient: From Reward Tuning to Reward Learning0.20CATTO: Balancing Preferences and Confidence in Language Models0.17LogitsCoder: Towards Efficient Chain-of-Thought Path Search via Logits Preference Decoding for Code Generation0.14
Analysis: SLIME’s Position in the Preference Optimization Landscape
SLIME directly addresses a fundamental problem in the evolution of preference optimization methods documented in your vault notes. While the Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) literature review outlines the progression from RLHF to more efficient direct methods, SLIME identifies a critical failure mode that these methods share: the objective mismatch between optimizing relative margins and preserving absolute likelihood. This represents a refinement within the DPO paradigm rather than a departure from it. The paper’s core insight—that margin-based optimization can degrade high-quality outputs through “unlearning” and “formatting collapse”—echoes concerns about drift and model degradation mentioned in the RLHF overview, but identifies the specific mechanism where this occurs in modern direct preference methods.
The technical bridge between SLIME and prior work appears in its three-pronged objective design. The anchoring term that maximizes preferred response likelihood directly operationalizes a principle implicit in both DPO and the Test-Time Preference Optimization (TPO) approach: that alignment should preserve generation quality, not sacrifice it for preference signals. SLIME’s stabilizing penalty—preventing rejected token probabilities from collapsing to zero—is a novel contribution that addresses a pathology not explicitly solved by margin-based approaches. The dual-margin mechanism (hard and soft constraints) represents a methodological sophistication beyond simple relative preference ranking, suggesting that effective alignment requires nuanced boundary control rather than monolithic margin enforcement.
SLIME’s reference-free design and emphasis on generation stability also aligns with the broader shift documented in your notes: moving away from complex auxiliary models (like the reward model in RLHF) toward simpler, more direct optimization objectives. However, where LLM Alignment as Retriever Optimization reframes alignment through information retrieval theory, SLIME remains within the preference optimization framework but adds explicit stability constraints. This suggests a convergence point: effective alignment methods must simultaneously optimize for preference discrimination and output quality preservation—a principle that could be tested across different theoretical framings (retriever optimization, margin enforcement, or likelihood-based objectives).

literature/2026-02-03-VisionTrim-Unified.title: VisionTrim: Unified Vision Token Compression for Training-Free MLLM Acceleration
literature/2026-02-03-VisionTrim-Unified.content:
VisionTrim: Unified Vision Token Compression for Training-Free MLLM Acceleration
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-30
Rank: #6 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Embeddings, LLM
Code: github.com/hanxunyu/VisionTrim
Abstract
Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) suffer from high computational costs due to excessive visual tokens, particularly in high-resolution and video-based scenarios. Existing token reduction methods typically focus on isolated pipeline components and often neglect textual alignment, leading to performance degradation. In this paper, we propose VisionTrim, a unified framework for training-free MLLM acceleration, integrating two effective plug-and-play modules: 1) the Dominant Vision Token Selection (DVTS) module, which preserves essential visual tokens via a global-local view, and 2) the Text-Guided Vision Complement (TGVC) module, which facilitates context-aware token merging guided by textual cues. Extensive experiments across diverse image and video multimodal benchmarks demonstrate the performance superiority of our VisionTrim, advancing practical MLLM deployment in real-world applications. The code is available at: github.com/hanxunyu/VisionTrim.
Key Techniques

Dominant Vision Token Selection (DVTS)
Text-Guided Vision Complement (TGVC)
