Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityNüwa: Mending the Spatial Integrity Torn by VLM Token Pruning0.45ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.34VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.33FlashVID: Efficient Video Large Language Models via Training-free Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging0.33SToRM: Supervised Token Reduction for Multi-modal LLMs toward efficient end-to-end autonomous driving0.31
Text-Guided Vision Complement (TGVC)
Core Idea: Merge redundant visual tokens via context-aware fusion, where textual input (query/prompt) guides which visual information to preserve during token combination.
Problem It Solves: Token reduction methods operate independently of textual context, risking removal of tokens relevant to specific questions or tasks. Conversely, naive token merging loses spatial resolution. TGVC aligns vision-language understanding by using text to inform which visual tokens should be combined.
How It Works:

Receive reduced token set from DVTS module and text embeddings from language model (query/prompt)
Compute cross-modal attention between text embeddings and remaining visual tokens
Identify clusters of visually similar tokens that are jointly irrelevant to the text query
Merge tokens within each cluster via weighted averaging (weights based on attention and similarity)
Maintain tokens with high text-vision relevance unchanged to preserve discriminative features
Output merged token set passed to language model decoder

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Reduced Vision<br/>Tokens"] --> B["Cross-Modal<br/>Attention"]
    C["Text Embeddings<br/>(query)"] --> B
    B --> D["Identify Mergeable<br/>Token Clusters"]
    D --> E["Weighted Merging<br/>per Cluster"]
    E --> F["Final Token Set<br/>(m' tokens, m' ≤ m)"]

Where It’s Useful:

Question-answering over images (visual QA) where certain regions are irrelevant to the question
Video captioning where temporal frames contain redundancy relative to caption requirements
Instruction-tuned MLLMs where user prompts vary in focus and detail demands
Fine-grained vs. coarse-grained understanding tasks with varying token budgets

Limitations / Caveats:

Cross-modal attention computation adds overhead; must be efficient to avoid negating compression gains
Text bias: if prompt is generic or vague, merging decisions may be suboptimal
Merging is lossy—cannot recover fine-grained spatial details post-merge if needed downstream
Assumes text and vision are sufficiently aligned; may fail for abstract/artistic images without clear textual grounding

Discussions & References

huggingface.co
github.com

literature/2026-02-04-A-Field-Guide-to-A.title: A Field Guide to Automatic Evaluation of LLM-Generated Summaries
literature/2026-02-04-A-Field-Guide-to-A.content:
A Field Guide to Automatic Evaluation of LLM-Generated Summaries
Source: S2:Annual International ACM SIGIR Conference on Research and Development in Information Retrieval
Published: 2024
Rank: #5 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RecSys
Abstract
Large Language models (LLMs) are rapidly being adopted for tasks such as text summarization, in a wide range of industries. This has driven the need for scalable, automatic, reliable, and cost-effective methods to evaluate the quality of LLM-generated text. What is meant by evaluating an LLM is not yet well defined and there are widely different expectations about what kind of information evaluation will produce. Evaluation methods that were developed for traditional Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks (before the rise of LLMs) remain applicable but are not sufficient for capturing high-level semantic qualities of summaries. Emerging evaluation methods that use LLMs to evaluate LLM-output, appear to be powerful but lacking in reliability. New elements of LLM generated text that were not an element of previous NLP tasks, such as the artifacts of hallucination, need to be considered. We outline the different types of LLM evaluation currently used in the literature but focus on offline, system-level evaluation of the text generated by LLMs. Evaluating LLM-generated summaries is a complex and fast-evolving area, and we propose strategies for applying evaluation methods to avoid common pitfalls. Despite having promising strategies for evaluating LLM summaries, we highlight some open challenges that remain.
Key Techniques

Automated evaluation metrics
LLM-based evaluation methods
Hallucination detection

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityJADE: Expert-Grounded Dynamic Evaluation for Open-Ended Professional Tasks0.19Rethinking Perplexity: Revealing the Impact of Input Length on Perplexity Evaluation in LLMs0.15TAM-Eval: Evaluating LLMs for Automated Unit Test Maintenance0.15ReNeuIR at SIGIR 2024: The Third Workshop on Reaching Efficiency in Neural Information Retrieval0.15A Task-Centric Perspective on Recommendation Systems0.14
Multi-Dimensional LLM-Based Summary Evaluation Framework
Core Idea: A systematic approach to evaluating LLM-generated summaries using a combination of traditional NLP metrics, LLM-as-judge methods, and specialized hallucination detection, structured around dimensions like faithfulness, relevance, coherence, and fluency.
Problem It Solves: Traditional metrics (ROUGE, BLEU) only capture lexical overlap and miss semantic quality, while purely LLM-based evaluation lacks reliability and standardization. The rise of hallucinations in LLM outputs requires new evaluation primitives not covered by legacy NLP methods.
How It Works:

Dimension Selection - Choose evaluation dimensions based on use case (faithfulness for factual summaries, coherence for narrative text, coverage for completeness)
Multi-Method Assessment - Apply layered evaluation: reference-free metrics for fluency/grammar → semantic similarity embeddings → LLM-as-judge for nuanced qualities → specialized detectors for hallucinations
System-Level Aggregation - Combine scores across test sets for offline, system-level comparison rather than relying on individual instance judgments
Calibration & Validation - Ground automated scores against human judgments on representative samples to establish reliability thresholds

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Source Text + Summary] --> B[Traditional Metrics<br/>ROUGE/BLEU]
    A --> C[Embedding-Based<br/>Semantic Similarity]
    A --> D[LLM-as-Judge<br/>Dimension Scoring]
    A --> E[Hallucination<br/>Detection]
    B --> F[Aggregated<br/>System Score]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Human Calibration<br/>Validation]

Where It’s Useful:

Production LLM summarization systems requiring continuous quality monitoring without per-instance human review
A/B testing different prompting strategies or model versions for summarization tasks
Domain-specific applications (medical, legal, news) where factual accuracy (faithfulness) is critical
Multi-document summarization where coverage and coherence are key quality dimensions

Limitations / Caveats:

LLM-as-judge methods inherit biases from the evaluator LLM (position bias, verbosity bias, self-preference)
Lack of standardized dimension definitions across research/industry creates reproducibility issues
High computational cost for LLM-based evaluation at scale; may require smaller specialized evaluator models
Correlation with human judgment varies significantly by domain and summary type
Hallucination detection remains an unsolved problem with high false positive/negative rates

Internal Use Cases:

Evaluate LLM-generated item descriptions or review summaries in RecSys pipelines using faithfulness + relevance dimensions
Monitor quality degradation in production ranking explanation generation (e.g., “why we recommended this”) via automated coherence + fluency scoring
Create pseudo-labels for summarization tasks in cold-start scenarios, then validate quality with this framework before using as training data
Build multi-metric evaluation dashboards for LLM experimentation, combining cheap reference-free metrics for rapid iteration with periodic LLM-judge validation

literature/2026-02-04-ATACompressor-Adap.title: ATACompressor: Adaptive Task-Aware Compression for Efficient Long-Context Processing in LLMs
literature/2026-02-04-ATACompressor-Adap.content:
ATACompressor: Adaptive Task-Aware Compression for Efficient Long-Context Processing in LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #3 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03226v1
Abstract
Long-context inputs in large language models (LLMs) often suffer from the “lost in the middle” problem, where critical information becomes diluted or ignored due to excessive length. Context compression methods aim to address this by reducing input size, but existing approaches struggle with balancing information preservation and compression efficiency. We propose Adaptive Task-Aware Compressor (ATACompressor), which dynamically adjusts compression based on the specific requirements of the task. ATACompressor employs a selective encoder that compresses only the task-relevant portions of long contexts, ensuring that essential information is preserved while reducing unnecessary content. Its adaptive allocation controller perceives the length of relevant content and adjusts the compression rate accordingly, optimizing resource utilization. We evaluate ATACompressor on three QA datasets: HotpotQA, MSMARCO, and SQUAD-showing that it outperforms existing methods in terms of both compression efficiency and task performance. Our approach provides a scalable solution for long-context processing in LLMs. Furthermore, we perform a range of ablation studies and analysis experiments to gain deeper insights into the key components of ATACompressor.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques:

Selective encoder for task-relevant compression

Adaptive allocation controller for dynamic compression rate adjustment
Evaluation on multiple QA datasets (HotpotQA
MSMARCO

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.34SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.32Data Distribution Matters: A Data-Centric Perspective on Context Compression for Large Language Model0.31SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.28Read As Human: Compressing Context via Parallelizable Close Reading and Skimming0.27
Adaptive Allocation Controller
Core Idea: Dynamically adjusts the compression rate based on the perceived length of relevant content in the input, allocating computational budget proportionally to input complexity.
Problem It Solves: Fixed compression ratios waste resources on inputs with little relevant content and degrade on content-dense inputs. Adaptive allocation ensures efficient resource utilization across variable-length relevant segments.
How It Works:

Estimate Relevant Length: Analyze the distribution of relevance scores from the selective encoder to estimate total relevant content length
Compute Adaptive Compression Rate: Given estimated length L_rel and target budget B, set compression ratio r = B / L_rel
Apply Variable Compression: Use the computed ratio to guide the encoder’s compression strength, allocating more tokens to dense relevant regions

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Relevance<br/>Score Dist."] --> B["Estimate<br/>Relevant Length"]
    B --> C["Length<br/>Estimate"]
    C --> D["Compute<br/>Compression Ratio"]
    D --> E["Ratio"]
    E --> F["Apply Adaptive<br/>Encoding"]
    F --> G["Compressed<br/>Output"]

Where It’s Useful:

Handling variable-length document collections without per-item tuning
Budget-constrained inference where context window allocation must scale with content density
Multi-task scenarios where compression needs differ by task complexity

Limitations / Caveats:

Length estimation may be inaccurate with bimodal relevance distributions
Assumes target budget B is fixed; requires re-tuning for different deployment constraints
Overhead of computing adaptation may not justify savings on short inputs

Internal Use Cases:

Dynamic token budgeting for multi-hop recommendation retrieval paths
Query-dependent indexing for semantic search backends


Combined Selective Encoding + Adaptive Allocation Pipeline
Core Idea: Integrates task-aware token filtering with dynamic compression rate adjustment into a unified framework that preserves information while scaling compression efficiency.
Problem It Solves: The “lost in the middle” problem in long-context LLMs, where critical information is diluted by excessive context length. Existing methods sacrifice either compression efficiency or information preservation.
How It Works:

Task-Aware Selection: Filter context to task-relevant segments using relevance scoring (selective encoder)
Length Perception: Measure the density/length of selected relevant content
Rate Adaptation: Compute compression ratio inversely proportional to relevant content length
Optimized Encoding: Apply compression at the adaptive rate to the selected segments
Inference: Feed compressed context + task query to LLM for task-specific decoding

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Long Context"] --> B["Selective<br/>Encoder"]
    C["Task Query"] --> B
    B --> D["Selected<br/>Segments"]
    D --> E["Adaptive<br/>Controller"]
    E --> F["Compression<br/>Rate"]
    F --> G["Dense<br/>Encoder"]
    G --> H["Compressed<br/>Context"]
    H --> I["LLM<br/>Decoder"]
    C --> I
    I --> J["Task Output"]

Where It’s Useful:

Open-domain QA (HotpotQA, MSMARCO, SQuAD)
Multi-document summarization with task-specific focus
Long-context retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires labeled task queries during compression; may not generalize to unseen task types
Cascaded pipeline introduces cumulative latency (selection → rate computation → encoding)
Performance gains depend on sufficient variance in task-relevant content distribution

Internal Use Cases:

Compressing retrieved job postings for semantic job search (reference: Scaling Up Efficient Small Language Models Serving)
Dynamic context filtering in conversational recommendation systems
Query-aware candidate ranking in large-scale retrieval pipelines

literature/2026-02-04-Accordion-Thinking.title: Accordion-Thinking: Self-Regulated Step Summaries for Efficient and Readable LLM Reasoning
literature/2026-02-04-Accordion-Thinking.content:
Accordion-Thinking: Self-Regulated Step Summaries for Efficient and Readable LLM Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #4 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03249v1
Abstract
Scaling test-time compute via long Chain-ofThought unlocks remarkable gains in reasoning capabilities, yet it faces practical limits due to the linear growth of KV cache and quadratic attention complexity. In this paper, we introduce Accordion-Thinking, an end-to-end framework where LLMs learn to self-regulate the granularity of the reasoning steps through dynamic summarization. This mechanism enables a Fold inference mode, where the model periodically summarizes its thought process and discards former thoughts to reduce dependency on historical tokens. We apply reinforcement learning to incentivize this capability further, uncovering a critical insight: the accuracy gap between the highly efficient Fold mode and the exhaustive Unfold mode progressively narrows and eventually vanishes over the course of training. This phenomenon demonstrates that the model learns to encode essential reasoning information into compact summaries, achieving effective compression of the reasoning context. Our Accordion-Thinker demonstrates that with learned self-compression, LLMs can tackle complex reasoning tasks with minimal dependency token overhead without compromising solution quality, and it achieves a 3x throughput while maintaining accuracy on a 48GB GPU memory configuration, while the structured step summaries provide a human-readable account of the reasoning process.
Key Techniques

Dynamic summarization
Fold inference mode
Reinforcement learning for self-compression
Structured step summaries

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMs0.28Fast-Slow Efficient Training for Multimodal Large Language Models via Visual Token Pruning0.27ImgCoT: Compressing Long Chain of Thought into Compact Visual Tokens for Efficient Reasoning of Large Language Model0.22LLaDA2.1: Speeding Up Text Diffusion via Token Editing0.21DeltaKV: Residual-Based KV Cache Compression via Long-Range Similarity0.20
Fold/Unfold Inference Modes
Core Idea: A dual-mode inference strategy where Fold mode uses learned compression and token discarding for efficiency, while Unfold mode maintains full CoT history for comparison and evaluation.
Problem It Solves: No standard mechanism exists to measure whether compressed reasoning actually retains necessary information, or to provide a training target for learning optimal compression.
How It Works:

Unfold Mode: Standard greedy or sampling-based decoding of full CoT without intermediate token pruning. Serves as the reference for accuracy measurement and RL reward signal.
Fold Mode: Modified decoding that executes summarization steps at predefined intervals, where the summary replaces all prior reasoning steps in the KV cache. Enables 3x throughput improvement through reduced attention computation.
Comparative Training: Both modes are executed on the same input during RL training. The difference in final accuracy (accuracy_Fold - accuracy_Unfold) is used as the reward signal, directly incentivizing the model to learn compression without information loss.

Where It’s Useful:

Training frameworks where you need both efficient inference and ground-truth accuracy baselines
Benchmarking compression effectiveness without requiring separate validation models
Scenarios where interpretability requires showing alternative reasoning paths (Unfold as explainability reference)

Limitations / Caveats:

Running both modes during training doubles computational cost; training efficiency gains appear only post-training
Unfold mode may hit memory limits on larger models, limiting scalability of the training procedure itself
The assumption that accuracy_gap → 0 may not hold if the task requires information that cannot be compressed

literature/2026-02-04-Anytime-Pretrainin.title: Anytime Pretraining: Horizon-Free Learning-Rate Schedules with Weight Averaging
literature/2026-02-04-Anytime-Pretrainin.content:
Anytime Pretraining: Horizon-Free Learning-Rate Schedules with Weight Averaging
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #2 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, RAG, LLM
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03702v1
Abstract
Large language models are increasingly trained in continual or open-ended settings, where the total training horizon is not known in advance. Despite this, most existing pretraining recipes are not anytime: they rely on horizon-dependent learning rate schedules and extensive tuning under a fixed compute budget. In this work, we provide a theoretical analysis demonstrating the existence of anytime learning schedules for overparameterized linear regression, and we highlight the central role of weight averaging - also known as model merging - in achieving the minimax convergence rates of stochastic gradient descent. We show that these anytime schedules polynomially decay with time, with the decay rate determined by the source and capacity conditions of the problem. Empirically, we evaluate 150M and 300M parameter language models trained at 1-32x Chinchilla scale, comparing constant learning rates with weight averaging and 1/\sqrt{t} schedules with weight averaging against a well-tuned cosine schedule. Across the full training range, the anytime schedules achieve comparable final loss to cosine decay. Taken together, our results suggest that weight averaging combined with simple, horizon-free step sizes offers a practical and effective anytime alternative to cosine learning rate schedules for large language model pretraining.
Key Techniques

Anytime learning schedules
weight averaging (model merging)
polynomial decay learning rates

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityInverse Depth Scaling From Most Layers Being Similar0.19Differentiable Semantic ID for Generative Recommendation0.16Self-Distillation Enables Continual Learning0.11Semi-Supervised Learning on Graphs using Graph Neural Networks0.11ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.09
Weight Averaging for Anytime Learning
Core Idea: Periodically merge model snapshots during training—averaging weights across checkpoints—to smooth the optimization landscape and recover minimax convergence rates without horizon-dependent schedules.
Problem It Solves: Polynomially-decaying learning rates alone do not achieve optimal convergence; weight averaging stabilizes the trajectory and enables anytime schedules to match the performance of well-tuned cosine schedules.
How It Works:

At regular intervals during training, save model checkpoints at timesteps (t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k)
Compute the averaged model: (\bar{w} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_{t_i})
Continue training from the averaged weights (or use the averaged model at evaluation)
The averaging acts as an implicit regularization, centering the model in a well-conditioned region of the loss landscape
This combination with polynomial decay achieves convergence rates matching horizon-aware baselines

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Training Step t"] --> B["Save Checkpoint"]
    B --> C{"Averaging Interval?"}
    C -->|No| A
    C -->|Yes| D["Average Weights"]
    D --> E["Merged Model"]
    E --> F["Continue Training"]
    F --> A

Where It’s Useful:

LLM pretraining with anytime schedules
Federated learning (averaging client models)
Continual learning with periodic consolidation
Model ensembling and robustness improvement

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires storing multiple checkpoints, increasing memory overhead
Averaging frequency is a hyperparameter that must be tuned
May slow convergence in early training phases if checkpoints are poor quality
Theoretical guarantees are strongest for convex/overparameterized settings

Internal Use Cases:

LLM pretraining at variable scales: Can enable training recipes that don’t require fixing Chinchilla-optimal compute budgets upfront, allowing flexible scaling
Continual learning for ranking models: Weight averaging could stabilize embedding updates in online learning systems where training horizon is unknown


Horizon-Free Convergence Analysis for Overparameterized Linear Regression
Core Idea: Provide theoretical guarantees showing that polynomially-decaying learning rates combined with weight averaging achieve minimax convergence rates for overparameterized linear regression without dependence on the training horizon T.
Problem It Solves: Previous convergence analyses of SGD assume knowledge of T to set learning rates; this work decouples the convergence rate from T, enabling truly anytime algorithms.
How It Works:

Model: overparameterized linear regression (\min_w \mathbb{E}[\ell(w; z)]) where (w \in \mathbb{R}^d), (d > n) (samples)
Apply SGD with polynomial decay (\eta_t = \eta_0 \cdot t^{-\alpha})
Prove that under source condition (|w^|_{\mathcal{H}K} \leq R) and capacity condition (\lambda{\min}(H) \geq \lambda), the expected excess risk satisfies: (\mathbb{E}[L(w_t) - L(w^)] = O(t^{-\beta})) where (\beta) depends only on problem structure, not T
Show that weight averaging preserves (or improves) these rates
Constants in convergence rate scale polynomially with problem-dependent quantities, not exponentially with dimension

Where It’s Useful:

Theoretical foundations for anytime learning in deep learning
Analysis of continual learning algorithms
Understanding convergence under unknown horizons

Limitations / Caveats:

Restricted to linear models; extension to deep networks is empirical only
Requires explicit source and capacity conditions that may not hold in practice
Gap between theory (linear regression) and practice (300M-parameter Transformers)
Convergence rates may be loose compared to empirical optimization behavior

literature/2026-02-04-Beyond-Tokens-Sema.title: Beyond Tokens: Semantic-Aware Speculative Decoding for Efficient Inference by Probing Internal States
literature/2026-02-04-Beyond-Tokens-Sema.content:
Beyond Tokens: Semantic-Aware Speculative Decoding for Efficient Inference by Probing Internal States
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #8 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03708v1
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across many tasks but suffer from high inference latency due to autoregressive decoding. The issue is exacerbated in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs), which generate lengthy chains of thought. While speculative decoding accelerates inference by drafting and verifying multiple tokens in parallel, existing methods operate at the token level and ignore semantic equivalence (i.e., different token sequences expressing the same meaning), leading to inefficient rejections. We propose SemanticSpec, a semantic-aware speculative decoding framework that verifies entire semantic sequences instead of tokens. SemanticSpec introduces a semantic probability estimation mechanism that probes the model’s internal hidden states to assess the likelihood of generating sequences with specific meanings.Experiments on four benchmarks show that SemanticSpec achieves up to 2.7x speedup on DeepSeekR1-32B and 2.1x on QwQ-32B, consistently outperforming token-level and sequence-level baselines in both efficiency and effectiveness.
Key Techniques

Semantic-aware speculative decoding
Semantic probability estimation from hidden states
Parallel drafting and verification of semantic sequences

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBeyond Tokens: Semantic-Aware Speculative Decoding for Efficient Inference by Probing Internal States0.81DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.38DART: Diffusion-Inspired Speculative Decoding for Fast LLM Inference0.37PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models0.35SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration0.34
SemanticSpec: Semantic-Aware Speculative Decoding
Core Idea: Estimate semantic equivalence probability by probing LLM hidden states, enabling speculative decoding to accept semantically equivalent tokens even when they don’t match exactly.
Problem It Solves: Traditional speculative decoding rejects draft tokens that don’t exactly match the target model’s output, even when the draft expresses the same semantic meaning—wasting computation on valid but lexically different completions (e.g., “don’t” vs “do not”).
How It Works:

Hidden State Probing: Train a lightweight probe on intermediate hidden states to estimate P(semantic_match | hidden_state) without running full forward pass
Semantic Probability Estimation: At each draft position, compute probability that draft token sequence is semantically equivalent to what target would generate
Modified Acceptance Criterion: Accept draft tokens if semantic equivalence probability exceeds threshold, not just exact token match
Parallel Verification: Batch verify multiple draft tokens simultaneously using semantic probability estimates

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    Draft["Draft Model"] --> Tokens["Draft Tokens"]
    Tokens --> Probe["Hidden State<br/>Probe"]
    Probe --> SemProb["Semantic<br/>Probability"]
    SemProb --> Accept{"Accept?"}
    Accept -->|Yes| Output["Output"]
    Accept -->|No| Target["Target Model"]

Where It’s Useful:

Speculative decoding systems where semantic equivalence matters more than exact token matching
LLM serving with high throughput requirements
Applications tolerant of paraphrase-level variation in outputs

Limitations / Caveats:

Probe training requires semantic equivalence labels (potentially expensive to collect)
May introduce subtle semantic drift over long sequences
Threshold tuning needed to balance speedup vs semantic fidelity

Internal Use Cases:

Semantic ID systems where multiple token sequences can represent the same semantic content
Ranking explanation generation where paraphrase-level equivalence is acceptable
Efficient verification in retrieval-augmented generation where semantic match matters more than lexical match

literature/2026-02-04-CL-bench-A-Benchma.title: CL-bench: A Benchmark for Context Learning
literature/2026-02-04-CL-bench-A-Benchma.content:
CL-bench: A Benchmark for Context Learning
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, LLM
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03587v1
Abstract
Current language models (LMs) excel at reasoning over prompts using pre-trained knowledge. However, real-world tasks are far more complex and context-dependent: models must learn from task-specific context and leverage new knowledge beyond what is learned during pre-training to reason and resolve tasks. We term this capability context learning, a crucial ability that humans naturally possess but has been largely overlooked. To this end, we introduce CL-bench, a real-world benchmark consisting of 500 complex contexts, 1,899 tasks, and 31,607 verification rubrics, all crafted by experienced domain experts. Each task is designed such that the new content required to resolve it is contained within the corresponding context. Resolving tasks in CL-bench requires models to learn from the context, ranging from new domain-specific knowledge, rule systems, and complex procedures to laws derived from empirical data, all of which are absent from pre-training. This goes far beyond long-context tasks that primarily test retrieval or reading comprehension, and in-context learning tasks, where models learn simple task patterns via instructions and demonstrations. Our evaluations of ten frontier LMs find that models solve only 17.2% of tasks on average. Even the best-performing model, GPT-5.1, solves only 23.7%, revealing that LMs have yet to achieve effective context learning, which poses a critical bottleneck for tackling real-world, complex context-dependent tasks. CL-bench represents a step towards building LMs with this fundamental capability, making them more intelligent and advancing their deployment in real-world scenarios.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques/methods:

Context learning from task-specific domains

In-context learning with instructions and demonstrations
Long-context retrieval and reading comprehension

Related Litnotes

literature/2026-02-04-CSR-Bench-A-Benchm.title: CSR-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating the Cross-modal Safety and Reliability of MLLMs
literature/2026-02-04-CSR-Bench-A-Benchm.content:
CSR-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating the Cross-modal Safety and Reliability of MLLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #4 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Embeddings, RAG, LLM
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03263v1
Abstract
Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) enable interaction over both text and images, but their safety behavior can be driven by unimodal shortcuts instead of true joint intent understanding. We introduce CSR-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating cross-modal reliability through four stress-testing interaction patterns spanning Safety, Over-rejection, Bias, and Hallucination, covering 61 fine-grained types. Each instance is constructed to require integrated image-text interpretation, and we additionally provide paired text-only controls to diagnose modality-induced behavior shifts. We evaluate 16 state-of-the-art MLLMs and observe systematic cross-modal alignment gaps. Models show weak safety awareness, strong language dominance under interference, and consistent performance degradation from text-only controls to multimodal inputs. We also observe a clear trade-off between reducing over-rejection and maintaining safe, non-discriminatory behavior, suggesting that some apparent safety gains may come from refusal-oriented heuristics rather than robust intent understanding. WARNING: This paper contains unsafe contents.
Key Techniques

Cross-modal stress-testing
Paired text-only controls
Multimodal alignment evaluation
Safety-bias trade-off analysis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityThe Side Effects of Being Smart: Safety Risks in MLLMs’ Multi-Image Reasoning0.29GOSV: Global Optimization for Safety Vector Extraction in LLMs0.24Risk Awareness Injection: Calibrating Vision-Language Models for Safety without Compromising Utility0.22Embed-RL: Reinforcement Learning for Reasoning-Driven Multimodal Embeddings0.16ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.14

literature/2026-02-04-Can-Custom-Models-.title: Can Custom Models Learn In-Context? An Exploration of Hybrid Architecture Performance on In-Context Learning Tasks
literature/2026-02-04-Can-Custom-Models-.content:
Can Custom Models Learn In-Context? An Exploration of Hybrid Architecture Performance on In-Context Learning Tasks
Source: OpenReview:Submitted to NeurIPS 2024
Published:
Rank: #3 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search, Embeddings
Code: github.com/anonymousforneurips64/neurips2024-submission21757}
Abstract
In-Context Learning (ICL) is a phenomenon where task learning occurs through a prompt sequence without the necessity of parameter updates. ICL in Multi-Headed Attention (MHA) with absolute positional embedding has been the focus of more study than other sequence model varieties. We examine implications of architectural differences between GPT-2 and LLaMa as well as Llama and Mamba. We extend work done by Garg et al. (2022) and Park et al. (2024) to GPT-2/LLaMa hybrid and LLaMa/Mamba hybrid models — examining the interplay between sequence transformation blocks and regressive performance in-context. We note that certain architectural changes cause degraded training efficiency/ICL accuracy by converging to suboptimal predictors or converging slower. We also find certain hybrids showing optimistic performance improvements, informing potential future ICL-focused architecture modifications. Additionally, we propose the “ICL regression score”, a scalar metric describing a model’s whole performance on a specific task. Compute limitations impose restrictions on our architecture-space, training duration, number of training runs, function class complexity, and benchmark complexity. To foster reproducible and extensible research, we provide a typed, modular, and extensible Python package on which we run all experiments. This code is available at \url{github.com/anonymousforneurips64/neurips2024-submission21757}.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques or methods:

In-Context Learning (ICL)

Multi-Headed Attention (MHA) with absolute positional embedding
ICL regression score
Hybrid architecture analysis (GPT-2/LLaMa and LLaMa/Mamba)

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityIn-Context Reinforcement Learning From Suboptimal Historical Data0.212Mamba2Furious: Linear in Complexity, Competitive in Accuracy0.21CL-bench: A Benchmark for Context Learning0.14Self-Distillation Enables Continual Learning0.13VeSX: A Framework Featured by Verification, Self-Correction and In-context Learning for Web Automation Tasks0.13
Hybrid Architecture In-Context Learning Analysis
Core Idea: A systematic empirical framework for evaluating how architectural mixing (GPT-2/LLaMA and LLaMA/Mamba block combinations) affects in-context learning performance on regression tasks, introducing the “ICL regression score” as a unified metric.
Problem It Solves: Lack of understanding about which architectural components (MHA vs. other sequence transformers like Mamba) are critical for in-context learning capability, and how hybrid architectures compare to homogeneous designs.
How It Works:

Create hybrid models by interleaving different transformer block types (e.g., alternating LLaMA attention blocks with Mamba state-space blocks)
Train on ICL regression tasks from Garg et al. (2022) without parameter updates during inference
Measure convergence speed, final accuracy, and compute the ICL regression score (scalar aggregating performance across prompt lengths/task variants)
Compare hybrid performance against baseline homogeneous architectures to identify architectural dependencies

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input Sequence] --> B[Block 1: Arch A]
    B --> C[Block 2: Arch B]
    C --> D[Block 3: Arch A]
    D --> E[...]
    E --> F[Output Predictions]
    
    G[ICL Task Examples] --> A
    F --> H[ICL Regression Score]

Where It’s Useful:

Designing efficient ICL-optimized architectures for resource-constrained deployments
Understanding minimal architectural requirements for few-shot learning capabilities
Identifying which components can be replaced with cheaper alternatives (e.g., Mamba) without degrading ICL

Limitations / Caveats:

Compute constraints limit architecture search space, training duration, and task complexity
Only evaluates regression tasks, may not generalize to classification or generation ICL scenarios
Suboptimal convergence in some hybrids suggests architectural compatibility issues not fully characterized

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys few-shot personalization: test if cheaper Mamba blocks can handle user-specific in-context adaptation while preserving ranking quality
Efficient prompt engineering: identify minimal attention layers needed for task-specific ICL in production LLMs
Hybrid inference optimization: deploy models with critical attention layers for ICL tasks and cheaper SSM layers for throughput-heavy operations

literature/2026-02-04-CoDa-Constrained-G.title: CoDa: Constrained Generation based Data Augmentation for Low-Resource NLP
literature/2026-02-04-CoDa-Constrained-G.content:
CoDa: Constrained Generation based Data Augmentation for Low-Resource NLP
Source: S2:NAACL-HLT
Published: 2024
Rank: #7 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG
Code: github.com/Sreyan88/CoDa
Abstract
We present CoDa (Constrained Generation based Data Augmentation), a controllable, effective, and training-free data augmentation technique for low-resource (data-scarce) NLP. Our approach is based on prompting off-the-shelf instruction-following Large Language Models (LLMs) for generating text that satisfies a set of constraints. Precisely, we extract a set of simple constraints from every instance in the low-resource dataset and verbalize them to prompt an LLM to generate novel and diverse training instances. Our findings reveal that synthetic data that follows simple constraints in the downstream dataset act as highly effective augmentations, and CoDa can achieve this without intricate decoding-time constrained generation techniques or fine-tuning with complex algorithms that eventually make the model biased toward the small number of training instances. Additionally, CoDa is the first framework that provides users explicit control over the augmentation generation process, thereby also allowing easy adaptation to several domains. We demonstrate the effectiveness of CoDa across 11 datasets spanning 3 tasks and 3 low-resource settings. CoDa outperforms all our baselines, qualitatively and quantitatively, with improvements of 0.12%-7.19%. Code is available here: github.com/Sreyan88/CoDa
Key Techniques

Constraint extraction and verbalization
Instruction-following LLM prompting
Synthetic data generation with constraint satisfaction

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHybrid-Gym: Training Coding Agents to Generalize Across Tasks0.18Towards Personalized Bangla Book Recommendation: A Large-Scale Multi-Entity Book Graph Dataset0.17Corpus-Based Approaches to Igbo Diacritic Restoration0.17ScrapeGraphAI-100k: A Large-Scale Dataset for LLM-Based Web Information Extraction0.15Using tournaments to calculate AUROC for zero-shot classification with LLMs0.15
CoDa (Constrained Generation based Data Augmentation)
Core Idea: Generate synthetic training data for low-resource NLP tasks by extracting simple constraints from existing instances and prompting instruction-following LLMs to create new examples that satisfy those constraints.
Problem It Solves: Low-resource NLP tasks lack sufficient training data, and existing augmentation methods either require expensive fine-tuning, produce biased outputs, or fail to provide explicit control over the generation process.
How It Works:

Constraint Extraction: Extract simple, verbalizable constraints from each instance in the low-resource dataset (e.g., entity types, sentence structure, label-relevant patterns)
Constraint Verbalization: Convert extracted constraints into natural language prompts that instruct an off-the-shelf LLM
Constrained Generation: Prompt the LLM to generate novel training instances that satisfy the verbalized constraints without requiring fine-tuning or complex decoding algorithms
Augmentation: Use generated synthetic instances alongside original data for training downstream models

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Low-Resource Dataset] --> B[Constraint Extractor]
    B --> C[Constraint Verbalizer]
    C --> D[Off-the-shelf LLM]
    D --> E[Synthetic Training Instances]
    A --> F[Augmented Dataset]
    E --> F

Where It’s Useful:

Low-resource classification tasks with limited labeled data
Domain adaptation where target domain has few examples
Few-shot learning scenarios across NLP tasks (text classification, NER, relation extraction)
Cold-start problems in specialized domains (medical, legal, technical)

Limitations / Caveats:

Quality depends on the instruction-following capability of the base LLM
Constraint extraction quality directly impacts augmentation effectiveness
May not capture complex implicit patterns in the original data
Performance gains vary by task (0.12%-7.19% improvement range suggests task dependency)

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys cold-start: Generate synthetic user-item interaction descriptions when launching new content categories with sparse engagement data
Query understanding: Augment training data for rare query intents or new product categories
Content moderation: Bootstrap classifiers for emerging policy violations with few labeled examples
Ranking signal generation: Create synthetic training queries for new ranking features in low-traffic segments

literature/2026-02-04-Context-Compressio.title: Context Compression via Explicit Information Transmission
literature/2026-02-04-Context-Compressio.content:
Context Compression via Explicit Information Transmission
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #11 (score: 0.90, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03784v1
Abstract
Long-context inference with Large Language Models (LLMs) is costly due to quadratic attention and growing key-value caches, motivating context compression. In this work, we study soft context compression, where a long context is condensed into a small set of continuous representations. Existing methods typically re-purpose the LLM itself as a trainable compressor, relying on layer-by-layer self-attention to iteratively aggregate information. We argue that this paradigm suffers from two structural limitations: (i) progressive representation overwriting across layers (ii) uncoordinated allocation of compression capacity across tokens. We propose ComprExIT (Context Compression via Explicit Information Transmission), a lightweight framework that formulates soft compression into a new paradigm: explicit information transmission over frozen LLM hidden states. This decouples compression from the model’s internal self-attention dynamics. ComprExIT performs (i) depth-wise transmission to selectively transmit multi-layer information into token anchors, mitigating progressive overwriting, and (ii) width-wise transmission to aggregate anchors into a small number of slots via a globally optimized transmission plan, ensuring coordinated allocation of information. Across six question-answering benchmarks, ComprExIT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art context compression methods while introducing only ~1% additional parameters, demonstrating that explicit and coordinated information transmission enables more effective and robust long-context compression.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques:

Soft context compression

Depth-wise transmission
Width-wise transmission
Explicit information transmission

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityATACompressor: Adaptive Task-Aware Compression for Efficient Long-Context Processing in LLMs0.23Data Distribution Matters: A Data-Centric Perspective on Context Compression for Large Language Model0.19Less Is More — Until It Breaks: Security Pitfalls of Vision Token Compression in Large Vision-Language Models0.19SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.18SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.18
Width-Wise Transmission with Globally Optimized Allocation
Core Idea: Aggregate token anchors into a small set of slots via a globally optimized transmission plan that coordinates compression capacity allocation across all tokens, rather than independently treating each token.
Problem It Solves: Naive soft compression allocates compression capacity independently per token, leading to imbalanced information distribution where some tokens consume excessive capacity while others are underutilized. This uncoordinated allocation wastes the limited compression budget.
How It Works:

Initialize a set of learnable compression slots (e.g., 4-8 slots) that will hold the final compressed context
Compute transmission costs or affinities between each token anchor and each slot using learned parameters
Formulate a bipartite matching or assignment problem: assign each token anchor to slots to maximize total information transmission while respecting capacity constraints
Solve the assignment problem globally (e.g., via optimal transport, Hungarian algorithm, or learned routing) to produce a transmission plan
Execute the plan: aggregate anchors into slots according to the optimized assignments, combining information from multiple anchors per slot if beneficial
Return the small set of populated slots as the compressed context representation

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Token Anchors<br/>from Depth-Wise"] --> Aff["Affinity Matrix<br/>Anchor × Slot"]
    Aff --> Opt["Global Optimization<br/>Assignment Problem"]
    Opt --> Plan["Transmission Plan<br/>Anchor → Slot Mapping"]
    Plan --> Agg["Aggregate Anchors<br/>into Slots"]
    Agg --> Out["Final Compressed<br/>Representation"]

Where It’s Useful:

Compressing retrieval contexts where different documents need unequal compression (some redundant, some critical)
Budget-constrained inference where the number of final tokens is strictly limited
Multi-document question-answering where slot capacity must be optimally distributed across sources

Limitations / Caveats:

Computing global optimal assignment can be expensive (though approximations exist); must balance optimality with computational cost
Requires differentiable solvers or relaxations to integrate into end-to-end training pipelines
Fixed number of slots may not adapt well to varying input lengths or complexity levels without additional mechanisms

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys ranking where compressed context summaries must fit within a token budget, using globally coordinated allocation to prioritize high-value item features
Long-context RAG systems where compressed documents are allocated slots based on relevance, not uniformly across all retrieved passages

literature/2026-02-04-FASA-Frequency-awa.title: FASA: Frequency-aware Sparse Attention
literature/2026-02-04-FASA-Frequency-awa.content:
FASA: Frequency-aware Sparse Attention
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #10 (score: 0.91, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03152v1
Abstract
The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) faces a critical bottleneck when handling lengthy inputs: the prohibitive memory footprint of the Key Value (KV) cache. To address this bottleneck, the token pruning paradigm leverages attention sparsity to selectively retain a small, critical subset of tokens. However, existing approaches fall short, with static methods risking irreversible information loss and dynamic strategies employing heuristics that insufficiently capture the query-dependent nature of token importance. We propose FASA, a novel framework that achieves query-aware token eviction by dynamically predicting token importance. FASA stems from a novel insight into RoPE: the discovery of functional sparsity at the frequency-chunk (FC) level. Our key finding is that a small, identifiable subset of “dominant” FCs consistently exhibits high contextual agreement with the full attention head. This provides a robust and computationally free proxy for identifying salient tokens. %making them a powerful and efficient proxy for token importance. Building on this insight, FASA first identifies a critical set of tokens using dominant FCs, and then performs focused attention computation solely on this pruned subset. % Since accessing only a small fraction of the KV cache, FASA drastically lowers memory bandwidth requirements and computational cost. Across a spectrum of long-context tasks, from sequence modeling to complex CoT reasoning, FASA consistently outperforms all token-eviction baselines and achieves near-oracle accuracy, demonstrating remarkable robustness even under constraint budgets. Notably, on LongBench-V1, FASA reaches nearly 100% of full-KV performance when only keeping 256 tokens, and achieves 2.56\times speedup using just 18.9% of the cache on AIME24.
Key Techniques

here are the 2-4 key techniques/methods:

**Token pruning with attention sparsity

Query-aware token importance prediction
Functional sparsity at frequency-chunk level
Focused attention computation on pruned subset**

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.33FOCUS: DLLMs Know How to Tame Their Compute Bound0.31HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.31Hierarchical Adaptive Eviction for KV Cache Management in Multimodal Language Models0.30CLAA: Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation for Accelerating LLM Prefill0.25
Query-Dependent KV Cache Compression via Selective Token Retention
Core Idea: Dynamically prune the KV cache during inference by retaining only query-dependent critical tokens, reducing memory footprint and bandwidth while maintaining near-oracle accuracy.
Problem It Solves:

Full KV cache grows linearly with sequence length, creating memory bottleneck in long-context LLM inference
Static compression (e.g., fixed token positions) cannot adapt to query-specific relevance
Existing dynamic methods lack a principled, computationally free proxy for token importance

How It Works:

Importance Scoring (via dominant FCs, as described above): Assign importance weights to each token based on query-KV interactions in the dominant frequency chunk subspace.
Ranking & Selection: Rank tokens by importance score; select the top-K tokens by score, and always retain boundary tokens (first/last few) to preserve structural signals.
KV Cache Eviction: Remove tokens below the threshold from storage; only the retained token embeddings remain in memory.
Attention Computation: At each generation step, compute attention only over the retained token set, reducing both memory access and computation.

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context question answering and retrieval tasks
Multi-turn dialogue where context window expands
Few-shot in-context learning with large example sets
Streaming/online inference where early tokens accumulate

Limitations / Caveats:

Evicted tokens cannot be recovered; errors in importance prediction are irreversible
Query-dependent scoring adds per-query latency (though claimed to be “computationally free” via dominant FC shortcuts)
Requires re-tuning K (number of retained tokens) per task/model for optimal accuracy-efficiency trade-off
Boundary token retention heuristic may not be optimal for all domains (e.g., instruction-following tasks with question at the end)

Internal Use Cases:

Feed Ranking with Context: Compress user history before running final ranking model; importance determined by query (current context) rather than static recency
LLM-as-Judge: Reduce context size when evaluating candidate items by pruning non-salient historical information

literature/2026-02-04-Fast-Slow-Efficien.title: Fast-Slow Efficient Training for Multimodal Large Language Models via Visual Token Pruning
literature/2026-02-04-Fast-Slow-Efficien.content:
Fast-Slow Efficient Training for Multimodal Large Language Models via Visual Token Pruning
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #5 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03815v1
Abstract
Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) suffer from severe training inefficiency issue, which is associated with their massive model sizes and visual token numbers. Existing efforts in efficient training focus on reducing model sizes or trainable parameters. Inspired by the success of Visual Token Pruning (VTP) in improving inference efficiency, we are exploring another substantial research direction for efficient training by reducing visual tokens. However, applying VTP at the training stage results in a training-inference mismatch: pruning-trained models perform poorly when inferring on non-pruned full visual token sequences. To close this gap, we propose DualSpeed, a fast-slow framework for efficient training of MLLMs. The fast-mode is the primary mode, which incorporates existing VTP methods as plugins to reduce visual tokens, along with a mode isolator to isolate the model’s behaviors. The slow-mode is the auxiliary mode, where the model is trained on full visual sequences to retain training-inference consistency. To boost its training, it further leverages self-distillation to learn from the sufficiently trained fast-mode. Together, DualSpeed can achieve both training efficiency and non-degraded performance. Experiments show DualSpeed accelerates the training of LLaVA-1.5 by 2.1\times and LLaVA-NeXT by 4.0\times, retaining over 99% performance. Code: github.com/dingkun-zhang/DualSpeed
Key Techniques

Visual Token Pruning
Mode Isolator
Self-Distillation
Fast-Slow Training Framework

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDo MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.41ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.39SwiftVLM: Efficient Vision-Language Model Inference via Cross-Layer Token Bypass0.35Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs0.34VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.33
DualSpeed Fast-Slow Training Framework
Core Idea: Train MLLMs with two parallel modes—a fast mode using visual token pruning (VTP) for efficiency and a slow mode on full tokens for inference consistency—connected via self-distillation.
Problem It Solves: Applying visual token pruning during training creates a training-inference mismatch: models trained on pruned sequences perform poorly when inferring on full visual token sequences at deployment time.
How It Works:

Fast-Mode Training: Apply existing VTP methods as plugins to reduce visual tokens during forward pass, achieving 2-4x training speedup
Mode Isolator: Insert a learnable projection layer that decouples fast/slow pathway representations, preventing negative interference between modes
Slow-Mode Training: Train on full visual sequences in parallel (less frequently) to maintain training-inference consistency
Self-Distillation: The slow-mode learns from the sufficiently-trained fast-mode via knowledge distillation, accelerating its convergence without full compute cost

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    Input["Visual Tokens"] --> VTP["VTP Pruning"]
    VTP --> FastMode["Fast-Mode<br/>(Primary)"]
    Input --> SlowMode["Slow-Mode<br/>(Auxiliary)"]
    FastMode --> Isolator["Mode Isolator"]
    Isolator --> LLM["Shared LLM"]
    SlowMode --> LLM
    FastMode -.->|Self-Distill| SlowMode

Where It’s Useful:

Training efficiency for multimodal models with high visual token counts
Maintaining deployment flexibility (can serve pruned OR full sequences)
Adapting VTP research for training-time benefits without inference penalties

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful balancing of fast/slow mode training ratios
Mode isolator adds small parameter overhead
Benefits scale with visual token count; less impactful for text-only models

Internal Use Cases:

Training semantic token compressors where fast-mode prunes to essential features while slow-mode ensures generalization
Dual-pathway training for efficient embedding models that need to handle both compressed and full representations

literature/2026-02-04-ForesightKV-Optimi.title: ForesightKV: Optimizing KV Cache Eviction for Reasoning Models by Learning Long-Term Contribution
literature/2026-02-04-ForesightKV-Optimi.content:
ForesightKV: Optimizing KV Cache Eviction for Reasoning Models by Learning Long-Term Contribution
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #7 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03203v1
Abstract
Recently, large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable reasoning abilities by producing long reasoning traces. However, as the sequence length grows, the key-value (KV) cache expands linearly, incurring significant memory and computation costs. Existing KV cache eviction methods mitigate this issue by discarding less important KV pairs, but often fail to capture complex KV dependencies, resulting in performance degradation. To better balance efficiency and performance, we introduce ForesightKV, a training-based KV cache eviction framework that learns to predict which KV pairs to evict during long-text generations. We first design the Golden Eviction algorithm, which identifies the optimal eviction KV pairs at each step using future attention scores. These traces and the scores at each step are then distilled via supervised training with a Pairwise Ranking Loss. Furthermore, we formulate cache eviction as a Markov Decision Process and apply the GRPO algorithm to mitigate the significant language modeling loss increase on low-entropy tokens. Experiments on AIME2024 and AIME2025 benchmarks of three reasoning models demonstrate that ForesightKV consistently outperforms prior methods under only half the cache budget, while benefiting synergistically from both supervised and reinforcement learning approaches.
Key Techniques

KV cache eviction prediction
Golden Eviction algorithm
Pairwise Ranking Loss
Markov Decision Process with GRPO

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLearning to Evict from Key-Value Cache0.41Hierarchical Adaptive Eviction for KV Cache Management in Multimodal Language Models0.41Predicting Future Utility: Global Combinatorial Optimization for Task-Agnostic KV Cache Eviction0.32Fast KVzip: Efficient and Accurate LLM Inference with Gated KV Eviction0.28Fast KVzip: Efficient and Accurate LLM Inference with Gated KV Eviction0.26
Pairwise Ranking Loss for KV Cache Eviction
Core Idea: Trains a neural predictor to rank KV pairs by eviction priority using contrastive learning, where pairs marked for eviction are ranked lower than those to retain.
Problem It Solves: Multi-class classification (evict/keep) struggles with class imbalance in KV cache (only a few tokens should be evicted). Pairwise ranking naturally models the ordinal preference: “this pair is more evict-able than that pair.”
How It Works:

Feature extraction: Encode each KV pair with contextual features (e.g., position embeddings, attention statistics, layer-wise cumulative attention scores)
Ranking model: Pass features through a neural network to produce a scalar eviction score s_i for each KV pair i
Pairwise loss: For each training example, construct pairs where one KV is marked for eviction (positive) and one is marked for retention (negative):
\mathcal{L}_{\text{pair}} = \sum_{(i,j) \in \text{pairs}} \log(1 + \exp(s_i - s_j))
where i should be evicted and j should be retained
Training: Minimize pairwise ranking loss over all (evict, keep) pairs from Golden Eviction labels
Inference: Rank all cached KV pairs by score and evict bottom-k at each step

Where It’s Useful:

Learning to rank KV pairs under cache budget constraints
Any ordinal preference learning where absolute thresholds are unknown
Combining multiple weak signals (recency, attention pattern, position) into a unified eviction score

Limitations / Caveats:

Pairwise loss is quadratic in the number of KV pairs per sequence; can be expensive on very long contexts
Only captures relative preference, not calibrated eviction thresholds
Requires careful negative sampling for stable training on imbalanced datasets

Internal Use Cases:

Learning to rank for recommendations: train pairwise loss to rank items by relevance, similar to how KV pairs are ranked for eviction priority
Query ranking in sparse retrieval: learn which documents/KV-equivalents are most relevant without explicit thresholds


GRPO (Generalized Reinforcement Policy Optimization) for KV Cache Distillation
Core Idea: Applies group relative policy optimization (a reinforcement learning algorithm) to fine-tune eviction policies on language modeling loss, mitigating performance degradation on low-entropy (easy) tokens caused by supervised learning alone.
Problem It Solves: Supervised training on Golden Eviction labels optimizes for AIME reasoning benchmarks (high-entropy, complex reasoning steps) but causes performance collapse on low-entropy tokens (common words, templates). GRPO allows online adaptation to improve overall language modeling perplexity while respecting the cache budget.
How It Works:

Policy initialization: Start with the supervised-trained eviction ranker from Pairwise Ranking Loss
Rollout: Generate sequences using the current eviction policy, receiving reward signal r_t (e.g., log probability of generated token, or task-specific accuracy)
Advantage estimation: Compute group-relative advantages by comparing token-level performance against a baseline (e.g., no eviction or uniform baseline):
A_t = r_t - \mathbb{E}[r_t | \text{prompt}]
Policy gradient update: Apply policy gradient optimization to maximize expected reward while staying close to supervised policy:
\mathcal{L}_{\text{RL}} = -\mathbb{E}[\min(A_t \cdot \pi_t / \pi_0, \text{clip}(A_t, \epsilon)) \cdot \log \pi(\text{evict} | s_t)]
Repeat: Alternate between supervised loss (on AIME) and RL loss (on LM objectives) until convergence

Where It’s Useful:

Balancing multiple objectives in inference optimization (cache efficiency vs. generation quality)
Online adaptation during deployment when supervised labels don’t capture all failure modes
Fine-tuning learned policies to handle distribution shift in token difficulty

Limitations / Caveats:

RL training is sample-inefficient; requires many forward passes to converge
Reward signal design is critical; poorly chosen rewards can degrade performance
Risk of policy collapse to local optima or reward hacking if objective is misaligned
Computational overhead during training (multiple generations per batch)

Internal Use Cases:

Multi-objective optimization in ranking: use GRPO-like policy gradient to balance click-through rate (supervised from data) with long-term user engagement (RL signal)
Cache eviction for recommendation inference: supervised on relevance (pairwise loss) + RL on latency constraints

literature/2026-02-04-GoT-Effective-Grap.title: GoT: Effective Graph-of-Thought Reasoning in Language Models
literature/2026-02-04-GoT-Effective-Grap.content:
GoT: Effective Graph-of-Thought Reasoning in Language Models
Source: S2:NAACL-HLT
Published: 2024
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search
Code: https://github
Abstract
With the widespread use of language models (LMs) in NLP tasks, researchers have discovered the potential of Chain-of-thought (CoT) to assist LMs in accomplishing complex reasoning tasks by generating intermediate steps. However, human thought processes are often non-linear, rather than simply sequential chains of thoughts. Therefore, we propose Graph-of-Thought (GoT) reasoning, which models human thought processes not only as a chain but also as a graph. By representing thought units as nodes and connections between them as edges, our approach captures the non-sequential nature of human thinking and allows for a more realistic modeling of thought processes. GoT adopts a two-stage framework with an additional GoT encoder for thought graph representation and fuses the graph representation with the original input representation through a gated fusion mechanism. We evaluate GoT’s performance on a text-only reasoning task (AQUA-RAT) and a multimodal reasoning task (ScienceQA). Our model achieves significant improvement over the strong CoT baseline on the AQUA-RAT test set and boosts accuracy from 85.19% to 87.59% using the T5-base model over the state-of-the-art Multimodal-CoT (Zhang et al., 2023) on the ScienceQA test set. Our code is publicly available at https://github
Key Techniques

Graph-of-Thought reasoning
graph-based thought representation with nodes and edges
two-stage framework with GoT encoder
gated fusion mechanism

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityImgCoT: Compressing Long Chain of Thought into Compact Visual Tokens for Efficient Reasoning of Large Language Model0.32SwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMs0.24The Potential of CoT for Reasoning: A Closer Look at Trace Dynamics0.23Why Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models0.21Why Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models0.20
Graph-of-Thought (GoT) Reasoning
Core Idea: Represent reasoning processes as a graph structure rather than a linear chain, where thought units are nodes and their relationships are edges, capturing the non-sequential nature of complex reasoning.
Problem It Solves: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning assumes a linear, sequential thought process, but human reasoning often involves non-linear connections, parallel considerations, and revisiting earlier thoughts—which CoT cannot model.
How It Works:

Generate intermediate reasoning steps as thought units (similar to CoT)
Construct a graph representation where each thought unit is a node and semantic/logical connections between thoughts form edges
Encode the thought graph using a specialized GoT encoder to capture graph structure
Fuse the graph representation with the original input representation via a gated fusion mechanism
Use the combined representation for final answer prediction

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input + Question] --> B[Generate Thought Units]
    B --> C[Construct Thought Graph]
    C --> D[GoT Encoder]
    A --> E[Input Encoder]
    D --> F[Gated Fusion]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Answer Prediction]

Where It’s Useful:

Mathematical reasoning tasks (e.g., AQUA-RAT)
Multimodal reasoning (e.g., ScienceQA with text + images)
Complex problem-solving requiring multiple interconnected reasoning steps
Tasks where reasoning paths may loop back or branch

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires explicit graph construction mechanism (more complex than linear CoT)
May require additional training data with graph annotations or heuristics to infer edges
Two-stage framework adds computational overhead compared to standard CoT

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking explanation systems where multiple signals (user history, item features, contextual factors) interconnect non-linearly
RecSys reasoning chains that need to model “if X then consider Y, which affects Z” feedback loops
LLM-based decision systems where multiple candidate actions have interdependencies
Multi-hop query understanding in search/recommendation where relationships between query terms form a semantic graph

literature/2026-02-04-HySparse-A-Hybrid-.title: HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing
literature/2026-02-04-HySparse-A-Hybrid-.content:
HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #6 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03560v1
Abstract
This work introduces Hybrid Sparse Attention (HySparse), a new architecture that interleaves each full attention layer with several sparse attention layers. While conceptually simple, HySparse strategically derives each sparse layer’s token selection and KV caches directly from the preceding full attention layer. This architecture resolves two fundamental limitations of prior sparse attention methods. First, conventional approaches typically rely on additional proxies to predict token importance, introducing extra complexity and potentially suboptimal performance. In contrast, HySparse uses the full attention layer as a precise oracle to identify important tokens. Second, existing sparse attention designs often reduce computation without saving KV cache. HySparse enables sparse attention layers to reuse the full attention KV cache, thereby reducing both computation and memory. We evaluate HySparse on both 7B dense and 80B MoE models. Across all settings, HySparse consistently outperforms both full attention and hybrid SWA baselines. Notably, in the 80B MoE model with 49 total layers, only 5 layers employ full attention, yet HySparse achieves substantial performance gains while reducing KV cache storage by nearly 10x.
Key Techniques

Hybrid Sparse Attention (HySparse) interleaving
Full attention as oracle for token selection
KV cache reuse across sparse layers

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.89Superlinear Multi-Step Attention0.41SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.40SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.39CLAA: Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation for Accelerating LLM Prefill0.38
Oracle Token Selection via Attention Magnitude (Heavy Hitter Oracle)
Core Idea: Select important tokens for sparse attention by directly using cumulative attention weights from the full attention layer as an oracle signal, identifying “heavy hitter” tokens that contribute most to downstream attention computations.
Problem It Solves:

Sparse attention methods typically rely on separate proxy models (e.g., learned importance scorers, heuristics like recent tokens or position encoding) that add overhead and may diverge from true token importance
No ground truth signal exists until actual attention is computed; HySparse leverages the full attention layer as a precise oracle

How It Works:

Compute Full Attention Scores: For all queries in the full attention layer, compute attention weight matrix A ∈ ℝ^(N×N) where A_ij = softmax(Q_i · K_j^T / √d), representing each query’s distribution over all key/value tokens
Aggregate Token Importance: For each token position j, compute its cumulative importance score: importance_j = Σ_i A_ij (sum of attention weights across all query positions)
Top-k Selection: Sort tokens by importance_j in descending order and select the top-k tokens, where k is the sparse attention budget (e.g., 25% or 50% of original sequence length)
Reuse for Sparse Layers: Feed the selected token indices and their corresponding K, V embeddings from the full attention KV cache to subsequent sparse attention layers without recomputation

Where It’s Useful:

Any sparse attention scheme where oracle supervision is available from preceding dense computation
Progressive refinement pipelines where full-precision computation precedes efficient approximations
Budget-constrained inference where the top-k most relevant tokens must be identified reliably

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires executing at least one full attention layer, which dominates computational cost; the sparsity benefit only applies to subsequent layers
Attention magnitude in one layer may not perfectly predict relevance for future layers; as the model processes information through layers, token importance can shift
Heavy hitter selection is greedy and one-shot; it does not adapt dynamically based on query-specific importance, only aggregate importance across all queries
In very short sequences (< 128 tokens), the overhead of oracle selection and sparse computation may exceed the savings

Internal Use Cases:

Efficient KV Cache Management: Extends Heavy Hitter Oracle (H2O) by grounding eviction decisions in actual full attention rather than running averages or heuristics
Multi-Stage Ranking: In recommendation ranking, a full dense scorer (full attention) can identify top candidates, then sparse refinement applies learned rerankers efficiently
Knowledge-Grounded Retrieval: For RAG systems, full attention selects relevant document passages, then sparse attention refines reasoning, reducing memory overhead of full context

literature/2026-02-04-Instruction-Anchor.title: Instruction Anchors: Dissecting the Causal Dynamics of Modality Arbitration
literature/2026-02-04-Instruction-Anchor.content:
Instruction Anchors: Dissecting the Causal Dynamics of Modality Arbitration
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #3 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03677v1
Abstract
Modality following serves as the capacity of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to selectively utilize multimodal contexts based on user instructions. It is fundamental to ensuring safety and reliability in real-world deployments. However, the underlying mechanisms governing this decision-making process remain poorly understood. In this paper, we investigate its working mechanism through an information flow lens. Our findings reveal that instruction tokens function as structural anchors for modality arbitration: Shallow attention layers perform non-selective information transfer, routing multimodal cues to these anchors as a latent buffer; Modality competition is resolved within deep attention layers guided by the instruction intent, while MLP layers exhibit semantic inertia, acting as an adversarial force. Furthermore, we identify a sparse set of specialized attention heads that drive this arbitration. Causal interventions demonstrate that manipulating a mere 5\% of these critical heads can decrease the modality-following ratio by 60\% through blocking, or increase it by 60\% through targeted amplification of failed samples. Our work provides a substantial step toward model transparency and offers a principled framework for the orchestration of multimodal information in MLLMs.
Key Techniques

Instruction token anchoring
modality competition resolution in deep layers
sparse specialized attention head manipulation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMultimodal Generative Retrieval Model with Staged Pretraining for Food Delivery on Meituan0.28Closing the Modality Gap Aligns Group-Wise Semantics0.23HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.20HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.18GOSV: Global Optimization for Safety Vector Extraction in LLMs0.18
Modality Competition Resolution (MCR)
Core Idea: A layered mechanism where deep attention layers resolve conflicts between competing modalities by applying instruction-intent-guided attention weights and gating.
Problem It Solves: When multiple modalities contain contradictory or conflicting information, MLLMs must choose which to prioritize—this decision must respect user instructions rather than arbitrary modality dominance.
How It Works:

Multimodal information is buffered at instruction anchor positions in shallow layers
Deep attention layers examine instruction tokens alongside multimodal cues to extract instruction intent (semantic understanding of what the user asked for)
Instruction intent is used to compute modality-specific attention weights: w_m = \text{softmax}(f(\text{instruction\_intent}, m)) where m is each modality
MLP layers apply these weights but exhibit “semantic inertia”—a bias toward preserving input semantic patterns that can work against explicit instructions

Where It’s Useful:

Vision-language model design with instruction-following guarantees
Multi-sensory embodied AI systems requiring instruction-based mode selection
Safety-critical applications where incorrect modality reliance causes failures

Limitations / Caveats:

MLP layers act as an adversarial force, reducing modality-following fidelity; addressing this requires either MLP redesign or stronger instruction signals
The mechanism may struggle with ambiguous or contradictory instructions
Effectiveness depends on instruction clarity in the input

Internal Use Cases:

Complements Collaborative Instruction Tuning by grounding instruction following in mechanistic layer-wise understanding
Could inform ranking systems where instructions conflict with collaborative signals


Causal Intervention on Critical Attention Heads (CIAH)
Core Idea: Identification and targeted manipulation of a sparse set of critical attention heads (≈5% of total) to dramatically shift modality-following behavior through blocking or amplification.
Problem It Solves: Understanding which model components actually drive modality arbitration decisions; enables precise, surgical interventions without full model retraining.
How It Works:

Identify all attention heads across all layers: H = \{h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_N\}
Define causal intervention I: either blocking (zeroing attention outputs) or amplification (scaling gradients on failed samples)
Apply intervention to subsets of heads and measure impact on modality-following ratio (MFR): \Delta \text{MFR} = \text{MFR}_{\text{intervened}} - \text{MFR}_{\text{baseline}}
Identify critical heads as those where |\Delta \text{MFR}| exceeds threshold when intervened (paper shows ±60% shifts possible)
Blocking: Set \text{head}_i(\text{query}, \text{key}) = 0 to disable modality routing
Amplification: Increase gradient signal to critical heads during training on samples where modality-following failed

Where It’s Useful:

Model steering and behavioral control without retraining entire models
Safety intervention in deployed MLLMs
Understanding attention mechanisms in other transformer-based multimodal systems
Interpretability research on which components drive specific behaviors

Limitations / Caveats:
