Interventions are task/dataset-specific; critical heads may differ across modality combinations
Blocking all critical heads may cause catastrophic failure in other capabilities (side effects not discussed in abstract)
Requires clear definition and measurement of modality-following ratio for the target domain
Sparse set size (5%) suggests high variance—not all heads contribute equally

Internal Use Cases:

Could apply to ranking systems where specific “arbitration heads” choose between collaborative signals and content features
Relates to Dynamic Context Assembly by potentially controlling which information components get routed through attention pathways


Semantic Inertia in MLP Layers (SI)
Core Idea: MLP layers exhibit a resistance to changing semantic patterns from their inputs, acting as an adversarial force that opposes instruction-driven modality arbitration decisions.
Problem It Solves: MLPs preserve input semantic structure through their projections, which can override or contradict explicit instruction intent, reducing overall modality-following fidelity.
How It Works:

Instruction-guided attention layers compute target modality weights and route information accordingly
MLP layer processes these routed representations through: \text{MLP}(x) = \sigma(W_1 x + b_1) W_2 + b_2
The learned weights W_1, W_2 encode statistical patterns from training data that favor certain modality combinations
This causes semantic “stickiness”—the MLP output preserves the original modality dominance patterns despite attention-layer routing decisions
Result: instruction-following is weakened because MLPs revert to learned priors

Where It’s Useful:

MLP redesign for instruction-aligned systems
Understanding why adapter-based instruction-tuning sometimes fails
Improving modality arbitration robustness in multimodal fine-tuning

Limitations / Caveats:

The exact mechanism of semantic inertia (dropout in training? weight initialization? learned priors?) is not fully characterized
May not apply uniformly to all MLP layers or all types of semantic patterns
Fixing this may require architectural changes (e.g., modality-specific MLP branches, gating) which add complexity

Internal Use Cases:

Similar to issues in recommendation systems where collaborative filtering MLPs override content-based instructions
Could inform Collaborative Instruction Tuning by addressing how to make MLPs honor instruction signals rather than inertial collaborative patterns

literature/2026-02-04-LRAgent-Efficient-.title: LRAgent: Efficient KV Cache Sharing for Multi-LoRA LLM Agents
literature/2026-02-04-LRAgent-Efficient-.content:
LRAgent: Efficient KV Cache Sharing for Multi-LoRA LLM Agents
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-01
Rank: #1 (score: 0.54, max_sim: 0.80)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Role specialization in multi-LLM agent systems is often realized via multi-LoRA, where agents share a pretrained backbone and differ only through lightweight adapters. Despite sharing base model weights, each agent independently builds and stores its own KV cache for the same long, tool-augmented trajectories, incurring substantial memory and compute overhead. Existing KV cache sharing methods largely overlook this multi-LoRA setting. We observe that, across agents, cache differences are dominated by adapter outputs, while activations from the shared pretrained backbone remain highly similar. Based on this observation, we propose LRAgent, a KV cache sharing framework for multi-LoRA agents that decomposes the cache into a shared base component from the pretrained weights and an adapter-dependent component from LoRA weights. LRAgent reduces memory overhead by sharing the base component and storing the adapter component in its inherent low-rank form, and further reduces compute overhead, enabled by shared-A multi-LoRA architectures, by also sharing the low-rank cache and avoiding redundant computations for contexts already processed by other agents. To efficiently reconstruct adapter contributions at runtime, we introduce Flash-LoRA-Attention, a kernel that reorders attention computation to avoid materializing the low-rank cache to full dimension. LRAgent achieves throughput and time-to-first-token latency close to fully shared caching, while preserving accuracy near the non-shared caching baseline across agentic question-answering benchmarks.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques:

KV cache decomposition

LoRA weight sharing
Flash-LoRA-Attention

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityRedVisor: Reasoning-Aware Prompt Injection Defense via Zero-Copy KV Cache Reuse0.26Deep Kernel Fusion for Transformers0.23Learning to Evict from Key-Value Cache0.22HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.22PrefillShare: A Shared Prefill Module for KV Reuse in Multi-LLM Disaggregated Serving0.21
Flash-LoRA-Attention Kernel
Core Idea: A fused attention kernel that reorders attention computation to compute low-rank adapter contributions directly without materializing them to full dimension, reducing memory bandwidth and avoiding redundant full-rank cache reconstruction.
Problem It Solves: Naive reconstruction of K_full = K_base + (U @ V^T) and V_full = V_base + (U @ V^T) requires materializing low-rank products to full dimension (expensive in terms of memory bandwidth), creating a bottleneck that offsets gains from storing low-rank cache.
How It Works:


Standard Path (Baseline):

Materialize: K_full = K_base + U_k @ V_k^T (d × d operation)
Compute: Attention = softmax(Q @ K_full^T / √d) @ V_full
Cost: O(N·d²) for materialization + O(N·d²) for attention



Flash-LoRA Path (Proposed):

Reorder computation: Q @ K_full^T = (Q @ K_base^T) + (Q @ U_k) @ V_k
Compute base attention separately: Attention_base = softmax(Q @ K_base^T / √d) @ V_base
Compute low-rank correction: ΔAttention = softmax(…) @ (U_v @ V_v^T) [only materialize for fusion]
Fused operation: Combine via tiling to avoid intermediate materialization
Cost: O(N·d·r + N·d²) where r ≪ d (only low-rank dimension in hot path)



Kernel Implementation: Tile-based computation keeping K_base and (U_k, V_k) in register/shared memory; stream output directly without materializing full K


Where It’s Useful:

Inference serving where per-token latency matters (time-to-first-token latency critical in agents)
Multi-agent batching where shared base cache is accessed by many agents simultaneously
Memory-bandwidth-bound scenarios (typical for long-context / long trajectory inference)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires custom CUDA/GPU kernel implementation; not portable to all hardware
Benefits most pronounced when rank r is very small (r < 32); diminishing returns for larger ranks
Introduces kernel development overhead; marginal gains if attention computation is not the bottleneck

Internal Use Cases:

LLM-dCache: similar philosophy of materializing only necessary components; Flash-LoRA extends to low-rank structure
Optimization for long-trajectory Agent systems where repeated contexts are processed by multiple specialized agents


Shared-A Multi-LoRA Architecture
Core Idea: Implement LoRA adapters with a shared low-rank projection matrix A across all agents, differing only in the output projection matrix B per agent, enabling reuse of A @ X computations across the entire agent ensemble.
Problem It Solves: Even with low-rank cache storage, computing adapter contributions for each agent independently requires redundant matrix-vector products (computing A @ X multiple times for identical input X), doubling compute cost proportional to the number of agents.
How It Works:


Standard LoRA: Each agent i has independent (A_i, B_i) where A_i ∈ ℝ^(r×d), B_i ∈ ℝ^(d×r)

Adapter output: LoRA_i(X) = X + α·B_i @ A_i @ X
For n agents on same input X: compute A_i @ X independently n times



Shared-A LoRA:

All agents share identical A ∈ ℝ^(r×d) (same low-rank input projection)
Each agent has unique B_i ∈ ℝ^(d×r) (different output projection)
Adapter output: LoRA_i(X) = X + α·B_i @ (A @ X)
For n agents on same input X: compute A @ X once, reuse for all; compute B_i @ (A@X) n times



Compute Sharing:

Shared computation: A @ X (O(r·d) once)
Agent-specific computation: B_i @ (A@X) for each of n agents (O(d·r) × n)
Speedup: ~1.5-2× depending on n and ratio of A vs B cost



Integration with Shared KV Cache:

Low-rank cache stores (U, V) directly (not full K, V)
Shared-A structure ensures (A@X) is computed once for all agents’ cache contributions
Only B_i projection differs per agent



Where It’s Useful:

Multi-agent ensemble inference where many specialized agents process identical contexts
Tool-augmented agent systems where agents share observation processing but differ in action policies
Scaling agent count without proportional compute cost

Limitations / Caveats:

Constrains LoRA architecture design; not all adaptation patterns benefit from shared A
Effectiveness depends on degree of agent diversity; if agents require very different input projections, shared-A hurts expressiveness
Modest gains if the A @ X computation is not a primary bottleneck

Internal Use Cases:

Agentic RAG: Taxonomy of Architectures: enables efficient scaling of multi-agent systems with consistent input processing
Applicable to specialized agent roles (e.g., retriever + generator) where shared input embedding but different output transformation is desirable

literature/2026-02-04-Learning-to-Reason.title: Learning to Reason via Program Generation, Emulation, and Search
literature/2026-02-04-Learning-to-Reason.content:
Learning to Reason via Program Generation, Emulation, and Search
Source: OpenReview:NeurIPS 2024 poster
Published:
Rank: #2 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search
Abstract
Program synthesis with language models (LMs) has unlocked a large set of reasoning abilities; code-tuned LMs have proven adept at generating programs that solve a wide variety of algorithmic symbolic manipulation tasks (e.g. word concatenation). However, not all reasoning tasks are easily expressible as code, e.g. tasks involving commonsense reasoning, moral decision-making, and sarcasm understanding. Our goal is to extend a LM’s program synthesis skills to such tasks and evaluate the results via pseudo-programs, namely Python programs where some leaf function calls are left undefined. To that end, we propose, Code Generation and Emulated EXecution (COGEX). COGEX works by (1) training LMs to generate pseudo-programs and (2) teaching them to emulate their generated program’s execution, including those leaf functions, allowing the LM’s knowledge to fill in the execution gaps; and (3) using them to search over many programs to find an optimal one. To adapt the COGEX model to a new task, we introduce a method for performing program search to find a single program whose pseudo-execution yields optimal performance when applied to all the instances of a given dataset. We show that our approach yields large improvements compared to standard in-context learning approaches on a battery of tasks, both algorithmic and soft reasoning. This result thus demonstrates that code synthesis can be applied to a much broader class of problems than previously considered.
Key Techniques

Pseudo-program generation
Execution emulation
Program search optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityAgentStepper: Interactive Debugging of Software Development Agents0.19SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.18Scaling Agentic Verifier for Competitive Coding0.18Algorithmic Prompt-Augmentation for Efficient LLM-Based Heuristic Design for A* Search0.15CL-bench: A Benchmark for Context Learning0.14
COGEX (Code Generation and Emulated EXecution)
Core Idea: Extend program synthesis to soft reasoning tasks by generating Python pseudo-programs with undefined leaf functions, then training the LM to emulate execution by filling in those gaps with its own knowledge.
Problem It Solves: Standard program synthesis only works for algorithmic tasks with well-defined operations; it cannot handle reasoning tasks involving commonsense, moral judgment, or subjective interpretation that don’t map cleanly to executable code.
How It Works:

Pseudo-Program Generation: Train LM to generate Python programs where leaf function calls (e.g., is_sarcastic(), morally_acceptable()) are left undefined/unimplemented
Emulated Execution: Train the LM to simulate program execution by using its internal knowledge to resolve undefined function calls during trace-through
Program Search: Search over multiple candidate pseudo-programs and select the one whose emulated execution yields optimal performance across all dataset instances

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Task Instance] --> B[Generate Pseudo-Programs]
    B --> C[Emulate Execution]
    C --> D[Evaluate Performance]
    D --> E{Search}
    E -->|Try Next| B
    E -->|Best Program| F[Apply to Dataset]

Where It’s Useful:

Commonsense reasoning tasks (e.g., Winograd schemas, social situations)
Subjective judgment tasks (moral reasoning, sarcasm detection)
Hybrid symbolic-semantic reasoning requiring both algorithmic structure and world knowledge
Tasks where decomposition strategy is learnable but primitives are fuzzy

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires training LMs specifically for pseudo-program generation and emulation (not pure prompting)
Program search can be computationally expensive; needs heuristics to prune search space
Quality depends on LM’s ability to accurately emulate undefined functions; may hallucinate incorrect behavior
Best suited for tasks where optimal program structure generalizes across instances

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys feature engineering: Generate pseudo-programs that combine defined metrics (CTR, engagement) with emulated semantic functions (content_quality(), user_intent_match()) for candidate scoring
Ranking explanation: Synthesize interpretable pseudo-code that mirrors ranking logic, with emulated calls for subjective factors (“is_high_quality()”) to aid debugging
LLM agent planning: Structure reasoning chains as pseudo-programs where high-level control flow is explicit but leaf actions are emulated, enabling search over reasoning strategies

literature/2026-02-04-Nüwa-Mending-the-S.title: Nüwa: Mending the Spatial Integrity Torn by VLM Token Pruning
literature/2026-02-04-Nüwa-Mending-the-S.content:
Nüwa: Mending the Spatial Integrity Torn by VLM Token Pruning
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #9 (score: 0.91, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.02951v1
Abstract
Vision token pruning has proven to be an effective acceleration technique for the efficient Vision Language Model (VLM). However, existing pruning methods demonstrate excellent performance preservation in visual question answering (VQA) and suffer substantial degradation on visual grounding (VG) tasks. Our analysis of the VLM’s processing pipeline reveals that strategies utilizing global semantic similarity and attention scores lose the global spatial reference frame, which is derived from the interactions of tokens’ positional information. Motivated by these findings, we propose \text{Nüwa}, a two-stage token pruning framework that enables efficient feature aggregation while maintaining spatial integrity. In the first stage, after the vision encoder, we apply three operations, namely separation, alignment, and aggregation, which are inspired by swarm intelligence algorithms to retain information-rich global spatial anchors. In the second stage, within the LLM, we perform text-guided pruning to retain task-relevant visual tokens. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \text{Nüwa} achieves SOTA performance on multiple VQA benchmarks (from 94% to 95%) and yields substantial improvements on visual grounding tasks (from 7% to 47%).
Key Techniques

Separation
alignment
aggregation
text-guided pruning

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityVisionTrim: Unified Vision Token Compression for Training-Free MLLM Acceleration0.45VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.33SwiftVLM: Efficient Vision-Language Model Inference via Cross-Layer Token Bypass0.33ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.32Do MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.30
Text-Guided Token Pruning (TGTP)
Core Idea: In the second stage (within the LLM), selectively prune vision tokens based on task-relevant guidance from the language model to retain only semantically useful visual information.
Problem It Solves: Even after preserving spatial anchors, an VLM still processes all tokens through the LLM. Text-guided pruning reduces the computational burden of LLM processing by removing vision tokens that don’t contribute to answering the specific query or task.
How It Works:

Input task-specific text (question, instruction) and all spatial-anchor-preserving vision tokens into the LLM decoder
Compute attention weights or relevance scores between the text input and each vision token based on LLM’s internal representations
Select and retain only vision tokens with high task relevance scores
Process remaining tokens through downstream LLM layers for final output generation

Where It’s Useful:

Visual question answering (VQA) where questions provide strong guidance on which image regions matter
Image captioning with specific focus instructions
Visual reasoning tasks where the query constrains relevant regions
Grounding tasks where text descriptions guide which image areas to attend to

Limitations / Caveats:

Text-guided approach may miss important spatial context not explicitly mentioned in the task text
Requires text prompt to generate guidance (may underperform on tasks without clear textual context)
Risk of over-pruning if relevance thresholds are too aggressive

Internal Use Cases:

Query-aware image retrieval where the search query guides which image regions to index
Contextualized visual ranking in search/recommendation where the user query determines spatial importance
Multi-modal information retrieval where linguistic cues determine visual region relevance

literature/2026-02-04-Ontology-to-tools-.title: Ontology-to-tools compilation for executable semantic constraint enforcement in LLM agents
literature/2026-02-04-Ontology-to-tools-.content:
Ontology-to-tools compilation for executable semantic constraint enforcement in LLM agents
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #5 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Graph, Embeddings, LLM
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03439v1
Abstract
We introduce ontology-to-tools compilation as a proof-of-principle mechanism for coupling large language models (LLMs) with formal domain knowledge. Within The World Avatar (TWA), ontological specifications are compiled into executable tool interfaces that LLM-based agents must use to create and modify knowledge graph instances, enforcing semantic constraints during generation rather than through post-hoc validation. Extending TWA’s semantic agent composition framework, the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and associated agents are integral components of the knowledge graph ecosystem, enabling structured interaction between generative models, symbolic constraints, and external resources. An agent-based workflow translates ontologies into ontology-aware tools and iteratively applies them to extract, validate, and repair structured knowledge from unstructured scientific text. Using metal-organic polyhedra synthesis literature as an illustrative case, we show how executable ontological semantics can guide LLM behaviour and reduce manual schema and prompt engineering, establishing a general paradigm for embedding formal knowledge into generative systems.
Key Techniques

Ontology-to-tools compilation
Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration
Agent-based workflow for knowledge extraction and validation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityOpaqueToolsBench: Learning Nuances of Tool Behavior Through Interaction0.26AgentStepper: Interactive Debugging of Software Development Agents0.23Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.21Toward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning0.20TVCACHE: A Stateful Tool-Value Cache for Post-Training LLM Agents0.20
Agent-Based Iterative Knowledge Repair (AIKR)
Core Idea: Use a multi-turn agentic workflow to iteratively extract, validate, and repair structured knowledge from unstructured text by cycling between LLM extraction, constraint checking via ontology-aware tools, and targeted text-to-fix-mapping for error correction.
Problem It Solves: Single-pass extraction of structured knowledge from unstructured text produces errors; validating and repairing errors requires human intervention or multiple disjoint passes that lose context.
How It Works:

Extraction Phase: LLM agent reads unstructured text (e.g., synthesis paper) and calls ontology-aware tools to instantiate knowledge graph entities and relationships
Validation Phase: Ontology-to-tools compilation catches constraint violations (cardinality, type, domain mismatches) during tool invocation; failures are returned to agent
Repair Phase: Agent receives error context and re-reads source text to identify and correct the offending statement; updates are re-submitted via tool invocation
Iteration: Repeat until knowledge graph passes all constraints or maximum iterations reached

Architecture (if applicable):
flowchart LR
    A["Unstructured Text<br/>(Scientific Paper)"] --> B["LLM Agent:<br/>Extract"]
    B --> C["Call Ontology<br/>Tools"]
    C --> D{"Constraints<br/>Satisfied?"}
    D -->|Yes| E["Knowledge Graph<br/>Instance"]
    D -->|No| F["Error Report<br/>+ Source Context"]
    F --> G["LLM Agent:<br/>Diagnose & Repair"]
    G --> H["Revised Tool<br/>Calls"]
    H --> C
    E --> I["Validated<br/>Output"]

Where It’s Useful:

Information extraction from scientific literature with hard semantic requirements (materials science, chemistry, biology)
Knowledge graph population from diverse unstructured sources where single-pass extraction is insufficient
Scenarios where ground-truth labels are expensive or unavailable; ontology constraints serve as weak supervision signal

Limitations / Caveats:

Assumes ontology is correct and complete; ontology bugs manifest as repeated failed repairs
Agent may cycle indefinitely if constraint violation is unfixable given the source text (e.g., information simply not present)
Repair phase requires agent to re-read and reason about source text; context windows may limit ability to handle long documents
Performance depends heavily on LLM’s ability to diagnose constraint failures and identify correct repair—weaker models may loop ineffectively

Internal Use Cases:

Extends Reflection pattern: agent reflects on constraint failures and attempts self-correction via re-reading source material
Relates to Coding Agent for Tool-Augmented Reasoning: specialized ontology tool agents handle constraint checking, freeing main LLM to focus on text understanding and repair strategy
For RecSys: iterative repair of user-item interaction graphs, correcting invalid edges or attributes via ontology-constrained tool calls


Semantic Agent Composition via Model Context Protocol (MCP)
Core Idea: Integrate ontology-aware tools into a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server, enabling modular, composable agent workflows where semantic constraints are enforced through standardized tool interfaces accessible to any MCP-compatible LLM client.
Problem It Solves: Point-to-point integration of constraint-enforcing tools into agent systems is brittle and non-reusable; MCP provides a standard interface for agents to discover and invoke semantic tools across heterogeneous environments.
How It Works:

Tool Definition: Define ontology-constrained tools (create entity, add relationship, update property) with MCP-compliant schemas and constraint logic
MCP Server Registration: Package tools as MCP server exposing them via standard RPC protocol (JSON-RPC over stdio or HTTP)
Agent Discovery: LLM agent queries MCP server for available tools, retrieves their signatures, parameter schemas, and constraint documentation
Constrained Invocation: Agent calls tools via MCP interface; server executes constraints, returns success (updated KG) or error (constraint violation with context)
Composition: Multiple specialized MCP servers (entity tools, relationship tools, validation tools, external data sources) can be composed in a single agent workflow

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-agent systems requiring shared, versioned semantic tool libraries
Ecosystem development where third-party tools need to enforce domain semantics (chemistry, biology, engineering ontologies)
Decoupling tool implementation from agent logic; tools can be updated or swapped without retraining or re-prompting agents
Cross-organization knowledge graph construction where semantic standards must be enforced uniformly

Limitations / Caveats:

MCP server overhead (RPC latency, serialization) may increase per-tool-call latency, impacting agent throughput
Tool discovery and schema negotiation adds complexity; agents must parse and reason about tool capabilities before invocation
Debugging constraint failures across MCP boundaries is harder than monolithic tool implementations; error messages must traverse protocol layers
Requires standardization on MCP; heterogeneous systems using proprietary tool interfaces cannot interoperate

Internal Use Cases:

Modular extension of ranking/recommendation systems: ontology tools enforce valid user segments, item categories, interaction types without modifying core ranking logic
Multi-team development: different teams own MCP servers for their domain (user ontology, item ontology, interaction ontology); composed into unified agent workflow
Parallels Chain-of-Agents (CoA): each specialized MCP server acts as a domain-specific agent, coordinated by a higher-level reasoning LLM

literature/2026-02-04-ReNeuIR-at-SIGIR-2.title: ReNeuIR at SIGIR 2024: The Third Workshop on Reaching Efficiency in Neural Information Retrieval
literature/2026-02-04-ReNeuIR-at-SIGIR-2.content:
ReNeuIR at SIGIR 2024: The Third Workshop on Reaching Efficiency in Neural Information Retrieval
Source: S2:Annual International ACM SIGIR Conference on Research and Development in Information Retrieval
Published: 2024
Rank: #6 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search, RAG
Abstract
The Information Retrieval (IR) community has a rich history of empirically measuring novel retrieval methods in terms of effectiveness and efficiency. However, as the search ecosystem is developing rapidly, comparatively little attention has been paid to evaluating efficiency in recent years, which raises the question of the cost-benefit ratio between effectiveness and efficiency. In this regard, it has become difficult to compare and contrast systems in an empirically fair way. Factors including hardware configurations, software versioning, experimental settings, and measurement methods all contribute to the difficulty of meaningfully comparing search systems, especially where efficiency is a key component of the evaluation. Furthermore, efficiency is no longer limited to time and space but has found new, challenging dimensions that stretch to resource, sample, and energy efficiency and have implications for users, researchers, and the environment. Examining algorithms and models through the lens of efficiency and its trade-off with effectiveness requires revisiting and establishing new standards and principles, from defining relevant concepts, to designing measures, to creating guidelines for making sense of the significance of findings. The third iteration of ReNeuIR aims to bring the community together to debate these questions and collaboratively test and improve a benchmarking framework for efficiency derived from the discussions of the first two iterations of this workshop. We provide a first prototype of this framework by organizing a shared task track focused on comparability and reproducibility at the workshop.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques or methods:

Empirical measurement of retrieval methods

Efficiency evaluation frameworks
Benchmarking standardization for search systems

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBenchmarking the Energy Savings with Speculative Decoding Strategies0.27A Field Guide to Automatic Evaluation of LLM-Generated Summaries0.15KDD 2025 Workshop on Inference Optimization for Generative AI0.15Semantic Search At LinkedIn0.13Nanbeige4.1-3B: A Small General Model that Reasons, Aligns, and Acts0.12
ReNeuIR Efficiency Benchmarking Framework
Core Idea: A standardized framework for measuring and comparing efficiency metrics (time, space, resource, sample, and energy) alongside effectiveness in neural information retrieval systems, addressing the lack of fair comparability due to varying hardware, software, and experimental settings.
Problem It Solves: Modern IR evaluations focus heavily on effectiveness (relevance, ranking quality) while neglecting efficiency, making it impossible to fairly compare the cost-benefit ratio of different neural retrieval methods across different experimental setups and hardware configurations.
How It Works:

Multi-dimensional efficiency measurement: Captures not just time/space complexity but also resource utilization, sample efficiency (data requirements), and energy consumption
Standardized experimental protocol: Defines fixed hardware configurations, software versions, measurement methodologies, and reporting standards for reproducibility
Shared task evaluation: Provides benchmark datasets and evaluation harness where participants submit systems that are tested under identical conditions
Trade-off analysis: Explicitly measures and reports the effectiveness-efficiency Pareto frontier to quantify whether gains in relevance justify computational costs

Where It’s Useful:

Comparing dense retrieval models vs. sparse retrieval methods on production constraints
Evaluating neural reranking architectures for latency-sensitive applications
Assessing retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines where inference cost matters
Green AI initiatives measuring carbon footprint of information retrieval systems

Limitations / Caveats:

Standardization may not capture all real-world deployment scenarios (edge devices, distributed systems)
Framework adoption requires community consensus and maintained infrastructure
Energy measurement methodology varies significantly across hardware vendors
May not generalize across vastly different retrieval paradigms (e.g., symbolic vs. neural)

Internal Use Cases:

Benchmark embedding models for RecSys against latency budgets (e.g., <50ms p99 for real-time ranking)
Evaluate LLM-based reranking vs. lightweight cross-encoders on GPU memory and throughput
Quantify sample efficiency of contrastive learning methods for retrieval when labeled data is scarce
Report energy consumption for dense retrieval training to guide model selection under sustainability constraints

literature/2026-02-04-Reshape-and-Adapt-.title: Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems
literature/2026-02-04-Reshape-and-Adapt-.content:
Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems
Source: OpenReview:ICML 2024 Poster
Published:
Rank: #2 (score: 0.09, max_sim: 0.75)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
In-memory computing (IMC) has emerged as a promising solution to address both computation and data-movement challenges, by performing computation on data in-place directly in the memory array. IMC typically relies on analog operation, which makes analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) necessary, for converting results back to the digital domain. However, ADCs maintain computational efficiency by having limited precision, leading to substantial quantization errors in compute outputs. This work proposes RAOQ (Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization) to overcome this issue, which comprises two classes of mechanisms including: 1) mitigating ADC quantization error by adjusting the statistics of activations and weights, through an activation-shifting approach (A-shift) and a weight reshaping technique (W-reshape); 2) adapting AI models to better tolerate ADC quantization through a bit augmentation method (BitAug), complemented by the introduction of ADC-LoRA, a low-rank approximation technique, to reduce the training overhead. RAOQ demonstrates consistently high performance across different scales and domains of neural network models for computer vision and natural language processing (NLP) tasks at various bit precisions, achieving state-of-the-art results with practical IMC implementations.
Key Techniques

Activation-shifting (A-shift)
Weight reshaping (W-reshape)
Bit augmentation (BitAug)
ADC-LoRA

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.29QuEPT: Quantized Elastic Precision Transformers with One-Shot Calibration for Multi-Bit Switching0.27OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization0.25BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.241-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization0.24
Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ)
Core Idea: Mitigate ADC quantization errors in in-memory computing systems through activation-shifting and weight-reshaping to control distribution statistics, combined with bit augmentation during quantization-aware training to improve model tolerance.
Problem It Solves: Analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) in in-memory computing (IMC) systems use limited precision for efficiency, causing substantial quantization errors in neural network outputs that degrade accuracy.
How It Works:

A-shift (Activation Shifting): Shift activation distributions to better align with ADC quantization bins, reducing quantization error by optimizing where values fall in the discrete output space
W-reshape (Weight Reshaping): Adjust weight statistics to control the dynamic range and distribution of pre-ADC outputs, making them more amenable to low-precision quantization
BitAug (Bit Augmentation): During training, randomly vary the ADC bit precision to expose the model to different quantization noise levels, improving robustness
ADC-LoRA: Apply low-rank approximation to reduce the training overhead of adaptation layers, making quantization-aware training practical for large models

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Weights] --> B[W-reshape]
    B --> C[IMC Array]
    D[Activations] --> E[A-shift]
    E --> C
    C --> F[ADC Quantization]
    F --> G[Output]
    H[BitAug Training] -.->|adapts| B
    H -.->|adapts| E
    I[ADC-LoRA] -.->|efficient tuning| H

Where It’s Useful:

Edge AI deployment on in-memory computing hardware with constrained ADC precision
Neural network acceleration on analog computing systems (ReRAM, SRAM-based IMC)
Computer vision and NLP models requiring efficient inference on resource-limited hardware

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires quantization-aware training with BitAug, adding training complexity
Performance depends on specific ADC characteristics of target IMC hardware
Benefits are most pronounced at very low bit precisions (2-4 bits); diminishing returns at higher precision

Internal Use Cases:

Deploying ranking models or embeddings to edge devices with IMC accelerators
Reducing serving costs for LLM inference by targeting analog computing backends
Optimizing RecSys models for deployment on energy-constrained hardware with limited ADC precision

literature/2026-02-04-Risk-Awareness-Inj.title: Risk Awareness Injection: Calibrating Vision-Language Models for Safety without Compromising Utility
literature/2026-02-04-Risk-Awareness-Inj.content:
Risk Awareness Injection: Calibrating Vision-Language Models for Safety without Compromising Utility
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #12 (score: 0.65, max_sim: 0.80)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03402v1
Abstract
Vision language models (VLMs) extend the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) to cross-modal settings, yet remain highly vulnerable to multimodal jailbreak attacks. Existing defenses predominantly rely on safety fine-tuning or aggressive token manipulations, incurring substantial training costs or significantly degrading utility. Recent research shows that LLMs inherently recognize unsafe content in text, and the incorporation of visual inputs in VLMs frequently dilutes risk-related signals. Motivated by this, we propose Risk Awareness Injection (RAI), a lightweight and training-free framework for safety calibration that restores LLM-like risk recognition by amplifying unsafe signals in VLMs. Specifically, RAI constructs an Unsafe Prototype Subspace from language embeddings and performs targeted modulation on selected high-risk visual tokens, explicitly activating safety-critical signals within the cross-modal feature space. This modulation restores the model’s LLM-like ability to detect unsafe content from visual inputs, while preserving the semantic integrity of original tokens for cross-modal reasoning. Extensive experiments across multiple jailbreak and utility benchmarks demonstrate that RAI substantially reduces attack success rate without compromising task performance.
Key Techniques

Unsafe Prototype Subspace construction
targeted modulation of high-risk visual tokens
cross-modal feature space safety-critical signal activation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityCSR-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating the Cross-modal Safety and Reliability of MLLMs0.22Fail-Closed Alignment for Large Language Models0.20The Side Effects of Being Smart: Safety Risks in MLLMs’ Multi-Image Reasoning0.18GOSV: Global Optimization for Safety Vector Extraction in LLMs0.16Why Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models0.15
Unsafe Prototype Subspace Construction
Core Idea: Create a learned subspace in the LLM’s embedding space that captures the essential features distinguishing unsafe from safe content by aggregating language embeddings of unsafe examples.
Problem It Solves: VLMs lose the LLM’s native ability to recognize unsafe content because visual context drowns out text-based risk signals. Without an explicit unsafe prototype, the model cannot reliably activate safety mechanisms when processing visual input. This construction provides an interpretable, language-grounded reference for what “unsafe” means in the model’s own feature space.
How It Works:

Unsafe Example Collection: Gather a set of unsafe text prompts or descriptions (e.g., prompts known to trigger jailbreaks or harmful behaviors).
Language Embedding Extraction: Pass each unsafe example through the VLM’s text encoder to obtain embedding vectors in the shared cross-modal space.
Subspace Basis Computation: Apply dimensionality reduction (e.g., PCA, SVD, or mean centering) to the collection of unsafe embeddings to extract principal unsafe characteristics, forming an orthogonal or principal component basis.
Prototype Vector Definition: Compute a representative prototype vector (e.g., mean or centroid of unsafe embeddings in the principal subspace) that serves as the “unsafe signal anchor.”

Where It’s Useful:

Defining safety boundaries in black-box or frozen LLMs without modifying weights
Interpretable safety calibration where the unsafe subspace can be visualized or audited
Few-shot or zero-shot safety adaptation for new harm categories (by adding examples and recomputing the subspace)

Limitations / Caveats:

Quality and coverage depend entirely on the unsafe examples used; rare or novel attack patterns may not be captured
Subspace construction is sensitive to the choice of dimensionality reduction method and hyperparameters
Static subspace does not adapt to adversarial examples or concept drift over time
Computational cost grows with the number of unsafe examples; scalability for very large harm datasets requires efficient approximations

Internal Use Cases:

Collaborative Filtering Safety: In recommendation systems, an “unsafe item prototype subspace” could be constructed from item embeddings known to violate policies, then used to filter candidate rankings
Query Intent Understanding: Similar to behavior-aware collaborative embeddings in vault notes, unsafe prototypes could capture implicit “harmful intent” dimensions and be layered throughout the ranking stack

literature/2026-02-04-SCASRec-A-Self-Cor.title: SCASRec: A Self-Correcting and Auto-Stopping Model for Generative Route List Recommendation
literature/2026-02-04-SCASRec-A-Self-Cor.content:
SCASRec: A Self-Correcting and Auto-Stopping Model for Generative Route List Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #8 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RecSys, LLM, RL
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03324v1
Abstract
Route recommendation systems commonly adopt a multi-stage pipeline involving fine-ranking and re-ranking to produce high-quality ordered recommendations. However, this paradigm faces three critical limitations. First, there is a misalignment between offline training objectives and online metrics. Offline gains do not necessarily translate to online improvements. Actual performance must be validated through A/B testing, which may potentially compromise the user experience. Second, redundancy elimination relies on rigid, handcrafted rules that lack adaptability to the high variance in user intent and the unstructured complexity of real-world scenarios. Third, the strict separation between fine-ranking and re-ranking stages leads to sub-optimal performance. Since each module is optimized in isolation, the fine-ranking stage remains oblivious to the list-level objectives (e.g., diversity) targeted by the re-ranker, thereby preventing the system from achieving a jointly optimized global optimum. To overcome these intertwined challenges, we propose \textbf{SCASRec} (\textbf{S}elf-\textbf{C}orrecting and \textbf{A}uto-\textbf{S}topping \textbf{Rec}ommendation), a unified generative framework that integrates ranking and redundancy elimination into a single end-to-end process. SCASRec introduces a stepwise corrective reward (SCR) to guide list-wise refinement by focusing on hard samples, and employs a learnable End-of-Recommendation (EOR) token to terminate generation adaptively when no further improvement is expected. Experiments on two large-scale, open-sourced route recommendation datasets demonstrate that SCASRec establishes an SOTA in offline and online settings. SCASRec has been fully deployed in a real-world navigation app, demonstrating its effectiveness.
Key Techniques

Stepwise corrective reward (SCR)
learnable End-of-Recommendation (EOR) token
unified generative framework

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityOneMall: One Architecture, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.25OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.23OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.23S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation0.16Answer First, Reason Later: Aligning Search Relevance via Mode-Balanced Reinforcement Learning0.16
End-of-Recommendation (EOR) Token with Adaptive Stopping
Core Idea: A learnable special token that signals when to terminate generation, allowing the model to dynamically decide list length based on learned quality cues rather than fixed output constraints.
Problem It Solves: Traditional ranking systems output a fixed number of candidates, including low-quality or redundant items that degrade list quality. Rigid cutoff rules cannot adapt to variance in user intent and recommendation difficulty across different scenarios.
How It Works:

Introduce a special learnable token \text{EOR} with embeddings e_{\text{EOR}} \in \mathbb{R}^d
During generation at step t, compute logits for all candidate routes plus the EOR token: \text{logits}_t = \text{score}(h_t, C) \cup \{\text{score}(h_t, e_{\text{EOR}})\}
Apply softmax to all candidates + EOR token and sample next action (either select a route or generate EOR)
If EOR is sampled, terminate list generation immediately; otherwise continue with selected route
Train EOR token via policy gradient or reinforcement learning to predict when marginal list quality gain falls below a learned threshold

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Hidden State h_t"] --> B["Score All Routes + EOR"]
    B --> C["Softmax over Routes ∪ {EOR}"]
    C --> D{Sample Action}
    D -->|Select Route| E["Append Route to List"]
    D -->|EOR Token| F["Terminate Generation"]
    E --> G["Update h_{t+1}"]
    G --> C
    F --> H["Output Final List"]

Where It’s Useful:

Sequential recommendation where list length should vary by user/context
Route recommendation with variable scenario complexity
Any ranking task where quality-per-item degrades and optimal list length is data-dependent
Real-world systems where showing too many low-value options harms UX

Limitations / Caveats:

EOR token must be trained jointly with ranking—poor initialization can lead to premature or excessive termination
Requires reward signal that clearly indicates when to stop (avoids ambiguity)
May require curriculum learning or careful initialization to prevent collapse to always-stopping or never-stopping behavior

Internal Use Cases:

Addresses User Feedback Alignment by allowing the model to learn when user exploration should stop based on learned satisfaction signals
Improves the Two-Stage LLM Sequential Recommendation paradigm by making next-item stopping decisions part of a unified generative process


Unified Generative Ranking Framework (End-to-End Fine-Ranking + Re-Ranking Integration)
Core Idea: A single generative model that jointly performs ranking and redundancy elimination in one pass, replacing the traditional multi-stage pipeline with an integrated end-to-end process that optimizes for both relevance and list-level objectives simultaneously.
Problem It Solves: Classical two-stage pipelines (fine-ranking → re-ranking) suffer from: (1) stage separation causing each to ignore downstream objectives, (2) misalignment between offline metrics and online A/B test results, and (3) rigid handcrafted redundancy rules that lack adaptability.
How It Works:

Encode user context, query, and candidate routes into a shared representation space
Use a transformer-based decoder that autoregressively generates the recommendation list token-by-token
At each step t, condition on the current partial list \mathcal{L}_{<t} and hidden state h_t to score remaining candidates
Integrate Stepwise Corrective Reward (SCR) into the loss to backpropagate list-level objectives (diversity, redundancy) into ranking decisions
Use End-of-Recommendation (EOR) token to determine when to stop generation
Train end-to-end with RL or policy gradient methods that directly optimize online metrics (rather than offline ranking metrics)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["User Context + Query"] --> B["Shared Encoder"]
    B --> C["Autoregressive Decoder"]
    C --> D["Score Candidates"]
    D --> E{"Hard Sample?"}
    E -->|Yes| F["Apply SCR Loss Upweighting"]
    E -->|No| G["Standard Loss"]
    F --> H["Generate Next Route or EOR"]
    G --> H
    H --> I{EOR Token?}
    I -->|Yes| J["Stop"]
    I -->|No| K["Append Route, Update h_t"]
    K --> D
    J --> L["Final Ranked List"]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale recommendation systems (navigation, e-commerce, content recommendation) where multi-stage pipelines introduce latency and suboptimality
Scenarios requiring joint optimization of relevance + diversity + coverage
Real-world deployed systems where A/B testing cost is high and offline-online alignment is critical
Route recommendation and navigation where redundancy matters (avoid repeated turns/highways)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires joint training signal that includes both ranking and list-level objectives—harder to train than modular pipelines
Generative decoding is slower than indexing-based retrieval; inference latency may increase
End-to-end learning with RL introduces training complexity and potential instability
Requires careful design of reward functions to avoid gaming or unexpected behavior

Internal Use Cases:

Directly unifies insights from LLM-Driven Post-hoc Re-ranking and Explanation (re-ranking as a learned stage) with Two-Stage LLM Sequential Recommendation (sequential generation)
Applies User Feedback Alignment by making the stopping decision (when to end list) part of the core generative objective, enabling adaptive exploration-exploitation tradeoffs

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-04-SLiM-Speculative-D.title: SLiM: Speculative Decoding with Hypothesis Reduction
literature/2026-02-04-SLiM-Speculative-D.content:
SLiM: Speculative Decoding with Hypothesis Reduction
Source: S2:NAACL-HLT
Published: 2024
Rank: #1 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Speculative decoding has emerged as a prominent alternative to autoregressive decoding for expediting inference in large language models (LLMs). However, prevailing assumptions often focus solely on latency reduction, neglecting the computational expenses. In this paper, we present S peculate L ess, val i date M ore (SLiM), a speculative decoding enhancement to reduce the speculation set while validating more effective tokens. SLiM is designed to mitigate LLMs’ computation costs associated with the token verification by introducing hypothesis reduction based on a fast posterior estimation. It consistently surpasses counterparts lacking cost reduction across a spectrum from CPU to GPU. Our evaluation with diverse conversational datasets shows that SLiM can achieve a substantial 70% reduction in FLOPs while generating more effective predictions on top of prior arts.
Key Techniques

Speculative decoding
hypothesis reduction
fast posterior estimation
token verification

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySpeculative Decoding with a Speculative Vocabulary0.34SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration0.32Variational Speculative Decoding: Rethinking Draft Training from Token Likelihood to Sequence Acceptance0.31DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.31Greedy Multi-Path Block Verification for Faster Decoding in Speculative Sampling0.31
SLiM (Speculate Less, validate More)
Core Idea: Reduces computational cost in speculative decoding by pruning the hypothesis set using fast posterior estimation before full verification, eliminating unpromising draft tokens early rather than verifying all speculated tokens.
Problem It Solves: Traditional speculative decoding focuses on latency reduction but incurs high computational costs from verifying all draft tokens with the large target model, especially when many drafts have low acceptance probability.
How It Works:

Draft model generates a speculation set of K candidate tokens as usual
Fast posterior estimation (lightweight approximation) scores each hypothesis and prunes unlikely candidates before full verification
Target LLM validates only the reduced hypothesis set, accepting tokens that pass the verification threshold
Process repeats with accepted tokens appended to context

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Context] --> B[Draft Model]
    B --> C[K Speculative Tokens]
    C --> D[Fast Posterior Estimation]
    D --> E[Pruned Hypothesis Set]
    E --> F[Target LLM Verification]
    F --> G[Accepted Tokens]
    G --> A

Where It’s Useful:

Resource-constrained environments (CPU inference, edge devices) where FLOPs matter as much as latency
Conversational AI systems requiring both speed and efficiency
Batch inference scenarios where compute cost dominates over wall-clock time

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires tuning the pruning threshold to balance speculation reduction vs. acceptance rate
Fast posterior estimation adds a small overhead that must be cheaper than full verification to provide net benefit
Effectiveness depends on the quality of the posterior approximation—poor estimation may prune valid candidates

Internal Use Cases:

LLM serving optimization: reduce inference costs in production chat/completion endpoints by 70% FLOPs while maintaining generation quality
Multi-stage ranking: apply hypothesis reduction principle to prune candidate sets between reranking stages using fast scoring functions
Retrieval-augmented generation: prune retrieved passages before expensive LLM verification using lightweight relevance estimators

literature/2026-02-04-SWE-Master-Unleash.title: SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training
literature/2026-02-04-SWE-Master-Unleash.content:
SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #7 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, Search
Code: github.com/RUCAIBox/SWE-Master
Abstract
In this technical report, we present SWE-Master, an open-source and fully reproducible post-training framework for building effective software engineering agents. SWE-Master systematically explores the complete agent development pipeline, including teacher-trajectory synthesis and data curation, long-horizon SFT, RL with real execution feedback, and inference framework design. Starting from an open-source base model with limited initial SWE capability, SWE-Master demonstrates how systematical optimization method can elicit strong long-horizon SWE task solving abilities. We evaluate SWE-Master on SWE-bench Verified, a standard benchmark for realistic software engineering tasks. Under identical experimental settings, our approach achieves a resolve rate of 61.4% with Qwen2.5-Coder-32B, substantially outperforming existing open-source baselines. By further incorporating test-time scaling~(TTS) with LLM-based environment feedback, SWE-Master reaches 70.8% at TTS@8, demonstrating a strong performance potential. SWE-Master provides a practical and transparent foundation for advancing reproducible research on software engineering agents. The code is available at github.com/RUCAIBox/SWE-Master.
Key Techniques

Teacher-trajectory synthesis
Long-horizon SFT
RL with real execution feedback
Inference framework design

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.33AgentStepper: Interactive Debugging of Software Development Agents0.32ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.30ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.29Hybrid-Gym: Training Coding Agents to Generalize Across Tasks0.28
Long-Horizon Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
Core Idea: Apply supervised fine-tuning to directly optimize the base model’s ability to predict complete solution trajectories end-to-end, enabling agents to learn extended reasoning and tool-use chains without separate policy learning.
Problem It Solves: Base models have limited ability to generate long sequences of coherent reasoning and tool interactions; standard SFT on short examples doesn’t transfer to multi-step planning and execution.
How It Works:

Use curated teacher trajectories as training targets
Fine-tune the base model (Qwen2.5-Coder-32B) to predict the next token in the trajectory conditioned on task context and previous steps
Train on full trajectories (not truncated) to preserve long-range dependencies and planning patterns
Optimize for sequence-level match rather than step-level accuracy to encourage coherent solution paths

Where It’s Useful:

Agent behavior cloning from expert demonstrations
Multi-step planning tasks in any domain
Transfer learning for long-horizon reasoning

Limitations / Caveats:

Exposure bias: agent only trains on trajectories from teachers, not from its own errors
Trajectory diversity limited by curated data; doesn’t explore novel solution paths
Computational cost scales with trajectory length and dataset size

Internal Use Cases:

Similar to training rankers or recommendation models on diverse curated ranking trajectories with quality filtering


Reinforcement Learning with Real Execution Feedback
Core Idea: Optimize the fine-tuned model using reinforcement learning where rewards come from actual task execution outcomes (pass/fail on test cases, code correctness) rather than proxy metrics, enabling direct optimization for real-world task success.
Problem It Solves: SFT alone doesn’t optimize for actual task completion; RL with execution feedback allows the agent to learn from its own mistakes and explore beyond teacher trajectories to find better solutions.
How It Works:

Deploy the SFT-trained model to attempt SWE-bench tasks
Execute generated code and run test suites to obtain binary or scalar rewards (1 if tests pass, 0 otherwise)
Use reward signal to compute policy gradients (e.g., PPO or similar algorithm) to update model weights
Iteratively refine the model by sampling trajectories, collecting execution feedback, and optimizing for higher task completion rates
Filter trajectories using execution outcomes to identify which decision points led to success/failure

Where It’s Useful:

Any task where ground-truth execution feedback is available
Correcting distribution shift between training trajectories and deployment behavior
Exploring solutions beyond teacher demonstrations

Limitations / Caveats:

Expensive: requires running code and test suites for each sampled trajectory
Sparse rewards: binary success signals provide weak learning signals early in training
Exploration challenges: high-dimensional action space makes random exploration unlikely to find solutions

Internal Use Cases:

Extending to recommendation ranking by using online A/B test results or user feedback as execution rewards during RL fine-tuning


Test-Time Scaling with LLM-Based Environment Feedback (TTS@8)
Core Idea: During inference, generate multiple candidate solution trajectories and use LLM-based environment feedback (simulated execution or verification) to select the highest-quality solution, trading compute at test time for improved accuracy without retraining.
Problem It Solves: Single-pass agent inference produces suboptimal solutions; test-time scaling allows the agent to explore multiple hypotheses and self-correct using external feedback without expensive retraining.
How It Works:

At inference time, sample K solution trajectories from the trained model (K=8 in experiments)
Execute each trajectory (run generated code, test against benchmarks)
Use LLM to evaluate trajectory quality based on execution feedback (test results, error messages, code analysis)
Select the trajectory with highest quality score from the LLM evaluator
Return the best solution as the agent’s final answer

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Task Input] --> B[Sample K Trajectories]
    B --> C[Execute Each Trajectory]
    C --> D[Collect Execution Feedback]
    D --> E[LLM Evaluates Quality]
    E --> F[Select Best Solution]
    F --> G[Return Answer]

Where It’s Useful:

Tasks where execution feedback is available and fast
Improving performance of weak agents without retraining
Verification and ranking problems with clear quality signals

Limitations / Caveats:

Linear cost increase with K (8× compute for TTS@8)
Quality depends on LLM evaluator accuracy; poor evaluation feedback misleads selection
Assumes execution is deterministic; doesn’t work well with stochastic environments

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking systems can use test-time scaling by generating multiple candidate rankings and scoring with LLM feedback on diversity and relevance metrics


Inference Framework Design for SWE Agents
Core Idea: Design a structured inference pipeline that orchestrates tool use, code execution, error handling, and environment interaction specifically optimized for solving software engineering tasks at scale.
Problem It Solves: Generic language model inference is insufficient for SWE tasks; a specialized framework must handle tool sandboxing, dependency management, test execution, error recovery, and solution iteration.
How It Works:

Parse model outputs to extract tool calls (edit files, run tests, search codebase, etc.)
Execute tools in a sandboxed environment (isolate code execution to prevent system damage)
Capture execution results (stdout, stderr, test pass/fail) and return as feedback to the model
Implement error recovery: if execution fails, pass error context back to the model for retry
Maintain session state (filesystem, git state, environment variables) across multiple model interactions
Implement termination conditions (max iterations, task completion) to prevent infinite loops

Where It’s Useful:

Software engineering agent systems
Code generation and debugging workflows
Multi-turn tool-use task execution

Limitations / Caveats:

Dependency on robust tool implementations; poor tool quality limits agent effectiveness
Security and sandbox overhead can slow execution
Requires careful design to maintain consistent state across tool calls

Internal Use Cases:

Recommendation systems can adopt similar structured frameworks for multi-turn tool-use workflows (e.g., querying databases, filtering candidates, re-ranking)

Discussions & References

huggingface.co
github.com

literature/2026-02-04-SwiftVLM-Efficient.title: SwiftVLM: Efficient Vision-Language Model Inference via Cross-Layer Token Bypass
literature/2026-02-04-SwiftVLM-Efficient.content:
SwiftVLM: Efficient Vision-Language Model Inference via Cross-Layer Token Bypass
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #2 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Code: arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03134v1
Abstract
Visual token pruning is a promising approach for reducing the computational cost of vision-language models (VLMs), and existing methods often rely on early pruning decisions to improve efficiency. While effective on coarse-grained reasoning tasks, they suffer from significant performance degradation on tasks requiring fine-grained visual details. Through layer-wise analysis, we reveal substantial discrepancies in visual token importance across layers, showing that tokens deemed unimportant at shallow layers can later become highly relevant for text-conditioned reasoning. To avoid irreversible critical information loss caused by premature pruning, we introduce a new pruning paradigm, termed bypass, which preserves unselected visual tokens and forwards them to subsequent pruning stages for re-evaluation. Building on this paradigm, we propose SwiftVLM, a simple and training-free method that performs pruning at model-specific layers with strong visual token selection capability, while enabling independent pruning decisions across layers. Experiments across multiple VLMs and benchmarks demonstrate that SwiftVLM consistently outperforms existing pruning strategies, achieving superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs and more faithful visual token selection behavior.
Key Techniques

Visual token pruning
layer-wise analysis
bypass paradigm
training-free pruning method

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.40From LLMs to LRMs: Rethinking Pruning for Reasoning-Centric Models0.37Fast-Slow Efficient Training for Multimodal Large Language Models via Visual Token Pruning0.35Nüwa: Mending the Spatial Integrity Torn by VLM Token Pruning0.33Do MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.28
SwiftVLM Visual Token Bypass
Core Idea: Preserve pruned visual tokens and forward them to subsequent layers for re-evaluation, avoiding irreversible information loss from early pruning decisions in vision-language models.
Problem It Solves: Existing visual token pruning methods make early, permanent pruning decisions that discard tokens deemed unimportant at shallow layers—but these tokens often become critical for text-conditioned reasoning at deeper layers, causing significant performance degradation on fine-grained visual tasks.
How It Works:

Layer-wise Importance Analysis: Identify layers with strong visual token selection capability through empirical analysis of token importance variance across layers
Bypass Mechanism: Instead of discarding pruned tokens, preserve them in a bypass pathway and forward to subsequent pruning stages
Re-evaluation at Deeper Layers: At each pruning stage, re-evaluate all tokens (including previously bypassed ones) based on current layer’s text-conditioned attention
Training-Free Deployment: Apply pruning decisions at model-specific optimal layers without requiring fine-tuning

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    V["Visual Tokens"] --> L1["Layer N<br/>(Prune Stage 1)"]
    L1 --> Selected1["Selected"]
    L1 --> Bypass1["Bypassed"]
    Selected1 --> L2["Layer M<br/>(Prune Stage 2)"]
    Bypass1 --> L2
    L2 --> Final["Final Tokens"]

Where It’s Useful:

Vision-language models requiring fine-grained visual understanding
Inference efficiency optimization without sacrificing accuracy on detail-oriented tasks
Training-free deployment on pre-trained VLMs

Limitations / Caveats:

Bypass pathway adds memory overhead for stored tokens
Optimal pruning layers are model-specific, requiring per-model analysis
Benefits diminish on coarse-grained tasks where early pruning is sufficient

Internal Use Cases:

Designing multi-stage retrieval systems where candidates bypassed at early stages can be re-evaluated with richer context
Layer-wise importance analysis methodology applicable to understanding which embedding layers capture task-relevant features

literature/2026-02-04-To-Search-or-Not-t.title: To Search or Not to Search: Aligning the Decision Boundary of Deep Search Agents via Causal Intervention
literature/2026-02-04-To-Search-or-Not-t.content:
To Search or Not to Search: Aligning the Decision Boundary of Deep Search Agents via Causal Intervention
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #6 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RecSys, RAG, Search
Code: github.com/Applied-Machine-Learning-Lab/WWW2026_DAS
Abstract
Deep search agents, which autonomously iterate through multi-turn web-based reasoning, represent a promising paradigm for complex information-seeking tasks. However, current agents suffer from critical inefficiency: they conduct excessive searches as they cannot accurately judge when to stop searching and start answering. This stems from outcome-centric training that prioritize final results over the search process itself. We identify the root cause as misaligned decision boundaries, the threshold determining when accumulated information suffices to answer. This causes over-search (redundant searching despite sufficient knowledge) and under-search (premature termination yielding incorrect answers). To address these errors, we propose a comprehensive framework comprising two key components. First, we introduce causal intervention-based diagnosis that identifies boundary errors by comparing factual and counterfactual trajectories at each decision point. Second, we develop Decision Boundary Alignment for Deep Search agents (DAS), which constructs preference datasets from causal feedback and aligns policies via preference optimization. Experiments on public datasets demonstrate that decision boundary errors are pervasive across state-of-the-art agents. Our DAS method effectively calibrates these boundaries, mitigating both over-search and under-search to achieve substantial gains in accuracy and efficiency. Our code and data are publicly available at: github.com/Applied-Machine-Learning-Lab/WWW2026_DAS.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques or methods:

Causal intervention-based diagnosis

Decision Boundary Alignment for Deep Search agents (DAS)
Preference optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySAGE: Steerable Agentic Data Generation for Deep Search with Execution Feedback0.26PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.26SLIME: Stabilized Likelihood Implicit Margin Enforcement for Preference Optimization0.21PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.20Cold-Start Personalization via Training-Free Priors from Structured World Models0.19
Decision Boundary Alignment for Deep Search Agents (DAS)
Core Idea: Train search agents to correctly judge when to stop searching by constructing preference datasets from causal feedback and optimizing policies via preference-based learning.
Problem It Solves: Standard supervised learning (outcome-centric) trains agents to maximize final answer quality, but doesn’t penalize inefficient search processes. DAS aligns the decision boundary—the threshold at which the agent transitions from searching to answering—by making the agent prefer efficient trajectories with correct outcomes over inefficient ones.
How It Works:

Use causal intervention diagnosis (above) to identify over-search and under-search errors across a training corpus
Construct preference pairs:

Over-search case: Pair (trajectory with unnecessary search, trajectory with search removed) — prefer the shorter trajectory since both reach correct answer
Under-search case: Pair (premature termination, trajectory with additional search) — prefer the longer trajectory since only it reaches correct answer


Aggregate these preference pairs into a preference dataset D = {(y_inefficient, y_efficient, ≻)}
Apply preference optimization (e.g., DPO, IPO, or similar) to align the agent policy π(y_t | s_t) such that:

P(y_efficient | context) > P(y_inefficient | context)


Update the agent’s search-stopping criterion to match the learned preference boundary

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A["Factual Trajectories"] --> B["Causal Intervention<br/>Diagnosis"]
    B --> C["Preference Pairs:<br/>Efficient vs Inefficient"]
    C --> D["Preference Dataset"]
    D --> E["Preference<br/>Optimization"]
    E --> F["Aligned Policy π"]
    F --> G["Calibrated Decision<br/>Boundary"]

Where It’s Useful:

Training search/retrieval agents that must balance accuracy and latency
Multi-turn dialogue systems where excessive querying degrades user experience
Knowledge-intensive QA where search cost is high (API calls, latency)
Resource-constrained agentic systems (e.g., mobile, edge deployment)

Limitations / Caveats:

Preference optimization assumes a ranking loss (e.g., DPO) is available; may not work with all policy learning algorithms
Requires diverse training trajectories with varied over/under-search patterns; sparse error cases may lead to poor boundary calibration
Aligns decision boundary but doesn’t fix underlying retrieval quality; if search results are irrelevant, more searches won’t help
Transferability unclear: boundaries learned on one domain/task may not generalize to different query types or search engines

Internal Use Cases:

Extending Agentic Reasoning frameworks to optimize the “when to stop reasoning” decision point
Improving Graph-R1 agents to avoid redundant knowledge graph traversals while ensuring sufficient hops for reasoning quality
Calibrating search intensity in Web-Search Agent with Query Breakdown and Rerank systems where relevance + search cost must be traded off

literature/2026-02-04-Using-tournaments-.title: Using tournaments to calculate AUROC for zero-shot classification with LLMs
literature/2026-02-04-Using-tournaments-.content:
Using tournaments to calculate AUROC for zero-shot classification with LLMs
Source: S2:Findings of ACL. EMNLP. Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
Published: 2025
Rank: #8 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, RecSys, RAG
Abstract
Large language models perform surprisingly well on many zero-shot classification tasks, but are difficult to fairly compare to supervised classifiers due to the lack of a modifiable decision boundary. In this work, we propose and evaluate a method that transforms binary classification tasks into pairwise comparisons between instances within a dataset, using LLMs to produce relative rankings of those instances. Repeated pairwise comparisons can be used to score instances using the Elo rating system (used in chess and other competitions), inducing a confidence ordering over instances in a dataset. We evaluate scheduling algorithms for their ability to minimize comparisons, and show that our proposed algorithm leads to improved classification performance, while also providing more information than traditional zero-shot classification.
Key Techniques

Pairwise comparisons
Elo rating system
zero-shot classification
scheduling algorithms

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityCoDa: Constrained Generation based Data Augmentation for Low-Resource NLP0.15ForesightKV: Optimizing KV Cache Eviction for Reasoning Models by Learning Long-Term Contribution0.15CATTO: Balancing Preferences and Confidence in Language Models0.11ProtoQuant: Quantization of Prototypical Parts For General and Fine-Grained Image Classification0.11BitDance: Scaling Autoregressive Generative Models with Binary Tokens0.09
Tournament-Based AUROC for Zero-Shot LLM Classification
Core Idea: Convert zero-shot binary classification into pairwise instance comparisons, then use the Elo rating system to rank instances and compute AUROC, enabling proper evaluation of LLMs without requiring probability calibration or modifiable decision boundaries.
Problem It Solves: LLMs lack calibrated probabilities and adjustable decision thresholds for zero-shot classification, making it impossible to fairly compare them to supervised classifiers using standard metrics like AUROC that require ranking all instances by confidence.
How It Works:

Pairwise Tournament Setup: For a binary classification dataset, create pairwise comparisons asking the LLM “which instance is more likely to belong to the positive class?”
Elo Rating Calculation: Treat each comparison as a tournament match, updating Elo scores for instances based on comparison outcomes (winner gains points, loser loses points)
Ranking & AUROC: Final Elo scores induce a confidence ordering over all instances; compute AUROC by comparing this ranking against ground truth labels
Adaptive Scheduling: Use scheduling algorithms (e.g., Swiss system, skill-based matchmaking) to minimize number of comparisons needed while maintaining ranking accuracy

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Dataset Instances] --> B[Pairwise Comparison Scheduler]
    B --> C[LLM: Which is more likely positive?]
    C --> D[Elo Rating Updates]
    D --> E{More comparisons needed?}
    E -->|Yes| B
    E -->|No| F[Final Elo Scores = Confidence Ordering]
    F --> G[Compute AUROC vs Ground Truth]

Where It’s Useful:

Zero-shot classification evaluation where LLMs replace traditional classifiers
Ranking/preference learning tasks where pairwise comparisons are more natural than absolute scoring
Active learning scenarios where comparison budget is limited
Domains requiring interpretable confidence scores from LLMs

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires O(n log n) to O(n²) LLM calls depending on scheduling algorithm (expensive for large datasets)
Assumes transitivity in LLM preferences (A > B and B > C implies A > C), which may not hold
Elo ratings depend on initialization and comparison order (though Swiss scheduling mitigates this)
Only applicable to binary classification or tasks reducible to pairwise comparisons

Internal Use Cases:

Evaluate LLM-based rankers without relevance labels by comparing them in tournaments
Bootstrap training data for supervised rankers by using tournament-derived confidence scores as pseudo-labels
Quality control for LLM raters: detect inconsistent preferences by analyzing tournament transitivity violations
Hybrid ranking systems: combine Elo scores from LLM tournaments with traditional features for final ranking

literature/2026-02-04-VeSX-A-Framework-F.title: VeSX: A Framework Featured by Verification, Self-Correction and In-context Learning for Web Automation Tasks
literature/2026-02-04-VeSX-A-Framework-F.content:
VeSX: A Framework Featured by Verification, Self-Correction and In-context Learning for Web Automation Tasks
Source: OpenReview:Submitted to ICLR 2025
Published:
Rank: #4 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, RAG
Abstract
While large language models have achieved remarkable success in tasks such as reasoning and question answering, applying LLMs to interactive tasks like web automation remains challenging. In web automation, existing planning-execution workflow often faces limitations due to the infeasible subtasks. We propose VeSX, a framework designed to enhance subtask feasibility through verification, self-correction, and in-context learning. VeSX introduces three key improvements: (1) subgoal-guided verification, which verifies the execution results of subtasks based on the preset subgoals; (2) hierarchical self-correction, which combines reflection and replanning, targeting to self-correct mistakes in both planning and execution phases; (3) exemplar bank, which improves in-context learning by partitioning execution trajectories and heuristically generating metadata for exemplars. We evaluate VeSX on WebArena benchmark and achieve the state-of-the-art average success rate of 0.34, which significantly outperforms existing methods without human guidance on all five scenarios.
Key Techniques

Subgoal-guided verification
hierarchical self-correction
exemplar bank

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.23In-Context Reinforcement Learning From Suboptimal Historical Data0.17DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.16DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.15Reliability by Design: Quantifying and Eliminating Fabrication Risk in LLMs0.14
VeSX (Verification, Self-Correction and In-context Learning)
Core Idea: A three-component framework that enhances LLM-based web automation by verifying subtask completion against subgoals, hierarchically correcting planning and execution errors through reflection and replanning, and improving in-context learning via trajectory partitioning and metadata-enriched exemplar retrieval.
Problem It Solves: Addresses the infeasibility of subtasks in LLM-driven web automation where traditional planning-execution workflows fail due to incorrect plans, failed executions, or inability to learn from past trajectories effectively.
How It Works:

Subgoal-Guided Verification: After each subtask execution, verify whether preset subgoals are achieved by comparing expected vs. actual web states
Hierarchical Self-Correction: When verification fails, apply two-level correction: (a) reflection to identify whether the error was in planning or execution, (b) replanning to generate corrected subtasks or execution strategies
