Exemplar Bank with Metadata: Partition successful execution trajectories into reusable segments, attach heuristically-generated metadata (task type, domain, actions), and retrieve relevant exemplars for in-context learning during new task planning

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Task Input] --> B[Planner with Exemplars]
    B --> C[Subtask + Subgoal]
    C --> D[Executor]
    D --> E[Verification]
    E -->|Success| F[Next Subtask]
    E -->|Failure| G[Reflection]
    G --> H[Replanning]
    H --> C
    F --> I[Task Complete]

Where It’s Useful:

Web automation and browser-based task completion (form filling, navigation, data extraction)
Interactive agent tasks requiring multi-step planning with environment feedback
Robotic process automation (RPA) where subtask verification is critical
Any LLM-driven workflow requiring error recovery and plan adaptation

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires well-defined subgoals to be specified upfront for verification to work
Reflection and replanning add computational overhead and latency to task completion
Exemplar bank quality depends on trajectory partitioning heuristics and metadata accuracy
Evaluated only on WebArena benchmark; generalization to other interactive environments unverified

Internal Use Cases:

Automated testing workflows for ML pipelines where verification of intermediate outputs against expected states is needed
LLM-based data annotation systems that require self-correction when labeling tasks fail quality checks
Multi-stage recommendation system debugging where planner-executor loops need verification and replanning (e.g., feature engineering → model training → evaluation)
Agent-based retrieval systems where failed queries can trigger reflection and query reformulation

literature/2026-02-05-BEHAV-E-You-are-No.title: BEHAV-E! You are Not Just a Number to Us, but an R2048 Embedding
literature/2026-02-05-BEHAV-E-You-are-No.content:
BEHAV-E! You are Not Just a Number to Us, but an R2048 Embedding
Source: S2:Proceedings of the Recommender Systems 2025
Published: 2025
Rank: #10 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, LLM, Search, RecSys
Code: github.com/knife982000/RecSys2025Challenge
Abstract
Universal user representations hold the promise of reducing the need for task-specific modelling in personalized systems, enabling a single embedding to support a wide range of predictive tasks such as churn prediction, product propensity, and category-level recommendations. In this paper, we introduce BEHAV-E (Behavioural Embedding via Hybrid Action Variational Encoder), a self-supervised architecture that learns rich, 2048-dimensional user embeddings from multi-event behavioural data. BEHAV-E integrates both temporal and semantic signals by combining kernel density estimation (KDE) of user activity timelines, product and category embeddings via shared embedding bags, and an LSTM-based autoencoder for modelling search query semantics. We evaluate our approach within the RecSys Universal Behavioural modelling Challenge. Our results demonstrate that BEHAV-E effectively captures complex, multi-modal user behaviour in a compact and transferable embedding format. This paper provides an overview of the approach we used as team DArgk1 for the ACM RecSys Challenge 2025. Our submission achieved the 13th rank and sum of scores of 4.5713 (borda count 483) in the competition academia-track final results. We release our source code at: github.com/knife982000/RecSys2025Challenge
Key Techniques

Kernel Density Estimation (KDE)
Shared Embedding Bags
LSTM-based Autoencoder
Self-Supervised Learning

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityOne Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral Profiles0.29R2LED: Equipping Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs for CTR Prediction0.20The Case for Semantic Tokens in Modern Ranking Systems, How Embedding Size Affects Dense Retrieval, and More!0.20Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.19DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.18
BEHAV-E (Behavioural Embedding via Hybrid Action Variational Encoder)
Core Idea: A self-supervised architecture that learns universal 2048-dimensional user embeddings from multi-event behavioral data by fusing temporal activity patterns (via kernel density estimation), categorical item/category embeddings, and semantic search query representations (via LSTM autoencoder).
Problem It Solves: Traditional user representations either require task-specific modeling for each downstream prediction task (churn, propensity, recommendations) or fail to capture the multi-modal nature of user behavior (temporal patterns, item interactions, search intent), limiting transferability and expressiveness.
How It Works:

Temporal encoding: Apply kernel density estimation (KDE) to user activity timelines to capture when users are active and behavioral rhythm patterns
Categorical encoding: Use shared embedding bags to encode product IDs and category IDs from user interaction history into dense vectors
Semantic encoding: Feed user search queries into an LSTM-based autoencoder to extract latent representations of search intent and textual preferences
Fusion: Combine temporal (KDE), categorical (embedding bags), and semantic (LSTM) signals into a single 2048-dimensional embedding vector per user
Self-supervised training: Train the variational encoder to reconstruct behavioral signals without task-specific labels, enabling universal representation learning

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[User Behavioral Data] --> B[KDE Module<br/>Temporal Patterns]
    A --> C[Embedding Bags<br/>Products & Categories]
    A --> D[LSTM Autoencoder<br/>Search Queries]
    B --> E[Fusion Layer]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[2048-dim<br/>User Embedding]

Where It’s Useful:

Cross-task personalization systems requiring a single user representation for multiple downstream tasks (churn, propensity scoring, recommendations)
Cold-start scenarios where transferable embeddings enable quick adaptation to new prediction tasks
Large-scale recommendation platforms needing compact yet expressive user representations

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires diverse behavioral signals (activity timestamps, item interactions, search queries) to be effective; degrades with sparse or single-modality data
Fixed 2048-dimensional embedding may be overparameterized for simpler tasks or underparameterized for extremely complex user behavior
Self-supervised training without explicit task supervision may not optimize for specific downstream metrics without fine-tuning

Internal Use Cases:

Foundation model user towers: BEHAV-E-style fusion could replace or augment static user embeddings in two-tower retrieval models by encoding temporal, categorical, and semantic signals into a single dense representation
Cross-domain knowledge transfer: Universal embeddings trained on one product vertical (e.g., e-commerce) could initialize models for related domains (e.g., content recommendations)
Session-aware ranking: KDE temporal encoding could complement session partitioning strategies by explicitly modeling user activity rhythms and engagement patterns

literature/2026-02-05-BPDQ-Bit-Plane-Dec.title: BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models
literature/2026-02-05-BPDQ-Bit-Plane-Dec.content:
BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-04
Rank: #10 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Large language model (LLM) inference is often bounded by memory footprint and memory bandwidth in resource-constrained deployments, making quantization a fundamental technique for efficient serving. While post-training quantization (PTQ) maintains high fidelity at 4-bit, it deteriorates at 2-3 bits. Fundamentally, existing methods enforce a shape-invariant quantization grid (e.g., the fixed uniform intervals of UINT2) for each group, severely restricting the feasible set for error minimization. To address this, we propose Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization (BPDQ), which constructs a variable quantization grid via bit-planes and scalar coefficients, and iteratively refines them using approximate second-order information while progressively compensating quantization errors to minimize output discrepancy. In the 2-bit regime, BPDQ enables serving Qwen2.5-72B on a single RTX 3090 with 83.85% GSM8K accuracy (vs. 90.83% at 16-bit). Moreover, we provide theoretical analysis showing that the variable grid expands the feasible set, and that the quantization process consistently aligns with the optimization objective in Hessian-induced geometry. Code: github.com/KingdalfGoodman/BPDQ.
Key Techniques

Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization
variable quantization grid construction
iterative refinement with second-order information

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.92OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization0.30Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems0.291-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization0.28Quant VideoGen: Auto-Regressive Long Video Generation via 2-Bit KV-Cache Quantization0.25
Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization (BPDQ)
Core Idea: Construct a variable quantization grid via bit-plane decomposition and scalar coefficients instead of fixed uniform intervals, enabling more flexible error minimization for extreme low-bit (2-3 bit) LLM quantization.
Problem It Solves: Existing post-training quantization (PTQ) methods enforce shape-invariant grids (e.g., fixed UINT2 intervals) that severely restrict the feasible optimization space, causing quality deterioration below 4 bits. This memory-bandwidth bottleneck prevents efficient serving of large models on consumer hardware.
How It Works:

Variable Grid Construction: Decompose quantization levels into bit-planes with learnable scalar coefficients rather than uniform intervals (e.g., levels can be [0, α₁, α₂, α₁+α₂] instead of [0, 1, 2, 3])
Iterative Refinement: Use approximate second-order Hessian information to optimize both the bit-plane coefficients and quantization assignments jointly
Progressive Error Compensation: Iteratively compensate quantization errors at each layer to minimize cumulative output discrepancy across the network

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[FP16 Weights] --> B[Bit-Plane Decomposition]
    B --> C[Scalar Coefficient Init]
    C --> D[Hessian-Guided Optimization]
    D --> E[Error Compensation]
    E --> F{Converged?}
    F -->|No| D
    F -->|Yes| G[2-3 bit Quantized Model]

Where It’s Useful:

Serving 70B+ parameter LLMs on consumer GPUs (e.g., RTX 3090 with 24GB VRAM)
Memory-bandwidth constrained inference deployments
Extreme low-bit (2-3 bit) post-training quantization scenarios
Edge device deployment of large language models

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires Hessian approximation computation during quantization (higher PTQ cost vs. naive uniform quantization)
Effectiveness demonstrated primarily in 2-3 bit regime; diminishing returns at 4+ bits where uniform quantization already works well
Variable grid introduces irregular memory access patterns that may not leverage hardware INT2/INT4 optimizations as efficiently

Internal Use Cases:

LLM serving optimization: Deploy larger embedding models or rankers on limited GPU memory by quantizing to 2-3 bits while maintaining accuracy
Multi-model serving: Pack multiple 2-bit quantized models in single GPU for A/B testing or ensemble ranking
Hybrid precision systems: Use 2-bit BPDQ for early-stage retrieval/filtering, reserve FP16 for final reranking stages

literature/2026-02-05-Beyond-Tokens-Sema.title: Beyond Tokens: Semantic-Aware Speculative Decoding for Efficient Inference by Probing Internal States
literature/2026-02-05-Beyond-Tokens-Sema.content:
Beyond Tokens: Semantic-Aware Speculative Decoding for Efficient Inference by Probing Internal States
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #5 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across many tasks but suffer from high inference latency due to autoregressive decoding. The issue is exacerbated in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs), which generate lengthy chains of thought. While speculative decoding accelerates inference by drafting and verifying multiple tokens in parallel, existing methods operate at the token level and ignore semantic equivalence (i.e., different token sequences expressing the same meaning), leading to inefficient rejections. We propose SemanticSpec, a semantic-aware speculative decoding framework that verifies entire semantic sequences instead of tokens. SemanticSpec introduces a semantic probability estimation mechanism that probes the model’s internal hidden states to assess the likelihood of generating sequences with specific meanings. Experiments on four benchmarks show that SemanticSpec achieves up to 2.7x speedup on DeepSeekR1-32B and 2.1x on QwQ-32B, consistently outperforming token-level and sequence-level baselines in both efficiency and effectiveness.
Key Techniques

Semantic-aware speculative decoding
semantic probability estimation from hidden states
chain-of-thought verification at semantic level

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBeyond Tokens: Semantic-Aware Speculative Decoding for Efficient Inference by Probing Internal States0.81DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.38DART: Diffusion-Inspired Speculative Decoding for Fast LLM Inference0.38PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models0.36SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration0.36
SemanticSpec (Semantic-Aware Speculative Decoding)
Core Idea: Verifies entire semantic sequences instead of individual tokens during speculative decoding by probing the target model’s internal hidden states to estimate the probability of generating sequences with specific meanings, enabling acceptance of semantically equivalent but token-wise different draft sequences.
Problem It Solves: Traditional speculative decoding operates at the token level and rejects drafts that are semantically equivalent but use different tokens (e.g., “4” vs “four”), leading to inefficient verification and low acceptance rates, especially problematic for Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) that generate lengthy chains of thought.
How It Works:

Draft Generation: A smaller draft model generates candidate token sequences using standard autoregressive decoding
Semantic Clustering: Group draft tokens into semantic units (e.g., complete numbers, phrases) rather than verifying token-by-token
Hidden State Probing: Extract internal hidden states from the target model at sequence boundaries and pass them through trained probe classifiers to estimate P(semantic_meaning | context)
Semantic Verification: Accept the entire semantic sequence if the probed probability of that meaning exceeds a threshold, even if exact tokens differ
Parallel Verification: Process multiple semantic units in a single forward pass through the target model, leveraging batch computation

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Context] --> B[Draft Model]
    B --> C[Semantic Grouping]
    A --> D[Target Model Forward Pass]
    D --> E[Hidden State Extraction]
    E --> F[Semantic Probe Classifiers]
    C --> G{Semantic Match?}
    F --> G
    G -->|Yes| H[Accept Sequence]
    G -->|No| I[Reject & Rollback]

Where It’s Useful:

Accelerating Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) like DeepSeek-R1 and QwQ that generate verbose chain-of-thought reasoning
Mathematical reasoning tasks where numeric expressions have many equivalent forms (“2+2=4” vs “four” vs “2*2”)
Long-form generation where semantic consistency matters more than exact token matching
Multi-turn dialogue systems requiring fast response times with large models

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires training probe classifiers on the target model’s hidden states, adding upfront overhead and model-specific tuning
Semantic clustering heuristics may be domain-specific (numbers, dates, entities) and require careful design
Probing accuracy degrades if training data distribution differs from inference-time semantic patterns
Still requires draft model to generate semantically plausible candidates; poor draft quality limits gains
Memory overhead for storing and processing hidden states during verification

Internal Use Cases:

Accelerating LLM-based reasoning pipelines that generate structured outputs (SQL queries, code, mathematical proofs) where semantic equivalence is common
Ranking/reranking systems using LLMs where multiple phrasings of the same concept should be treated identically
RecSys explanation generation where speed matters but exact wording flexibility is acceptable (e.g., “users who bought X” vs “customers purchasing X”)
Reducing latency in production LLM APIs by accepting semantically equivalent completions from smaller draft models while maintaining quality

literature/2026-02-05-DOS-Dual-Flow-Orth.title: DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan
literature/2026-02-05-DOS-Dual-Flow-Orth.content:
DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-04
Rank: #1 (score: 0.91, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Semantic IDs serve as a key component in generative recommendation systems. They not only incorporate open-world knowledge from large language models (LLMs) but also compress the semantic space to reduce generation difficulty. However, existing methods suffer from two major limitations: (1) the lack of contextual awareness in generation tasks leads to a gap between the Semantic ID codebook space and the generation space, resulting in suboptimal recommendations; and (2) suboptimal quantization methods exacerbate semantic loss in LLMs. To address these issues, we propose Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs (DOS) method. Specifically, DOS employs a user-item dual flow-framework that leverages collaborative signals to align the Semantic ID codebook space with the generation space. Furthermore, we introduce an orthogonal residual quantization scheme that rotates the semantic space to an appropriate orientation, thereby maximizing semantic preservation. Extensive offline experiments and online A/B testing demonstrate the effectiveness of DOS. The proposed method has been successfully deployed in Meituan’s mobile application, serving hundreds of millions of users.
Key Techniques

Dual-flow framework for Semantic IDs
Orthogonal residual quantization scheme
Collaborative signals alignment

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityRethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs0.44GLASS: A Generative Recommender for Long-sequence Modeling via SID-Tier and Semantic Search0.41R2LED: Equipping Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs for CTR Prediction0.40End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.39Rethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs0.39
Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs (DOS)
Core Idea: A dual-flow framework that uses collaborative signals from both user and item representations to align semantic ID codebook space with generation space, combined with orthogonal residual quantization to minimize semantic loss during ID compression.
Problem It Solves: Existing semantic ID methods suffer from (1) lack of contextual awareness causing misalignment between the codebook space and generation space, and (2) suboptimal quantization methods that lose semantic information when compressing LLM embeddings into discrete IDs.
How It Works:

Dual-Flow Architecture: Simultaneously encode user historical interactions and candidate items through separate flows, generating user representations and item semantic IDs that incorporate collaborative filtering signals rather than pure content embeddings
Orthogonal Residual Quantization: Apply rotation transformations to the semantic embedding space before residual vector quantization (RQ-VAE), orienting vectors to maximize orthogonality between codebook entries and minimize reconstruction error
Context-Aware Codebook Learning: Train the semantic ID codebook jointly with user-item interaction data, ensuring generated IDs are optimized for the downstream recommendation generation task rather than pure semantic reconstruction

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    U[User History] --> UE[User Encoder]
    I[Item Features] --> IE[Item Encoder]
    UE --> CS[Collaborative Signals]
    IE --> CS
    CS --> OQ[Orthogonal Rotation]
    OQ --> RQ[Residual Quantization]
    RQ --> SID[Semantic IDs]
    UE --> GEN[LLM Generator]
    SID --> GEN
    GEN --> REC[Recommendations]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale industrial recommendation systems requiring semantic item representations (e.g., food delivery, e-commerce)
Generative recommendation models that need to compress items into discrete tokens for LLM-based generation
Systems requiring interpretable item IDs that preserve both semantic and collaborative information
Cold-start scenarios where semantic similarity helps with new item recommendations

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires joint training of user/item encoders with the quantization codebook, increasing training complexity
Orthogonal rotation adds computational overhead during encoding
Effectiveness may depend on sufficient collaborative signal density (user-item interactions)
Codebook size trades off between semantic expressiveness and generation difficulty

Internal Use Cases:

Feed Ranking: Generate semantic IDs for content items that encode both content features and user engagement patterns, enabling LLM-based ranking models to better capture user preferences
Sequential Recommendation: Use dual-flow architecture to create context-aware item IDs that understand position in user session sequences
Cross-Domain Transfer: Orthogonal quantization could improve semantic ID stability when transferring learned representations across different recommendation domains (e.g., reels → stories)
Vocabulary Efficiency: Apply to constrained decoding scenarios where minimizing codebook size while preserving semantics is critical for generation latency

literature/2026-02-05-Decomposing-Query-.title: Decomposing Query-Key Feature Interactions Using Contrastive Covariances
literature/2026-02-05-Decomposing-Query-.content:
Decomposing Query-Key Feature Interactions Using Contrastive Covariances
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-04
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG, Embeddings, Search, Transformer, LLM
Abstract
Despite the central role of attention heads in Transformers, we lack tools to understand why a model attends to a particular token. To address this, we study the query-key (QK) space — the bilinear joint embedding space between queries and keys. We present a contrastive covariance method to decompose the QK space into low-rank, human-interpretable components. It is when features in keys and queries align in these low-rank subspaces that high attention scores are produced. We first study our method both analytically and empirically in a simplified setting. We then apply our method to large language models to identify human-interpretable QK subspaces for categorical semantic features and binding features. Finally, we demonstrate how attention scores can be attributed to our identified features.
Key Techniques

Query-Key (QK) space analysis
Contrastive covariance method
Low-rank decomposition
Attention score attribution

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.28HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.28FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion0.27SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.27SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.26
Contrastive Covariance Decomposition for Query-Key Space
Core Idea: Decompose the bilinear query-key (QK) space of attention heads into low-rank, human-interpretable subspaces by computing contrastive covariances between features that produce high vs. low attention scores.
Problem It Solves: Attention mechanisms lack interpretability at the feature level—we can see which tokens receive high attention but not why specific query and key features align to produce those attention scores.
How It Works:

Partition tokens by attention: For a given attention head, separate key tokens into high-attention and low-attention groups based on their attention scores from a query
Compute contrastive covariance: Calculate the difference between covariance matrices of high-attention and low-attention key features, weighted by their respective attention distributions
Low-rank decomposition: Apply eigendecomposition or SVD to the contrastive covariance matrix to extract dominant subspaces (eigenvectors with largest eigenvalues)
Identify interpretable features: Project query and key representations onto these subspaces to identify which semantic features (e.g., categorical properties, binding relationships) align to produce high attention

Where It’s Useful:

Interpretability analysis of transformer-based language models
Debugging attention mechanisms in multi-head attention architectures
Feature attribution for attention scores in question-answering and reasoning tasks
Understanding specialized attention heads (e.g., induction heads, previous token heads)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires sufficient data to compute stable covariance estimates across attention distributions
Low-rank approximation may miss higher-order or nonlinear feature interactions
Interpretability of identified subspaces depends on the semantic coherence of input features

Internal Use Cases:

Analyzing which product/item features drive attention in cross-encoders for ranking (e.g., price vs. category vs. brand alignment)
Understanding user-query-item attention in personalized recommendation systems
Debugging why certain query-document pairs receive high relevance scores in neural IR models
Identifying binding features between user context and candidate items in multi-head attention layers

literature/2026-02-05-Diffusion-Generati.title: Diffusion Generative Recommendation with Continuous Tokens
literature/2026-02-05-Diffusion-Generati.content:
Diffusion Generative Recommendation with Continuous Tokens
Source: S2:RecSys
Published: 2025
Rank: #11 (score: 0.91, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Recent advances in generative artificial intelligence, particularly large language models (LLMs), have opened new opportunities for enhancing recommender systems (RecSys). Most existing LLM-based RecSys approaches operate in a discrete space, using vector-quantized tokenizers to align with the inherent discrete nature of language models. However, these quantization methods often result in lossy tokenization and suboptimal learning, primarily due to inaccurate gradient propagation caused by the non-differentiable argmin operation in standard vector quantization. Inspired by the emerging trend of embracing continuous tokens in language models, we propose ContRec, a novel framework that seamlessly integrates continuous tokens into LLM-based RecSys. Specifically, ContRec consists of two key modules: a sigma-VAE Tokenizer, which encodes users/items with continuous tokens; and a Dispersive Diffusion module, which captures implicit user preference. The tokenizer is trained with a continuous Variational Auto-Encoder (VAE) objective, where three effective techniques are adopted to avoid representation collapse. By conditioning on the previously generated tokens of the LLM backbone during user modeling, the Dispersive Diffusion module performs a conditional diffusion process with a novel Dispersive Loss, enabling high-quality user preference generation through next-token diffusion. Finally, ContRec leverages both the textual reasoning output from the LLM and the latent representations produced by the diffusion model for Top-K item retrieval, thereby delivering comprehensive recommendation results. Extensive experiments on four datasets demonstrate that ContRec consistently outperforms both traditional and SOTA LLM-based recommender systems. Our results highlight the potential of continuous tokenization and generative modeling for advancing the next generation of recommender systems.
Key Techniques

sigma-VAE Tokenizer
Dispersive Diffusion module
continuous Variational Auto-Encoder objective
conditional diffusion process with Dispersive Loss

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDiffusion In Diffusion: Reclaiming Global Coherence in Semi-Autoregressive Diffusion0.41Geometry-Free Conditional Diffusion Modeling for Solving the Inverse Electrocardiography Problem0.35BitDance: Scaling Autoregressive Generative Models with Binary Tokens0.34FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion0.28Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.24
ContRec (Continuous Token Diffusion Generative Recommendation)
Core Idea: Replace discrete vector-quantized tokens with continuous VAE-encoded representations and use conditional diffusion modeling to generate user preferences in LLM-based recommender systems.
Problem It Solves: Existing LLM-based recommender systems rely on vector quantization (VQ) for tokenization, which introduces lossy compression and suboptimal learning due to non-differentiable argmin operations that break gradient flow. This limits the quality of user/item representations and preference modeling.
How It Works:

Sigma-VAE Tokenizer: Encodes users and items into continuous latent representations using a Variational Auto-Encoder with three regularization techniques to prevent representation collapse, enabling differentiable end-to-end training
Dispersive Diffusion Module: Performs conditional diffusion conditioned on previously generated LLM tokens during user modeling, using a novel Dispersive Loss to generate high-quality user preference representations through next-token diffusion
Hybrid Retrieval: Combines textual reasoning outputs from the LLM backbone with latent representations from the diffusion model for Top-K item retrieval

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[User/Item Data] --> B[Sigma-VAE Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Continuous Tokens]
    C --> D[LLM Backbone]
    D --> E[Dispersive Diffusion]
    E --> F[User Preference Latents]
    D --> G[Textual Reasoning]
    F --> H[Hybrid Top-K Retrieval]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Recommendations]

Where It’s Useful:

Cold-start recommendation scenarios where continuous representations provide richer semantic encoding
LLM-based recommender systems requiring differentiable end-to-end training
Multi-modal recommendation combining behavioral signals with textual reasoning
Next-generation generative RecSys leveraging both discrete (text) and continuous (latent) representations

Limitations / Caveats:

Increased computational cost from VAE encoding and diffusion sampling compared to discrete VQ approaches
Requires careful tuning of three anti-collapse techniques in sigma-VAE to avoid representation degeneration
Diffusion inference latency may limit real-time serving without distillation or caching strategies

Internal Use Cases:

Upgrading vector-quantized RecSys to continuous token representations for improved gradient flow and representation quality
Applying Dispersive Diffusion to model implicit user preferences in next-token prediction for behavioral sequence modeling
Hybrid retrieval architectures combining LLM reasoning (explainability) with diffusion-generated embeddings (ranking quality)
Transfer learning from pre-trained diffusion models for cold-start user/item modeling

literature/2026-02-05-Efficient-Autoregr.title: Efficient Autoregressive Video Diffusion with Dummy Head
literature/2026-02-05-Efficient-Autoregr.content:
Efficient Autoregressive Video Diffusion with Dummy Head
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-28
Rank: #8 (score: 0.61, max_sim: 0.79)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The autoregressive video diffusion model has recently gained considerable research interest due to its causal modeling and iterative denoising. In this work, we identify that the multi-head self-attention in these models under-utilizes historical frames: approximately 25% heads attend almost exclusively to the current frame, and discarding their KV caches incurs only minor performance degradation. Building upon this, we propose Dummy Forcing, a simple yet effective method to control context accessibility across different heads. Specifically, the proposed heterogeneous memory allocation reduces head-wise context redundancy, accompanied by dynamic head programming to adaptively classify head types. Moreover, we develop a context packing technique to achieve more aggressive cache compression. Without additional training, our Dummy Forcing delivers up to 2.0x speedup over the baseline, supporting video generation at 24.3 FPS with less than 0.5% quality drop. Project page is available at csguoh.github.io/project/DummyForcing/.
Key Techniques

Dummy Forcing
heterogeneous memory allocation
dynamic head programming
context packing

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityQuant VideoGen: Auto-Regressive Long Video Generation via 2-Bit KV-Cache Quantization0.35Flow caching for autoregressive video generation0.32Predicting Future Utility: Global Combinatorial Optimization for Task-Agnostic KV Cache Eviction0.31Light Forcing: Accelerating Autoregressive Video Diffusion via Sparse Attention0.30FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion0.29
Dummy Forcing
Core Idea: A training-free optimization for autoregressive video diffusion models that identifies and exploits the fact that ~25% of attention heads focus almost exclusively on the current frame, enabling heterogeneous KV cache allocation across heads to reduce memory and increase throughput.
Problem It Solves: Autoregressive video diffusion models suffer from significant computational overhead due to redundant attention patterns—many heads unnecessarily cache historical frame information they rarely use, leading to memory bottlenecks and slow generation speeds.
How It Works:

Head Classification: Analyze attention patterns in multi-head self-attention to identify which heads attend primarily to current frames vs. historical frames
Heterogeneous Memory Allocation: Allocate reduced KV cache (or no cache) to “current-frame-focused” heads (~25%), while maintaining full cache for history-dependent heads
Dynamic Head Programming: Adaptively classify head types during generation to adjust cache strategies
Context Packing: Apply aggressive cache compression techniques by exploiting the identified redundancy patterns

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Historical Frames] --> B[Multi-Head Attention]
    C[Current Frame] --> B
    B --> D{Head Classifier}
    D -->|~75% heads| E[Full KV Cache]
    D -->|~25% heads| F[Minimal/No Cache]
    E --> G[Autoregressive Denoising]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Generated Frame]

Where It’s Useful:

Autoregressive video generation models requiring real-time or near-real-time performance
Resource-constrained deployment scenarios for video diffusion models
Long-video generation where cumulative KV cache grows prohibitively large

Limitations / Caveats:

Assumes heterogeneous attention patterns exist (may not generalize to all video diffusion architectures)
Head classification overhead may reduce benefits for very short videos
Quality degradation (~0.5%) may be unacceptable for certain high-fidelity applications

Internal Use Cases:

Could inspire similar head-level cache optimization in long-context video recommendation models (e.g., YouTube Shorts user sequence models)
Heterogeneous memory allocation principle applicable to transformer-based sequential recommendation with long user histories
Dynamic programming approach could inform adaptive context window strategies in multi-interest user modeling

literature/2026-02-05-Enforcing-Monotoni.title: Enforcing Monotonic Progress in Legal Cross-Examination: Preventing Long-Horizon Stagnation in LLM-Based Inquiry
literature/2026-02-05-Enforcing-Monotoni.content:
Enforcing Monotonic Progress in Legal Cross-Examination: Preventing Long-Horizon Stagnation in LLM-Based Inquiry
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-04
Rank: #3 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG, LLM
Abstract
Large language models (LLMs) exhibit impressive linguistic fluency but struggle to reliably complete long-horizon tasks under explicit procedural constraints. In legal cross-examination, purely proba-bilistic generation often maintains behavioral coherence while failing to ensure procedural advancement. We characterize this failure as procedural stagnation and propose Soft-FSM, a neuro-symbolic architecture that enforces monotonic progress over accumulated Key Information Units (KIUs) via an external deterministic state controller. Experiments on three real-world Taiwanese criminal homicide cases show that baseline methods collapse below 40% completeness, while Soft-FSM consistently achieves over 97% with near-zero redundancy. These results suggest that, in such domains, reliable task completion cannot be guaranteed by emergent LLM behavior alone, and can be reliably enforced through explicit and verifiable external state control.
Key Techniques

Soft-FSM architecture
Key Information Units (KIUs)
neuro-symbolic approach
deterministic state controller

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPABU: Progress-Aware Belief Update for Efficient LLM Agents0.16AgentLongBench: A Controllable Long Benchmark For Long-Contexts Agents via Environment Rollouts0.11Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.10Quecto-V1: Empirical Analysis of 8-bit Quantized Small Language Models for On-Device Legal Retrieval0.10Paying Less Generalization Tax: A Cross-Domain Generalization Study of RL Training for LLM Agents0.10
Soft-FSM (Soft Finite State Machine)
Core Idea: A neuro-symbolic architecture that enforces monotonic progress in long-horizon LLM tasks by combining probabilistic LLM generation with an external deterministic state controller that tracks accumulated Key Information Units (KIUs).
Problem It Solves: Addresses procedural stagnation in LLMs—where models maintain linguistic coherence but fail to make verifiable progress toward task completion, especially in constrained domains like legal cross-examination that require explicit advancement through mandatory information-gathering steps.
How It Works:

Define task as a set of Key Information Units (KIUs) that must be collected through dialogue
LLM generates candidate utterances/questions in natural language
External deterministic FSM controller validates each utterance, updates KIU state only when new information is verifiably obtained, and enforces monotonic progress (no regression to already-covered topics)
System terminates only when all KIUs are collected, preventing premature completion

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[LLM Generator] --> B[Candidate Utterance]
    B --> C[FSM Controller]
    C --> D{New KIU?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Update State]
    D -->|No| F[Reject/Regenerate]
    E --> G[Next KIU Required]
    G --> A
    C --> H[KIU Tracker: 0/N → N/N]

Where It’s Useful:

Legal proceedings requiring structured information elicitation (depositions, witness examination)
Medical diagnosis workflows with mandatory symptom/history checkpoints
Compliance interviews and regulatory audits with required coverage criteria
Structured customer service interactions with resolution verification

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires explicit pre-definition of all KIUs (domain knowledge engineering overhead)
May reduce conversational naturalness due to strict procedural enforcement
KIU verification logic needs domain-specific implementation
Not suitable for open-ended creative tasks where procedural constraints are undesirable

Internal Use Cases:

User onboarding flows: Ensure LLM assistants collect all required user preferences/information before proceeding to recommendations (preventing incomplete profiles in RecSys)
Content moderation pipelines: Guarantee all policy violation criteria are checked before final ranking decisions
Debugging assistants: Enforce systematic root-cause analysis (collect logs → reproduce → isolate → fix) rather than jumping to solutions
A/B test analysis: Ensure all statistical validity checks (sample size, AA test, metric movement) are verified before shipping ranking changes

literature/2026-02-05-From-Competition-t.title: From Competition to Collaboration: Designing Sustainable Mechanisms Between LLMs and Online Forums
literature/2026-02-05-From-Competition-t.content:
From Competition to Collaboration: Designing Sustainable Mechanisms Between LLMs and Online Forums
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-04
Rank: #2 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG, RecSys, LLM
Abstract
While Generative AI (GenAI) systems draw users away from (Q&A) forums, they also depend on the very data those forums produce to improve their performance. Addressing this paradox, we propose a framework of sequential interaction, in which a GenAI system proposes questions to a forum that can publish some of them. Our framework captures several intricacies of such a collaboration, including non-monetary exchanges, asymmetric information, and incentive misalignment. We bring the framework to life through comprehensive, data-driven simulations using real Stack Exchange data and commonly used LLMs. We demonstrate the incentive misalignment empirically, yet show that players can achieve roughly half of the utility in an ideal full-information scenario. Our results highlight the potential for sustainable collaboration that preserves effective knowledge sharing between AI systems and human knowledge platforms.
Key Techniques

Sequential interaction framework
data-driven simulations with Stack Exchange data
LLM-based question proposal system
incentive alignment analysis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityKDD 2025 Workshop on Inference Optimization for Generative AI0.16SAGE: Steerable Agentic Data Generation for Deep Search with Execution Feedback0.10Cold-Start Personalization via Training-Free Priors from Structured World Models0.10Dynamics of Human-AI Collective Knowledge on the Web: A Scalable Model and Insights for Sustainable Growth0.10MergeMix: Optimizing Mid-Training Data Mixtures via Learnable Model Merging0.09
Sequential Interaction Framework for LLM-Forum Collaboration
Core Idea: A game-theoretic mechanism design framework where LLMs propose questions to Q&A forums, which selectively publish them to balance their own utility (community engagement, content quality) against the LLM’s utility (training data acquisition), enabling sustainable knowledge exchange despite incentive misalignment.
Problem It Solves: Addresses the paradox where GenAI systems cannibalize traffic from Q&A forums while depending on forum data for improvement, leading to unsustainable knowledge ecosystems where forums lose incentive to generate the high-quality content that LLMs need.
How It Works:

Question Proposal: LLM generates and proposes questions to the forum based on its current knowledge gaps and training objectives
Strategic Selection: Forum evaluates proposals using a utility function balancing engagement potential, content diversity, and community value, then publishes a subset
Data Exchange: Published questions generate human answers, which become training data for the LLM in the next iteration
Iterative Optimization: Both parties adapt their strategies over time—LLM learns which questions forums value, forum learns which questions improve its ecosystem

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[LLM Knowledge State] --> B[Question Generator]
    B --> C[Forum Utility Evaluator]
    C --> D{Publish?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Community Answers]
    D -->|No| F[Rejected Pool]
    E --> G[Training Data]
    G --> A
    E --> H[Forum Engagement Metrics]
    H --> C

Where It’s Useful:

Sustainable AI-human knowledge platforms (Stack Overflow, Reddit, Quora)
Active learning systems where data sources have agency and costs
Multi-stakeholder data marketplaces with non-monetary exchanges
Collaborative filtering scenarios with strategic data providers

Limitations / Caveats:

Achieves only ~50% of full-information optimal utility due to asymmetric information
Requires forums to accurately model question value prospectively (before answers exist)
Assumes LLMs can generate answerable, forum-appropriate questions
Does not address long-term equilibrium stability or forum sustainability thresholds

Internal Use Cases:

Recommendation cold-start: Design mechanisms where users are incentivized to answer system-proposed preference questions that maximize catalog coverage
Human-in-the-loop ranking: Strategic query selection for human labeling that balances annotation cost with model uncertainty reduction
LLM training data acquisition: Active learning frameworks that negotiate with data providers (users, platforms) who have strategic interests beyond model performance

literature/2026-02-05-HySparse-A-Hybrid-.title: HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing
literature/2026-02-05-HySparse-A-Hybrid-.content:
HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #4 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
This work introduces Hybrid Sparse Attention (HySparse), a new architecture that interleaves each full attention layer with several sparse attention layers. While conceptually simple, HySparse strategically derives each sparse layer’s token selection and KV caches directly from the preceding full attention layer. This architecture resolves two fundamental limitations of prior sparse attention methods. First, conventional approaches typically rely on additional proxies to predict token importance, introducing extra complexity and potentially suboptimal performance. In contrast, HySparse uses the full attention layer as a precise oracle to identify important tokens. Second, existing sparse attention designs often reduce computation without saving KV cache. HySparse enables sparse attention layers to reuse the full attention KV cache, thereby reducing both computation and memory. We evaluate HySparse on both 7B dense and 80B MoE models. Across all settings, HySparse consistently outperforms both full attention and hybrid SWA baselines. Notably, in the 80B MoE model with 49 total layers, only 5 layers employ full attention, yet HySparse achieves substantial performance gains while reducing KV cache storage by nearly 10x.
Key Techniques

Hybrid sparse and full attention interleaving
Token importance oracle from full attention layers
KV cache reuse and reduction

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.89Superlinear Multi-Step Attention0.41SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.40SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.40FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion0.40
HySparse (Hybrid Sparse Attention)
Core Idea: Interleave sparse attention layers with periodic full attention layers, where each full attention layer acts as an oracle to identify important tokens and provides shared KV caches for subsequent sparse layers, eliminating proxy-based token selection and reducing both computation and memory.
Problem It Solves: Existing sparse attention methods either (1) use suboptimal heuristics/proxies to predict token importance, or (2) reduce computation but not KV cache memory; HySparse addresses both by leveraging full attention layers as precise oracles for token selection and enabling KV cache reuse across sparse layers.
How It Works:

Full Attention Oracle: Every N layers, perform full attention and capture attention scores/patterns to identify “heavy hitter” tokens that should be retained.
Token Selection Propagation: Extract top-k important tokens from the full attention layer’s scores and use this selection mask for all subsequent sparse layers until the next full attention layer.
KV Cache Sharing: Sparse attention layers directly reuse the KV cache computed by the preceding full attention layer, computing attention only over the oracle-selected subset of tokens.
Repeat Pattern: The architecture cycles through [1 full attention → M sparse attention] blocks throughout the model depth.

Architecture:
flowchart TD
    A[Input Tokens] --> B[Full Attention Layer 1]
    B --> |"Attention Scores<br/>(Oracle)"| C{Token Selection}
    C --> |"Top-k Tokens"| D[Sparse Attention Layer 2]
    B --> |"KV Cache"| D
    D --> E[Sparse Attention Layer 3]
    B --> |"Shared KV Cache"| E
    E --> F[Sparse Attention Layer 4]
    B --> |"Shared KV Cache"| F
    F --> G[Full Attention Layer 5]
    G --> |"New Oracle"| H[Next Cycle...]
    
    style B fill:#4a9eff
    style G fill:#4a9eff
    style D fill:#ffb84d
    style E fill:#ffb84d
    style F fill:#ffb84d

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context LLM inference (retrieval, summarization, multi-document QA) where KV cache becomes memory bottleneck
Resource-constrained deployment of large models (7B–80B+ parameter scale)
Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures with high layer counts (e.g., 80B MoE with 49 layers)
Scenarios requiring 10x KV cache reduction with minimal accuracy degradation

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires periodic full attention computation, so cannot eliminate dense attention entirely (typically 1 full layer per 5–10 sparse layers)
Token selection is static between oracle layers, potentially missing emergent importance shifts within sparse layer sequences
Performance depends on choosing optimal full-to-sparse layer ratio (architecture hyperparameter tuning needed)
KV cache sharing assumes token importance remains stable across multiple layers

Internal Use Cases:

LLM Serving: Deploy 10x smaller KV cache footprint for production systems, enabling higher batch sizes and throughput on fixed GPU memory
Long-Context Ranking: Apply to sequence re-rankers or cross-encoders over hundreds of candidates, using full attention on query layer and sparse attention for candidate scoring
Hybrid RecSys Models: Use full attention for user-item interactions in one layer, then sparse attention (reusing KV cache) for multi-hop reasoning over knowledge graphs or session sequences
Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Combine with retrieval systems where full attention encodes retrieved documents, then sparse attention reuses those representations for answer generation

literature/2026-02-05-KDD-2025-Workshop-.title: KDD 2025 Workshop on Inference Optimization for Generative AI
literature/2026-02-05-KDD-2025-Workshop-.content:
KDD 2025 Workshop on Inference Optimization for Generative AI
Source: S2:Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining
Published: 2025
Rank: #6 (score: 0.87, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The demand for efficient Large Language Model (LLM) inference has surged with the rising adoption of Generative AI (GenAI) applications, particularly in areas such as agents and retrieval-augmented generation. Efficient inference serves two crucial purposes: it enables the deployment of LLM-centered applications that address critical business needs, while also facilitating rapid experimentation for researchers to extract valuable insights and new understandings. However, despite the field’s rapid advancement and interdisciplinary nature, there remains a limited exchange of ideas and methodologies between production-facing practitioners and researchers seeking to experiment with new GenAI concepts quickly. To bridge this gap, we are introducing the first KDD workshop on Inference Optimization for Generative AI. Our goal is to create a collaborative platform where researchers and practitioners working across various use cases and stacks of efficient inference can come together to exchange research ideas, establish connections between different disciplines, and identify challenges and research questions that will shape future work.
Key Techniques

Generative AI inference optimization
LLM deployment efficiency
RAG systems

Related Litnotes

literature/2026-02-05-LiteToken-Removing.title: LiteToken: Removing Intermediate Merge Residues From BPE Tokenizers
literature/2026-02-05-LiteToken-Removing.content:
LiteToken: Removing Intermediate Merge Residues From BPE Tokenizers
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-04
Rank: #9 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG, LLM
Abstract
Tokenization is fundamental to how language models represent and process text, yet the behavior of widely used BPE tokenizers has received far less study than model architectures and training. In this paper, we investigate intermediate merge residues in BPE vocabularies: tokens that are frequent during merge learning so that retained in the final vocabulary, but are mostly further merged and rarely emitted when tokenizing the corpus during tokenizer usage. Such low-frequency tokens not only waste vocabulary capacity but also increase vulnerability to adversarial or atypical inputs. We present a systematic empirical characterization of this phenomenon across commonly used tokenizers and introduce LiteToken, a simple method for removing residue tokens. Because the affected tokens are rarely used, pretrained models can often accommodate the modified tokenizer without additional fine-tuning. Experiments show that LiteToken reduces token fragmentation, reduces parameters, and improves robustness to noisy or misspelled inputs, while preserving overall performance.
Key Techniques

BPE tokenization
intermediate merge residues removal
vocabulary optimization
adversarial robustness improvement

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySpeculative Decoding with a Speculative Vocabulary0.20UniWeTok: An Unified Binary Tokenizer with Codebook Size 2^{128} for Unified Multimodal Large Language Model0.15LGQ: Learning Discretization Geometry for Scalable and Stable Image Tokenization0.14ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.13FASA: Frequency-aware Sparse Attention0.12
LiteToken
Core Idea: Prune BPE vocabulary by removing intermediate merge residues—tokens that are retained in the vocabulary but rarely emitted during actual tokenization because they get further merged.
Problem It Solves: BPE tokenizers accumulate tokens that were frequent during merge learning but are rarely used in practice, wasting vocabulary capacity (thus embedding parameters) and creating vulnerabilities to adversarial or atypical inputs that trigger fragmented tokenization.
How It Works:

Identify residue tokens: Tokenize the training corpus with the learned BPE vocabulary and measure actual emission frequency for each token
Filter low-frequency tokens: Remove tokens below a frequency threshold (tokens that appear in vocabulary but are rarely emitted during tokenization)
Deploy without retraining: Replace the tokenizer in pretrained models directly—because residue tokens are rarely used, the model’s learned embeddings for them are minimally activated, allowing graceful degradation

Architecture (if applicable):
flowchart LR
    A[BPE Vocab] --> B[Corpus Tokenization]
    B --> C[Frequency Analysis]
    C --> D{Emission Freq\n> Threshold?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Keep Token]
    D -->|No| F[Remove Token]
    E --> G[Pruned Vocab]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Drop-in Replacement]

Where It’s Useful:

Reducing embedding layer parameters in memory-constrained deployments
Improving robustness to noisy, misspelled, or adversarial text inputs
Retrofitting existing pretrained models without expensive retraining

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires access to the original training corpus (or representative proxy) to measure true emission frequencies
Pruning too aggressively may degrade performance on rare domain-specific terminology
Benefits are most pronounced when tokenizer was trained on very large corpora where merge artifacts accumulate

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys: compress tokenizers for user-generated content (reviews, queries) that contain frequent typos—reducing fragmentation improves semantic consistency in embeddings
LLM serving: reduce embedding table size for cost-sensitive inference workloads while maintaining robustness to malformed inputs
Ranking: improve term matching for misspelled queries by preventing excessive subword fragmentation that disrupts similarity scoring

literature/2026-02-05-NeuroCanvas-VLLM-P.title: NeuroCanvas: VLLM-Powered Robust Seizure Detection by Reformulating Multichannel EEG as Image
literature/2026-02-05-NeuroCanvas-VLLM-P.content:
NeuroCanvas: VLLM-Powered Robust Seizure Detection by Reformulating Multichannel EEG as Image
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-04
Rank: #3 (score: 0.16, max_sim: 0.76)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Accurate and timely seizure detection from Electroencephalography (EEG) is critical for clinical intervention, yet manual review of long-term recordings is labor-intensive. Recent efforts to encode EEG signals into large language models (LLMs) show promise in handling neural signals across diverse patients, but two significant challenges remain: (1) multi-channel heterogeneity, as seizure-relevant information varies substantially across EEG channels, and (2) computing inefficiency, as the EEG signals need to be encoded into a massive number of tokens for the prediction. To address these issues, we draw the EEG signal and propose the novel NeuroCanvas framework. Specifically, NeuroCanvas consists of two modules: (i) The Entropy-guided Channel Selector (ECS) selects the seizure-relevant channels input to LLM and (ii) the following Canvas of Neuron Signal (CNS) converts selected multi-channel heterogeneous EEG signals into structured visual representations. The ECS module alleviates the multi-channel heterogeneity issue, and the CNS uses compact visual tokens to represent the EEG signals that improve the computing efficiency. We evaluate NeuroCanvas across multiple seizure detection datasets, demonstrating a significant improvement of 20\% in F1 score and reductions of 88\% in inference latency. These results highlight NeuroCanvas as a scalable and effective solution for real-time and resource-efficient seizure detection in clinical practice.The code will be released at github.com/Yanchen30247/seizure_detect.
Key Techniques

Entropy-guided Channel Selector
Canvas of Neuron Signal
Visual token representation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDo MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.24ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.23VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.23The Vision Wormhole: Latent-Space Communication in Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Systems0.22Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs0.22
NeuroCanvas
Core Idea: Convert multi-channel EEG signals into visual representations (images) that Vision-Language Models can process efficiently, using entropy-guided channel selection to identify seizure-relevant signals.
Problem It Solves:

Multi-channel heterogeneity - seizure information varies across EEG channels, making it hard for models to identify relevant signals
Computing inefficiency - encoding raw EEG into LLM tokens creates massive token sequences that slow inference

How It Works:

Entropy-guided Channel Selector (ECS): Analyzes information entropy across EEG channels to automatically select the subset most relevant for seizure detection
Canvas of Neuron Signal (CNS): Transforms selected multi-channel time-series EEG data into structured 2D visual representations (images)
VLLM Processing: Vision-Language Model processes the compact image representation using visual tokens instead of sequential signal tokens

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Multi-channel EEG] --> B[ECS: Entropy-guided<br/>Channel Selector]
    B --> C[Selected Channels]
    C --> D[CNS: Canvas of<br/>Neuron Signal]
    D --> E[Visual Representation]
    E --> F[Vision-Language Model]
    F --> G[Seizure Detection]

Where It’s Useful:

Real-time clinical seizure monitoring from long-term EEG recordings
Patient-agnostic epilepsy detection across diverse patient populations
Resource-constrained medical devices requiring fast inference
Time-series medical signal analysis (ECG, EMG) where channel selection matters

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires pre-training a VLLM on EEG-as-image representations
Entropy-based channel selection may miss channels with low-entropy but clinically-relevant artifacts
Image conversion may lose fine-grained temporal dynamics present in raw signals
Performance depends on quality of visual encoding scheme for EEG patterns

Internal Use Cases:

Multi-modal time-series ranking: Convert user behavior sequences (clicks, watches, scrolls) into visual heatmaps for vision models instead of sequential transformers
Sensor fusion for recommendations: Use channel selection to identify most predictive signals from multi-source user data (app usage, location, purchases)
Efficient LLM inference: Apply visual token compression to reduce context length for long-form content understanding in search/assistant applications

literature/2026-02-05-OAT-Ordered-Action.title: OAT: Ordered Action Tokenization
literature/2026-02-05-OAT-Ordered-Action.content:
OAT: Ordered Action Tokenization
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-04
Rank: #12 (score: 0.68, max_sim: 0.80)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Autoregressive policies offer a compelling foundation for scalable robot learning by enabling discrete abstraction, token-level reasoning, and flexible inference. However, applying autoregressive modeling to continuous robot actions requires an effective action tokenization scheme. Existing approaches either rely on analytical discretization methods that produce prohibitively long token sequences, or learned latent tokenizers that lack structure, limiting their compatibility with next-token prediction. In this work, we identify three desiderata for action tokenization - high compression, total decodability, and a left-to-right causally ordered token space - and introduce Ordered Action Tokenization (OAT), a learned action tokenizer that satisfies all three. OAT discretizes action chunks into an ordered sequence of tokens using transformer with registers, finite scalar quantization, and ordering-inducing training mechanisms. The resulting token space aligns naturally with autoregressive generation and enables prefix-based detokenization, yielding an anytime trade-off between inference cost and action fidelity. Across more than 20 tasks spanning four simulation benchmarks and real-world settings, autoregressive policies equipped with OAT consistently outperform prior tokenization schemes and diffusion-based baselines, while offering significantly greater flexibility at inference time.
Key Techniques

Ordered Action Tokenization (OAT)
Transformer with Registers
Finite Scalar Quantization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySTATe-of-Thoughts: Structured Action Templates for Tree-of-Thoughts0.31AugVLA-3D: Depth-Driven Feature Augmentation for Vision-Language-Action Models0.26	extsc{NaVIDA}: Vision-Language Navigation with Inverse Dynamics Augmentation0.23Diffusion Generative Recommendation with Continuous Tokens0.17Unleash the Potential of Long Semantic IDs for Generative Recommendation0.16
Ordered Action Tokenization (OAT)
Core Idea: A learned action tokenizer that discretizes continuous robot action chunks into an ordered, causally-structured token sequence using transformer registers, finite scalar quantization, and ordering-inducing training, enabling efficient autoregressive policy learning with anytime decoding.
Problem It Solves: Existing action tokenization methods either produce prohibitively long sequences (analytical discretization) or lack causal structure (learned latent tokenizers like VQ-VAE), making them incompatible with efficient autoregressive next-token prediction for robot control policies.
How It Works:

Input: Continuous action chunks (sequences of robot actions across multiple timesteps)
Encoding: Transformer encoder with register tokens processes the action chunk and produces a fixed number of latent representations
Quantization: Finite scalar quantization (FSQ) discretizes each latent into a discrete token, creating an ordered sequence
Ordering Training: Apply causal masking and ordering-inducing losses during training to ensure tokens have left-to-right dependency structure
Prefix Decoding: At inference, decode any prefix of the token sequence to get progressively refined action predictions (anytime property)
Output: Discrete token sequence that can be generated autoregressively, then decoded back to continuous actions

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Action Chunk] --> B[Transformer Encoder + Registers]
    B --> C[Latent Representations]
    C --> D[Finite Scalar Quantization]
    D --> E[Ordered Token Sequence]
    E --> F[Autoregressive Policy]
    F --> G[Generated Tokens]
    G --> H[Prefix Decoder]
    H --> I[Continuous Actions]

Where It’s Useful:

Robot learning and manipulation tasks requiring continuous action control
Autoregressive policy architectures (transformer-based behavioral cloning)
Settings where inference-time flexibility is needed (trading accuracy for speed)
Multi-task robot learning across diverse manipulation benchmarks

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires training an additional tokenizer model before policy learning
Finite scalar quantization introduces discretization error in action space
Ordering constraint may not be optimal for all action modalities or task structures
Effectiveness depends on quality of causal ordering learned during training

Internal Use Cases:

Could adapt for sequential recommendation: tokenize user action sequences with causal ordering for autoregressive user modeling
Ranking systems: compress feature vectors or ranking decisions into ordered tokens for transformer-based rankers
LLM reasoning: apply ordered tokenization to continuous embeddings in multimodal models where actions/predictions need discrete abstraction

literature/2026-02-05-PaperSearchQA-Lear.title: PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR
literature/2026-02-05-PaperSearchQA-Lear.content:
PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-26
Rank: #7 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, RL, Search, LLM
Code: huggingface.co/collections/jmhb/papersearchqa
Abstract
Search agents are language models (LMs) that reason and search knowledge bases (or the web) to answer questions; recent methods supervise only the final answer accuracy using reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). Most RLVR search agents tackle general-domain QA, which limits their relevance to technical AI systems in science, engineering, and medicine. In this work we propose training agents to search and reason over scientific papers — this tests technical question-answering, it is directly relevant to real scientists, and the capabilities will be crucial to future AI Scientist systems. Concretely, we release a search corpus of 16 million biomedical paper abstracts and construct a challenging factoid QA dataset called PaperSearchQA with 60k samples answerable from the corpus, along with benchmarks. We train search agents in this environment to outperform non-RL retrieval baselines; we also perform further quantitative analysis and observe interesting agent behaviors like planning, reasoning, and self-verification. Our corpus, datasets, and benchmarks are usable with the popular Search-R1 codebase for RLVR training and released on huggingface.co/collections/jmhb/papersearchqa. Finally, our data creation methods are scalable and easily extendable to other scientific domains.
Key Techniques

Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR)
Scientific Paper Search Corpus Construction
Factoid Question-Answering Dataset Creation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.85PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.85PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.82PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.77PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.74
PaperSearchQA with RLVR-based Search Agents
Core Idea: Train language model agents to search scientific paper databases and answer technical questions using reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where agents learn to iteratively query, retrieve, and reason over academic abstracts without supervision on intermediate search steps.
Problem It Solves: Existing RLVR search agents focus on general-domain QA and don’t handle the technical vocabulary, reasoning complexity, and citation-grounded verification needed for scientific question-answering. Traditional supervised retrieval requires expensive step-by-step annotations, while end-to-end RLVR only needs final answer labels.
How It Works:

Corpus Construction: Index 16 million biomedical paper abstracts from PubMed as the searchable knowledge base
Dataset Creation: Generate 60k factoid QA pairs (PaperSearchQA) where answers are verifiable against the corpus using exact-match or semantic similarity
RLVR Training: Agent generates search queries → retrieves paper abstracts → reasons over results → produces answer; receives reward only for final answer correctness
Learned Behaviors: Through trial-and-error, agents discover search strategies (query reformulation, multi-hop reasoning, self-verification) without explicit supervision

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    Q[Question] --> A[LM Agent]
    A -->|generates| S[Search Queries]
    S --> C[PubMed Corpus<br/>16M abstracts]
    C -->|retrieves| R[Relevant Papers]
    R --> A
    A -->|reasons| Ans[Final Answer]
    Ans --> V[Verifier]
    V -->|reward signal| A

Where It’s Useful:

Scientific literature review and hypothesis generation for AI researchers
Medical question-answering systems requiring evidence from peer-reviewed sources
Automated research assistants for technical domains (engineering, chemistry, law)
Building blocks for autonomous AI Scientist systems that plan experiments based on literature

Limitations / Caveats:

Trained only on abstracts, not full-text papers (limits depth of reasoning)
Factoid QA format doesn’t capture open-ended research questions
RLVR requires verifiable ground-truth answers (harder for subjective/interpretive questions)
Biomedical domain may not transfer directly to other scientific fields without retraining

Internal Use Cases:

LLM-powered research tools: Build agents that search internal paper repositories or arXiv to answer engineering questions during model development
RecSys feature engineering: Train agents to retrieve relevant academic papers for cold-start recommendation scenarios (e.g., recommending papers to new users based on query understanding)
Automated literature monitoring: Deploy agents to track new publications and extract actionable insights for ranking algorithm improvements
Citation-grounded response generation: Enhance LLM outputs with verifiable scientific references, reducing hallucination in technical documentation

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-05-Quant-VideoGen-Aut.title: Quant VideoGen: Auto-Regressive Long Video Generation via 2-Bit KV-Cache Quantization
literature/2026-02-05-Quant-VideoGen-Aut.content:
Quant VideoGen: Auto-Regressive Long Video Generation via 2-Bit KV-Cache Quantization
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #2 (score: 0.47, max_sim: 0.78)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Despite rapid progress in autoregressive video diffusion, an emerging system algorithm bottleneck limits both deployability and generation capability: KV cache memory. In autoregressive video generation models, the KV cache grows with generation history and quickly dominates GPU memory, often exceeding 30 GB, preventing deployment on widely available hardware. More critically, constrained KV cache budgets restrict the effective working memory, directly degrading long horizon consistency in identity, layout, and motion. To address this challenge, we present Quant VideoGen (QVG), a training free KV cache quantization framework for autoregressive video diffusion models. QVG leverages video spatiotemporal redundancy through Semantic Aware Smoothing, producing low magnitude, quantization friendly residuals. It further introduces Progressive Residual Quantization, a coarse to fine multi stage scheme that reduces quantization error while enabling a smooth quality memory trade off. Across LongCat Video, HY WorldPlay, and Self Forcing benchmarks, QVG establishes a new Pareto frontier between quality and memory efficiency, reducing KV cache memory by up to 7.0 times with less than 4% end to end latency overhead while consistently outperforming existing baselines in generation quality.
Key Techniques

Semantic Aware Smoothing
Progressive Residual Quantization
KV cache quantization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityFlashVID: Efficient Video Large Language Models via Training-free Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging0.36Efficient Autoregressive Video Diffusion with Dummy Head0.35Flow caching for autoregressive video generation0.33Light Forcing: Accelerating Autoregressive Video Diffusion via Sparse Attention0.27OneVision-Encoder: Codec-Aligned Sparsity as a Foundational Principle for Multimodal Intelligence0.26
Quant VideoGen (QVG)
Core Idea: Training-free KV cache quantization framework for autoregressive video diffusion models that achieves 2-bit precision through semantic-aware smoothing and progressive residual quantization, reducing memory by up to 7× while maintaining generation quality.
Problem It Solves: KV cache memory explosion in autoregressive video generation (often >30GB), preventing deployment on consumer hardware and limiting long-horizon consistency in identity, layout, and motion due to constrained working memory budgets.
How It Works:

Semantic Aware Smoothing: Exploits video spatiotemporal redundancy to transform KV activations into low-magnitude, quantization-friendly residuals by leveraging temporal and spatial correlation patterns
Progressive Residual Quantization: Applies coarse-to-fine multi-stage quantization scheme to the smoothed residuals, iteratively refining quantization bins to minimize error accumulation
2-bit KV Storage: Compresses each KV cache entry to 2 bits while maintaining reconstruction quality through the residual representation, enabling smooth quality-memory tradeoff curve

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[KV Cache] --> B[Semantic Aware<br/>Smoothing]
    B --> C[Low-magnitude<br/>Residuals]
    C --> D[Progressive<br/>Residual Quantization]
    D --> E[2-bit Quantized<br/>KV Cache]
    E --> F[Video Generation<br/>with 7x Memory<br/>Reduction]

Where It’s Useful:

Autoregressive video diffusion models (e.g., LongCat Video, WorldPlay)
Long-form video generation requiring extended context windows
Edge deployment of video generation models on consumer GPUs
Multi-user serving scenarios with tight memory budgets per request

Limitations / Caveats:

Training-free approach may not achieve same compression as quantization-aware training methods
4% end-to-end latency overhead from quantization/dequantization operations
Effectiveness depends on degree of spatiotemporal redundancy in video domain (may not transfer directly to text/audio)
Quality-memory tradeoff still exists—extreme quantization (<2 bits) would likely degrade generation quality

Internal Use Cases:

Apply progressive quantization strategy to KV caches in LLM serving for recommendation/ranking models with long context
Adapt semantic-aware smoothing to exploit temporal patterns in user behavior sequences for session-based RecSys
Memory-efficient deployment of multimodal models (video understanding for content ranking) on inference clusters

literature/2026-02-05-ReThinker-Scientif.title: ReThinker: Scientific Reasoning by Rethinking with Guided Reflection and Confidence Control
literature/2026-02-05-ReThinker-Scientif.content:
ReThinker: Scientific Reasoning by Rethinking with Guided Reflection and Confidence Control
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-04
Rank: #5 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG, Search, RecSys, LLM
Abstract
Expert-level scientific reasoning remains challenging for large language models, particularly on benchmarks such as Humanity’s Last Exam (HLE), where rigid tool pipelines, brittle multi-agent coordination, and inefficient test-time scaling often limit performance. We introduce ReThinker, a confidence-aware agentic framework that orchestrates retrieval, tool use, and multi-agent reasoning through a stage-wise Solver-Critic-Selector architecture. Rather than following a fixed pipeline, ReThinker dynamically allocates computation based on model confidence, enabling adaptive tool invocation, guided multi-dimensional reflection, and robust confidence-weighted selection. To support scalable training without human annotation, we further propose a reverse data synthesis pipeline and an adaptive trajectory recycling strategy that transform successful reasoning traces into high-quality supervision. Experiments on HLE, GAIA, and XBench demonstrate that ReThinker consistently outperforms state-of-the-art foundation models with tools and existing deep research systems, achieving state-of-the-art results on expert-level reasoning tasks.
Key Techniques

Confidence-aware agentic framework
Solver-Critic-Selector architecture
Reverse data synthesis pipeline
Adaptive trajectory recycling strategy

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityImproving Data and Reward Design for Scientific Reasoning in Large Language Models0.20LogitsCoder: Towards Efficient Chain-of-Thought Path Search via Logits Preference Decoding for Code Generation0.20SPARC: Separating Perception And Reasoning Circuits for Test-time Scaling of VLMs0.18OpaqueToolsBench: Learning Nuances of Tool Behavior Through Interaction0.18Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.17
ReThinker: Confidence-Aware Agentic Scientific Reasoning
