NoteSimilaritySwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMs0.24S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation0.23LogitsCoder: Towards Efficient Chain-of-Thought Path Search via Logits Preference Decoding for Code Generation0.23ImgCoT: Compressing Long Chain of Thought into Compact Visual Tokens for Efficient Reasoning of Large Language Model0.23RelayGen: Intra-Generation Model Switching for Efficient Reasoning0.20
Recurrence Quantification Analysis for Reasoning Token Dynamics (RQA)
Core Idea: Analyze the latent dynamics of reasoning token generation in LLMs by treating it as a dynamical system and applying RQA to hidden embedding trajectories to quantify patterns of repetition, stalling, and determinism.
Problem It Solves: Response length is a crude proxy for reasoning effort that fails to capture the quality, dynamics, or effectiveness of chain-of-thought reasoning. Traditional text-based analysis becomes impractical as reasoning models generate longer, less human-interpretable token sequences.
How It Works:

Extract Hidden Embeddings: During token generation, capture the hidden state embeddings at each generation step to form a trajectory in latent space
Construct Recurrence Plot: Build a recurrence matrix by computing pairwise distances between embedding states across the trajectory
Compute RQA Metrics: Calculate quantitative measures including:

Determinism: Proportion of recurrent points forming diagonal line structures (indicates predictable, systematic reasoning)
Laminarity: Proportion of recurrent points forming vertical/horizontal lines (indicates stalling or repetitive states)
Recurrence Rate: Overall density of recurrent patterns


Correlate with Task Performance: Use RQA metrics to predict task complexity and reasoning effectiveness

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Token Generation] --> B[Hidden State Extraction]
    B --> C[Embedding Trajectory]
    C --> D[Recurrence Matrix]
    D --> E[RQA Metrics]
    E --> F[Determinism/Laminarity]
    F --> G[Task Complexity Prediction]

Where It’s Useful:

Diagnosing reasoning quality in test-time compute systems without parsing generated text
Predicting task difficulty based on latent generation dynamics (8% improvement over length-based metrics)
Identifying “mind blurting” (unproductive token generation) vs. genuine reasoning progress
Monitoring Chain-of-Thought effectiveness in production systems where reasoning traces are opaque

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires access to hidden states during generation (not just final outputs)
Computational overhead of extracting and analyzing embedding trajectories
RQA metrics require calibration per model family and task domain
Does not directly explain what the model is reasoning about, only how it’s reasoning dynamically

Internal Use Cases:

LLM reasoning evaluation: Monitor whether extended generation in o1-style models reflects genuine reasoning or repetitive stalling
Test-time compute optimization: Identify when to truncate generation based on RQA signals rather than arbitrary token limits
Model debugging: Detect pathological generation patterns (high laminarity = stuck states) in ranking/reranking models using chain-of-thought
Adaptive inference: Dynamically allocate compute budget based on real-time RQA metrics predicting task complexity

literature/2026-02-09-JADE-Expert-Ground.title: JADE: Expert-Grounded Dynamic Evaluation for Open-Ended Professional Tasks
literature/2026-02-09-JADE-Expert-Ground.content:
JADE: Expert-Grounded Dynamic Evaluation for Open-Ended Professional Tasks
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-06
Rank: #3 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM
Code: github.com/smiling-world/JADE
Abstract
Evaluating agentic AI on open-ended professional tasks faces a fundamental dilemma between rigor and flexibility. Static rubrics provide rigorous, reproducible assessment but fail to accommodate diverse valid response strategies, while LLM-as-a-judge approaches adapt to individual responses yet suffer from instability and bias. Human experts address this dilemma by combining domain-grounded principles with dynamic, claim-level assessment. Inspired by this process, we propose JADE, a two-layer evaluation framework. Layer 1 encodes expert knowledge as a predefined set of evaluation skills, providing stable evaluation criteria. Layer 2 performs report-specific, claim-level evaluation to flexibly assess diverse reasoning strategies, with evidence-dependency gating to invalidate conclusions built on refuted claims. Experiments on BizBench show that JADE improves evaluation stability and reveals critical agent failure modes missed by holistic LLM-based evaluators. We further demonstrate strong alignment with expert-authored rubrics and effective transfer to a medical-domain benchmark, validating JADE across professional domains. Our code is publicly available at github.com/smiling-world/JADE.
Key Techniques

Evaluation skills
claim-level assessment
evidence-dependency gating

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHybrid-Gym: Training Coding Agents to Generalize Across Tasks0.21MemSkill: Learning and Evolving Memory Skills for Self-Evolving Agents0.20Improving Data and Reward Design for Scientific Reasoning in Large Language Models0.20A Field Guide to Automatic Evaluation of LLM-Generated Summaries0.19ResearchGym: Evaluating Language Model Agents on Real-World AI Research0.18
JADE (Expert-Grounded Dynamic Evaluation)
Core Idea: A two-layer evaluation framework that combines predefined expert-grounded evaluation skills (Layer 1) with dynamic claim-level assessment (Layer 2) to rigorously yet flexibly evaluate diverse agent strategies on open-ended professional tasks.
Problem It Solves: Static rubrics fail to accommodate valid diverse response strategies in open-ended tasks, while LLM-as-a-judge approaches suffer from instability and bias. JADE bridges this gap by grounding evaluation in domain principles while dynamically adapting to individual response structures.
How It Works:

Layer 1 (Skill Definition): Encode expert knowledge as a predefined set of evaluation skills (e.g., “data accuracy verification,” “logical reasoning assessment”) that provide stable, domain-grounded criteria applicable across responses.
Layer 2 (Claim-Level Evaluation): Dynamically decompose each agent response into atomic claims, then evaluate each claim against relevant Layer 1 skills with evidence-dependency tracking.
Evidence-Dependency Gating: Invalidate downstream conclusions built on refuted claims—if Claim B depends on Claim A and Claim A is false, Claim B’s score is automatically penalized or nullified.
Aggregation: Combine claim-level scores into overall assessment, weighted by claim importance and correctness propagation through the dependency graph.

Architecture:
flowchart TB
    A[Agent Response] --> B[Layer 2: Claim Decomposition]
    B --> C[Claim 1]
    B --> D[Claim 2]
    B --> E[Claim N]
    F[Layer 1: Expert Evaluation Skills] --> G[Skill-Based Assessment]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    G --> H[Evidence-Dependency Graph]
    H --> I[Propagate Refutations]
    I --> J[Final Score]

Where It’s Useful:

Evaluating business analysis agents (BizBench domain: strategy consulting, market research)
Medical diagnosis and clinical reasoning agents (demonstrated transfer to medical benchmark)
Legal research and case analysis agents
Any open-ended professional task requiring multi-step reasoning with diverse valid approaches

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires upfront expert effort to define Layer 1 evaluation skills for each domain (though transferable within domain)
Claim decomposition quality depends on LLM’s ability to parse complex responses into atomic units
Evidence-dependency graph construction may miss implicit dependencies between claims
Computational cost higher than holistic scoring due to claim-level granularity

Internal Use Cases:

Evaluating recommendation explanation quality: Layer 1 skills could encode “factual accuracy of user history,” “logical validity of preference inference,” with dependency gating ensuring explanations built on wrong user data are penalized
Ranking quality assessment for complex queries: decompose ranking decisions into claims (relevance signals, diversity rationale), evaluate each against expert-defined criteria
LLM agent evaluation for internal tools: assess multi-step research/analysis tasks where agents may take valid but different approaches to reach conclusions

literature/2026-02-09-LogicSkills-A-Stru.title: LogicSkills: A Structured Benchmark for Formal Reasoning in Large Language Models
literature/2026-02-09-LogicSkills-A-Stru.content:
LogicSkills: A Structured Benchmark for Formal Reasoning in Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-06
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG, LLM
Abstract
Large language models have demonstrated notable performance across various logical reasoning benchmarks. However, it remains unclear which core logical skills they truly master. To address this, we introduce LogicSkills, a unified benchmark designed to isolate three fundamental skills in formal reasoning: (i) \textit{formal symbolization}\unicode{x2014}translating premises into first-order logic; (ii) \textit{countermodel construction}\unicode{x2014}formulating a finite structure in which all premises are true while the conclusion is false; and (iii) \textit{validity assessment}\unicode{x2014}deciding whether a conclusion follows from a given set of premises. Items are drawn from the two-variable fragment of first-order logic (without identity) and are presented in both natural English and a Carroll-style language with nonce words. All examples are verified for correctness and non-triviality using the SMT solver Z3. Across leading models, performance is high on validity but substantially lower on symbolization and countermodel construction, suggesting reliance on surface-level patterns rather than genuine symbolic or rule-based reasoning.
Key Techniques

formal symbolization
countermodel construction
validity assessment

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityThinking with Drafting: Optical Decompression via Logical Reconstruction0.17JADE: Expert-Grounded Dynamic Evaluation for Open-Ended Professional Tasks0.17SwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMs0.14LLMStructBench: Benchmarking Large Language Model Structured Data Extraction0.13MATA: A Trainable Hierarchical Automaton System for Multi-Agent Visual Reasoning0.13
LogicSkills Benchmark
Core Idea: A structured benchmark that decomposes formal reasoning into three isolated, verifiable skills—symbolization, countermodel construction, and validity assessment—to diagnose which logical capabilities LLMs truly possess versus superficial pattern matching.
Problem It Solves: Existing logical reasoning benchmarks provide only end-to-end accuracy scores, making it impossible to determine whether models succeed through genuine symbolic reasoning or by exploiting surface-level linguistic patterns. This prevents targeted improvement of logical reasoning capabilities.
How It Works:

Task Decomposition: Isolates three core skills from formal logic:

Formal symbolization: Translate natural language premises into first-order logic formulas
Countermodel construction: Generate a finite structure where all premises are true but the conclusion is false
Validity assessment: Determine whether a conclusion logically follows from premises


Dual Language Testing: Presents each problem in both natural English and Carroll-style language with nonce words (e.g., “All blicks are flurbs”) to detect reliance on world knowledge versus pure logical structure
Automated Verification: Uses Z3 SMT solver to verify correctness and ensure non-triviality of all test items, restricting problems to the two-variable fragment of first-order logic (without identity)

Where It’s Useful:

Diagnosing specific weaknesses in LLM reasoning pipelines (e.g., symbolization failure vs. inference failure)
Evaluating formal verification and proof assistant systems
Training curricula for logical reasoning in language models
Benchmarking neurosymbolic systems that combine neural and symbolic reasoning

Limitations / Caveats:

Restricted to two-variable first-order logic fragment, which may not capture complexity of real-world reasoning
High performance on validity assessment but low on symbolization/countermodels suggests models may use shortcuts rather than genuine logical processing
Carroll-style nonce words may introduce unnatural linguistic structures that don’t reflect real reasoning scenarios

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys/Ranking: Test whether ranking models can properly symbolize user preferences and constraints for explainable recommendations
LLM Guardrails: Evaluate whether safety models can correctly formalize policy rules into logical constraints before applying them
Retrieval Augmentation: Assess if query understanding modules can translate complex multi-constraint queries into formal search predicates

literature/2026-02-09-MSign-An-Optimizer.title: MSign: An Optimizer Preventing Training Instability in Large Language Models via Stable Rank Restoration
literature/2026-02-09-MSign-An-Optimizer.content:
MSign: An Optimizer Preventing Training Instability in Large Language Models via Stable Rank Restoration
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-02
Rank: #8 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RecSys, LLM
Abstract
Training instability remains a critical challenge in large language model (LLM) pretraining, often manifesting as sudden gradient explosions that waste significant computational resources. We study training failures in a 5M-parameter NanoGPT model scaled via μP, identifying two key phenomena preceding collapse: (1) rapid decline in weight matrix stable rank (ratio of squared Frobenius norm to squared spectral norm), and (2) increasing alignment between adjacent layer Jacobians. We prove theoretically that these two conditions jointly cause exponential gradient norm growth with network depth. To break this instability mechanism, we propose MSign, a new optimizer that periodically applies matrix sign operations to restore stable rank. Experiments on models from 5M to 3B parameters demonstrate that MSign effectively prevents training failures with a computational overhead of less than 7.0%.
Key Techniques

Matrix sign operations
Stable rank restoration
Layer Jacobian alignment monitoring
Periodic normalization for gradient stability

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySpanNorm: Reconciling Training Stability and Performance in Deep Transformers0.17Quartet II: Accurate LLM Pre-Training in NVFP4 by Improved Unbiased Gradient Estimation0.16On Surprising Effectiveness of Masking Updates in Adaptive Optimizers0.15S2D: Selective Spectral Decay for Quantization-Friendly Conditioning of Neural Activations0.14ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.14
MSign (Matrix Sign Optimizer)
Core Idea: A gradient optimizer that periodically applies matrix sign decomposition to weight matrices during training to restore their stable rank and prevent the gradient explosion instability that causes LLM training collapse.
Problem It Solves: Training instability in large language models, where sudden gradient explosions occur due to the joint effect of declining weight matrix stable rank (ratio of squared Frobenius to spectral norm) and increasing alignment between adjacent layer Jacobians, leading to exponential gradient growth with depth.
How It Works:

Detect instability triggers: Monitor weight matrix stable rank (‖W‖²_F / ‖W‖²_2) and inter-layer Jacobian alignment during training
Periodic intervention: At predetermined intervals, apply matrix sign operation to weight matrices: W ← sign(W) = U·sign(Σ)·V^T from SVD decomposition W = UΣV
Rank restoration: The sign operation equalizes singular values while preserving singular vectors, restoring stable rank to its maximum value (rank of matrix) and breaking the instability feedback loop
Resume training: Continue standard gradient updates with restored matrices until next intervention cycle

Where It’s Useful:

Pretraining large language models (5M to 3B+ parameters) where gradient explosions waste compute
Scaled architectures using μP (Maximal Update Parameterization) that are particularly sensitive to rank degradation
Long training runs where preventing even rare failures has high ROI
Training with aggressive learning rates that push stability boundaries

Limitations / Caveats:

Adds 7% computational overhead for periodic matrix decomposition operations
Requires tuning intervention frequency hyperparameter (too frequent may disrupt convergence, too rare may miss instabilities)
Theoretical analysis proven only for specific depth-dependent exponential growth regime; may not address all instability modes
Sign operation is discontinuous and may interfere with smooth optimization dynamics if applied too aggressively
Effectiveness may depend on architecture specifics (number of layers, width, activation functions)

Internal Use Cases:

Apply to LLM pretraining pipelines to reduce wasted compute from training restarts after gradient explosions
Monitor stable rank as an early warning signal for instability in any deep neural architecture (RecSys deep models, ranking towers)
Use Jacobian alignment metrics to diagnose depth-related optimization issues in multi-layer recommendation models
Adapt rank restoration concept to embedding tables that may degrade during training (low stable rank → redundant dimensions)

literature/2026-02-09-Multimodal-Generat.title: Multimodal Generative Retrieval Model with Staged Pretraining for Food Delivery on Meituan
literature/2026-02-09-Multimodal-Generat.content:
Multimodal Generative Retrieval Model with Staged Pretraining for Food Delivery on Meituan
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-06
Rank: #4 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, RAG
Abstract
Multimodal retrieval models are becoming increasingly important in scenarios such as food delivery, where rich multimodal features can meet diverse user needs and enable precise retrieval. Mainstream approaches typically employ a dual-tower architecture between queries and items, and perform joint optimization of intra-tower and inter-tower tasks. However, we observe that joint optimization often leads to certain modalities dominating the training process, while other modalities are neglected. In addition, inconsistent training speeds across modalities can easily result in the one-epoch problem. To address these challenges, we propose a staged pretraining strategy, which guides the model to focus on specialized tasks at each stage, enabling it to effectively attend to and utilize multimodal features, and allowing flexible control over the training process at each stage to avoid the one-epoch problem. Furthermore, to better utilize the semantic IDs that compress high-dimensional multimodal embeddings, we design both generative and discriminative tasks to help the model understand the associations between SIDs, queries, and item features, thereby improving overall performance. Extensive experiments on large-scale real-world Meituan data demonstrate that our method achieves improvements of 3.80%, 2.64%, and 2.17% on R@5, R@10, and R@20, and 5.10%, 4.22%, and 2.09% on N@5, N@10, and N@20 compared to mainstream baselines. Online A/B testing on the Meituan platform shows that our approach achieves a 1.12% increase in revenue and a 1.02% increase in click-through rate, validating the effectiveness and superiority of our method in practical applications.
Key Techniques

Staged pretraining strategy
Semantic ID utilization with generative and discriminative tasks
Dual-tower architecture with modality-balanced optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityClosing the Modality Gap Aligns Group-Wise Semantics0.28Instruction Anchors: Dissecting the Causal Dynamics of Modality Arbitration0.28End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.22Embed-RL: Reinforcement Learning for Reasoning-Driven Multimodal Embeddings0.22End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.21
Staged Pretraining for Multimodal Generative Retrieval (SPMGR)
Core Idea: Decouples multimodal training into sequential stages where each stage focuses on specialized tasks (e.g., single-modality encoding, then cross-modal alignment, then semantic ID generation), preventing modality dominance and the one-epoch problem in multimodal retrieval systems.
Problem It Solves: Joint optimization of multimodal features causes certain modalities (typically text) to dominate training while others are neglected, and inconsistent convergence rates across modalities lead to the one-epoch problem where some modalities overfit before others have properly learned.
How It Works:

Stage 1 - Intra-Tower Pretraining: Train separate encoders for each modality (text, image, structured features) independently on modality-specific reconstruction or classification tasks to build robust single-modality representations.
Stage 2 - Inter-Tower Alignment: Freeze or fine-tune modality encoders while training cross-modal attention mechanisms to align query and item embeddings across modalities using contrastive learning.
Stage 3 - Semantic ID (SID) Training: Train both generative (query → SID sequence generation) and discriminative (SID → item feature matching) tasks to compress high-dimensional multimodal embeddings into discrete semantic identifiers.
Stage 4 - Joint Fine-tuning: End-to-end refinement with weighted loss functions across all tasks, with per-stage learning rate schedules to prevent modality collapse.

Architecture:
flowchart TB
    Q[Query: text + image] --> QT[Text Encoder]
    Q --> QI[Image Encoder]
    I[Item: text + image + features] --> IT[Item Text Encoder]
    I --> II[Item Image Encoder]
    I --> IF[Item Feature Encoder]
    
    QT --> Stage1[Stage 1: Intra-Tower]
    QI --> Stage1
    IT --> Stage1
    II --> Stage1
    IF --> Stage1
    
    Stage1 --> Stage2[Stage 2: Cross-Modal Alignment]
    Stage2 --> QE[Query Embedding]
    Stage2 --> IE[Item Embedding]
    
    QE --> Stage3[Stage 3: SID Tasks]
    IE --> Stage3
    Stage3 --> Gen[Generative: Q→SID]
    Stage3 --> Disc[Discriminative: SID→Item]
    
    Gen --> Stage4[Stage 4: Joint Fine-tuning]
    Disc --> Stage4
    Stage4 --> R[Ranked Results]

Where It’s Useful:

E-commerce and food delivery platforms with rich multimodal item catalogs (images, text descriptions, structured attributes)
Search systems requiring fusion of user text queries with visual preferences
Recommendation systems with heterogeneous feature sets where modalities have imbalanced data availability

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful hyperparameter tuning for stage transition timing and learning rate schedules per modality
Increased training complexity and wall-clock time due to multi-stage pipeline (though enables better modality balance)
Stage boundaries may need domain-specific customization based on modality convergence characteristics

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys/ranking: Apply staged training to prevent text features from dominating visual/behavioral signals in hybrid recommenders
LLM fine-tuning: Use stage-wise approach when adapting vision-language models to prevent catastrophic forgetting of visual capabilities during text-heavy instruction tuning
Semantic ID systems: Adopt generative+discriminative SID training to improve compressed representation quality in large-scale retrieval

literature/2026-02-09-NanoQuant-Efficien.title: NanoQuant: Efficient Sub-1-Bit Quantization of Large Language Models
literature/2026-02-09-NanoQuant-Efficien.content:
NanoQuant: Efficient Sub-1-Bit Quantization of Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-06
Rank: #11 (score: 0.90, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Weight-only quantization has become a standard approach for efficiently serving large language models (LLMs). However, existing methods fail to efficiently compress models to binary (1-bit) levels, as they either require large amounts of data and compute or incur additional storage. In this work, we propose NanoQuant, the first post-training quantization (PTQ) method to compress LLMs to both binary and sub-1-bit levels. NanoQuant formulates quantization as a low-rank binary factorization problem, and compresses full-precision weights to low-rank binary matrices and scales. Specifically, it utilizes an efficient alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) method to precisely initialize latent binary matrices and scales, and then tune the initialized parameters through a block and model reconstruction process. Consequently, NanoQuant establishes a new Pareto frontier in low-memory post-training quantization, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy even at sub-1-bit compression rates. NanoQuant makes large-scale deployment feasible on consumer hardware. For example, it compresses Llama2-70B by 25.8\times in just 13 hours on a single H100, enabling a 70B model to operate on a consumer 8 GB GPU.
Key Techniques

Low-rank binary factorization
ADMM initialization
Block and model reconstruction

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBitDance: Scaling Autoregressive Generative Models with Binary Tokens0.33RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs0.30RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs0.29OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization0.25QuEPT: Quantized Elastic Precision Transformers with One-Shot Calibration for Multi-Bit Switching0.23
NanoQuant
Core Idea: A post-training quantization method that compresses LLM weights to binary and sub-1-bit levels by formulating quantization as a low-rank binary factorization problem, using ADMM initialization followed by block-wise reconstruction.
Problem It Solves: Existing weight-only quantization methods cannot efficiently compress LLMs to binary (1-bit) or sub-1-bit levels without requiring massive amounts of calibration data, extensive compute resources, or incurring additional storage overhead for codebooks and auxiliary structures.
How It Works:

Low-Rank Binary Factorization: Decompose full-precision weight matrix W into low-rank binary matrices B and diagonal scaling matrices S, where W ≈ S₁ · B₁ · B₂ · … · Bₙ · S₂
ADMM Initialization: Use alternating direction method of multipliers to efficiently initialize the binary matrices and scales with high precision, avoiding random initialization pitfalls
Block Reconstruction: Fine-tune the initialized binary parameters through layer-wise block reconstruction, minimizing reconstruction error on small calibration data
Model-Level Optimization: Apply final model-level reconstruction to recover accuracy across the entire network

Where It’s Useful:

Deploying 70B+ parameter models on consumer GPUs (e.g., Llama2-70B on 8GB GPU)
Edge deployment scenarios requiring minimal memory footprint
Large-scale model serving where memory bandwidth is the bottleneck
Research on extreme compression for neural architecture understanding

Limitations / Caveats:

Sub-1-bit compression still incurs some accuracy degradation compared to higher-bit quantization
Requires 13+ hours on H100 GPU for 70B models (though one-time offline cost)
Performance gains depend on hardware support for efficient binary matrix operations
May not preserve all emergent capabilities at extreme compression ratios

Internal Use Cases:

Compress large ranking/reranking LLMs for real-time inference in recommendation pipelines
Enable A/B testing of 70B+ models in production without provisioning expensive GPU infrastructure
Deploy multiple model variants simultaneously within memory budget for ensemble methods
Reduce serving costs for embedding models in retrieval systems while maintaining recall

literature/2026-02-09-PANC-Prior-Aware-N.title: PANC: Prior-Aware Normalized Cut for Object Segmentation
literature/2026-02-09-PANC-Prior-Aware-N.content:
PANC: Prior-Aware Normalized Cut for Object Segmentation
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-06
Rank: #7 (score: 0.52, max_sim: 0.79)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Fully unsupervised segmentation pipelines naively seek the most salient object, should this be present. As a result, most of the methods reported in the literature deliver non-deterministic partitions that are sensitive to initialization, seed order, and threshold heuristics.   We propose PANC, a weakly supervised spectral segmentation framework that uses a minimal set of annotated visual tokens to produce stable, controllable, and reproducible object masks. From the TokenCut approach, we augment the token-token affinity graph with a handful of priors coupled to anchor nodes. By manipulating the graph topology, we bias the spectral eigenspace toward partitions that are consistent with the annotations. Our approach preserves the global grouping enforced by dense self-supervised visual features, trading annotated tokens for significant gains in reproducibility, user control, and segmentation quality.   Using 5 to 30 annotations per dataset, our training-free method achieves state-of-the-art performance among weakly and unsupervised approaches on standard benchmarks (e.g., DUTS-TE, ECSSD, MS COCO). Contrarily, it excels in domains where dense labels are costly or intra-class differences are subtle. We report strong and reliable results on homogeneous, fine-grained, and texture-limited domains, achieving 96.8% (+14.43% over SotA), 78.0% (+0.2%), and 78.8% (+0.37%) average mean intersection-over-union (mIoU) on CrackForest (CFD), CUB-200-2011, and HAM10000 datasets, respectively. For multi-object benchmarks, the framework showcases explicit, user-controllable semantic segmentation.
Key Techniques

Spectral segmentation with graph augmentation
Weakly supervised learning with minimal annotations
Dense self-supervised visual features
Token-based affinity graph manipulation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityVIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.29Do MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.18ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.16Thinking with Drafting: Optical Decompression via Logical Reconstruction0.15Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs0.14
PANC (Prior-Aware Normalized Cut)
Core Idea: A weakly supervised spectral segmentation method that augments token-token affinity graphs with a minimal set of annotated visual tokens as anchor nodes, biasing the spectral eigenspace toward partitions consistent with user annotations while preserving global grouping from self-supervised features.
Problem It Solves: Fully unsupervised segmentation methods like TokenCut produce non-deterministic, initialization-sensitive partitions that lack user control and reproducibility. PANC addresses this by trading minimal annotations (5-30 tokens per dataset) for stable, controllable segmentation without requiring full training.
How It Works:

Input: Dense self-supervised visual features (e.g., from DINO or other vision transformers) and a handful of annotated visual tokens indicating target object regions.
Graph Construction: Build token-token affinity graph from feature similarities, then augment topology by connecting annotated tokens to anchor nodes with prescribed edge weights.
Spectral Biasing: The anchor nodes and their connections manipulate the graph Laplacian, steering the normalized cut solution toward partitions that respect the sparse annotations.
Output: Extract eigenvectors corresponding to smallest eigenvalues, perform spectral clustering to obtain stable, reproducible object masks aligned with priors.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Self-Supervised Features] --> B[Token-Token Affinity Graph]
    C[Annotated Tokens] --> D[Anchor Nodes]
    B --> E[Augmented Graph]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Normalized Cut / Spectral Clustering]
    F --> G[Object Masks]

Where It’s Useful:

Domains with costly dense labels (medical imaging, infrastructure defect detection)
Fine-grained visual categorization with subtle intra-class differences (CUB-200-2011 birds)
Texture-limited or homogeneous images (crack detection achieving 96.8% mIoU on CrackForest)
Multi-object scenarios requiring explicit user control over semantic segmentation

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires manual annotation of 5-30 visual tokens per dataset (not fully unsupervised)
Training-free but depends on quality of pretrained self-supervised features
Performance sensitive to annotation selection and anchor node placement
May struggle with highly cluttered scenes or low-contrast boundaries despite priors

Internal Use Cases:

Content Understanding: Segment product images or visual assets with minimal labeling for catalog enrichment or quality control
User Intent Modeling: Use sparse user clicks/annotations as priors to guide content segmentation in recommendation interfaces
Embedding Space Refinement: Apply spectral methods with anchor nodes to partition user or item embeddings based on sparse behavioral signals, improving neighborhood quality in retrieval systems

literature/2026-02-09-ProtoQuant-Quantiz.title: ProtoQuant: Quantization of Prototypical Parts For General and Fine-Grained Image Classification
literature/2026-02-09-ProtoQuant-Quantiz.content:
ProtoQuant: Quantization of Prototypical Parts For General and Fine-Grained Image Classification
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-06
Rank: #12 (score: 0.35, max_sim: 0.78)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Prototypical parts-based models offer a “this looks like that” paradigm for intrinsic interpretability, yet they typically struggle with ImageNet-scale generalization and often require computationally expensive backbone finetuning. Furthermore, existing methods frequently suffer from “prototype drift,” where learned prototypes lack tangible grounding in the training distribution and change their activation under small perturbations. We present ProtoQuant, a novel architecture that achieves prototype stability and grounded interpretability through latent vector quantization. By constraining prototypes to a discrete learned codebook within the latent space, we ensure they remain faithful representations of the training data without the need to update the backbone. This design allows ProtoQuant to function as an efficient, interpretable head that scales to large-scale datasets. We evaluate ProtoQuant on ImageNet and several fine-grained benchmarks (CUB-200, Cars-196). Our results demonstrate that ProtoQuant achieves competitive classification accuracy while generalizing to ImageNet and comparable interpretability metrics to other prototypical-parts-based methods.
Key Techniques

Latent vector quantization
Discrete learned codebook
Prototype stability through codebook constraints
Interpretable head architecture

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPrice of universality in vector quantization is at most 0.11 bit0.26LGQ: Learning Discretization Geometry for Scalable and Stable Image Tokenization0.22DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.20Rethinking ANN-based Retrieval: Multifaceted Learnable Index for Large-scale Recommendation System0.17Pailitao-VL: Unified Embedding and Reranker for Real-Time Multi-Modal Industrial Search0.17
ProtoQuant: Quantized Prototypical Parts Classification
Core Idea: Constrain learned prototypes to a discrete codebook in latent space via vector quantization, preventing prototype drift and enabling interpretable “this looks like that” classification without backbone finetuning.
Problem It Solves: Traditional prototypical parts methods suffer from prototype drift (prototypes lack grounding in training data and change under small perturbations), struggle to scale to ImageNet-level datasets, and require expensive backbone finetuning to achieve competitive accuracy.
How It Works:

Extract latent features from input images using a frozen pretrained backbone (e.g., ResNet, Vision Transformer)
Apply vector quantization to map continuous latent features to discrete codebook entries, where each codebook entry represents a grounded prototype
Classify by measuring similarity between quantized features and learned prototypes, producing “this looks like that” explanations where prototypes are guaranteed to exist in the training distribution
Train only the classification head and codebook, keeping the backbone frozen to reduce computational cost

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input Image] --> B[Frozen Backbone]
    B --> C[Latent Features]
    C --> D[Vector Quantization]
    D --> E[Discrete Codebook]
    E --> F[Prototype Matching]
    F --> G[Classification + Explanation]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale interpretable image classification (ImageNet-scale)
Fine-grained recognition tasks (birds, cars, products) where “this looks like that” explanations are valuable
Safety-critical domains requiring explainable decisions (medical imaging, autonomous vehicles)
Production scenarios where backbone finetuning is computationally prohibitive

Limitations / Caveats:

Discrete codebook limits expressiveness compared to continuous prototype spaces
Quantization introduces information bottleneck that may reduce accuracy on highly complex datasets
Interpretability relies on human ability to understand visual similarity to discrete prototypes
Performance depends heavily on codebook size and initialization strategy

Internal Use Cases:

Content Understanding: Interpretable image classification for feed ranking (explain why an image was classified as “food” by showing similar training examples)
Embedding Compression: Discrete codebook approach similar to vector quantization in DV365 for compressing high-dimensional embeddings
Multi-Surface Models: Frozen backbone + lightweight head enables sharing features across multiple classification tasks (similar to shared embedding approach in DV365)
Trust & Safety: Explainable content moderation where moderators can audit decisions via “this violates policy because it looks like [prototype example]”

literature/2026-02-09-QuantLRM-Quantizat.title: QuantLRM: Quantization of Large Reasoning Models via Fine-Tuning Signals
literature/2026-02-09-QuantLRM-Quantizat.content:
QuantLRM: Quantization of Large Reasoning Models via Fine-Tuning Signals
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-01-31
Rank: #9 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Weight-only quantization is important for compressing Large Language Models (LLMs). Inspired by the spirit of classical magnitude pruning, we study whether the magnitude of weight updates during reasoning-incentivized fine-tuning can provide valuable signals for quantizing Large Reasoning Models (LRMs). We hypothesize that the smallest and largest weight updates during fine-tuning are more important than those of intermediate magnitude, a phenomenon we term “protecting both ends”. Upon hypothesis validation, we introduce QuantLRM, which stands for weight quantization of LRMs via fine-tuning signals. We fit simple restricted quadratic functions on weight updates to protect both ends. By multiplying the average quadratic values with the count of zero weight updates of channels, we compute channel importance that is more effective than using activation or second-order information. We run QuantLRM to quantize various fine-tuned models (including supervised, direct preference optimization, and reinforcement learning fine-tuning) over four reasoning benchmarks (AIME-120, FOLIO, temporal sequences, and GPQA-Diamond) and empirically find that QuantLRM delivers a consistent improvement for LRMs quantization, with an average improvement of 6.55% on a reinforcement learning fine-tuned model. Also supporting non-fine-tuned LRMs, QuantLRM gathers effective signals via pseudo-fine-tuning, which greatly enhances its applicability.
Key Techniques

Magnitude-based weight update analysis
Quadratic function fitting for importance scoring
Pseudo-fine-tuning for signal extraction

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityOJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization0.23ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.18S2D: Selective Spectral Decay for Quantization-Friendly Conditioning of Neural Activations0.18ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.17BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.16
QuantLRM (Quantization of Large Reasoning Models via Fine-Tuning Signals)
Core Idea: Use the magnitude of weight updates during reasoning-incentivized fine-tuning as signals to identify which weights are important for quantization, protecting weights with both the smallest and largest update magnitudes.
Problem It Solves: Traditional weight-only quantization methods for LLMs don’t account for the unique properties of Large Reasoning Models (LRMs), where reasoning capabilities developed during fine-tuning may be lost when using generic importance metrics like activation statistics or second-order Hessian information.
How It Works:

Collect fine-tuning signals: Track weight updates (Δw) during reasoning-focused fine-tuning (supervised fine-tuning, DPO, or RL fine-tuning) or simulate them via pseudo-fine-tuning for non-fine-tuned models
Compute channel importance: Fit restricted quadratic functions on weight update magnitudes to identify “both ends” (smallest and largest updates), then multiply average quadratic values by the count of zero weight updates per channel
Apply quantization: Use the computed channel importance scores to protect critical channels during weight-only quantization, preserving reasoning capabilities while compressing the model

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Pre-trained LRM] --> B[Fine-tuning / Pseudo-FT]
    B --> C[Track Weight Updates Δw]
    C --> D[Fit Quadratic Function]
    D --> E[Compute Channel Importance]
    E --> F[Weight Quantization]
    F --> G[Compressed LRM]

Where It’s Useful:

Compressing reasoning models fine-tuned with RL (e.g., models trained with process reward models)
Quantizing instruction-tuned or DPO-aligned reasoning models for deployment
Reducing memory footprint of mathematical/logical reasoning models (AIME, GPQA benchmarks)
Edge deployment of reasoning-capable LLMs

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires access to fine-tuning data or ability to simulate pseudo-fine-tuning, adding computational overhead
The “protecting both ends” hypothesis may not generalize to all model architectures or reasoning task types
Quadratic function fitting introduces hyperparameters that may need task-specific tuning
Primarily validated on weight-only quantization; unclear if approach extends to activation quantization

Internal Use Cases:

Compressing reasoning-enhanced ranking models where multi-step inference is needed (e.g., query understanding with chain-of-thought)
Quantizing LLM-based feature extraction models that have been fine-tuned on domain-specific reasoning tasks
Deploying compressed reasoning models for real-time recommendation explanations while preserving logical coherence
Creating efficient student models via distillation where the teacher uses reasoning capabilities that must be preserved during compression

literature/2026-02-09-R2LED-Equipping-Re.title: R2LED: Equipping Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs for CTR Prediction
literature/2026-02-09-R2LED-Equipping-Re.content:
R2LED: Equipping Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs for CTR Prediction
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-06
Rank: #2 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, RAG, LLM
Code: github.com/abananbao/R2LED
Abstract
Lifelong user modeling, which leverages users’ long-term behavior sequences for CTR prediction, has been widely applied in personalized services. Existing methods generally adopted a two-stage “retrieval-refinement” strategy to balance effectiveness and efficiency. However, they still suffer from (i) noisy retrieval due to skewed data distribution and (ii) lack of semantic understanding in refinement. While semantic enhancement, e.g., LLMs modeling or semantic embeddings, offers potential solutions to these two challenges, these approaches face impractical inference costs or insufficient representation granularity. Obsorbing multi-granularity and lightness merits of semantic identity (SID), we propose a novel paradigm that equips retrieval and refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with SEmantic IDs (R2LED) to address these issues. First, we introduce a Multi-route Mixed Retrieval for the retrieval stage. On the one hand, it captures users’ interests from various granularities by several parallel recall routes. On the other hand, a mixed retrieval mechanism is proposed to efficiently retrieve candidates from both collaborative and semantic views, reducing noise. Then, for refinement, we design a Bi-level Fusion Refinement, including a target-aware cross-attention for route-level fusion and a gate mechanism for SID-level fusion. It can bridge the gap between semantic and collaborative spaces, exerting the merits of SID. The comprehensive experimental results on two public datasets demonstrate the superiority of our method in both performance and efficiency. To facilitate the reproduction, we have released the code online github.com/abananbao/R2LED.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques/methods:

Multi-route Mixed Retrieval

Bi-level Fusion Refinement
Semantic IDs (SID)

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.40GLASS: A Generative Recommender for Long-sequence Modeling via SID-Tier and Semantic Search0.39End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.35End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.32Unleash the Potential of Long Semantic IDs for Generative Recommendation0.30
R2LED (Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs)
Core Idea: Use multi-granularity semantic IDs to enhance both the retrieval and refinement stages of lifelong user modeling for CTR prediction, enabling mixed collaborative-semantic retrieval and bi-level fusion.
Problem It Solves:

Noisy retrieval in lifelong user modeling caused by skewed data distribution in long behavior sequences
Lack of semantic understanding in refinement stage where collaborative filtering alone misses content meaning
Impractical inference costs of LLM-based semantic enhancement at scale
Insufficient granularity of simple semantic embeddings for capturing multi-level user interests

How It Works:

Multi-route Mixed Retrieval: Generate semantic IDs (SIDs) for items using RQ-VAE to create hierarchical codes. Build parallel recall routes at different granularities (coarse to fine-grained SIDs). For each route, retrieve candidates using both collaborative signals (item ID embeddings) and semantic signals (SID-based retrieval), then merge results.
Bi-level Fusion Refinement: Apply target-aware cross-attention to fuse information across different retrieval routes (route-level fusion). Use gate mechanism to blend semantic ID representations with collaborative ID embeddings (SID-level fusion), bridging semantic and collaborative spaces.
CTR Prediction: Feed fused user representation and target item features into final ranking model for click-through rate prediction.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Long User History] --> B[Multi-route Retrieval]
    B --> C1[Route 1: Coarse SIDs]
    B --> C2[Route 2: Medium SIDs]
    B --> C3[Route 3: Fine SIDs]
    C1 --> D[Mixed Retrieval<br/>Collab + Semantic]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Bi-level Fusion]
    E --> F1[Route-level<br/>Cross-Attention]
    E --> F2[SID-level<br/>Gate Fusion]
    F1 --> G[CTR Model]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Prediction]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale recommendation systems with long user behavior histories (e-commerce, video platforms)
CTR prediction tasks requiring both collaborative filtering and semantic understanding
Cold-start scenarios where semantic similarity helps bridge missing collaborative signals
Systems needing efficient inference despite long sequence modeling

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires pre-training RQ-VAE to generate semantic IDs, adding infrastructure complexity
Multi-route retrieval increases memory footprint compared to single-route methods
Effectiveness depends on quality of semantic ID codebook learned from data distribution
Additional hyperparameters (number of routes, granularity levels, fusion weights) require tuning

Internal Use Cases:

Prompt compression for history-based retrieval: Replace long concatenated text histories with compact semantic ID sequences, dramatically reducing token count while preserving semantic information for two-tower models
Multi-granularity interest modeling: Capture user interests at different abstraction levels (topic-level to item-level) using hierarchical SIDs from RQ-VAE codebooks
Hybrid retrieval systems: Combine collaborative filtering (user-item interactions) with semantic matching (content similarity) by indexing both ID embeddings and SID codes
Cold-start mitigation: Leverage semantic IDs to retrieve relevant items for new users based on content similarity when behavioral signals are sparse

literature/2026-02-09-RaBiT-Residual-Awa.title: RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs
literature/2026-02-09-RaBiT-Residual-Awa.content:
RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-05
Rank: #8 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, LLM, RAG
Abstract
Efficient deployment of large language models (LLMs) requires extreme quantization, forcing a critical trade-off between low-bit efficiency and performance. Residual binarization enables hardware-friendly, matmul-free inference by stacking binary (pm1) layers, but is plagued by pathological feature co-adaptation. We identify a key failure mode, which we term inter-path adaptation: during quantization-aware training (QAT), parallel residual binary paths learn redundant features, degrading the error-compensation structure and limiting the expressive capacity of the model. While prior work relies on heuristic workarounds (e.g., path freezing) that constrain the solution space, we propose RaBiT, a novel quantization framework that resolves co-adaptation by algorithmically enforcing a residual hierarchy. Its core mechanism sequentially derives each binary path from a single shared full-precision weight, which ensures that every path corrects the error of the preceding one. This process is stabilized by a robust initialization that prioritizes functional preservation over mere weight approximation. RaBiT redefines the 2-bit accuracy-efficiency frontier: it achieves state-of-the-art performance, rivals even hardware-intensive Vector Quantization (VQ) methods, and delivers a 4.49times inference speed-up over full-precision models on an RTX 4090.
Key Techniques

Residual binarization with sequential path derivation
quantization-aware training with inter-path adaptation enforcement
robust initialization for functional preservation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityRaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs0.90NanoQuant: Efficient Sub-1-Bit Quantization of Large Language Models0.29BitDance: Scaling Autoregressive Generative Models with Binary Tokens0.241-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization0.21Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning0.21
RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training
Core Idea: A quantization framework that achieves 2-bit LLM inference by sequentially deriving binary (±1) residual paths from a single shared full-precision weight, ensuring each path corrects the error of the preceding one rather than learning redundant features.
Problem It Solves: Addresses inter-path adaptation in residual binarization—a pathological failure mode where parallel binary paths learn redundant features during quantization-aware training (QAT), degrading error-compensation structure and limiting model expressiveness. Previous methods relied on heuristic workarounds like path freezing that constrain the solution space.
How It Works:

Sequential Path Derivation: Rather than training multiple binary paths in parallel, each binary path is sequentially derived from a single shared full-precision weight matrix W
Error Correction Hierarchy: Path k is explicitly optimized to correct the quantization error left by paths 1 through k-1, enforcing a strict residual hierarchy: W ≈ α₁B₁ + α₂B₂ + … + αₖBₖ where each Bᵢ ∈ {-1,+1}
Robust Initialization: Uses a functional-preservation initialization strategy that prioritizes maintaining model output quality over naive weight approximation during the initial binarization step
Matmul-Free Inference: At inference, binary weights enable hardware-friendly operations using only additions/subtractions (no floating-point multiplications)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Full-Precision Weight W] --> B[Binary Path 1: B₁]
    A --> C[Compute Error: ε₁ = W - α₁B₁]
    C --> D[Binary Path 2: B₂ corrects ε₁]
    D --> E[Compute Error: ε₂ = ε₁ - α₂B₂]
    E --> F[Binary Path k corrects ε₂...]
    F --> G[Final 2-bit Model: Σ αᵢBᵢ]
    G --> H[Matmul-Free Inference]

Where It’s Useful:

Edge deployment of large language models where memory and compute are severely constrained
Real-time inference scenarios requiring minimal latency and power consumption
Hardware accelerators optimized for binary operations (FPGAs, custom ASICs)
Serving LLMs at scale where 4.49× speedup translates to significant cost savings

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires quantization-aware training (QAT) which adds training complexity compared to post-training quantization methods
2-bit quantization still incurs some accuracy degradation compared to full-precision, though RaBiT minimizes this gap
Sequential path derivation may increase training time compared to parallel path optimization
Effectiveness at scales beyond tested models (exact size not specified in abstract) remains to be validated

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: Deploy embedding models and neural rankers in 2-bit for low-latency candidate scoring at massive scale (billions of items)
LLM Serving: Reduce inference cost for text generation, summarization, and semantic search backends where GPU memory is bottleneck
Federated Learning: Enable on-device LLM fine-tuning and inference on mobile/IoT devices with limited compute
A/B Testing: Run multiple compressed model variants simultaneously within same memory budget to accelerate experimentation

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-09-RelayGen-Intra-Gen.title: RelayGen: Intra-Generation Model Switching for Efficient Reasoning
literature/2026-02-09-RelayGen-Intra-Gen.content:
RelayGen: Intra-Generation Model Switching for Efficient Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-06
Rank: #5 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Large reasoning models (LRMs) achieve strong performance on complex reasoning tasks by generating long, multi-step reasoning trajectories, but inference-time scaling incurs substantial deployment cost. A key challenge is that generation difficulty varies within a single output, whereas existing efficiency-oriented approaches either ignore this intra-generation variation or rely on supervised token-level routing with high system complexity. We present \textbf{RelayGen}, a training-free, segment-level runtime model switching framework that exploits difficulty variation in long-form reasoning. Through offline analysis of generation uncertainty using token probability margins, we show that coarse-grained segment-level control is sufficient to capture difficulty transitions within a reasoning trajectory. RelayGen identifies model-specific switch cues that signal transitions to lower-difficulty segments and dynamically delegates their continuation to a smaller model, while preserving high-difficulty reasoning on the large model. Across multiple reasoning benchmarks, RelayGen substantially reduces inference latency while preserving most of the accuracy of large models. When combined with speculative decoding, RelayGen achieves up to 2.2\times end-to-end speedup with less than 2% accuracy degradation, without requiring additional training or learned routing components.
Key Techniques

Training-free segment-level runtime model switching
token probability margin analysis for difficulty detection
dynamic delegation to smaller models
speculative decoding integration

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityIs my model “mind blurting”? Interpreting the dynamics of reasoning tokens with Recurrence Quantification Analysis (RQA)0.20SwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMs0.20S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation0.19Improving Data and Reward Design for Scientific Reasoning in Large Language Models0.19LogitsCoder: Towards Efficient Chain-of-Thought Path Search via Logits Preference Decoding for Code Generation0.17
RelayGen (Relay Generation)
Core Idea: Training-free segment-level model switching that dynamically delegates low-difficulty portions of long reasoning trajectories to smaller models while keeping high-difficulty reasoning on large models, guided by offline-analyzed token probability margins.
Problem It Solves: Large reasoning models (LRMs) generate expensive long-form reasoning chains where generation difficulty varies significantly within a single output, but existing efficiency methods either ignore this intra-generation variation or require complex supervised token-level routing.
How It Works:

Offline Analysis: Analyze token probability margins across reasoning trajectories to identify model-specific difficulty patterns and switch cues that signal transitions to easier segments
Runtime Monitoring: During generation with the large model, monitor for learned switch cues that indicate upcoming low-difficulty segments
Dynamic Delegation: When a switch cue is detected, transfer generation control to a smaller model for the easier continuation, then return to the large model when difficulty increases or segment ends
Coarse-Grained Control: Operate at segment level (multi-token chunks) rather than token-by-token, reducing switching overhead while capturing meaningful difficulty transitions

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input Query] --> B[Large Model<br/>High-Difficulty<br/>Reasoning]
    B --> C{Switch Cue<br/>Detected?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Small Model<br/>Low-Difficulty<br/>Continuation]
    C -->|No| B
    D --> E{Difficulty<br/>Increase?}
    E -->|Yes| B
    E -->|No| D
    B --> F[Final Output]
    D --> F

Where It’s Useful:

Long-form reasoning tasks (math, code generation, multi-step problem solving)
Production deployments where inference cost dominates
Scenarios requiring response quality with tight latency budgets
Compatible with speculative decoding for compounded speedups (2.2× observed)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires offline analysis phase to identify model-specific switch cues and difficulty patterns
Segment-level switching may miss fine-grained difficulty variations within segments
Effectiveness depends on difficulty variation within reasoning trajectories (less benefit for uniformly hard problems)
Switch cue detection accuracy affects overall performance preservation
Up to 2% accuracy degradation observed even with best configurations

Internal Use Cases:

Hybrid reasoning systems where expensive LRM calls can be selectively replaced with cheaper models for straightforward explanation/justification portions
Multi-stage ranking pipelines that generate textual reasoning (e.g., why this item matches) - delegate simple match explanations to small models
LLM-based feature generation where some features require deep reasoning while others are formulaic - route accordingly
Real-time recommendation explanations where latency matters but quality degradation must stay minimal

literature/2026-02-09-SPARC-Separating-P.title: SPARC: Separating Perception And Reasoning Circuits for Test-time Scaling of VLMs
literature/2026-02-09-SPARC-Separating-P.content:
SPARC: Separating Perception And Reasoning Circuits for Test-time Scaling of VLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-06
Rank: #6 (score: 0.92, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Despite recent successes, test-time scaling - i.e., dynamically expanding the token budget during inference as needed - remains brittle for vision-language models (VLMs): unstructured chains-of-thought about images entangle perception and reasoning, leading to long, disorganized contexts where small perceptual mistakes may cascade into completely wrong answers. Moreover, expensive reinforcement learning with hand-crafted rewards is required to achieve good performance. Here, we introduce SPARC (Separating Perception And Reasoning Circuits), a modular framework that explicitly decouples visual perception from reasoning. Inspired by sequential sensory-to-cognitive processing in the brain, SPARC implements a two-stage pipeline where the model first performs explicit visual search to localize question-relevant regions, then conditions its reasoning on those regions to produce the final answer. This separation enables independent test-time scaling with asymmetric compute allocation (e.g., prioritizing perceptual processing under distribution shift), supports selective optimization (e.g., improving the perceptual stage alone when it is the bottleneck for end-to-end performance), and accommodates compressed contexts by running global search at lower image resolutions and allocating high-resolution processing only to selected regions, thereby reducing total visual tokens count and compute. Across challenging visual reasoning benchmarks, SPARC outperforms monolithic baselines and strong visual-grounding approaches. For instance, SPARC improves the accuracy of Qwen3VL-4B on the V^* VQA benchmark by 6.7 percentage points, and it surpasses “thinking with images” by 4.6 points on a challenging OOD task despite requiring a 200\times lower token budget.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques or methods:

Visual search and region localization

Two-stage pipeline separation of perception and reasoning
Asymmetric compute allocation during test-time scaling
Multi-resolution processing with selective high-resolution focus

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDo MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.45Thinking with Drafting: Optical Decompression via Logical Reconstruction0.35ClueTracer: Question-to-Vision Clue Tracing for Training-Free Hallucination Suppression in Multimodal Reasoning0.33VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.32ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.30
SPARC (Separating Perception And Reasoning Circuits)
Core Idea: Decouple visual perception from reasoning in VLMs by explicitly separating inference into two stages—visual search to localize relevant regions, then reasoning conditioned only on those regions—enabling independent test-time scaling and more robust error correction.
Problem It Solves: Traditional VLMs entangle perception and reasoning in unstructured chains-of-thought, causing small perceptual errors to cascade into wrong answers, requiring expensive RL with hand-crafted rewards, and making test-time compute allocation brittle across distribution shifts.
How It Works:

Visual Search Stage: Model performs explicit visual grounding to identify and localize question-relevant regions in the image (can run at lower resolution globally to reduce token count)
Region Selection: Extract and potentially process selected regions at higher resolution, creating a compressed visual context focused only on relevant areas
Reasoning Stage: Condition reasoning chain generation only on the localized regions (not the full image), producing the final answer with reduced context and fewer cascading errors

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Image + Question] --> B[Visual Search<br/>Low-res global scan]
    B --> C[Region Selection<br/>High-res crops]
    C --> D[Reasoning Circuit<br/>Conditioned on regions]
    D --> E[Final Answer]

Where It’s Useful:

Visual question answering with complex spatial reasoning (e.g., V* VQA benchmark)
Out-of-distribution visual tasks where perceptual grounding is the bottleneck
Resource-constrained inference scenarios requiring efficient token budgets (200× reduction vs. full-image reasoning)
Applications requiring interpretable visual grounding before reasoning

Limitations / Caveats:

Two-stage pipeline adds latency compared to single-pass inference (though total tokens may be lower)
Effectiveness depends on quality of visual search stage—if relevant regions are missed, reasoning fails regardless of compute
May struggle with holistic image understanding tasks where global context is essential (the separation assumes localized perception suffices)

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys/Ranking: Apply staged filtering—coarse retrieval (visual search analog) → fine-grained ranking (reasoning analog)—with asymmetric compute allocation based on confidence or domain shift
LLM Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Separate “search for relevant context” from “reason over context,” enabling independent scaling of retrieval vs. generation compute
Multimodal recommendation: First stage localizes relevant visual features in product images (logo, texture, color), second stage reasons about fit with user preferences—allows high-res processing only where needed

literature/2026-02-09-Stop-the-Flip-Flop.title: Stop the Flip-Flop: Context-Preserving Verification for Fast Revocable Diffusion Decoding
literature/2026-02-09-Stop-the-Flip-Flop.content:
Stop the Flip-Flop: Context-Preserving Verification for Fast Revocable Diffusion Decoding
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-05
Rank: #3 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Parallel diffusion decoding can accelerate diffusion language model inference by unmasking multiple tokens per step, but aggressive parallelism often harms quality. Revocable decoding mitigates this by rechecking earlier tokens, yet we observe that existing verification schemes frequently trigger flip-flop oscillations, where tokens are remasked and later restored unchanged. This behaviour slows inference in two ways: remasking verified positions weakens the conditioning context for parallel drafting, and repeated remask cycles consume the revision budget with little net progress. We propose COVER (Cache Override Verification for Efficient Revision), which performs leave-one-out verification and stable drafting within a single forward pass. COVER constructs two attention views via KV cache override: selected seeds are masked for verification, while their cached key value states are injected for all other queries to preserve contextual information, with a closed form diagonal correction preventing self leakage at the seed positions. COVER further prioritises seeds using a stability aware score that balances uncertainty, downstream influence, and cache drift, and it adapts the number of verified seeds per step. Across benchmarks, COVER markedly reduces unnecessary revisions and yields faster decoding while preserving output quality.
Key Techniques

Cache Override Verification
Leave-One-Out Verification
Stability-Aware Seed Prioritization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityStop the Flip-Flop: Context-Preserving Verification for Fast Revocable Diffusion Decoding0.91Greedy Multi-Path Block Verification for Faster Decoding in Speculative Sampling0.23DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.22FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion0.20Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.20
COVER (Cache Override Verification for Efficient Revision)
Core Idea: Performs leave-one-out verification and parallel drafting in a single forward pass by constructing dual attention views via KV cache override—masking selected seeds for verification while injecting their cached states for all other positions to preserve context.
Problem It Solves: Existing revocable diffusion decoding schemes cause flip-flop oscillations where tokens are repeatedly remasked and later restored unchanged, wasting both the revision budget and degrading conditioning context for parallel token generation.
How It Works:

Dual-view construction: For a set of selected seed positions, construct two attention patterns simultaneously—mask the seeds in their own queries (for verification), but inject their cached key-value states for all non-seed queries (to preserve context).
Self-leakage prevention: Apply a closed-form diagonal correction that prevents seed positions from attending to their own cached states during verification while maintaining full context for drafting new tokens.
Stability-aware seed selection: Prioritize which tokens to verify using a composite score that balances token uncertainty, downstream influence on other positions, and KV cache drift since the token was last generated.
Adaptive verification budget: Dynamically adjust the number of seeds verified per step based on model confidence and remaining generation steps.

Where It’s Useful:

Accelerating diffusion-based language model inference where quality-speed tradeoffs matter
Parallel decoding scenarios requiring adaptive token revision without context degradation
Applications needing faster controllable text generation (e.g., interactive editing, constrained generation)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires KV cache manipulation capabilities in the inference framework
Overhead of dual attention view construction may not amortize for very small models or short sequences
Stability scoring adds compute compared to naive random seed selection

Internal Use Cases:

LLM serving optimization: Apply COVER’s cache-preserving verification to speculative decoding or draft-then-verify inference patterns to reduce wasted speculation cycles
Batch inference: Stability-aware seed selection could inform dynamic batching strategies—prioritize verifying high-uncertainty tokens that block downstream generation
Retrieval-augmented generation: Cache override technique could enable efficient re-verification of retrieved context without full recomputation when documents are updated

literature/2026-02-09-Uncertainty-Drives.title: Uncertainty Drives Social Bias Changes in Quantized Large Language Models
literature/2026-02-09-Uncertainty-Drives.content:
Uncertainty Drives Social Bias Changes in Quantized Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-05
Rank: #10 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.80)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Post-training quantization reduces the computational cost of large language models but fundamentally alters their social biases in ways that aggregate metrics fail to capture. We present the first large-scale study of 50 quantized models evaluated on PostTrainingBiasBench, a unified benchmark of 13 closed- and open-ended bias datasets. We identify a phenomenon we term quantization-induced masked bias flipping, in which up to 21% of responses flip between biased and unbiased states after quantization, despite showing no change in aggregate bias scores. These flips are strongly driven by model uncertainty, where the responses with high uncertainty are 3-11x more likely to change than the confident ones. Quantization strength amplifies this effect, with 4-bit quantized models exhibiting 4-6x more behavioral changes than 8-bit quantized models. Critically, these changes create asymmetric impacts across demographic groups, where bias can worsen by up to 18.6% for some groups while improving by 14.1% for others, yielding misleadingly neutral aggregate outcomes. Larger models show no consistent robustness advantage, and group-specific shifts vary unpredictably across model families. Our findings demonstrate that compression fundamentally alters bias patterns, requiring crucial post-quantization evaluation and interventions to ensure reliability in practice.
Key Techniques

Post-training quantization
PostTrainingBiasBench benchmark
Quantization-induced masked bias flipping
Model uncertainty analysis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityToward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning0.16BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.15Toward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning0.14QuEPT: Quantized Elastic Precision Transformers with One-Shot Calibration for Multi-Bit Switching0.14OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization0.14
Quantization-Induced Masked Bias Flipping
Core Idea: Post-training quantization causes up to 21% of individual LLM responses to flip between biased and unbiased states without changing aggregate bias scores, driven by model uncertainty and creating asymmetric demographic impacts.
Problem It Solves: Aggregate bias metrics (e.g., average bias scores) mask critical instance-level changes after quantization, failing to detect that compression fundamentally alters which specific responses exhibit bias and which demographic groups are affected.
How It Works:

Quantize model using post-training quantization (PTQ) at different bit-widths (4-bit, 8-bit)
Evaluate both versions (full-precision and quantized) on the same bias benchmark instances across 13 datasets (PostTrainingBiasBench)
Compare instance-level responses to identify flips: responses that were biased → unbiased or unbiased → biased
Measure uncertainty (e.g., token probability distributions) to identify that high-uncertainty responses are 3-11× more likely to flip
Track group-specific changes revealing that bias can worsen by 18.6% for some demographic groups while improving 14.1% for others

Where It’s Useful:

Model compression auditing: Ensuring quantized models deployed in production don’t introduce hidden demographic harms
Uncertainty-aware bias mitigation: Prioritizing bias interventions on high-uncertainty predictions most likely to change after compression
Fair deployment of compressed models: Mobile/edge AI systems where quantization is necessary but bias consistency is critical

Limitations / Caveats:

Larger models show no consistent robustness advantage against bias flipping
Group-specific shifts vary unpredictably across model families (no universal pattern)
Requires instance-level evaluation infrastructure, not just aggregate metrics
4-bit quantization exhibits 4-6× more flips than 8-bit, limiting aggressive compression strategies

Internal Use Cases:

Recommendation models: Compressed ranking models may flip which content gets promoted to different demographic segments, creating inconsistent user experiences
On-device LLMs: Quantized assistant models on mobile could exhibit different bias patterns than cloud versions, requiring separate bias audits
A/B testing compressed models: Traditional aggregate metrics may show “no change” while individual user interactions experience significant bias shifts

literature/2026-02-09-When-RL-Meets-Adap.title: When RL Meets Adaptive Speculative Training: A Unified Training-Serving System
literature/2026-02-09-When-RL-Meets-Adap.content:
When RL Meets Adaptive Speculative Training: A Unified Training-Serving System
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-06
Rank: #1 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Speculative decoding can significantly accelerate LLM serving, yet most deployments today disentangle speculator training from serving, treating speculator training as a standalone offline modeling problem. We show that this decoupled formulation introduces substantial deployment and adaptation lag: (1) high time-to-serve, since a speculator must be trained offline for a considerable period before deployment; (2) delayed utility feedback, since the true end-to-end decoding speedup is only known after training and cannot be inferred reliably from acceptance rate alone due to model-architecture and system-level overheads; and (3) domain-drift degradation, as the target model is repurposed to new domains and the speculator becomes stale and less effective.   To address these issues, we present Aurora, a unified training-serving system that closes the loop by continuously learning a speculator directly from live inference traces. Aurora reframes online speculator learning as an asynchronous reinforcement-learning problem: accepted tokens provide positive feedback, while rejected speculator proposals provide implicit negative feedback that we exploit to improve sample efficiency. Our design integrates an SGLang-based inference server with an asynchronous training server, enabling hot-swapped speculator updates without service interruption. Crucially, Aurora supports day-0 deployment: a speculator can be served immediately and rapidly adapted to live traffic, improving system performance while providing immediate utility feedback. Across experiments, Aurora achieves a 1.5x day-0 speedup on recently released frontier models (e.g., MiniMax M2.1 229B and Qwen3-Coder-Next 80B). Aurora also adapts effectively to distribution shifts in user traffic, delivering an additional 1.25x speedup over a well-trained but static speculator on widely used models (e.g., Qwen3 and Llama3).
Key Techniques

Asynchronous reinforcement learning
Live inference trace feedback
Hot-swapped speculator updates

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityVariational Speculative Decoding: Rethinking Draft Training from Token Likelihood to Sequence Acceptance0.19DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.17SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration0.17PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models0.16Speculative Decoding with a Speculative Vocabulary0.16
Aurora: RL-Based Online Speculative Decoding
Core Idea: A unified training-serving system that continuously learns and updates a speculative decoder directly from live inference traces using reinforcement learning, enabling day-0 deployment and runtime adaptation to distribution shifts.
Problem It Solves: Traditional speculative decoding separates training from serving, causing (1) high time-to-serve since speculators must be fully trained offline before deployment, (2) delayed utility feedback—true speedup is only known after training completes, and (3) domain-drift degradation when the target LLM is repurposed to new domains.
How It Works:

Immediate Deployment: Deploy an initial speculator (even untrained) alongside the target LLM to start serving traffic immediately
