Live Trace Collection: During inference, collect accepted and rejected draft tokens from the speculative decoding process
Asynchronous RL Training: Frame speculator learning as an RL problem where accepted tokens provide positive rewards and rejected proposals provide negative feedback; train the speculator asynchronously on these traces
Hot-Swap Updates: Periodically update the serving speculator with improved checkpoints without service interruption
Continuous Adaptation: As traffic patterns or target LLM domains shift, the speculator automatically adapts through ongoing learning

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Inference Traffic] --> B[SGLang Serving]
    B --> C[Draft Acceptance/Rejection]
    C --> D[Trace Buffer]
    D --> E[Async RL Trainer]
    E --> F[Updated Speculator]
    F -.Hot Swap.-> B
    C --> G[Accelerated Generation]

Where It’s Useful:

Production LLM serving where target models evolve or get repurposed across domains
Scenarios requiring immediate deployment without long offline training periods
Multi-tenant or multi-domain serving where traffic distributions vary over time
Frontier models where pre-training domain-specific speculators offline is impractical

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires infrastructure to support asynchronous training and hot-swapping of models during serving
Initial day-0 performance depends on the quality of the starting speculator (though any speculator can be deployed)
RL training efficiency depends on sufficient traffic volume to collect diverse acceptance/rejection signals
System complexity increases compared to static speculative decoding

Internal Use Cases:

LLM Serving: Deploy new LLMs to production immediately with adaptive speculation, eliminating the offline training bottleneck for each new model variant
Multi-Domain RecSys/Ranking: Apply the RL-from-feedback paradigm to continuously adapt lightweight draft models for candidate generation in recommendation systems as user behavior shifts
A/B Testing: Rapidly iterate on speculator architectures by deploying candidates and measuring true end-to-end latency metrics, not just proxy metrics like acceptance rate

literature/2026-02-10-Attn-GS-Attention-.title: Attn-GS: Attention-Guided Context Compression for Efficient Personalized LLMs
literature/2026-02-10-Attn-GS-Attention-.content:
Attn-GS: Attention-Guided Context Compression for Efficient Personalized LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-08
Rank: #7 (score: 0.92, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Personalizing large language models (LLMs) to individual users requires incorporating extensive interaction histories and profiles, but input token constraints make this impractical due to high inference latency and API costs. Existing approaches rely on heuristic methods such as selecting recent interactions or prompting summarization models to compress user profiles. However, these methods treat context as a monolithic whole and fail to consider how LLMs internally process and prioritize different profile components. We investigate whether LLMs’ attention patterns can effectively identify important personalization signals for intelligent context compression. Through preliminary studies on representative personalization tasks, we discover that (a) LLMs’ attention patterns naturally reveal important signals, and (b) fine-tuning enhances LLMs’ ability to distinguish between relevant and irrelevant information. Based on these insights, we propose Attn-GS, an attention-guided context compression framework that leverages attention feedback from a marking model to mark important personalization sentences, then guides a compression model to generate task-relevant, high-quality compressed user contexts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Attn-GS significantly outperforms various baselines across different tasks, token limits, and settings, achieving performance close to using full context while reducing token usage by 50 times.
Key Techniques

Attention-guided context compression
attention pattern analysis
fine-tuning for signal distinction

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityData Distribution Matters: A Data-Centric Perspective on Context Compression for Large Language Model0.25ATACompressor: Adaptive Task-Aware Compression for Efficient Long-Context Processing in LLMs0.24LASER: An Efficient Target-Aware Segmented Attention Framework for End-to-End Long Sequence Modeling0.24SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.23Less Is More — Until It Breaks: Security Pitfalls of Vision Token Compression in Large Vision-Language Models0.22
Attn-GS (Attention-Guided Context Compression)
Core Idea: Use attention patterns from a fine-tuned marking model to identify and preserve important personalization signals, then guide a compression model to generate condensed user contexts that retain task-relevant information.
Problem It Solves: Existing context compression methods use heuristic selection (e.g., recency) or blind summarization that treats user profiles as monolithic text, failing to leverage how LLMs internally prioritize information during personalization tasks, resulting in poor quality-compression tradeoffs.
How It Works:

Marking Phase: Fine-tune a marking model on personalization tasks to produce attention patterns that highlight important sentences in long user profiles/histories
Extraction: Aggregate attention weights across layers/heads to compute sentence-level importance scores and mark high-scoring sentences as salient
Compression Phase: Feed marked context (with importance annotations) into a compression model that generates a condensed summary preserving marked information while discarding irrelevant details
Inference: Use compressed context for downstream personalization tasks (recommendation, QA, generation)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Long User Profile] --> B[Marking Model\nFine-tuned LLM]
    B --> C[Attention Analysis\nSentence Scoring]
    C --> D[Marked Context\nImportant sentences tagged]
    D --> E[Compression Model]
    E --> F[Compressed Context\n50x smaller]
    F --> G[Task LLM\nPersonalized output]

Where It’s Useful:

Personalized chatbots with extensive user interaction histories
Recommendation systems requiring user profile context
Long-document QA where user preferences guide relevance
API-based LLM applications with strict token budgets

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires task-specific fine-tuning of the marking model to produce useful attention patterns
Two-stage pipeline adds latency compared to direct compression
Effectiveness depends on attention patterns actually correlating with semantic importance (may not generalize to all task types)
Compression quality degrades at extreme compression ratios beyond 50x

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys personalization: Compress user behavioral histories (clicks, purchases, reviews) to fit within ranking model context windows while preserving signals for preference learning
LLM-based ranking: Reduce token costs for query-document pairs by compressing document contexts guided by query-relevant attention patterns
Conversational AI: Maintain long conversation histories in compressed form, expanding only attention-marked relevant turns for context-aware responses

literature/2026-02-10-DeltaKV-Residual-B.title: DeltaKV: Residual-Based KV Cache Compression via Long-Range Similarity
literature/2026-02-10-DeltaKV-Residual-B.content:
DeltaKV: Residual-Based KV Cache Compression via Long-Range Similarity
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-08
Rank: #1 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The deployment of efficient long-context LLMs in applications like autonomous agents, long-chain reasoning, and creative writing is fundamentally bottlenecked by the linear growth of KV cache memory. Existing compression and eviction methods often struggle to balance accuracy, compression ratio, and hardware efficiency. We propose DeltaKV, a residual-based KV cache compression framework motivated by two empirical findings: long-range inter-token similarity and highly shared latent components in KV representations. Instead of discarding tokens, DeltaKV encodes semantic residuals relative to retrieved historical references, preserving fidelity while substantially reducing storage. To translate compression gains into real system speedups, we further introduce Sparse-vLLM, a high-performance inference engine with decoupled memory management and kernels optimized for sparse and irregular KV layouts. Experiments show that DeltaKV reduces KV cache memory to 29% of the original while maintaining near-lossless accuracy on LongBench, SCBench, and AIME. When integrated with Sparse-vLLM, it achieves up to 2\times throughput improvement over vLLM in long-context scenarios, demonstrating a practical path toward scalable long-context LLM deployment. Code, model checkpoints, and datasets are available at github.com/CURRENTF/Sparse-vLLM.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques:

Residual-based KV cache compression

Sparse kernel optimization
Historical reference retrieval

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLearning to Evict from Key-Value Cache0.28SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.26SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.24Hierarchical Adaptive Eviction for KV Cache Management in Multimodal Language Models0.23FASA: Frequency-aware Sparse Attention0.23
DeltaKV: Residual-Based KV Cache Compression
Core Idea: Compress KV cache by storing only semantic residuals relative to similar historical tokens rather than full key-value vectors, exploiting long-range inter-token similarity to reduce memory while preserving accuracy.
Problem It Solves: KV cache memory grows linearly with context length, creating a fundamental bottleneck for long-context LLM inference (100K+ tokens) in applications like autonomous agents and long-chain reasoning. Existing eviction methods sacrifice accuracy, while naive compression lacks hardware efficiency.
How It Works:

Similarity Detection: For each new token’s KV pair, retrieve the most similar historical token from cache using learned similarity metrics
Residual Encoding: Compute and store only the difference (residual) between the new KV vector and the retrieved reference: ΔKV = KV_new - KV_reference
Reference Indexing: Maintain lightweight pointers to reference tokens, exploiting the observation that KV representations share highly similar latent components across long distances
Reconstruction: During attention, reconstruct full KV vectors on-demand: KV_reconstructed = ΔKV + KV_reference

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[New Token KV] --> B[Similarity Search]
    C[Historical KV Cache] --> B
    B --> D[Find Best Reference]
    D --> E[Compute Residual]
    E --> F[Store ΔKV + Pointer]
    F --> G[Compressed Cache]
    G --> H[On-demand Reconstruct]
    H --> I[Attention Computation]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context document understanding (legal, medical, technical documents)
Autonomous agents with persistent memory across extended conversations
Creative writing and story generation requiring coherence over 50K+ tokens
Long-chain reasoning tasks (math proofs, multi-hop QA)
Code analysis over entire repositories

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires training/fine-tuning for optimal similarity metric learning
Reconstruction overhead adds latency per token if not properly optimized (hence need for Sparse-vLLM engine)
Effectiveness depends on actual inter-token similarity distribution in the workload
May underperform on completely random or adversarial sequences where similarity assumptions break down

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys context compression: Compress user interaction history embeddings in session-based recommendation by storing residuals relative to archetypal user behavior patterns
Ranking model serving: Reduce memory for document encoders in multi-stage ranking by caching only residuals relative to cluster centroids
LLM inference optimization: Deploy for internal long-context RAG systems, reducing serving costs for documentation QA and code assistance tools
Embedding cache efficiency: Apply residual encoding to frequently-accessed embedding tables in real-time feature stores

literature/2026-02-10-Do-MLLMs-Really-Se.title: Do MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs
literature/2026-02-10-Do-MLLMs-Really-Se.content:
Do MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-09
Rank: #3 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RL, LLM, RAG
Abstract
While chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning has substantially improved multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on complex reasoning tasks, existing approaches largely rely on long textual reasoning trajectories and provide limited mechanisms for learning stable visual attention policies. Our analysis shows that current MLLMs exhibit weak visual focus: early-stage visual misalignment is rarely corrected during subsequent reasoning, leading to error propagation and failed inferences. We argue that this limitation stems from inadequate credit assignment for visual attention during training. To address this issue, we propose SAYO, a visual reasoning model trained with a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that introduces a region-level visual attention-based reward. This reward explicitly aligns optimization signals with visually grounded reasoning steps, enabling the model to learn more reliable attention behaviors. Extensive experiments across multiple multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that SAYO consistently improves performance on diverse reasoning and perception tasks.
Key Techniques

Chain-of-thought reasoning
Reinforcement learning with region-level visual attention rewards
Visual attention policy learning
Credit assignment for visual attention

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityVIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.51Thinking with Drafting: Optical Decompression via Logical Reconstruction0.50ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.47ClueTracer: Question-to-Vision Clue Tracing for Training-Free Hallucination Suppression in Multimodal Reasoning0.46SPARC: Separating Perception And Reasoning Circuits for Test-time Scaling of VLMs0.45
SAYO (Self-Adjusted Yielding Observation)
Core Idea: A reinforcement learning framework that trains multimodal LLMs with explicit region-level visual attention rewards to learn stable visual grounding policies during chain-of-thought reasoning.
Problem It Solves: Current MLLMs exhibit weak visual attention during reasoning—early-stage visual misalignment propagates through reasoning chains without correction because training lacks proper credit assignment for where the model looks, leading to failed inferences even with correct reasoning structure.
How It Works:

During chain-of-thought generation, track region-level visual attention at each reasoning step
Apply RL-based training with a novel attention-based reward signal that explicitly scores visual grounding quality (which image regions are attended to)
Backpropagate reward signals to optimize both reasoning trajectory generation and visual attention policy simultaneously
The model learns to self-correct visual focus during multi-step reasoning by associating final task success with intermediate attention patterns

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Image + Question] --> B[MLLM with Attention Tracking]
    B --> C[CoT Reasoning Steps]
    C --> D[Region-Level Attention Map]
    D --> E[Visual Attention Reward]
    E --> F[RL Optimization]
    F --> B
    C --> G[Final Answer]

Where It’s Useful:

Complex visual reasoning tasks requiring multi-hop inference over image regions
Multimodal perception benchmarks where visual grounding accuracy matters
Training MLLMs for structured visual question answering with interpretable attention
Applications requiring reliable visual alignment (medical imaging, document understanding, spatial reasoning)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires ground-truth region annotations or proxy signals to compute attention rewards during training
RL training adds computational overhead compared to supervised fine-tuning alone
Performance gains depend on task requiring explicit visual grounding (less benefit for purely semantic tasks)

Internal Use Cases:

Improve visual grounding in multimodal recommendation systems that need to understand product images or user-generated content
Train ranking models to focus on relevant visual regions when scoring image-text pairs
Enable LLM-based visual agents to maintain stable attention across multi-step tool use (e.g., UI navigation, visual search)
Apply RL-based attention optimization to teach retrieval models which visual features matter most for semantic matching

literature/2026-02-10-DynamiQ-Accelerati.title: DynamiQ: Accelerating Gradient Synchronization using Compressed Multi-hop All-reduce
literature/2026-02-10-DynamiQ-Accelerati.content:
DynamiQ: Accelerating Gradient Synchronization using Compressed Multi-hop All-reduce
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-09
Rank: #10 (score: 0.52, max_sim: 0.79)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Multi-hop all-reduce is the de facto backbone of large model training. As the training scale increases, the network often becomes a bottleneck, motivating reducing the volume of transmitted data. Accordingly, recent systems demonstrated significant acceleration of the training process using gradient quantization. However, these systems are not optimized for multi-hop aggregation, where entries are partially summed multiple times along their aggregation topology.   This paper presents DynamiQ, a quantization framework that bridges the gap between quantization best practices and multi-hop aggregation. DynamiQ introduces novel techniques to better represent partial sums, co-designed with a decompress-accumulate-recompress fused kernel to facilitate fast execution.   We extended PyTorch DDP to support DynamiQ over NCCL P2P, and across different LLMs, tasks, and scales, we demonstrate consistent improvement of up to 34.2% over the best among state-of-the-art methods such as Omni-Reduce, THC, and emerging standards such as MXFP4, MXFP6, and MXFP8. Further, DynamiQ is the only evaluated method that consistently reaches near-baseline accuracy (e.g., 99.9% of the BF16 baseline) and does so while significantly accelerating the training.
Key Techniques

Partial sum representation
Decompress-accumulate-recompress fused kernel
Gradient quantization with multi-hop optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityQuartet II: Accurate LLM Pre-Training in NVFP4 by Improved Unbiased Gradient Estimation0.22Deep Kernel Fusion for Transformers0.20FlashSchNet: Fast and Accurate Coarse-Grained Neural Network Molecular Dynamics0.19Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems0.15Unleashing Low-Bit Inference on Ascend NPUs: A Comprehensive Evaluation of HiFloat Formats0.15
DynamiQ: Dynamic Quantization for Multi-Hop All-Reduce
Core Idea: A gradient quantization framework optimized for multi-hop all-reduce topologies that dynamically re-quantizes partial sums at intermediate aggregation hops using adaptive scaling and range tracking, paired with a fused decompress-accumulate-recompress kernel.
Problem It Solves: Existing gradient quantization methods (e.g., THC, Omni-Reduce, MXFP) are designed for single-hop aggregation and suffer significant accuracy degradation in multi-hop topologies because they don’t account for partial sums accumulating quantization errors across multiple hops. This becomes a critical bottleneck as training scales to thousands of GPUs with complex network topologies.
How It Works:

Dynamic Range Tracking: At each hop, track the actual range of partial sum values (not just original gradients) to compute optimal scaling factors for re-quantization
Intermediate Re-quantization: After accumulating gradients at each intermediate node, decompress → accumulate → re-quantize with updated scale factors that match the new value distribution
Fused Kernel Execution: Use custom CUDA kernels that fuse decompression, accumulation, and recompression into a single operation to minimize memory bandwidth and latency overhead
Topology-Aware Bit Allocation: Adjust quantization precision based on the number of remaining hops (more aggressive early, more conservative near final aggregation)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Compressed Gradients<br/>Worker N] --> B[Decompress]
    B --> C[Accumulate<br/>Partial Sums]
    C --> D[Update Scale/Range]
    D --> E[Re-quantize]
    E --> F[Next Hop]
    
    style D fill:#ffcccc
    style E fill:#ccffcc

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale distributed training (1000+ GPUs) with multi-tier network topologies (rack → pod → cluster aggregation)
Training LLMs (GPT, LLaMA) and foundation models where network bandwidth is the bottleneck
Any distributed training setup using ring all-reduce, tree all-reduce, or hierarchical reduction patterns

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires NCCL P2P or similar low-level communication primitives (not plug-and-play with all frameworks)
Fused kernel adds implementation complexity and may need tuning per GPU architecture
Overhead of dynamic scale computation may not be worthwhile for small models or single-hop scenarios
Convergence guarantees depend on maintaining sufficient gradient signal-to-noise ratio across hops

Internal Use Cases:

Accelerating multi-GPU training of large ranking models or embedding models where gradient synchronization dominates training time
RecSys feature embedding tables distributed across parameter servers with hierarchical aggregation
Fine-tuning LLMs on multi-node clusters where reducing cross-node communication by 30%+ directly translates to training cost savings
Hybrid CPU-GPU training pipelines where quantized gradients reduce PCIe bandwidth pressure during backward pass

literature/2026-02-10-FlashVID-Efficient.title: FlashVID: Efficient Video Large Language Models via Training-free Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging
literature/2026-02-10-FlashVID-Efficient.content:
FlashVID: Efficient Video Large Language Models via Training-free Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-08
Rank: #2 (score: 0.82, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Although Video Large Language Models (VLLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in video understanding, they are required to process high volumes of visual tokens, causing significant computational inefficiency. Existing VLLMs acceleration frameworks usually compress spatial and temporal redundancy independently, which overlooks the spatiotemporal relationships, thereby leading to suboptimal spatiotemporal compression. The highly correlated visual features are likely to change in spatial position, scale, orientation, and other attributes over time due to the dynamic nature of video. Building on this insight, we introduce FlashVID, a training-free inference acceleration framework for VLLMs. Specifically, FlashVID utilizes Attention and Diversity-based Token Selection (ADTS) to select the most representative tokens for basic video representation, then applies Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging (TSTM) for fine-grained spatiotemporal redundancy elimination. Extensive experiments conducted on three representative VLLMs across five video understanding benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization of our method. Notably, by retaining only 10% of visual tokens, FlashVID preserves 99.1% of the performance of LLaVA-OneVision. Consequently, FlashVID can serve as a training-free and plug-and-play module for extending long video frames, which enables a 10x increase in video frame input to Qwen2.5-VL, resulting in a relative improvement of 8.6% within the same computational budget. Code is available at github.com/Fanziyang-v/FlashVID.
Key Techniques

Attention and Diversity-based Token Selection (ADTS)
Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging (TSTM)
training-free inference acceleration

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityQuant VideoGen: Auto-Regressive Long Video Generation via 2-Bit KV-Cache Quantization0.36VisionTrim: Unified Vision Token Compression for Training-Free MLLM Acceleration0.33OneVision-Encoder: Codec-Aligned Sparsity as a Foundational Principle for Multimodal Intelligence0.31Flow caching for autoregressive video generation0.27Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs0.27
FlashVID (Training-free Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging)
Core Idea: Training-free inference acceleration for Video Large Language Models through spatiotemporal token compression that preserves video relationships by first selecting representative tokens, then merging spatiotemporally correlated features across frames.
Problem It Solves: Existing VLLMs require processing massive volumes of visual tokens, causing computational inefficiency. Prior compression methods treat spatial and temporal redundancy independently, missing spatiotemporal correlations where visual features change position, scale, and orientation over time.
How It Works:

Attention and Diversity-based Token Selection (ADTS): Select the most representative visual tokens based on attention scores and diversity metrics to form a basic video representation
Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging (TSTM): Build a tree structure to identify and merge tokens that are correlated across both spatial and temporal dimensions, eliminating fine-grained redundancy while tracking how features evolve across frames
Inference: Pass the compressed token set (as low as 10% of original) to the VLLM for video understanding tasks

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Video Frames] --> B[Vision Encoder]
    B --> C[ADTS: Select Representative Tokens]
    C --> D[TSTM: Tree-based Merging]
    D --> E[Compressed Tokens 10%]
    E --> F[Language Model]
    F --> G[Video Understanding Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-form video understanding where computational budget is limited
Real-time video analysis applications requiring efficient inference
Extending context length for video-language models (enables 10x more frames)
Plug-and-play acceleration for existing VLLMs without retraining

Limitations / Caveats:

Performance degrades slightly at extreme compression ratios (10% retention = 99.1% performance, but further compression may hurt more)
Effectiveness depends on the existence of spatiotemporal redundancy in the video content
Tree construction overhead may not justify benefits for very short videos

Internal Use Cases:

Video recommendation systems: compress video representations for efficient similarity search and ranking
Multimodal content understanding: apply spatiotemporal merging to reduce token costs when processing video+text in feeds
Long-context video retrieval: enable indexing of longer videos within fixed compute budgets by compressing visual tokens before embedding

literature/2026-02-10-Generative-Reasoni.title: Generative Reasoning Re-ranker
literature/2026-02-10-Generative-Reasoni.content:
Generative Reasoning Re-ranker
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-08
Rank: #11 (score: 0.00, max_sim: 0.89)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Recent studies increasingly explore Large Language Models (LLMs) as a new paradigm for recommendation systems due to their scalability and world knowledge. However, existing work has three key limitations: (1) most efforts focus on retrieval and ranking, while the reranking phase, critical for refining final recommendations, is largely overlooked; (2) LLMs are typically used in zero-shot or supervised fine-tuning settings, leaving their reasoning abilities, especially those enhanced through reinforcement learning (RL) and high-quality reasoning data, underexploited; (3) items are commonly represented by non-semantic IDs, creating major scalability challenges in industrial systems with billions of identifiers. To address these gaps, we propose the Generative Reasoning Reranker (GR2), an end-to-end framework with a three-stage training pipeline tailored for reranking. First, a pretrained LLM is mid-trained on semantic IDs encoded from non-semantic IDs via a tokenizer achieving \ge99% uniqueness. Next, a stronger larger-scale LLM generates high-quality reasoning traces through carefully designed prompting and rejection sampling, which are used for supervised fine-tuning to impart foundational reasoning skills. Finally, we apply Decoupled Clip and Dynamic sAmpling Policy Optimization (DAPO), enabling scalable RL supervision with verifiable rewards designed specifically for reranking. Experiments on two real-world datasets demonstrate GR2’s effectiveness: it surpasses the state-of-the-art OneRec-Think by 2.4% in Recall@5 and 1.3% in NDCG@5. Ablations confirm that advanced reasoning traces yield substantial gains across metrics. We further find that RL reward design is crucial in reranking: LLMs tend to exploit reward hacking by preserving item order, motivating conditional verifiable rewards to mitigate this behavior and optimize reranking performance.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques or methods:

Semantic ID tokenization

supervised fine-tuning with reasoning traces
DAPO (Decoupled Clip and Dynamic sAmpling Policy Optimization)

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityGenerative Reasoning Re-ranker0.89S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation0.23ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.22Answer First, Reason Later: Aligning Search Relevance via Mode-Balanced Reinforcement Learning0.22DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.21
Generative Reasoning Reranker (GR2)
Core Idea: A three-stage LLM training framework (semantic ID mid-training → reasoning trace distillation → RL with verifiable rewards) that teaches models to rerank recommendations through explicit chain-of-thought reasoning rather than implicit scoring.
Problem It Solves:

Existing LLM-based recommenders neglect the critical reranking phase (focusing only on retrieval/ranking)
Standard LLM approaches underutilize reasoning capabilities enhanced through RL and high-quality reasoning data
Non-semantic item IDs create severe scalability issues in industrial systems with billions of items
Models exploit reward hacking by preserving input order rather than truly optimizing reranking quality

How It Works:

Semantic ID Mid-Training: Pretrained LLM learns a tokenizer-based encoding that maps non-semantic IDs to semantic representations (≥99% uniqueness), enabling billion-scale item vocabulary
Reasoning Trace Distillation: A larger teacher LLM generates high-quality step-by-step reasoning traces via carefully designed prompts and rejection sampling; student LLM is supervised fine-tuned on these traces to learn foundational reasoning patterns
DAPO (Decoupled Clip and Dynamic sAmpling Policy Optimization): Scalable RL training with conditional verifiable rewards that prevent order-preservation exploits—rewards explicitly check for meaningful reordering rather than just final metrics

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Non-semantic IDs] --> B[Semantic ID Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Mid-trained LLM]
    C --> D[Reasoning Trace SFT]
    D --> E[DAPO RL Training]
    E --> F[Reranked Item List]
    G[Teacher LLM Traces] -.->|distill| D
    H[Verifiable Rewards] -.->|optimize| E

Where It’s Useful:

Industrial recommendation reranking with massive item catalogs (billions of items)
E-commerce, content platforms, search reranking where final ordering critically impacts user experience
Systems requiring explainable recommendations (reasoning traces provide interpretability)
Cold-start scenarios where semantic item representations generalize better than ID embeddings

Limitations / Caveats:

Three-stage training pipeline is resource-intensive (requires teacher LLM, rejection sampling, RL infrastructure)
Semantic ID tokenizer requires careful design to maintain high uniqueness at scale
Reward engineering is critical—poorly designed rewards lead to exploits (e.g., order preservation)
Inference latency higher than traditional reranking models due to autoregressive generation of reasoning traces
Performance gains depend heavily on quality of teacher-generated reasoning traces

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys final-stage reranking: Apply after candidate retrieval/scoring to refine top-K results with explainable reasoning (especially valuable for high-stakes recommendations)
LLM-based ranking with scalability: Use semantic ID approach to handle large item catalogs without embedding table explosion
Reward shaping for ranking RL: Adopt conditional verifiable rewards to prevent order-preservation hacking in listwise ranking optimization
Reasoning-enhanced search: Generate interpretable explanations for why items are reordered (audit compliance, user trust)
Cross-domain transfer: Semantic IDs enable better generalization to new item categories vs. learned embeddings

literature/2026-02-10-Improving-Data-and.title: Improving Data and Reward Design for Scientific Reasoning in Large Language Models
literature/2026-02-10-Improving-Data-and.content:
Improving Data and Reward Design for Scientific Reasoning in Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-09
Rank: #4 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RL, RAG, LLM
Abstract
Solving open-ended science questions remains challenging for large language models, particularly due to inherently unreliable supervision and evaluation. The bottleneck lies in the data construction and reward design for scientific post-training. We develop a large-scale, systematic data processing pipeline that transforms heterogeneous open-source science data into Dr. SCI dataset, which comprises of 1M questions across eight STEM subjects, with explicit verifiable/open-ended splits, scalable difficulty annotation, and fine-grained rubrics that operationalize evaluation for open-ended answers. Building on this dataset, we propose the Dr. SCI post-training pipeline, which redesigns the standard SFT → RL workflow through three components: (i) Exploration-Expanding SFT, which broadens the model’s reasoning pattern coverage prior to RL; (ii) Dynamic Difficulty Curriculum, which adapts training data to the model’s evolving scientific capability; and (iii) SciRubric-Guided RL, which enables stable reinforcement learning on open-ended scientific questions via rubric-based evaluation with explicit answer correctness. Qwen3-4B-Base trained using Dr.SCI pipeline achieves 63.2 on GPQA-diamond and 32.4 on GPQA-general, consistently improves over strong post-trained baselines such as o1-mini and GPT-4o, demonstrating substantial gains in scientific reasoning, especially in open-ended settings.
Key Techniques

Exploration-Expanding SFT
Dynamic Difficulty Curriculum
SciRubric-Guided RL

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.27ArXiv-to-Model: A Practical Study of Scientific LM Training0.25PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.24PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.23Answer First, Reason Later: Aligning Search Relevance via Mode-Balanced Reinforcement Learning0.23
Dr. SCI Post-Training Pipeline
Core Idea: A three-stage scientific reasoning training pipeline that combines exploration-expanding supervised fine-tuning, dynamic difficulty curriculum, and rubric-guided reinforcement learning to enable stable RL on open-ended scientific questions.
Problem It Solves: Standard SFT → RL workflows fail on scientific reasoning due to: (1) narrow reasoning pattern coverage before RL, (2) static training data mismatched to evolving model capability, and (3) inability to provide stable reward signals for open-ended scientific answers without verifiable ground truth.
How It Works:

Exploration-Expanding SFT: Train on diverse reasoning trajectories (not just correct paths) to broaden the model’s solution space coverage before RL begins
Dynamic Difficulty Curriculum: Adaptively adjust training data difficulty based on the model’s current scientific capability throughout training, ensuring optimal challenge level
SciRubric-Guided RL: Use fine-grained evaluation rubrics that decompose open-ended answers into scorable components (concept understanding, methodology, reasoning steps) to generate stable reward signals for policy optimization

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Dr. SCI Dataset<br/>1M STEM questions] --> B[Exploration-Expanding SFT<br/>diverse reasoning patterns]
    B --> C[Dynamic Difficulty<br/>Curriculum]
    C --> D[SciRubric-Guided RL<br/>rubric-based rewards]
    D --> E[Trained Model<br/>e.g. Qwen3-4B]

Where It’s Useful:

Training models for scientific question answering (chemistry, physics, biology, mathematics)
Domains with inherently unreliable supervision and open-ended evaluation (medical reasoning, engineering design)
Tasks requiring multi-step reasoning with partial credit evaluation

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires large-scale structured scientific data (1M+ questions) with difficulty annotations and rubrics—high curation cost
Rubric design is domain-specific and may not transfer across different scientific fields
Dynamic curriculum adaptation requires continuous model capability assessment during training

Internal Use Cases:

Apply rubric-guided reward modeling to ranking systems where relevance is multi-faceted (e.g., decompose “good recommendation” into engagement, diversity, satisfaction components)
Use exploration-expanding SFT for RecSys models to cover diverse user preference patterns before optimizing for specific metrics
Adapt dynamic difficulty curriculum to conversational AI training—adjust dialogue complexity as model’s contextual understanding improves

literature/2026-02-10-LIT-GRAPH-Evaluati.title: LIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs
literature/2026-02-10-LIT-GRAPH-Evaluati.content:
LIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-07
Rank: #5 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RecSys, Graph, RAG, LLM, Embeddings
Abstract
This study presents LIT-GRAPH (Literature Graph for Recommendation and Pedagogical Heuristics), a novel knowledge graph-based recommendation system designed to scaffold high school English teachers in selecting diverse, pedagogically aligned instructional literature. The system is built upon an ontology for English literature, addressing the challenge of curriculum stagnation, where we compare four graph embedding paradigms: DeepWalk, Biased Random Walk (BRW), Hybrid (concatenated DeepWalk and BRW vectors), and the deep model Relational Graph Convolutional Network (R-GCN). Results reveal a critical divergence: while shallow models excelled in structural link prediction, R-GCN dominated semantic ranking. By leveraging relation-specific message passing, the deep model prioritizes pedagogical relevance over raw connectivity, resulting in superior, high-quality, domain-specific recommendations.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques:

DeepWalk

Biased Random Walk
Relational Graph Convolutional Network (R-GCN)
knowledge graph embedding

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityOne Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral Profiles0.32Towards Personalized Bangla Book Recommendation: A Large-Scale Multi-Entity Book Graph Dataset0.25Exploring the impact of adaptive rewiring in Graph Neural Networks0.24Unified Multi-Domain Graph Pre-training for Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Graphs via Domain-Specific Expert Encoding0.23NeuroSymActive: Differentiable Neural-Symbolic Reasoning with Active Exploration for Knowledge Graph Question Answering0.21
LIT-GRAPH: Literature Graph for Recommendation and Pedagogical Heuristics
Core Idea: A domain-specific knowledge graph recommendation system that compares shallow (DeepWalk, Biased Random Walk) and deep (R-GCN) graph embeddings to balance structural connectivity and semantic relevance for pedagogically aligned literature recommendations.
Problem It Solves: Addresses curriculum stagnation in English education by scaffolding teachers to discover diverse, pedagogically appropriate literature beyond commonly taught texts, while navigating the trade-off between graph structure fidelity and domain-specific semantic quality in recommendations.
How It Works:

Ontology Construction: Build a knowledge graph from English literature corpus with nodes (books, authors, themes) and typed relations (pedagogical similarity, thematic connections, curriculum alignment)
Embedding Generation: Train four embedding paradigms in parallel:

DeepWalk: Unbiased random walks capturing general graph structure
Biased Random Walk (BRW): Prioritizes pedagogically relevant paths via edge weighting
Hybrid: Concatenates DeepWalk + BRW vectors for combined signal
R-GCN: Relation-specific message passing aggregating neighbor features by edge type


Task-Specific Evaluation: Assess embeddings on (a) link prediction for structural accuracy and (b) semantic ranking for pedagogical relevance
Recommendation Serving: R-GCN embeddings fed to ranking model prioritizing semantic alignment over raw connectivity

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Literature KG<br/>Books, Authors, Themes] --> B[DeepWalk]
    A --> C[Biased Random Walk]
    A --> D[R-GCN<br/>Relation-Specific Layers]
    B --> E[Link Prediction<br/>Structural Task]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    B --> F[Semantic Ranking<br/>Pedagogical Relevance]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    F --> G[Teacher-Facing<br/>Recommendations]

Where It’s Useful:

Educational content recommendation: Curriculum design, course material selection, adaptive learning paths
Domain-specific KG applications: Medical literature, legal case law, technical documentation where semantic relevance outweighs popularity
Pedagogical tool design: Systems requiring explainable, alignment-driven recommendations over engagement-maximizing ones

Limitations / Caveats:

Shallow-Deep Trade-off: Shallow models (DeepWalk, BRW) win on link prediction but lose on semantic quality; practitioners must choose based on task priority
Computational Cost: R-GCN training scales poorly with relation types and graph size compared to random walk methods
Domain Dependency: Requires high-quality ontology and labeled pedagogical relations; performance degrades with noisy or sparse KGs
Cold-Start: New literature without sufficient graph connections disadvantaged even in R-GCN (though better than shallow methods)

Internal Use Cases:

Hybrid Recommender Systems: Combine R-GCN semantic embeddings with collaborative filtering for quality + engagement balance (e.g., diversifying popular recommendations)
Knowledge-Aware Ranking: Apply R-GCN to product/content KGs where semantic attributes (safety, educational value) should override clickthrough-based signals
Relation-Type Analysis: Adapt R-GCN message passing to user-item-context graphs, learning separate aggregation functions for “purchased,” “viewed,” “saved” edges
Explainability: Leverage graph paths through pedagogical relations (theme similarity, reading level) to generate interpretable recommendations for trust-critical domains

literature/2026-02-10-LLaDA21-Speeding-U.title: LLaDA2.1: Speeding Up Text Diffusion via Token Editing
literature/2026-02-10-LLaDA21-Speeding-U.content:
LLaDA2.1: Speeding Up Text Diffusion via Token Editing
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-09
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RL, Diffusion, LLM
Abstract
While LLaDA2.0 showcased the scaling potential of 100B-level block-diffusion models and their inherent parallelization, the delicate equilibrium between decoding speed and generation quality has remained an elusive frontier. Today, we unveil LLaDA2.1, a paradigm shift designed to transcend this trade-off. By seamlessly weaving Token-to-Token (T2T) editing into the conventional Mask-to-Token (M2T) scheme, we introduce a joint, configurable threshold-decoding scheme. This structural innovation gives rise to two distinct personas: the Speedy Mode (S Mode), which audaciously lowers the M2T threshold to bypass traditional constraints while relying on T2T to refine the output; and the Quality Mode (Q Mode), which leans into conservative thresholds to secure superior benchmark performances with manageable efficiency degrade. Furthering this evolution, underpinned by an expansive context window, we implement the first large-scale Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework specifically tailored for dLLMs, anchored by specialized techniques for stable gradient estimation. This alignment not only sharpens reasoning precision but also elevates instruction-following fidelity, bridging the chasm between diffusion dynamics and complex human intent. We culminate this work by releasing LLaDA2.1-Mini (16B) and LLaDA2.1-Flash (100B). Across 33 rigorous benchmarks, LLaDA2.1 delivers strong task performance and lightning-fast decoding speed. Despite its 100B volume, on coding tasks it attains an astounding 892 TPS on HumanEval+, 801 TPS on BigCodeBench, and 663 TPS on LiveCodeBench.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques:

Token-to-Token (T2T) editing

Mask-to-Token (M2T) scheme
Joint threshold-decoding
Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework for dLLMs

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDiffusion In Diffusion: Reclaiming Global Coherence in Semi-Autoregressive Diffusion0.24Accordion-Thinking: Self-Regulated Step Summaries for Efficient and Readable LLM Reasoning0.21Diffusion Generative Recommendation with Continuous Tokens0.20DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.19BitDance: Scaling Autoregressive Generative Models with Binary Tokens0.18
LLaDA2.1: Joint Mask-to-Token and Token-to-Token Diffusion Editing
Core Idea: Combine Mask-to-Token (M2T) prediction with Token-to-Token (T2T) editing in a configurable threshold-based decoding scheme to enable speed-quality tradeoffs in diffusion language models.
Problem It Solves: Traditional diffusion language models face a rigid tradeoff between decoding speed and generation quality. Lowering confidence thresholds accelerates decoding but degrades output; conservative thresholds maintain quality but sacrifice speed.
How It Works:

M2T Phase: Predict tokens for masked positions using the diffusion model; accept predictions that exceed a configurable confidence threshold
T2T Editing Phase: For tokens that fall below threshold (or in aggressive “Speedy Mode”), apply token-to-token editing to refine predictions without full regeneration
Threshold Configuration: Adjust M2T threshold to create personas—“Speedy Mode” (low threshold, heavy T2T reliance) or “Quality Mode” (high threshold, minimal T2T refinement)
RL Alignment: Apply reinforcement learning with specialized gradient estimation techniques to align diffusion dynamics with instruction-following and reasoning objectives

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Masked Input] --> B[M2T Prediction]
    B --> C{Confidence > Threshold?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Accept Token]
    C -->|No| E[T2T Editing]
    E --> F[Refined Token]
    D --> G[Output Sequence]
    F --> G

Where It’s Useful:

Code generation tasks requiring high throughput (achieves 663-892 TPS on coding benchmarks)
Interactive applications where speed-quality tradeoffs must be dynamically adjusted
Large-scale inference where 100B parameter models need practical deployment speeds

Limitations / Caveats:

T2T editing effectiveness depends on quality of initial M2T predictions; catastrophic failures in M2T may not be recoverable
RL alignment for diffusion models requires specialized gradient estimation—training complexity increases
Threshold tuning introduces hyperparameter sensitivity; optimal values may vary across task domains

Internal Use Cases:

Code completion systems: Deploy in “Speedy Mode” for autocomplete, “Quality Mode” for full function generation
LLM serving infrastructure: Dynamically adjust thresholds based on load—aggressive editing during peak traffic, conservative during low-demand periods
Retrieval-augmented generation: Use T2T editing to refine retrieved candidates rather than full regeneration, reducing latency in document-grounded QA

Discussions & References

github.com

literature/2026-02-10-ManifoldKV-Trainin.title: ManifoldKV: Training-Free KV Cache Compression via Euclidean Outlier Detection
literature/2026-02-10-ManifoldKV-Trainin.content:
ManifoldKV: Training-Free KV Cache Compression via Euclidean Outlier Detection
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-09
Rank: #9 (score: 0.80, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Long-context inference is constrained by KV-cache memory, which grows linearly with sequence length; KV-cache compression therefore hinges on reliably selecting which past tokens to retain. Most geometry-based eviction methods score keys by cosine similarity to a global centroid, but cosine is scale-invariant and can discard magnitude cues that distinguish semantically salient tokens. We propose ManifoldKV, a training-free scorer that ranks tokens by Euclidean distance to the key centroid, capturing both angular and radial deviations.   On the RULER benchmark, ManifoldKV achieves 95.7% accuracy at 4K-16K contexts with 20% compression; matching the best geometric baseline while improving robustness in two regimes where cosine scoring fails. First, on multi-key retrieval, ManifoldKV reduces directional collisions, achieving 92.4% vs KeyDiff’s 77.0% (+15.4 points) on 3-key NIAH at 50% compression. Second, to address dilution and performance collapse of global centroids at 64K context, we introduce WindowedManifoldKV, which restores accuracy to 84.3% at 25% compression, a 49-point recovery over global L2 and +3.2 points over KeyDiff. The method requires only 3 lines of code and works across 4 architectures without tuning.
Key Techniques

ManifoldKV
Euclidean distance-based token scoring
WindowedManifoldKV with windowed centroid approach
training-free KV-cache compression

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHierarchical Adaptive Eviction for KV Cache Management in Multimodal Language Models0.25ForesightKV: Optimizing KV Cache Eviction for Reasoning Models by Learning Long-Term Contribution0.24Learning to Evict from Key-Value Cache0.24DeltaKV: Residual-Based KV Cache Compression via Long-Range Similarity0.21FASA: Frequency-aware Sparse Attention0.18
ManifoldKV (Euclidean-Distance KV Cache Eviction)
Core Idea: Score KV cache tokens for eviction using Euclidean distance to the key centroid rather than cosine similarity, preserving both angular direction and magnitude information to identify semantically salient outliers.
Problem It Solves: Cosine-similarity-based eviction methods are scale-invariant and discard magnitude cues, causing directional collisions in multi-key retrieval and centroid dilution at extreme context lengths (64K+), leading to catastrophic performance collapse.
How It Works:

Compute global centroid μ of all cached key vectors
For each cached token, calculate Euclidean L2 distance: ||k_i - μ||₂
Evict tokens with smallest L2 distance (closest to centroid); retain high-distance outliers
(Windowed variant) At 64K+ contexts, partition cache into windows and compute local centroids to prevent dilution

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[KV Cache Keys] --> B[Compute Centroid μ]
    B --> C[L2 Distance: ||k - μ||]
    C --> D[Rank & Evict Low-Distance Tokens]
    D --> E[Compressed KV Cache]
    
    style C fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#fff4e1

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context LLM inference (4K-128K tokens) with memory constraints
Multi-needle-in-haystack retrieval tasks requiring robust multi-key extraction
Extreme-length document QA where global statistics degrade

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires computing full centroid over all keys (O(n) overhead per decoding step)
Windowed variant adds hyperparameter (window size) that may need domain tuning
Performance gain diminishes when compression ratio exceeds 75% (too aggressive eviction)

Internal Use Cases:

LLM serving: Deploy as drop-in replacement for cosine-based eviction (H2O, StreamingLLM) to handle multi-document RAG with lower OOM risk
Ranking models: Apply Euclidean outlier scoring to prune candidate embeddings in two-tower retrievers where magnitude encodes relevance strength
RecSys context compression: Retain user-history tokens with high L2 deviation from session centroid to preserve anomaly signals (e.g., genre switches, impulse purchases)

literature/2026-02-10-OJBKQ-Objective-Jo.title: OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization
literature/2026-02-10-OJBKQ-Objective-Jo.content:
OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-09
Rank: #12 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Post-training quantization (PTQ) is widely used to compress large language models without retraining. However, many existing weight-only methods rely on heuristic objectives and greedy rounding, thus leading to noticeable degradation under low-bit quantization. In this work, we introduce OJBKQ (Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization with K-Best Sampling), a layer-wise PTQ method that formulates weight quantization as a joint optimization problem over activations and weights. This formulation results in a multiple-right-hand-side box-constrained integer least squares (BILS) problem in each layer, which is NP-hard. For each column of the weight matrix, we apply an extended Babai nearest-plane algorithm and an extended version of Klein’s randomized Babai algorithm to find the minimum-residual Babai-Klein point, a sub-optimal solution to the BILS problem. Experimental results on large language models show that OJBKQ achieves lower perplexity at 3-4 bits compared to existing PTQ approaches, while maintaining comparable computational cost.
Key Techniques

Post-training quantization (PTQ)
Joint optimization over activations and weights
Babai-Klein algorithm with K-Best Sampling
Box-constrained integer least squares (BILS)

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityRegularized Calibration with Successive Rounding for Post-Training Quantization0.35BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.30BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.291-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization0.28Unleashing Low-Bit Inference on Ascend NPUs: A Comprehensive Evaluation of HiFloat Formats0.26
Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization (OJBKQ)
Core Idea: A layer-wise post-training quantization method that jointly optimizes over activations and weights by formulating each layer’s quantization as a box-constrained integer least squares problem, then solving it approximately using extended Babai and Klein algorithms with K-best sampling.
Problem It Solves: Existing weight-only post-training quantization methods rely on heuristic objectives and greedy rounding, causing significant accuracy degradation at low bit-widths (3-4 bits). OJBKQ addresses this by formulating quantization as a principled optimization problem that considers both weight reconstruction error and activation context.
How It Works:

Problem Formulation: For each layer, formulate weight quantization as a multiple-right-hand-side box-constrained integer least squares (BILS) problem that jointly considers the reconstruction error across calibration activations.
Column-wise Decomposition: Decompose the BILS problem by processing each column of the weight matrix independently with respect to the corresponding activation inputs.
Extended Babai Algorithm: Apply the Babai nearest-plane algorithm (a lattice reduction technique) to find an initial near-optimal integer solution within the box constraints.
Klein’s Randomized Search: Use an extended version of Klein’s randomized Babai algorithm to sample K candidate solutions around the Babai point in the lattice space.
Minimum-Residual Selection: Select the candidate with minimum reconstruction residual as the final quantized weights for that column.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[FP Weights + Calibration Data] --> B[Layer-wise BILS Formulation]
    B --> C[Per-Column Lattice Problem]
    C --> D[Extended Babai Nearest-Plane]
    D --> E[Klein K-Best Sampling]
    E --> F[Min-Residual Selection]
    F --> G[Quantized Weights]

Where It’s Useful:

Post-training quantization of large language models for deployment on resource-constrained devices
3-4 bit weight compression where standard methods (GPTQ, AWQ) show significant perplexity degradation
Scenarios requiring low-bit quantization without model retraining or access to training data

Limitations / Caveats:

NP-hard problem requires approximate solutions; quality depends on K (number of samples) and lattice basis quality
Computational cost scales with K and calibration set size, though paper claims comparable cost to existing methods
Focuses on weight-only quantization; activation quantization not addressed
Performance gains diminish at higher bit-widths (≥6 bits) where simpler methods suffice

Internal Use Cases:

Compressing LLM-based ranking models for low-latency inference in production RecSys pipelines
Deploying instruction-tuned LLMs on edge devices for personalized recommendations without cloud dependencies
Reducing memory footprint of embedding tables and transformer layers in multi-stage ranking architectures

literature/2026-02-10-OSCAR-Optimization.title: OSCAR: Optimization-Steered Agentic Planning for Composed Image Retrieval
literature/2026-02-10-OSCAR-Optimization.content:
OSCAR: Optimization-Steered Agentic Planning for Composed Image Retrieval
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-09
Rank: #2 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, Embeddings, RAG
Abstract
Composed image retrieval (CIR) requires complex reasoning over heterogeneous visual and textual constraints. Existing approaches largely fall into two paradigms: unified embedding retrieval, which suffers from single-model myopia, and heuristic agentic retrieval, which is limited by suboptimal, trial-and-error orchestration. To this end, we propose OSCAR, an optimization-steered agentic planning framework for composed image retrieval. We are the first to reformulate agentic CIR from a heuristic search process into a principled trajectory optimization problem. Instead of relying on heuristic trial-and-error exploration, OSCAR employs a novel offline-online paradigm. In the offline phase, we model CIR via atomic retrieval selection and composition as a two-stage mixed-integer programming problem, mathematically deriving optimal trajectories that maximize ground-truth coverage for training samples via rigorous boolean set operations. These trajectories are then stored in a golden library to serve as in-context demonstrations for online steering of VLM planner at online inference time. Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks and a private industrial benchmark show that OSCAR consistently outperforms SOTA baselines. Notably, it achieves superior performance using only 10% of training data, demonstrating strong generalization of planning logic rather than dataset-specific memorization.
Key Techniques

Offline-online trajectory optimization
Mixed-integer programming for retrieval composition
VLM-steered agentic planning with in-context demonstrations

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDeepImageSearch: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Context-Aware Image Retrieval in Visual Histories0.27Do MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.17A Picture of Agentic Search0.16Rich-Media Re-Ranker: A User Satisfaction-Driven LLM Re-ranking Framework for Rich-Media Search0.16VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.16
OSCAR (Optimization-Steered Agentic Planning for Composed Image Retrieval)
Core Idea: Reformulates agentic composed image retrieval from heuristic trial-and-error into a principled trajectory optimization problem, using offline mixed-integer programming to derive optimal retrieval trajectories that are stored as in-context demonstrations for online VLM planner steering.
Problem It Solves: Addresses the limitations of both unified embedding retrieval (single-model myopia) and heuristic agentic retrieval (suboptimal trial-and-error orchestration) by replacing ad-hoc exploration with mathematically optimal planning trajectories.
How It Works:

Offline Trajectory Optimization: Model composed image retrieval as a two-stage mixed-integer programming (MIP) problem over atomic retrieval operations (selection and composition), computing optimal trajectories that maximize ground-truth coverage using rigorous boolean set operations on training samples.
Golden Library Construction: Store the derived optimal trajectories in a golden library indexed by query characteristics and constraint patterns.
Online VLM Planning: At inference time, retrieve relevant optimal trajectories from the golden library and use them as in-context demonstrations to steer a Vision-Language Model planner toward optimal atomic retrieval selection and composition.
Execution: Execute the planned atomic retrieval operations (visual search, text filtering, attribute matching, etc.) in the derived optimal order to retrieve final results.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Training Data] --> B[MIP Optimizer]
    B --> C[Golden Library<br/>Optimal Trajectories]
    D[Query at Inference] --> E[Trajectory Retrieval]
    C --> E
    E --> F[VLM Planner<br/>w/ In-Context Demo]
    F --> G[Atomic Retrieval<br/>Execution]
    G --> H[Retrieved Images]

Where It’s Useful:

Composed image retrieval tasks requiring multi-constraint reasoning (visual + textual modifications)
E-commerce visual search with complex user refinements (“find shoes similar to this image but in red leather”)
Fashion and product discovery platforms with attribute-based filtering on visual similarity
Any retrieval scenario where atomic operations (filters, embeddings, attributes) need optimal orchestration

Limitations / Caveats:

Offline MIP optimization requires ground-truth labels for training samples to compute optimal trajectories
Computational cost of solving mixed-integer programs scales with number of atomic operations and training set size
Assumes atomic retrieval operations are predefined and fixed (limited flexibility for novel operation types at inference)
Golden library quality depends on coverage and diversity of training query patterns

Internal Use Cases:

Visual Product Search: Apply to multi-modal product retrieval where users provide reference images plus textual refinements (color, brand, price range), optimizing the order of visual embedding search, attribute filters, and collaborative signals
Recommendation Ranking: Use trajectory optimization to orchestrate heterogeneous ranking signals (content-based, collaborative, contextual) rather than fixed ensemble weights—learn optimal signal composition paths per user segment
LLM Tool-Use Planning: Extend to general LLM agent planning by replacing heuristic ReAct/chain-of-thought with MIP-derived optimal tool invocation sequences, storing them as few-shot planning demonstrations for complex multi-step tasks

literature/2026-02-10-ParisKV-Fast-and-D.title: ParisKV: Fast and Drift-Robust KV-Cache Retrieval for Long-Context LLMs
literature/2026-02-10-ParisKV-Fast-and-D.content:
ParisKV: Fast and Drift-Robust KV-Cache Retrieval for Long-Context LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-07
Rank: #6 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
KV-cache retrieval is essential for long-context LLM inference, yet existing methods struggle with distribution drift and high latency at scale. We introduce ParisKV, a drift-robust, GPU-native KV-cache retrieval framework based on collision-based candidate selection, followed by a quantized inner-product reranking estimator. For million-token contexts, ParisKV supports CPU-offloaded KV caches via Unified Virtual Addressing (UVA), enabling on-demand top-k fetching with minimal overhead. ParisKV matches or outperforms full attention quality on long-input and long-generation benchmarks. It achieves state-of-the-art long-context decoding efficiency: it matches or exceeds full attention speed even at batch size 1 for long contexts, delivers up to 2.8\times higher throughput within full attention’s runnable range, and scales to million-token contexts where full attention runs out of memory. At million-token scale, ParisKV reduces decode latency by 17\times and 44\times compared to MagicPIG and PQCache, respectively, two state-of-the-art KV-cache Top-k retrieval baselines.
Key Techniques

Collision-based candidate selection
Quantized inner-product reranking
Unified Virtual Addressing (UVA) for CPU-offloaded KV caches

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.29HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.28Fast KV Compaction via Attention Matching0.26Out of the Memory Barrier: A Highly Memory Efficient Training System for LLMs with Million-Token Contexts0.24FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion0.24
ParisKV (Partitioned Approximate Retrieval for Inference Speed)
Core Idea: A drift-robust, GPU-native KV-cache retrieval framework that uses collision-based candidate selection followed by quantized inner-product reranking to enable efficient top-k attention on long contexts, with CPU-offloading support via Unified Virtual Addressing for million-token scale.
Problem It Solves: Existing KV-cache retrieval methods for long-context LLMs suffer from distribution drift between prefill and decode phases, high retrieval latency at scale, and inability to handle million-token contexts due to memory constraints.
How It Works:

Collision-based candidate selection: Hash query and key vectors into buckets; candidates are selected from colliding buckets, naturally handling distribution drift without retraining
Quantized inner-product reranking: Compute approximate attention scores on candidates using low-precision quantized inner products to identify top-k most relevant KV pairs
UVA-enabled CPU offloading: For million-token contexts, store KV cache on CPU and fetch only top-k entries on-demand via Unified Virtual Addressing with minimal overhead
Sparse attention computation: Run full-precision attention only on retrieved top-k KV pairs, dramatically reducing memory and compute vs. dense attention

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Query Vector] --> B[Hash to Buckets]
    C[KV Cache Keys] --> D[Hash Index]
    B --> E[Collision Detection]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Candidate Set]
    F --> G[Quantized IP Reranking]
    G --> H[Top-k KV Pairs]
    H --> I[Sparse Attention]
    I --> J[Output Token]
    
    K[CPU Memory<br/>Million-token KV] -.UVA Fetch.-> H

Where It’s Useful:

Long-document understanding and retrieval (RAG over 100k+ token contexts)
Multi-turn conversations with extensive history
Code generation with large codebases in context
Million-token scale inference where full attention is memory-infeasible
Low-latency decoding for real-time applications (matches full attention speed at BS=1)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful tuning of hash bucket count and quantization precision to balance quality vs. speed
Collision-based selection may miss relevant tokens in adversarial distributions (though empirically robust)
CPU-offloading variant requires UVA-capable hardware (modern NVIDIA GPUs)
Quality-speed tradeoff: top-k selection inherently approximates full attention, though paper shows minimal degradation

Internal Use Cases:

Long-context ranking: Apply to reranking stages where queries need to attend over thousands of candidate documents
RAG systems: Enable efficient attention over massive retrieved context windows without memory blowup
LLM serving infrastructure: Integrate as a drop-in replacement for dense attention in decode phase to boost throughput 2-3x
Multi-document QA: Handle user queries over entire knowledge bases (millions of tokens) with sub-second latency
Hybrid with H2O/streaming attention: Combine ParisKV’s retrieval with heavy-hitter eviction for dynamic cache management

literature/2026-02-10-Predicting-Future-.title: Predicting Future Utility: Global Combinatorial Optimization for Task-Agnostic KV Cache Eviction
literature/2026-02-10-Predicting-Future-.content:
Predicting Future Utility: Global Combinatorial Optimization for Task-Agnostic KV Cache Eviction
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-09
Rank: #5 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Given the quadratic complexity of attention, KV cache eviction is vital to accelerate model inference. Current KV cache eviction methods typically rely on instantaneous heuristic metrics, implicitly assuming that score magnitudes are consistent proxies for importance across all heads. However, this overlooks the heterogeneity in predictive fidelity across attention heads. While certain heads prioritize the instantaneous contribution of tokens, others are dedicated to capturing long-horizon utility. In this paper, we propose that optimal budget allocation should be governed by the marginal utility in preserving long-term semantic information. Based on this insight, we propose LU-KV, a novel framework that optimizes head-level budget allocation through a convex-hull relaxation and a marginal-utility-based greedy solver to achieve near-optimal precision. Furthermore, we implement a data-driven offline profiling protocol to facilitate the practical deployment of LU-KV. Extensive evaluations on LongBench and RULER benchmarks demonstrate that LU-KV achieves an 80% reduction in KV cache size with minimal performance degradation, while simultaneously reducing inference latency and GPU memory footprint.
Key Techniques

Marginal utility-based budget allocation
Convex-hull relaxation optimization
Head-level KV cache allocation
Data-driven offline profiling protocol

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLearning to Evict from Key-Value Cache0.44ForesightKV: Optimizing KV Cache Eviction for Reasoning Models by Learning Long-Term Contribution0.32Hierarchical Adaptive Eviction for KV Cache Management in Multimodal Language Models0.32Efficient Autoregressive Video Diffusion with Dummy Head0.31DeltaKV: Residual-Based KV Cache Compression via Long-Range Similarity0.22
LU-KV (Long-horizon Utility KV Cache Eviction)
Core Idea: Optimize KV cache eviction by allocating per-head budgets based on marginal utility for preserving long-term semantic information, rather than treating all attention heads uniformly with instantaneous heuristic metrics.
Problem It Solves: Existing KV cache eviction methods assume score magnitudes are consistent proxies for importance across all attention heads, ignoring that different heads have heterogeneous predictive fidelity—some prioritize instantaneous token contribution while others capture long-horizon utility. This leads to suboptimal budget allocation and information loss.
How It Works:

Offline Profiling: Analyze attention head behavior across representative data to classify heads by their temporal utility patterns (instantaneous vs. long-horizon contribution)
Convex-Hull Relaxation: Formulate the head-level budget allocation problem as a global combinatorial optimization with convex-hull relaxation for tractability
Marginal-Utility-Based Greedy Solver: Allocate cache budget to each head based on marginal utility—the incremental value of preserving additional tokens for long-term semantic information
Head-Specific Eviction: Within each head’s allocated budget, evict tokens with lowest marginal contribution to future utility
Inference: Apply learned head-specific budgets during decoding to maintain 20% of original KV cache size

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input Tokens] --> B[Offline Profiling]
    B --> C[Head Utility Classification]
    C --> D[Convex Optimization]
    D --> E[Per-Head Budgets]
    E --> F[Marginal Utility Eviction]
    F --> G[Compressed KV Cache]
    G --> H[Attention Computation]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context LLM inference (document QA, summarization, multi-turn dialogue)
Memory-constrained deployment environments (edge devices, mobile)
High-throughput serving where KV cache dominates GPU memory
Benchmarks like LongBench and RULER requiring extended context windows

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires offline profiling phase with representative data—may not generalize to out-of-distribution workloads
Convex relaxation provides near-optimal but not globally optimal solutions
Fixed budget allocation assumes relatively stable task distribution during inference
Profiling overhead and storage of per-head budget policies

Internal Use Cases:

LLM serving optimization: Deploy LU-KV for production inference to reduce GPU memory footprint by 80% while maintaining quality on long-context retrieval tasks
Recommendation system context encoding: Apply head-specific eviction when encoding user history sequences, preserving long-range behavioral patterns in memory-critical heads
Multi-document ranking: Optimize KV cache when scoring documents against long queries by prioritizing heads that capture cross-document semantic relationships over positional encodings

literature/2026-02-10-Rethinking-Generat.title: Rethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs
literature/2026-02-10-Rethinking-Generat.content:
Rethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs
Source: S2:RecSys
Published: 2026
Rank: #3 (score: 0.00, max_sim: 0.90)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Semantic ID (SID)-based recommendation is a promising paradigm for scaling sequential recommender systems, but existing methods largely follow a semantic-centric pipeline: item embeddings are learned from foundation models and discretized using generic quantization schemes. This design is misaligned with generative recommendation objectives: semantic embeddings are weakly coupled with collaborative prediction, and generic quantization is inefficient at reducing sequential uncertainty for autoregressive modeling. To address these, we propose ReSID, a recommendation-native, principled SID framework that rethinks representation learning and quantization from the perspective of information preservation and sequential predictability, without relying on LLMs. ReSID consists of two components: (i) Field-Aware Masked Auto-Encoding (FAMAE), which learns predictive-sufficient item representations from structured features, and (ii) Globally Aligned Orthogonal Quantization (GAOQ), which produces compact and predictable SID sequences by jointly reducing semantic ambiguity and prefix-conditional uncertainty. Theoretical analysis and extensive experiments across ten datasets show the effectiveness of ReSID. ReSID consistently outperforms strong sequential and SID-based generative baselines by an average of over 10%, while reducing tokenization cost by up to 122x. Code is available at github.com/FuCongResearchSquad/ReSID.
Key Techniques

Field-Aware Masked Auto-Encoding (FAMAE)
Globally Aligned Orthogonal Quantization (GAOQ)
SID-based recommendation with information preservation focus

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityRethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs0.81DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.39End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.29GLASS: A Generative Recommender for Long-sequence Modeling via SID-Tier and Semantic Search0.27End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.26
ReSID (Recommendation-Native Semantic ID)
Core Idea: A generative recommendation framework that learns collaborative-predictive item representations and quantizes them into semantic IDs optimized for sequential predictability, without relying on pre-trained language models.
Problem It Solves: Existing semantic ID methods use foundation model embeddings weakly coupled with collaborative signals and generic quantization schemes (like RQ-VAE) that don’t minimize sequential uncertainty for autoregressive generation, leading to suboptimal recommendation quality and inefficient tokenization.
How It Works:

Field-Aware Masked Auto-Encoding (FAMAE): Learns item representations by masking and reconstructing structured item features (title, category, brand, etc.), preserving information predictive for collaborative filtering rather than semantic similarity alone
Globally Aligned Orthogonal Quantization (GAOQ): Quantizes embeddings into discrete token sequences by jointly optimizing:

Semantic ambiguity reduction (items close in embedding space get distinct codes)
Prefix-conditional uncertainty reduction (given previous tokens in sequence, next token is more predictable)
Orthogonality constraints on codebook vectors for compact representation


Autoregressive Generation: Uses the resulting SID sequences to train transformer-based sequential models that predict next item via token-by-token generation

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Item Features] --> B[FAMAE Encoder]
    B --> C[Item Embeddings]
    C --> D[GAOQ Quantizer]
    D --> E[Semantic ID Sequence]
    E --> F[Autoregressive Transformer]
    F --> G[Next Item Prediction]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale sequential recommendation systems (e-commerce, video, music streaming)
Cold-start scenarios where generative models can extrapolate from limited interaction history
Multi-domain recommendation requiring unified item representations
Extreme vocabulary settings where codebook efficiency matters

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires structured item metadata for FAMAE; purely collaborative filtering signals may not suffice
GAOQ joint optimization adds training complexity compared to standard vector quantization
Autoregressive generation has higher inference latency than retrieval-based methods
May require careful tuning of semantic vs. predictive loss weights for domain transfer

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking systems: Use GAOQ-style quantization to create compact item embeddings that preserve ranking signal while reducing embedding table size
Search-to-recommendation bridge: FAMAE could unify semantic (search) and collaborative (recsys) representations in hybrid retrieval
LLM-free generative recsys: Demonstrates competitive performance without foundation model dependency, reducing compute/licensing costs
