Embedding stability: Orthogonal quantization constraints may improve embedding drift issues in dynamic catalogs

literature/2026-02-10-Semantic-Search-At.title: Semantic Search At LinkedIn
literature/2026-02-10-Semantic-Search-At.content:
Semantic Search At LinkedIn
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-07
Rank: #8 (score: 0.90, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Semantic search with large language models (LLMs) enables retrieval by meaning rather than keyword overlap, but scaling it requires major inference efficiency advances. We present LinkedIn’s LLM-based semantic search framework for AI Job Search and AI People Search, combining an LLM relevance judge, embedding-based retrieval, and a compact Small Language Model trained via multi-teacher distillation to jointly optimize relevance and engagement. A prefill-oriented inference architecture co-designed with model pruning, context compression, and text-embedding hybrid interactions boosts ranking throughput by over 75x under a fixed latency constraint while preserving near-teacher-level NDCG, enabling one of the first production LLM-based ranking systems with efficiency comparable to traditional approaches and delivering significant gains in quality and user engagement.
Key Techniques

Semantic search with LLMs
embedding-based retrieval
small language model distillation
prefill-oriented inference architecture

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySemantic Search At LinkedIn, LLM-Driven Autonomous Optimization for Industrial-Scale Recommendation Systems, and More!0.29Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.25Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.24GLASS: A Generative Recommender for Long-sequence Modeling via SID-Tier and Semantic Search0.23Comparing how Large Language Models perform against keyword-based searches for social science research data discovery0.22
Prefill-Oriented LLM Ranking Architecture
Core Idea: A co-designed inference system that treats ranking as a prefill-dominated workload, using context compression and hybrid text-embedding interactions to enable 75x throughput improvement for production LLM-based semantic search.
Problem It Solves: Traditional LLM ranking suffers from poor throughput (10-100x slower than classical models) due to sequential token generation and large context windows when scoring multiple candidates, making it infeasible for high-QPS production retrieval systems.
How It Works:

Multi-teacher distillation: Train a compact Small Language Model (SLM) using signals from both an LLM relevance judge (quality) and engagement models (user behavior)
Context compression: Reduce input token count through pruning and efficient query-document representation while preserving semantic signals
Prefill optimization: Eliminate or minimize decode phase by structuring the task as context processing rather than generation, leveraging GPU parallelism for batch scoring
Hybrid interaction: Combine text-embedding retrieval (for recall) with LLM ranking (for precision) in a two-stage pipeline

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Query + Candidates] --> B[Context Compression]
    B --> C[SLM Prefill Engine]
    C --> D[Batch Scoring]
    D --> E[Ranked Results]
    F[LLM Teacher] -.distillation.-> C
    G[Engagement Teacher] -.distillation.-> C

Where It’s Useful:

Production semantic search with strict latency SLAs (job search, people search, product retrieval)
High-QPS ranking services where traditional LLM inference is cost-prohibitive
Two-stage retrieval systems requiring millions of daily inferences

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires offline distillation pipeline with multiple teacher models
Context compression may lose nuanced signals for complex queries
Prefill-only approach limits generative capabilities (trade-off for throughput)
Quality ceiling bounded by teacher model performance

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys candidate ranking where embedding retrieval provides initial candidates but LLM ranking improves relevance
Multi-objective optimization combining predicted engagement (CTR/conversion) with semantic relevance
Replacing traditional GBDT/neural rankers with semantically-aware SLMs in latency-constrained environments

literature/2026-02-10-ViCA-Efficient-Mul.title: ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention
literature/2026-02-10-ViCA-Efficient-Mul.content:
ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-07
Rank: #4 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Modern multimodal large language models (MLLMs) adopt a unified self-attention design that processes visual and textual tokens at every Transformer layer, incurring substantial computational overhead. In this work, we revisit the necessity of such dense visual processing and show that projected visual embeddings are already well-aligned with the language space, while effective vision-language interaction occurs in only a small subset of layers. Based on these insights, we propose ViCA (Vision-only Cross-Attention), a minimal MLLM architecture in which visual tokens bypass all self-attention and feed-forward layers, interacting with text solely through sparse cross-attention at selected layers. Extensive evaluations across three MLLM backbones, nine multimodal benchmarks, and 26 pruning-based baselines show that ViCA preserves 98% of baseline accuracy while reducing visual-side computation to 4%, consistently achieving superior performance-efficiency trade-offs. Moreover, ViCA provides a regular, hardware-friendly inference pipeline that yields over 3.5x speedup in single-batch inference and over 10x speedup in multi-batch inference, reducing visual grounding to near-zero overhead compared with text-only LLMs. It is also orthogonal to token pruning methods and can be seamlessly combined for further efficiency gains. Our code is available at github.com/EIT-NLP/ViCA.
Key Techniques

Sparse cross-attention
Visual token bypass
Selective layer interaction

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDo MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.47SwiftVLM: Efficient Vision-Language Model Inference via Cross-Layer Token Bypass0.40Fast-Slow Efficient Training for Multimodal Large Language Models via Visual Token Pruning0.39VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.39Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs0.37
ViCA (Vision-only Cross-Attention)
Core Idea: A sparse multimodal architecture where visual tokens bypass all self-attention and feed-forward layers in the language model, interacting with text only through cross-attention at a small subset of strategically selected layers.
Problem It Solves: Existing multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) process visual tokens through every Transformer layer alongside text tokens, causing massive computational overhead despite visual embeddings already being well-aligned with the language space after projection. Dense visual processing wastes 96% of visual computation on redundant operations.
How It Works:

Visual Projection Only: Input images are encoded by a vision encoder (e.g., CLIP ViT) and projected into the language model’s embedding space, but these visual tokens are NOT fed into the standard Transformer layers
Sparse Cross-Attention Insertion: At a small subset of pre-selected layers (identified via sensitivity analysis), dedicated cross-attention modules allow text tokens to attend to the frozen visual embeddings
Text-Only Processing: Language tokens flow through all standard self-attention and FFN layers as usual, while visual tokens remain static and only provide context when queried by cross-attention
Layer Selection: Automatically identify critical layers by measuring validation loss change when removing visual inputs at each layer; select top-k layers with highest sensitivity

Architecture:
flowchart TB
    A[Image] --> B[Vision Encoder]
    B --> C[Projection Layer]
    C --> D[Visual Embeddings - Static]
    
    T[Text Tokens] --> L1[Layer 1: Self-Attn + FFN]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2: Self-Attn + FFN]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Self-Attn + Cross-Attn + FFN]
    D -.->|Cross-Attn| L3
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Self-Attn + FFN]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Self-Attn + Cross-Attn + FFN]
    D -.->|Cross-Attn| L5
    L5 --> O[Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Vision-language tasks requiring efficient inference: VQA, image captioning, visual reasoning
High-throughput multimodal serving where batch processing is critical (10x+ speedup in batched scenarios)
Edge deployment of MLLMs with limited compute resources
Applications where visual grounding overhead must be minimized (approaching text-only LLM speed)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires initial profiling phase to identify optimal cross-attention layer positions (though analysis shows patterns generalize across model families)
May underperform on tasks requiring fine-grained spatial reasoning at every reasoning step
Assumes vision encoder produces sufficiently well-aligned embeddings (holds for modern CLIP-based encoders but may not generalize to all vision encoders)

Internal Use Cases:

Multimodal ranking/reranking: Apply ViCA for efficient image-text relevance scoring in search/recommendation where visual understanding is needed but throughput is critical
Content moderation at scale: Process text-image pairs with near-text-only latency while maintaining multimodal reasoning capability
Hybrid RAG systems: Use ViCA to efficiently ground retrieved visual context with minimal overhead compared to text-only retrieval pipelines
Token budget optimization: Combine with existing token pruning methods (orthogonal approach) for compounding efficiency gains in production LLM serving

literature/2026-02-11-A-Behavioral-Finge.title: A Behavioral Fingerprint for Large Language Models: Provenance Tracking via Refusal Vectors
literature/2026-02-11-A-Behavioral-Finge.content:
A Behavioral Fingerprint for Large Language Models: Provenance Tracking via Refusal Vectors
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-10
Rank: #6 (score: 0.54, max_sim: 0.80)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Protecting the intellectual property of large language models (LLMs) is a critical challenge due to the proliferation of unauthorized derivative models. We introduce a novel fingerprinting framework that leverages the behavioral patterns induced by safety alignment, applying the concept of refusal vectors for LLM provenance tracking. These vectors, extracted from directional patterns in a model’s internal representations when processing harmful versus harmless prompts, serve as robust behavioral fingerprints. Our contribution lies in developing a fingerprinting system around this concept and conducting extensive validation of its effectiveness for IP protection. We demonstrate that these behavioral fingerprints are highly robust against common modifications, including finetunes, merges, and quantization. Our experiments show that the fingerprint is unique to each model family, with low cosine similarity between independently trained models. In a large-scale identification task across 76 offspring models, our method achieves 100% accuracy in identifying the correct base model family. Furthermore, we analyze the fingerprint’s behavior under alignment-breaking attacks, finding that while performance degrades significantly, detectable traces remain. Finally, we propose a theoretical framework to transform this private fingerprint into a publicly verifiable, privacy-preserving artifact using locality-sensitive hashing and zero-knowledge proofs.
Key Techniques

Refusal vectors
behavioral fingerprinting
locality-sensitive hashing with zero-knowledge proofs

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityFail-Closed Alignment for Large Language Models0.34GOSV: Global Optimization for Safety Vector Extraction in LLMs0.18ANN v3: 200ms p99 query latency over 100 billion vectors0.12TensorCommitments: A Lightweight Verifiable Inference for Language Models0.09SPILLage: Agentic Oversharing on the Web0.09
Refusal Vector Fingerprinting for LLM Provenance
Core Idea: Extract behavioral fingerprints from LLMs by computing directional vectors in activation space that represent the difference between processing harmful versus harmless prompts, leveraging safety alignment artifacts as a unique model signature.
Problem It Solves: Protects intellectual property of proprietary LLMs against unauthorized derivative models (finetuned, merged, or quantized versions) by providing a robust, alignment-based identification method that doesn’t require watermarking or explicit model access.
How It Works:

Refusal Vector Extraction: Compute mean difference vectors between model activations on harmful prompts (that trigger refusals) and harmless prompts across residual stream layers
Fingerprint Construction: The resulting directional patterns form a high-dimensional behavioral signature unique to each base model’s safety alignment training
Provenance Verification: Compare cosine similarity between a suspect model’s refusal vectors and known base model fingerprints; high similarity (>threshold) indicates derivative relationship
Optional Privacy Layer: Transform private fingerprint into publicly verifiable artifact using locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) combined with zero-knowledge proofs for non-interactive verification

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Harmful Prompts] --> B[Model Activations]
    C[Harmless Prompts] --> B
    B --> D[Mean Difference Vectors]
    D --> E[Refusal Vector Fingerprint]
    E --> F{Cosine Similarity}
    G[Known Base Models] --> F
    F --> H[Provenance Match]

Where It’s Useful:

Detecting unauthorized model derivatives in commercial LLM ecosystems
Model family attribution in multi-vendor deployments
Forensic analysis of model origins for compliance and auditing
IP protection for model providers without embedded watermarks

Limitations / Caveats:

Degrades under alignment-breaking attacks (jailbreaking, adversarial finetuning), though detectable traces remain
Requires the target model to retain some safety alignment behavior; fully uncensored derivatives may evade detection
Similarity thresholds need calibration per model family and attack scenario
ZK proof construction adds computational overhead for public verification

Internal Use Cases:

Verify provenance of third-party model APIs or self-hosted deployments before integration
Monitor for unauthorized derivatives of internal LLM assets in production
Audit supply chain integrity for models used in ranking/recommendation pipelines
Complement existing model versioning with behavioral consistency checks across finetuning cycles

literature/2026-02-11-AfriNLLB-Efficient.title: AfriNLLB: Efficient Translation Models for African Languages
literature/2026-02-11-AfriNLLB-Efficient.content:
AfriNLLB: Efficient Translation Models for African Languages
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-10
Rank: #7 (score: 0.10, max_sim: 0.75)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
In this work, we present AfriNLLB, a series of lightweight models for efficient translation from and into African languages. AfriNLLB supports 15 language pairs (30 translation directions), including Swahili, Hausa, Yoruba, Amharic, Somali, Zulu, Lingala, Afrikaans, Wolof, and Egyptian Arabic, as well as other African Union official languages such as Arabic (MSA), French, Portuguese, and Spanish. Our training data covers bidirectional translation between English and 13 languages, and between French and two languages (Lingala and Wolof).   AfriNLLB models are based on NLLB-200 600M, which we compress using iterative layer pruning and quantization. We fine-tune the pruned models on parallel corpora we curated for African languages, employing knowledge distillation from a larger teacher model. Our work aims at enabling efficient deployment of translation models for African languages in resource-constrained settings.   Our evaluation results demonstrate that AfriNLLB models achieve performance comparable to the baseline while being significantly faster. We release two versions of the AfriNLLB models, a Transformers version that allows further fine-tuning and a CTranslate2 version for efficient inference. Moreover, we release all the training data that we used for fine-tuning the baseline and pruned models to facilitate further research.
Key Techniques

Layer pruning
quantization
knowledge distillation
parallel corpus curation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDicta-LM 3.0: Advancing The Frontier of Hebrew Sovereign LLMs0.25DHPLT: large-scale multilingual diachronic corpora and word representations for semantic change modelling0.20Corpus-Based Approaches to Igbo Diacritic Restoration0.20MultiLexNorm++: A Unified Benchmark and a Generative Model for Lexical Normalization for Asian Languages0.18jina-embeddings-v5-text: Task-Targeted Embedding Distillation0.16
Iterative Layer Pruning with Knowledge Distillation for Neural Machine Translation
Core Idea: Compress large multilingual translation models by iteratively removing transformer layers while fine-tuning with knowledge distillation from a larger teacher model to maintain translation quality.
Problem It Solves: Large multilingual translation models (like NLLB-200) are too computationally expensive and memory-intensive to deploy in resource-constrained settings, particularly for serving African languages where infrastructure may be limited.
How It Works:

Layer Pruning: Start with NLLB-200 600M baseline model and iteratively remove transformer layers to reduce model size and inference latency
Knowledge Distillation: Fine-tune the pruned model on curated parallel corpora for African languages, using predictions from the larger unpruned model as soft targets to preserve translation quality
Quantization: Apply quantization techniques to further reduce model size and memory footprint
Dual Deployment: Release both Transformers version (for further fine-tuning) and CTranslate2 version (for efficient inference)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[NLLB-200 600M<br/>Teacher Model] --> B[Layer Pruning]
    B --> C[Pruned Model<br/>Student]
    A -.Soft Targets.-> D[Knowledge Distillation]
    C --> D
    E[African Language<br/>Parallel Corpora] --> D
    D --> F[Fine-tuned Model]
    F --> G[Quantization]
    G --> H[AfriNLLB<br/>Compressed Model]

Where It’s Useful:

Deploying multilingual NMT systems in low-resource settings with limited compute/memory
Mobile or edge translation applications for African languages
Cost-efficient serving of translation models across 30 translation directions
Scenarios requiring fast inference with acceptable quality trade-offs

Limitations / Caveats:

Performance degradation from layer pruning may not be uniform across all 15 language pairs
Requires access to larger teacher model during training (though not at inference)
Quality-speed trade-off depends on how aggressively layers are pruned
May require language-specific tuning to determine optimal pruning ratios

Internal Use Cases:

Feed Localization: Compress multilingual content understanding models for real-time feed translation/summarization across diverse language markets
RecSys Efficiency: Apply iterative pruning + distillation to compress large ranking models or LLM-based recommenders for lower latency serving
Inference Optimization: Use CTranslate2-style quantization approach for deploying compressed LLMs in production ranking systems where millisecond latency matters
Multi-Market Models: Build single compressed models serving multiple geographic markets (similar to 15 language pairs here) instead of maintaining separate models per locale

literature/2026-02-11-Answer-First-Reaso.title: Answer First, Reason Later: Aligning Search Relevance via Mode-Balanced Reinforcement Learning
literature/2026-02-11-Answer-First-Reaso.content:
Answer First, Reason Later: Aligning Search Relevance via Mode-Balanced Reinforcement Learning
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-10
Rank: #5 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, LLM, RL, Search
Abstract
Building a search relevance model that achieves both low latency and high performance is a long-standing challenge in the search industry. To satisfy the millisecond-level response requirements of online systems while retaining the interpretable reasoning traces of Large Language Models (LLMs), we propose a novel \textbf{Answer-First, Reason Later (AFRL)} paradigm. This paradigm requires the model to output the definitive relevance score in the very first token, followed by a structured logical explanation. Inspired by the success of reasoning models, we adopt a “Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) + Reinforcement Learning (RL)” pipeline to achieve AFRL. However, directly applying existing RL training often leads to \textbf{mode collapse} in the search relevance task, where the model forgets complex long-tail rules in pursuit of high rewards. From an information theory perspective: RL inherently minimizes the \textbf{Reverse KL divergence}, which tends to seek probability peaks (mode-seeking) and is prone to “reward hacking.” On the other hand, SFT minimizes the \textbf{Forward KL divergence}, forcing the model to cover the data distribution (mode-covering) and effectively anchoring expert rules. Based on this insight, we propose a \textbf{Mode-Balanced Optimization} strategy, incorporating an SFT auxiliary loss into Stepwise-GRPO training to balance these two properties. Furthermore, we construct an automated instruction evolution system and a multi-stage curriculum to ensure expert-level data quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our 32B teacher model achieves state-of-the-art performance. Moreover, the AFRL architecture enables efficient knowledge distillation, successfully transferring expert-level logic to a 0.6B model, thereby reconciling reasoning depth with deployment latency.
Key Techniques

Answer-First
Reason Later (AFRL) paradigm
Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) + Reinforcement Learning (RL) pipeline
Mode-Balanced Optimization strategy
Automated instruction evolution system with multi-stage curriculum

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityTowards On-Policy SFT: Distribution Discriminant Theory and its Applications in LLM Training0.23ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.23Improving Data and Reward Design for Scientific Reasoning in Large Language Models0.23ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.22ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.22
Answer-First, Reason Later (AFRL) with Mode-Balanced Optimization
Core Idea: A search relevance paradigm that outputs the definitive relevance score in the first token followed by reasoning traces, trained with a hybrid RL+SFT approach that balances mode-seeking (reward maximization) and mode-covering (expert knowledge retention) to prevent mode collapse.
Problem It Solves: Traditional search relevance models face a latency-performance tradeoff: small models are fast but lack reasoning capability, while LLM-based models provide interpretable reasoning but are too slow for millisecond-level requirements. Standard RL training (e.g., GRPO) suffers from mode collapse where models forget complex long-tail relevance rules while pursuing high rewards.
How It Works:

AFRL Architecture: Model is constrained to emit the relevance score as the very first output token, followed by structured logical explanation tokens
Two-Stage Training: First apply Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on expert-annotated relevance judgments with reasoning traces to establish baseline coverage of the data distribution
Mode-Balanced GRPO: Augment Stepwise Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with an auxiliary SFT loss term to balance reverse KL (mode-seeking, reward hacking prone) with forward KL (mode-covering, expert rule anchoring)
Curriculum Learning: Use automated instruction evolution system to generate high-quality training data across multiple difficulty stages
Knowledge Distillation: The AFRL structure (answer-first) enables efficient transfer from 32B teacher to 0.6B student model by focusing distillation on the critical first-token decision

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Query + Document] --> B[AFRL Model]
    B --> C[Token 1: Relevance Score]
    C --> D[Tokens 2-N: Reasoning Trace]
    D --> E[Structured Explanation]
    
    F[Mode-Balanced Training] --> B
    F --> G[GRPO Loss<br/>reverse KL]
    F --> H[SFT Auxiliary Loss<br/>forward KL]

Where It’s Useful:

Search relevance scoring systems requiring both sub-millisecond latency and interpretability
Any ranking task where first-token decisions enable early-exit optimizations
Production systems needing to distill large reasoning models into deployable small models
Scenarios with long-tail complex rules that must be preserved during RL optimization

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires high-quality expert-annotated reasoning traces for SFT initialization
The balance between SFT auxiliary loss weight and GRPO requires careful tuning to avoid undermining RL optimization
AFRL paradigm constrains model flexibility by forcing answer-first structure, which may not suit all reasoning tasks
Mode-balanced optimization adds computational overhead compared to pure RL training

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys ranking: Train recommendation models that output relevance scores instantly while providing interpretable reasoning for debugging and user trust
Search quality: Build latency-critical search rankers that maintain coverage of complex edge-case rules (e.g., temporal relevance, domain-specific jargon) during RL fine-tuning
Model compression: Use AFRL structure to distill reasoning capabilities from large teacher models into mobile-deployable rankers without losing explanation quality
RL stability: Apply mode-balanced optimization to any RL-based ranking pipeline prone to reward hacking or forgetting rare-but-important relevance patterns

literature/2026-02-11-Benchmarking-the-E.title: Benchmarking the Energy Savings with Speculative Decoding Strategies
literature/2026-02-11-Benchmarking-the-E.content:
Benchmarking the Energy Savings with Speculative Decoding Strategies
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-09
Rank: #4 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Speculative decoding has emerged as an effective method to reduce latency and inference cost of LLM inferences. However, there has been inadequate attention towards the energy requirements of these models. To address this gap, this paper presents a comprehensive survey of energy requirements of speculative decoding strategies, with detailed analysis on how various factors — model size and family, speculative decoding strategies, and dataset characteristics — influence the energy optimizations.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques or methods:

Speculative decoding

Energy optimization analysis
Model size and family considerations
Dataset characteristics analysis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.34PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models0.34Speculative Decoding with a Speculative Vocabulary0.33SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration0.31DART: Diffusion-Inspired Speculative Decoding for Fast LLM Inference0.31
Energy-Aware Speculative Decoding Benchmarking
Core Idea: Systematic empirical framework for measuring and comparing the energy consumption of speculative decoding strategies across model families, draft lengths, and datasets, revealing that energy savings don’t always align with latency improvements.
Problem It Solves: Prior work on speculative decoding focused exclusively on latency/throughput metrics, ignoring energy consumption—a critical factor for deployment costs, carbon footprint, and edge/mobile inference where power budgets are constrained.
How It Works:

Instrumentation: Measure energy consumption (via hardware power monitors or software estimators) during both prefill and decode phases for baseline autoregressive decoding vs. speculative decoding variants
Experimental matrix: Vary independent variables—target model size (e.g., 7B, 13B, 70B), draft model size, draft length (k), acceptance rate, dataset characteristics (prompt length, generation length)
Energy decomposition: Attribute energy to draft model calls, target model verification, rejected token overhead, and KV-cache operations
Comparative analysis: Quantify energy-latency tradeoffs across strategies (independent drafting, shared-prefix drafting, tree-based speculation, retrieval-augmented drafting)

Where It’s Useful:

Production LLM deployments where electricity costs dominate TCO (hyperscaler inference)
On-device/edge AI applications with strict power budgets (mobile, IoT)
Sustainability-conscious model serving (carbon footprint optimization)
Cost-performance optimization when choosing between speculative decoding strategies

Limitations / Caveats:

Energy measurements are hardware-dependent (GPU architecture, TDP, utilization levels)
Acceptance rate heavily influences results—low acceptance can make speculative decoding energy-negative
Draft model energy overhead can dominate savings for small target models or short generations
Static analysis doesn’t capture dynamic batching effects or memory bandwidth bottlenecks

Internal Use Cases:

LLM serving cost modeling: Incorporate energy cost alongside latency when selecting draft strategies for production inference endpoints
Multi-objective serving optimization: Build Pareto frontiers trading off latency, throughput, and energy for different workload SLAs
Sustainable ML research: Establish energy budgets for experimentation, report energy/token alongside latency in model cards

literature/2026-02-11-Decomposing-Reason.title: Decomposing Reasoning Efficiency in Large Language Models
literature/2026-02-11-Decomposing-Reason.content:
Decomposing Reasoning Efficiency in Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-10
Rank: #3 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RecSys, LLM
Abstract
Large language models trained for reasoning trade off inference tokens against accuracy, yet standard evaluations report only final accuracy, obscuring where tokens are spent or wasted. We introduce a trace-optional framework that decomposes token efficiency into interpretable factors: completion under a fixed token budget (avoiding truncation), conditional correctness given completion, and verbosity (token usage). When benchmark metadata provides per-instance workload proxies, we further factor verbosity into two components: mean verbalization overhead (tokens per work unit) and a coupling coefficient capturing how overhead scales with task workload. When reasoning traces are available, we add deterministic trace-quality measures (grounding, repetition, prompt copying) to separate degenerate looping from verbose-but-engaged reasoning, avoiding human labeling and LLM judges. Evaluating 25 models on CogniLoad, we find that accuracy and token-efficiency rankings diverge (Spearman ρ=0.63), efficiency gaps are often driven by conditional correctness, and verbalization overhead varies by about 9 times (only weakly related to model scale). Our decomposition reveals distinct bottleneck profiles that suggest different efficiency interventions.
Key Techniques

Token budget completion analysis
conditional correctness factorization
verbosity decomposition with workload coupling
deterministic trace-quality measures

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityIs my model “mind blurting”? Interpreting the dynamics of reasoning tokens with Recurrence Quantification Analysis (RQA)0.19LogitsCoder: Towards Efficient Chain-of-Thought Path Search via Logits Preference Decoding for Code Generation0.18S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation0.17ImgCoT: Compressing Long Chain of Thought into Compact Visual Tokens for Efficient Reasoning of Large Language Model0.16ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.15
Trace-Optional Efficiency Decomposition
Core Idea: A systematic framework that decomposes LLM reasoning efficiency into interpretable factors (completion rate, conditional correctness, verbosity, and optional trace quality) to reveal where inference tokens are spent or wasted, enabling targeted optimization interventions.
Problem It Solves: Standard reasoning benchmarks report only final accuracy, obscuring critical efficiency bottlenecks—models may fail by running out of tokens, making reasoning errors, being unnecessarily verbose, or generating degenerate outputs. Without decomposition, practitioners cannot diagnose whether to optimize truncation handling, reasoning quality, or token overhead.
How It Works:

Completion & Conditional Correctness: Measure completion rate under fixed token budgets (avoiding truncation) and accuracy conditional on completion, isolating budget failures from reasoning failures
Verbosity Factorization: When workload proxies exist (e.g., proof steps, code lines), decompose total tokens into mean overhead per work unit and a coupling coefficient capturing how overhead scales with task difficulty
Trace Quality Metrics (optional): For models with reasoning traces, compute deterministic metrics (grounding in problem context, repetition rate, prompt copying) to separate degenerate looping from engaged-but-verbose reasoning—no human annotation or LLM judges required
Bottleneck Profiling: Combine factors to create efficiency profiles revealing whether models fail on budgets, correctness, or verbosity

Architecture:
flowchart TB
    A[Model Output + Token Budget] --> B[Completion Rate]
    A --> C[Conditional Correctness | Completion]
    A --> D[Verbosity = Total Tokens]
    D --> E{Workload Proxy Available?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Mean Overhead per Unit<br/>+ Coupling Coefficient]
    E -->|No| G[Aggregate Token Count]
    A --> H{Reasoning Trace Available?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Trace Quality:<br/>Grounding, Repetition,<br/>Prompt Copying]
    H -->|No| J[Skip Trace Metrics]
    B --> K[Efficiency Profile]
    C --> K
    F --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K

Where It’s Useful:

Reasoning model development: Diagnosing whether o1/R1-style models waste tokens on verbosity vs. poor reasoning vs. budget mismanagement
Inference optimization: Identifying which models benefit from token budget increases vs. better stopping criteria vs. conciseness training
Benchmark design: Creating evaluations that measure not just “does it work” but “how efficiently does it work”
Model selection: Choosing between models with different accuracy/efficiency tradeoffs for production deployment

Limitations / Caveats:

Workload proxies (proof steps, code lines) must be available for fine-grained verbosity decomposition—not all benchmarks provide this metadata
Trace quality metrics assume reasoning is externalized in text; opaque models or models with implicit reasoning cannot be analyzed at this level
Coupling coefficient interpretation requires sufficient task workload variance; constant-difficulty benchmarks lose this signal
Framework focuses on single-pass reasoning; iterative refinement or multi-attempt strategies require extended analysis

Internal Use Cases:

LLM ranking systems: Decompose efficiency of candidate generation models to balance latency budgets—measure if verbosity comes from thoughtful feature consideration or repetitive looping
RecSys reasoning agents: Profile whether recommendation explanation models waste tokens on grounding failures vs. genuine complexity in multi-constraint optimization
Code generation pipelines: Separate verbosity from correctness in code-LLMs—identify if token overhead scales with code complexity (good coupling) or reflects degenerate patterns
Production model selection: Build efficiency profiles for model portfolios (e.g., Haiku vs. Sonnet vs. Opus) to route queries based on complexity workload proxies, maximizing throughput under SLA constraints

literature/2026-02-11-Generalizing-GNNs-.title: Generalizing GNNs with Tokenized Mixture of Experts
literature/2026-02-11-Generalizing-GNNs-.content:
Generalizing GNNs with Tokenized Mixture of Experts
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-09
Rank: #7 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Embeddings, Graph, RAG
Abstract
Deployed graph neural networks (GNNs) are frozen at deployment yet must fit clean data, generalize under distribution shifts, and remain stable to perturbations. We show that static inference induces a fundamental tradeoff: improving stability requires reducing reliance on shift-sensitive features, leaving an irreducible worst-case generalization floor. Instance-conditional routing can break this ceiling, but is fragile because shifts can mislead routing and perturbations can make routing fluctuate. We capture these effects via two decompositions separating coverage vs selection, and base sensitivity vs fluctuation amplification. Based on these insights, we propose STEM-GNN, a pretrain-then-finetune framework with a mixture-of-experts encoder for diverse computation paths, a vector-quantized token interface to stabilize encoder-to-head signals, and a Lipschitz-regularized head to bound output amplification. Across nine node, link, and graph benchmarks, STEM-GNN achieves a stronger three-way balance, improving robustness to degree/homophily shifts and to feature/edge corruptions while remaining competitive on clean graphs.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques:

**Mixture-of-experts encoder

Vector-quantized token interface
Lipschitz-regularized head**

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityGRIP: Algorithm-Agnostic Machine Unlearning for Mixture-of-Experts0.18Exploring the impact of adaptive rewiring in Graph Neural Networks0.18Unified Multi-Domain Graph Pre-training for Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Graphs via Domain-Specific Expert Encoding0.17MoE-Spec: Expert Budgeting for Efficient Speculative Decoding0.16Semi-Supervised Learning on Graphs using Graph Neural Networks0.15
STEM-GNN (Stabilized Tokenized Mixture-of-Experts GNN)
Core Idea: A pretrain-then-finetune framework that uses mixture-of-experts (MoE) routing for diverse computation paths, vector quantization to stabilize encoder-to-head signals, and Lipschitz regularization to bound output amplification, achieving simultaneous robustness to distribution shifts and input perturbations without sacrificing clean-data performance.
Problem It Solves: Static GNN inference faces a fundamental tradeoff where improving stability to perturbations requires reducing reliance on shift-sensitive features, creating an irreducible generalization floor under distribution shifts; instance-conditional routing can break this ceiling but is fragile because shifts mislead routing and perturbations cause routing fluctuation.
How It Works:

Pretraining with MoE Encoder: Train a mixture-of-experts GNN encoder where each input instance is routed to a subset of expert sub-networks, creating diverse computation paths that can specialize to different data characteristics.
Vector Quantization Interface: Insert a vector-quantized (VQ) tokenization layer between encoder and task head that discretizes continuous embeddings into tokens from a learned codebook, stabilizing the signal and preventing routing fluctuations from propagating to outputs.
Lipschitz-Regularized Head: Apply Lipschitz constraint regularization to the task head (classifier/predictor) to bound the amplification factor from input perturbations to output changes.
Finetuning: Finetune the full model (MoE encoder + VQ layer + constrained head) on downstream tasks, where the VQ bottleneck forces routing decisions to remain stable despite input noise.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Graph Input] --> B[MoE GNN Encoder]
    B --> C[Vector Quantization Layer]
    C --> D[Lipschitz-Regularized Head]
    D --> E[Predictions]
    
    B -.->|Expert 1| B1[Expert Path 1]
    B -.->|Expert 2| B2[Expert Path 2]
    B -.->|Expert K| B3[Expert Path K]
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    
    C -.->|Codebook Lookup| CB[Discrete Tokens]

Where It’s Useful:

Node classification under degree or homophily distribution shifts (e.g., deploying models trained on one social network to another with different connectivity patterns)
Link prediction with adversarial edge/feature perturbations (e.g., robust fraud detection where attackers inject fake edges)
Graph-level prediction requiring stability to both clean generalization and noisy inputs (e.g., molecular property prediction across different datasets and experimental noise)

Limitations / Caveats:

MoE routing adds computational overhead during inference (must evaluate routing decisions and multiple expert paths)
Vector quantization introduces a discretization bottleneck that may lose fine-grained embedding information for tasks requiring high representation precision
Lipschitz regularization can limit model expressiveness by constraining gradient magnitudes
Requires two-stage training (pretrain then finetune) which increases overall training cost compared to end-to-end single-stage approaches

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys under distribution shift: Deploy recommender GNNs trained on one user population to new demographics with different interaction patterns (degree shift) or homophily characteristics, while remaining robust to spam/adversarial fake interactions
Ranking with user-item graph features: Use MoE to specialize experts for different user segments (new vs. returning, high vs. low activity) and VQ to prevent noisy features (clicks with high variance) from destabilizing rankings
LLM-augmented graph retrieval: When using GNNs to encode knowledge graphs or document graphs for retrieval-augmented generation, ensure embeddings remain stable to perturbations in graph construction (OCR errors, entity linking mistakes) and generalize across different document collections

literature/2026-02-11-Generative-Reasoni.title: Generative Reasoning Re-ranker
literature/2026-02-11-Generative-Reasoni.content:
Generative Reasoning Re-ranker
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-08
Rank: #5 (score: 0.00, max_sim: 0.89)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Recent studies increasingly explore Large Language Models (LLMs) as a new paradigm for recommendation systems due to their scalability and world knowledge. However, existing work has three key limitations: (1) most efforts focus on retrieval and ranking, while the reranking phase, critical for refining final recommendations, is largely overlooked; (2) LLMs are typically used in zero-shot or supervised fine-tuning settings, leaving their reasoning abilities, especially those enhanced through reinforcement learning (RL) and high-quality reasoning data, underexploited; (3) items are commonly represented by non-semantic IDs, creating major scalability challenges in industrial systems with billions of identifiers. To address these gaps, we propose the Generative Reasoning Reranker (GR2), an end-to-end framework with a three-stage training pipeline tailored for reranking. First, a pretrained LLM is mid-trained on semantic IDs encoded from non-semantic IDs via a tokenizer achieving \ge99% uniqueness. Next, a stronger larger-scale LLM generates high-quality reasoning traces through carefully designed prompting and rejection sampling, which are used for supervised fine-tuning to impart foundational reasoning skills. Finally, we apply Decoupled Clip and Dynamic sAmpling Policy Optimization (DAPO), enabling scalable RL supervision with verifiable rewards designed specifically for reranking. Experiments on two real-world datasets demonstrate GR2’s effectiveness: it surpasses the state-of-the-art OneRec-Think by 2.4% in Recall@5 and 1.3% in NDCG@5. Ablations confirm that advanced reasoning traces yield substantial gains across metrics. We further find that RL reward design is crucial in reranking: LLMs tend to exploit reward hacking by preserving item order, motivating conditional verifiable rewards to mitigate this behavior and optimize reranking performance.
Key Techniques

semantic ID tokenization
supervised fine-tuning with reasoning traces
Decoupled Clip and Dynamic sAmpling Policy Optimization (DAPO)

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityGenerative Reasoning Re-ranker0.89ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.22ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.22ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.20Answer First, Reason Later: Aligning Search Relevance via Mode-Balanced Reinforcement Learning0.20
Generative Reasoning Reranker (GR2)
Core Idea: A three-stage LLM training pipeline for recommendation reranking that combines semantic ID encoding, reasoning trace generation via supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards to optimize final ranking quality.
Problem It Solves: Existing LLM-based recommendation systems focus on retrieval/ranking while neglecting reranking, fail to leverage reasoning abilities enhanced through RL, suffer from scalability issues with non-semantic IDs in billion-scale industrial systems, and are vulnerable to reward hacking where models preserve input order instead of truly reranking.
How It Works:

Mid-training on Semantic IDs: Pretrained LLM learns to encode non-semantic item IDs into semantic representations via a tokenizer achieving ≥99% uniqueness, solving scalability for billions of items
Reasoning Trace SFT: A larger-scale LLM generates high-quality reasoning traces through carefully designed prompting and rejection sampling; these traces are used for supervised fine-tuning to teach foundational reasoning skills
DAPO Reinforcement Learning: Apply Decoupled Clip and Dynamic sAmpling Policy Optimization with conditional verifiable rewards specifically designed to prevent order preservation exploitation and optimize actual reranking performance

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Non-semantic IDs] --> B[Semantic ID Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Mid-trained LLM]
    C --> D[Reasoning Trace Generator]
    D --> E[SFT with Traces]
    E --> F[DAPO RL Training]
    F --> G[Reranked Item List]
    H[Verifiable Rewards] --> F

Where It’s Useful:

Industrial-scale recommendation systems requiring final-stage reranking with billions of items
E-commerce, content platforms, and search systems where ranking quality directly impacts business metrics
Any LLM-based recommender needing to exploit reasoning capabilities while avoiding reward hacking

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires multi-stage training pipeline with access to both smaller base LLM and larger reasoning LLM for trace generation
Reward design is critical and domain-specific; poorly designed rewards lead to order preservation instead of meaningful reranking
Semantic ID encoding may lose some information from original non-semantic IDs despite 99% uniqueness
Computational cost of RL training stage may be prohibitive for frequent retraining

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys final-stage reranking where candidate lists need refinement using world knowledge and reasoning
Multi-objective ranking optimization using RL with verifiable business metrics as rewards
Scaling LLM recommenders to handle massive item catalogs via semantic ID compression
Addressing reward hacking in ranking RL by designing conditional rewards that penalize order preservation

literature/2026-02-11-Long-Chain-of-Thou.title: Long Chain-of-Thought Compression via Fine-Grained Group Policy Optimization
literature/2026-02-11-Long-Chain-of-Thou.content:
Long Chain-of-Thought Compression via Fine-Grained Group Policy Optimization
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-10
Rank: #4 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, RL
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) often generate unnecessarily verbose Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning that increases computational costs and latency without proportional performance gains. In this paper, we propose \textbf{F}ine-grained \textbf{G}roup policy \textbf{O}ptimization (\textbf{FGO}), a Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithm that refines group responses by subdividing them and assigning appropriate weights based on length and entropy, thereby enabling effective CoT compression. Meanwhile, as an enhanced variant of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), FGO successfully addresses two major limitations of the GRPO: inefficient data utilization and entropy collapse. We evaluate FGO on multiple reasoning LLMs and benchmarks, including MATH500, AIME24, AMC23, and Minerva. Experimental results show that FGO achieves efficient CoT compression without degrading performance, and simultaneously resolves the key limitations of GRPO.
Key Techniques

Chain-of-Thought compression
Group policy optimization with length and entropy weighting
Reinforcement learning for response refinement
Entropy collapse prevention

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityImgCoT: Compressing Long Chain of Thought into Compact Visual Tokens for Efficient Reasoning of Large Language Model0.26Divide-and-Conquer CoT: RL for Reducing Latency via Parallel Reasoning0.24The Potential of CoT for Reasoning: A Closer Look at Trace Dynamics0.24Data Distribution Matters: A Data-Centric Perspective on Context Compression for Large Language Model0.23Why Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models0.21
Fine-Grained Group Policy Optimization (FGO)
Core Idea: An RL algorithm that compresses verbose Chain-of-Thought reasoning by subdividing group responses into fine-grained segments and assigning length/entropy-based weights to prioritize concise, informative reasoning paths.
Problem It Solves:

LLMs generate unnecessarily verbose CoT reasoning that increases computational costs and latency without performance gains
Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) suffers from inefficient data utilization and entropy collapse during training

How It Works:

Group Response Generation: Generate multiple CoT reasoning responses for the same input prompt
Fine-Grained Subdivision: Split each response into smaller segments (rather than treating entire responses uniformly)
Weighted Scoring: Assign weights to segments based on:

Length penalty (shorter reasoning preferred)
Entropy metrics (diverse, informative reasoning preferred)


Policy Optimization: Update the policy using weighted advantages that encourage compression while maintaining reasoning quality

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Prompt] --> B[Generate Group Responses]
    B --> C[Subdivide into Segments]
    C --> D[Compute Length + Entropy Weights]
    D --> E[Weighted Advantage Calculation]
    E --> F[Policy Update]
    F --> G[Compressed CoT Model]

Where It’s Useful:

Mathematical reasoning tasks (MATH500, AIME24, AMC23)
Scientific problem solving (Minerva benchmark)
Production LLM systems requiring low-latency inference with reasoning capabilities
Any application where CoT verbosity creates computational bottlenecks

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful tuning of length vs. entropy weight balance to avoid over-compression
May sacrifice reasoning transparency if compression is too aggressive
Effectiveness depends on quality of initial verbose CoT generations

Internal Use Cases:

LLM Serving Optimization: Reduce inference costs for reasoning-heavy applications by training models to generate concise yet accurate reasoning traces
Ranking/Retrieval Systems: Apply fine-grained weighting principles to multi-stage ranking where different stages have varying importance and computational budgets
Model Distillation: Use FGO’s segment-level weighting approach when distilling verbose teacher models into efficient student models for production deployment

literature/2026-02-11-One-Paradigm-for-A.title: One Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral Profiles
literature/2026-02-11-One-Paradigm-for-A.content:
One Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral Profiles
Source: S2:Proceedings of the Recommender Systems 2025
Published: 2025
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, RecSys, LLM, Graph
Abstract
Modern digital platforms generate vast amounts of user interaction data every day. Although this data is closely related, predictive tasks such as recommendation and churn prediction are often modeled separately, resulting in inconsistent user representations. The RecSys Challenge 2025 explores this issue by promoting the creation of Universal Behavioral Profiles: single, versatile embeddings that generalize across multiple tasks. To exploit the relational nature of interaction data stored in tables, our team “sisinflabpoliba” proposed a solution rooted in the Relational Deep Learning (RDL) paradigm, which treats the user’s multi-faceted journey as a unified heterogeneous graph. Our approach enhances the LightGCN architecture to perform relation-aware message passing across diverse entities like users, products, and queries. We then create a hybrid profile by fusing these learned deep embeddings with a robust, feature-engineered baseline. This strategy effectively balances the complex relational patterns captured by the Graph Neural Network model with the stability of handcrafted features, yielding powerful, general-purpose user profiles that demonstrate strong performance across the challenge’s open and hidden tasks.
Key Techniques

Relational Deep Learning (RDL)
Enhanced LightGCN with relation-aware message passing
Hybrid profile fusion of graph embeddings with feature-engineered baseline

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs0.32BEHAV-E! You are Not Just a Number to Us, but an R2048 Embedding0.29Unified Multi-Domain Graph Pre-training for Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Graphs via Domain-Specific Expert Encoding0.28Towards Personalized Bangla Book Recommendation: A Large-Scale Multi-Entity Book Graph Dataset0.21Semi-Supervised Learning on Graphs using Graph Neural Networks0.21
Relation-Aware LightGCN for Universal Behavioral Profiles
Core Idea: Extend the LightGCN graph neural network architecture with relation-aware message passing to learn unified user embeddings that generalize across multiple prediction tasks (recommendation, churn, search) by modeling multi-faceted user journeys as heterogeneous graphs.
Problem It Solves: Traditional recommender systems model different prediction tasks (e.g., product recommendation vs. churn prediction) separately, resulting in inconsistent and task-specific user representations. This approach addresses the challenge of creating single, versatile user embeddings that perform well across diverse downstream tasks without requiring separate models for each.
How It Works:

Graph Construction: Transform tabular user interaction data into a unified heterogeneous graph with multiple entity types (users, products, queries, sessions) and diverse edge types (purchase, view, search, add-to-cart)
Relation-Aware Message Passing: Enhance standard LightGCN by incorporating edge type information during neighborhood aggregation, allowing the model to distinguish between different interaction semantics (e.g., a purchase signal vs. a view signal)
Multi-Layer Propagation: Perform K layers of graph convolution to capture multi-hop relational patterns, then aggregate layer-wise embeddings to produce final entity representations
Hybrid Profile Fusion: Combine the learned deep graph embeddings with feature-engineered baseline features to create robust universal behavioral profiles that balance pattern complexity with stability

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Tabular User Data] --> B[Heterogeneous Graph]
    B --> C[Relation-Aware LightGCN]
    C --> D[Deep Graph Embeddings]
    E[Feature Engineering] --> F[Baseline Features]
    D --> G[Fusion Layer]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Universal User Profile]
    H --> I1[Task 1: Recommendation]
    H --> I2[Task 2: Churn Prediction]
    H --> I3[Task 3: Search Ranking]

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-task learning scenarios where a single user representation must support multiple downstream objectives
E-commerce platforms with diverse interaction types (browsing, searching, purchasing, reviewing)
User profiling for cross-domain personalization where consistency across tasks is critical
Cold-start scenarios where relational graph structure provides signal beyond sparse feature vectors

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires sufficient graph connectivity; sparse interaction graphs may limit message passing effectiveness
Computational cost scales with graph size and number of relation types, potentially challenging for billion-scale platforms
Hybrid fusion approach requires careful tuning of combination weights between deep and engineered features
May struggle with highly dynamic user preferences that evolve faster than the graph can be updated

Internal Use Cases:

Unified User Embeddings: Create single user representations for Anthropic products that power multiple downstream tasks (content recommendation, usage prediction, engagement forecasting)
Cross-Modal Interaction Modeling: Apply relation-aware GNNs to model diverse user interactions with Claude (chat, API calls, feature usage, feedback) as a heterogeneous graph
Multi-Behavior Sequential Recommendation: Extend our existing sequential models to explicitly encode different interaction types (message send, regenerate, edit, copy) as distinct edge relations rather than treating all actions uniformly
Transfer Learning for New Tasks: Use pre-trained universal profiles as initialization when adding new prediction objectives, reducing cold-start training requirements

literature/2026-02-11-PABU-Progress-Awar.title: PABU: Progress-Aware Belief Update for Efficient LLM Agents
literature/2026-02-11-PABU-Progress-Awar.content:
PABU: Progress-Aware Belief Update for Efficient LLM Agents
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-09
Rank: #2 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG
Abstract
Large Language Model (LLM) agents commonly condition actions on full action-observation histories, which introduce task-irrelevant information that easily leads to redundant actions and higher inference cost. We propose Progress-Aware Belief Update (PABU), a belief-state framework that compactly represents an agent’s state by explicitly modeling task progress and selectively retaining past actions and observations. At each step, the agent predicts its relative progress since the previous round and decides whether the newly encountered interaction should be stored, conditioning future decisions only on the retained subset. Across eight environments in the AgentGym benchmark, and using identical training trajectories, PABU achieves an 81.0% task completion rate, outperforming previous State of the art (SoTA) models with full-history belief by 23.9%. Additionally, PABU’s progress-oriented action selection improves efficiency, reducing the average number of interaction steps to 9.5, corresponding to a 26.9% reduction. Ablation studies show that both explicit progress prediction and selective retention are necessary for robust belief learning and performance gains.
Key Techniques

Progress-aware belief update
explicit progress prediction
selective retention of past actions and observations

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityEnforcing Monotonic Progress in Legal Cross-Examination: Preventing Long-Horizon Stagnation in LLM-Based Inquiry0.16SPILLage: Agentic Oversharing on the Web0.15Towards Reducible Uncertainty Modeling for Reliable Large Language Model Agents0.14Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.13ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.12
Progress-Aware Belief Update (PABU)
Core Idea: A belief-state framework for LLM agents that compresses action-observation histories by explicitly tracking task progress and selectively retaining only relevant past interactions, rather than conditioning on full histories.
Problem It Solves: Full action-observation histories introduce task-irrelevant information that leads to redundant actions, higher inference costs, and cognitive overload—particularly in multi-step agent environments where context windows become bloated with unnecessary details.
How It Works:

Progress Prediction: At each interaction step, the agent predicts its relative progress since the previous round (e.g., “30% closer to goal”)
Selective Retention: Based on the progress signal, the agent decides whether the current action-observation pair should be stored in the belief state or discarded
Compact Conditioning: Future actions are conditioned only on the retained subset of interactions plus the explicit progress estimate, rather than the full history

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Full History] --> B[Progress Predictor]
    B --> C{Store or Discard?}
    A --> C
    C -->|Store| D[Belief State]
    C -->|Discard| E[/Dropped/]
    D --> F[Next Action]
    B --> F

Where It’s Useful:

Long-horizon agent tasks (web navigation, tool use, game playing)
Resource-constrained environments where inference cost matters
Multi-step reasoning where intermediate states vary in importance
Any sequential decision-making task with redundant or repetitive observations

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires training to learn effective progress prediction and retention policies (not zero-shot)
May discard information that becomes relevant later in unexpected task branches
Performance depends on how well progress can be quantified for a given task domain

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys agents that iteratively refine user preferences—retain only breakthrough interactions, discard redundant refinements
Multi-turn LLM conversations where belief compression could track “conversation progress” and drop repetitive clarifications
Ranking pipeline agents that explore feature spaces—keep only informative experiments, prune redundant A/B tests

literature/2026-02-11-Stop-the-Flip-Flop.title: Stop the Flip-Flop: Context-Preserving Verification for Fast Revocable Diffusion Decoding
literature/2026-02-11-Stop-the-Flip-Flop.content:
Stop the Flip-Flop: Context-Preserving Verification for Fast Revocable Diffusion Decoding
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-05
Rank: #1 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Parallel diffusion decoding can accelerate diffusion language model inference by unmasking multiple tokens per step, but aggressive parallelism often harms quality. Revocable decoding mitigates this by rechecking earlier tokens, yet we observe that existing verification schemes frequently trigger flip-flop oscillations, where tokens are remasked and later restored unchanged. This behaviour slows inference in two ways: remasking verified positions weakens the conditioning context for parallel drafting, and repeated remask cycles consume the revision budget with little net progress. We propose COVER (Cache Override Verification for Efficient Revision), which performs leave-one-out verification and stable drafting within a single forward pass. COVER constructs two attention views via KV cache override: selected seeds are masked for verification, while their cached key value states are injected for all other queries to preserve contextual information, with a closed form diagonal correction preventing self leakage at the seed positions. COVER further prioritises seeds using a stability aware score that balances uncertainty, downstream influence, and cache drift, and it adapts the number of verified seeds per step. Across benchmarks, COVER markedly reduces unnecessary revisions and yields faster decoding while preserving output quality.
Key Techniques

Here are the 2-4 key techniques from the paper:

**KV cache override verification

leave-one-out verification with diagonal correction
stability-aware seed prioritization**

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityStop the Flip-Flop: Context-Preserving Verification for Fast Revocable Diffusion Decoding0.91DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.26Greedy Multi-Path Block Verification for Faster Decoding in Speculative Sampling0.23DART: Diffusion-Inspired Speculative Decoding for Fast LLM Inference0.23FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion0.21
COVER (Cache Override Verification for Efficient Revision)
Core Idea: A single-pass verification and drafting method for revocable diffusion decoding that uses KV cache override to simultaneously verify selected seed tokens (by masking them) and draft new tokens (using cached context), preventing flip-flop oscillations.
Problem It Solves: Existing revocable diffusion decoding methods trigger flip-flop oscillations where tokens are unnecessarily remasked and later restored unchanged, wasting the revision budget and degrading parallel drafting quality by removing verified context.
How It Works:

Stability-Aware Seed Selection: Score candidate seed positions using uncertainty, downstream influence (attention weights), and cache drift; adaptively select top-k seeds to verify.
Leave-One-Out Verification via Cache Override: Construct dual attention views in one forward pass—mask selected seed positions for verification queries, but inject their cached KV states for all other queries to preserve context.
Diagonal Correction: Apply closed-form correction to prevent self-leakage at seed positions (mask×cached attention preventing seeds from attending to themselves).
Stable Parallel Drafting: Draft new tokens at masked positions using the preserved context from cache override, avoiding the context degradation of naive remasking.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Partially Decoded Sequence] --> B[Stability-Aware Scoring]
    B --> C[Select k Seeds]
    C --> D[Cache Override: Dual View]
    D --> E[Verify Seeds + Draft Tokens]
    E --> F[Accept/Remask Decision]
    F --> G[Updated Sequence]

Where It’s Useful:

Parallel decoding in diffusion language models (e.g., Plaid, SEDD)
Any iterative refinement generation where aggressive parallelism causes quality loss
Low-latency text generation requiring quality-speed tradeoff optimization

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires KV cache infrastructure and attention mechanism modification for override
Stability scoring adds per-step overhead (though amortized by reduced flip-flops)
Effectiveness depends on cache quality—stale caches reduce verification accuracy

Internal Use Cases:

LLM Inference Acceleration: Adapt cache override pattern to speculative decoding verification—verify multiple draft tokens while preserving context for subsequent predictions
Batch Verification: Extend to verify multiple candidate sequences simultaneously by constructing per-candidate cache views
Ranking Model Distillation: Use stability-aware scoring to identify which input features most influence model predictions under perturbation

literature/2026-02-11-When-Less-is-More-.title: When Less is More: The LLM Scaling Paradox in Context Compression
literature/2026-02-11-When-Less-is-More-.content:
When Less is More: The LLM Scaling Paradox in Context Compression
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-10
Rank: #2 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, LLM
Abstract
Scaling up model parameters has long been a prevalent training paradigm driven by the assumption that larger models yield superior generation capabilities. However, under lossy context compression in a compressor-decoder setup, we observe a Size-Fidelity Paradox: increasing the compressor size can lessen the faithfulness of reconstructed contexts though training loss decreases. Through extensive experiments across models from 0.6B to 90B, we coin this paradox arising from two dominant factors: 1) knowledge overwriting: larger models increasingly replace source facts with their own prior beliefs, e.g., the white strawberry'' $\to$ the red strawberry”; and 2) semantic drift: larger models tend to paraphrase or restructure content instead of reproducing it verbatim, e.g., Alice hit Bob'' $\to$ Bob hit Alice”. By holding model size fixed, we reflect on the emergent properties of compressed context representations. We show that the culprit is not parameter count itself, but the excessive semantic capacity and amplified generative uncertainty that accompany scaling. Specifically, the increased rank of context embeddings facilitates prior knowledge intrusion, whereas higher entropy over token prediction distributions promotes rewriting. Our results complement existing evaluations over context compression paradigm, underpinning a breakdown in scaling laws for faithful preservation in open-ended generation.
Key Techniques

knowledge overwriting
semantic drift
context embedding rank analysis
token prediction entropy evaluation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityData Distribution Matters: A Data-Centric Perspective on Context Compression for Large Language Model0.32ATACompressor: Adaptive Task-Aware Compression for Efficient Long-Context Processing in LLMs0.22Attn-GS: Attention-Guided Context Compression for Efficient Personalized LLMs0.19Context Compression via Explicit Information Transmission0.18SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.17
Size-Fidelity Paradox in Lossy Context Compression
Core Idea: In compressor-decoder setups for context compression, increasing the compressor’s model size counter-intuitively reduces reconstruction faithfulness despite lower training loss, due to knowledge overwriting and semantic drift.
Problem It Solves: Challenges the assumption that larger models always produce better outputs; reveals that scaling up compressor models in context compression systems leads to unfaithful reconstructions where the decoder replaces source facts with prior knowledge or paraphrases content incorrectly.
How It Works:

Compressor-Decoder Setup: A compressor LLM encodes input context into compressed representations, then a decoder LLM reconstructs the original context from these representations
Knowledge Overwriting: Larger compressor models increasingly substitute source facts with their own learned priors (e.g., transforming “white strawberry” → “red strawberry” based on common knowledge)
Semantic Drift: Larger models paraphrase or restructure content instead of verbatim reproduction (e.g., “Alice hit Bob” → “Bob hit Alice”), driven by higher entropy in token prediction distributions
Root Cause: Increased embedding rank facilitates prior knowledge intrusion, while higher prediction entropy promotes rewriting behavior

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Source Context] --> B[Compressor LLM<br/>0.6B-90B params]
    B --> C[Compressed<br/>Representations]
    C --> D[Decoder LLM]
    D --> E[Reconstructed Context]
    E -.Fidelity Degradation<br/>as size ↑.-> A

Where It’s Useful:

Context compression systems for long-document processing
Prompt caching and context optimization pipelines
Memory-augmented LLM architectures requiring faithful context preservation
Evaluating scaling laws for compression vs. generation tasks

Limitations / Caveats:

Paradox is specific to lossy compression with faithfulness requirements; may not apply to summarization or semantic compression where paraphrasing is acceptable
Trade-off between training loss (which improves with scale) and reconstruction fidelity creates optimization conflicts
Mitigation strategies not fully explored in the paper

Internal Use Cases:

RAG pipeline optimization: When compressing retrieved documents for LLM context, smaller compressor models may preserve factual accuracy better than larger ones
Context window management: Designing compression strategies that prioritize verbatim preservation over semantic understanding for critical information retrieval
Model selection for caching: Choosing appropriate model sizes for prompt compression layers to avoid knowledge contamination

literature/2026-02-11-WildCat-Near-Linea.title: WildCat: Near-Linear Attention in Theory and Practice
literature/2026-02-11-WildCat-Near-Linea.content:
WildCat: Near-Linear Attention in Theory and Practice
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-10
Rank: #3 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
We introduce WildCat, a high-accuracy, low-cost approach to compressing the attention mechanism in neural networks. While attention is a staple of modern network architectures, it is also notoriously expensive to deploy due to resource requirements that scale quadratically with the input sequence length n. WildCat avoids these quadratic costs by only attending over a small weighted coreset. Crucially, we select the coreset using a fast but spectrally-accurate subsampling algorithm — randomly pivoted Cholesky — and weight the elements optimally to minimise reconstruction error. Remarkably, given bounded inputs, WildCat approximates exact attention with super-polynomial O(n^{-\sqrt{\log(\log(n))}}) error decay while running in near-linear O(n^{1+o(1)}) time. In contrast, prior practical approximations either lack error guarantees or require quadratic runtime to guarantee such high fidelity. We couple this advance with a GPU-optimized PyTorch implementation and a suite of benchmark experiments demonstrating the benefits of WildCat for image generation, image classification, and language model KV cache compression.
Key Techniques

Weighted coreset selection
randomly pivoted Cholesky
optimal weighting for reconstruction error minimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.29HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.29SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.28SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.28Superlinear Multi-Step Attention0.27
WildCat (Weighted Coreset Attention via Randomly Pivoted Cholesky)
Core Idea: Approximates full attention by attending only over a small weighted coreset selected via randomly pivoted Cholesky decomposition, achieving super-polynomial error decay with near-linear runtime complexity.
Problem It Solves: Existing attention approximations either lack provable error guarantees or require quadratic runtime to achieve high fidelity; WildCat provides both theoretical guarantees (O(n^{-\sqrt{\log(\log(n))}}) error) and practical efficiency (O(n^{1+o(1)}) time).
How It Works:

Coreset Selection: Apply randomly pivoted Cholesky decomposition to the kernel matrix (derived from query-key similarities) to select a small subset of m \ll n representative tokens, where the algorithm greedily selects tokens that maximize remaining spectral information.
Optimal Weighting: Compute importance weights for each selected coreset token to minimize reconstruction error when approximating the full attention output, ensuring the weighted subset preserves critical spectral properties of the original attention matrix.
Sparse Attention Computation: Perform attention only between queries and the weighted coreset of keys/values, reducing computational cost from O(n^2) to O(nm) where m = O(n^{o(1)}) for sublinear coreset size.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Query/Key Matrices] --> B[Randomly Pivoted Cholesky]
    B --> C[Coreset Selection m << n]
    C --> D[Optimal Weight Computation]
    D --> E[Weighted Sparse Attention]
    E --> F[Output ≈ Full Attention]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context language modeling with KV cache compression for reduced memory footprint during inference
High-resolution image generation and classification where spatial tokens create quadratic bottlenecks
Any Transformer-based architecture requiring attention over sequences with n > 10^4 tokens

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires bounded input assumption for theoretical guarantees (e.g., normalized queries/keys)
Coreset selection adds preprocessing overhead proportional to O(m^2 n), which may dominate for very small sequence lengths
Optimal weighting computation requires solving a linear system, adding numerical stability considerations
Effectiveness depends on spectral structure of attention matrices (works best when attention has low effective rank)

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys candidate ranking: Compress cross-attention between user history (long sequence) and candidate items, enabling real-time inference over thousands of historical interactions
LLM serving: Apply to KV cache compression in multi-turn conversations, reducing memory bandwidth and latency for long-context retrieval-augmented generation
Document ranking: Efficient cross-attention between query tokens and large document collections without materialized quadratic similarity matrices

literature/2026-02-12-Asymmetric-Prompt-.title: Asymmetric Prompt Weighting for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
literature/2026-02-12-Asymmetric-Prompt-.content:
Asymmetric Prompt Weighting for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #7 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RL, LLM
Abstract
Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has driven recent advances in LLM post-training, in particular for reasoning. Policy optimization algorithms generate a number of responses for a given prompt and then effectively weight the corresponding gradients depending on the rewards. The most popular algorithms including GRPO, DAPO, and RLOO focus on ambiguous prompts, i.e., prompts with intermediate success probability, while downgrading gradients with very easy and very hard prompts. In this paper, we consider asymmetric prompt weightings that assign higher weights to prompts with low, or even zero, empirical success probability. We find that asymmetric weighting particularly benefits from-scratch RL (as in R1-Zero), where training traverses a wide accuracy range, and less so in post-SFT RL where the model already starts at high accuracy. We also provide theory that characterizes prompt weights which minimize the time needed to raise success probability from an initial level to a target accuracy under a fixed update budget. In low-success regimes, where informative responses are rare and response cost dominates, these optimal weights become asymmetric, upweighting low success probabilities and thereby accelerating effective-time convergence.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques/methods:

Asymmetric prompt weighting

Policy optimization algorithms (GRPO/DAPO/RLOO)
From-scratch RL training
Gradient weighting based on reward success probability

Related Litnotes
