NoteSimilarityReinforcement Learning from Meta-Evaluation: Aligning Language Models Without Ground-Truth Labels0.22Long Chain-of-Thought Compression via Fine-Grained Group Policy Optimization0.21Learning to Configure Agentic AI Systems0.15Algorithmic Prompt-Augmentation for Efficient LLM-Based Heuristic Design for A* Search0.14Generative Reasoning Re-ranker0.14
Asymmetric Prompt Weighting (APW)
Core Idea: Weight policy gradient updates asymmetrically by assigning higher importance to prompts with low or zero empirical success probability, accelerating learning in low-success regimes where informative responses are rare.
Problem It Solves: Existing RL algorithms (GRPO, DAPO, RLOO) focus on ambiguous prompts with intermediate success probability, downweighting very hard prompts. This slows convergence in from-scratch RL (e.g., R1-Zero) where models start with low accuracy and must efficiently learn from rare correct responses.
How It Works:

Sample responses: Generate multiple responses per prompt and compute verifiable rewards (e.g., correctness on math/reasoning tasks)
Estimate success probability: Calculate empirical success rate p̂(prompt) from the sampled responses
Apply asymmetric weighting: Assign higher gradient weights to prompts with low p̂, inverting the typical symmetric weighting that favors p̂ ≈ 0.5
Update policy: Apply weighted policy gradients, prioritizing learning signal from hard prompts where correct responses are most informative

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Prompt] --> B[Sample N responses]
    B --> C[Compute rewards]
    C --> D[Estimate p̂ success]
    D --> E[Asymmetric weight w p̂]
    E --> F[Weighted gradient update]
    F --> G[Updated policy]

Where It’s Useful:

From-scratch RL training (R1-Zero style) where model traverses wide accuracy ranges from near-zero to high performance
Reasoning tasks (math, coding) with verifiable binary rewards where hard problems provide crucial learning signal
Cold-start scenarios where initial model competence is low and symmetric weighting wastes compute on already-solved easy prompts

Limitations / Caveats:

Less beneficial for post-SFT RL where models already start at high accuracy (most prompts already have high p̂)
Requires verifiable rewards (correct/incorrect) - doesn’t apply to subjective preference optimization
Theoretical optimality assumes low-success regime; may need adaptive weighting schedules as training progresses
Increased variance in gradient estimates from rare successes may require larger batch sizes or careful tuning

Internal Use Cases:

Cold-start LLM reasoning fine-tuning: When deploying R1-style RL from weak base models, prioritize learning from hardest problems first
Curriculum learning for RecSys: Analogously weight hard-to-rank queries more heavily when training ranking models
Active learning: Use asymmetric weighting principles to prioritize labeling budget on low-confidence examples where model signal is weakest

literature/2026-02-12-AugVLA-3D-Depth-Dr.title: AugVLA-3D: Depth-Driven Feature Augmentation for Vision-Language-Action Models
literature/2026-02-12-AugVLA-3D-Depth-Dr.content:
AugVLA-3D: Depth-Driven Feature Augmentation for Vision-Language-Action Models
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #6 (score: 0.47, max_sim: 0.78)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have recently achieved remarkable progress in robotic perception and control, yet most existing approaches primarily rely on VLM trained using 2D images, which limits their spatial understanding and action grounding in complex 3D environments. To address this limitation, we propose a novel framework that integrates depth estimation into VLA models to enrich 3D feature representations. Specifically, we employ a depth estimation baseline called VGGT to extract geometry-aware 3D cues from standard RGB inputs, enabling efficient utilization of existing large-scale 2D datasets while implicitly recovering 3D structural information. To further enhance the reliability of these depth-derived features, we introduce a new module called action assistant, which constrains the learned 3D representations with action priors and ensures their consistency with downstream control tasks. By fusing the enhanced 3D features with conventional 2D visual tokens, our approach significantly improves the generalization ability and robustness of VLA models. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method not only strengthens perception in geometrically ambiguous scenarios but also leads to superior action prediction accuracy. This work highlights the potential of depth-driven data augmentation and auxiliary expert supervision for bridging the gap between 2D observations and 3D-aware decision-making in robotic systems.
Key Techniques

Depth estimation integration (VGGT)
Action assistant module
Depth-driven 3D feature fusion

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySTATe-of-Thoughts: Structured Action Templates for Tree-of-Thoughts0.27OAT: Ordered Action Tokenization0.26TwinBrainVLA: Unleashing the Potential of Generalist VLMs for Embodied Tasks via Asymmetric Mixture-of-Transformers0.24	extsc{NaVIDA}: Vision-Language Navigation with Inverse Dynamics Augmentation0.20Nüwa: Mending the Spatial Integrity Torn by VLM Token Pruning0.16
Depth-Driven Feature Augmentation for Vision-Language-Action Models (AugVLA-3D)
Core Idea: Augment 2D vision-language-action models with depth-estimated 3D features using a geometry-aware depth estimator (VGGT) and an action assistant module to improve spatial understanding and robotic control in 3D environments.
Problem It Solves: Existing VLA models rely primarily on 2D visual representations from vision-language models, limiting their spatial understanding and action grounding in complex 3D environments where geometric reasoning is critical for robotic manipulation tasks.
How It Works:

Depth Extraction: Apply VGGT (Vision-Geometry-Grounding Transformer) depth estimation baseline to standard RGB inputs to extract geometry-aware 3D cues, implicitly recovering 3D structural information from existing 2D datasets
Action-Constrained Refinement: Use an “action assistant” module to constrain the learned 3D depth representations with action priors, ensuring consistency between geometric features and downstream control tasks
Multi-Modal Fusion: Fuse the enhanced depth-derived 3D features with conventional 2D visual tokens from the base VLM to create enriched representations for action prediction

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[RGB Input] --> B[Base VLM<br/>2D Features]
    A --> C[VGGT Depth<br/>Estimation]
    C --> D[Action Assistant<br/>Action-Constrained 3D Features]
    B --> E[Feature Fusion]
    D --> E
    E --> F[VLA Model<br/>Action Prediction]

Where It’s Useful:

Robotic manipulation in geometrically ambiguous scenarios (occlusions, texture-less surfaces, similar-looking objects at different depths)
Vision-language-action models that need 3D spatial reasoning without access to explicit 3D sensors
Data-efficient robotics training leveraging existing large-scale 2D image datasets with implicit 3D recovery

Limitations / Caveats:

Depends on quality of depth estimation from VGGT; errors in monocular depth prediction propagate to action prediction
Requires designing effective action priors for the action assistant module, which may be task-specific
Fusion mechanism adds computational overhead compared to pure 2D VLA models

Internal Use Cases:

Multimodal Recommendation: Augment product image embeddings with depth-estimated 3D features to better understand spatial attributes (furniture placement, clothing fit, product dimensions)
Vision-Language Models for Content Understanding: Enhance 2D vision encoders with geometric priors to improve spatial reasoning in image/video understanding tasks (scene layout, object relationships)
Action/Click Prediction: Apply action-constrained auxiliary modules (similar to action assistant) to align visual features with user interaction patterns in UI/UX applications

literature/2026-02-12-C2ROPE-Causal-Cont.title: C^2ROPE: Causal Continuous Rotary Positional Encoding for 3D Large Multimodal-Models Reasoning
literature/2026-02-12-C2ROPE-Causal-Cont.content:
C^2ROPE: Causal Continuous Rotary Positional Encoding for 3D Large Multimodal-Models Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #7 (score: 0.42, max_sim: 0.80)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Recent advances in 3D Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) built on Large Language Models (LLMs) have established the alignment of 3D visual features with LLM representations as the dominant paradigm. However, the inherited Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) introduces limitations for multimodal processing. Specifically, applying 1D temporal positional indices disrupts the continuity of visual features along the column dimension, resulting in spatial locality loss. Moreover, RoPE follows the prior that temporally closer image tokens are more causally related, leading to long-term decay in attention allocation and causing the model to progressively neglect earlier visual tokens as the sequence length increases. To address these issues, we propose C^2RoPE, an improved RoPE that explicitly models local spatial Continuity and spatial Causal relationships for visual processing. C^2RoPE introduces a spatio-temporal continuous positional embedding mechanism for visual tokens. It first integrates 1D temporal positions with Cartesian-based spatial coordinates to construct a triplet hybrid positional index, and then employs a frequency allocation strategy to encode spatio-temporal positional information across the three index components. Additionally, we introduce Chebyshev Causal Masking, which determines causal dependencies by computing the Chebyshev distance of image tokens in 2D space. Evaluation results across various benchmarks, including 3D scene reasoning and 3D visual question answering, demonstrate C^2RoPE’s effectiveness. The code is be available at github.com/ErikZ719/C2RoPE.
Key Techniques

Continuous Positional Embedding
Triplet Hybrid Positional Index
Chebyshev Causal Masking

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityNüwa: Mending the Spatial Integrity Torn by VLM Token Pruning0.26Do MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.24OneVision-Encoder: Codec-Aligned Sparsity as a Foundational Principle for Multimodal Intelligence0.22VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.20ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.20
C²RoPE (Causal Continuous Rotary Positional Encoding)
Core Idea: A spatially-aware extension of RoPE that preserves 2D visual continuity and models causal relationships via Chebyshev distance, preventing locality loss and long-term attention decay in vision-language models.
Problem It Solves: Standard RoPE applies 1D temporal indices to 2D image patches, breaking spatial continuity across columns and causing progressive neglect of earlier visual tokens due to temporal-only causal assumptions; this harms 3D scene reasoning where spatial relationships are critical.
How It Works:

Hybrid Positional Index Construction: Combine 1D temporal position t with 2D Cartesian spatial coordinates (x, y) to form a triplet (t, x, y) for each image token
Frequency Allocation Strategy: Partition RoPE’s frequency bands across the three positional components (temporal + 2D spatial) to encode spatio-temporal information in the rotary embeddings
Chebyshev Causal Masking: Replace temporal distance with Chebyshev distance max(|x₁-x₂|, |y₁-y₂|) in 2D space to determine causal dependencies, preserving spatial locality while maintaining autoregressive properties

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Image Patches] --> B[Triplet Index: t,x,y]
    B --> C[Frequency-Partitioned RoPE]
    C --> D[Spatial Embeddings]
    E[Token Pairs] --> F[Chebyshev Distance]
    F --> G[Causal Mask]
    D --> H[Attention + Mask]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Spatially-Aware Features]

Where It’s Useful:

3D visual question answering and scene reasoning tasks requiring spatial understanding
Multi-image or video understanding where column-wise continuity matters (e.g., video frames as grid)
Any vision-language model suffering from spatial locality loss in long visual sequences

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires modifying the base model’s positional encoding scheme (not a drop-in replacement)
Chebyshev distance assumes 2D grid structure; unclear how to extend to irregular 3D point clouds or non-grid visual representations
Frequency allocation strategy adds hyperparameter choices (how to split frequencies across t, x, y dimensions)

Internal Use Cases:

Multimodal RecSys: When ranking items represented as 2D grids (e.g., product image galleries, fashion layouts), C²RoPE could preserve spatial relationships between items better than 1D positional encoding
Document Understanding: For ranking or extracting information from documents with 2D layout (tables, forms), spatial continuity matters more than pure temporal order
Video Recommendation: Frame-level features arranged in grids could benefit from spatial positional encoding to capture shot composition, not just temporal sequence

literature/2026-02-12-Compute-Only-Once-.title: Compute Only Once: UG-Separation for Efficient Large Recommendation Models
literature/2026-02-12-Compute-Only-Once-.content:
Compute Only Once: UG-Separation for Efficient Large Recommendation Models
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #9 (score: 0.91, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Driven by scaling laws, recommender systems increasingly rely on large-scale models to capture complex feature interactions and user behaviors, but this trend also leads to prohibitive training and inference costs. While long-sequence models(e.g., LONGER) can reuse user-side computation through KV caching, such reuse is difficult in dense feature interaction architectures(e.g., RankMixer), where user and group (candidate item) features are deeply entangled across layers. In this work, we propose User-Group Separation (UG-Sep), a novel framework that enables reusable user-side computation in dense interaction models for the first time. UG-Sep introduces a masking mechanism that explicitly disentangles user-side and item-side information flows within token-mixing layers, ensuring that a subset of tokens to preserve purely user-side representations across layers. This design enables corresponding token computations to be reused across multiple samples, significantly reducing redundant inference cost. To compensate for potential expressiveness loss induced by masking, we further propose an Information Compensation strategy that adaptively reconstructs suppressed user-item interactions. Moreover, as UG-Sep substantially reduces user-side FLOPs and exposes memory-bound components, we incorporate W8A16 (8-bit weight, 16-bit activation) weight-only quantization to alleviate memory bandwidth bottlenecks and achieve additional acceleration. We conduct extensive offline evaluations and large-scale online A/B experiments at ByteDance, demonstrating that UG-Sep reduces inference latency by up to 20 percent without degrading online user experience or commercial metrics across multiple business scenarios, including feed recommendation and advertising systems.
Key Techniques

User-Group Separation (UG-Sep)
Masking mechanism for token disentanglement
Information Compensation strategy
W8A16 weight-only quantization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMixFormer: Co-Scaling Up Dense and Sequence in Industrial Recommenders0.27Differentially Private and Communication Efficient Large Language Model Split Inference via Stochastic Quantization and Soft Prompt0.17Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.15Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.14Domain-Adaptive and Scalable Dense Retrieval for Content-Based Recommendation0.14
User-Group Separation (UG-Sep)
Core Idea: A masking framework that disentangles user-side and item-side computation in dense feature interaction models, enabling user representations to be computed once and reused across multiple candidate items during inference.
Problem It Solves: Dense feature interaction architectures (e.g., RankMixer) entangle user and item features across all layers, preventing the computation reuse benefits available in sequence models with KV caching; this leads to redundant user-side computation for every user-item pair during inference.
How It Works:

Masking Mechanism: Apply explicit masks within token-mixing layers to separate information flows—certain tokens preserve purely user-side representations while others handle item-side or mixed interactions.
User Computation Reuse: Compute the user-side token subset once per user and cache it; reuse these cached representations across all candidate items in the same inference batch.
Information Compensation: Adaptively reconstruct suppressed user-item cross-interactions through a learned compensation module to recover expressiveness lost from masking.
W8A16 Quantization: Apply 8-bit weight quantization to memory-bound components exposed after reducing user-side FLOPs, further accelerating inference.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[User + Item Features] --> B[Masked Token Mixing]
    B --> C[User Tokens - Cached]
    B --> D[Item/Mixed Tokens]
    C --> E[Compensation Module]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Final Ranking Score]
    C -.Reused across items.-> E

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale recommendation systems with dense feature interactions (feed ranking, ads ranking)
Real-time inference scenarios where the same user is scored against many candidate items
Production environments prioritizing latency reduction without quality degradation

Limitations / Caveats:

Masking may suppress some user-item interaction expressiveness, requiring compensation strategies
Effectiveness depends on the proportion of user-side vs. item-side computation in the architecture
W8A16 quantization may introduce minor accuracy trade-offs depending on model sensitivity

Internal Use Cases:

Apply to ByteDance-style RankMixer or token-based ranking models to reduce serving latency by ~20% without metric degradation
Enable longer user histories or richer features within the same inference budget by eliminating redundant user computation
Combine with KV caching strategies in hybrid sequential + dense architectures for maximal reuse

literature/2026-02-12-End-to-End-Semanti.title: End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation
literature/2026-02-12-End-to-End-Semanti.content:
End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #2 (score: 0.00, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Generative Recommendation (GR) has excelled by framing recommendation as next-token prediction. This paradigm relies on Semantic IDs (SIDs) to tokenize large-scale items into discrete sequences. Existing GR approaches predominantly generate SIDs via Residual Quantization (RQ), where items are encoded into embeddings and then quantized to discrete SIDs. However, this paradigm suffers from inherent limitations: 1) Objective misalignment and semantic degradation stemming from the two-stage compression; 2) Error accumulation inherent in the structure of RQ. To address these limitations, we propose UniSID, a Unified SID generation framework for generative advertisement recommendation. Specifically, we jointly optimize embeddings and SIDs in an end-to-end manner from raw advertising data, enabling semantic information to flow directly into the SID space and thus addressing the inherent limitations of the two-stage cascading compression paradigm. To capture fine-grained semantics, a multi-granularity contrastive learning strategy is introduced to align distinct items across SID levels. Finally, a summary-based ad reconstruction mechanism is proposed to encourage SIDs to capture high-level semantic information that is not explicitly present in advertising contexts. Experiments demonstrate that UniSID consistently outperforms state-of-the-art SID generation methods, yielding up to a 4.62% improvement in Hit Rate metrics across downstream advertising scenarios compared to the strongest baseline.
Key Techniques

End-to-end joint optimization of embeddings and SIDs
Multi-granularity contrastive learning
Summary-based ad reconstruction

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityEnd-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.90DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.39R2LED: Equipping Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs for CTR Prediction0.35GLASS: A Generative Recommender for Long-sequence Modeling via SID-Tier and Semantic Search0.32Rethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs0.32
UniSID (Unified Semantic ID Generation)
Core Idea: End-to-end joint optimization of item embeddings and discrete Semantic IDs directly from raw advertising data, eliminating the two-stage compression paradigm of traditional RQ-VAE approaches to prevent semantic degradation and error accumulation.
Problem It Solves: Addresses two fundamental limitations of existing Residual Quantization (RQ)-based Semantic ID generation: (1) objective misalignment and semantic loss from cascading item embedding learning and quantization as separate stages, and (2) error propagation across RQ layers where early-layer quantization mistakes compound in later layers.
How It Works:

Joint End-to-End Learning: Simultaneously optimize item embeddings and codebook vectors using a unified loss function that combines recommendation objectives with ID generation, allowing semantic signals to flow directly into the discrete SID space
Multi-Granularity Contrastive Learning: Apply contrastive losses across different SID hierarchy levels (coarse to fine-grained) to ensure semantically similar items share similar SID prefixes while distinct items remain separated at appropriate granularity levels
Summary-Based Ad Reconstruction: Train SIDs to reconstruct high-level semantic summaries of advertising content (rather than raw features), encouraging the discrete codes to capture abstract semantic concepts not explicitly present in the original ad attributes

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Ad Data] --> B[Joint Encoder]
    B --> C[Embedding Space]
    C --> D[Hierarchical Codebooks]
    D --> E[Multi-Level SIDs]
    C -.-> F[Contrastive Learning<br/>across SID levels]
    E --> G[Summary Reconstruction]
    F --> D
    G -.-> D

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale advertisement recommendation systems requiring semantic item representation
Generative recommendation frameworks using autoregressive next-token prediction
Cold-start and long-tail item scenarios in e-commerce and content platforms
Multi-task recommendation systems requiring transferable item representations

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires end-to-end training infrastructure which may be more computationally expensive than two-stage approaches during initial training
Summary generation mechanism depends on availability or quality of high-level semantic descriptions for items
Performance gains may diminish for extremely sparse interaction data where semantic signals are weak

Internal Use Cases:

Improving semantic coherence of item tokenization in LLM-based recommender systems by avoiding quantization-induced information loss
Enhancing cold-start item representation in ranking models through better semantic ID compression that preserves multi-level semantic relationships
Building transferable item representations across different recommendation contexts (e.g., ads, videos, products) by learning semantics end-to-end rather than quantizing pre-trained embeddings

literature/2026-02-12-Enhancing-Predicta.title: Enhancing Predictability of Multi-Tenant DNN Inference for Autonomous Vehicles' Perception
literature/2026-02-12-Enhancing-Predicta.content:
Enhancing Predictability of Multi-Tenant DNN Inference for Autonomous Vehicles’ Perception
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #10 (score: 0.53, max_sim: 0.80)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Autonomous vehicles (AVs) rely on sensors and deep neural networks (DNNs) to perceive their surrounding environment and make maneuver decisions in real time. However, achieving real-time DNN inference in the AV’s perception pipeline is challenging due to the large gap between the computation requirement and the AV’s limited resources. Most, if not all, of existing studies focus on optimizing the DNN inference time to achieve faster perception by compressing the DNN model with pruning and quantization. In contrast, we present a Predictable Perception system with DNNs (PP-DNN) that reduce the amount of image data to be processed while maintaining the same level of accuracy for multi-tenant DNNs by dynamically selecting critical frames and regions of interest (ROIs). PP-DNN is based on our key insight that critical frames and ROIs for AVs vary with the AV’s surrounding environment. However, it is challenging to identify and use critical frames and ROIs in multi-tenant DNNs for predictable inference. Given image-frame streams, PP-DNN leverages an ROI generator to identify critical frames and ROIs based on the similarities of consecutive frames and traffic scenarios. PP-DNN then leverages a FLOPs predictor to predict multiply-accumulate operations (MACs) from the dynamic critical frames and ROIs. The ROI scheduler coordinates the processing of critical frames and ROIs with multiple DNN models. Finally, we design a detection predictor for the perception of non-critical frames. We have implemented PP-DNN in an ROS-based AV pipeline and evaluated it with the BDD100K and the nuScenes dataset. PP-DNN is observed to significantly enhance perception predictability, increasing the number of fusion frames by up to 7.3x, reducing the fusion delay by >2.6x and fusion-delay variations by >2.3x, improving detection completeness by 75.4% and the cost-effectiveness by up to 98% over the baseline.
Key Techniques

Dynamic frame selection
Region of Interest (ROI) identification
FLOPs prediction
Detection prediction for non-critical frames

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySPARC: Separating Perception And Reasoning Circuits for Test-time Scaling of VLMs0.16SToRM: Supervised Token Reduction for Multi-modal LLMs toward efficient end-to-end autonomous driving0.12Efficient Autoregressive Video Diffusion with Dummy Head0.11FlashVID: Efficient Video Large Language Models via Training-free Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging0.10Native LLM and MLLM Inference at Scale on Apple Silicon0.10
PP-DNN (Predictable Perception with DNNs)
Core Idea: Dynamically select critical frames and regions of interest (ROIs) in multi-tenant DNN inference for autonomous vehicles to reduce computation while maintaining accuracy and achieving predictable real-time performance.
Problem It Solves: Closes the gap between high computation requirements of multi-tenant DNN inference in AVs and limited onboard resources, while addressing unpredictable inference times that hinder real-time perception and sensor fusion.
How It Works:

ROI Generator analyzes consecutive frame similarities and traffic scenarios to identify critical frames and spatial regions requiring full DNN processing
FLOPs Predictor estimates multiply-accumulate operations (MACs) from the dynamic critical frames/ROIs to enable predictable scheduling
ROI Scheduler coordinates multi-tenant DNN model execution on prioritized critical regions
Detection Predictor performs lightweight inference on non-critical frames using temporal predictions from previous frames
System outputs detection results with bounded latency for downstream sensor fusion

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Image Stream] --> B[ROI Generator]
    B --> C{Critical Frame?}
    C -->|Yes| D[FLOPs Predictor]
    C -->|No| E[Detection Predictor]
    D --> F[ROI Scheduler]
    F --> G[Multi-Tenant DNNs]
    G --> H[Detection Output]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Sensor Fusion]

Where It’s Useful:

Real-time autonomous vehicle perception pipelines requiring multi-model inference (object detection, segmentation, depth estimation)
Edge computing scenarios with hard deadline constraints and limited GPU resources
Multi-tenant DNN serving where predictable latency matters more than average-case throughput

Limitations / Caveats:

Relies on temporal coherence between frames; performance degrades with abrupt scene changes or occlusions
ROI selection accuracy depends on traffic scenario classification; novel scenarios may reduce effectiveness
Adds overhead from ROI generator, FLOPs predictor, and scheduler components
Requires offline profiling of DNN MACs for accurate prediction

Internal Use Cases:

Multi-model ranking systems where different models process different query types - selective invocation based on query criticality
Real-time recommendation pipelines with SLA constraints - predict compute cost and schedule models to meet deadlines
LLM serving with heterogeneous requests - route critical queries to full models, use prediction/caching for non-critical ones

literature/2026-02-12-Exploring-the-impa.title: Exploring the impact of adaptive rewiring in Graph Neural Networks
literature/2026-02-12-Exploring-the-impa.content:
Exploring the impact of adaptive rewiring in Graph Neural Networks
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #4 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, RAG, Graph
Abstract
This paper explores sparsification methods as a form of regularization in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to address high memory usage and computational costs in large-scale graph applications. Using techniques from Network Science and Machine Learning, including Erdős-Rényi for model sparsification, we enhance the efficiency of GNNs for real-world applications. We demonstrate our approach on N-1 contingency assessment in electrical grids, a critical task for ensuring grid reliability. We apply our methods to three datasets of varying sizes, exploring Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN) and Graph Isomorphism Networks (GIN) with different degrees of sparsification and rewiring. Comparison across sparsification levels shows the potential of combining insights from both research fields to improve GNN performance and scalability. Our experiments highlight the importance of tuning sparsity parameters: while sparsity can improve generalization, excessive sparsity may hinder learning of complex patterns. Our adaptive rewiring approach, particularly when combined with early stopping, proves promising by allowing the model to adapt its connectivity structure during training. This research contributes to understanding how sparsity can be effectively leveraged in GNNs for critical applications like power grid reliability analysis.
Key Techniques

Sparsification
Erdős-Rényi models
Adaptive rewiring

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityUnified Multi-Domain Graph Pre-training for Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Graphs via Domain-Specific Expert Encoding0.25LIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs0.24Semi-Supervised Learning on Graphs using Graph Neural Networks0.22One Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral Profiles0.20Towards Personalized Bangla Book Recommendation: A Large-Scale Multi-Entity Book Graph Dataset0.20
Adaptive Rewiring with Erdős-Rényi Sparsification
Core Idea: Apply probabilistic edge pruning based on Erdős-Rényi random graph theory during GNN training, allowing the model to dynamically adjust its connectivity structure to balance memory efficiency with learning capacity for critical pattern recognition tasks.
Problem It Solves: GNNs on large-scale graphs (e.g., electrical grids) face prohibitive memory and computational costs; naive sparsification can destroy critical structural patterns needed for tasks like N-1 contingency assessment, while dense graphs are infeasible at scale.
How It Works:

Initial Sparsification: Apply Erdős-Rényi probabilistic edge sampling to the input graph, creating a sparse adjacency matrix with controlled edge probability p (sparsity parameter)
Adaptive Training: During GNN training (GCN or GIN), allow the model to rewire connections—adding or removing edges based on gradient signals and learned importance
Early Stopping Integration: Combine adaptive rewiring with early stopping to prevent over-sparsification; halt training when validation performance plateaus to preserve learned connectivity patterns
Sparsity-Accuracy Trade-off Tuning: Systematically explore sparsity levels to find the optimal balance where regularization improves generalization without preventing learning of complex structural patterns

Where It’s Useful:

Critical infrastructure reliability analysis (power grid N-1 contingency assessment)
Large-scale graph applications where memory constraints limit model depth/width
Domains requiring interpretable graph structure after training (rewired edges reveal learned importance)

Limitations / Caveats:

Excessive sparsity can hinder learning of complex patterns—requires careful tuning of edge probability p
Erdős-Rényi random sampling may discard structurally critical edges (e.g., bridges in power grids); domain-aware sampling could improve results
Adaptive rewiring adds hyperparameters (rewiring rate, rewiring strategy) that require validation-based tuning
Performance gains are task-dependent; works best when input graphs have redundant edges

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys graph pruning: Apply to user-item or social graphs to reduce memory in GNN-based collaborative filtering while maintaining recommendation quality through adaptive rewiring
Knowledge graph completion: Sparsify dense KG embeddings during pre-training, allowing model to rewire and focus on high-signal relations for downstream retrieval/ranking
LLM retrieval graphs: Build sparse document similarity graphs for retrieval-augmented generation, using adaptive rewiring to discover non-obvious semantic connections during fine-tuning

Discussions & References
