literature/2026-02-12-Flow-caching-for-a.title: Flow caching for autoregressive video generation
literature/2026-02-12-Flow-caching-for-a.content:
Flow caching for autoregressive video generation
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #5 (score: 0.47, max_sim: 0.78)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Autoregressive models, often built on Transformer architectures, represent a powerful paradigm for generating ultra-long videos by synthesizing content in sequential chunks. However, this sequential generation process is notoriously slow. While caching strategies have proven effective for accelerating traditional video diffusion models, existing methods assume uniform denoising across all frames-an assumption that breaks down in autoregressive models where different video chunks exhibit varying similarity patterns at identical timesteps. In this paper, we present FlowCache, the first caching framework specifically designed for autoregressive video generation. Our key insight is that each video chunk should maintain independent caching policies, allowing fine-grained control over which chunks require recomputation at each timestep. We introduce a chunkwise caching strategy that dynamically adapts to the unique denoising characteristics of each chunk, complemented by a joint importance-redundancy optimized KV cache compression mechanism that maintains fixed memory bounds while preserving generation quality. Our method achieves remarkable speedups of 2.38 times on MAGI-1 and 6.7 times on SkyReels-V2, with negligible quality degradation (VBench: 0.87 increase and 0.79 decrease respectively). These results demonstrate that FlowCache successfully unlocks the potential of autoregressive models for real-time, ultra-long video generation-establishing a new benchmark for efficient video synthesis at scale. The code is available at github.com/mikeallen39/FlowCache.
Key Techniques

Chunkwise caching strategy
Joint importance-redundancy optimized KV cache compression
Adaptive denoising policies

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityQuant VideoGen: Auto-Regressive Long Video Generation via 2-Bit KV-Cache Quantization0.33Light Forcing: Accelerating Autoregressive Video Diffusion via Sparse Attention0.32Efficient Autoregressive Video Diffusion with Dummy Head0.32FlashVID: Efficient Video Large Language Models via Training-free Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging0.27FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion0.23
FlowCache
Core Idea: A caching framework for autoregressive video generation that maintains independent caching policies per video chunk, allowing fine-grained control over which chunks require recomputation at each denoising timestep, rather than assuming uniform denoising across all frames.
Problem It Solves: Existing video diffusion caching methods assume uniform denoising across frames, but autoregressive video models generate content in sequential chunks with varying similarity patterns at identical timesteps, making traditional caching strategies ineffective and leaving autoregressive generation prohibitively slow.
How It Works:

Chunkwise Caching Strategy: Each video chunk maintains its own independent caching policy that dynamically adapts to that chunk’s unique denoising characteristics across timesteps
Similarity-Based Cache Decisions: At each timestep, determine per-chunk whether to reuse cached features or recompute based on the chunk’s specific denoising trajectory
KV Cache Compression: Apply joint importance-redundancy optimization to compress key-value caches while maintaining fixed memory bounds and preserving generation quality

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Video Chunks] --> B[Chunk 1 Cache Policy]
    A --> C[Chunk 2 Cache Policy]
    A --> D[Chunk N Cache Policy]
    B --> E[KV Cache Compression]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Autoregressive Generation]
    F --> G[Ultra-Long Video Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Autoregressive video generation models (e.g., MAGI-1, SkyReels-V2) requiring real-time synthesis
Ultra-long video generation where sequential chunking is necessary
Resource-constrained video generation scenarios needing memory-efficient caching

Limitations / Caveats:

Specifically designed for autoregressive architectures; may not transfer to non-autoregressive video models
Requires tuning of chunk-specific caching thresholds and importance-redundancy tradeoffs
Achieves speedups with “negligible” but non-zero quality degradation (VBench score shifts of ~0.8)

Internal Use Cases:

Could inspire chunk-wise caching for sequential recommendation models processing long user interaction sequences (similar to StreamingLLM’s attention sink concept)
Joint importance-redundancy optimization principle applicable to compressing KV caches in serving large language models for ranking/retrieval
Dynamic per-segment caching policies relevant for YouTube Shorts user sequence modeling where engagement patterns vary across temporal windows

literature/2026-02-12-GraphSeek-Next-Gen.title: GraphSeek: Next-Generation Graph Analytics with LLMs
literature/2026-02-12-GraphSeek-Next-Gen.content:
GraphSeek: Next-Generation Graph Analytics with LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #8 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, RAG, LLM
Abstract
Graphs are foundational across domains but remain hard to use without deep expertise. LLMs promise accessible natural language (NL) graph analytics, yet they fail to process industry-scale property graphs effectively and efficiently: such datasets are large, highly heterogeneous, structurally complex, and evolve dynamically. To address this, we devise a novel abstraction for complex multi-query analytics over such graphs. Its key idea is to replace brittle generation of graph queries directly from NL with planning over a Semantic Catalog that describes both the graph schema and the graph operations. Concretely, this induces a clean separation between a Semantic Plane for LLM planning and broader reasoning, and an Execution Plane for deterministic, database-grade query execution over the full dataset and tool implementations. This design yields substantial gains in both token efficiency and task effectiveness even with small-context LLMs. We use this abstraction as the basis of the first LLM-enhanced graph analytics framework called GraphSeek. GraphSeek achieves substantially higher success rates (e.g., 86% over enhanced LangChain) and points toward the next generation of affordable and accessible graph analytics that unify LLM reasoning with database-grade execution over large and complex property graphs.
Key Techniques

Semantic Catalog abstraction
Semantic Plane for LLM planning
Execution Plane for query execution

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityUnified Multi-Domain Graph Pre-training for Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Graphs via Domain-Specific Expert Encoding0.28NeuroSymActive: Differentiable Neural-Symbolic Reasoning with Active Exploration for Knowledge Graph Question Answering0.20Exploring the impact of adaptive rewiring in Graph Neural Networks0.20One Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral Profiles0.20LIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs0.20
GraphSeek
Core Idea: Separates LLM planning from query execution using a Semantic Catalog abstraction—LLMs reason over schema and operation descriptions to generate execution plans, while a separate deterministic layer executes queries over the full property graph.
Problem It Solves: LLMs struggle with large-scale, heterogeneous property graphs due to token inefficiency, inability to process full datasets, and unreliable direct query generation from natural language; existing approaches fail on complex multi-query analytics workflows.
How It Works:

Semantic Catalog Creation: Build a catalog describing graph schema (node types, edge types, properties) and available operations (traversals, aggregations, filters) with natural language descriptions
Semantic Plane Planning: LLM consumes the compact catalog (not the full graph) to decompose user queries into execution plans over catalog operations
Execution Plane: Deterministic database-grade engine executes planned operations over the complete property graph and returns results
Iterative Refinement: LLM reasons over intermediate results to generate follow-up operations for multi-step analytics

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Natural Language Query] --> B[Semantic Plane: LLM Planner]
    C[Semantic Catalog] --> B
    B --> D[Execution Plan]
    D --> E[Execution Plane: Graph DB]
    F[Property Graph] --> E
    E --> G[Results]
    G --> B
    B --> H[Final Answer]

Where It’s Useful:

Enterprise knowledge graphs with heterogeneous schemas and millions of nodes
Multi-hop analytics workflows requiring sequential query refinement
Domains where users lack graph query language expertise (Cypher, SPARQL, Gremlin)
Dynamic graphs with evolving schemas

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires upfront construction and maintenance of the Semantic Catalog
Catalog quality directly impacts planning success—incomplete or ambiguous operation descriptions degrade performance
Still requires LLM for planning phase, though token usage is drastically reduced vs. graph-in-prompt approaches

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys: Enable product managers to query user-item interaction graphs via NL (“Find clusters of users who abandoned carts after viewing competitor pricing”)
Feature Engineering: Let data scientists explore multi-hop entity relationships without writing complex SQL joins or graph queries
Content Graph Analytics: Query content similarity graphs, topic hierarchies, and user engagement patterns using natural language for content strategy insights
Model Explainability: Navigate prediction dependency graphs to trace feature importance chains and model behavior patterns

literature/2026-02-12-Kalman-Linear-Atte.title: Kalman Linear Attention: Parallel Bayesian Filtering For Efficient Language Modelling and State Tracking
literature/2026-02-12-Kalman-Linear-Atte.content:
Kalman Linear Attention: Parallel Bayesian Filtering For Efficient Language Modelling and State Tracking
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #5 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Transformer, LLM
Abstract
State-space language models such as Mamba and gated linear attention (GLA) offer efficient alternatives to transformers due to their linear complexity and parallel training, but often lack the expressivity and robust state-tracking needed for complex reasoning. We address these limitations by reframing sequence modelling through a probabilistic lens, using Bayesian filters as a core primitive. While classical filters such as Kalman filters provide principled state estimation and uncertainty tracking, they are typically viewed as inherently sequential. We show that reparameterising the Kalman filter in information form enables its updates to be computed via an associative scan, allowing efficient parallel training. Building on this insight, we introduce the Kalman Linear Attention (KLA) layer, a neural sequence-modelling primitive that performs time-parallel probabilistic inference while maintaining explicit belief-state uncertainty. KLA offers strictly more expressive nonlinear updates and gating than GLA variants while retaining their computational advantages. On language modelling tasks, KLA matches or outperforms modern SSMs and GLAs across representative discrete token-manipulation and state-tracking benchmarks.
Key Techniques

Bayesian filters with information form reparameterization
Kalman Linear Attention (KLA) layer
associative scan for parallel training

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarity2Mamba2Furious: Linear in Complexity, Competitive in Accuracy0.29SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.21SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.19Towards Reducible Uncertainty Modeling for Reliable Large Language Model Agents0.18Constrained Process Maps for Multi-Agent Generative AI Workflows0.14
Kalman Linear Attention (KLA)
Core Idea: A neural sequence-modelling layer that reformulates the Kalman filter in information form to enable parallel training via associative scan, providing principled probabilistic state tracking with explicit uncertainty while maintaining linear complexity.
Problem It Solves: State-space models like Mamba and gated linear attention (GLA) achieve efficient linear complexity but lack the expressivity and robust uncertainty-aware state tracking needed for complex reasoning tasks, while traditional Kalman filters are inherently sequential and cannot be parallelized for training.
How It Works:

Information-form reparameterization: Convert the classical Kalman filter from covariance form to information form (precision matrix and information vector representation)
Associative scan decomposition: Express the information-form update equations as an associative operation, enabling parallel computation across the sequence via scan algorithms during training
Neural parameterization: Learn the measurement model, transition dynamics, and noise parameters as functions of the input tokens through neural networks
Belief-state propagation: Maintain explicit uncertainty estimates (covariance/precision) alongside hidden states, enabling more expressive nonlinear gating and updates than GLA variants
Parallel training, sequential inference: Leverage associative scan for efficient parallel training while supporting O(1) per-step recurrent inference at deployment

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input Tokens] --> B[Neural Parameterization]
    B --> C[Information Form Updates]
    C --> D[Associative Scan]
    D --> E[Belief States + Uncertainty]
    E --> F[Gated Outputs]

Where It’s Useful:

Language modelling tasks requiring state tracking (variable binding, counting, copying)
Long-context sequence processing where uncertainty estimation improves robustness
Domains where principled probabilistic inference is valuable (e.g., reasoning over partially observed states)
Streaming inference scenarios requiring O(1) per-step recurrence after parallel training

Limitations / Caveats:

Still inherits linear attention’s expressivity limitations compared to full softmax attention for in-context learning and arbitrary token interactions
Information-form precision matrices may become ill-conditioned without careful numerical stabilization
Requires more sophisticated implementation (associative scan kernels) than standard attention
Unclear how the explicit uncertainty tracking scales to very large hidden dimensions

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys temporal modelling: Track user preference dynamics with uncertainty for exploration-exploitation in recommendations
LLM state tracking: Improve long-context reasoning and variable binding in code generation or multi-step reasoning tasks
Ranking with uncertainty: Incorporate explicit confidence estimates into neural ranking models for calibrated scoring
Streaming feature extraction: Efficient online feature computation for real-time ranking with bounded memory

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-12-LHAW-Controllable-.title: LHAW: Controllable Underspecification for Long-Horizon Tasks
literature/2026-02-12-LHAW-Controllable-.content:
LHAW: Controllable Underspecification for Long-Horizon Tasks
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, LLM
Abstract
Long-horizon workflow agents that operate effectively over extended periods are essential for truly autonomous systems. Their reliable execution critically depends on the ability to reason through ambiguous situations in which clarification seeking is necessary to ensure correct task execution. However, progress is limited by the lack of scalable, task-agnostic frameworks for systematically curating and measuring the impact of ambiguity across custom workflows. We address this gap by introducing LHAW (Long-Horizon Augmented Workflows), a modular, dataset-agnostic synthetic pipeline that transforms any well-specified task into controllable underspecified variants by systematically removing information across four dimensions - Goals, Constraints, Inputs, and Context - at configurable severity levels. Unlike approaches that rely on LLM predictions of ambiguity, LHAW validates variants through empirical agent trials, classifying them as outcome-critical, divergent, or benign based on observed terminal state divergence. We release 285 task variants from TheAgentCompany, SWE-Bench Pro and MCP-Atlas according to our taxonomy alongside formal analysis measuring how current agents detect, reason about, and resolve underspecification across ambiguous settings. LHAW provides the first systematic framework for cost-sensitive evaluation of agent clarification behavior in long-horizon settings, enabling development of reliable autonomous systems.
Key Techniques

synthetic information removal
empirical agent trial validation
terminal state divergence classification
systematic ambiguity taxonomy

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHybrid-Gym: Training Coding Agents to Generalize Across Tasks0.19ResearchGym: Evaluating Language Model Agents on Real-World AI Research0.17SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.16Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.16SPILLage: Agentic Oversharing on the Web0.14
LHAW (Long-Horizon Augmented Workflows)
Core Idea: A synthetic pipeline that systematically transforms well-specified tasks into controllable underspecified variants by removing information across four dimensions (Goals, Constraints, Inputs, Context) at configurable severity levels, then validates ambiguity impact through empirical agent trials.
Problem It Solves: Existing frameworks lack scalable, task-agnostic methods to curate and measure how ambiguity affects long-horizon agent performance—current approaches rely on unreliable LLM predictions of ambiguity rather than empirical validation of whether underspecification actually causes divergent outcomes.
How It Works:

Synthetic Variant Generation: Takes any well-specified task and systematically removes information along four dimensions (Goals, Constraints, Inputs, Context) at multiple severity levels to create underspecified variants
Empirical Validation: Runs agent trials comparing terminal states between original and underspecified variants to observe actual execution divergence
Taxonomy Classification: Categorizes variants as outcome-critical (divergent terminal states requiring clarification), divergent (different paths but acceptable outcomes), or benign (no meaningful impact) based on observed behavior rather than predicted ambiguity

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Well-Specified Task] --> B[Dimension-Based Removal]
    B --> C[Underspecified Variants]
    C --> D[Agent Trial Execution]
    D --> E[Terminal State Comparison]
    E --> F[Taxonomy: Critical/Divergent/Benign]

Where It’s Useful:

Evaluating clarification-seeking behavior in autonomous agents operating over extended workflows
Benchmarking long-horizon reasoning systems on ambiguity detection and resolution
Cost-sensitive evaluation of when agents should pause for human input vs. proceed autonomously
Dataset augmentation for training agents on underspecified task handling

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires initial well-specified task corpus as starting point for synthetic generation
Validation through agent trials is computationally expensive for large-scale benchmarking
Taxonomy depends on observable terminal state divergence, may miss internal reasoning failures that don’t manifest in outcomes
Released dataset covers 285 variants from three sources (TheAgentCompany, SWE-Bench Pro, MCP-Atlas)—generalization to broader task distributions not yet validated

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys evaluation: Generate underspecified user preference queries (missing constraints like “budget under $X” or context like “for outdoor use”) to benchmark how recommendation agents handle ambiguity vs. over-assuming user intent
LLM agent orchestration: Measure when retrieval/ranking pipelines should clarify underspecified search intents (“best hotels in Paris”—missing dates, budget, purpose) vs. return diverse results
Interactive ranking systems: Train models to detect outcome-critical vs. benign missing information (e.g., missing “sort by price” is benign if default ranking is acceptable, but missing “vegetarian” is critical for restaurant recommendations)

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-12-Learning-to-Evict-.title: Learning to Evict from Key-Value Cache
literature/2026-02-12-Learning-to-Evict-.content:
Learning to Evict from Key-Value Cache
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-10
Rank: #3 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The growing size of Large Language Models (LLMs) makes efficient inference challenging, primarily due to the memory demands of the autoregressive Key-Value (KV) cache. Existing eviction or compression methods reduce cost but rely on heuristics, such as recency or past attention scores, which serve only as indirect proxies for a token’s future utility and introduce computational overhead. We reframe KV cache eviction as a reinforcement learning (RL) problem: learning to rank tokens by their predicted usefulness for future decoding. To this end, we introduce KV Policy (KVP), a framework of lightweight per-head RL agents trained on pre-computed generation traces using only key and value vectors. Each agent learns a specialized eviction policy guided by future utility, which evaluates the quality of the ranking across all cache budgets, requiring no modifications to the underlying LLM or additional inference. Evaluated across two different model families on the long-context benchmark RULER and the multi-turn dialogue benchmark OASST2-4k, KVP significantly outperforms baselines. Furthermore, zero-shot tests on standard downstream tasks (e.g., LongBench, BOOLQ, ARC) indicate that KVP generalizes well beyond its training distribution and to longer context lengths. These results demonstrate that learning to predict future token utility is a powerful and scalable paradigm for adaptive KV cache management.
Key Techniques

KV cache eviction as reinforcement learning
per-head RL agents with future utility prediction
adaptive ranking of tokens by usefulness
zero-shot generalization across tasks

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPredicting Future Utility: Global Combinatorial Optimization for Task-Agnostic KV Cache Eviction0.44ForesightKV: Optimizing KV Cache Eviction for Reasoning Models by Learning Long-Term Contribution0.41Hierarchical Adaptive Eviction for KV Cache Management in Multimodal Language Models0.41DeltaKV: Residual-Based KV Cache Compression via Long-Range Similarity0.28Fast KVzip: Efficient and Accurate LLM Inference with Gated KV Eviction0.26
KV Policy (KVP)
Core Idea: Train lightweight per-head RL agents to learn eviction policies that rank KV cache tokens by predicted future utility, using only key and value vectors from pre-computed generation traces.
Problem It Solves: Existing KV cache eviction methods rely on heuristic proxies (recency, past attention scores) that don’t directly predict which tokens will be useful for future decoding, leading to suboptimal memory-accuracy tradeoffs.
How It Works:

Trace Collection: Pre-compute generation traces from the base LLM, recording key/value vectors and ground-truth future attention patterns
Per-Head Agent Training: Train separate lightweight RL agents (one per attention head) that take key and value vectors as input and output eviction rankings
Utility-Guided Reward: Agents receive rewards based on how well their rankings preserve future decoding quality across all cache budgets, measured against actual future attention usage
Inference-Time Eviction: During generation, each agent ranks tokens in its head’s cache; low-ranked tokens are evicted to maintain budget constraints

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[K/V vectors] --> B[Per-Head RL Agent 1]
    A --> C[Per-Head RL Agent 2]
    A --> D[Per-Head RL Agent N]
    B --> E[Token Rankings]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Evict Low-Ranked Tokens]
    F --> G[Compressed KV Cache]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context LLM inference where KV cache memory is the primary bottleneck
Multi-turn dialogue systems requiring efficient memory management across conversation history
Resource-constrained deployment scenarios (edge devices, batch inference) needing adaptive cache compression
Applications requiring better accuracy-memory tradeoffs than heuristic eviction methods

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires pre-computing generation traces for training, adding upfront computational cost
Training is task/domain-specific; generalization to out-of-distribution contexts depends on training data diversity
Adds per-head agent overhead during inference (though agents are lightweight)
Performance gains depend on quality of future utility prediction; may degrade on highly unpredictable generation patterns

Internal Use Cases:

LLM Serving: Deploy alongside production models to reduce memory footprint in multi-tenant serving environments while maintaining response quality
Ranking Systems: Adapt the “learned utility prediction” paradigm to feature selection in deep ranking models—train lightweight agents to identify which cached embeddings/features matter most for future ranking decisions
RecSys Context Management: Apply similar RL-based eviction to manage user interaction history caches, learning which past behaviors are most predictive for next-item recommendations

literature/2026-02-12-MoToRec-Sparse-Reg.title: MoToRec: Sparse-Regularized Multimodal Tokenization for Cold-Start Recommendation
literature/2026-02-12-MoToRec-Sparse-Reg.content:
MoToRec: Sparse-Regularized Multimodal Tokenization for Cold-Start Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #4 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Graph neural networks (GNNs) have revolutionized recommender systems by effectively modeling complex user-item interactions, yet data sparsity and the item cold-start problem significantly impair performance, particularly for new items with limited or no interaction history. While multimodal content offers a promising solution, existing methods result in suboptimal representations for new items due to noise and entanglement in sparse data. To address this, we transform multimodal recommendation into discrete semantic tokenization. We present Sparse-Regularized Multimodal Tokenization for Cold-Start Recommendation (MoToRec), a framework centered on a sparsely-regularized Residual Quantized Variational Autoencoder (RQ-VAE) that generates a compositional semantic code of discrete, interpretable tokens, promoting disentangled representations. MoToRec’s architecture is enhanced by three synergistic components: (1) a sparsely-regularized RQ-VAE that promotes disentangled representations, (2) a novel adaptive rarity amplification that promotes prioritized learning for cold-start items, and (3) a hierarchical multi-source graph encoder for robust signal fusion with collaborative signals. Extensive experiments on three large-scale datasets demonstrate MoToRec’s superiority over state-of-the-art methods in both overall and cold-start scenarios. Our work validates that discrete tokenization provides an effective and scalable alternative for mitigating the long-standing cold-start challenge.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques:

Residual Quantized Variational Autoencoder (RQ-VAE)

Adaptive Rarity Amplification
Hierarchical Multi-Source Graph Encoder

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHi-SAM: A Hierarchical Structure-Aware Multi-modal Framework for Large-Scale Recommendation0.28The Case for Semantic Tokens in Modern Ranking Systems, How Embedding Size Affects Dense Retrieval, and More!0.28End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.28DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.27Variable-Length Semantic IDs for Recommender Systems0.25
MoToRec (Sparse-Regularized Multimodal Tokenization for Cold-Start Recommendation)
Core Idea: Transform multimodal recommendation into discrete semantic tokenization using a sparsely-regularized Residual Quantized Variational Autoencoder (RQ-VAE) that generates compositional codes of interpretable tokens for disentangled item representations.
Problem It Solves: Addresses the item cold-start problem in recommendation systems where new items with limited or no interaction history suffer from suboptimal representations due to noise and entanglement in sparse multimodal data, limiting GNN-based recommender performance.
How It Works:

Sparsely-Regularized RQ-VAE: Encodes multimodal item features (visual, text) into discrete semantic tokens using residual quantization with sparse regularization to promote disentangled, interpretable representations
Adaptive Rarity Amplification: Applies prioritized learning that dynamically upweights cold-start items during training to ensure adequate gradient signal for items with sparse interactions
Hierarchical Multi-Source Graph Encoder: Fuses the discrete token representations with collaborative filtering signals from user-item interaction graphs to combine semantic and behavioral information
Token Composition: Reconstructs final item embeddings from the compositional semantic codes for downstream recommendation tasks

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Multimodal Features] --> B[RQ-VAE Encoder]
    B --> C[Discrete Token Codebook]
    C --> D[Semantic Codes]
    D --> E[Graph Encoder]
    F[User-Item Graph] --> E
    E --> G[Recommendation Scores]
    D -.Rarity Amplification.-> E

Where It’s Useful:

Cold-start recommendation scenarios with new items lacking interaction history
Multimodal e-commerce platforms with rich product images and descriptions
Content platforms (streaming, news) where new items arrive continuously
Recommendation systems requiring interpretable item representations

Limitations / Caveats:

Discrete tokenization introduces quantization error that may lose fine-grained feature details
Requires sufficient multimodal content (images, text) for effective tokenization
Codebook size and residual depth are hyperparameters requiring tuning per dataset
Additional computational overhead from RQ-VAE training compared to direct feature embedding

Internal Use Cases:

Apply discrete tokenization to cold-start items in ranking systems to create more robust embeddings from content features
Use adaptive rarity amplification as a training strategy for improving tail item performance in RecSys models
Leverage compositional token codes as interpretable debugging tools to understand what semantic aspects drive recommendations
Combine with existing graph-based collaborative filtering by treating discrete tokens as auxiliary features

literature/2026-02-12-Search-or-Accelera.title: Search or Accelerate: Confidence-Switched Position Beam Search for Diffusion Language Models
literature/2026-02-12-Search-or-Accelera.content:
Search or Accelerate: Confidence-Switched Position Beam Search for Diffusion Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #2 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Search, RAG, Diffusion, LLM
Abstract
Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) generate text by iteratively denoising a masked sequence, repeatedly deciding which positions to commit at each step. Standard decoding follows a greedy rule: unmask the most confident positions, yet this local choice can lock the model into a suboptimal unmasking order, especially on reasoning-heavy prompts. We present SOAR, a training-free decoding algorithm that adapts its behavior to the model’s uncertainty. When confidence is low, SOAR briefly widens the search over alternative unmasking decisions to avoid premature commitments; when confidence is high, it collapses the search and decodes many positions in parallel to reduce the number of denoising iterations. Across mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks (GSM8K, MBPP, HumanEval) on Dream-7B and LLaDA-8B, SOAR improves generation quality while maintaining competitive inference speed, offering a practical way to balance quality and efficiency in DLM decoding.
Key Techniques

Iterative denoising with selective unmasking
adaptive uncertainty-driven search widening
confidence-based parallel decoding

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySwordsman: Entropy-Driven Adaptive Block Partition for Efficient Diffusion Language Models0.33Improving Diffusion Language Model Decoding through Joint Search in Generation Order and Token Space0.33UnMaskFork: Test-Time Scaling for Masked Diffusion via Deterministic Action Branching0.32Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.26CATTO: Balancing Preferences and Confidence in Language Models0.24
SOAR (Confidence-Switched Position Beam Search)
Core Idea: A training-free decoding algorithm for Diffusion Language Models that dynamically switches between beam search over unmasking orders when confidence is low and parallel greedy decoding when confidence is high, balancing generation quality and inference speed.
Problem It Solves: Standard greedy position selection in DLMs commits to unmasking the most confident tokens at each step, which can lock the model into suboptimal unmasking trajectories—especially on reasoning-heavy tasks where early greedy choices propagate errors through subsequent denoising iterations.
How It Works:

At each denoising step, compute confidence scores for all masked positions (typically via model’s predicted token probabilities)
Low-confidence regime: When maximum confidence falls below threshold τ, maintain a beam of K alternative unmasking orders, scoring each trajectory and pruning to top-K candidates
High-confidence regime: When confidence exceeds τ, collapse to greedy decoding and unmask multiple high-confidence positions in parallel to accelerate iteration
Repeat until sequence is fully unmasked, adaptively switching between search (quality) and acceleration (speed) modes

Architecture:
flowchart TD
    A[Masked Sequence] --> B{Compute Position Confidence}
    B --> C{Confidence > τ?}
    C -->|No| D[Beam Search Mode:<br/>Expand K unmasking orders]
    C -->|Yes| E[Greedy Acceleration Mode:<br/>Unmask top-M positions in parallel]
    D --> F[Score & Prune to Top-K]
    F --> G[Denoise Selected Positions]
    E --> G
    G --> H{All Unmasked?}
    H -->|No| B
    H -->|Yes| I[Final Text Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Mathematical reasoning tasks (GSM8K) where multi-step logic requires careful unmasking order
Code generation (MBPP, HumanEval) where syntactic dependencies benefit from search over decoding paths
Any Diffusion LM deployment requiring quality-speed tradeoffs without retraining

Limitations / Caveats:

Introduces hyperparameters (confidence threshold τ, beam width K, parallel unmask count M) requiring task-specific tuning
Beam search overhead still increases compute vs. pure greedy, though mitigated by adaptive switching
Only evaluated on 7B-8B scale models (Dream-7B, LLaDA-8B); scaling behavior to larger DLMs unclear

Internal Use Cases:

Structured output generation: Apply to diffusion models generating JSON schemas or SQL queries where malformed early tokens cascade into syntax errors
Multi-step recommendation explanations: Use low-confidence beam search for reasoning chains justifying item recommendations, collapse to greedy for boilerplate phrasing
Hybrid ranking pipelines: Adapt confidence-switching logic to re-ranking—beam search over candidate orders when top scores are close, commit greedily when a clear winner emerges

literature/2026-02-12-SnapMLA-Efficient-.title: SnapMLA: Efficient Long-Context MLA Decoding via Hardware-Aware FP8 Quantized Pipelining
literature/2026-02-12-SnapMLA-Efficient-.content:
SnapMLA: Efficient Long-Context MLA Decoding via Hardware-Aware FP8 Quantized Pipelining
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #1 (score: 0.90, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
While FP8 attention has shown substantial promise in innovations like FlashAttention-3, its integration into the decoding phase of the DeepSeek Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) architecture presents notable challenges. These challenges include numerical heterogeneity arising from the decoupling of positional embeddings, misalignment of quantization scales in FP8 PV GEMM, and the need for optimized system-level support. In this paper, we introduce SnapMLA, an FP8 MLA decoding framework optimized to improve long-context efficiency through the following hardware-aware algorithm-kernel co-optimization techniques: (i) RoPE-Aware Per-Token KV Quantization, where the RoPE part is maintained in high precision, motivated by our comprehensive analysis of the heterogeneous quantization sensitivity inherent to the MLA KV cache. Furthermore, per-token granularity is employed to align with the autoregressive decoding process and maintain quantization accuracy. (ii) Quantized PV Computation Pipeline Reconstruction, which resolves the misalignment of quantization scale in FP8 PV computation stemming from the shared KV structure of the MLA KV cache. (iii) End-to-End Dataflow Optimization, where we establish an efficient data read-and-write workflow using specialized kernels, ensuring efficient data flow and performance gains. Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art MLA LLMs show that SnapMLA achieves up to a 1.91x improvement in throughput, with negligible risk of performance degradation in challenging long-context tasks, including mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks. Code is available at github.com/meituan-longcat/SGLang-FluentLLM.
Key Techniques

RoPE-Aware Per-Token KV Quantization
Quantized PV Computation Pipeline Reconstruction
End-to-End Dataflow Optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityExtending Puzzle for Mixture-of-Experts Reasoning Models with Application to GPT-OSS Acceleration0.30ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.23Is Finer Better? The Limits of Microscaling Formats in Large Language Models0.22Quant VideoGen: Auto-Regressive Long Video Generation via 2-Bit KV-Cache Quantization0.19Quartet II: Accurate LLM Pre-Training in NVFP4 by Improved Unbiased Gradient Estimation0.19
SnapMLA (Efficient Long-Context MLA Decoding)
Core Idea: A hardware-aware FP8 quantization framework for DeepSeek’s Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) architecture that optimizes long-context decoding through RoPE-aware quantization, pipeline reconstruction, and end-to-end dataflow optimization.
Problem It Solves: Addresses the challenges of applying FP8 quantization to MLA decoding, including numerical heterogeneity from decoupled positional embeddings (RoPE), quantization scale misalignment in FP8 PV GEMM operations, and inefficient memory access patterns during autoregressive generation with long contexts.
How It Works:

RoPE-Aware Per-Token KV Quantization: Maintains the RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) component in high precision while quantizing the rest of the KV cache to FP8 at per-token granularity, preserving positional information accuracy while reducing memory footprint.
Quantized PV Pipeline Reconstruction: Resolves quantization scale misalignment in the Packed-Value (PV) computation by restructuring the pipeline to account for MLA’s shared KV cache structure, ensuring correct FP8 GEMM operations.
End-to-End Dataflow Optimization: Implements specialized kernels that optimize the complete read-write workflow for quantized KV cache access, maximizing hardware utilization and minimizing memory bandwidth bottlenecks during autoregressive decoding.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input Tokens] --> B[RoPE-Aware Quantization]
    B --> C[FP8 KV Cache]
    C --> D[Quantized PV GEMM]
    D --> E[Optimized Dataflow Kernels]
    E --> F[Output Logits]
    
    style B fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#ffe1f5
    style E fill:#f5ffe1

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context inference for DeepSeek-V3 and other MLA-based LLMs (up to 128K+ tokens)
Production serving scenarios requiring high throughput with constrained GPU memory
Mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks with extended context windows
Real-time applications where decoding latency is critical (chatbots, code completion)

Limitations / Caveats:

Specifically designed for MLA architecture; not directly applicable to standard Multi-Head Attention (MHA) or Grouped-Query Attention (GQA)
Requires hardware support for FP8 computation (modern GPUs like H100, H200)
The RoPE component must remain in higher precision, limiting maximum compression ratio
Performance gains are most significant at longer context lengths (>32K tokens); benefits diminish for short sequences

Internal Use Cases:

LLM Serving Infrastructure: Deploy long-context models (e.g., document Q&A, codebase analysis) with 1.91x throughput improvement, reducing serving costs and latency
Recommendation System Context Modeling: If migrating to MLA-based architectures for user history encoding, SnapMLA enables handling longer user interaction sequences without memory explosion
Ranking Model Inference: For transformer-based rankers processing long documents or session histories, the per-token quantization strategy could inform similar FP8 optimization approaches
Code Intelligence Tools: Support large codebase context windows (entire files, multiple dependencies) for code completion and analysis with minimal accuracy loss

literature/2026-02-12-TVCACHE-A-Stateful.title: TVCACHE: A Stateful Tool-Value Cache for Post-Training LLM Agents
literature/2026-02-12-TVCACHE-A-Stateful.content:
TVCACHE: A Stateful Tool-Value Cache for Post-Training LLM Agents
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #6 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG, RL, LLM
Abstract
In RL post-training of LLM agents, calls to external tools take several seconds or even minutes, leaving allocated GPUs idle and inflating post-training time and cost. While many tool invocations repeat across parallel rollouts and could in principle be cached, naively caching their outputs for reuse is incorrect since tool outputs depend on the environment state induced by prior agent interactions. We present TVCACHE, a stateful tool-value cache for LLM agent post-training. TVCACHE maintains a tree of observed tool-call sequences and performs longest-prefix matching for cache lookups: a hit occurs only when the agent’s full tool history matches a previously executed sequence, guaranteeing identical environment state. On three diverse workloads-terminal-based tasks, SQL generation, and video understanding. TVCACHE achieves cache hit rates of up to 70% and reduces median tool call execution time by up to 6.9X, with no degradation in post-training reward accumulation.
Key Techniques

Stateful tool-value cache with tree structure
Longest-prefix matching for cache lookups
Environment state validation through tool history matching

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityOpaqueToolsBench: Learning Nuances of Tool Behavior Through Interaction0.29AgentStepper: Interactive Debugging of Software Development Agents0.22SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.22Ontology-to-tools compilation for executable semantic constraint enforcement in LLM agents0.20LRAgent: Efficient KV Cache Sharing for Multi-LoRA LLM Agents0.19
TVCACHE (Tool-Value Cache)
Core Idea: A stateful caching system for LLM agent post-training that uses longest-prefix matching on tool-call sequences to safely reuse tool outputs only when the environment state is guaranteed to be identical.
Problem It Solves: During RL post-training of LLM agents, external tool calls (taking seconds to minutes) leave GPUs idle, but naively caching tool outputs is incorrect because tool results depend on accumulated environment state from prior agent interactions.
How It Works:

Tree Construction: Build a tree structure storing observed sequences of tool calls with their parameters and corresponding outputs
Longest-Prefix Matching: On each new tool call, traverse the tree to find the longest matching prefix of the agent’s current tool-call history
State-Safe Reuse: Return cached output only if the entire prefix matches (guaranteeing identical environment state); otherwise execute the tool, observe output, and extend the tree
Parallel Rollout Sharing: Cache is shared across parallel RL rollouts, maximizing hit rates for repeated exploration patterns

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Agent Tool Call] --> B{Prefix Match in Tree?}
    B -->|Full Match| C[Return Cached Output]
    B -->|No Match| D[Execute Tool]
    D --> E[Store in Tree]
    E --> F[Return Fresh Output]
    C --> G[Continue Rollout]
    F --> G

Where It’s Useful:

RL post-training of LLM agents with expensive external tools (databases, APIs, simulators)
Terminal/shell command environments where state accumulates across commands
SQL generation tasks where database state changes with each query
Multi-step agent workflows with deterministic tool behavior

Limitations / Caveats:

Only works for deterministic tools (same input + same state → same output)
Requires exact parameter matching (no semantic equivalence)
Memory overhead scales with number of unique tool-call sequences explored
Not applicable to non-stateful tools (where naive output caching would already be safe)

Internal Use Cases:

Accelerating RL fine-tuning of code agents that invoke compilers, test suites, or linters repeatedly across rollouts
Speeding up agentic workflows that query internal databases or APIs during training
Reducing cloud costs in agent post-training pipelines where tool latency dominates wall-clock time

Discussions & References

literature/2026-02-12-When-Fusion-Helps-.title: When Fusion Helps and When It Breaks: View-Aligned Robustness in Same-Source Financial Imaging
literature/2026-02-12-When-Fusion-Helps-.content:
When Fusion Helps and When It Breaks: View-Aligned Robustness in Same-Source Financial Imaging
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #8 (score: 0.20, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
We study same-source multi-view learning and adversarial robustness for next-day direction prediction with financial image representations. On Shanghai Gold Exchange (SGE) spot gold data (2005-2025), we construct two window-aligned views from each rolling window: an OHLCV-rendered price/volume chart and a technical-indicator matrix. To ensure reliable evaluation, we adopt leakage-resistant time-block splits with embargo and use Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC). We find that results depend strongly on the label-noise regime: we apply an ex-post minimum-movement filter that discards samples with realized next-day absolute return below tau to define evaluation subsets with reduced near-zero label ambiguity. This induces a non-monotonic data-noise trade-off that can reveal predictive signal but eventually increases variance as sample size shrinks; the filter is used for offline benchmark construction rather than an inference-time decision rule. In the stabilized subsets, fusion is regime dependent: early fusion by channel stacking can exhibit negative transfer, whereas late fusion with dual encoders and a fusion head provides the dominant clean-performance gains; cross-view consistency regularization has secondary, backbone-dependent effects. We further evaluate test-time L-infinity perturbations using FGSM and PGD under two threat scenarios: view-constrained attacks that perturb one view and joint attacks that perturb both. We observe severe vulnerability at tiny budgets with strong view asymmetry. Late fusion consistently improves robustness under view-constrained attacks, but joint attacks remain challenging and can still cause substantial worst-case degradation.
Key Techniques

time-block splits with embargo and Matthews correlation coefficient evaluation
minimum-movement filter for label-noise reduction
late fusion with dual encoders and fusion head
FGSM and PGD adversarial perturbation testing

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityR2LED: Equipping Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs for CTR Prediction0.16Beyond Correlations: A Downstream Evaluation Framework for Query Performance Prediction0.15Generalizing GNNs with Tokenized Mixture of Experts0.14One Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral Profiles0.11Unified Multi-Domain Graph Pre-training for Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Graphs via Domain-Specific Expert Encoding0.11
Same-Source Multi-View Fusion with Minimum-Movement Filtering
Core Idea: Constructs multiple aligned views (price charts and technical indicators) from the same rolling window of financial time series, then systematically evaluates early vs. late fusion architectures under a leakage-resistant train-test regime and ex-post minimum-movement label filtering to isolate predictive signal from near-zero-return noise.
Problem It Solves: Financial direction prediction suffers from (1) label ambiguity when next-day moves are near zero, (2) train-test leakage in time series, and (3) unclear guidance on when multi-view fusion helps versus harms performance. This method addresses all three by establishing a rigorous evaluation protocol and comparing fusion strategies under controlled noise regimes.
How It Works:

Window-aligned view construction: From each rolling window of OHLC/volume data, generate two synchronized views—a rendered price/volume chart (image) and a technical-indicator matrix (tabular/image).
Leakage-resistant time-block splits: Use temporal blocking with embargo periods to prevent information leakage between train and test sets.
Ex-post minimum-movement filtering: Apply threshold τ on realized absolute next-day returns to discard samples with |r_t+1| < τ, creating evaluation subsets with reduced label noise (non-monotonic trade-off: reduces noise but eventually increases variance as sample shrinks).
Fusion architecture comparison:

Early fusion: Channel-stack both views into single encoder → can exhibit negative transfer
Late fusion: Dual encoders (one per view) + fusion head → dominant clean-performance gains
Cross-view consistency regularization: Secondary, backbone-dependent improvements


Adversarial robustness evaluation: Test L∞ perturbations (FGSM, PGD) under view-constrained (perturb one view) and joint (perturb both views) threat models.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Rolling Window t] --> B[Chart View]
    A --> C[Indicator View]
    B --> D[Encoder 1]
    C --> E[Encoder 2]
    D --> F[Fusion Head]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Direction Prediction]
    G --> H{|r_t+1| ≥ τ?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Evaluate]
    H -->|No| J[Discard]

Where It’s Useful:

Financial time-series direction/return prediction with multiple representations (technical, fundamental, sentiment)
Any domain where same-source data can be viewed through multiple lenses (medical imaging: raw + processed scans; recommendation: user history as sequence + graph)
Robustness benchmarking for multi-modal financial models under adversarial market conditions

Limitations / Caveats:

Minimum-movement filter τ is applied ex-post for evaluation, not as an inference-time rule—cannot reject ambiguous samples in deployment
Non-monotonic data-noise trade-off: too-high τ reduces sample size and increases variance, masking true performance
Joint adversarial attacks remain highly effective even under late fusion; view-constrained robustness improvements do not generalize to coordinated perturbations
Results are specific to SGE spot gold (2005–2025); generalization to other assets or markets requires re-validation

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys multi-view fusion: Combine user behavior sequences, graph embeddings, and content features via late fusion with consistency regularization
LLM multi-modal grounding: Dual encoders for text + structured data (tables, knowledge graphs) with adversarial robustness tests
Ranking model robustness: Evaluate sensitivity to feature perturbations (adversarial impressions, click noise) using view-constrained attack protocols
Label-noise handling: Adopt minimum-movement filtering logic to identify and down-weight ambiguous training samples (e.g., CTR predictions near decision boundary)

literature/2026-02-12-When-to-Memorize-a.title: When to Memorize and When to Stop: Gated Recurrent Memory for Long-Context Reasoning
literature/2026-02-12-When-to-Memorize-a.content:
When to Memorize and When to Stop: Gated Recurrent Memory for Long-Context Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #3 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, RL, LLM
Abstract
While reasoning over long context is crucial for various real-world applications, it remains challenging for large language models (LLMs) as they suffer from performance degradation as the context length grows. Recent work MemAgent has tried to tackle this by processing context chunk-by-chunk in an RNN-like loop and updating a textual memory for final answering. However, this naive recurrent memory update faces two crucial drawbacks: (i) memory can quickly explode because it can update indiscriminately, even on evidence-free chunks; and (ii) the loop lacks an exit mechanism, leading to unnecessary computation after even sufficient evidence is collected. To address these issues, we propose GRU-Mem, which incorporates two text-controlled gates for more stable and efficient long-context reasoning. Specifically, in GRU-Mem, the memory only updates when the update gate is open and the recurrent loop will exit immediately once the exit gate is open. To endow the model with such capabilities, we introduce two reward signals r^{\text{update}} and r^{\text{exit}} within end-to-end RL, rewarding the correct updating and exiting behaviors respectively. Experiments on various long-context reasoning tasks demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of GRU-Mem, which generally outperforms the vanilla MemAgent with up to 400% times inference speed acceleration.
Key Techniques

Gated memory updates
Exit gate mechanism
End-to-end reinforcement learning with reward signals

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityRecurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation0.24FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.23Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation0.22FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.18FBS: Modeling Native Parallel Reading inside a Transformer0.17
GRU-Mem (Gated Recurrent Memory)
Core Idea: A gated recurrent memory system for long-context LLM reasoning that uses text-controlled update and exit gates to selectively update memory on informative chunks and terminate processing early when sufficient evidence is collected.
Problem It Solves: Naive recurrent memory approaches like MemAgent suffer from memory explosion (updating on irrelevant chunks) and computational waste (continuing to process after gathering sufficient evidence), leading to degraded performance and inefficiency on long-context reasoning tasks.
How It Works:

Process long context chunk-by-chunk in an RNN-like loop, maintaining a textual memory state
At each chunk, compute an update gate (text-controlled) that determines whether the memory should be updated based on chunk informativeness
Apply gated update: memory changes only when update gate is open, preventing pollution from evidence-free chunks
Compute an exit gate (text-controlled) that signals when sufficient evidence has been collected
Terminate the recurrent loop immediately when exit gate opens, avoiding unnecessary computation
Train both gates end-to-end via RL with two reward signals: r^{\text{update}} (rewards correct updating behavior) and r^{\text{exit}} (rewards correct early termination)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Context Chunk t] --> B[Update Gate]
    A --> C[Memory Update]
    B -->|Gate Open?| C
    C --> D[Memory State t]
    D --> E[Exit Gate]
    E -->|Open?| F[Terminate & Answer]
    E -->|Closed?| G[Next Chunk t+1]
    G --> A

Where It’s Useful:

Long-document question answering where evidence is sparse
Multi-hop reasoning over extended contexts (books, reports, legal documents)
Retrieval-augmented generation with large candidate sets
Any task requiring selective information accumulation over long sequences

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires RL training with carefully designed reward signals for gate control
Text-controlled gates may struggle with implicit or ambiguous relevance signals
Early exit mechanism assumes evidence sufficiency can be reliably detected mid-stream
Performance depends on chunk segmentation strategy

Internal Use Cases:

Long-context RAG: Gate updates to only accumulate relevant retrieved chunks, exit early when answer is found
Session-based recommendation: Update user memory only on informative interactions, exit session modeling when intent is clear
Document ranking: Process candidate documents sequentially, exit when top-k confidence threshold is met

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-13-DMAP-A-Distributio.title: DMAP: A Distribution Map for Text
literature/2026-02-13-DMAP-A-Distributio.content:
DMAP: A Distribution Map for Text
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #2 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, Search, LLM, RecSys
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) are a powerful tool for statistical text analysis, with derived sequences of next-token probability distributions offering a wealth of information. Extracting this signal typically relies on metrics such as perplexity, which do not adequately account for context; how one should interpret a given next-token probability is dependent on the number of reasonable choices encoded by the shape of the conditional distribution. In this work, we present DMAP, a mathematically grounded method that maps a text, via a language model, to a set of samples in the unit interval that jointly encode rank and probability information. This representation enables efficient, model-agnostic analysis and supports a range of applications. We illustrate its utility through three case studies: (i) validation of generation parameters to ensure data integrity, (ii) examining the role of probability curvature in machine-generated text detection, and (iii) a forensic analysis revealing statistical fingerprints left in downstream models that have been subject to post-training on synthetic data. Our results demonstrate that DMAP offers a unified statistical view of text that is simple to compute on consumer hardware, widely applicable, and provides a foundation for further research into text analysis with LLMs.
Key Techniques

DMAP (mapping text via language model to unit interval samples)
next-token probability distributions
probability curvature analysis
synthetic data fingerprint detection

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPretrainRL: Alleviating Factuality Hallucination of Large Language Models at the Beginning0.22CoDa: Constrained Generation based Data Augmentation for Low-Resource NLP0.13CATTO: Balancing Preferences and Confidence in Language Models0.12Beyond Tokens: Semantic-Aware Speculative Decoding for Efficient Inference by Probing Internal States0.12Towards On-Policy SFT: Distribution Discriminant Theory and its Applications in LLM Training0.11
Distribution Map (DMAP)
Core Idea: Maps text through a language model into a set of samples in the unit interval [0,1] that jointly encode both rank and probability information from next-token distributions, enabling model-agnostic statistical analysis.
Problem It Solves: Traditional metrics like perplexity fail to account for context—interpreting a next-token probability requires understanding the shape of the conditional distribution (i.e., how many reasonable choices exist). DMAP provides a mathematically grounded representation that captures this distributional context.
How It Works:

Input: Pass text through an LLM to extract next-token probability distributions at each position
Transform: For each token, compute a sample value in [0,1] that encodes both the token’s rank (position in sorted probability list) and its probability mass
Output: A sequence of unit-interval samples representing the full text, preserving distributional shape information beyond single-point statistics

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input Text] --> B[LLM Forward Pass]
    B --> C[Next-Token Distributions]
    C --> D[Rank + Probability Encoding]
    D --> E[DMAP Samples in 0,1]
    E --> F[Statistical Analysis]

Where It’s Useful:

Generation parameter validation: Detecting contaminated or improperly generated training data
Machine-generated text detection: Analyzing probability curvature patterns unique to synthetic text
Model forensics: Identifying statistical fingerprints in models fine-tuned on synthetic data
Text analysis on consumer hardware: Efficient computation without requiring large model infrastructure

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires access to next-token probability distributions (not just final outputs)
Computational cost scales with text length × vocabulary size for distribution extraction
Interpretation depends on the base LLM used for generating distributions

Internal Use Cases:

Training data quality control: Validate that generated queries/passages for dense retrieval training maintain expected probability distributions
Synthetic negative detection: Identify if hard negatives were generated vs. mined, potentially revealing biases in training data
Model drift monitoring: Track statistical fingerprints when fine-tuning ranking models on LLM-generated data to detect distribution shift
Relevance signal extraction: Use distributional shape (not just perplexity) as features for learning-to-rank or pseudo-relevance feedback

literature/2026-02-13-Deep-Kernel-Fusion.title: Deep Kernel Fusion for Transformers
literature/2026-02-13-Deep-Kernel-Fusion.content:
Deep Kernel Fusion for Transformers
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #9 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Agentic LLM inference with long contexts is increasingly limited by memory bandwidth rather than compute. In this setting, SwiGLU MLP blocks, whose large weights exceed cache capacity, become a major yet under-optimized bottleneck. We propose DeepFusionKernel, a deeply fused kernel that cuts HBM traffic and boosts cache reuse, delivering up to 13.2% speedup on H100 and 9.7% on A100 over SGLang. Integrated with SGLang and paired with a kernel scheduler, DeepFusionKernel ensures consistent accelerations over generation lengths, while remaining adaptable to diverse models, inference configurations, and hardware platforms.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques/methods:

DeepFusionKernel

kernel fusion for SwiGLU MLP blocks
HBM traffic reduction
cache reuse optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLRAgent: Efficient KV Cache Sharing for Multi-LoRA LLM Agents0.23RedVisor: Reasoning-Aware Prompt Injection Defense via Zero-Copy KV Cache Reuse0.21DynamiQ: Accelerating Gradient Synchronization using Compressed Multi-hop All-reduce0.20FlashSchNet: Fast and Accurate Coarse-Grained Neural Network Molecular Dynamics0.17DeltaKV: Residual-Based KV Cache Compression via Long-Range Similarity0.14
DeepFusionKernel
Core Idea: A deeply fused CUDA kernel that co-optimizes the entire SwiGLU MLP forward pass (gate projection → SiLU activation → elementwise multiply → down projection) to minimize HBM traffic and maximize L1/L2 cache reuse during memory-bound LLM inference.
Problem It Solves: In long-context agentic LLM inference, token generation is memory-bandwidth bottleneck rather than compute-bound. SwiGLU MLP blocks—which dominate parameter count—require repeated HBM reads of large weight matrices across separate kernel launches, wasting bandwidth and cache capacity. Standard implementations (e.g., SGLang) launch 3-4 separate kernels per MLP block, each reloading overlapping data.
How It Works:

Fuse gate/up projections: Combine W_gate and W_up matrix multiplications into a single kernel launch, computing both linear transformations in one pass over input activations
Inline SwiGLU activation: Apply SiLU(gate) ⊗ up elementwise operation within the same kernel, keeping intermediate results in registers/shared memory instead of writing to HBM
Tile-aware down projection: Chain the down projection (W_down) directly after activation, reusing cached intermediate outputs before they evict from L1/L2
Kernel scheduling: Dynamically select fused vs. unfused kernels based on batch size, sequence length, and model dimensions to avoid register pressure on small batches

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context agentic inference (RAG, code generation, multi-turn dialogue) where batch size is small (1-8) and generation length is high
Memory-bandwidth-constrained GPUs (H100, A100) running Llama-style models with SwiGLU MLPs
Production serving systems optimizing per-token latency over throughput

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful tuning of tile sizes and register allocation per GPU architecture (H100 vs A100 have different cache hierarchies)
Speedup diminishes at large batch sizes where compute becomes bottleneck and kernel fusion increases register pressure
Only applies to SwiGLU/GELU-style activations; not directly applicable to older ReLU FFNs
Integration complexity: needs inference engine modifications (SGLang, vLLM) to route to fused kernels selectively

Internal Use Cases:

Accelerating single-user or low-batch inference for coding assistants, research agents, or interactive chatbots where latency matters more than throughput
Optimizing speculative decoding or draft-model generation where small-batch MLP calls dominate
Benchmarking memory-vs-compute tradeoffs: if SwiGLU speedup is significant, it confirms inference is memory-bound and other bandwidth optimizations (quantization, KV cache compression) will compound gains

literature/2026-02-13-Differentially-Pri.title: Differentially Private and Communication Efficient Large Language Model Split Inference via Stochastic Quantization and Soft Prompt
literature/2026-02-13-Differentially-Pri.content:
Differentially Private and Communication Efficient Large Language Model Split Inference via Stochastic Quantization and Soft Prompt
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #10 (score: 0.79, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable performance and received significant research interest. The enormous computational demands, however, hinder the local deployment on devices with limited resources. The current prevalent LLM inference paradigms require users to send queries to the service providers for processing, which raises critical privacy concerns. Existing approaches propose to allow the users to obfuscate the token embeddings before transmission and utilize local models for denoising. Nonetheless, transmitting the token embeddings and deploying local models may result in excessive communication and computation overhead, preventing practical implementation. In this work, we propose \textbf{DEL}, a framework for \textbf{D}ifferentially private and communication \textbf{E}fficient \textbf{L}LM split inference. More specifically, an embedding projection module and a differentially private stochastic quantization mechanism are proposed to reduce the communication overhead in a privacy-preserving manner. To eliminate the need for local models, we adapt soft prompt at the server side to compensate for the utility degradation caused by privacy. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that utilizes soft prompt to improve the trade-off between privacy and utility in LLM inference, and extensive experiments on text generation and natural language understanding benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.
Key Techniques

Token embedding obfuscation
stochastic quantization
soft prompt adaptation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityCompute Only Once: UG-Separation for Efficient Large Recommendation Models0.17Context Compression via Explicit Information Transmission0.17FedSGM: A Unified Framework for Constraint Aware, Bidirectionally Compressed, Multi-Step Federated Optimization0.14SPILLage: Agentic Oversharing on the Web0.13Quantization-Aware Collaborative Inference for Large Embodied AI Models0.11
DEL (Differentially Private and Communication Efficient LLM Split Inference)
Core Idea: Reduces communication overhead and preserves privacy in LLM split inference by projecting and quantizing token embeddings with differential privacy guarantees, while using server-side soft prompts to recover utility without requiring local denoising models.
Problem It Solves: Existing privacy-preserving LLM split inference methods require transmitting high-dimensional token embeddings and deploying resource-intensive local denoising models, creating excessive communication and computation overhead that prevents deployment on resource-constrained devices.
How It Works:

Embedding Projection: User-side lightweight projection module compresses token embeddings from high-dimensional space to lower-dimensional representations before transmission
Differentially Private Stochastic Quantization: Apply DP-SGD inspired stochastic quantization mechanism to projected embeddings, adding calibrated noise to achieve (ε, δ)-differential privacy while reducing bit-width for transmission
Soft Prompt Adaptation: Server-side trainable soft prompt tokens prepended to the LLM compensate for utility degradation from privacy noise and quantization, eliminating need for user-side denoising models

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[User Query] --> B[Token Embeddings]
    B --> C[Projection Module]
    C --> D[DP Stochastic Quantization]
    D -->|Compressed + Noisy| E[Transmission]
    E --> F[Server LLM with Soft Prompt]
    F --> G[Generated Response]

Where It’s Useful:

Resource-constrained edge devices requiring private LLM inference (mobile, IoT)
Healthcare/finance domains with strict privacy requirements and limited bandwidth
Federated or split learning scenarios where users cannot deploy large local models

Limitations / Caveats:

Privacy-utility trade-off: stronger privacy (lower ε) degrades generation quality despite soft prompt compensation
Requires server-side soft prompt training for each privacy budget and task combination
Projection module still requires some user-side computation, though minimal compared to full denoising models

Internal Use Cases:

Private recommendation query encoding: users could send compressed, DP-protected preference embeddings for personalized ranking without exposing raw queries
Federated LLM serving: compress and privatize user embeddings in multi-tenant LLM APIs to reduce bandwidth costs while maintaining privacy SLAs
Hybrid retrieval-generation: apply quantization to dense retrieval query embeddings before transmission to ranking/LLM stages

literature/2026-02-13-End-to-End-Semanti.title: End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation
literature/2026-02-13-End-to-End-Semanti.content:
End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #4 (score: 0.00, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Generative Recommendation (GR) has excelled by framing recommendation as next-token prediction. This paradigm relies on Semantic IDs (SIDs) to tokenize large-scale items into discrete sequences. Existing GR approaches predominantly generate SIDs via Residual Quantization (RQ), where items are encoded into embeddings and then quantized to discrete SIDs. However, this paradigm suffers from inherent limitations: 1) Objective misalignment and semantic degradation stemming from the two-stage compression; 2) Error accumulation inherent in the structure of RQ. To address these limitations, we propose UniSID, a Unified SID generation framework for generative advertisement recommendation. Specifically, we jointly optimize embeddings and SIDs in an end-to-end manner from raw advertising data, enabling semantic information to flow directly into the SID space and thus addressing the inherent limitations of the two-stage cascading compression paradigm. To capture fine-grained semantics, a multi-granularity contrastive learning strategy is introduced to align distinct items across SID levels. Finally, a summary-based ad reconstruction mechanism is proposed to encourage SIDs to capture high-level semantic information that is not explicitly present in advertising contexts. Experiments demonstrate that UniSID consistently outperforms state-of-the-art SID generation methods, yielding up to a 4.62% improvement in Hit Rate metrics across downstream advertising scenarios compared to the strongest baseline.
Key Techniques

End-to-end joint optimization of embeddings and SIDs
Multi-granularity contrastive learning
Summary-based ad reconstruction

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityEnd-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.90S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation0.32R2LED: Equipping Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs for CTR Prediction0.32DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.30GLASS: A Generative Recommender for Long-sequence Modeling via SID-Tier and Semantic Search0.30
UniSID (Unified Semantic ID)
Core Idea: End-to-end learnable Semantic ID generation that jointly optimizes item embeddings and discrete SIDs directly from raw advertising data, eliminating the two-stage compression bottleneck of traditional Residual Quantization-based approaches.
Problem It Solves: Addresses objective misalignment, semantic degradation, and error accumulation inherent in cascading two-stage SID generation (embedding learning → quantization) used by existing Generative Recommendation systems.
How It Works:

Joint Optimization: Simultaneously learn item embeddings and discrete SID assignments end-to-end from raw ad features (text, images, metadata) using a unified loss function, allowing semantic information to flow directly into the SID space
Multi-Granularity Contrastive Learning: Apply contrastive losses at each level of the SID hierarchy to ensure semantically similar items share common SID prefixes while distinct items are separated, capturing fine-grained semantic relationships
Summary-Based Reconstruction: Train SIDs to reconstruct high-level semantic summaries (not just raw features) of advertising contexts, encouraging capture of abstract semantic information beyond explicit feature matching
Generative Recommendation: Use learned SIDs as discrete tokens in next-token prediction for recommendation, where the LLM autoregressively generates sequences of SID tokens representing recommended items

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Ad Data] --> B[Joint Embedding<br>& SID Learner]
    B --> C[Multi-Granularity<br>Contrastive Loss]
    B --> D[Summary<br>Reconstruction]
    C --> E[Hierarchical SIDs]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Generative RecSys<br>Next-Token Prediction]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale advertisement recommendation systems requiring semantic item tokenization
Generative recommender systems (LLM-based) needing discrete item representations
Cold-start and long-tail item recommendations in advertising platforms
Cross-domain recommendation where semantic transfer is critical

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires sufficient training data to learn meaningful semantic SID spaces end-to-end
Computational overhead during training compared to two-stage methods (though inference remains efficient)
Effectiveness depends on quality of semantic summaries used in reconstruction objective
Still operates within discrete SID paradigm, limiting expressiveness compared to continuous embeddings

Internal Use Cases:

Replace RQ-VAE-based SID generation in generative ranking models with end-to-end learned SIDs for better semantic alignment
Apply multi-granularity contrastive learning to improve hierarchical clustering of ads/content in recommendation pipelines
Use summary-based reconstruction to train item representations that capture high-level user intent rather than just surface features
Extend to cold-start scenarios where semantic SIDs enable better generalization for new items with limited interaction history

literature/2026-02-13-Extending-Puzzle-f.title: Extending Puzzle for Mixture-of-Experts Reasoning Models with Application to GPT-OSS Acceleration
literature/2026-02-13-Extending-Puzzle-f.content:
Extending Puzzle for Mixture-of-Experts Reasoning Models with Application to GPT-OSS Acceleration
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #1 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, RL, LLM, RAG
Abstract
Reasoning-focused LLMs improve answer quality by generating longer reasoning traces, but the additional tokens dramatically increase serving cost, motivating inference optimization. We extend and apply Puzzle, a post-training neural architecture search (NAS) framework, to gpt-oss-120B to produce gpt-oss-puzzle-88B, a deployment-optimized derivative. Our approach combines heterogeneous MoE expert pruning, selective replacement of full-context attention with window attention, FP8 KV-cache quantization with calibrated scales, and post-training reinforcement learning to recover accuracy, while maintaining low generation length. In terms of per-token speeds, on an 8XH100 node we achieve 1.63X and 1.22X throughput speedups in long-context and short-context settings, respectively. gpt-oss-puzzle-88B also delivers throughput speedups of 2.82X on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU. However, because token counts can change with reasoning effort and model variants, per-token throughput (tok/s) and latency (ms/token) do not necessarily lead to end-to-end speedups: a 2X throughput gain is erased if traces grow 2X. Conversely, throughput gains can be spent on more reasoning tokens to improve accuracy; we therefore advocate request-level efficiency metrics that normalize throughput by tokens generated and trace an accuracy—speed frontier across reasoning efforts. We show that gpt-oss-puzzle-88B improves over gpt-oss-120B along the entire frontier, delivering up to 1.29X higher request-level efficiency. Across various benchmarks, gpt-oss-puzzle-88B matches or slightly exceeds the parent on suite-average accuracy across reasoning efforts, with retention ranging from 100.8% (high) to 108.2% (low), showing that post-training architecture search can substantially reduce inference costs without sacrificing quality.
Key Techniques

MoE expert pruning
window attention replacement
FP8 KV-cache quantization
post-training reinforcement learning

Related Litnotes
