NoteSimilaritySnapMLA: Efficient Long-Context MLA Decoding via Hardware-Aware FP8 Quantized Pipelining0.30From LLMs to LRMs: Rethinking Pruning for Reasoning-Centric Models0.24Accordion-Thinking: Self-Regulated Step Summaries for Efficient and Readable LLM Reasoning0.19HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.19ParisKV: Fast and Drift-Robust KV-Cache Retrieval for Long-Context LLMs0.18
Puzzle-Extended Neural Architecture Search for Reasoning Model Optimization
Core Idea: Apply post-training neural architecture search to reasoning-focused LLMs by combining heterogeneous MoE expert pruning, selective window attention replacement, FP8 KV-cache quantization, and reinforcement learning recovery to reduce inference cost while maintaining accuracy across varying reasoning efforts.
Problem It Solves: Reasoning models generate long traces that increase serving cost dramatically; traditional per-token throughput metrics are misleading because reasoning token counts vary with model architecture changes, making it unclear whether optimizations actually improve end-to-end efficiency.
How It Works:

Heterogeneous MoE Expert Pruning: Selectively remove experts from mixture-of-experts layers in gpt-oss-120B, reducing active parameters from 120B to 88B while preserving critical reasoning pathways
Selective Window Attention: Replace full-context attention with window attention in non-critical layers to reduce KV-cache memory and computation (context-dependent architectural modification)
FP8 KV-Cache Quantization: Apply calibrated FP8 quantization to key-value caches to reduce memory bandwidth and storage requirements
Post-Training RL Recovery: Run reinforcement learning fine-tuning on the pruned/quantized model to restore accuracy lost during architecture modifications, while constraining generation length to prevent trace explosion

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[gpt-oss-120B<br/>Base Model] --> B[MoE Expert<br/>Pruning]
    B --> C[Window Attention<br/>Replacement]
    C --> D[FP8 KV-Cache<br/>Quantization]
    D --> E[Post-Training<br/>RL Recovery]
    E --> F[gpt-oss-puzzle-88B<br/>88B params]
    
    G[Request-Level<br/>Efficiency Metric] -.evaluates.-> F

Where It’s Useful:

Deploying reasoning-focused LLMs (o1-style models) in production where long traces dominate cost
Multi-tenant serving environments where throughput per GPU needs optimization
Applications requiring accuracy-speed tradeoffs across different reasoning budgets (e.g., low-effort fast responses vs. high-effort complex reasoning)
Single-GPU deployment scenarios where memory and compute are severely constrained

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires access to base model architecture and training infrastructure for post-training RL recovery phase
Accuracy retention varies by reasoning effort level (100.8%-108.2%), meaning some configurations may show slight degradation
Per-token speedups (1.22X-2.82X) don’t guarantee end-to-end request speedups if reasoning traces grow longer
Window attention replacement may harm performance on tasks requiring very long-range dependencies
Requires careful calibration of FP8 quantization scales to avoid accuracy collapse

Internal Use Cases:

LLM Serving Optimization: Apply heterogeneous pruning to reduce serving costs for reasoning-augmented ranking models or recommendation explanation systems
Request-Level Efficiency Metrics: Adopt the paper’s accuracy-speed frontier methodology to evaluate RecSys model variants where computation varies by request complexity (e.g., cold-start vs. warm users)
Selective Attention Optimization: Use window attention in lower layers of transformer-based rankers where full context may not be necessary
Post-Training Recovery: Apply RL fine-tuning after model compression/quantization to recover ranking metrics (NDCG, MRR) lost during efficiency optimizations

literature/2026-02-13-Hi-SAM-A-Hierarchi.title: Hi-SAM: A Hierarchical Structure-Aware Multi-modal Framework for Large-Scale Recommendation
literature/2026-02-13-Hi-SAM-A-Hierarchi.content:
Hi-SAM: A Hierarchical Structure-Aware Multi-modal Framework for Large-Scale Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #5 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Multi-modal recommendation has gained traction as items possess rich attributes like text and images. Semantic ID-based approaches effectively discretize this information into compact tokens. However, two challenges persist: (1) Suboptimal Tokenization: existing methods (e.g., RQ-VAE) lack disentanglement between shared cross-modal semantics and modality-specific details, causing redundancy or collapse; (2) Architecture-Data Mismatch: vanilla Transformers treat semantic IDs as flat streams, ignoring the hierarchy of user interactions, items, and tokens. Expanding items into multiple tokens amplifies length and noise, biasing attention toward local details over holistic semantics. We propose Hi-SAM, a Hierarchical Structure-Aware Multi-modal framework with two designs: (1) Disentangled Semantic Tokenizer (DST): unifies modalities via geometry-aware alignment and quantizes them via a coarse-to-fine strategy. Shared codebooks distill consensus while modality-specific ones recover nuances from residuals, enforced by mutual information minimization; (2) Hierarchical Memory-Anchor Transformer (HMAT): splits positional encoding into inter- and intra-item subspaces via Hierarchical RoPE to restore hierarchy. It inserts Anchor Tokens to condense items into compact memory, retaining details for the current item while accessing history only through compressed summaries. Experiments on real-world datasets show consistent improvements over SOTA baselines, especially in cold-start scenarios. Deployed on a large-scale social platform serving millions of users, Hi-SAM achieved a 6.55% gain in the core online metric.
Key Techniques

Disentangled Semantic Tokenizer (DST)
Hierarchical Memory-Anchor Transformer (HMAT)
Hierarchical RoPE with Anchor Tokens

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMoToRec: Sparse-Regularized Multimodal Tokenization for Cold-Start Recommendation0.28Unleash the Potential of Long Semantic IDs for Generative Recommendation0.25Unleash the Potential of Long Semantic IDs for Generative Recommendation0.25DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.23The Case for Semantic Tokens in Modern Ranking Systems, How Embedding Size Affects Dense Retrieval, and More!0.22
Hi-SAM (Hierarchical Structure-Aware Multi-modal Framework)
Core Idea: A multi-modal recommendation framework that disentangles cross-modal semantics from modality-specific details during tokenization, then preserves user interaction hierarchy through memory-compressed attention over semantic ID sequences.
Problem It Solves: Existing semantic ID approaches suffer from (1) tokenization redundancy/collapse due to lack of disentanglement between shared and modality-specific information, and (2) flattened attention that ignores the hierarchical structure of user sequences (user → items → tokens), causing bias toward local token details over holistic item semantics.
How It Works:

Disentangled Semantic Tokenizer (DST): Aligns multi-modal features (text/images) via geometry-aware alignment, then applies coarse-to-fine residual quantization where shared codebooks capture cross-modal consensus and modality-specific codebooks encode residual details, enforced by mutual information minimization to prevent information leakage
Hierarchical RoPE: Splits positional encodings into inter-item (sequence position) and intra-item (token position within item) subspaces to restore the two-level hierarchy of interactions
Anchor Token Compression: Inserts learnable anchor tokens between items that compress each item’s multi-token representation into compact memory, allowing full attention within current item while accessing historical items only through compressed summaries

Architecture:
flowchart TB
    subgraph DST["Disentangled Semantic Tokenizer"]
        A[Multi-modal Inputs] --> B[Geometry-Aware Alignment]
        B --> C[Shared Codebook<br/>coarse semantics]
        C --> D[Modality-Specific Codebooks<br/>residual details]
        D --> E[Semantic ID Tokens]
    end
    subgraph HMAT["Hierarchical Memory-Anchor Transformer"]
        E --> F[Hierarchical RoPE<br/>inter-item + intra-item]
        F --> G[Anchor Tokens]
        G --> H[Compressed Memory]
        H --> I[Hierarchical Attention]
    end
    I --> J[Recommendation Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale multi-modal recommendation systems with text, image, and video content
Cold-start scenarios where semantic understanding compensates for sparse interaction data
Social platforms with long user histories requiring efficient sequence modeling
E-commerce systems with rich product attributes across modalities

Limitations / Caveats:

Adds architectural complexity with hierarchical positional encoding and anchor token mechanisms
Requires careful tuning of mutual information minimization loss to balance disentanglement vs. information retention
Memory compression through anchors may lose fine-grained details from historical items, trading recall for efficiency

Internal Use Cases:

Semantic ID compression for RecSys: Apply DST’s disentanglement strategy to improve upon existing RQ-VAE approaches, ensuring modality-specific codebooks capture residuals orthogonal to shared semantics
LLM-based generative recommendation: Use HMAT’s hierarchical structure to improve constrained decoding efficiency—anchor tokens can serve as item-level boundaries for constrained grammar parsing
Cold-start ranking: Leverage geometry-aware alignment to create more robust semantic IDs that generalize across content types
Context length optimization: Adopt anchor token compression to enable longer user history in Transformer-based ranking models without quadratic attention cost

literature/2026-02-13-Improving-Neural-R.title: Improving Neural Retrieval with Attribution-Guided Query Rewriting
literature/2026-02-13-Improving-Neural-R.content:
Improving Neural Retrieval with Attribution-Guided Query Rewriting
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #10 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG, Search, LLM, RecSys
Abstract
Neural retrievers are effective but brittle: underspecified or ambiguous queries can misdirect ranking even when relevant documents exist. Existing approaches address this brittleness only partially: LLMs rewrite queries without retriever feedback, and explainability methods identify misleading tokens but are used for post-hoc analysis. We close this loop and propose an attribution-guided query rewriting method that uses token-level explanations to guide query rewriting. For each query, we compute gradient-based token attributions from the retriever and then use these scores as soft guidance in a structured prompt to an LLM that clarifies weak or misleading query components while preserving intent. Evaluated on BEIR collections, the resulting rewrites consistently improve retrieval effectiveness over strong baselines, with larger gains for implicit or ambiguous information needs.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques or methods:

Gradient-based token attribution

LLM-guided query rewriting
Attribution-driven prompt engineering
Retriever feedback integration

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityAgentic-R: Learning to Retrieve for Agentic Search0.20A Picture of Agentic Search0.20Breaking Flat: A Generalised Query Performance Prediction Evaluation Framework0.18Real-Time Trend Prediction via Continually-Aligned LLM Query Generation0.16Domain-Adaptive and Scalable Dense Retrieval for Content-Based Recommendation0.14
Attribution-Guided Query Rewriting (AGQR)
Core Idea: Use gradient-based token attributions from a neural retriever to identify weak or misleading query terms, then feed those attribution scores as soft guidance into an LLM prompt that rewrites the query to improve retrieval effectiveness while preserving user intent.
Problem It Solves: Neural retrievers are brittle to underspecified or ambiguous queries, and existing solutions operate in isolation—LLMs rewrite queries without retriever feedback, while explainability methods (gradient attributions, attention) are used only for post-hoc analysis rather than actively improving retrieval.
How It Works:

Compute Token Attributions: For a given query, perform gradient-based attribution (e.g., integrated gradients or saliency) on the neural retriever to score each query token’s contribution to the retrieval ranking.
Structure Guided Prompt: Pass the original query plus token-level attribution scores to an LLM in a structured prompt that highlights which terms are weak (low attribution) or potentially misleading (negative attribution).
Generate Rewrite: The LLM produces a clarified query that strengthens underspecified components, removes or rephrases misleading terms, and preserves the original search intent.
Re-retrieve: Use the rewritten query to perform a second retrieval pass with the same neural retriever.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Original Query] --> B[Neural Retriever]
    B --> C[Gradient-Based Attribution]
    C --> D[Token Scores]
    D --> E[LLM with Structured Prompt]
    A --> E
    E --> F[Rewritten Query]
    F --> G[Neural Retriever - Second Pass]
    G --> H[Improved Rankings]

Where It’s Useful:

Open-domain search with implicit or ambiguous information needs (BEIR benchmarks)
Domain-specific retrieval where terminology is vague or polysemous
Interactive search systems where users provide short, underspecified queries
Any neural retrieval pipeline that can produce gradient-based explanations

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires differentiable neural retriever (not applicable to black-box BM25 or closed-API systems)
Adds latency from attribution computation + LLM rewrite call (not suitable for ultra-low-latency search)
LLM may still hallucinate or drift from user intent despite attribution guidance
Gains are largest for ambiguous queries; already-clear queries see minimal benefit

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys/Ranking: Apply attribution to collaborative filtering or neural ranking models to explain why certain items rank low, then use LLM to reformulate user profiles or item queries
LLM-Augmented Retrieval: Close the loop between retrieval model gradients and prompt engineering—use attributions to dynamically adjust RAG prompts or HyDE hypothetical documents
Query Debugging: Surface which query tokens confuse the ranker and auto-suggest clarifications in production search UI
Multi-turn Search Agents: Combine with Multi-step Agentic Retrieval and Rewriting for Massive GraphRAG to iteratively refine queries based on accumulated evidence and attribution feedback

Discussions & References

github.com

literature/2026-02-13-LASER-An-Efficient.title: LASER: An Efficient Target-Aware Segmented Attention Framework for End-to-End Long Sequence Modeling
literature/2026-02-13-LASER-An-Efficient.content:
LASER: An Efficient Target-Aware Segmented Attention Framework for End-to-End Long Sequence Modeling
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #6 (score: 0.94, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Modeling ultra-long user behavior sequences is pivotal for capturing evolving and lifelong interests in modern recommendation systems. However, deploying such models in real-time industrial environments faces a strict “Latency Wall”, constrained by two distinct bottlenecks: the high I/O latency of retrieving massive user histories and the quadratic computational complexity of standard attention mechanisms. To break these bottlenecks, we present LASER, a full-stack optimization framework developed and deployed at Xiaohongshu (RedNote). Our approach tackles the challenges through two complementary innovations: (1) System efficiency: We introduce SeqVault, a unified schema-aware serving infrastructure for long user histories. By implementing a hybrid DRAM-SSD indexing strategy, SeqVault reduces retrieval latency by 50% and CPU usage by 75%, ensuring millisecond-level access to full real-time and life-cycle user histories. (2) Algorithmic efficiency: We propose a Segmented Target Attention (STA) mechanism to address the computational overhead. Motivated by the inherent sparsity of user interests, STA employs a sigmoid-based gating strategy that acts as a silence mechanism to filter out noisy items. Subsequently, a lightweight Global Stacked Target Attention (GSTA) module refines these compressed segments to capture cross-segment dependencies without incurring high computational costs. This design performs effective sequence compression, reducing the complexity of long-sequence modeling while preserving critical signals. Extensive offline evaluations demonstrate that LASER consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. In large-scale online A/B testing serving over 100 million daily active users, LASER achieved a 2.36% lift in ADVV and a 2.08% lift in revenue, demonstrating its scalability and significant commercial impact.
Key Techniques

SeqVault hybrid DRAM-SSD indexing
Segmented Target Attention with sigmoid gating
Global Stacked Target Attention for cross-segment dependencies

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.24Superlinear Multi-Step Attention0.24Attn-GS: Attention-Guided Context Compression for Efficient Personalized LLMs0.24HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.23SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.22
LASER: Segmented Target Attention with Hybrid Storage
Core Idea: A full-stack framework combining hybrid DRAM-SSD storage infrastructure (SeqVault) with a sparse attention mechanism (Segmented Target Attention) to enable efficient modeling of ultra-long user behavior sequences in real-time recommendation systems.
Problem It Solves: Deploying long-sequence models in production faces a dual “Latency Wall”: (1) high I/O latency retrieving massive user histories from storage, and (2) quadratic computational complexity of attention mechanisms over sequences with millions of interactions.
How It Works:

Storage Layer (SeqVault): Schema-aware serving infrastructure uses hybrid DRAM-SSD indexing to store and retrieve full user histories, reducing retrieval latency by 50% and CPU usage by 75%
Attention Filtering (STA): Sigmoid-based gating mechanism filters noisy/irrelevant items from user history segments, exploiting interest sparsity to compress sequences
Cross-Segment Fusion (GSTA): Global Stacked Target Attention module processes compressed segments to capture dependencies across time periods without full quadratic complexity
End-to-End Integration: Combined system enables millisecond-level access to lifetime user histories while maintaining linear or sub-quadratic attention complexity

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[User History<br/>Millions of Items] --> B[SeqVault<br/>DRAM-SSD Hybrid]
    B --> C[Retrieved Segments]
    C --> D[STA Gating<br/>Sigmoid Filter]
    D --> E[Compressed Segments]
    E --> F[GSTA<br/>Cross-Segment Attention]
    F --> G[Recommendation<br/>Prediction]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale recommendation systems requiring lifelong user modeling (e-commerce, social media, streaming)
Real-time serving environments with strict latency constraints (<100ms)
Systems with massive user histories (millions of interactions per user)
Industrial deployments balancing accuracy and computational cost

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires infrastructure investment in hybrid storage system (SeqVault) for full benefit
Gating mechanism may filter out weak but relevant signals in cold-start or sparse interaction scenarios
Effectiveness depends on inherent sparsity structure of user interests (may vary across domains)
System-algorithm co-design makes it harder to adopt piecewise (full benefits require both components)

Internal Use Cases:

Long-context ranking: Apply segmented attention to model full user session histories in feed ranking without latency penalty
LLM context extension: Adapt gating strategy to filter retrieval candidates before feeding to LLM-based recommenders
Hybrid storage patterns: Implement DRAM-SSD tiering for user embeddings, feature stores, or cached model activations
Interest evolution modeling: Use segment-wise compression to track changing user preferences across lifecycle stages

literature/2026-02-13-Learning-to-Config.title: Learning to Configure Agentic AI Systems
literature/2026-02-13-Learning-to-Config.content:
Learning to Configure Agentic AI Systems
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #3 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Search, RL, LLM, RAG
Abstract
Configuring LLM-based agent systems involves choosing workflows, tools, token budgets, and prompts from a large combinatorial design space, and is typically handled today by fixed large templates or hand-tuned heuristics. This leads to brittle behavior and unnecessary compute, since the same cumbersome configuration is often applied to both easy and hard input queries. We formulate agent configuration as a query-wise decision problem and introduce ARC (Agentic Resource & Configuration learner), which learns a light-weight hierarchical policy using reinforcement learning to dynamically tailor these configurations. Across multiple benchmarks spanning reasoning and tool-augmented question answering, the learned policy consistently outperforms strong hand-designed and other baselines, achieving up to 25% higher task accuracy while also reducing token and runtime costs. These results demonstrate that learning per-query agent configurations is a powerful alternative to “one size fits all” designs.
Key Techniques

Query-wise configuration learning
Hierarchical policy with reinforcement learning
Dynamic token budget allocation
Tool selection optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLearning to Configure Agentic AI Systems0.78MATA: A Trainable Hierarchical Automaton System for Multi-Agent Visual Reasoning0.23Look Inward to Explore Outward: Learning Temperature Policy from LLM Internal States via Hierarchical RL0.21DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.19OpaqueToolsBench: Learning Nuances of Tool Behavior Through Interaction0.18
ARC (Agentic Resource & Configuration learner)
Core Idea: Learn a hierarchical reinforcement learning policy that dynamically configures LLM agent systems on a per-query basis—selecting workflows, tools, token budgets, and prompts—rather than applying fixed “one-size-fits-all” configurations.
Problem It Solves: Static agent configurations waste compute on simple queries and underperform on complex ones; hand-tuned heuristics are brittle across diverse inputs. The combinatorial design space of workflows × tools × budgets × prompts is too large for manual optimization.
How It Works:

State Representation: Encode each query and current configuration state (active tools, workflow stage, token usage) as input to the policy
Hierarchical Policy Learning: Train a lightweight policy network using RL (reward = task accuracy - cost penalty) to make sequential decisions: (a) which workflow to use, (b) which tools to enable, (c) token budget allocation, (d) prompt template selection
Query-Wise Adaptation: At inference, the policy observes the input query and dynamically tailors the agent configuration before execution, allowing cheap configs for easy queries and expensive configs for hard ones

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input Query] --> B[Policy Network]
    B --> C[Workflow Selection]
    B --> D[Tool Selection]
    B --> E[Token Budget]
    B --> F[Prompt Template]
    C --> G[Configured Agent]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Task Execution]
    H --> I[Reward Signal]
    I -.->|RL Update| B

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-turn reasoning systems with variable query complexity (e.g., SWE-bench, HotpotQA, tool-augmented QA)
Cost-sensitive agentic applications where token/runtime budgets must be optimized per request
Production LLM deployments requiring automatic adaptation to diverse user queries without manual tuning

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires training data with labeled task outcomes to learn the RL policy (offline RL or online exploration cost)
Policy quality depends on diversity of training queries; may not generalize to distribution shifts
Adds inference-time overhead for policy forward pass, though this is lightweight compared to LLM calls

Internal Use Cases:

Adaptive LLM routing: Learn when to use fast/cheap models vs. expensive reasoning models per query in production ranking or recommendations
Dynamic tool selection for RecSys agents: Configure retrieval tools, rerankers, and context windows based on query complexity (e.g., simple “top movies” vs. complex personalized discovery)
Cost-aware prompt engineering: Automatically choose between few-shot, CoT, or multi-agent workflows based on predicted query difficulty and budget constraints

literature/2026-02-13-PrefillShare-A-Sha.title: PrefillShare: A Shared Prefill Module for KV Reuse in Multi-LLM Disaggregated Serving
literature/2026-02-13-PrefillShare-A-Sha.content:
PrefillShare: A Shared Prefill Module for KV Reuse in Multi-LLM Disaggregated Serving
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #3 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG
Abstract
Multi-agent systems increasingly orchestrate multiple specialized language models to solve complex real-world problems, often invoking them over a shared context. This execution pattern repeatedly processes the same prompt prefix across models. Consequently, each model redundantly executes the prefill stage and maintains its own key-value (KV) cache, increasing aggregate prefill load and worsening tail latency by intensifying prefill-decode interference in existing LLM serving stacks. Disaggregated serving reduces such interference by placing prefill and decode on separate GPUs, but disaggregation does not fundamentally eliminate inter-model redundancy in computation and KV storage for the same prompt. To address this issue, we propose PrefillShare, a novel algorithm that enables sharing the prefill stage across multiple models in a disaggregated setting. PrefillShare factorizes the model into prefill and decode modules, freezes the prefill module, and fine-tunes only the decode module. This design allows multiple task-specific models to share a prefill module and the KV cache generated for the same prompt. We further introduce a routing mechanism that enables effective prefill sharing across heterogeneous models in a vLLM-based disaggregated system. PrefillShare not only matches full fine-tuning accuracy on a broad range of tasks and models, but also delivers 4.5x lower p95 latency and 3.9x higher throughput in multi-model agent workloads.
Key Techniques

Here are the key techniques from this paper:

**Disaggregated serving

Prefill-decode separation
KV cache sharing
Model factorization**

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHeterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.41Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.41Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.38RedVisor: Reasoning-Aware Prompt Injection Defense via Zero-Copy KV Cache Reuse0.27CLAA: Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation for Accelerating LLM Prefill0.25
PrefillShare
Core Idea: Share a frozen prefill module and its generated KV cache across multiple specialized models in disaggregated serving, eliminating redundant computation when multiple LLMs process the same prompt prefix.
Problem It Solves: Multi-agent systems that invoke multiple specialized models over a shared context suffer from redundant prefill computation and KV cache storage for identical prompts, leading to high aggregate prefill load, worse tail latency due to prefill-decode interference, and wasted GPU memory—even in disaggregated architectures.
How It Works:

Model Factorization: Split each LLM into two modules—a shared prefill module (freezes early transformer layers) and task-specific decode modules (trainable later layers)
Selective Fine-tuning: Freeze the prefill module weights across all models and fine-tune only the decode module for each specialized task
KV Cache Sharing: When multiple models process the same prompt, compute KV cache once in the shared prefill module and reuse it across all decode modules
Routing Mechanism: Implement vLLM-based routing to direct requests through the shared prefill stage before distributing to appropriate task-specific decode modules

Architecture:
flowchart TB
    A[Shared Prompt] --> B[Frozen Prefill Module<br/>Layers 1-N]
    B --> C[Shared KV Cache]
    C --> D1[Decode Module 1<br/>Task A - Layers N+1 to L]
    C --> D2[Decode Module 2<br/>Task B - Layers N+1 to L]
    C --> D3[Decode Module 3<br/>Task C - Layers N+1 to L]
    D1 --> E1[Output A]
    D2 --> E2[Output B]
    D3 --> E3[Output C]

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-agent orchestration systems (e.g., ReAct, AutoGPT) where specialized models collaborate on shared context
Mixture-of-experts serving with prompt-level routing to task-specific models
Multi-model ensembles processing identical input prefixes
Disaggregated LLM serving clusters with multiple fine-tuned variants

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires models to share compatible architecture (same prefill layers)
Accuracy depends on finding optimal split point between prefill/decode modules—too early reduces sharing benefit, too late may hurt task-specific performance
Only beneficial when prompt overlap is high across models; no gains for completely disjoint contexts
Freezing prefill limits adapter capacity compared to full fine-tuning (though paper shows accuracy matches full fine-tuning empirically)

Internal Use Cases:

Multi-Model Ranking: Share prefill across candidate generation, ranking, and reranking models processing same user query context
LLM Agent Workflows: Reuse KV cache when orchestrating specialist models (summarizer → fact-checker → formatter) over shared documents
A/B Testing Variants: Serve multiple fine-tuned variants (different training data/hyperparameters) with shared prompt encoding to reduce infrastructure cost
RecSys Hybrid Systems: Share context encoding (user history, item metadata) across multiple specialized models (CTR prediction, diversity scoring, explanation generation)

literature/2026-02-13-Recurrent-Preferen.title: Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation
literature/2026-02-13-Recurrent-Preferen.content:
Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #7 (score: 0.91, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Generative recommendation (GenRec) models typically model user behavior via full attention, but scaling to lifelong sequences is hindered by prohibitive computational costs and noise accumulation from stochastic interactions. To address these challenges, we introduce Rec2PM, a framework that compresses long user interaction histories into compact Preference Memory tokens. Unlike traditional recurrent methods that suffer from serial training, Rec2PM employs a novel self-referential teacher-forcing strategy: it leverages a global view of the history to generate reference memories, which serve as supervision targets for parallelized recurrent updates. This allows for fully parallel training while maintaining the capability for iterative updates during inference. Additionally, by representing memory as token embeddings rather than extensive KV caches, Rec2PM achieves extreme storage efficiency. Experiments on large-scale benchmarks show that Rec2PM significantly reduces inference latency and memory footprint while achieving superior accuracy compared to full-sequence models. Analysis reveals that the Preference Memory functions as a denoising Information Bottleneck, effectively filtering interaction noise to capture robust long-term interests.
Key Techniques

Preference Memory tokens
self-referential teacher-forcing strategy
denoising Information Bottleneck

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityRecurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation0.89Scene-Aware Memory Discrimination: Deciding Which Personal Knowledge Stays0.23MemFly: On-the-Fly Memory Optimization via Information Bottleneck0.23When to Memorize and When to Stop: Gated Recurrent Memory for Long-Context Reasoning0.22LASER: An Efficient Target-Aware Segmented Attention Framework for End-to-End Long Sequence Modeling0.20
Rec2PM (Recurrent Preference Memory)
Core Idea: Compresses long user interaction sequences into compact learnable memory tokens via self-referential teacher-forcing, enabling parallel training of recurrent models while maintaining O(1) inference memory.
Problem It Solves: Full-attention generative recommendation models face quadratic computational cost and cannot scale to lifelong user histories; traditional recurrent models require serial training and accumulate noise from stochastic interactions.
How It Works:

Global reference generation: A full-attention encoder processes the entire interaction history to generate reference memory states that capture denoised long-term preferences
Parallel recurrent training: Using the reference memories as supervision targets, the model learns to update memory tokens recurrently in a fully parallelizable manner (self-referential teacher-forcing)
Inference-time compression: During serving, new interactions incrementally update the compact memory tokens (not full KV cache), which are then used to condition next-item generation

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Long History] --> B[Global Encoder<br/>full attention]
    B --> C[Reference Memory<br/>supervision]
    A --> D[Recurrent Updates<br/>parallel training]
    C -.teacher forcing.-> D
    D --> E[Preference Memory<br/>tokens]
    E --> F[Next Item<br/>Generation]

Where It’s Useful:

Sequential recommendation with extremely long user histories (thousands+ interactions)
Generative retrieval systems requiring low-latency, memory-efficient inference
Session-based recommendation where continual learning from streaming interactions is needed

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires two-stage training (global encoder + recurrent model), adding architectural complexity
Memory compression may lose fine-grained short-term signals compared to full-sequence attention
Self-referential teacher-forcing assumes global view is available during training (not purely online learning)

Internal Use Cases:

Lifelong user modeling: Compress years of user behavior into fixed-size memory for real-time ranking/retrieval without storing full interaction histories
Information bottleneck for denoising: Filter noisy implicit feedback (accidental clicks, exploration) while preserving stable long-term preferences
Hybrid architectures: Combine Rec2PM memory tokens with short-term full-attention over recent sessions for best of both worlds

literature/2026-02-13-SToRM-Supervised-T.title: SToRM: Supervised Token Reduction for Multi-modal LLMs toward efficient end-to-end autonomous driving
literature/2026-02-13-SToRM-Supervised-T.content:
SToRM: Supervised Token Reduction for Multi-modal LLMs toward efficient end-to-end autonomous driving
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #2 (score: 0.46, max_sim: 0.78)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
In autonomous driving, end-to-end (E2E) driving systems that predict control commands directly from sensor data have achieved significant advancements. For safe driving in unexpected scenarios, these systems may additionally rely on human interventions such as natural language instructions. Using a multi-modal large language model (MLLM) facilitates human-vehicle interaction and can improve performance in such scenarios. However, this approach requires substantial computational resources due to its reliance on an LLM and numerous visual tokens from sensor inputs, which are limited in autonomous vehicles. Many MLLM studies have explored reducing visual tokens, but often suffer end-task performance degradation compared to using all tokens.   To enable efficient E2E driving while maintaining performance comparable to using all tokens, this paper proposes the first Supervised Token Reduction framework for multi-modal LLMs (SToRM). The proposed framework consists of three key elements. First, a lightweight importance predictor with short-term sliding windows estimates token importance scores. Second, a supervised training approach uses an auxiliary path to obtain pseudo-supervision signals from an all-token LLM pass. Third, an anchor-context merging module partitions tokens into anchors and context tokens, and merges context tokens into relevant anchors to reduce redundancy while minimizing information loss. Experiments on the LangAuto benchmark show that SToRM outperforms state-of-the-art E2E driving MLLMs under the same reduced-token budget, maintaining all-token performance while reducing computational cost by up to 30x.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques:

**Lightweight importance predictor with sliding windows

Supervised training with auxiliary path for pseudo-supervision
Anchor-context token merging module**

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityVisionTrim: Unified Vision Token Compression for Training-Free MLLM Acceleration0.31Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs0.26ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.26Nüwa: Mending the Spatial Integrity Torn by VLM Token Pruning0.24FlashVID: Efficient Video Large Language Models via Training-free Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging0.24
SToRM (Supervised Token Reduction for Multi-modal LLMs)
Core Idea: A supervised framework that learns to identify and remove redundant visual tokens from multi-modal LLMs while maintaining end-task performance through pseudo-supervision from an all-token reference pass.
Problem It Solves: Multi-modal LLMs for autonomous driving require processing thousands of visual tokens from camera inputs, creating computational bottlenecks unsuitable for resource-constrained vehicle platforms. Existing token reduction methods suffer significant performance degradation compared to using all tokens.
How It Works:

Importance Prediction: A lightweight predictor with short-term sliding windows estimates importance scores for each visual token from sensor inputs
Supervised Training: An auxiliary path processes all tokens through the LLM, generating pseudo-supervision signals (intermediate representations/outputs) that guide the reduced-token path
Anchor-Context Merging: High-importance tokens become “anchors” while low-importance tokens are “context”; context tokens are merged into their most relevant anchors to preserve information while reducing token count
End-to-End Optimization: The importance predictor learns which tokens are critical for downstream driving tasks (steering, acceleration) through gradients from both the task loss and supervision loss

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Multi-Camera Images] --> B[Vision Encoder]
    B --> C[All Visual Tokens]
    C --> D[Importance Predictor<br/>with Sliding Window]
    D --> E[Top-K Selection]
    E --> F[Anchor-Context Merging]
    F --> G[Reduced Tokens]
    G --> H[LLM Decoder]
    H --> I[Driving Commands]
    C -.Auxiliary Path.-> J[Full-Token LLM]
    J -.Pseudo-Supervision.-> H

Where It’s Useful:

End-to-end autonomous driving systems requiring multi-modal understanding (vision + language instructions)
Resource-constrained deployment of vision-language models (edge devices, robotics)
Any multi-modal LLM application where visual input contains high redundancy (surveillance, video understanding)
Human-in-the-loop systems requiring language-conditioned visual reasoning under latency constraints

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires training with an auxiliary all-token path, increasing training cost (though inference is efficient)
Performance depends on quality of pseudo-supervision signals from the teacher model
Sliding window approach assumes temporal locality in importance patterns (may not generalize to non-sequential modalities)
Tested primarily on LangAuto benchmark; effectiveness on other vision-language tasks unclear

Internal Use Cases:

Multi-modal RecSys: Apply token reduction to product image understanding in recommendation pipelines where visual tokens from high-res images create bottlenecks
Video content ranking: Reduce computational cost of processing video frames for content understanding/classification while maintaining ranking quality
LLM-based assistants with vision: Enable efficient processing of screenshots/images in conversational agents by identifying and merging redundant visual patches
Supervised distillation for ranking models: Adapt the auxiliary-path supervision technique to distill large rankers into efficient student models that maintain performance

literature/2026-02-13-Scene-Aware-Memory.title: Scene-Aware Memory Discrimination: Deciding Which Personal Knowledge Stays
literature/2026-02-13-Scene-Aware-Memory.content:
Scene-Aware Memory Discrimination: Deciding Which Personal Knowledge Stays
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #5 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, Search, LLM, RecSys
Abstract
Intelligent devices have become deeply integrated into everyday life, generating vast amounts of user interactions that form valuable personal knowledge. Efficient organization of this knowledge in user memory is essential for enabling personalized applications. However, current research on memory writing, management, and reading using large language models (LLMs) faces challenges in filtering irrelevant information and in dealing with rising computational costs. Inspired by the concept of selective attention in the human brain, we introduce a memory discrimination task. To address large-scale interactions and diverse memory standards in this task, we propose a Scene-Aware Memory Discrimination method (SAMD), which comprises two key components: the Gating Unit Module (GUM) and the Cluster Prompting Module (CPM). GUM enhances processing efficiency by filtering out non-memorable interactions and focusing on the salient content most relevant to application demands. CPM establishes adaptive memory standards, guiding LLMs to discern what information should be remembered or discarded. It also analyzes the relationship between user intents and memory contexts to build effective clustering prompts. Comprehensive direct and indirect evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization of our approach. We independently assess the performance of memory discrimination, showing that SAMD successfully recalls the majority of memorable data and remains robust in dynamic scenarios. Furthermore, when integrated into personalized applications, SAMD significantly enhances both the efficiency and quality of memory construction, leading to better organization of personal knowledge.
Key Techniques

Gating Unit Module (GUM)
Cluster Prompting Module (CPM)
Selective Attention Filtering

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMemFly: On-the-Fly Memory Optimization via Information Bottleneck0.26Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation0.23Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation0.22LASER: An Efficient Target-Aware Segmented Attention Framework for End-to-End Long Sequence Modeling0.20Language Model Memory and Memory Models for Language0.19
Scene-Aware Memory Discrimination (SAMD)
Core Idea: A two-stage method that filters non-memorable user interactions via a lightweight gating unit and establishes adaptive memory standards through clustered prompting, enabling efficient personal knowledge organization at scale.
Problem It Solves: Addresses the dual challenge of filtering irrelevant information from large-scale user interactions and managing rising computational costs in LLM-based memory systems, while adapting to diverse memory standards across different application contexts.
How It Works:

Gating Unit Module (GUM) performs initial filtering by classifying incoming user interactions as memorable or non-memorable, discarding low-salience content before expensive LLM processing
Cluster Prompting Module (CPM) analyzes relationships between user intents and existing memory contexts to group similar interactions, then generates context-specific prompts that establish adaptive memory standards
LLM processes only the filtered, clustered interactions with tailored prompts to decide which information should be permanently stored, updated, or discarded from long-term memory

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[User Interactions] --> B[Gating Unit Module]
    B -->|Memorable| C[Cluster Prompting Module]
    B -->|Non-memorable| X[Discard]
    C --> D[Intent-Context Analysis]
    D --> E[Clustered Prompts]
    E --> F[LLM Memory Decision]
    F --> G[Personal Knowledge Base]

Where It’s Useful:

Personal assistant systems that accumulate user interaction history over time
Smart home devices managing diverse user preferences and behaviors
Personalized recommendation systems requiring efficient memory organization
Multi-turn dialogue agents maintaining long-term user context

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires initial training data to calibrate the gating unit for specific application domains
Clustering effectiveness depends on sufficient diversity in user interaction patterns
May discard edge-case memorable interactions if gating threshold is too aggressive

Internal Use Cases:

User modeling: Filter noisy behavioral signals before updating user embeddings, reducing storage and improving profile quality
Session-based RecSys: Discriminate which session interactions warrant long-term memory vs. transient context
LLM agent memory: Implement selective attention for conversational agents, storing only decision-relevant context across multi-turn interactions
Cold-start personalization: Efficiently build initial user profiles by focusing on high-signal early interactions

literature/2026-02-13-TSR-Trajectory-Sea.title: TSR: Trajectory-Search Rollouts for Multi-Turn RL of LLM Agents
literature/2026-02-13-TSR-Trajectory-Sea.content:
TSR: Trajectory-Search Rollouts for Multi-Turn RL of LLM Agents
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, RL, LLM
Abstract
Advances in large language models (LLMs) are driving a shift toward using reinforcement learning (RL) to train agents from iterative, multi-turn interactions across tasks. However, multi-turn RL remains challenging as rewards are often sparse or delayed, and environments can be stochastic. In this regime, naive trajectory sampling can hinder exploitation and induce mode collapse. We propose TSR (Trajectory-Search Rollouts), a training-time approach that repurposes test-time scaling ideas for improved per-turn rollout generation. TSR performs lightweight tree-style search to construct high-quality trajectories by selecting high-scoring actions at each turn using task-specific feedback. This improves rollout quality and stabilizes learning while leaving the underlying optimization objective unchanged, making TSR optimizer-agnostic. We instantiate TSR with best-of-N, beam, and shallow lookahead search, and pair it with PPO and GRPO, achieving up to 15% performance gains and more stable learning on Sokoban, FrozenLake, and WebShop tasks at a one-time increase in training compute. By moving search from inference time to the rollout stage of training, TSR provides a simple and general mechanism for stronger multi-turn agent learning, complementary to existing frameworks and rejection-sampling-style selection methods.
Key Techniques

Tree-search rollouts
best-of-N selection
beam search
shallow lookahead search

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.22ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.20DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.20Search or Accelerate: Confidence-Switched Position Beam Search for Diffusion Language Models0.19UnMaskFork: Test-Time Scaling for Masked Diffusion via Deterministic Action Branching0.18
TSR (Trajectory-Search Rollouts)
Core Idea: Move test-time search techniques (beam search, lookahead) into the training-time rollout generation phase to construct higher-quality trajectories for multi-turn RL of LLM agents.
Problem It Solves: Multi-turn RL suffers from sparse/delayed rewards and stochastic environments, where naive trajectory sampling leads to poor exploitation, mode collapse, and unstable learning. Standard rollout generation doesn’t leverage task-specific feedback during trajectory construction.
How It Works:

At each turn during rollout generation: Sample multiple candidate actions (N samples for best-of-N, beam width K for beam search, or depth-D lookahead)
Score candidates using task-specific feedback: Apply immediate reward signals, heuristics, or intermediate metrics to evaluate each candidate action
Select high-scoring actions: Choose best action(s) to continue trajectory construction (top-1 for best-of-N, top-K for beam, or best according to lookahead utility)
Feed resulting trajectories to standard RL optimizer: Use improved rollouts with PPO, GRPO, or any policy gradient method without modifying the optimization objective

Architecture:
flowchart TD
    A[Current State s_t] --> B[Sample N/K Candidate Actions]
    B --> C[Execute & Score with Task Feedback]
    C --> D{Selection Strategy}
    D -->|Best-of-N| E[Top-1 Action]
    D -->|Beam Search| F[Top-K Actions]
    D -->|Lookahead| G[Action with Best Future Utility]
    E --> H[Continue Trajectory]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Episode Done?}
    I -->|No| A
    I -->|Yes| J[Complete Trajectory → RL Optimizer]
    J --> K[PPO/GRPO Update]

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-turn agent tasks with sparse rewards (planning, navigation, puzzle-solving)
Sequential decision-making environments where immediate feedback is available (web navigation, code generation, interactive games)
Any RL training setup where rollout quality is a bottleneck (compatible with PPO, GRPO, or other on-policy methods)

Limitations / Caveats:

One-time compute overhead: Increases training compute by factor of N (best-of-N) or K×T (beam search), though amortized over fewer training steps
Requires task-specific feedback: Needs meaningful intermediate signals for action scoring; doesn’t help if only terminal rewards exist
Optimizer-agnostic but on-policy focused: Demonstrated with PPO/GRPO; off-policy methods may need adaptation
May over-exploit early in training: If feedback signals are noisy or misaligned, search can amplify suboptimal patterns

Internal Use Cases:

LLM agent fine-tuning: Apply to multi-turn dialogue systems, tool-use agents, or ReAct-style reasoning where we can score intermediate steps (tool success, factuality checks, retrieval relevance)
RecSys trajectory optimization: For session-based recommendation RL where we can score candidate next-items using immediate engagement signals before committing to full episode
Ranking policy learning: In contextual bandit → RL setups, use lightweight search over candidate ranking actions scored by fast proxy metrics (diversity, coverage) during rollout generation

literature/2026-02-13-Temperature-as-a-M.title: Temperature as a Meta-Policy: Adaptive Temperature in LLM Reinforcement Learning
literature/2026-02-13-Temperature-as-a-M.content:
Temperature as a Meta-Policy: Adaptive Temperature in LLM Reinforcement Learning
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #7 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RL, LLM, RAG
Abstract
Temperature is a crucial hyperparameter in large language models (LLMs), controlling the trade-off between exploration and exploitation during text generation. High temperatures encourage diverse but noisy outputs, while low temperatures produce focused outputs but may cause premature convergence. Yet static or heuristic temperature schedules fail to adapt to the dynamic demands of reinforcement learning (RL) throughout training, often limiting policy improvement. We propose Temperature Adaptive Meta Policy Optimization (TAMPO), a new framework that recasts temperature control as a learnable meta-policy. TAMPO operates through a hierarchical two-loop process. In the inner loop, the LLM policy is updated (e.g., using GRPO) with trajectories sampled at the temperature selected by the meta-policy. In the outer loop, meta-policy updates the distribution over candidate temperatures by rewarding those that maximize the likelihood of high-advantage trajectories. This trajectory-guided, reward-driven mechanism enables online adaptation without additional rollouts, directly aligning exploration with policy improvement. On five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, TAMPO outperforms baselines using fixed or heuristic temperatures, establishing temperature as an effective learnable meta-policy for adaptive exploration in LLM reinforcement learning. Accepted at ICLR 2026.
Key Techniques

Temperature Adaptive Meta Policy Optimization (TAMPO)
Hierarchical two-loop RL framework
Trajectory-guided reward mechanism for temperature selection
Meta-policy distribution over candidate temperatures

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLook Inward to Explore Outward: Learning Temperature Policy from LLM Internal States via Hierarchical RL0.63Differentiable Semantic ID for Generative Recommendation0.24DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.16DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.15Learning to Configure Agentic AI Systems0.15
Temperature Adaptive Meta Policy Optimization (TAMPO)
Core Idea: Treat temperature selection as a learnable meta-policy that adaptively chooses exploration/exploitation trade-offs during LLM reinforcement learning by rewarding temperatures that maximize the likelihood of high-advantage trajectories.
Problem It Solves: Static or heuristic temperature schedules fail to adapt to the dynamic exploration needs throughout RL training—high temperatures generate noisy exploration when exploitation is needed, while low temperatures cause premature convergence when exploration is required, limiting policy improvement.
How It Works:

Outer Loop (Meta-Policy): Maintain a distribution over candidate temperatures {τ₁, τ₂, …, τₖ} that gets updated by rewarding temperatures proportional to how often they sample trajectories with high advantage values
Inner Loop (Policy Update): Sample temperature τ from the meta-policy distribution, generate trajectories using the LLM at that temperature, compute advantages, and update the LLM policy (e.g., via GRPO)
Meta-Gradient: Update temperature distribution by increasing probability mass on temperatures that produced high-advantage trajectories, enabling trajectory-guided adaptation without additional rollouts

Architecture:
flowchart TB
    A[Meta-Policy θ_meta] -->|samples τ| B[LLM Policy π_θ]
    B -->|generates at temp τ| C[Trajectories]
    C -->|compute| D[Advantages A_t]
    D -->|GRPO/PPO| E[Update π_θ]
    D -->|reward high-A temps| F[Update θ_meta]
    F --> A

Where It’s Useful:

Mathematical reasoning tasks requiring adaptive exploration (GSM8K, MATH, ARC)
Multi-stage RL training where early vs. late training needs different exploration strategies
Complex sequential decision-making where optimal temperature varies by problem difficulty

Limitations / Caveats:

Adds meta-policy overhead and requires tuning the candidate temperature set
Benefits depend on whether the task genuinely requires varying exploration levels across training
Assumes advantage estimation is reliable enough to guide temperature selection

Internal Use Cases:

LLM ranking/reranking: Adapt temperature during online learning to balance exploration of new ranking strategies vs. exploitation of known good orderings
Prompt optimization: Use meta-policy to select generation temperature when iteratively refining prompts based on reward feedback
Recommendation diversity: Control exploration/exploitation in RL-based RecSys by learning when to show diverse (high-temp) vs. personalized (low-temp) recommendations

literature/2026-02-13-Think-like-a-Scien.title: Think like a Scientist: Physics-guided LLM Agent for Equation Discovery
literature/2026-02-13-Think-like-a-Scien.content:
Think like a Scientist: Physics-guided LLM Agent for Equation Discovery
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #4 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, RAG
Abstract
Explaining observed phenomena through symbolic, interpretable formulas is a fundamental goal of science. Recently, large language models (LLMs) have emerged as promising tools for symbolic equation discovery, owing to their broad domain knowledge and strong reasoning capabilities. However, most existing LLM-based systems try to guess equations directly from data, without modeling the multi-step reasoning process that scientists often follow: first inferring physical properties such as symmetries, then using these as priors to restrict the space of candidate equations. We introduce KeplerAgent, an agentic framework that explicitly follows this scientific reasoning process. The agent coordinates physics-based tools to extract intermediate structure and uses these results to configure symbolic regression engines such as PySINDy and PySR, including their function libraries and structural constraints. Across a suite of physical equation benchmarks, KeplerAgent achieves substantially higher symbolic accuracy and greater robustness to noisy data than both LLM and traditional baselines.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques:

Physics-based tool coordination

symmetry and property inference
symbolic regression with structural constraints
function library configuration

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityNeuroSymActive: Differentiable Neural-Symbolic Reasoning with Active Exploration for Knowledge Graph Question Answering0.17Beyond Message Passing: A Symbolic Alternative for Expressive and Interpretable Graph Learning0.15ArXiv-to-Model: A Practical Study of Scientific LM Training0.15Ontology-to-tools compilation for executable semantic constraint enforcement in LLM agents0.14Learning to Configure Agentic AI Systems0.13
KeplerAgent
Core Idea: An agentic framework that mimics scientific reasoning by first extracting physical properties (symmetries, units, conservation laws) from data using physics-based tools, then using these properties as structured priors to guide symbolic regression engines toward interpretable equations.
Problem It Solves: Existing LLM-based equation discovery systems try to guess formulas directly from data without modeling the multi-step reasoning process scientists use, leading to poor symbolic accuracy and brittleness to noise. Traditional symbolic regression lacks domain knowledge to restrict the search space effectively.
How It Works:

Property Inference Phase: Agent uses physics-based tools (symmetry detectors, dimensional analysis, conservation law checkers) to extract intermediate structural properties from observational data
Prior Configuration: Extracted properties are translated into constraints for symbolic regression engines (PySINDy, PySR) including allowed function libraries, variable transformations, and structural templates
Guided Search: Symbolic regression runs with restricted search space defined by physical priors, discovering equations that respect the inferred properties
Validation & Iteration: Agent evaluates discovered equations against data and physical consistency, refining constraints if needed

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Observational Data] --> B[Physics Tool Suite]
    B --> C[Symmetry Detection]
    B --> D[Dimensional Analysis]
    B --> E[Conservation Laws]
    C --> F[Constraint Generator]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Configured PySINDy/PySR]
    G --> H[Candidate Equations]
    H --> I[Physical Validation]
    I --> J[Final Symbolic Equation]

Where It’s Useful:

Scientific discovery from experimental physics data (mechanics, electromagnetism, fluid dynamics)
Automated model discovery in domains with known physical constraints (robotics, control systems)
Interpretable surrogate modeling where governing equations must respect conservation laws
Educational tools that demonstrate scientific reasoning processes

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires access to sufficient observational data to reliably infer properties
Physics-based tools may fail on highly noisy data or phenomena outside their domain assumptions
Performance depends on quality of symbolic regression backend and function library choices
May struggle with emergent phenomena where fundamental properties aren’t easily extractable from raw data

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys feature engineering: discover interpretable interaction formulas that respect user preference symmetries or budget constraints
Ranking model distillation: extract symbolic approximations of neural rankers with known monotonicity/dimensional properties
LLM reasoning enhancement: template for multi-step agentic workflows that extract structured priors before generation (e.g., extract code patterns before synthesis, extract schema before SQL generation)

literature/2026-02-13-Thinking-with-Draf.title: Thinking with Drafting: Optical Decompression via Logical Reconstruction
literature/2026-02-13-Thinking-with-Draf.content:
Thinking with Drafting: Optical Decompression via Logical Reconstruction
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #8 (score: 0.52, max_sim: 0.79)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Existing multimodal large language models have achieved high-fidelity visual perception and exploratory visual generation. However, a precision paradox persists in complex reasoning tasks: optical perception systems transcribe symbols without capturing logical topology, while pixel-based generative models produce visual artifacts lacking mathematical exactness. To bridge this gap, we propose that reasoning over visual inputs be reconceptualized as optical decompression-the process of reconstructing latent logical structures from compressed visual tokens. Guided by the axiom that Parsing is Reasoning, we introduce Thinking with Drafting (TwD), which utilizes a minimalist Domain-Specific Language (DSL) as a grounding intermediate representation. Unlike standard approaches that hallucinate answers directly, TwD forces the model to draft its mental model into executable code, rendering deterministic visual proofs for self-verification. To validate this, we present VisAlg, a visual algebra benchmark. Experiments demonstrate that TwD serve as a superior cognitive scaffold. Our work establishes a closed-loop system where visual generation acts not as a creative output but as a logical verifier, offering a generalizable path for visual reasoning.
Key Techniques

Optical decompression
Domain-Specific Language (DSL) grounding
Thinking with Drafting (TwD)

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDo MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.50Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs0.40VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.37ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.36SPARC: Separating Perception And Reasoning Circuits for Test-time Scaling of VLMs0.35
Thinking with Drafting (TwD)
Core Idea: Reconceptualizes visual reasoning as optical decompression by forcing models to draft their mental model into executable Domain-Specific Language (DSL) code, which then generates deterministic visual proofs for self-verification rather than directly hallucinating answers.
Problem It Solves: Addresses the precision paradox in multimodal LLMs where optical perception transcribes symbols without capturing logical topology, and pixel-based generation produces visual artifacts lacking mathematical exactness—resulting in reasoning failures on complex visual tasks requiring precise logical structure.
How It Works:

Parse Visual Input → DSL: Model translates compressed visual tokens (e.g., algebraic diagrams, geometric figures) into minimalist DSL code that explicitly represents the underlying logical structure
Execute DSL → Visual Proof: DSL code is executed deterministically to generate a visual artifact that serves as a mathematical proof or verification target
Self-Verification Loop: Model compares generated visual proof against expected output or uses it to validate reasoning steps, creating a closed-loop system where generation acts as logical verifier

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Visual Input] --> B[Multimodal LLM]
    B --> C[DSL Code Draft]
    C --> D[DSL Executor]
    D --> E[Visual Proof/Artifact]
    E --> F[Verification]
    F -->|mismatch| B
    F -->|valid| G[Final Answer]

Where It’s Useful:

Visual algebra and symbolic mathematics problems requiring exact symbolic manipulation
Geometric reasoning tasks where precise spatial relationships must be preserved
Any domain with well-defined visual-logical correspondences (circuit diagrams, molecular structures, graph theory)
Educational systems requiring explainable step-by-step visual proofs

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires well-designed DSL for each domain (not trivially generalizable across arbitrary visual reasoning tasks)
DSL execution environment must be deterministic and complete for the target domain
May struggle with ambiguous or underspecified visual inputs that don’t map cleanly to formal representations
Computational overhead from generation-verification loop

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys constraint reasoning: Draft user preference constraints as executable rules, generate candidate sets, verify coverage
LLM structured output: Force models to emit parseable intermediate representations before final answers (reduces hallucination in structured data extraction)
Ranking model explainability: Generate visual proofs of feature importance or decision boundaries for interpretable ranking

literature/2026-02-13-Towards-On-Policy-.title: Towards On-Policy SFT: Distribution Discriminant Theory and its Applications in LLM Training
literature/2026-02-13-Towards-On-Policy-.content:
Towards On-Policy SFT: Distribution Discriminant Theory and its Applications in LLM Training
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #9 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RL, LLM, RAG
Code: github.com/zhangmiaosen2000/Towards-On-Policy-SFT
Abstract
Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is computationally efficient but often yields inferior generalization compared to reinforcement learning (RL). This gap is primarily driven by RL’s use of on-policy data. We propose a framework to bridge this chasm by enabling On-Policy SFT. We first present \textbf{\textit{Distribution Discriminant Theory (DDT)}}, which explains and quantifies the alignment between data and the model-induced distribution. Leveraging DDT, we introduce two complementary techniques: (i) \textbf{\textit{In-Distribution Finetuning (IDFT)}}, a loss-level method to enhance generalization ability of SFT, and (ii) \textbf{\textit{Hinted Decoding}}, a data-level technique that can re-align the training corpus to the model’s distribution. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves generalization performance on par with prominent offline RL algorithms, including DPO and SimPO, while maintaining the efficiency of an SFT pipeline. The proposed framework thus offers a practical alternative in domains where RL is infeasible. We open-source the code here: github.com/zhangmiaosen2000/Towards-On-Policy-SFT
Key Techniques

Distribution Discriminant Theory (DDT)
In-Distribution Finetuning (IDFT)
Hinted Decoding

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySelf-Distillation Enables Continual Learning0.27Answer First, Reason Later: Aligning Search Relevance via Mode-Balanced Reinforcement Learning0.23ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.18ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.18Improving Data and Reward Design for Scientific Reasoning in Large Language Models0.17
On-Policy SFT via Distribution Discriminant Theory
Core Idea: Enable supervised fine-tuning to achieve RL-level generalization by quantifying and correcting the mismatch between training data distribution and the model’s own output distribution, without requiring expensive online sampling.
Problem It Solves: SFT is efficient but generalizes poorly because it trains on off-policy data (static datasets that don’t reflect what the model actually generates). RL methods like PPO achieve better generalization through on-policy sampling but are computationally expensive and unstable. This framework bridges that gap.
How It Works:

Distribution Discriminant Theory (DDT): Formalize the alignment between training data distribution and model-induced distribution using a discriminant score that measures how “on-policy” each training sample is relative to the current model
In-Distribution Finetuning (IDFT): Modify the SFT loss to weight samples by their DDT scores, down-weighting off-policy samples and focusing learning on data the model would naturally generate
Hinted Decoding: Generate synthetic training data by sampling from the model with token-level hints from the original corpus, creating a re-aligned dataset that matches the model’s distribution while preserving target behaviors

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Static Dataset] --> B[DDT Scoring]
    B --> C{Apply Technique}
    C -->|Loss-level| D[IDFT: Reweight Loss]
    C -->|Data-level| E[Hinted Decoding: Resample]
    D --> F[Updated Model]
    E --> G[On-Policy Dataset]
    G --> F
    F -.->|Iterate| B

Where It’s Useful:

Domains where RL is infeasible (limited compute, reward modeling is expensive, unstable training)
Offline learning scenarios with fixed datasets where you want RL-like generalization
Production LLM fine-tuning pipelines prioritizing efficiency over pure performance ceiling

Limitations / Caveats:

Still requires some form of quality signal (reward model or preference labels) to define what “good” distribution means
DDT scoring adds computational overhead compared to vanilla SFT (though much less than full RL)
Hinted Decoding requires carefully tuned hint ratios to balance distribution shift vs. preserving desired behaviors
Performance matches but doesn’t exceed offline RL methods like DPO/SimPO

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys ranking model fine-tuning where exploration is costly but you want policies aligned with production serving distribution
LLM instruction-following when PPO/RLHF infrastructure is unavailable but you have preference data
Continuous learning scenarios where you want to adapt to model’s evolving distribution without full RL retraining cycles

literature/2026-02-13-Towards-Personaliz.title: Towards Personalized Bangla Book Recommendation: A Large-Scale Multi-Entity Book Graph Dataset
literature/2026-02-13-Towards-Personaliz.content:
Towards Personalized Bangla Book Recommendation: A Large-Scale Multi-Entity Book Graph Dataset
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #11 (score: 0.70, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Personalized book recommendation in Bangla literature has been constrained by the lack of structured, large-scale, and publicly available datasets. This work introduces RokomariBG, a large-scale, multi-entity heterogeneous book graph dataset designed to support research on personalized recommendation in a low-resource language setting. The dataset comprises 127,302 books, 63,723 users, 16,601 authors, 1,515 categories, 2,757 publishers, and 209,602 reviews, connected through eight relation types and organized as a comprehensive knowledge graph.   To demonstrate the utility of the dataset, we provide a systematic benchmarking study on the Top-N recommendation task, evaluating a diverse set of representative recommendation models, including classical collaborative filtering methods, matrix factorization models, content-based approaches, graph neural networks, a hybrid matrix factorization model with side information, and a neural two-tower retrieval architecture. The benchmarking results highlight the importance of leveraging multi-relational structure and textual side information, with neural retrieval models achieving the strongest performance (NDCG@10 = 0.204). Overall, this work establishes a foundational benchmark and a publicly available resource for Bangla book recommendation research, enabling reproducible evaluation and future studies on recommendation in low-resource cultural domains. The dataset and code are publicly available at github.com/backlashblitz/Bangla-Book-Recommendation-Dataset
Key Techniques

Collaborative filtering
Matrix factorization
Graph neural networks
Neural two-tower retrieval

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs0.25One Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral Profiles0.21Unified Multi-Domain Graph Pre-training for Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Graphs via Domain-Specific Expert Encoding0.21Exploring the impact of adaptive rewiring in Graph Neural Networks0.20Idea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives0.19
RokomariBG: Multi-Entity Heterogeneous Book Graph for Low-Resource Recommendations
Core Idea: A large-scale heterogeneous knowledge graph dataset for Bangla book recommendations that connects books, users, authors, categories, publishers, and reviews through eight relation types to enable graph-based and hybrid recommendation models in a low-resource language setting.
Problem It Solves: Personalized book recommendation in Bangla literature has been severely limited by the lack of structured, large-scale, publicly available datasets. Most recommendation research focuses on high-resource languages (English), leaving low-resource cultural domains without proper benchmarks or training data for modern neural and graph-based models.
How It Works:

Data Collection: Aggregate 127,302 books, 63,723 users, 16,601 authors, 1,515 categories, 2,757 publishers, and 209,602 reviews from the Rokomari platform (major Bangla book retailer)
Graph Construction: Structure entities and their interactions into a heterogeneous knowledge graph with eight relation types (e.g., user-rates-book, book-has-author, book-belongs-to-category, book-published-by-publisher)
Multi-Modal Features: Incorporate textual side information (book titles, descriptions, review text) alongside relational structure
Benchmarking: Provide systematic evaluation of collaborative filtering, matrix factorization, content-based, graph neural networks (GNN), hybrid models, and neural two-tower retrieval architectures on Top-N recommendation task

Architecture:
flowchart TB
    U[Users<br/>63,723] -->|rates| B[Books<br/>127,302]
    A[Authors<br/>16,601] -->|wrote| B
    B -->|belongs to| C[Categories<br/>1,515]
    B -->|published by| P[Publishers<br/>2,757]
    R[Reviews<br/>209,602] -->|about| B
    U -->|wrote| R
    B -->|text features| T[Titles, Descriptions]
    
    subgraph KG [Heterogeneous Book Graph]
        U
        B
        A
        C
        P
        R
        T
    end

Where It’s Useful:

Low-resource language recommendation systems (cultural/regional content platforms)
Graph neural network research for heterogeneous recommendation graphs
Hybrid recommendation architectures that combine collaborative filtering with content features
Cross-lingual recommendation research and domain adaptation studies
Knowledge graph-based recommendation in specialized domains (books, academic papers, cultural products)

Limitations / Caveats:

Single-domain (Bangla literature only) - cross-domain generalization unclear
Language-specific textual features require Bangla NLP models; many pre-trained models are English-centric
Best model (neural two-tower retrieval) achieves NDCG@10 = 0.204, showing significant room for improvement
Dataset from single source (Rokomari platform) may have platform-specific biases
Review sparsity and cold-start problems still challenging despite large scale

Internal Use Cases:

Template for building multi-entity recommendation graphs in specialized domains (e.g., research papers with authors, venues, citations, topics)
Benchmarking framework for evaluating how well GNNs leverage heterogeneous relations vs. simpler collaborative filtering
Testing cross-lingual recommendation approaches: train on English book data, adapt to Bangla using this dataset
Hybrid retrieval architecture evaluation: comparing two-tower neural models against graph-based and matrix factorization baselines
Side information integration patterns: measuring impact of textual features (titles, reviews) on graph-based recommendations

literature/2026-02-13-Where-Bits-Matter-.title: Where Bits Matter in World Model Planning: A Paired Mixed-Bit Study for Efficient Spatial Reasoning
literature/2026-02-13-Where-Bits-Matter-.content:
Where Bits Matter in World Model Planning: A Paired Mixed-Bit Study for Efficient Spatial Reasoning
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #12 (score: 0.60, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Efficient spatial reasoning requires world models that remain reliable under tight precision budgets. We study whether low-bit planning behavior is determined mostly by total bitwidth or by where bits are allocated across modules. Using DINO-WM on the Wall planning task, we run a paired-goal mixed-bit evaluation across uniform, mixed, asymmetric, and layerwise variants under two planner budgets. We observe a consistent three-regime pattern: 8-bit and 6-bit settings remain close to FP16, 3-bit settings collapse, and 4-bit settings are allocation-sensitive. In that transition region, preserving encoder precision improves planning relative to uniform quantization, and near-size asymmetric variants show the same encoder-side direction. In a later strict 22-cell replication with smaller per-cell episode count, the mixed-versus-uniform INT4 sign becomes budget-conditioned, which further highlights the sensitivity of this transition regime. These findings motivate module-aware, budget-aware quantization policies as a broader research direction for efficient spatial reasoning. Code and run artifacts are available at github.com/suraj-ranganath/DINO-MBQuant.
Key Techniques

Mixed-bit quantization
Encoder precision preservation
Budget-aware allocation

Related Litnotes
