NoteSimilarityBPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.25Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems0.22Unleashing Low-Bit Inference on Ascend NPUs: A Comprehensive Evaluation of HiFloat Formats0.20QuEPT: Quantized Elastic Precision Transformers with One-Shot Calibration for Multi-Bit Switching0.19BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.18
Mixed-Bit Module-Aware Quantization for World Models
Core Idea: Allocate quantization bits strategically across world model modules (encoder, dynamics, decoder) rather than uniformly, preserving encoder precision to maintain spatial reasoning quality in the 4-bit transition regime.
Problem It Solves: Uniform low-bit quantization (e.g., INT4) causes catastrophic collapse in world model planning performance, but blindly increasing bits everywhere is computationally wasteful. The question is where to spend limited precision budget for maximum planning reliability.
How It Works:

Identify transition regime: Determine the bitwidth boundary where uniform quantization becomes unstable (3-4 bits for DINO-WM on spatial tasks)
Asymmetric allocation: Within fixed budget, prioritize encoder precision over dynamics/decoder (e.g., ENC6-DYN4-DEC2 vs uniform INT4)
Budget-conditioned evaluation: Test mixed configurations across multiple planner budgets and goal densities to validate allocation policy holds

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Observation] --> B[Encoder<br/>6-bit]
    B --> C[Latent State]
    C --> D[Dynamics<br/>4-bit]
    D --> E[Next Latent]
    E --> F[Decoder<br/>2-bit]
    F --> G[Reconstructed State]
    
    style B fill:#90EE90
    style D fill:#FFD700
    style F fill:#FFA07A

Where It’s Useful:

Real-time robotics planning under memory/compute constraints (embedded vision-based navigation)
Mobile AR applications requiring on-device spatial reasoning
Large-scale multi-agent simulations where per-agent model size must be minimized
Edge deployment of model-based RL agents

Limitations / Caveats:

Transition regime bitwidth is task/architecture-dependent (DINO-WM on Wall shows 4-bit sensitivity, other models may differ)
Budget-conditioned behavior: optimal allocation changes with planner search budget and goal density
Requires paired-goal evaluation protocol to detect allocation effects reliably
No theoretical framework yet for predicting which modules matter most

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys: Quantize user/item encoders differently than interaction dynamics in sequential recommenders
Ranking: Preserve query encoder precision while compressing document tower in dual-encoder retrieval
LLM: Asymmetric KV-cache quantization where key precision > value precision for attention stability
Multimodal models: Allocate more bits to vision encoder than text decoder when visual grounding is critical

literature/2026-02-14-Dissecting-Subject.title: Dissecting Subjectivity and the"Ground Truth"Illusion in Data Annotation
literature/2026-02-14-Dissecting-Subject.content:
Dissecting Subjectivity and the”Ground Truth”Illusion in Data Annotation
Source: S2:NeurIPS
Published: 2026
Rank: #3 (score: 0.28, max_sim: 0.76)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search
Abstract
In machine learning,“ground truth”refers to the assumed correct labels used to train and evaluate models. However, the foundational”ground truth”paradigm rests on a positivistic fallacy that treats human disagreement as technical noise rather than a vital sociotechnical signal. This systematic literature review analyzes research published between 2020 and 2025 across seven premier venues: ACL, AIES, CHI, CSCW, EAAMO, FAccT, and NeurIPS, investigating the mechanisms in data annotation practices that facilitate this”consensus trap”. Our identification phase captured 30,897 records, which were refined via a tiered keyword filtration schema to a high-recall corpus of 3,042 records for manual screening, resulting in a final included corpus of 346 papers for qualitative synthesis. Our reflexive thematic analysis reveals that systemic failures in positional legibility, combined with the recent architectural shift toward human-as-verifier models, specifically the reliance on model-mediated annotations, introduce deep-seated anchoring bias and effectively remove human voices from the loop. We further demonstrate how geographic hegemony imposes Western norms as universal benchmarks, often enforced by the performative alignment of precarious data workers who prioritize requester compliance over honest subjectivity to avoid economic penalties. Critiquing the”noisy sensor”fallacy, where statistical models misdiagnose cultural pluralism as random error, we argue for reclaiming disagreement as a high-fidelity signal essential for building culturally competent models. To address these systemic tensions, we propose a roadmap for pluralistic annotation infrastructures that shift the objective from discovering a singular”right”answer to mapping the diversity of human experience.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques or methods:

Tiered keyword filtration schema

reflexive thematic analysis
qualitative synthesis

NoteSimilarityToward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning0.17Toward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning0.16Toward Culturally Aligned LLMs through Ontology-Guided Multi-Agent Reasoning0.15Constrained Process Maps for Multi-Agent Generative AI Workflows0.14ToxiTwitch: Toward Emote-Aware Hybrid Moderation for Live Streaming Platforms0.14
Pluralistic Annotation Infrastructure
Core Idea: Replace consensus-seeking annotation pipelines with systems that explicitly capture, preserve, and model legitimate disagreement as a signal of cultural and perspectival diversity rather than noise to be resolved.
Problem It Solves: Traditional “ground truth” paradigms treat annotator disagreement as random error to be minimized through majority voting or expert adjudication, systematically erasing minority perspectives and cultural variation. This creates models that enforce dominant viewpoints while failing to recognize valid subjective differences in tasks like hate speech detection, toxicity labeling, or content moderation.
How It Works:

Disaggregated Collection: Gather annotations from diverse annotators without forcing consensus; preserve individual labels and demographic/positionality metadata
Positional Legibility: Make annotators’ social positions, cultural backgrounds, and lived experiences visible and queryable as structured context
Distribution Modeling: Train models to predict the full distribution of human judgments rather than a single “correct” label, representing disagreement patterns as first-class outputs
Human-in-the-Loop Verification: Use humans to validate model outputs against the diversity of perspectives, not just against majority-voted labels

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Diverse Annotators] --> B[Preserve Individual Labels]
    B --> C[Positionality Metadata]
    C --> D[Distribution Model]
    D --> E[Multiple Valid Outputs]
    E --> F[Human Verification]
    F --> D

Where It’s Useful:

Subjective NLP tasks (hate speech, toxicity, sentiment, offensiveness detection)
Content moderation systems operating across cultural contexts
Medical diagnosis where multiple valid interpretations exist
Any domain where “correctness” depends on perspective or lived experience

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires significantly more annotation budget to collect sufficient samples per perspective
Increases model complexity (predicting distributions vs. single labels)
Demands transparent positionality collection, which may raise privacy concerns or feel invasive to annotators
Does not resolve how to make final decisions in production systems (still need policy choices about which perspectives to prioritize)

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys: Model diverse user preferences as legitimate variation rather than noise; avoid collapsing to dominant taste profiles
Content Ranking: Surface multiple perspectives on controversial content instead of single “authoritative” view
LLM Alignment: Train on disagreement distributions for subjective prompts (e.g., “Is this offensive?”) to avoid imposing single cultural norm; enable models to acknowledge uncertainty and plural viewpoints

literature/2026-02-14-MemFly-On-the-Fly-.title: MemFly: On-the-Fly Memory Optimization via Information Bottleneck
literature/2026-02-14-MemFly-On-the-Fly-.content:
MemFly: On-the-Fly Memory Optimization via Information Bottleneck
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-08
Rank: #2 (score: 0.86, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RecSys, LLM, RAG
Abstract
Long-term memory enables large language model agents to tackle complex tasks through historical interactions. However, existing frameworks encounter a fundamental dilemma between compressing redundant information efficiently and maintaining precise retrieval for downstream tasks. To bridge this gap, we propose MemFly, a framework grounded in information bottleneck principles that facilitates on-the-fly memory evolution for LLMs. Our approach minimizes compression entropy while maximizing relevance entropy via a gradient-free optimizer, constructing a stratified memory structure for efficient storage. To fully leverage MemFly, we develop a hybrid retrieval mechanism that seamlessly integrates semantic, symbolic, and topological pathways, incorporating iterative refinement to handle complex multi-hop queries. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that MemFly substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in memory coherence, response fidelity, and accuracy.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques:

Information bottleneck principles

gradient-free optimizer
stratified memory structure
hybrid retrieval mechanism

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityScene-Aware Memory Discrimination: Deciding Which Personal Knowledge Stays0.26Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation0.23Language Model Memory and Memory Models for Language0.21Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation0.21ER-MIA: Black-Box Adversarial Memory Injection Attacks on Long-Term Memory-Augmented Large Language Models0.20
MemFly (Memory-on-the-Fly)
Core Idea: Applies information bottleneck principles to dynamically compress LLM agent memory by minimizing compression entropy while maximizing task-relevant entropy, creating a stratified memory structure with hybrid semantic-symbolic-topological retrieval.
Problem It Solves: Existing LLM memory frameworks struggle to balance efficient compression of redundant historical interactions with maintaining precise retrieval for complex downstream tasks, leading to either bloated context windows or loss of critical information.
How It Works:

On-the-fly Compression: Apply gradient-free optimizer to compress incoming interactions, minimizing mutual information between raw history and compressed representation while preserving task-relevant signals
Stratified Memory Construction: Organize compressed memories into hierarchical layers (sensory/short-term/long-term) based on information bottleneck scores and temporal decay
Hybrid Retrieval: Query memory via three parallel pathways:

Semantic: Vector similarity search on embeddings
Symbolic: Keyword/entity-based retrieval
Topological: Graph-based multi-hop traversal for relational queries


Iterative Refinement: Recursively expand retrieved memories for complex multi-hop questions until information gain plateaus

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Interactions] --> B[Information Bottleneck<br/>Compressor]
    B --> C[Stratified Memory<br/>Short/Long-term]
    D[Query] --> E[Hybrid Retriever]
    E --> F[Semantic Path]
    E --> G[Symbolic Path]
    E --> H[Topological Path]
    C --> F
    C --> G
    C --> H
    F --> I[Iterative Refinement]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Relevant Context]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-horizon agentic tasks requiring coherent memory over hundreds of interactions (customer support, personal assistants)
Multi-session dialogue systems needing efficient storage with precise recall
Knowledge-intensive applications where retrieval accuracy directly impacts task performance

Limitations / Caveats:

Gradient-free optimization may be computationally expensive for very high-frequency memory updates
Information bottleneck requires tuning the compression-relevance tradeoff β parameter per domain
Topological retrieval assumes knowledge can be graphically structured (may not suit all memory types)

Internal Use Cases:

Session-based RecSys: Compress long user browsing histories while preserving signals predictive of next action
Conversational search: Maintain multi-turn context without repeatedly re-encoding entire dialogue history
LLM agent workflows: Enable agents to reference distant interactions without hitting context limits, improving consistency in long research/coding tasks

Production-Scale LLM Ranking for Semantic Search
Core Idea: Deploy large language models as rerankers in production search systems by using a two-stage retrieval architecture where fast embedding-based retrieval narrows candidates before LLM-based scoring.
Problem It Solves: Traditional semantic search relies on embedding similarity (e.g., cosine distance) which captures topical relevance but fails to model nuanced ranking signals like query intent, freshness, personalization, and multi-faceted relevance that require reasoning over multiple signals simultaneously.
How It Works:

Fast Retrieval Stage: Use embedding-based retrieval (e.g., dense vectors, ANN search) to retrieve top-k candidates (typically 100-1000) from the full corpus in milliseconds
LLM Reranking Stage: Feed query and candidate documents into an LLM (fine-tuned for ranking) that outputs relevance scores by reasoning over query semantics, document content, and metadata
Inference Optimization: Apply model compression (quantization, distillation), batching, and hardware optimization (GPU serving) to meet production latency budgets (p99 < 100ms)
Online A/B Testing: Gradually ramp LLM-based ranking with feature flags while monitoring quality metrics (NDCG, MRR) and infrastructure costs

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[User Query] --> B[Embedding Retrieval]
    B --> C[Top-k Candidates]
    C --> D[LLM Reranker]
    D --> E[Ranked Results]
    F[Query Context] --> D
    G[User Features] --> D

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale search systems (LinkedIn Jobs, Google Search, e-commerce product search)
Conversational search where query intent is ambiguous
Domain-specific search requiring specialized ranking logic (legal, medical, enterprise)

Limitations / Caveats:

Inference cost scales linearly with candidate set size—requires careful tuning of retrieval stage recall
LLM rerankers need continuous retraining to avoid distribution shift as user behavior and content evolve
Latency constraints may require distillation or switching to smaller models, sacrificing quality

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys reranking: Use LLM to score top-k candidates from collaborative filtering/embedding retrieval
Multi-objective ranking: Train LLM to balance engagement, diversity, and business metrics in a unified model
Cold-start recommendations: LLM can reason over item metadata when interaction data is sparse

NoteSimilarityLLMs as the new high level language0.15SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.14Software factories and the agentic moment0.13Empowering Medical Equipment Sustainability in Low-Resource Settings: An AI-Powered Diagnostic and Support Platform for Biomedical Technicians0.12AgentStepper: Interactive Debugging of Software Development Agents0.12
Amsterdam Compiler Kit (ACK)
Core Idea: A portable, retargetable compiler infrastructure using an intermediate representation (EM bytecode) that decouples front-ends (language parsers) from back-ends (target machine code generators), enabling write-once compile-anywhere toolchains.
Problem It Solves:

N×M compiler problem: Building compilers for N languages and M architectures traditionally requires N×M implementations
Code portability: Programs written once should run on diverse hardware without rewriting
Compiler construction overhead: Writing machine-specific optimizers and code generators for each language/platform pair

How It Works:

Front-end compilation: Language-specific front-ends (C, Pascal, Modula-2, etc.) compile source to EM (Encoding Machine) bytecode, a stack-based intermediate representation
EM bytecode optimization: Platform-independent peephole optimizer applies pattern-based transformations to the intermediate code
Back-end code generation: Target-specific back-ends translate optimized EM bytecode to native assembly for architectures (6502, 68000, i386, etc.)
Linking and runtime: ACK’s linker and runtime libraries provide platform-specific support (I/O, memory management)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[C/Pascal/Modula-2 Source] --> B[Language Front-end]
    B --> C[EM Bytecode]
    C --> D[Peephole Optimizer]
    D --> E[Platform Back-end]
    E --> F[6502/68k/i386/etc Assembly]
    F --> G[Linker + Runtime]
    G --> H[Executable]

Where It’s Useful:

Cross-platform development: Building software for embedded systems, microcomputers, and diverse CPU architectures from a single codebase
Language experimentation: Adding new languages requires only implementing a front-end to EM, not per-platform code generators
Educational compiler construction: Modular design separates concerns (parsing vs. code generation)
Resource-constrained systems: Targets include 8-bit and 16-bit processors where hand-optimized compilers were previously required

Limitations / Caveats:

Stack-based IR overhead: EM’s stack machine model may not efficiently map to register-based architectures without sophisticated back-end optimizations
Limited global optimization: Peephole optimization works on local instruction patterns; lacks modern whole-program analysis (inlining, dead code elimination across modules)
Portability vs. performance trade-off: Generated code may be slower than hand-tuned native compilers due to IR abstraction layer
Maintenance burden: Supporting legacy architectures (6502, Z80) became less relevant as hardware evolved

Internal Use Cases:

Multi-target model deployment: Similar architecture could compile ML models (PyTorch, TensorFlow) to an intermediate representation, then generate optimized code for CPUs, GPUs, TPUs, edge devices—reducing engineering overhead for supporting new hardware
Ranking model portability: Compile ranking/RecSys models to an IR, then deploy to web (WASM), mobile (ARM), and server (x86) without rewriting inference logic
LLM inference optimization: Front-end parses model definitions, IR represents compute graphs, back-ends generate optimized kernels for CUDA, Metal, CPU SIMD—ACK’s peephole optimizer analogy applies to graph-level fusion passes

literature/2026-02-16-BPDQ-Bit-Plane-Dec.title: BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models
literature/2026-02-16-BPDQ-Bit-Plane-Dec.content:
BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-04
Rank: #7 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Large language model (LLM) inference is often bounded by memory footprint and memory bandwidth in resource-constrained deployments, making quantization a fundamental technique for efficient serving. While post-training quantization (PTQ) maintains high fidelity at 4-bit, it deteriorates at 2-3 bits. Fundamentally, existing methods enforce a shape-invariant quantization grid (e.g., the fixed uniform intervals of UINT2) for each group, severely restricting the feasible set for error minimization. To address this, we propose Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization (BPDQ), which constructs a variable quantization grid via bit-planes and scalar coefficients, and iteratively refines them using approximate second-order information while progressively compensating quantization errors to minimize output discrepancy. In the 2-bit regime, BPDQ enables serving Qwen2.5-72B on a single RTX 3090 with 83.85% GSM8K accuracy (vs. 90.83% at 16-bit). Moreover, we provide theoretical analysis showing that the variable grid expands the feasible set, and that the quantization process consistently aligns with the optimization objective in Hessian-induced geometry. Code: github.com/KingdalfGoodman/BPDQ.
Key Techniques

Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization (BPDQ)
Variable Quantization Grid Construction
Iterative Refinement with Second-Order Information

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.92OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization0.291-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization0.26Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems0.24Quant VideoGen: Auto-Regressive Long Video Generation via 2-Bit KV-Cache Quantization0.22
Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization (BPDQ)
Core Idea: Construct a variable quantization grid using bit-plane decomposition and scalar coefficients, iteratively refined with second-order information to minimize quantization error, rather than enforcing fixed uniform intervals.
Problem It Solves: Post-training quantization (PTQ) methods deteriorate at 2-3 bits because fixed quantization grids (e.g., UINT2 uniform intervals) severely restrict the feasible set for error minimization, limiting memory-bandwidth-constrained LLM deployments.
How It Works:

Bit-plane decomposition: Decompose weight matrix into multiple bit-planes with scalar coefficients, creating a variable quantization grid instead of fixed uniform intervals
Iterative refinement: Use approximate second-order (Hessian) information to optimize bit-plane coefficients and grid positioning
Progressive error compensation: Iteratively compensate quantization errors across layers to minimize output discrepancy from original model
Group-level optimization: Apply variable grid per weight group, expanding feasible solution space while maintaining hardware efficiency

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[FP16 Weights] --> B[Bit-Plane Decomposition]
    B --> C[Variable Grid Construction]
    C --> D[Hessian-Guided Refinement]
    D --> E[Error Compensation]
    E --> F[2-3 bit Quantized Model]
    D -.->|Iterate| C

Where It’s Useful:

Extremely memory-constrained LLM inference (e.g., serving 72B models on consumer GPUs with 24GB VRAM)
Edge deployment requiring 2-3 bit quantization with minimal accuracy degradation
High-throughput serving where memory bandwidth is the primary bottleneck

Limitations / Caveats:

Increased PTQ complexity due to iterative Hessian-based refinement (longer quantization time)
Requires custom kernels or runtime support for variable grid decoding, not directly compatible with standard integer operations
Benefits diminish at 4-bit and above where uniform grids already achieve high fidelity

Internal Use Cases:

Deploying large embedding models or rankers in memory-constrained production environments
Enabling sub-4-bit quantization for recommendation model serving without major accuracy drops
Applying variable-grid principles to activation quantization in KV-cache compression for long-context LLM inference

literature/2026-02-16-Closing-the-Loop-A.title: Closing the Loop: A Control-Theoretic Framework for Provably Stable Time Series Forecasting with LLMs
literature/2026-02-16-Closing-the-Loop-A.content:
Closing the Loop: A Control-Theoretic Framework for Provably Stable Time Series Forecasting with LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #5 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently shown exceptional potential in time series forecasting, leveraging their inherent sequential reasoning capabilities to model complex temporal dynamics. However, existing approaches typically employ a naive autoregressive generation strategy. We identify a critical theoretical flaw in this paradigm: during inference, the model operates in an open-loop manner, consuming its own generated outputs recursively. This leads to inevitable error accumulation (exposure bias), where minor early deviations cascade into significant trajectory drift over long horizons. In this paper, we reformulate autoregressive forecasting through the lens of control theory, proposing \textbf{F-LLM} (Feedback-driven LLM), a novel closed-loop framework. Unlike standard methods that passively propagate errors, F-LLM actively stabilizes the trajectory via a learnable residual estimator (Observer) and a feedback controller. Furthermore, we provide a theoretical guarantee that our closed-loop mechanism ensures uniformly bounded error, provided the base model satisfies a local Lipschitz constraint. Extensive experiments demonstrate that F-LLM significantly mitigates error propagation, achieving good performance on time series benchmarks.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques or methods:

Closed-loop feedback control mechanism

Learnable residual estimator (Observer)
Feedback controller
Lipschitz constraint-based error bounding

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityGenerative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.23Dual-Prototype Disentanglement: A Context-Aware Enhancement Framework for Time Series Forecasting0.20Conformal Prediction Algorithms for Time Series Forecasting: Methods and Benchmark0.20Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.19Generative Chain of Behavior for User Trajectory Prediction0.19
F-LLM (Feedback-driven LLM)
Core Idea: A closed-loop control-theoretic framework that stabilizes autoregressive time series forecasting by actively correcting prediction drift through a learnable residual estimator (Observer) and feedback controller.
Problem It Solves: Addresses error accumulation (exposure bias) in standard autoregressive LLM forecasting where models operate open-loop, recursively consuming their own predictions and causing minor early deviations to cascade into significant trajectory drift over long horizons.
How It Works:

Base Forecasting: LLM generates initial autoregressive predictions for the time series
Observer Module: A learnable residual estimator monitors the gap between predicted trajectory and ideal stable trajectory, computing an error signal
Feedback Controller: Applies corrective adjustments to the predicted trajectory based on the Observer’s error estimate, actively stabilizing the forecast
Closed-Loop Iteration: The corrected predictions are fed back as context for subsequent timesteps, forming a stable feedback loop

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Time Series Input] --> B[LLM Base Model]
    B --> C[Predicted Trajectory]
    C --> D[Observer/Residual Estimator]
    D --> E[Error Signal]
    E --> F[Feedback Controller]
    F --> G[Corrected Predictions]
    G --> B
    G --> H[Final Forecast]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-horizon time series forecasting where error accumulation is critical (financial markets, weather prediction)
Autoregressive generation tasks requiring trajectory stability (multi-step planning, sequential decision making)
Any sequential prediction task with compounding errors in open-loop systems

Limitations / Caveats:

Theoretical stability guarantee requires base LLM to satisfy local Lipschitz constraint (may not hold for all architectures)
Additional computational overhead from Observer and Controller modules
Requires training the feedback components, adding complexity to the pipeline

Internal Use Cases:

Multi-step user engagement forecasting in RecSys where small early prediction errors compound over session trajectories
Sequential content recommendation where autoregressive ranking models suffer from exposure bias
LLM-based trajectory prediction for user behavior modeling where closed-loop correction could stabilize long-term predictions

literature/2026-02-16-FlashSchNet-Fast-a.title: FlashSchNet: Fast and Accurate Coarse-Grained Neural Network Molecular Dynamics
literature/2026-02-16-FlashSchNet-Fast-a.content:
FlashSchNet: Fast and Accurate Coarse-Grained Neural Network Molecular Dynamics
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #12 (score: 0.26, max_sim: 0.76)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Graph neural network (GNN) potentials such as SchNet improve the accuracy and transferability of molecular dynamics (MD) simulation by learning many-body interactions, but remain slower than classical force fields due to fragmented kernels and memory-bound pipelines that underutilize GPUs. We show that a missing principle is making GNN-MD IO-aware, carefully accounting for reads and writes between GPU high-bandwidth memory (HBM) and on-chip SRAM. We present FlashSchNet, an efficient and accurate IO-aware SchNet-style GNN-MD framework built on four techniques: (1) flash radial basis, which fuses pairwise distance computation, Gaussian basis expansion, and cosine envelope into a single tiled pass, computing each distance once and reusing it across all basis functions; (2) flash message passing, which fuses cutoff, neighbor gather, filter multiplication, and reduction to avoid materializing edge tensors in HBM; (3) flash aggregation, which reformulates scatter-add via CSR segment reduce, reducing atomic writes by a factor of feature dimension and enabling contention-free accumulation in both forward and backward passes; (4) channel-wise 16-bit quantization that exploits the low per-channel dynamic range in SchNet MLP weights to further improve throughput with negligible accuracy loss. On a single NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000, FlashSchNet achieves 1000 ns/day aggregate simulation throughput over 64 parallel replicas on coarse-grained (CG) protein containing 269 beads (6.5x faster than CGSchNet baseline with 80% reduction of peak memory), surpassing classical force fields (e.g. MARTINI) while retaining SchNet-level accuracy and transferability.
Key Techniques

Flash radial basis
flash message passing
flash aggregation
channel-wise 16-bit quantization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDynamiQ: Accelerating Gradient Synchronization using Compressed Multi-hop All-reduce0.19Deep Kernel Fusion for Transformers0.17Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems0.13Quartet II: Accurate LLM Pre-Training in NVFP4 by Improved Unbiased Gradient Estimation0.13One Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral Profiles0.12
FlashSchNet: IO-Aware GNN Molecular Dynamics
Core Idea: Accelerate graph neural network molecular dynamics by redesigning SchNet operations to minimize GPU memory transfers through kernel fusion and CSR-based aggregation.
Problem It Solves: GNN-based molecular dynamics potentials (like SchNet) are orders of magnitude slower than classical force fields because they fragment computation into many small kernels that repeatedly read/write intermediate tensors to GPU high-bandwidth memory (HBM), creating a memory-bound bottleneck that underutilizes GPU compute.
How It Works:

Flash Radial Basis: Fuse pairwise distance calculation, Gaussian basis expansion, and cosine cutoff envelope into a single tiled kernel that computes each distance once and reuses it across all basis functions in SRAM
Flash Message Passing: Fuse neighbor gathering, cutoff filtering, edge feature multiplication, and aggregation into one kernel to avoid materializing large edge tensors (N×K×F) in HBM
Flash Aggregation: Reformulate scatter-add as CSR segment reduction, reducing atomic writes by a factor of feature dimension F and enabling contention-free parallel accumulation in both forward/backward passes
Channel-wise Quantization: Apply 16-bit quantization to SchNet MLP weights exploiting low per-channel dynamic range to further boost throughput with <1% accuracy loss

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Atom Positions] --> B[Flash Radial Basis<br/>fused: distance + basis + cutoff]
    B --> C[Flash Message Passing<br/>fused: gather + filter + reduce]
    C --> D[Flash Aggregation<br/>CSR segment reduce]
    D --> E[Quantized MLP<br/>16-bit channel-wise]
    E --> F[Forces/Energy]

Where It’s Useful:

Coarse-grained protein molecular dynamics (269-bead systems achieving 1000 ns/day on single GPU)
Any GNN-based physical simulation requiring high throughput (materials, drug discovery)
Applications needing classical force field speed with ML accuracy/transferability
Multi-replica MD workflows (parallel tempering, ensemble sampling)

Limitations / Caveats:

Optimization focused on SchNet architecture; other GNN variants (e.g., DimeNet++, GemNet) may need different fusion strategies
CSR aggregation requires neighbor list pre-sorting by destination atom
Quantization benefits depend on weight distribution; some GNN layers may have higher dynamic range
Achieves parity with classical force fields only on coarse-grained systems (269 beads); all-atom scaling not demonstrated

Internal Use Cases:

GPU kernel fusion patterns: Same principle applies to fusing embedding lookup + MLP in recommendation models to avoid materializing user/item features
Scatter-gather optimization: CSR-based aggregation technique directly applicable to graph-based ranking (e.g., knowledge graph embeddings, GraphSAGE for user-item graphs)
Quantization strategy: Per-channel 16-bit quantization can accelerate LLM inference in ranking rerankers while maintaining accuracy
Memory-compute tradeoff: Flash attention pattern (compute on-the-fly vs materialize) applies to large-batch scoring in retrieval pipelines

literature/2026-02-16-LCSB-Layer-Cyclic-.title: LCSB: Layer-Cyclic Selective Backpropagation for Memory-Efficient On-Device LLM Fine-Tuning
literature/2026-02-16-LCSB-Layer-Cyclic-.content:
LCSB: Layer-Cyclic Selective Backpropagation for Memory-Efficient On-Device LLM Fine-Tuning
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, Transformer
Abstract
Memory-efficient backpropagation (MeBP) has enabled first-order fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) on mobile devices with less than 1GB memory. However, MeBP requires backward computation through all transformer layers at every step, where weight decompression alone accounts for 32—42% of backward time. We propose Layer-Cyclic Selective Backpropagation (LCSB), which computes gradients for only a subset of layers per step. Our key insight is that residual connections guarantee gradient flow through identity paths, while AdamW momentum provides implicit updates for non-selected layers. We interpret LCSB as Block Coordinate Descent on the LoRA parameter space, providing theoretical justification for convergence. LCSB achieves up to 1.40\times speedup with less than 2% quality degradation across five models and three tasks. Surprisingly, in 4-bit quantized settings, LCSB exhibits superior stability: a 3B model that completely diverges under full backpropagation converges smoothly with LCSB, suggesting an implicit regularization effect from selective gradient computation.
Key Techniques

Layer-Cyclic Selective Backpropagation (LCSB)
Residual connections for gradient flow
AdamW momentum updates
Block Coordinate Descent optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityQuantized Evolution Strategies: High-precision Fine-tuning of Quantized LLMs at Low-precision Cost0.23On Surprising Effectiveness of Masking Updates in Adaptive Optimizers0.20Quartet II: Accurate LLM Pre-Training in NVFP4 by Improved Unbiased Gradient Estimation0.19The Fine-Grained Complexity of Gradient Computation for Training Large Language Models0.17SpanNorm: Reconciling Training Stability and Performance in Deep Transformers0.17
Layer-Cyclic Selective Backpropagation (LCSB)
Core Idea: Compute gradients for only a subset of transformer layers per training step while relying on residual connections for gradient flow and AdamW momentum for implicit updates to non-selected layers.
Problem It Solves: Memory-efficient backpropagation (MeBP) enables on-device LLM fine-tuning but still requires backward computation through all layers at every step, with weight decompression consuming 32-42% of backward time—a critical bottleneck for mobile devices with <1GB memory.
How It Works:

Layer selection: At each training step, select only a cyclic subset of transformer layers for gradient computation (e.g., layers 0-7 at step 1, layers 8-15 at step 2, etc.)
Selective backpropagation: Compute gradients and update LoRA parameters only for selected layers; skip gradient computation for non-selected layers entirely
Gradient flow preservation: Residual connections provide identity paths that allow gradients to flow through non-selected layers without explicit computation
Implicit updates: AdamW momentum accumulates gradient information across steps, providing implicit parameter updates for layers not selected in the current step

Architecture:
flowchart TB
    A[Forward Pass: All Layers] --> B{Step t mod K}
    B -->|Cycle 0| C[Backward: Layers 0 to L/K]
    B -->|Cycle 1| D[Backward: Layers L/K to 2L/K]
    B -->|Cycle K-1| E[Backward: Layers (K-1)L/K to L]
    C --> F[Update LoRA params for selected layers only]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[AdamW momentum provides implicit updates to non-selected layers]

Where It’s Useful:

On-device LLM fine-tuning on mobile/edge devices with severe memory constraints (<1GB)
LoRA-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning where gradient computation dominates training time
4-bit quantized model training where full backpropagation exhibits instability

Limitations / Caveats:

Theoretical convergence relies on Block Coordinate Descent assumptions (convexity, coordinate-wise smoothness) that may not fully hold for non-convex neural network optimization
Quality degradation up to 2% observed in some settings compared to full backpropagation
Cyclic scheduling strategy and cycle length K require tuning per model/task
Effectiveness depends on AdamW momentum providing sufficient implicit updates; may not generalize to other optimizers

Internal Use Cases:

Enable federated learning or personalized fine-tuning of recommendation models on user devices without requiring high-memory hardware
Accelerate multi-task LoRA training for ranking models by cycling through task-specific adapter layers
Reduce training infrastructure costs for continuous model updates by trading modest quality for significant speedup (1.4× demonstrated)
Explore implicit regularization effects for stabilizing low-bit quantized LLM training in production systems

Discussions & References

github.com

literature/2026-02-16-Look-Inward-to-Exp.title: Look Inward to Explore Outward: Learning Temperature Policy from LLM Internal States via Hierarchical RL
literature/2026-02-16-Look-Inward-to-Exp.content:
Look Inward to Explore Outward: Learning Temperature Policy from LLM Internal States via Hierarchical RL
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #3 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, RL
Abstract
Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) trains large language models (LLMs) from sampled trajectories, making decoding strategy a core component of learning rather than a purely inference-time choice. Sampling temperature directly controls the exploration—exploitation trade-off by modulating policy entropy, yet existing methods rely on static values or heuristic adaptations that are decoupled from task-level rewards. We propose Introspective LLM, a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that learns to control sampling temperature during generation. At each decoding step, the model selects a temperature based on its hidden state and samples the next token from the resulting distribution. Temperature and token policies are jointly optimized from downstream rewards using a coordinate ascent scheme. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that learned temperature policies outperform fixed and heuristic baselines, while exhibiting interpretable exploration behaviors aligned with reasoning uncertainty.
Key Techniques

Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning
Learned Temperature Control
Coordinate Ascent Optimization
Policy Entropy Modulation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityTemperature as a Meta-Policy: Adaptive Temperature in LLM Reinforcement Learning0.63Learning to Configure Agentic AI Systems0.21PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.19STATe-of-Thoughts: Structured Action Templates for Tree-of-Thoughts0.18Learning to Configure Agentic AI Systems0.18
Introspective LLM (Hierarchical Temperature Control via RL)
Core Idea: A hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that learns to dynamically control sampling temperature at each decoding step by conditioning on the LLM’s hidden states, jointly optimizing both temperature selection and token generation from downstream task rewards.
Problem It Solves: Existing RLVR methods use static temperature values or heuristic schedules that are decoupled from task rewards, leading to suboptimal exploration-exploitation trade-offs during trajectory sampling for policy learning.
How It Works:

Hierarchical Policy: At each decoding step t, a high-level policy π_temp(τ_t | h_t) selects temperature τ_t conditioned on hidden state h_t; a low-level policy π_token(x_t | τ_t, context) samples the next token from the temperature-modulated distribution.
Coordinate Ascent Optimization: Alternates between (a) fixing token policy and updating temperature policy via policy gradients on trajectory rewards, and (b) fixing temperature policy and updating token policy via standard RLVR (e.g., PPO, DPO).
Joint Training: Both policies receive the same downstream task reward (e.g., correctness on mathematical reasoning), enabling the temperature controller to learn task-aligned exploration strategies based on internal uncertainty signals.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Hidden State h_t] --> B[Temperature Policy π_temp]
    B --> C[Sample τ_t]
    C --> D[Token Policy π_token]
    E[Context] --> D
    D --> F[Sample Token x_t]
    F --> G[Reward R]
    G --> H[Update π_temp via PG]
    G --> I[Update π_token via RLVR]

Where It’s Useful:

Mathematical reasoning tasks requiring adaptive exploration (proof search, multi-step problem solving)
RLVR training pipelines where sampling quality directly impacts policy optimization efficiency
Any LLM application with verifiable rewards where uncertainty-aware generation improves sample efficiency

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires verifiable downstream rewards (not applicable to open-ended generation without ground truth)
Coordinate ascent may be sensitive to alternation schedule and hyperparameters
Adds training complexity compared to fixed-temperature baselines
Interpretability of learned temperature policies depends on hidden state quality and task structure

Internal Use Cases:

LLM reasoning pipelines: Learn when to explore diverse reasoning paths vs. exploit high-confidence steps in multi-hop QA or code generation
RLHF data collection: Improve trajectory diversity during preference data generation by adapting temperature based on model uncertainty
Retrieval-augmented generation: Modulate exploration when model hidden states indicate low confidence or ambiguous context

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-16-OneVision-Encoder-.title: OneVision-Encoder: Codec-Aligned Sparsity as a Foundational Principle for Multimodal Intelligence
literature/2026-02-16-OneVision-Encoder-.content:
OneVision-Encoder: Codec-Aligned Sparsity as a Foundational Principle for Multimodal Intelligence
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-09
Rank: #5 (score: 0.36, max_sim: 0.78)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Hypothesis. Artificial general intelligence is, at its core, a compression problem. Effective compression demands resonance: deep learning scales best when its architecture aligns with the fundamental structure of the data. These are the fundamental principles. Yet, modern vision architectures have strayed from these truths: visual signals are highly redundant, while discriminative information, the surprise, is sparse. Current models process dense pixel grids uniformly, wasting vast compute on static background rather than focusing on the predictive residuals that define motion and meaning. We argue that to solve visual understanding, we must align our architectures with the information-theoretic principles of video, i.e., Codecs.
Method. OneVision-Encoder encodes video by compressing predictive visual structure into semantic meaning. By adopting Codec Patchification, OV-Encoder abandons uniform computation to focus exclusively on the 3.1%-25% of regions rich in signal entropy. To unify spatial and temporal reasoning under irregular token layouts, OneVision-Encoder employs a shared 3D RoPE and is trained with a large-scale cluster discrimination objective over more than one million semantic concepts, jointly capturing object permanence and motion dynamics.
Evidence. The results validate our core hypothesis: efficiency and accuracy are not a trade-off; they are positively correlated. When integrated into LLM, it consistently outperforms strong vision backbones such as Qwen3-ViT and SigLIP2 across 16 image, video, and document understanding benchmarks, despite using substantially fewer visual tokens and pretraining data. Notably, on video understanding tasks, OV-Encoder achieves an average improvement of 4.1% over Qwen3-ViT. Codec-aligned, patch-level sparsity is a foundational principle, enabling OV-Encoder as a scalable engine for next-generation visual generalists.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques or methods:

Codec Patchification

shared 3D RoPE
cluster discrimination objective

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityFlashVID: Efficient Video Large Language Models via Training-free Tree-based Spatiotemporal Token Merging0.31VisionTrim: Unified Vision Token Compression for Training-Free MLLM Acceleration0.26Quant VideoGen: Auto-Regressive Long Video Generation via 2-Bit KV-Cache Quantization0.26Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs0.23C^2ROPE: Causal Continuous Rotary Positional Encoding for 3D Large Multimodal-Models Reasoning0.22
Codec-Aligned Sparsity (OneVision-Encoder)
Core Idea: Align vision encoder architecture with video codec principles by processing only high-entropy regions (3.1%-25% of patches) where predictive residuals concentrate, rather than uniformly processing all pixels.
Problem It Solves: Current vision transformers waste computation on redundant, static background regions instead of focusing on sparse, information-rich areas where motion and semantic changes occur. This creates an architectural misalignment with the fundamental information-theoretic structure of visual data.
How It Works:

Codec Patchification: Decompose video frames into patches and identify regions with high signal entropy (predictive residuals) similar to I-frames/P-frames in video codecs, discarding 75-97% of redundant patches
Irregular Token Layout Processing: Apply 3D Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) shared across spatial and temporal dimensions to handle non-uniform token distributions while preserving geometric relationships
Large-Scale Cluster Discrimination: Train with contrastive objective over 1M+ semantic concepts to jointly learn object permanence and motion dynamics on the sparse token set

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Video Frames] --> B[Codec Patchification]
    B --> C[Sparse Tokens<br/>3.1%-25%]
    C --> D[3D RoPE Encoding]
    D --> E[Cluster Discrimination<br/>1M+ concepts]
    E --> F[Semantic Embeddings]

Where It’s Useful:

Video understanding tasks (4.1% improvement over dense baselines)
Real-time video processing systems requiring low latency
Multimodal LLMs where vision token budget constrains context length
Document understanding with mixed static/dynamic content

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires careful tuning of entropy threshold to balance sparsity vs. information retention
May struggle with scenes where semantic information is uniformly distributed (e.g., texture classification)
Codec-style decomposition assumes temporal redundancy exists (less effective on single images)
Irregular token layouts may complicate integration with architectures assuming dense grids

Internal Use Cases:

Video recommendation ranking: Extract sparse motion/scene-change features for behavioral signals without processing full frame sequences
Content moderation: Focus compute budget on high-change regions where policy violations occur (faces, actions, text overlays)
Multimodal LLM serving: Reduce vision token count by 4-32× while maintaining accuracy, enabling longer context windows for RAG applications
Thumbnail selection: Identify high-entropy frames as candidates for video previews using codec residual magnitude

literature/2026-02-16-QuEPT-Quantized-El.title: QuEPT: Quantized Elastic Precision Transformers with One-Shot Calibration for Multi-Bit Switching
literature/2026-02-16-QuEPT-Quantized-El.content:
QuEPT: Quantized Elastic Precision Transformers with One-Shot Calibration for Multi-Bit Switching
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #10 (score: 0.35, max_sim: 0.78)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Elastic precision quantization enables multi-bit deployment via a single optimization pass, fitting diverse quantization scenarios.Yet, the high storage and optimization costs associated with the Transformer architecture, research on elastic quantization remains limited, particularly for large language models.This paper proposes QuEPT, an efficient post-training scheme that reconstructs block-wise multi-bit errors with one-shot calibration on a small data slice. It can dynamically adapt to various predefined bit-widths by cascading different low-rank adapters, and supports real-time switching between uniform quantization and mixed precision quantization without repeated optimization. To enhance accuracy and robustness, we introduce Multi-Bit Token Merging (MB-ToMe) to dynamically fuse token features across different bit-widths, improving robustness during bit-width switching. Additionally, we propose Multi-Bit Cascaded Low-Rank adapters (MB-CLoRA) to strengthen correlations between bit-width groups, further improve the overall performance of QuEPT. Extensive experiments demonstrate that QuEPT achieves comparable or better performance to existing state-of-the-art post-training quantization methods.Our code is available at github.com/xuke225/QuEPT
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques:

Block-wise multi-bit error reconstruction

Multi-Bit Token Merging (MB-ToMe)
Multi-Bit Cascaded Low-Rank adapters (MB-CLoRA)

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityReshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems0.27OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization0.251-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization0.25BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.24Quantization-Aware Collaborative Inference for Large Embodied AI Models0.24
QuEPT (Quantized Elastic Precision Transformers)
Core Idea: Enable dynamic multi-bit quantization switching in Transformers through one-shot post-training calibration using cascaded low-rank adapters, eliminating the need for separate optimization per bit-width.
Problem It Solves: Traditional quantization methods require retraining or re-optimization for each target bit-width (2-bit, 3-bit, 4-bit, etc.), making it expensive to deploy models across diverse hardware constraints. Elastic precision quantization has remained underexplored for large language models due to high storage and optimization costs.
How It Works:

Block-wise Multi-Bit Error Reconstruction: Perform one-shot calibration on a small data slice to reconstruct quantization errors across multiple bit-widths simultaneously at the block level
Multi-Bit Cascaded Low-Rank Adapters (MB-CLoRA): Deploy cascaded LoRA modules where each adapter corresponds to a specific bit-width group, strengthening correlations between different quantization levels
Multi-Bit Token Merging (MB-ToMe): Dynamically fuse token features across different bit-width representations to improve robustness during runtime bit-width switching
Runtime Switching: Select appropriate cascaded adapters to switch between uniform quantization (single bit-width) and mixed precision quantization (layer-specific bit-widths) without re-optimization

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input Tokens] --> B[Quantized Transformer Block]
    B --> C[MB-ToMe<br/>Token Fusion]
    C --> D[MB-CLoRA<br/>Bit-Width Adapters]
    D --> E{Bit-Width<br/>Selection}
    E -->|2-bit| F[2-bit Output]
    E -->|3-bit| G[3-bit Output]
    E -->|4-bit| H[4-bit Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Deploying LLMs across heterogeneous edge devices with varying memory/compute constraints
Dynamic resource allocation in multi-tenant serving systems (allocate higher precision to priority requests)
A/B testing different quantization levels without maintaining separate model checkpoints

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires predefined set of target bit-widths during calibration (cannot switch to arbitrary bit-widths post-deployment)
MB-CLoRA introduces additional storage overhead compared to single bit-width quantization (though still lower than maintaining separate quantized models)
Performance at extreme low bit-widths (2-bit) may still degrade compared to precision-specific optimization

Internal Use Cases:

Multi-tenant LLM serving: Dynamically adjust model precision based on request priority/SLA without model swapping
Ranking model deployment: Switch between high-precision (online ranking) and low-precision (candidate generation) modes in the same model
Progressive model loading: Start serving with low-bit weights for fast cold-start, progressively load higher-precision adapters as resources permit

literature/2026-02-16-Quantization-Aware.title: Quantization-Aware Collaborative Inference for Large Embodied AI Models
literature/2026-02-16-Quantization-Aware.content:
Quantization-Aware Collaborative Inference for Large Embodied AI Models
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #11 (score: 0.35, max_sim: 0.78)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Large artificial intelligence models (LAIMs) are increasingly regarded as a core intelligence engine for embodied AI applications. However, the massive parameter scale and computational demands of LAIMs pose significant challenges for resource-limited embodied agents. To address this issue, we investigate quantization-aware collaborative inference (co-inference) for embodied AI systems. First, we develop a tractable approximation for quantization-induced inference distortion. Based on this approximation, we derive lower and upper bounds on the quantization rate-inference distortion function, characterizing its dependence on LAIM statistics, including the quantization bit-width. Next, we formulate a joint quantization bit-width and computation frequency design problem under delay and energy constraints, aiming to minimize the distortion upper bound while ensuring tightness through the corresponding lower bound. Extensive evaluations validate the proposed distortion approximation, the derived rate-distortion bounds, and the effectiveness of the proposed joint design. Particularly, simulations and real-world testbed experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed joint design in balancing inference quality, latency, and energy consumption in edge embodied AI systems.
Key Techniques

Quantization-aware collaborative inference
Quantization rate-distortion bounds
Joint quantization bit-width and computation frequency design

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityQuEPT: Quantized Elastic Precision Transformers with One-Shot Calibration for Multi-Bit Switching0.24Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems0.231-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization0.19Benchmarking the Energy Savings with Speculative Decoding Strategies0.18OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization0.17
Quantization-Aware Collaborative Inference (QACI)
Core Idea: Jointly optimizes quantization bit-width and computation offloading frequency for large AI models running on resource-constrained embodied agents, using tractable distortion bounds to balance inference quality, latency, and energy consumption.
Problem It Solves: Large AI models (LAIMs) for embodied AI (robots, drones, AR/VR) require massive computation that exceeds edge device capabilities, but naive quantization or offloading strategies either degrade accuracy significantly or violate real-time latency/energy constraints.
How It Works:

Distortion Modeling: Develops a tractable approximation for quantization-induced inference distortion based on LAIM weight/activation statistics and bit-width
Rate-Distortion Bounds: Derives closed-form lower and upper bounds on the quantization rate-distortion function, characterizing how distortion decreases with increased bit-width
Joint Optimization: Formulates a constrained optimization problem that selects quantization bit-width per layer and offloading frequency (edge vs. cloud execution) to minimize the distortion upper bound while satisfying delay and energy budgets
Tightness Guarantee: Ensures the lower bound tracks the upper bound closely, validating the approximation quality

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Embodied Agent<br/>Local LAIM] -->|Quantized at b bits| B[Inference Task]
    B -->|Every f steps| C{Offload to Cloud?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Cloud LAIM<br/>Full Precision]
    C -->|No| E[Edge LAIM<br/>Quantized]
    D --> F[Result with<br/>Delay + Energy Cost]
    E --> F
    F -->|Distortion Bound| G[Quality-Energy-Latency<br/>Trade-off]

Where It’s Useful:

Embodied AI systems: Robotics, autonomous drones, AR/VR headsets running vision-language models (VLMs) or large foundation models
Edge AI inference: Mobile devices, IoT sensors running LLMs or multimodal models with strict latency/power budgets
Collaborative edge-cloud systems: Scenarios where occasional cloud offloading is acceptable but most inference must happen locally

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires upfront profiling of LAIM statistics (weight distributions, activation ranges) for distortion approximation
Assumes quasi-static network conditions for offloading decisions; dynamic network variability may require adaptive re-optimization
Distortion bounds are approximations—actual inference quality may deviate if quantization error correlates with input distribution shifts

Internal Use Cases:

On-device RecSys inference: Running personalized ranking models on mobile with mixed-precision quantization (4-bit for early layers, 8-bit for critical scoring layers)
Real-time LLM serving: Optimizing quantization schedules for multi-tenant inference where some requests tolerate 4-bit (W4A8) while others need 8-bit (W8A8) precision
Federated learning: Clients quantize local model updates at variable bit-widths based on communication budget and model layer sensitivity
Hybrid edge-cloud ranking: Offload complex reranking to cloud while serving candidate generation at edge with aggressive quantization

literature/2026-02-16-Quantization-Robus.title: Quantization-Robust LLM Unlearning via Low-Rank Adaptation
literature/2026-02-16-Quantization-Robus.content:
Quantization-Robust LLM Unlearning via Low-Rank Adaptation
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #6 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Large Language Model (LLM) unlearning aims to remove targeted knowledge from a trained model, but practical deployments often require post-training quantization (PTQ) for efficient inference. However, aggressive low-bit PTQ can mask or erase unlearning updates, causing quantized models to revert to pre-unlearning behavior. We show that standard full-parameter fine-tuning often induce parameter changes that are too small to survive 4-bit quantization. We propose quantization-robust unlearning via low-rank adaptation (LoRA): we freeze the base model and concentrate unlearning into trainable adapters so that the effective update is preserved after quantization. On Llama-2-7B evaluated with MUSE dataset (BOOKS and NEWS), LoRA improves 4-bit utility by up to 7.93 points (NPO+GDR on BOOKS: 50.17 to 58.10) and yields higher 4-bit utility on NEWS for GA+GDR (40.06 to 44.82, increase of 4.76). LoRA also substantially reduces privacy leakage under 4-bit PTQ, e.g., for GA+KLR on BOOKS, PrivLeak moves from -25.68 to -5.86 (closer to ideal 0), while maintaining strong forgetting (VerMem and KnowMem near 0). Thus, using LoRA for Machine Unlearning is beneficial for scenarios where quantization is necessary for model deployment.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text
here are the key techniques/methods:

**LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)

Quantization-Robust Unlearning
Post-Training Quantization (PTQ)
Fine-tuning with Adapter-based Updates**

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityGRIP: Algorithm-Agnostic Machine Unlearning for Mixture-of-Experts0.43QuEPT: Quantized Elastic Precision Transformers with One-Shot Calibration for Multi-Bit Switching0.22LRAgent: Efficient KV Cache Sharing for Multi-LoRA LLM Agents0.19BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.17Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems0.16
Quantization-Robust Unlearning via Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA-based Unlearning)
Core Idea: Freeze the base LLM and concentrate machine unlearning updates into trainable LoRA adapters, ensuring that unlearning edits remain effective after aggressive post-training quantization (PTQ) to 4-bit precision.
Problem It Solves: Standard full-parameter fine-tuning for LLM unlearning produces parameter changes too small to survive 4-bit quantization, causing quantized models to revert to pre-unlearning behavior and leak previously “forgotten” knowledge. This undermines privacy guarantees and model utility when deploying compressed models.
How It Works:

Freeze base model weights: Lock the original LLM parameters to prevent diffuse, small-magnitude updates across millions of parameters
Train LoRA adapters for unlearning: Apply unlearning algorithms (e.g., NPO, GA, GDR, KLR) exclusively to low-rank adapter matrices (ΔW = BA where B, A are low-rank)
Quantize the frozen base + adapters: Apply 4-bit PTQ to the base model; the concentrated LoRA updates maintain sufficient magnitude to survive quantization
Deploy merged or separate adapters: Either merge quantized adapters into the base or serve them separately, preserving unlearning behavior post-compression

Where It’s Useful:

Privacy-compliant LLM deployment requiring GDPR “right to be forgotten” compliance on edge devices or mobile hardware
Resource-constrained inference scenarios where both unlearning (e.g., removing copyrighted content, PII) and quantization are mandatory
Multi-tenant LLM serving where different unlearning profiles must be maintained efficiently across quantized model variants

Limitations / Caveats:

LoRA rank and alpha hyperparameters require tuning; suboptimal settings may still lose unlearning signal under extreme quantization
Evaluation relies on specific benchmarks (MUSE BOOKS/NEWS); generalization to other unlearning tasks or quantization schemes (e.g., 2-bit, GPTQ) not fully characterized
Adapter overhead adds minor latency/memory cost compared to fully merged models; trade-off depends on adapter rank

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys: Remove user interaction history from personalized LLM rankers while maintaining 4-bit deployment for mobile recommendations
Content moderation: Unlearn toxic or outdated training data from ranking LLMs without re-quantizing entire model stacks
Federated learning: Enable clients to request selective unlearning in quantized on-device models without degrading utility metrics

literature/2026-02-16-Quantized-Evolutio.title: Quantized Evolution Strategies: High-precision Fine-tuning of Quantized LLMs at Low-precision Cost
literature/2026-02-16-Quantized-Evolutio.content:
Quantized Evolution Strategies: High-precision Fine-tuning of Quantized LLMs at Low-precision Cost
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-03
Rank: #9 (score: 0.81, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) is essential for deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) on memory-constrained devices, yet it renders models static and difficult to fine-tune. Standard fine-tuning paradigms, including Reinforcement Learning (RL), fundamentally rely on backpropagation and high-precision weights to compute gradients. Thus they cannot be used on quantized models, where the parameter space is discrete and non-differentiable. While Evolution Strategies (ES) offer a backpropagation-free alternative, optimization of the quantized parameters can still fail due to vanishing or inaccurate gradient. This paper introduces Quantized Evolution Strategies (QES), an optimization paradigm that performs full-parameter fine-tuning directly in the quantized space. QES is based on two innovations: (1) it integrates accumulated error feedback to preserve high-precision gradient signals, and (2) it utilizes a stateless seed replay to reduce memory usage to low-precision inference levels. QES significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art zeroth-order fine-tuning method on arithmetic reasoning tasks, making direct fine-tuning for quantized models possible. It therefore opens up the possibility for scaling up LLMs entirely in the quantized space. The source code is available at github.com/dibbla/Quantized-Evolution-Strategies .
Key Techniques

Evolution Strategies
Accumulated Error Feedback
Stateless Seed Replay

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.30ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.26LCSB: Layer-Cyclic Selective Backpropagation for Memory-Efficient On-Device LLM Fine-Tuning0.23ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.21ECO: Quantized Training without Full-Precision Master Weights0.21
Quantized Evolution Strategies (QES)
Core Idea: A backpropagation-free optimization method that performs full-parameter fine-tuning directly on quantized LLMs by combining accumulated error feedback (to preserve high-precision gradient signals in discrete parameter space) with stateless seed replay (to maintain memory at low-precision inference levels).
Problem It Solves: Standard fine-tuning requires backpropagation through high-precision weights, making it incompatible with Post-Training Quantized (PTQ) models where parameters are discrete and non-differentiable. Naive zeroth-order methods like vanilla Evolution Strategies fail on quantized parameters due to vanishing or inaccurate gradients when the search space is discrete.
How It Works:

Perturbation Generation: Sample parameter perturbations using a seed-based random number generator (enables stateless replay without storing perturbation vectors)
Forward Evaluation: Evaluate fitness/loss of perturbed quantized models on task examples (e.g., arithmetic reasoning)
Gradient Estimation: Compute zeroth-order gradient estimate from fitness differences across perturbations
Error-Feedback Update: Apply gradient to a high-precision shadow accumulator, then quantize the result back to discrete weights—carrying forward quantization residuals to preserve update signal across iterations
Seed Replay: Reconstruct perturbations on-demand using stored seeds rather than caching full parameter tensors, keeping memory at inference levels

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Quantized Model θ_q] --> B[Seed-based Perturbation]
    B --> C[Forward Pass + Fitness]
    C --> D[ES Gradient Estimate ∇f]
    D --> E[High-Precision Accumulator θ_hp]
    E --> F[Quantize with Error Feedback]
    F --> A
    G[Seed Storage] -.stateless replay.-> B

Where It’s Useful:

Fine-tuning quantized LLMs on edge/mobile devices with limited memory
Domain adaptation or RLHF-style reward optimization when backpropagation is infeasible (discrete parameters, non-differentiable objectives)
Low-resource scenarios requiring full-parameter updates without gradient checkpointing or optimizer state storage

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires many forward passes (high sample complexity) compared to gradient-based methods—compute cost shifts from backward passes to repeated forward evaluations
Convergence speed may lag behind full-precision Adam/SGD on tasks with smooth loss landscapes
Effectiveness depends on task structure; demonstrated primarily on arithmetic reasoning, may require tuning for open-ended generation or multimodal tasks

Internal Use Cases:

Deploy fine-tunable recommendation/ranking LLMs on memory-constrained inference servers (e.g., INT4/INT8 models that adapt to user feedback without full-precision retraining)
Continual learning for quantized chat models using online RL signals (thumbs up/down) where gradient computation is blocked by quantization
Federated learning with quantized edge models—QES enables local parameter updates without transmitting high-precision weights or gradients

literature/2026-02-16-R-Diverse-Mitigati.title: R-Diverse: Mitigating Diversity Illusion in Self-Play LLM Training
literature/2026-02-16-R-Diverse-Mitigati.content:
R-Diverse: Mitigating Diversity Illusion in Self-Play LLM Training
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #6 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG
Code: github.com/Gengsheng-Li/R-Diverse
Abstract
Self-play bootstraps LLM reasoning through an iterative Challenger-Solver loop: the Challenger is trained to generate questions that target the Solver’s capabilities, and the Solver is optimized on the generated data to expand its reasoning skills. However, existing frameworks like R-Zero often exhibit non-sustained improvement, where early gains degrade as self-play continues. We identify a key failure mode, Diversity Illusion, where the Solver’s training signals appear diverse yet collapse into recurring underlying patterns. It manifests as (1) Local Diversity Illusion, where diversity is enforced only within-batch, inducing cross-iteration mode cycling; and (2) Surface Diversity Illusion, where questions vary superficially but require near-identical reasoning skills. To mitigate them, we propose R-Diverse with two aligned innovations: Memory-Augmented Penalty (MAP), which uses a persistent memory bank to discourage recycling across iterations, and Skill-Aware Measurement (SAM), which evaluates diversity by the reasoning skills exercised rather than surface variation of questions. Across 10 math and general reasoning benchmarks, R-Diverse sustains gains over more iterations and consistently outperforms prior self-play methods. Code is available at github.com/Gengsheng-Li/R-Diverse.
Key Techniques

Self-play Challenger-Solver loop
Memory-Augmented Penalty (MAP)
Skill-Aware Measurement (SAM)

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMemSkill: Learning and Evolving Memory Skills for Self-Evolving Agents0.23Temp-R1: A Unified Autonomous Agent for Complex Temporal KGQA via Reverse Curriculum Reinforcement Learning0.16Accordion-Thinking: Self-Regulated Step Summaries for Efficient and Readable LLM Reasoning0.16Dual-Prototype Disentanglement: A Context-Aware Enhancement Framework for Time Series Forecasting0.15SwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMs0.14
R-Diverse (Memory-Augmented Penalty + Skill-Aware Measurement)
Core Idea: Sustain self-play LLM training by preventing diversity illusion through a persistent memory bank that discourages cross-iteration pattern recycling (MAP) and a skill-based diversity metric that evaluates questions by reasoning skills required rather than surface variation (SAM).
Problem It Solves: Existing self-play frameworks (e.g., R-Zero) suffer from non-sustained improvement where early gains degrade as training continues, caused by (1) Local Diversity Illusion—within-batch diversity enforcement that induces cross-iteration mode cycling, and (2) Surface Diversity Illusion—superficially varied questions that exercise identical underlying reasoning skills.
How It Works:

Memory-Augmented Penalty (MAP): Maintain a persistent memory bank storing representations of previously generated training questions across all iterations; during Challenger training, penalize the generation of questions that are similar to those in the memory bank to prevent recycling of patterns across iterations.
Skill-Aware Measurement (SAM): Instead of measuring diversity based on surface-level question variation (e.g., text similarity), evaluate diversity by analyzing the underlying reasoning skills each question exercises; cluster questions by required reasoning capabilities and enforce diversity at the skill level.
Iterative Challenger-Solver Loop: Train the Challenger LLM to generate questions that (a) target the current Solver’s weak points, (b) avoid patterns in the memory bank, and (c) span diverse reasoning skills; then train the Solver on the generated dataset to expand its capabilities.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Current Solver] --> B[Challenger with MAP]
    B --> C[Generate Questions]
    C --> D[SAM Diversity Filter]
    D --> E[Training Dataset]
    E --> F[Updated Solver]
    F --> A
    C --> G[Memory Bank]
    G --> B

Where It’s Useful:

Self-supervised bootstrapping of reasoning capabilities in LLMs without large-scale human annotation
Mathematical reasoning and general problem-solving domains where diverse training signals are critical
Iterative curriculum learning where sustained improvement over many iterations is needed

Limitations / Caveats:

Memory bank grows over iterations, potentially requiring periodic pruning or summarization strategies
Skill taxonomy for SAM must be defined either manually or through auxiliary models, adding upfront complexity
Effectiveness depends on the Challenger’s ability to generate truly novel questions rather than minor perturbations

Internal Use Cases:

Bootstrapping query understanding models by generating diverse user intent patterns that avoid repeating common query structures
Training ranking models with synthetic hard negatives that require different reasoning skills (semantic vs. exact match vs. temporal reasoning)
Continual learning for recommendation systems where diversity of training signals prevents catastrophic forgetting and mode collapse

literature/2026-02-16-RQ-GMM-Residual-Qu.title: RQ-GMM: Residual Quantized Gaussian Mixture Model for Multimodal Semantic Discretization in CTR Prediction
literature/2026-02-16-RQ-GMM-Residual-Qu.content:
RQ-GMM: Residual Quantized Gaussian Mixture Model for Multimodal Semantic Discretization in CTR Prediction
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, RecSys
Abstract
Multimodal content is crucial for click-through rate (CTR) prediction. However, directly incorporating continuous embeddings from pre-trained models into CTR models yields suboptimal results due to misaligned optimization objectives and convergence speed inconsistency during joint training. Discretizing embeddings into semantic IDs before feeding them into CTR models offers a more effective solution, yet existing methods suffer from limited codebook utilization, reconstruction accuracy, and semantic discriminability. We propose RQ-GMM (Residual Quantized Gaussian Mixture Model), which introduces probabilistic modeling to better capture the statistical structure of multimodal embedding spaces. Through Gaussian Mixture Models combined with residual quantization, RQ-GMM achieves superior codebook utilization and reconstruction accuracy. Experiments on public datasets and online A/B tests on a large-scale short-video platform serving hundreds of millions of users demonstrate substantial improvements: RQ-GMM yields a 1.502% gain in Advertiser Value over strong baselines. The method has been fully deployed, serving daily recommendations for hundreds of millions of users.
Key Techniques

RQ-GMM
Residual Quantization
Gaussian Mixture Models
Embedding Discretization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.37R2LED: Equipping Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs for CTR Prediction0.28Rethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs0.24End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.24MoToRec: Sparse-Regularized Multimodal Tokenization for Cold-Start Recommendation0.24
Residual Quantized Gaussian Mixture Model (RQ-GMM)
Core Idea: Uses Gaussian Mixture Models combined with residual quantization to discretize multimodal embeddings into semantic IDs, modeling the probabilistic structure of embedding spaces for improved codebook utilization and reconstruction accuracy in CTR prediction.
Problem It Solves: Addresses three limitations of semantic discretization for multimodal embeddings: (1) poor codebook utilization (many codes unused), (2) low reconstruction accuracy when mapping continuous embeddings to discrete IDs, and (3) insufficient semantic discriminability between similar items. Direct use of continuous pre-trained embeddings in CTR models fails due to misaligned optimization objectives and convergence speed inconsistency.
How It Works:

Probabilistic Clustering: Model the multimodal embedding space as a Gaussian Mixture Model with K components, where each component represents a semantic cluster with learned mean μ_k and covariance Σ_k
First-Stage Quantization: Assign each embedding to the GMM component with highest posterior probability, obtaining the first semantic code ID
Residual Quantization: Compute the residual (difference between original embedding and GMM centroid), then recursively apply GMM quantization to residuals for multiple stages
Semantic ID Generation: Concatenate quantization codes across stages to form the final discrete semantic ID
CTR Model Integration: Feed semantic IDs as categorical features into the CTR model (e.g., DeepFM, DCN) alongside traditional user/item features

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Multimodal Embedding] --> B[GMM Clustering Stage 1]
    B --> C[Residual Computation]
    C --> D[GMM Clustering Stage 2]
    D --> E[Residual Computation]
    E --> F[GMM Clustering Stage N]
    F --> G[Concatenated Semantic ID]
    G --> H[CTR Model]
    
    B --> I[Code 1]
    D --> J[Code 2]
    F --> K[Code N]
