    I --> G
    J --> G
    K --> G

Where It’s Useful:

Click-through rate prediction with multimodal content (video, image, text)
E-commerce recommendation with visual and textual product features
Short-video platforms requiring semantic discretization of video embeddings
Any scenario requiring categorical representation of high-dimensional continuous embeddings

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires offline training of GMM parameters on representative embedding samples
Computational overhead of GMM inference at serving time (though discretization enables efficient lookup)
May require retraining when embedding distribution shifts significantly
Number of stages and codebook size are hyperparameters requiring tuning

Internal Use Cases:

Discretizing CLIP/ViT embeddings for video recommendation systems to align with collaborative filtering signals
Converting multimodal pre-trained model outputs into categorical IDs for easier integration with traditional CTR architectures
Improving cold-start item representation by maintaining semantic structure through probabilistic quantization
Enabling efficient feature hashing and embedding table lookup for large-scale serving systems handling hundreds of millions of users

literature/2026-02-16-Recurrent-Preferen.title: Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation
literature/2026-02-16-Recurrent-Preferen.content:
Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #3 (score: 0.91, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Generative recommendation (GenRec) models typically model user behavior via full attention, but scaling to lifelong sequences is hindered by prohibitive computational costs and noise accumulation from stochastic interactions. To address these challenges, we introduce Rec2PM, a framework that compresses long user interaction histories into compact Preference Memory tokens. Unlike traditional recurrent methods that suffer from serial training, Rec2PM employs a novel self-referential teacher-forcing strategy: it leverages a global view of the history to generate reference memories, which serve as supervision targets for parallelized recurrent updates. This allows for fully parallel training while maintaining the capability for iterative updates during inference. Additionally, by representing memory as token embeddings rather than extensive KV caches, Rec2PM achieves extreme storage efficiency. Experiments on large-scale benchmarks show that Rec2PM significantly reduces inference latency and memory footprint while achieving superior accuracy compared to full-sequence models. Analysis reveals that the Preference Memory functions as a denoising Information Bottleneck, effectively filtering interaction noise to capture robust long-term interests.
Key Techniques

Preference Memory tokens
self-referential teacher-forcing
parallel training with recurrent updates
Information Bottleneck denoising

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityRecurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation0.89When to Memorize and When to Stop: Gated Recurrent Memory for Long-Context Reasoning0.24Scene-Aware Memory Discrimination: Deciding Which Personal Knowledge Stays0.22MemFly: On-the-Fly Memory Optimization via Information Bottleneck0.21Language Model Memory and Memory Models for Language0.20
Rec2PM (Recurrent Preference Memory)
Core Idea: Compresses long user interaction sequences into compact Preference Memory tokens using self-referential teacher-forcing, enabling parallel training of recurrent models while maintaining constant-time inference complexity.
Problem It Solves: Full-attention generative recommendation models face quadratic computational costs with sequence length and accumulate noise from stochastic interactions, while traditional recurrent methods suffer from slow serial training that prevents efficient scaling.
How It Works:

Global Reference Generation: Process full interaction history with attention-based encoder to generate high-quality reference Preference Memory tokens (teacher signal)
Parallel Recurrent Training: Train recurrent preference updater to predict these reference memories from partial sequences, enabling fully parallelized training across all timesteps
Iterative Inference: During deployment, incrementally update compact memory tokens (e.g., 10-20 tokens) as new interactions arrive, avoiding full sequence reprocessing

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Long Interaction<br/>Sequence] --> B[Global Encoder<br/>full attention]
    B --> C[Reference<br/>Memory Tokens]
    A --> D[Recurrent Updater<br/>parallel training]
    C -.teacher forcing.-> D
    D --> E[Predicted<br/>Memory]
    E --> F[Generative<br/>Recommendation]

Where It’s Useful:

Lifelong user modeling in e-commerce and content platforms where users accumulate thousands of interactions
Real-time recommendation systems requiring low-latency inference with long user histories
Memory-constrained deployment scenarios (mobile, edge devices) where storing full KV caches is infeasible

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires two-stage training (global encoder + recurrent updater), adding complexity to the training pipeline
Fixed memory capacity (token count) may bottleneck representation of extremely diverse user interests
Effectiveness depends on quality of global reference memories during teacher-forcing phase

Internal Use Cases:

Session-based ranking: Replace full attention over session history with compact memory tokens, reducing P99 latency for long sessions
Cross-session personalization: Maintain lightweight user state (10-20 tokens) across sessions instead of full KV cache, dramatically reducing memory costs for billion-user platforms
Information Bottleneck regularization: Apply denoising memory compression to filter noisy engagement signals (accidental clicks, exploration) and extract robust preference signals for ranking models

literature/2026-02-16-SLA2-Sparse-Linear.title: SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT
literature/2026-02-16-SLA2-Sparse-Linear.content:
SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #8 (score: 0.89, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Sparse-Linear Attention (SLA) combines sparse and linear attention to accelerate diffusion models and has shown strong performance in video generation. However, (i) SLA relies on a heuristic split that assigns computations to the sparse or linear branch based on attention-weight magnitude, which can be suboptimal. Additionally, (ii) after formally analyzing the attention error in SLA, we identify a mismatch between SLA and a direct decomposition into sparse and linear attention. We propose SLA2, which introduces (I) a learnable router that dynamically selects whether each attention computation should use sparse or linear attention, (II) a more faithful and direct sparse-linear attention formulation that uses a learnable ratio to combine the sparse and linear attention branches, and (III) a sparse + low-bit attention design, where low-bit attention is introduced via quantization-aware fine-tuning to reduce quantization error. Experiments show that on video diffusion models, SLA2 can achieve 97% attention sparsity and deliver an 18.6x attention speedup while preserving generation quality.
Key Techniques

Learnable router for dynamic attention selection
Faithful sparse-linear attention formulation with learnable ratio
Sparse + low-bit attention design via quantization-aware fine-tuning

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.93FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion0.41HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.40HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.40Superlinear Multi-Step Attention0.39
SLA2 (Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT)
Core Idea: A hybrid attention mechanism that uses a learnable router to dynamically assign each attention computation to either sparse or linear attention branches, combined with a learnable mixing ratio and quantization-aware training to achieve extreme sparsity (97%) while maintaining generation quality in diffusion models.
Problem It Solves: Original SLA uses a suboptimal heuristic (attention-weight magnitude) to split computations between sparse and linear branches, and exhibits a mathematical mismatch between its formulation and the true sparse-linear decomposition; this leads to inefficient routing and degraded quality under high sparsity.
How It Works:

Learnable Router: A lightweight trainable network examines Q and K representations and predicts whether each attention computation should use the sparse attention branch or the linear attention branch (replacing magnitude-based heuristics)
Direct Sparse-Linear Formulation: Computes attention as Attention = α × Sparse_Attention + (1-α) × Linear_Attention where α is a learnable scalar parameter that directly combines the two branches with proper weighting
Quantization-Aware Fine-Tuning (QAT): Applies low-bit quantization to the linear attention branch during fine-tuning, reducing memory bandwidth and computation while minimizing quantization error through QAT
Output: Produces the same attention output shape with 18.6× speedup and 97% sparsity on video diffusion tasks

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Q, K, V] --> B[Learnable Router]
    A --> C[Sparse Attention Branch]
    A --> D[Linear Attention Branch + QAT]
    B --> E{Route Decision}
    E -->|Sparse| C
    E -->|Linear| D
    C --> F[Weighted Combiner]
    D --> F
    F -->|α sparse + 1-α linear| G[Attention Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Video generation models requiring long-context attention (e.g., text-to-video diffusion)
Large-scale diffusion models where attention is the computational bottleneck
Real-time or latency-sensitive generative applications needing inference speedup
Multi-modal transformers processing high-resolution spatial-temporal data

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires fine-tuning with the learnable router and mixing ratio (not a drop-in replacement)
Optimal router architecture and α initialization may be task-dependent
Quantization benefits depend on hardware support for low-bit operations
May not generalize to non-diffusion attention patterns (e.g., causal LLM decoding) without adaptation

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys ranking models: Apply learnable routing to decide per-query whether to use sparse (top-k candidate) or linear (LSH/kernel) attention over large item catalogs
Long-context LLMs: Route between sparse (attending to retrieved or top-k tokens) and linear (approximating global context) for efficient multi-document reasoning
Multimodal embeddings: Use QAT on cross-modal attention (image-text) to reduce memory footprint during inference while maintaining alignment quality
Real-time recommendation: Deploy quantized linear attention branch for low-latency scoring while using sparse branch for high-value personalization signals

literature/2026-02-16-TRACE-Temporal-Rea.title: TRACE: Temporal Reasoning via Agentic Context Evolution for Streaming Electronic Health Records (EHRs)
literature/2026-02-16-TRACE-Temporal-Rea.content:
TRACE: Temporal Reasoning via Agentic Context Evolution for Streaming Electronic Health Records (EHRs)
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #7 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) encode extensive medical knowledge but struggle to apply it reliably to longitudinal patient trajectories, where evolving clinical states, irregular timing, and heterogeneous events degrade performance over time. Existing adaptation strategies rely on fine-tuning or retrieval-based augmentation, which introduce computational overhead, privacy constraints, or instability under long contexts. We introduce TRACE (Temporal Reasoning via Agentic Context Evolution), a framework that enables temporal clinical reasoning with frozen LLMs by explicitly structuring and maintaining context rather than extending context windows or updating parameters. TRACE operates over a dual-memory architecture consisting of a static Global Protocol encoding institutional clinical rules and a dynamic Individual Protocol tracking patient-specific state. Four agentic components, Router, Reasoner, Auditor, and Steward, coordinate over this structured memory to support temporal inference and state evolution. The framework maintains bounded inference cost via structured state compression and selectively audits safety-critical clinical decisions. Evaluated on longitudinal clinical event streams from MIMIC-IV, TRACE significantly improves next-event prediction accuracy, protocol adherence, and clinical safety over long-context and retrieval-augmented baselines, while producing interpretable and auditable reasoning traces.
Key Techniques

Router
Reasoner
Auditor
Steward
dual-memory architecture

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySycoEval-EM: Sycophancy Evaluation of Large Language Models in Simulated Clinical Encounters for Emergency Care0.26BioPulse-QA: A Dynamic Biomedical Question-Answering Benchmark for Evaluating Factuality, Robustness, and Bias in Large Language Models0.17Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.15DeepImageSearch: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Context-Aware Image Retrieval in Visual Histories0.15ORCH: many analyses, one merge-a deterministic multi-agent orchestrator for discrete-choice reasoning with EMA-guided routing0.14
TRACE (Temporal Reasoning via Agentic Context Evolution)
Core Idea: A framework that enables frozen LLMs to perform temporal clinical reasoning over longitudinal patient data by maintaining a dual-memory architecture (static Global Protocol + dynamic Individual Protocol) coordinated by four specialized agentic components instead of extending context windows or fine-tuning.
Problem It Solves: LLMs struggle with longitudinal patient trajectories where evolving clinical states, irregular timing, and heterogeneous events cause performance degradation over time; existing solutions (fine-tuning, RAG) introduce computational overhead, privacy constraints, or instability under long contexts.
How It Works:

Initialize Dual Memory: Global Protocol stores static institutional clinical rules/guidelines; Individual Protocol maintains dynamic patient-specific state and history
Router triages incoming clinical events and decides which agentic component should handle the event based on event type and current context
Reasoner performs clinical inference using both protocols to predict next events, generate recommendations, or answer clinical queries
Auditor selectively reviews safety-critical decisions (e.g., medication orders, critical lab results) against protocol guidelines for compliance
Steward maintains the Individual Protocol via structured state compression, pruning outdated information while preserving clinically relevant history to bound inference cost
Output interpretable reasoning traces with protocol citations and audit flags for clinical validation

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Clinical Event Stream] --> B[Router]
    B --> C[Reasoner]
    B --> D[Auditor]
    B --> E[Steward]
    C --> F[Global Protocol<br/>Static Rules]
    C --> G[Individual Protocol<br/>Patient State]
    D --> F
    E --> G
    C --> H[Predictions +<br/>Reasoning Traces]
    D --> H
    F -.-> C
    G -.-> C

Where It’s Useful:

Longitudinal clinical decision support over streaming EHR data
Protocol adherence monitoring in hospital/ICU settings
Next-event prediction for patient trajectory modeling
Any temporal reasoning domain with evolving state and safety-critical decisions (financial fraud detection, autonomous systems)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires manual engineering of Global Protocol structure and clinical rules (institutional knowledge curation overhead)
Steward’s compression strategy may discard clinically relevant historical context in edge cases
Auditor operates selectively (not on all decisions) to maintain bounded cost—may miss non-flagged errors
Evaluation limited to MIMIC-IV; generalization to other EHR systems or medical domains not demonstrated

Internal Use Cases:

User state modeling for RecSys: Adapt dual-memory pattern to maintain static user preferences (Global Protocol analog) + dynamic session context (Individual Protocol) for personalized ranking
Streaming recommendation auditing: Use Auditor component to flag diversity/fairness violations or filter bubble risks in real-time ranking
Context compression for long-session LLM agents: Apply Steward’s structured compression to maintain bounded context in multi-turn conversational agents or code assistants
Multi-agent orchestration: Router pattern generalizes to task decomposition in agentic workflows (e.g., routing search queries vs. computation vs. memory updates)

literature/2026-02-16-TensorCommitments-.title: TensorCommitments: A Lightweight Verifiable Inference for Language Models
literature/2026-02-16-TensorCommitments-.content:
TensorCommitments: A Lightweight Verifiable Inference for Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #8 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM
Abstract
Most large language models (LLMs) run on external clouds: users send a prompt, pay for inference, and must trust that the remote GPU executes the LLM without any adversarial tampering. We critically ask how to achieve verifiable LLM inference, where a prover (the service) must convince a verifier (the client) that an inference was run correctly without rerunning the LLM. Existing cryptographic works are too slow at the LLM scale, while non-cryptographic ones require a strong verifier GPU. We propose TensorCommitments (TCs), a tensor-native proof-of-inference scheme. TC binds the LLM inference to a commitment, an irreversible tag that breaks under tampering, organized in our multivariate Terkle Trees. For LLaMA2, TC adds only 0.97% prover and 0.12% verifier time over inference while improving robustness to tailored LLM attacks by up to 48% over the best prior work requiring a verifier GPU.
Key Techniques

Tensor Commitments
Multivariate Merkle Trees
Proof-of-Inference Scheme

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityScaling Agentic Verifier for Competitive Coding0.13Nano-vLLM: How a vLLM-style inference engine works0.12PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models0.12Differentially Private and Communication Efficient Large Language Model Split Inference via Stochastic Quantization and Soft Prompt0.10MoE-Spec: Expert Budgeting for Efficient Speculative Decoding0.10
TensorCommitments (TC)
Core Idea: A tensor-native cryptographic commitment scheme that binds LLM inference to an irreversible tag organized in multivariate Terkle Trees, enabling lightweight verification that inference was executed correctly without rerunning the model.
Problem It Solves: Existing verifiable inference approaches either have prohibitive cryptographic overhead (too slow for LLM scale) or require the verifier to possess a strong GPU, making client-side verification impractical for users accessing cloud LLM services.
How It Works:

Commitment Generation: During inference, the prover (service) computes tensor-native commitments at each layer of the LLM, binding intermediate activations to cryptographic tags
Terkle Tree Construction: Organize commitments in multivariate Terkle Trees (tensor extension of Merkle trees) that aggregate proof data efficiently across the tensor dimensions
Lightweight Verification: Client verifies the commitment chain to detect tampering—any adversarial modification to model weights, activations, or outputs breaks the commitment irreversibly
Proof Transmission: Send compact proof alongside inference output; verifier checks consistency without GPU or model access

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input Prompt] --> B[LLM Layer 1 + TC]
    B --> C[LLM Layer 2 + TC]
    C --> D[...]
    D --> E[LLM Layer L + TC]
    E --> F[Output + Proof]
    F --> G[Verifier: Check TC Chain]
    G --> H{Valid?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Accept Output]
    H -->|No| J[Reject - Tampering Detected]

Where It’s Useful:

Cloud LLM inference services where users cannot trust the provider’s execution integrity
Regulated or safety-critical applications requiring provable model behavior (medical, legal, financial)
Decentralized AI marketplaces where miners/providers must prove correct inference
Federated or edge inference with untrusted compute nodes

Limitations / Caveats:

Adds 0.97% prover overhead and 0.12% verifier overhead over baseline inference (low but non-zero)
Robustness against tailored LLM attacks improved by 48% over prior GPU-based methods, but not 100% adversary-proof
Requires cryptographic integration into the inference stack (not drop-in compatible with existing serving frameworks)
Proof size and verification complexity scale with model depth (number of layers)

Internal Use Cases:

LLM Serving Audit Trails: If deploying LLMs via third-party inference providers, TC enables verifying that responses match the contracted model version without adversarial weight tampering
Model Integrity in RecSys: Extend to verify that ranking models or embedding networks haven’t been backdoored or modified between training and deployment
Federated Inference: For distributed or edge LLM deployments, ensure each node executes the correct model without centralized GPU verification
Red-teaming & Safety: Prove that safety filters or alignment layers were actually applied during inference, not bypassed

literature/2026-02-16-Unified-Multi-Doma.title: Unified Multi-Domain Graph Pre-training for Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Graphs via Domain-Specific Expert Encoding
literature/2026-02-16-Unified-Multi-Doma.content:
Unified Multi-Domain Graph Pre-training for Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Graphs via Domain-Specific Expert Encoding
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #4 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Graph pre-training has achieved remarkable success in recent years, delivering transferable representations for downstream adaptation. However, most existing methods are designed for either homogeneous or heterogeneous graphs, thereby hindering unified graph modeling across diverse graph types. This separation contradicts real-world applications, where mixed homogeneous and heterogeneous graphs are ubiquitous, and distribution shifts between upstream pre-training and downstream deployment are common. In this paper, we empirically demonstrate that a balanced mixture of homogeneous and heterogeneous graph pre-training benefits downstream tasks and propose a unified multi-domain \textbf{G}raph \textbf{P}re-training method across \textbf{H}omogeneous and \textbf{H}eterogeneous graphs (\mathbf{GPH^{2}}). To address the lack of a unified encoder for homogeneous and heterogeneous graphs, we propose a Unified Multi-View Graph Construction that simultaneously encodes both without explicit graph-type-specific designs. To cope with the increased cross-domain distribution discrepancies arising from mixed graphs, we introduce domain-specific expert encoding. Each expert is independently pre-trained on a single graph to capture domain-specific knowledge, thereby shielding the pre-training encoder from the adverse effects of cross-domain discrepancies. For downstream tasks, we further design a Task-oriented Expert Fusion Strategy that adaptively integrates multiple experts based on their discriminative strengths. Extensive experiments on mixed graphs demonstrate that \text{GPH}^{2} enables stable transfer across graph types and domains, significantly outperforming existing graph pre-training methods.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques:

Unified Multi-View Graph Construction

Domain-specific Expert Encoding
Task-oriented Expert Fusion Strategy

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityGraphSeek: Next-Generation Graph Analytics with LLMs0.28One Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral Profiles0.28Exploring the impact of adaptive rewiring in Graph Neural Networks0.25LIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs0.23Towards Personalized Bangla Book Recommendation: A Large-Scale Multi-Entity Book Graph Dataset0.21
GPH² (Graph Pre-training across Homogeneous and Heterogeneous graphs)
Core Idea: A unified multi-domain graph pre-training framework that trains domain-specific experts independently on both homogeneous and heterogeneous graphs, then adaptively fuses them for downstream tasks to handle cross-graph-type and cross-domain distribution shifts.
Problem It Solves: Existing graph pre-training methods are tailored to either homogeneous or heterogeneous graphs exclusively, failing to leverage mixed graph types during pre-training and struggling with distribution shifts when the upstream pre-training graph type differs from downstream deployment graphs.
How It Works:

Unified Multi-View Graph Construction: Transform both homogeneous and heterogeneous graphs into a unified representation by constructing multiple views (structural, semantic, attribute-based) without explicit graph-type-specific architectures, enabling a single encoder to process both graph types.
Domain-Specific Expert Encoding: Pre-train separate expert encoders, each on a single source graph (domain), to capture domain-specific knowledge while isolating the model from adverse cross-domain distribution discrepancies during pre-training.
Task-Oriented Expert Fusion: For downstream tasks, adaptively weight and combine representations from multiple pre-trained experts based on their discriminative power for the target task, measured via validation performance or attention mechanisms.

Architecture:
flowchart TB
    A[Mixed Homo/Hetero Graphs] --> B[Unified Multi-View Construction]
    B --> C1[Expert 1<br/>Domain 1]
    B --> C2[Expert 2<br/>Domain 2]
    B --> C3[Expert N<br/>Domain N]
    C1 --> D[Task-Oriented Fusion]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Downstream Task Prediction]

Where It’s Useful:

Cross-domain graph transfer learning where training and test graphs have different structures (e.g., molecular graphs → social networks)
Multi-task learning on mixed homogeneous and heterogeneous graph datasets
Zero-shot or few-shot graph learning scenarios with domain shift

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires training multiple expert models, increasing computational cost linearly with the number of source domains
Fusion strategy may require downstream task validation data to determine optimal expert weights, limiting true zero-shot applicability
Performance gain depends on diversity and relevance of source domains to the target task

Internal Use Cases:

Cross-platform RecSys: Pre-train experts on user-item graphs from different platforms (e.g., e-commerce, streaming, social media) with varying structures, then fuse for cold-start recommendations on new platforms
Multi-relational ranking: Handle heterogeneous knowledge graphs (entities with typed relations) and homogeneous user behavior graphs jointly for context-aware ranking
LLM-augmented graph reasoning: Combine graph-structured knowledge bases (heterogeneous) with citation/co-occurrence networks (homogeneous) to enhance retrieval-augmented generation

literature/2026-02-16-Unleashing-Low-Bit.title: Unleashing Low-Bit Inference on Ascend NPUs: A Comprehensive Evaluation of HiFloat Formats
literature/2026-02-16-Unleashing-Low-Bit.content:
Unleashing Low-Bit Inference on Ascend NPUs: A Comprehensive Evaluation of HiFloat Formats
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #4 (score: 0.72, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
As LLMs scale, low-bit floating-point formats like MXFP and NVFP4 offer new opportunities for precision and efficiency. In this work, we evaluate HiFloat (HiF8 and HiF4), a family of formats tailored for Ascend NPUs. Through rigorous comparison across weight-activation and KV-cache tasks, we provide three key insights: (1) INT8 suits narrow-range data, while floating-point formats excel with high-variance data; (2) in 4-bit regimes, HiF4’s hierarchical scaling prevents the accuracy collapse seen in integer formats; and (3) HiFloat is fully compatible with state-of-the-art post-training quantization frameworks. Overall, HiFloat provides a solution for high-efficiency LLM inference on NPUs.
Key Techniques

HiFloat formats
low-bit floating-point quantization
hierarchical scaling
post-training quantization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityIs Finer Better? The Limits of Microscaling Formats in Large Language Models0.28OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization0.261-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization0.26Quartet II: Accurate LLM Pre-Training in NVFP4 by Improved Unbiased Gradient Estimation0.26Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems0.23
HiFloat Low-Bit Quantization Formats (HiF8/HiF4)
Core Idea: A family of hierarchical floating-point formats (HiF8 and HiF4) designed for Ascend NPUs that use block-level scaling factors to preserve precision in high-variance data during LLM inference quantization.
Problem It Solves: Integer quantization (INT4/INT8) suffers from accuracy collapse on high-variance activations and KV-cache data; existing floating-point formats like MXFP and NVFP4 are not optimized for Ascend NPU hardware characteristics.
How It Works:

Block-based scaling: Divide weight/activation tensors into fixed-size blocks (e.g., 128 elements), compute a shared scaling factor per block
Hierarchical precision: HiF8 uses 8-bit floating-point representation within blocks; HiF4 uses 4-bit with more aggressive quantization while maintaining the block-scale hierarchy
Format-specific encoding: Apply exponent/mantissa bit allocation optimized for NPU compute units (different from MXFP’s tile-based or NVFP4’s GPU-centric design)
Runtime dequantization: NPU hardware natively supports HiFloat formats, performing scale-and-dequantize operations inline during matrix operations

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[FP16 Weights/Activations] --> B[Block Partitioning]
    B --> C[Per-Block Scale Computation]
    C --> D[HiF8/HiF4 Encoding]
    D --> E[NPU MatMul Units]
    E --> F[Scale-Aware Accumulation]
    F --> G[FP16 Output]

Where It’s Useful:

LLM weight-only quantization (W4A16, W8A8 configurations)
KV-cache compression for long-context inference
Activation quantization in post-training quantization (PTQ) frameworks
Deployment on Ascend 910B/C NPUs where native HiFloat ops provide 2-4× throughput gains

Limitations / Caveats:

Hardware-specific: requires Ascend NPU support, not portable to NVIDIA/AMD GPUs without emulation
Block size sensitivity: optimal block size (32-128) varies by layer type; too small increases overhead, too large loses precision
4-bit regime still shows 1-3% accuracy degradation vs FP16 on challenging benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K), though better than INT4

Internal Use Cases:

LLM serving optimization: Deploy quantized Llama/Mistral models on Ascend infrastructure with 2× memory reduction (HiF8) or 4× (HiF4) while maintaining <1% accuracy loss
KV-cache management: Compress attention cache in long-context retrieval tasks (RAG systems) where high variance in key/value distributions makes INT8 unsuitable
Hybrid quantization: Combine HiF4 for weights (static, calibratable) with HiF8 for activations (dynamic, high-variance) in W4A8 mixed-precision schemes

literature/2026-02-16-Visual-RAG-Toolkit.title: Visual RAG Toolkit: Scaling Multi-Vector Visual Retrieval with Training-Free Pooling and Multi-Stage Search
literature/2026-02-16-Visual-RAG-Toolkit.content:
Visual RAG Toolkit: Scaling Multi-Vector Visual Retrieval with Training-Free Pooling and Multi-Stage Search
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #2 (score: 0.92, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Multi-vector visual retrievers (e.g., ColPali-style late interaction models) deliver strong accuracy, but scale poorly because each page yields thousands of vectors, making indexing and search increasingly expensive. We present Visual RAG Toolkit, a practical system for scaling visual multi-vector retrieval with training-free, model-aware pooling and multi-stage retrieval. Motivated by Matryoshka Embeddings, our method performs static spatial pooling - including a lightweight sliding-window averaging variant - over patch embeddings to produce compact tile-level and global representations for fast candidate generation, followed by exact MaxSim reranking using full multi-vector embeddings.   Our design yields a quadratic reduction in vector-to-vector comparisons by reducing stored vectors per page from thousands to dozens, notably without requiring post-training, adapters, or distillation. Across experiments with interaction-style models such as ColPali and ColSmol-500M, we observe that over the limited ViDoRe v2 benchmark corpus 2-stage retrieval typically preserves NDCG and Recall @ 5/10 with minimal degradation, while substantially improving throughput (approximately 4x QPS); with sensitivity mainly at very large k. The toolkit additionally provides robust preprocessing - high resolution PDF to image conversion, optional margin/empty-region cropping and token hygiene (indexing only visual tokens) - and a reproducible evaluation pipeline, enabling rapid exploration of two-, three-, and cascaded retrieval variants. By emphasizing efficiency at common cutoffs (e.g., k ⇐ 10), the toolkit lowers hardware barriers and makes state-of-the-art visual retrieval more accessible in practice.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques:

**Static spatial pooling

Multi-stage retrieval with MaxSim reranking
Training-free model-aware pooling**

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDo MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.27The Case for Multi-Vector Contrastive Pre-Training, Eliminating Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search in Large-Scale Recommendation, and More!0.25ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.25Thinking with Drafting: Optical Decompression via Logical Reconstruction0.23VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.23
Visual RAG Toolkit: Training-Free Multi-Vector Pooling for Scalable Visual Retrieval
Core Idea: Reduce multi-vector visual retrieval costs by applying static spatial pooling over patch embeddings to create compact tile-level and global representations for fast candidate generation, followed by exact MaxSim reranking with full embeddings—achieving quadratic reduction in comparisons without requiring post-training.
Problem It Solves: Multi-vector visual retrievers (ColPali-style late interaction models) produce thousands of vectors per page, making indexing and search prohibitively expensive at scale. Traditional approaches require retraining, adapters, or distillation to compress representations.
How It Works:

Input: PDF documents converted to high-resolution images with optional margin/empty-region cropping
Embedding Generation: Extract full multi-vector patch embeddings using interaction-style models (ColPali, ColSmol-500M)
Training-Free Pooling: Apply static spatial pooling (including sliding-window averaging) over patches to generate:

Compact tile-level representations (dozens of vectors instead of thousands)
Global page-level representations


Multi-Stage Retrieval:

Stage 1: Fast candidate generation using pooled tile/global vectors
Stage 2: Exact MaxSim reranking on top-k candidates using full multi-vector embeddings


Token Hygiene: Index only visual tokens, filtering out padding/special tokens

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[PDF Pages] --> B[High-Res Image Conversion]
    B --> C[Multi-Vector Encoder]
    C --> D[Full Patch Embeddings<br/>thousands per page]
    D --> E[Spatial Pooling<br/>Training-Free]
    E --> F[Compact Tiles<br/>dozens per page]
    F --> G[Stage 1: Fast Search<br/>Pooled Vectors]
    G --> H[Top-k Candidates]
    H --> I[Stage 2: MaxSim Rerank<br/>Full Embeddings]
    I --> J[Final Results]
    D -.stored for rerank.-> I

Where It’s Useful:

Visual document retrieval systems requiring high accuracy with constrained compute (RAG over scanned documents, PDFs, screenshots)
Large-scale document search where indexing cost and query throughput matter (enterprise document management, legal discovery)
Applications targeting small k values (top 5-10 results) where recall preservation is critical

Limitations / Caveats:

Sensitivity increases at very large k (e.g., k > 20), where pooling may degrade recall
Evaluation limited to ViDoRe v2 benchmark corpus—generalization to other domains needs validation
Spatial pooling assumes structured visual layouts; may be less effective for highly irregular page structures
Requires careful tuning of pooling granularity (tile size) per model and use case

Internal Use Cases:

Visual content retrieval: Scale ColPali-style retrieval for screenshot/image search in documentation systems or design asset libraries
Hybrid text-visual RAG: Combine with text embeddings for multimodal document retrieval without expensive retraining
Two-stage ranking pipelines: Apply same pooling strategy to compress multi-vector representations in candidate generation stages before LLM reranking
Resource-constrained deployment: Enable state-of-the-art visual retrieval on edge devices or lower-tier infrastructure by reducing vector storage/compute by orders of magnitude

literature/2026-02-17-Algorithmic-Simpli.title: Algorithmic Simplification of Neural Networks with Mosaic-of-Motifs
literature/2026-02-17-Algorithmic-Simpli.content:
Algorithmic Simplification of Neural Networks with Mosaic-of-Motifs
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #5 (score: 0.75, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Large-scale deep learning models are well-suited for compression. Methods like pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation have been used to achieve massive reductions in the number of model parameters, with marginal performance drops across a variety of architectures and tasks. This raises the central question: \emph{Why are deep neural networks suited for compression?} In this work, we take up the perspective of algorithmic complexity to explain this behavior. We hypothesize that the parameters of trained models have more structure and, hence, exhibit lower algorithmic complexity compared to the weights at (random) initialization. Furthermore, that model compression methods harness this reduced algorithmic complexity to compress models. Although an unconstrained parameterization of model weights, \mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n, can represent arbitrary weight assignments, the solutions found during training exhibit repeatability and structure, making them algorithmically simpler than a generic program. To this end, we formalize the Kolmogorov complexity of \mathbf{w} by \mathcal{K}(\mathbf{w}). We introduce a constrained parameterization \widehat{\mathbf{w}}, that partitions parameters into blocks of size s, and restricts each block to be selected from a set of k reusable motifs, specified by a reuse pattern (or mosaic). The resulting method, \textit{Mosaic-of-Motifs} (MoMos), yields algorithmically simpler model parameterization compared to unconstrained models. Empirical evidence from multiple experiments shows that the algorithmic complexity of neural networks, measured using approximations to Kolmogorov complexity, can be reduced during training. This results in models that perform comparably with unconstrained models while being algorithmically simpler.
Key Techniques

Pruning
quantization
knowledge distillation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityCOMPOT: Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers Compression0.17COMPOT: Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers Compression0.14Superlinear Multi-Step Attention0.14Data Distribution Matters: A Data-Centric Perspective on Context Compression for Large Language Model0.14Algorithmic Prompt-Augmentation for Efficient LLM-Based Heuristic Design for A* Search0.14
Mosaic-of-Motifs (MoMos)
Core Idea: Constrain neural network weights by partitioning them into fixed-size blocks, where each block must be selected from a small learned dictionary of reusable “motifs,” reducing the Kolmogorov complexity of the weight tensor while maintaining model performance.
Problem It Solves: Existing compression methods (pruning, quantization, distillation) lack a theoretical grounding for why trained networks are compressible; MoMos addresses this by directly parameterizing models to have low algorithmic complexity, making the compression structure explicit and learnable from the start rather than applied post-hoc.
How It Works:

Partition: Divide the full weight vector w ∈ ℝⁿ into contiguous blocks of size s, yielding ⌈n/s⌉ blocks.
Motif dictionary: Maintain a learned set of k motifs (prototype vectors of size s); each block is constrained to be exactly one of these k motifs, selected via a reuse pattern (the “mosaic”).
Joint optimization: During training, simultaneously learn the k motif vectors and the discrete mosaic assignment (reuse pattern) end-to-end, so the model converges to a solution where its Kolmogorov complexity approximation K(w) is minimized alongside task loss.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    W[Weight Tensor w] --> P[Block Partitioner\nblock size s]
    P --> M[Mosaic\nreuse pattern]
    D[Motif Dictionary\nk prototypes of size s] --> M
    M --> WH[Reconstructed Weights ŵ]
    WH --> FW[Forward Pass / Loss]
    FW --> OPT[Optimizer\nupdates motifs + mosaic]
    OPT --> D
    OPT --> M

Where It’s Useful:

Compressing large-scale vision and language models where weight matrices exhibit repetitive structure learned during training
Settings requiring interpretable or theoretically grounded compression with explicit complexity bounds
Research into the relationship between generalization, training dynamics, and algorithmic complexity of learned representations

Limitations / Caveats:

The discrete motif assignment is non-differentiable, requiring relaxations (e.g., straight-through estimators or soft assignments) that may introduce optimization instability
Compression ratio is governed by the product of k and s; poor hyperparameter choice can yield either underfitting (too few motifs) or insufficient compression (too many)
Adds complexity to the training loop compared to standard unconstrained parameterization; not a drop-in post-training method

Internal Use Cases:

LLM embedding models: Apply MoMos on embedding tables (often the largest parameter block) to compress token/item embedding matrices while preserving retrieval quality, complementing the WANDA/SparseGPT pruning pipeline noted in vault
Ranking model weight sharing: In RecSys feature towers, motif reuse across user/item embedding sub-networks could reduce parameter count while encouraging shared representations across similar feature groups
Knowledge distillation target: Use a MoMos-parameterized student network as the compression target, inheriting both the distillation signal and the explicit low-complexity inductive bias simultaneously

literature/2026-02-17-BitDance-Scaling-A.title: BitDance: Scaling Autoregressive Generative Models with Binary Tokens
literature/2026-02-17-BitDance-Scaling-A.content:
BitDance: Scaling Autoregressive Generative Models with Binary Tokens
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-15
Rank: #2 (score: 0.47, max_sim: 0.78)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
We present BitDance, a scalable autoregressive (AR) image generator that predicts binary visual tokens instead of codebook indices. With high-entropy binary latents, BitDance lets each token represent up to 2^{256} states, yielding a compact yet highly expressive discrete representation. Sampling from such a huge token space is difficult with standard classification. To resolve this, BitDance uses a binary diffusion head: instead of predicting an index with softmax, it employs continuous-space diffusion to generate the binary tokens. Furthermore, we propose next-patch diffusion, a new decoding method that predicts multiple tokens in parallel with high accuracy, greatly speeding up inference. On ImageNet 256x256, BitDance achieves an FID of 1.24, the best among AR models. With next-patch diffusion, BitDance beats state-of-the-art parallel AR models that use 1.4B parameters, while using 5.4x fewer parameters (260M) and achieving 8.7x speedup. For text-to-image generation, BitDance trains on large-scale multimodal tokens and generates high-resolution, photorealistic images efficiently, showing strong performance and favorable scaling. When generating 1024x1024 images, BitDance achieves a speedup of over 30x compared to prior AR models. We release code and models to facilitate further research on AR foundation models. Code and models are available at: github.com/shallowdream204/BitDance.
Key Techniques

Binary token prediction
binary diffusion head
next-patch diffusion

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityUniWeTok: An Unified Binary Tokenizer with Codebook Size 2^{128} for Unified Multimodal Large Language Model0.35Diffusion Generative Recommendation with Continuous Tokens0.34NanoQuant: Efficient Sub-1-Bit Quantization of Large Language Models0.33Diffusion In Diffusion: Reclaiming Global Coherence in Semi-Autoregressive Diffusion0.32DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.30
Next-Patch Diffusion (NPD)
Core Idea: A parallel autoregressive decoding method that uses continuous-space diffusion to jointly generate multiple binary visual tokens per step, breaking the sequential token-by-token bottleneck of standard autoregressive generation.
Problem It Solves: Standard AR models decode one token at a time, making inference slow—especially at high resolutions (e.g., 1024×1024). Parallel AR methods exist but typically require large models or sacrifice quality. NPD achieves fast parallel decoding with a small model by operating in binary token space rather than categorical codebook space.
How It Works:

Binary tokenization: Images are encoded into high-entropy binary latent vectors (e.g., 256-bit), where each patch is a binary vector rather than a single codebook index—yielding up to 2^256 representable states per token.
Transformer backbone (next-token prediction): A standard causal transformer processes the sequence of binary patch tokens autoregressively, producing a conditioning context for each upcoming patch or group of patches.
Binary diffusion head: Instead of a softmax classifier over a codebook, a lightweight diffusion model runs in the continuous relaxation of binary space, conditioned on the transformer’s hidden state, and iteratively denoises a noise vector into the target binary token(s).
Patch-level parallelism: At each AR step, the diffusion head jointly denoises an entire patch group (multiple tokens) in parallel, conditioned on all previously generated patches, rather than decoding one token at a time.
Sampling: The denoised continuous vector is binarized (e.g., via sign function / straight-through estimator boundary at inference) to produce discrete binary tokens, which are appended to the sequence.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Image Patches] --> B[Binary Tokenizer\ne.g. 256-bit/patch]
    B --> C[Causal Transformer\nAR backbone]
    C --> D[Hidden State\ncontext vector]
    D --> E[Binary Diffusion Head\ncontinuous denoising]
    E --> F[Binarize\nsign / STE]
    F --> G[Next N Binary Tokens]
    G -->|append| C

Where It’s Useful:

High-resolution image generation (1024×1024) where sequential decoding is a latency bottleneck
Scenarios requiring small model footprint with competitive quality (260M params beating 1.4B baselines)
Any discrete-token AR generation task where the vocabulary is too large for softmax (genomics, molecular design, audio codecs with high-bitrate codes)

Limitations / Caveats:

Binary diffusion head adds inference complexity vs. a simple linear classifier; the diffusion steps add latency per patch group, partially offsetting parallelism gains
Binarization at inference requires careful calibration—threshold/STE behavior must be consistent with training to avoid distribution shift
The 2^256 state space expressiveness is theoretical; actual coverage depends on the tokenizer training and may cluster in practice
Requires a custom binary tokenizer training pipeline, which adds engineering overhead compared to off-the-shelf VQ-VAE codebooks

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys user embeddings: Binary token representations (cf. DV365’s 4-bit quantized embeddings) could be generated autoregressively from user history sequences using a diffusion head instead of a retrieval head—enabling richer per-user generative modeling
Ranking feature generation: Parallel diffusion decoding over item feature spaces could generate diverse candidate representations for exploration in retrieval stages
Large vocabulary softmax replacement: In LLM output heads over huge item catalogs (millions of SKUs), replacing softmax with a binary diffusion head sidesteps the O(V) bottleneck—predict a binary item code and decode to the item

literature/2026-02-17-Can-I-Have-Your-Or.title: Can I Have Your Order? Monte-Carlo Tree Search for Slot Filling Ordering in Diffusion Language Models
literature/2026-02-17-Can-I-Have-Your-Or.content:
Can I Have Your Order? Monte-Carlo Tree Search for Slot Filling Ordering in Diffusion Language Models
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #1 (score: 0.88, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Diffusion, Search, RAG, LLM
Abstract
While plan-and-infill decoding in Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs) shows promise for mathematical and code reasoning, performance remains highly sensitive to slot infilling order, often yielding substantial output variance. We introduce McDiffuSE, a framework that formulates slot selection as decision making and optimises infilling orders through Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). McDiffuSE uses look-ahead simulations to evaluate partial completions before commitment, systematically exploring the combinatorial space of generation orders. Experiments show an average improvement of 3.2% over autoregressive baselines and 8.0% over baseline plan-and-infill, with notable gains of 19.5% on MBPP and 4.9% on MATH500. Our analysis reveals that while McDiffuSE predominantly follows sequential ordering, incorporating non-sequential generation is essential for maximising performance. We observe that larger exploration constants, rather than increased simulations, are necessary to overcome model confidence biases and discover effective orderings. These findings establish MCTS-based planning as an effective approach for enhancing generation quality in MDMs.
Key Techniques

Masked Diffusion Models
Monte Carlo Tree Search
look-ahead simulations

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityUnMaskFork: Test-Time Scaling for Masked Diffusion via Deterministic Action Branching0.30Improving Diffusion Language Model Decoding through Joint Search in Generation Order and Token Space0.21Context Compression via Explicit Information Transmission0.18Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.17Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.17
McDiffuSE (Monte Carlo Diffusion Slot Exploration)
Core Idea: Formulate slot infilling order selection in Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs) as a sequential decision-making problem, optimized via Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with look-ahead simulations to discover orderings that maximize generation quality.
Problem It Solves: Plan-and-infill decoding in MDMs is highly sensitive to the order in which masked slots are filled — different orderings produce high output variance, and naive or sequential heuristics leave significant performance on the table.
How It Works:

Plan: Decompose the generation target into discrete masked slots (e.g., code blocks, mathematical sub-expressions) representing a structured skeleton.
MCTS Search over Orders: Treat each partial slot-selection sequence as a tree node; run selection, expansion, simulation (look-ahead rollouts using the MDM), and backpropagation to score candidate orderings without committing to them.
Commit & Infill: Execute the highest-scoring ordering found by MCTS, filling slots sequentially in that order using the MDM’s conditional generation.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Structured Prompt\nwith Masked Slots] --> B[MCTS Planner]
    B --> C{Selection &\nExpansion}
    C --> D[Look-ahead\nSimulation via MDM]
    D --> E[Backpropagation\nof Rollout Score]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Best Ordering\nSelected]
    F --> G[MDM Infills\nSlots in Order]
    G --> H[Final Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Code generation tasks where sub-routine dependencies make ordering critical (e.g., MBPP benchmarks)
Mathematical reasoning requiring structured step ordering (e.g., MATH500)
Any MDM plan-and-infill pipeline where slot interdependencies are non-trivial

Limitations / Caveats:

Computational overhead scales with tree depth and branching factor over slots; MCTS adds inference cost beyond a single MDM forward pass
Larger exploration constants (not more simulations) are needed to overcome model confidence bias — hyperparameter sensitivity shifts rather than disappears
Sequential ordering still dominates most cases; non-sequential gains are task-specific and hard to predict a priori

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: Slot ordering analogizes to feature revelation order in cascaded ranking pipelines — MCTS could optimize which features/signals to evaluate first under budget constraints
LLM structured generation: Apply to constrained generation tasks (e.g., filling template slots in product descriptions or query rewrites) where slot interdependency affects coherence
Context Slotting: Extend the existing context slotting framework by using MCTS to determine optimal slot population order when slots have dependencies (e.g., memory before retrieved docs)

literature/2026-02-17-Cold-Start-Persona.title: Cold-Start Personalization via Training-Free Priors from Structured World Models
literature/2026-02-17-Cold-Start-Persona.content:
Cold-Start Personalization via Training-Free Priors from Structured World Models
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #2 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RecSys, LLM, RL
Abstract
Cold-start personalization requires inferring user preferences through interaction when no user-specific historical data is available. The core challenge is a routing problem: each task admits dozens of preference dimensions, yet individual users care about only a few, and which ones matter depends on who is asking. With a limited question budget, asking without structure will miss the dimensions that matter. Reinforcement learning is the natural formulation, but in multi-turn settings its terminal reward fails to exploit the factored, per-criterion structure of preference data, and in practice learned policies collapse to static question sequences that ignore user responses. We propose decomposing cold-start elicitation into offline structure learning and online Bayesian inference. Pep (Preference Elicitation with Priors) learns a structured world model of preference correlations offline from complete profiles, then performs training-free Bayesian inference online to select informative questions and predict complete preference profiles, including dimensions never asked about. The framework is modular across downstream solvers and requires only simple belief models. Across medical, mathematical, social, and commonsense reasoning, Pep achieves 80.8% alignment between generated responses and users’ stated preferences versus 68.5% for RL, with 3-5x fewer interactions. When two users give different answers to the same question, Pep changes its follow-up 39-62% of the time versus 0-28% for RL. It does so with ~10K parameters versus 8B for RL, showing that the bottleneck in cold-start elicitation is the capability to exploit the factored structure of preference data.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques/methods:

**Offline structure learning

Online Bayesian inference
Preference correlation modeling
Informative question selection**

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySLIME: Stabilized Likelihood Implicit Margin Enforcement for Preference Optimization0.22To Search or Not to Search: Aligning the Decision Boundary of Deep Search Agents via Causal Intervention0.19Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation0.19Beyond Marginal Distributions: Evaluating Demographic-Aligned LLMs0.18The Trajectory Alignment Coefficient: From Reward Tuning to Reward Learning0.17
Preference Elicitation with Priors (Pep)
Core Idea: Decompose cold-start preference elicitation into offline structure learning (a world model of preference correlations) and online training-free Bayesian inference to adaptively select questions and impute unasked preference dimensions.
Problem It Solves: Cold-start personalization systems must infer which of many preference dimensions a user cares about within a tight question budget; RL-based approaches collapse to static question sequences that ignore responses and fail to exploit the factored, per-criterion structure of preference data.
How It Works:

Offline structure learning: Train a lightweight (~10K parameter) world model on complete user preference profiles to capture pairwise and higher-order correlations across preference dimensions (e.g., “users who prefer concise answers also prefer bullet points”).
Online question selection: Given a question budget, perform Bayesian inference over the learned prior to compute the expected information gain of each candidate question conditioned on answers so far; select the highest-gain question adaptively at each turn.
Profile imputation: After the budget is exhausted, use posterior beliefs to predict preferences on all unasked dimensions, then pass the complete inferred profile to a downstream solver (e.g., an LLM) to generate a personalized response.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Complete Preference\nProfiles - Offline] --> B[World Model\nStructure Learning\n~10K params]
    B --> C[Preference\nCorrelation Priors]
    C --> D[Bayesian Inference\nEngine - Online]
    E[User Answers\nSo Far] --> D
    D --> F[Next Question\nSelection\nmax info gain]
    F --> E
    D --> G[Full Profile\nImputation]
    G --> H[Downstream Solver\ne.g. LLM]
    H --> I[Personalized\nResponse]

Where It’s Useful:

Cold-start user onboarding in conversational assistants where no interaction history exists
Medical decision support requiring rapid preference inference across clinical criteria
Personalized reasoning systems (math tutoring, legal QA) where stylistic and depth preferences vary sharply across users

Limitations / Caveats:

Offline world model assumes training profiles are representative of deployment users; distribution shift degrades prior quality
Bayesian inference quality depends on the fidelity of the learned correlation structure — sparse or noisy offline data limits imputation accuracy for rare preference combinations
Framework assumes preference dimensions are discrete and enumerable; continuous or emergent preferences are not modeled

Internal Use Cases:

Cold-start recommendation: Replace or augment LLM-generated synthetic interactions (as in the vault notes) with structured Bayesian elicitation — ask a few preference questions at signup and impute a full preference profile rather than generating pairwise interaction data
Ranking personalization: Use the learned world model as a prior over user feature weights in a learning-to-rank setup, reducing the number of explicit feedback signals needed before a reliable ranking model is available
Prompt personalization for LLM pipelines: Infer user preferences for response style, verbosity, and domain framing via a short elicitation dialogue, then inject the imputed profile as a system prompt prefix

Discussions & References

literature/2026-02-17-DHPLT-large-scale-.title: DHPLT: large-scale multilingual diachronic corpora and word representations for semantic change modelling
literature/2026-02-17-DHPLT-large-scale-.content:
DHPLT: large-scale multilingual diachronic corpora and word representations for semantic change modelling
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #3 (score: 0.65, max_sim: 0.79)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RAG, Search, Embeddings, LLM
Code: data.hplt-project.org/three/diachronic/
Abstract
In this resource paper, we present DHPLT, an open collection of diachronic corpora in 41 diverse languages. DHPLT is based on the web-crawled HPLT datasets; we use web crawl timestamps as the approximate signal of document creation time. The collection covers three time periods: 2011-2015, 2020-2021 and 2024-present (1 million documents per time period for each language). We additionally provide pre-computed word type and token embeddings and lexical substitutions for our chosen target words, while at the same time leaving it open for the other researchers to come up with their own target words using the same datasets. DHPLT aims at filling in the current lack of multilingual diachronic corpora for semantic change modelling (beyond a dozen of high-resource languages). It opens the way for a variety of new experimental setups in this field. All the resources described in this paper are available at data.hplt-project.org/three/diachronic/, sorted by language.
Key Techniques

Web-crawled corpus construction
diachronic word embeddings
multilingual semantic change analysis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityCorpus-Based Approaches to Igbo Diacritic Restoration0.32MultiLexNorm++: A Unified Benchmark and a Generative Model for Lexical Normalization for Asian Languages0.21AfriNLLB: Efficient Translation Models for African Languages0.20Dicta-LM 3.0: Advancing The Frontier of Hebrew Sovereign LLMs0.18BEHAV-E! You are Not Just a Number to Us, but an R2048 Embedding0.14
DHPLT (Diachronic HPLT)
Core Idea: A large-scale multilingual diachronic corpus collection spanning 41 languages and three time periods, built from web-crawled data with timestamp-based temporal partitioning, paired with pre-computed word embeddings and lexical substitutions for semantic change modelling.
Problem It Solves: The scarcity of multilingual diachronic corpora for semantic change detection — most existing resources cover only a handful of high-resource languages, blocking cross-lingual and typologically diverse studies of how word meanings shift over time.
How It Works:

Source: Draw from HPLT (High-Performance Language Technologies) web-crawled datasets; use web crawl timestamps as proxy for document creation time.
Partition: Split documents into three temporal bins — 2011–2015, 2020–2021, and 2024–present — sampling 1 million documents per period per language (up to 41 languages).
Represent: Pre-compute word type embeddings, token embeddings, and lexical substitutions for a curated set of target words, enabling direct plug-in for semantic change detection pipelines without re-running costly embedding steps.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[HPLT Web Crawl] --> B[Timestamp Filtering]
    B --> C1[Corpus T1\n2011–2015]
    B --> C2[Corpus T2\n2020–2021]
    B --> C3[Corpus T3\n2024–present]
    C1 & C2 & C3 --> D[1M docs/lang/period]
    D --> E[Type Embeddings]
    D --> F[Token Embeddings]
    D --> G[Lexical Substitutions]
    E & F & G --> H[Semantic Change Models]

Where It’s Useful:

Cross-lingual semantic change detection and benchmarking
Low/mid-resource NLP — providing diachronic data for languages previously lacking it
Training and evaluating temporal word representation models (e.g., diachronic word2vec, BERT-based change detectors)
Sociolinguistic and computational humanities research on lexical drift

Limitations / Caveats:

Timestamp reflects crawl time, not necessarily document creation time — introduces temporal noise, especially for older cached pages
Web-crawled text carries domain/register bias (news, blogs, forums) and quality variance across languages
1M document cap per period may undersample rare languages relative to English-scale corpora
Target word list is pre-selected; generalization to arbitrary vocabulary requires users to run their own embedding pipelines on the raw corpora

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: Detecting semantic drift in query terms or product categories over time — e.g., how “tablet” or “streaming” shifted meaning — to keep retrieval models temporally calibrated
LLM training data curation: Using temporal partitions to study or control for training data recency bias
Embedding freshness: Benchmarking how quickly dense retrieval embeddings (as in the Dense Passage Retrieval vault note) degrade on temporally shifted vocabulary, motivating periodic re-indexing strategies

Discussions & References

literature/2026-02-17-DeepImageSearch-Be.title: DeepImageSearch: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Context-Aware Image Retrieval in Visual Histories
literature/2026-02-17-DeepImageSearch-Be.content:
DeepImageSearch: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Context-Aware Image Retrieval in Visual Histories
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-11
Rank: #1 (score: 0.94, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search, RAG
Abstract
Existing multimodal retrieval systems excel at semantic matching but implicitly assume that query-image relevance can be measured in isolation. This paradigm overlooks the rich dependencies inherent in realistic visual streams, where information is distributed across temporal sequences rather than confined to single snapshots. To bridge this gap, we introduce DeepImageSearch, a novel agentic paradigm that reformulates image retrieval as an autonomous exploration task. Models must plan and perform multi-step reasoning over raw visual histories to locate targets based on implicit contextual cues. We construct DISBench, a challenging benchmark built on interconnected visual data. To address the scalability challenge of creating context-dependent queries, we propose a human-model collaborative pipeline that employs vision-language models to mine latent spatiotemporal associations, effectively offloading intensive context discovery before human verification. Furthermore, we build a robust baseline using a modular agent framework equipped with fine-grained tools and a dual-memory system for long-horizon navigation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DISBench poses significant challenges to state-of-the-art models, highlighting the necessity of incorporating agentic reasoning into next-generation retrieval systems.
Key Techniques

Agentic image retrieval paradigm
vision-language model mining for context discovery
dual-memory system for long-horizon navigation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDo MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.29VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.28OSCAR: Optimization-Steered Agentic Planning for Composed Image Retrieval0.27Youtu-VL: Unleashing Visual Potential via Unified Vision-Language Supervision0.24Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs0.23
Agentic Context-Aware Image Retrieval (DeepImageSearch)
Core Idea: Reformulates image retrieval as an autonomous multi-step exploration task where an agent plans and navigates raw visual histories using implicit contextual cues, rather than performing isolated query-image semantic matching.
Problem It Solves: Standard retrieval systems measure query-image relevance in isolation, ignoring temporal dependencies and distributed information across visual streams — failing when the relevance of a target image depends on context from surrounding frames or sequences.
How It Works:

Query intake: Agent receives a context-dependent query containing implicit cues (e.g., “the image taken just before the meeting started”) that cannot be resolved without inspecting surrounding visual history.
Multi-step planning: A modular agent decomposes the retrieval task into sub-goals, issuing tool calls (e.g., temporal navigation, region inspection, metadata lookup) against the visual history corpus.
Dual-memory navigation: A short-term working memory tracks the current search state and intermediate findings; a long-term memory retains salient observations across steps to avoid redundant exploration and support backtracking.
Target localization: The agent synthesizes accumulated evidence to rank and return the target image, grounded in cross-frame contextual reasoning rather than single-image similarity.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    Q[Context-Dependent Query] --> P[Planning Module]
    P --> T[Fine-Grained Tool Suite\ne.g. temporal nav, region crop]
    T --> SM[Short-Term Memory\ncurrent search state]
    T --> LM[Long-Term Memory\ncross-step observations]
    SM & LM --> P
    P --> R[Target Image Output]
    VH[(Visual History Corpus)] --> T

Where It’s Useful:

Egocentric video retrieval (lifelogging, body-cam footage)
Personal photo library search with implicit temporal/contextual queries
Surveillance or dashcam event localization
Multimodal dialogue systems requiring episodic visual grounding

Limitations / Caveats:

Multi-step agent loops incur significant latency vs. single-pass retrieval
Dual-memory systems require careful design to avoid stale or contradictory state accumulation over long horizons
Benchmark (DISBench) relies on human-model collaborative annotation, so query quality depends on VLM accuracy in mining latent associations before human verification

Internal Use Cases:

Session-aware ranking: Apply the dual-memory paradigm to re-ranking pipelines where relevance signals from earlier in a user session inform later retrieval steps
LLM tool-use retrieval: The modular tool framework maps directly onto RAG agents that issue structured sub-queries against multimodal corpora (cf. Adaptive Multimodal Search Planning)
Temporal RecSys: Model user interest evolution across a viewing/interaction history as a “visual stream,” using agentic planning to locate relevant items given implicit contextual preference signals

literature/2026-02-17-Embed-RL-Reinforce.title: Embed-RL: Reinforcement Learning for Reasoning-Driven Multimodal Embeddings
literature/2026-02-17-Embed-RL-Reinforce.content:
Embed-RL: Reinforcement Learning for Reasoning-Driven Multimodal Embeddings
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-14
Rank: #9 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, LLM, RAG, RL
Abstract
Leveraging Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has become pivotal for advancing Universal Multimodal Embeddings (UME) in addressing diverse cross-modal tasks. Recent studies demonstrate that incorporating generative Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning can substantially enhance task-specific representations compared to discriminative methods. However, the generated reasoning CoTs of existing generative embedding methods are limited to the textual analysis of queries and are irrelevant to the retrieval of the targets. To address these limitations, we propose a reasoning-driven UME framework that integrates Embedder-Guided Reinforcement Learning (EG-RL) to optimize the Reasoner to produce evidential Traceability CoT (T-CoT). Our key contributions are threefold: (1) We design an EG-RL framework where the Embedder provides explicit supervision to the Reasoner, ensuring the generated CoT traces are aligned with embedding tasks. (2) We introduce T-CoT, which extracts critical multimodal cues to focus on retrieval-relevant elements and provides multimodal inputs for the Embedder. (3) With limited computational resources, our framework outperforms the pioneering embedding model on both MMEB-V2 and UVRB benchmarks. The integration of multimodal evidence in structured reasoning, paired with retrieval-oriented alignment, effectively strengthens cross-modal semantic consistency and boosts the fine-grained matching capability of the model as well as the generalization across complex scenarios. Our work demonstrates that targeted reasoning optimization can significantly improve multimodal embedding quality, providing a practical and efficient solution for reasoning-driven UME development.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques or methods:

Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning
Embedder-Guided Reinforcement Learning (EG-RL)
Traceability CoT (T-CoT)

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDo MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.29SwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMs0.27ImgCoT: Compressing Long Chain of Thought into Compact Visual Tokens for Efficient Reasoning of Large Language Model0.26VisionTrim: Unified Vision Token Compression for Training-Free MLLM Acceleration0.22Chain-of-thought obfuscation learned from output supervision can generalise to unseen tasks0.22
Embedder-Guided Reinforcement Learning (EG-RL)
Core Idea: A reinforcement learning framework where a frozen Embedder model provides reward signals to train a Reasoner (MLLM) to generate retrieval-aligned Chain-of-Thought traces, replacing task-agnostic text reasoning with embedding-task-aware multimodal evidence extraction.
Problem It Solves: Existing generative embedding methods generate CoT reasoning that analyzes queries in isolation — the reasoning is irrelevant to what makes a good retrieval match, so it fails to improve cross-modal semantic alignment or fine-grained matching.
How It Works:

Reasoner generates T-CoT: Given a multimodal query (image + text), the Reasoner MLLM produces a Traceability Chain-of-Thought that extracts retrieval-relevant visual and textual cues (e.g., specific objects, attributes, spatial relationships) rather than generic descriptions.
Embedder scores the reasoning: The T-CoT plus original query is passed to the Embedder, which computes similarity against candidate targets; the retrieval quality (e.g., ranking score) becomes the RL reward signal — the Embedder supervises the Reasoner directly.
Policy optimization closes the loop: The Reasoner is updated via RL (e.g., GRPO-style) to maximize Embedder reward, steering CoT generation toward traces that causally improve embedding retrieval rather than surface-level verbal descriptions.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    Q["Multimodal Query\n(image + text)"] --> R["Reasoner\n(MLLM, trainable)"]
    R --> TC["T-CoT\n(Traceability CoT)"]
    TC --> E["Embedder\n(frozen)"]
    Q --> E
    E --> S["Retrieval Score\n(reward signal)"]
    S -->|RL gradient| R
    E --> EMB["Query Embedding\n→ cross-modal retrieval"]

Where It’s Useful:

Universal multimodal retrieval where queries and targets span mixed image/text modalities (e.g., composed image retrieval, VQA-style search)
Fine-grained cross-modal matching requiring focus on specific visual attributes or relational cues
Any setting where embedding quality is the downstream objective but reasoning generation is the lever being optimized

Limitations / Caveats:

The Embedder must be strong enough to provide reliable reward signal — a weak Embedder produces noisy supervision that can mislead Reasoner training
T-CoT quality is bounded by the Reasoner’s visual grounding capability; if the MLLM cannot identify fine-grained visual cues, RL cannot elicit them
RL training instability and reward hacking risks apply — the Reasoner may learn to produce CoTs that exploit Embedder blind spots rather than genuinely improving retrieval

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: Use an EG-RL-style loop where a ranking model scores candidate items and its signal trains a query rewriter or reasoning module — directly optimizing for rank quality rather than generation fluency
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): Train a query expansion or chain-of-thought module with retrieval precision as RL reward, replacing heuristic prompt engineering
Multimodal product search: Ground reasoning in product-relevant attributes (color, shape, brand) by having the Embedder reward traces that recover visually similar catalog items

Discussions & References
