literature/2026-02-17-LLMStructBench-Ben.title: LLMStructBench: Benchmarking Large Language Model Structured Data Extraction
literature/2026-02-17-LLMStructBench-Ben.content:
LLMStructBench: Benchmarking Large Language Model Structured Data Extraction
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #6 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Search, LLM, RAG
Abstract
We present LLMStructBench, a novel benchmark for evaluating Large Language Models (LLMs) on extracting structured data and generating valid JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) outputs from natural-language text. Our open dataset comprises diverse, manually verified parsing scenarios of varying complexity and enables systematic testing across 22 models and five prompting strategies. We further introduce complementary performance metrics that capture both token-level accuracy and document-level validity, facilitating rigorous comparison of model, size, and prompting effects on parsing reliability.   In particular, we show that choosing the right prompting strategy is more important than standard attributes such as model size. This especially ensures structural validity for smaller or less reliable models but increase the number of semantic errors. Our benchmark suite is an step towards future research in the area of LLM applied to parsing or Extract, Transform and Load (ETL) applications.
Key Techniques

JSON schema validation
prompt engineering
structural validity metrics

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityScrapeGraphAI-100k: A Large-Scale Dataset for LLM-Based Web Information Extraction0.27Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.13LogicSkills: A Structured Benchmark for Formal Reasoning in Large Language Models0.13CoDa: Constrained Generation based Data Augmentation for Low-Resource NLP0.13A Field Guide to Automatic Evaluation of LLM-Generated Summaries0.12
LLMStructBench Benchmark Suite
Core Idea: A systematic benchmark that evaluates LLM structured data extraction by combining token-level accuracy and document-level validity metrics across diverse prompting strategies and model sizes.
Problem It Solves: No standardized benchmark existed for rigorously comparing LLMs on JSON extraction from natural language, making it difficult to assess prompting strategy vs. model size trade-offs for ETL/parsing pipelines.
How It Works:

Dataset Construction: Curate diverse, manually verified parsing scenarios of varying complexity (simple key-value extraction to nested/schema-constrained JSON), covering real-world ETL edge cases.
Multi-axis Evaluation: Run 22 models × 5 prompting strategies (e.g., zero-shot, few-shot, schema-constrained, chain-of-thought variants) against each scenario.
Dual Metric Scoring: Measure both token-level accuracy (field-by-field correctness) and document-level validity (is the output parseable, schema-conformant JSON?), separating structural errors from semantic errors.

Architecture (if applicable):
flowchart LR
    A[Natural Language Text] --> B[Prompting Strategy Layer\nzero-shot / few-shot / CoT / schema-constrained]
    B --> C[LLM Inference\n22 models]
    C --> D[JSON Output]
    D --> E1[Token-Level Accuracy\nfield correctness]
    D --> E2[Document-Level Validity\nparseable + schema-valid]
    E1 --> F[Benchmark Score]
    E2 --> F

Where It’s Useful:

ETL pipelines that ingest unstructured text and require reliable structured output
Selecting LLMs or prompting strategies for document parsing, form extraction, and data normalization tasks
Evaluating smaller/quantized models where JSON validity is a known failure mode

Limitations / Caveats:

Prompting strategies that improve structural validity tend to increase semantic errors — no single strategy dominates on both metrics simultaneously
Dataset is static; novel document domains or schema types not in the benchmark may not generalize
Evaluation focuses on JSON; findings may not transfer directly to other structured formats (XML, YAML, SQL)

Internal Use Cases:

Retriever/ranking pipelines: When LLMs are used to extract structured features (e.g., query intent, entity slots) for ranking, this benchmark’s prompting findings directly inform which strategy minimizes malformed outputs in production
LLM-as-ETL for RecSys: Ingesting product catalogs, user reviews, or interaction logs into structured schemas — benchmark guides model + prompt selection to balance field accuracy vs. parse reliability
Smaller model deployment: The finding that prompting strategy matters more than model size supports using smaller, cheaper models with better-engineered prompts in cost-sensitive serving environments

literature/2026-02-17-Language-Model-Mem.title: Language Model Memory and Memory Models for Language
literature/2026-02-17-Language-Model-Mem.content:
Language Model Memory and Memory Models for Language
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #2 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, LLM, RAG
Abstract
The ability of machine learning models to store input information in hidden layer vector embeddings, analogous to the concept of `memory’, is widely employed but not well characterized. We find that language model embeddings typically contain relatively little input information regardless of data and compute scale during training. In contrast, embeddings from autoencoders trained for input regeneration are capable of nearly perfect memory formation. The substitution of memory embeddings for token sequences leads to substantial computational efficiencies, motivating the introduction of a parallelizable encoder-decoder memory model architecture. Upon causal training these models contain information-poor embeddings incapable of arbitrary information access, but by combining causal and information retention objective functions they learn to form and decode information-rich memories. Training can be further streamlined by freezing a high fidelity encoder followed by a curriculum training approach where decoders first learn to process memories and then learn to additionally predict next tokens. We introduce the perspective that next token prediction training alone is poorly suited for accurate memory formation as the objective itself is non-invertible, motivating the use of combined objective functions for models where the entire input is not exposed.
Key Techniques

Encoder-decoder architecture
information retention objective functions
curriculum training approach

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMemFly: On-the-Fly Memory Optimization via Information Bottleneck0.21Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation0.20Recurrent Preference Memory for Efficient Long-Sequence Generative Recommendation0.20Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.20Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.20
Causal Encoder-Decoder Memory Model (CEDMM)
Core Idea: A parallelizable encoder-decoder architecture trained with combined causal language modeling and information-retention objectives, enabling language models to form and decode information-rich memory embeddings instead of relying solely on next-token prediction.
Problem It Solves: Standard causal language model training produces information-poor embeddings because next-token prediction is a non-invertible objective — the model never needs to recover full input content from a single embedding, so embeddings compress poorly and cannot serve as faithful memory representations.
How It Works:

Encoder pre-training: Train an autoencoder purely on input reconstruction (regeneration objective) to produce high-fidelity dense embeddings that capture nearly complete input information.
Freeze encoder, train decoder (Stage 1): Fix the encoder weights; train the decoder to process and reconstruct content from memory embeddings — teaching it to “read” memories before learning prediction.
Curriculum fine-tuning (Stage 2): Extend decoder training with a combined objective: information retention loss (reconstruct input from embedding) + causal next-token prediction loss, so the model learns both memory fidelity and generation.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    T[Token Sequence] --> E[Frozen Encoder]
    E --> M[Memory Embedding]
    M --> D[Decoder]
    D --> R[Reconstructed Input\n+ Next Token Prediction]
    subgraph Combined Objective
        R --> L1[Reconstruction Loss]
        R --> L2[Causal LM Loss]
    end

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context compression: replacing token sequences with compact memory embeddings reduces KV-cache and attention costs
Retrieval-augmented generation: storing retrieved passages as memory embeddings rather than raw tokens
Efficient inference over repeated or cached contexts

Limitations / Caveats:

Causal training alone degrades embedding informativeness; combined objectives add training complexity and require tuning loss weighting
The frozen-encoder assumption means encoder quality is a hard ceiling on memory fidelity
Curriculum staging adds implementation overhead and requires careful scheduling between reconstruction and prediction phases

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: User session histories could be compressed into memory embeddings rather than truncated token sequences, preserving more behavioral signal within fixed compute budgets
LLM serving: High-traffic repeated system prompts or product catalogs encoded once as memory embeddings, decoded on demand — reducing prefill cost substantially
RAG pipelines: Replace chunked passage tokens with encoder-produced memory embeddings as a more compute-efficient retrieval unit, especially relevant when combined with sparse autoencoder interpretability work for debugging what information is retained

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-17-LogitsCoder-Toward.title: LogitsCoder: Towards Efficient Chain-of-Thought Path Search via Logits Preference Decoding for Code Generation
literature/2026-02-17-LogitsCoder-Toward.content:
LogitsCoder: Towards Efficient Chain-of-Thought Path Search via Logits Preference Decoding for Code Generation
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-15
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, RAG
Abstract
Code generation remains a challenging task that requires precise and structured reasoning. Existing Test Time Scaling (TTS) methods, including structured tree search, have made progress in exploring reasoning paths but still face two major challenges: (1) underthinking, where reasoning chains tend to be shallow and fail to capture the full complexity of problems; and (2) overthinking, where overly verbose reasoning leads to inefficiency and increased computational costs. To address these issues, we propose LogitsCoder, a novel framework that enhances chain-of-thought reasoning through lightweight, logit-level control mechanisms for code generation. LogitsCoder iteratively generates and refines reasoning steps by first steering token selection toward statistically preferred patterns via Logits Preference Decoding, then selecting and aggregating diverse reasoning paths using Logits Rank Based Path Selection and Thoughts Aggregation. This results in coherent and effective reasoning chains that balance depth and efficiency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LogitsCoder produces more efficient and higher-quality reasoning chains, leading to superior code generation performance compared to baseline methods.
Key Techniques

Logits Preference Decoding
Logits Rank Based Path Selection
Thoughts Aggregation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning0.30Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning0.28Why Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models0.25Why Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models0.23Is my model “mind blurting”? Interpreting the dynamics of reasoning tokens with Recurrence Quantification Analysis (RQA)0.23
LogitsCoder
Core Idea: A test-time scaling framework that controls chain-of-thought reasoning at the logit level—biasing token selection toward statistically preferred patterns, then ranking and aggregating diverse reasoning paths—to balance reasoning depth against verbosity for code generation.
Problem It Solves: Existing tree-search TTS methods suffer from underthinking (shallow reasoning chains that miss problem complexity) and overthinking (verbose chains that waste compute); LogitsCoder addresses both by steering generation quality at the token distribution level rather than through coarse sampling or external verifiers.
How It Works:

Logits Preference Decoding: At each decoding step, adjust the raw logit distribution to upweight tokens that match statistically preferred patterns (e.g., tokens associated with correct reasoning steps in training data), steering the model toward higher-quality token choices without fine-tuning.
Logits Rank Based Path Selection: Generate multiple candidate reasoning chains, then score and rank them using logit-derived confidence signals—selecting paths where the model assigns consistently high probability mass to its own outputs, filtering low-confidence or incoherent chains.
Thoughts Aggregation: Merge the top-ranked diverse reasoning paths into a single coherent chain-of-thought, then decode the final code solution from the aggregated reasoning context.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Problem Prompt] --> B[Logits Preference Decoding]
    B --> C[N Candidate CoT Paths]
    C --> D[Logits Rank Based Path Selection]
    D --> E[Top-K Paths]
    E --> F[Thoughts Aggregation]
    F --> G[Final Code Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Code generation tasks requiring structured multi-step reasoning (e.g., competitive programming, algorithmic problems)
Any LLM inference pipeline where test-time compute budget must be controlled without model retraining
Scenarios where process reward models are unavailable or too costly to run

Limitations / Caveats:

Logit-level preference signals depend on the base model’s calibration—poorly calibrated models may produce misleading confidence scores for path ranking
The “statistically preferred patterns” used in Logits Preference Decoding require a reference distribution, implying some offline data collection or profiling step that may not generalize across domains
Aggregating diverse paths into a single coherent chain is non-trivial; incoherent merges could degrade rather than improve final output quality

Internal Use Cases:

LLM-based ranking/reranking: The logit rank signal is a lightweight proxy for a process reward model—applicable to ranking candidate responses in retrieval-augmented generation pipelines (cf. Monte Carlo Tree Search path scoring) without a separate verifier model
Self-consistency extension: Replaces naive majority voting in Self-Consistency Decoding with logit-confidence-weighted path selection, potentially improving answer quality on reasoning-heavy RecSys query understanding tasks
Query rewriting diversity control: The overthinking/underthinking framing maps directly to controlling verbosity in chain-of-thought query expansion for retrieval systems

literature/2026-02-17-MixFormer-Co-Scali.title: MixFormer: Co-Scaling Up Dense and Sequence in Industrial Recommenders
literature/2026-02-17-MixFormer-Co-Scali.content:
MixFormer: Co-Scaling Up Dense and Sequence in Industrial Recommenders
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-15
Rank: #10 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG, Transformer, RecSys
Abstract
As industrial recommender systems enter a scaling-driven regime, Transformer architectures have become increasingly attractive for scaling models towards larger capacity and longer sequence. However, existing Transformer-based recommendation models remain structurally fragmented, where sequence modeling and feature interaction are implemented as separate modules with independent parameterization. Such designs introduce a fundamental co-scaling challenge, as model capacity must be suboptimally allocated between dense feature interaction and sequence modeling under a limited computational budget. In this work, we propose MixFormer, a unified Transformer-style architecture tailored for recommender systems, which jointly models sequential behaviors and feature interactions within a single backbone. Through a unified parameterization, MixFormer enables effective co-scaling across both dense capacity and sequence length, mitigating the trade-off observed in decoupled designs. Moreover, the integrated architecture facilitates deep interaction between sequential and non-sequential representations, allowing high-order feature semantics to directly inform sequence aggregation and enhancing overall expressiveness. To ensure industrial practicality, we further introduce a user-item decoupling strategy for efficiency optimizations that significantly reduce redundant computation and inference latency. Extensive experiments on large-scale industrial datasets demonstrate that MixFormer consistently exhibits superior accuracy and efficiency. Furthermore, large-scale online A/B tests on two production recommender systems, Douyin and Douyin Lite, show consistent improvements in user engagement metrics, including active days and in-app usage duration.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text about MixFormer
Co-scaling strategy for dense capacity and sequence length
User-item decoupling for efficiency optimization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityCompute Only Once: UG-Separation for Efficient Large Recommendation Models0.27OneMall: One Model, More Scenarios — End-to-End Generative Recommender Family at Kuaishou E-Commerce0.23Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression0.22Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression0.22Accelerating Generative Recommendation via Simple Categorical User Sequence Compression0.22
MixFormer
Core Idea: A unified Transformer backbone that jointly parameterizes dense feature interaction and sequential behavior modeling in a single architecture, enabling co-scaling of both dimensions under a shared computational budget.
Problem It Solves: Existing Transformer-based recommenders decouple sequence modeling and feature interaction into separate modules with independent parameters, forcing suboptimal capacity allocation between the two when scaling under a fixed compute budget — you can’t scale both simultaneously without one cannibalizing the other.
How It Works:

Unified backbone: Rather than a separate MLP tower for feature interactions and a separate Transformer for sequence modeling, MixFormer processes both through shared Transformer layers — dense feature embeddings and sequential item tokens are co-encoded in a single forward pass.
Joint parameterization: Attention mechanisms simultaneously attend over non-sequential (dense) feature representations and sequential behavior tokens, allowing high-order feature semantics to directly gate and weight sequence aggregation (i.e., item importance in the history is informed by user/context features).
User-item decoupling for inference: At serving time, item-side representations are precomputed and cached independently of user-side computation, eliminating redundant re-encoding of items across user requests and reducing inference latency to practical industrial levels.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Dense Features\nuser/context/item attrs] --> C[Unified MixFormer\nTransformer Layers]
    B[Sequential Tokens\nuser history events] --> C
    C --> D[Joint Attention\ndense ↔ sequence]
    D --> E[Ranking Score]
    D --> F[User-side cache]
    D --> G[Item-side cache\nprecomputed]
    F & G --> E

Where It’s Useful:

Industrial-scale recommender systems where user history length and feature interaction depth both need to grow
Ranking models on platforms with rich behavioral logs (watch history, clicks, dwell time) alongside dense categorical/continuous features
Any retrieval or ranking pipeline where compute budget forces a trade-off between model capacity and context length

Limitations / Caveats:

Unified architecture increases training complexity — joint optimization of heterogeneous inputs (dense + sequential) requires careful initialization and potentially curriculum strategies
User-item decoupling assumes item representations can be usefully separated from user context at inference; this approximation may lose some cross-attention fidelity
Industrial results are on Douyin-scale data; benefits at smaller data regimes are unverified

Internal Use Cases:

Co-scaling experiments: MixFormer directly operationalizes the insight from Context-Length Scaling in Sequential Recommender Transformers — instead of choosing between longer sequences or bigger dense towers, MixFormer scales both jointly; worth testing whether the same scaling curves hold under unified vs. decoupled parameterization
HSTU comparison: Hierarchical Sequential Transduction Unit (HSTU) handles long histories via hierarchical simplifications for efficiency; MixFormer takes the opposite bet (full Transformer, but unified) — the user-item decoupling trick is the analogous efficiency lever; interesting ablation target
Parameter efficiency angle: TokenFormer’s tokenized-parameter framing suggests MixFormer’s dense features could themselves be treated as “parameter tokens,” potentially enabling incremental capacity scaling without full retraining

literature/2026-02-17-Nanbeige41-3B-A-Sm.title: Nanbeige4.1-3B: A Small General Model that Reasons, Aligns, and Acts
literature/2026-02-17-Nanbeige41-3B-A-Sm.content:
Nanbeige4.1-3B: A Small General Model that Reasons, Aligns, and Acts
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #2 (score: 0.71, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RL, Search
Abstract
We present Nanbeige4.1-3B, a unified generalist language model that simultaneously achieves strong agentic behavior, code generation, and general reasoning with only 3B parameters. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first open-source small language model (SLM) to achieve such versatility in a single model. To improve reasoning and preference alignment, we combine point-wise and pair-wise reward modeling, ensuring high-quality, human-aligned responses. For code generation, we design complexity-aware rewards in Reinforcement Learning, optimizing both correctness and efficiency. In deep search, we perform complex data synthesis and incorporate turn-level supervision during training. This enables stable long-horizon tool interactions, allowing Nanbeige4.1-3B to reliably execute up to 600 tool-call turns for complex problem-solving. Extensive experimental results show that Nanbeige4.1-3B significantly outperforms prior models of similar scale, such as Nanbeige4-3B-2511 and Qwen3-4B, even achieving superior performance compared to much larger models, such as Qwen3-30B-A3B. Our results demonstrate that small models can achieve both broad competence and strong specialization simultaneously, redefining the potential of 3B parameter models.
Key Techniques

Here are the key techniques from the paper:

Reward modeling (point-wise and pair-wise)

Complexity-aware reinforcement learning for code generation
Deep search with data synthesis and turn-level supervision

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.27ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.26ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.26SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.24On the Power of (Approximate) Reward Models for Inference-Time Scaling0.23
Complexity-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Code Generation
Core Idea: A reinforcement learning training scheme that rewards code solutions based on both correctness and computational efficiency, using problem complexity as a scaling factor for efficiency rewards.
Problem It Solves: Standard RL-for-code approaches optimize solely for pass/fail correctness, producing solutions that pass tests but may be algorithmically inefficient — failing to distinguish an O(n²) brute-force from an O(n log n) optimal solution.
How It Works:

Correctness signal: Execute generated code against test cases; award a binary pass/fail reward as the base signal.
Complexity classification: Classify each problem’s expected time/space complexity (e.g., O(n), O(n log n), O(n²)) using problem metadata or a trained classifier.
Efficiency reward shaping: Measure the runtime/memory of the generated solution and scale the efficiency bonus by the problem’s complexity tier — harder problems (where efficiency matters more) receive amplified rewards for efficient solutions.
Combined reward: Final reward = correctness signal + λ × complexity-scaled efficiency bonus, used to update the policy via standard RL (e.g., GRPO or PPO).

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    P[Problem Prompt] --> G[LLM Policy]
    G --> C[Generated Code]
    C --> E[Execution Engine]
    E --> CR[Correctness Reward]
    P --> CX[Complexity Classifier]
    CX --> W[Complexity Weight λ]
    E --> RT[Runtime / Memory]
    RT --> ER[Efficiency Reward]
    W --> ER
    CR --> R[Combined Reward]
    ER --> R
    R --> G

Where It’s Useful:

Competitive programming / algorithmic problem solving where runtime bounds matter
Code generation for resource-constrained environments (edge, mobile, embedded)
Training models where downstream code will run at scale and compute cost is a real concern

Limitations / Caveats:

Complexity classification is noisy — misclassifying a problem’s expected complexity can produce misaligned rewards
Efficiency measurement is environment-dependent (hardware, test input distribution); rewards may not generalize across execution environments
Only applicable where runtime/memory can be measured; ill-suited for code with side effects or non-deterministic behavior

Internal Use Cases:

Training a code-assist model for ranking/RecSys feature engineering, where generated feature transforms need to be efficient at inference scale (O(n) over millions of items)
Fine-tuning LLM agents that generate pipeline or ETL code, penalizing unnecessarily expensive joins or redundant passes over large datasets
Reward shaping for any RL-trained code agent operating in a latency-sensitive production environment

literature/2026-02-17-Pailitao-VL-Unifie.title: Pailitao-VL: Unified Embedding and Reranker for Real-Time Multi-Modal Industrial Search
literature/2026-02-17-Pailitao-VL-Unifie.content:
Pailitao-VL: Unified Embedding and Reranker for Real-Time Multi-Modal Industrial Search
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-14
Rank: #5 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, LLM, RAG, RecSys, Search
Abstract
In this work, we presented Pailitao-VL, a comprehensive multi-modal retrieval system engineered for high-precision, real-time industrial search. We here address three critical challenges in the current SOTA solution: insufficient retrieval granularity, vulnerability to environmental noise, and prohibitive efficiency-performance gap. Our primary contribution lies in two fundamental paradigm shifts. First, we transitioned the embedding paradigm from traditional contrastive learning to an absolute ID-recognition task. Through anchoring instances to a globally consistent latent space defined by billions of semantic prototypes, we successfully overcome the stochasticity and granularity bottlenecks inherent in existing embedding solutions. Second, we evolved the generative reranker from isolated pointwise evaluation to the compare-and-calibrate listwise policy. By synergizing chunk-based comparative reasoning with calibrated absolute relevance scoring, the system achieves nuanced discriminative resolution while circumventing the prohibitive latency typically associated with conventional reranking methods. Extensive offline benchmarks and online A/B tests on Alibaba e-commerce platform confirm that Pailitao-VL achieves state-of-the-art performance and delivers substantial business impact. This work demonstrates a robust and scalable path for deploying advanced MLLM-based retrieval architectures in demanding, large-scale production environments.
Key Techniques

Absolute ID-recognition embedding paradigm
Compare-and-calibrate listwise reranking policy
Multi-modal retrieval with semantic prototype anchoring

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPailitao-VL: Unified Embedding and Reranker for Real-Time Multi-Modal Industrial Search0.83Generative Reasoning Re-ranker0.20Mine and Refine: Optimizing Graded Relevance in E-commerce Search Retrieval0.17jina-embeddings-v5-text: Task-Targeted Embedding Distillation0.17ProtoQuant: Quantization of Prototypical Parts For General and Fine-Grained Image Classification0.17
Pailitao-VL: Unified Embedding and Reranker for Real-Time Multi-Modal Industrial Search
Core Idea: A two-stage multi-modal retrieval system that replaces contrastive embedding with absolute ID-recognition against semantic prototypes, and replaces pointwise reranking with a chunk-based listwise compare-and-calibrate policy.
Problem It Solves: Three failure modes in production multi-modal search: (1) contrastive learning’s stochastic, coarse-grained embedding space that lacks globally consistent anchoring; (2) sensitivity to environmental noise (background clutter, lighting, occlusion) in visual queries; (3) the latency-vs-quality tradeoff in reranking, where listwise methods are accurate but too slow for real-time use.
How It Works:

Embedding stage — ID-Recognition Paradigm: Rather than learning pairwise similarities via contrastive loss, each item is anchored to one of billions of semantic prototype IDs in a globally consistent latent space. The encoder (MLLM backbone) learns to classify/recognize which prototype a query or document maps to, yielding fine-grained, deterministic embeddings.
Retrieval: Approximate nearest-neighbor search over the prototype-anchored embedding space returns a candidate set; global consistency of the space makes retrieval robust to noise by grounding representations in semantics rather than relative instance distances.
Reranking stage — Compare-and-Calibrate Listwise Policy: Candidates are processed in chunks; within each chunk the model performs comparative reasoning (relative ordering) and simultaneously produces calibrated absolute relevance scores. The chunk-based decomposition keeps latency bounded while preserving listwise discriminative power.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    Q[Query\nimage+text] --> ENC[MLLM Encoder]
    ENC --> PROTO[Prototype ID\nRecognition\nbillions of anchors]
    PROTO --> ANN[ANN Retrieval\nfrom Index]
    ANN --> CANDS[Candidate Set]
    CANDS --> CHUNK[Chunk Splitter]
    CHUNK --> CMP[Compare-and-Calibrate\nListwise Reranker]
    CMP --> ABS[Absolute Score\nCalibration]
    ABS --> OUT[Ranked Results]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale e-commerce product search with noisy user-generated image queries (photos of items “in the wild”)
Any multi-modal retrieval pipeline where query distribution has high visual variance and low-latency SLA constraints
Industrial search systems requiring both recall quality (embedding) and precision (reranking) at scale

Limitations / Caveats:

The prototype space requires maintaining and updating billions of semantic anchors — significant infrastructure overhead for index freshness as catalog evolves
Chunk-based listwise reranking still adds latency over pointwise; chunk size becomes a tuning knob that trades quality for speed
Evaluation is on Alibaba’s proprietary e-commerce distribution; generalization to other domains (e.g., medical imaging, documents) is unverified
ID-recognition paradigm may struggle with genuinely novel items not represented in the prototype space (cold-start problem)

Internal Use Cases:

RecSys retrieval tower: Swap contrastive two-tower training for prototype ID-recognition to get more stable, granular item embeddings — directly applicable to candidate generation in recommendation
Multimodal ranking: The compare-and-calibrate listwise reranker pattern maps cleanly onto LLM-based reranking work (cf. MixLM’s mix-interaction approach) — could combine chunk-wise comparison with MixLM’s embedding-generation hybrid to reduce reranker latency
Addressing theoretical embedding limits: The prototype anchoring approach is a practical workaround for the fundamental single-vector limitations noted in the Weller et al. theoretical work — worth benchmarking against those failure cases

Discussions & References

literature/2026-02-17-Residual-Connectio.title: Residual Connections and the Causal Shift: Uncovering a Structural Misalignment in Transformers
literature/2026-02-17-Residual-Connectio.content:
Residual Connections and the Causal Shift: Uncovering a Structural Misalignment in Transformers
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Embeddings, LLM, Transformer
Abstract
Large Language Models (LLMs) are trained with next-token prediction, implemented in autoregressive Transformers via causal masking for parallelism. This creates a subtle misalignment: residual connections tie activations to the current token, while supervision targets the next token, potentially propagating mismatched information if the current token is not the most informative for prediction. In this work, we empirically localize this input-output alignment shift in pretrained LLMs, using decoding trajectories over tied embedding spaces and similarity-based metrics. Our experiments reveal that the hidden token representations switch from input alignment to output alignment deep within the network. Motivated by this observation, we propose a lightweight residual-path mitigation based on residual attenuation, implemented either as a fixed-layer intervention or as a learnable gating mechanism. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that these strategies alleviate the representation misalignment and yield improvements, providing an efficient and general architectural enhancement for autoregressive Transformers.
Key Techniques

Decoding trajectories over tied embedding spaces
similarity-based metrics
residual attenuation
learnable gating mechanism

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySpanNorm: Reconciling Training Stability and Performance in Deep Transformers0.20treaming-dLLM: Accelerating Diffusion LLMs via Suffix Pruning and Dynamic Decoding0.16RQ-GMM: Residual Quantized Gaussian Mixture Model for Multimodal Semantic Discretization in CTR Prediction0.14Inverse Depth Scaling From Most Layers Being Similar0.14Closing the Loop: A Control-Theoretic Framework for Provably Stable Time Series Forecasting with LLMs0.13
Residual Attenuation for Causal Shift Mitigation
Core Idea: Attenuate early-layer residual stream contributions to reduce the carry-forward influence of the current token’s representation, correcting the structural misalignment between residual connections (anchored to input token) and next-token prediction targets.
Problem It Solves: In autoregressive Transformers, residual connections propagate the current token’s embedding throughout the network, but the supervision signal targets the next token. This creates a representational misalignment where early layers bias hidden states toward the input token identity rather than the prediction target, degrading model output quality.
How It Works:

Localize the alignment shift: Use decoding trajectories over tied embedding spaces (logit lens) and cosine similarity metrics to identify the layer depth at which hidden states transition from input-token alignment to output-token alignment.
Apply residual attenuation: At a target layer (fixed or learned), scale down the residual path contribution — either via a fixed scalar multiplier applied to the residual stream, or a learnable gating vector that modulates per-dimension attenuation.
Fine-tune or directly deploy: The fixed-layer variant requires no additional training (direct intervention); the learnable gating is trained with a small parameter overhead, allowing the model to adapt the attenuation magnitude end-to-end.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Token Embedding + Residual Stream] --> B[Early Layers\ninput-aligned]
    B --> C{Residual Attenuation\nFixed scalar or\nLearnable gate}
    C --> D[Deep Layers\noutput-aligned]
    D --> E[Next-Token Logits]

Where It’s Useful:

Any pretrained autoregressive LLM where inference-time or lightweight fine-tune improvements are desired without full retraining
Settings with constrained compute budgets where architectural surgery (e.g., full retraining with modified residuals) is infeasible
Analysis of representational geometry in transformer hidden states (logit lens interpretability)

Limitations / Caveats:

The optimal intervention layer must be identified per-model, requiring profiling with similarity metrics before deployment
Fixed scalar attenuation is a blunt instrument — uniform scaling may suppress useful residual information alongside the misaligned signal
Gains are likely incremental; does not address other sources of train/inference mismatch (e.g., exposure bias, teacher forcing)

Internal Use Cases:

LLM-based ranking/reranking: If using an LLM’s hidden states as relevance signals, attenuation could shift representations toward output-predictive features earlier, improving signal quality for retrieval
Context Reordering (see vault note): Residual attenuation could complement context reordering by ensuring the model’s internal state is output-aligned before processing the most critical context placed at the end of the prompt
Early-Exit LLMs (see vault note): Knowing the input→output alignment transition layer informs where early exit thresholds should be placed — exits before the transition point would yield input-biased predictions; attenuation could lower that transition depth, enabling earlier reliable exits

Discussions & References

literature/2026-02-17-S2D-Selective-Spec.title: S2D: Selective Spectral Decay for Quantization-Friendly Conditioning of Neural Activations
literature/2026-02-17-S2D-Selective-Spec.content:
S2D: Selective Spectral Decay for Quantization-Friendly Conditioning of Neural Activations
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #4 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Activation outliers in large-scale transformer models pose a fundamental challenge to model quantization, creating excessively large ranges that cause severe accuracy drops during quantization. We empirically observe that outlier severity intensifies with pre-training scale (e.g., progressing from CLIP to the more extensively trained SigLIP and SigLIP2). Through theoretical analysis as well as empirical correlation studies, we establish the direct link between these activation outliers and dominant singular values of the weights. Building on this insight, we propose Selective Spectral Decay (S^2D), a geometrically-principled conditioning method that surgically regularizes only the weight components corresponding to the largest singular values during fine-tuning. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that S^2D significantly reduces activation outliers and produces well-conditioned representations that are inherently quantization-friendly. Models trained with S^2D achieve up to 7% improved PTQ accuracy on ImageNet under W4A4 quantization and 4% gains when combined with QAT. These improvements also generalize across downstream tasks and vision-language models, enabling the scaling of increasingly large and rigorously trained models without sacrificing deployment efficiency.
Key Techniques

Selective Spectral Decay (S²D)
weight singular value regularization
activation outlier reduction

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityOJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization0.19QuantLRM: Quantization of Large Reasoning Models via Fine-Tuning Signals0.18Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems0.151-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization0.15MSign: An Optimizer Preventing Training Instability in Large Language Models via Stable Rank Restoration0.14
Selective Spectral Decay (S2D)
Core Idea: Regularize only the weight components corresponding to dominant singular values during fine-tuning, suppressing activation outliers at their source to produce quantization-friendly neural representations.
Problem It Solves: Activation outliers in large transformers create extreme dynamic ranges that cause severe accuracy degradation under low-bit quantization (e.g., W4A4); this problem worsens with pre-training scale (CLIP → SigLIP → SigLIP2), making deployment of rigorously trained models increasingly expensive.
How It Works:

Identify dominant singular values: Perform SVD on each weight matrix W = UΣVᵀ; identify the top-k singular values σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ … ≥ σₖ that drive outlier activations.
Apply selective decay: During fine-tuning, add a regularization penalty only to the subspace spanned by these dominant singular vectors — leaving smaller singular components untouched to preserve representational capacity.
Condition activations at source: By reducing the spectral norm of the dominant components, the weight matrix becomes better-conditioned, which directly bounds the magnitude of output activations without post-hoc clipping or re-scaling.

Architecture (if applicable):
flowchart LR
    W[Weight Matrix W] --> SVD[SVD Decomposition\nW = UΣVᵀ]
    SVD --> SEL[Select Top-k\nSingular Values]
    SEL --> REG[Spectral Regularization\nLoss on Top-k Subspace]
    REG --> FT[Fine-tuning Update]
    FT --> W2[Conditioned Weights W*]
    W2 --> ACT[Outlier-Reduced\nActivations]
    ACT --> PTQ[PTQ / QAT\nW4A4 Quantization]

Where It’s Useful:

Post-training quantization (PTQ) and quantization-aware training (QAT) of vision transformers and vision-language models
Deploying large pretrained encoders (CLIP, SigLIP, SigLIP2) on edge/mobile hardware with 4-bit weight+activation constraints
Any fine-tuning regime where activation range control is needed without modifying the inference graph (no extra ops at runtime)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires a fine-tuning stage — not applicable as a purely post-training technique without additional training cost
Selecting the threshold k (how many singular values to regularize) introduces a hyperparameter sensitive to model and task
Effectiveness is tied to the singular value structure of pretrained weights; models without strongly dominant singular values may see smaller gains

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys embedding towers: Large embedding layers in two-tower models often develop activation outliers at scale — S2D could be applied during fine-tuning on interaction data to enable INT4 quantization of retrieval encoders without accuracy loss
LLM serving cost reduction: Attention projection matrices (Q/K/V/O) are prime candidates; conditioning them during instruction-tuning could unlock W4A4 inference on already-trained base models
Vision-language retrieval: Multimodal encoders used for image-text matching could be fine-tuned with S2D to reduce serving costs while maintaining cross-modal alignment quality

literature/2026-02-17-SPILLage-Agentic-O.title: SPILLage: Agentic Oversharing on the Web
literature/2026-02-17-SPILLage-Agentic-O.content:
SPILLage: Agentic Oversharing on the Web
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #2 (score: 0.77, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~30 min
Tags: RecSys, RAG, LLM
Code: github.com/jrohsc/SPILLage
Abstract
LLM-powered agents are beginning to automate user’s tasks across the open web, often with access to user resources such as emails and calendars. Unlike standard LLMs answering questions in a controlled ChatBot setting, web agents act “in the wild”, interacting with third parties and leaving behind an action trace. Therefore, we ask the question: how do web agents handle user resources when accomplishing tasks on their behalf across live websites? In this paper, we formalize Natural Agentic Oversharing — the unintentional disclosure of task-irrelevant user information through an agent trace of actions on the web. We introduce SPILLage, a framework that characterizes oversharing along two dimensions: channel (content vs. behavior) and directness (explicit vs. implicit). This taxonomy reveals a critical blind spot: while prior work focuses on text leakage, web agents also overshare behaviorally through clicks, scrolls, and navigation patterns that can be monitored. We benchmark 180 tasks on live e-commerce sites with ground-truth annotations separating task-relevant from task-irrelevant attributes. Across 1,080 runs spanning two agentic frameworks and three backbone LLMs, we demonstrate that oversharing is pervasive with behavioral oversharing dominates content oversharing by 5x. This effect persists — and can even worsen — under prompt-level mitigation. However, removing task-irrelevant information before execution improves task success by up to 17.9%, demonstrating that reducing oversharing improves task success. Our findings underscore that protecting privacy in web agents is a fundamental challenge, requiring a broader view of “output” that accounts for what agents do on the web, not just what they type. Our datasets and code are available at github.com/jrohsc/SPILLage.
Key Techniques

I’ll return a 2-4 item comma-separated list of the key techniques or methods.

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.20DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.20A Picture of Agentic Search0.19Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.17ResearchGym: Evaluating Language Model Agents on Real-World AI Research0.17
SPILLage: Agentic Oversharing Taxonomy and Benchmarking Framework
Core Idea: A two-dimensional taxonomy (channel × directness) that formally characterizes how LLM web agents unintentionally disclose task-irrelevant user information through both textual outputs and observable behavioral traces.
Problem It Solves: Prior privacy work on LLM agents focused exclusively on text leakage (what agents type), missing a larger and more insidious class of privacy violation: behavioral signals (clicks, scrolls, navigation) that third parties can monitor to infer sensitive user attributes even when no private text is transmitted.
How It Works:

Taxonomy definition: Classify oversharing along two axes — channel (content: text/data entered vs. behavior: interaction patterns) and directness (explicit: directly stated sensitive info vs. implicit: inferable from context or patterns)
Task construction: Define 180 tasks on live e-commerce sites with ground-truth annotations that partition user attributes into task-relevant vs. task-irrelevant; oversharing occurs when an agent exposes task-irrelevant attributes
Benchmarking: Run 1,080 agent trajectories (2 frameworks × 3 LLMs × 180 tasks), scoring each trajectory for content oversharing (text entered into forms/search) and behavioral oversharing (pages visited, items clicked, filters applied that reveal private attributes)
Mitigation testing: Compare prompt-level mitigations (instructions to be privacy-preserving) against context-stripping (removing task-irrelevant info from the agent’s input before execution)

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[User Task + Profile] --> B[Attribute Partitioner\ntask-relevant vs. irrelevant]
    B --> C[Web Agent\nFramework × LLM]
    C --> D[Action Trace\nclicks/scrolls/text]
    D --> E1[Content Oversharing\nScorer]
    D --> E2[Behavioral Oversharing\nScorer]
    E1 --> F[SPILLage Score]
    E2 --> F
    G[Mitigation: Context Stripping] --> B

Where It’s Useful:

Auditing LLM agent deployments that access personal data (email, calendar, health) before acting on the open web
Designing privacy-preserving agent architectures with minimal-disclosure task planning
Evaluating agentic frameworks (e.g., browser-use, OpenHands) for privacy compliance

Limitations / Caveats:

Behavioral oversharing requires a threat model where the website itself is the adversary — less applicable when the site is trusted
Prompt-level mitigation not only fails but can worsen oversharing, suggesting the problem is architectural, not instructable
Benchmark is scoped to e-commerce; generalization to other domains (healthcare, finance) is unvalidated

Internal Use Cases:

Agentic recommender systems: agents fetching user context to personalize recommendations may behaviorally signal preference signals to third-party sites — SPILLage’s taxonomy can audit what the agent’s navigation reveals about the user beyond what was explicitly shared
LLM-powered ranking pipelines: if agents query external catalogs or APIs on behalf of users, behavioral traces (query sequences, filter selections) could leak ranking signals or user segment membership
Context stripping as a design pattern: the finding that removing task-irrelevant attributes improves task success by 17.9% suggests that minimal-context prompting is both a privacy and performance optimization for agentic RAG pipelines

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-17-STATe-of-Thoughts-.title: STATe-of-Thoughts: Structured Action Templates for Tree-of-Thoughts
literature/2026-02-17-STATe-of-Thoughts-.content:
STATe-of-Thoughts: Structured Action Templates for Tree-of-Thoughts
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-15
Rank: #4 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, RAG
Code: github.com/zbambergerNLP/state-of-thoughts
Abstract
Inference-Time-Compute (ITC) methods like Best-of-N and Tree-of-Thoughts are meant to produce output candidates that are both high-quality and diverse, but their use of high-temperature sampling often fails to achieve meaningful output diversity. Moreover, existing ITC methods offer limited control over how to perform reasoning, which in turn limits their explainability. We present STATe-of-Thoughts (STATe), an interpretable ITC method that searches over high-level reasoning patterns. STATe replaces stochastic sampling with discrete and interpretable textual interventions: a controller selects actions encoding high-level reasoning choices, a generator produces reasoning steps conditioned on those choices, and an evaluator scores candidates to guide search. This structured approach yields three main advantages. First, action-guided textual interventions produce greater response diversity than temperature-based sampling. Second, in a case study on argument generation, STATe’s explicit action sequences capture interpretable features that are highly predictive of output quality. Third, estimating the association between performance and action choices allows us to identify promising yet unexplored regions of the action space and steer generation directly toward them. Together, these results establish STATe as a practical framework for generating high-quality, diverse, and interpretable text. Our framework is available at github.com/zbambergerNLP/state-of-thoughts.
Key Techniques

STATe (STATe-of-Thoughts)
action-guided textual interventions
discrete reasoning pattern search
evaluator-guided candidate scoring

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityOAT: Ordered Action Tokenization0.31UnMaskFork: Test-Time Scaling for Masked Diffusion via Deterministic Action Branching0.27AugVLA-3D: Depth-Driven Feature Augmentation for Vision-Language-Action Models0.27Temp-R1: A Unified Autonomous Agent for Complex Temporal KGQA via Reverse Curriculum Reinforcement Learning0.24LogitsCoder: Towards Efficient Chain-of-Thought Path Search via Logits Preference Decoding for Code Generation0.21
STATe-of-Thoughts (STATe)
Core Idea: Replace stochastic temperature sampling in tree search with discrete, interpretable “action” tokens that encode high-level reasoning strategies, enabling structured navigation of a reasoning space rather than random exploration.
Problem It Solves: Inference-Time Compute (ITC) methods like Tree-of-Thoughts rely on high-temperature sampling to generate diverse candidates, but temperature sampling produces surface-level lexical variation rather than meaningfully distinct reasoning approaches — and offers no interpretability into why one reasoning path outperforms another.
How It Works:

Controller selects an action from a discrete action vocabulary (e.g., REFRAME, COUNTERARGUMENT, ANALOGY) encoding a high-level reasoning choice for the next generation step
Generator conditions on the selected action token(s) to produce a reasoning step that follows that strategy — action is prepended or injected as a structured prompt intervention
Evaluator scores the resulting candidate (via a reward model or LLM judge) and returns the score to guide tree search — high-scoring action sequences are expanded, low-scoring ones pruned
Search proceeds over the action sequence space (not raw token space), building a tree of (action, reasoning-step) pairs; final output is the highest-scoring leaf

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    I[Input Prompt] --> C[Controller\nselects action]
    C -->|action token| G[Generator\nconditioned on action]
    G -->|reasoning step| E[Evaluator\nscores candidate]
    E -->|score| S[Tree Search\nexpand / prune]
    S -->|next node| C
    S --> O[Best Leaf Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Open-ended generation tasks requiring diverse, high-quality candidates (argument generation, essay writing, creative tasks)
Settings where reasoning interpretability is required (auditable AI, human-in-the-loop workflows)
Identifying underexplored but promising reasoning strategies via action-performance association analysis
Any ITC scenario where you want to steer generation away from degenerate modes without retraining

Limitations / Caveats:

Action vocabulary must be defined in advance; quality of the vocabulary caps the ceiling of reasoning diversity
Evaluator quality bottlenecks the entire framework — a weak reward model misdirects search
Tree search over action sequences can be expensive if the action space or tree depth is large
Evaluated primarily on argument generation; generalization to formal reasoning (math, code) is unproven

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: Use structured action templates to generate diverse explanation styles for recommendation rationales (e.g., SOCIAL_PROOF, FEATURE_MATCH, CONTRAST) and select the highest-scoring one per user context
LLM pipelines: Replace Best-of-N sampling in response generation with STATe to get interpretable action logs alongside outputs, enabling post-hoc analysis of which reasoning strategies correlate with high user satisfaction
Related to Self-Consistency Decoding: STATe can be seen as a structured alternative — instead of majority-voting over random samples, vote/select over action-guided samples where each candidate has a known provenance
Related to Hierarchical Reasoning Model: STATe’s controller→generator hierarchy mirrors HRM’s macro/micro level split; could combine STATe actions as macro-level plans feeding HRM’s micro-level execution

literature/2026-02-17-Speculative-Decodi.title: Speculative Decoding with a Speculative Vocabulary
literature/2026-02-17-Speculative-Decodi.content:
Speculative Decoding with a Speculative Vocabulary
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-14
Rank: #1 (score: 0.00, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Speculative decoding has rapidly emerged as a leading approach for accelerating language model (LM) inference, as it offers substantial speedups while yielding identical outputs. This relies upon a small draft model, tasked with predicting the outputs of the target model. State-of-the-art speculative decoding methods use a draft model consisting of a single decoder layer and output embedding matrix, with the latter dominating drafting time for the latest LMs. Recent work has sought to address this output distribution bottleneck by reducing the vocabulary of the draft model. Although this can improve throughput, it compromises speculation effectiveness when the target token is out-of-vocabulary. In this paper, we argue for vocabulary speculation as an alternative to a reduced vocabulary. We propose SpecVocab, an efficient and effective method that selects a vocabulary subset per decoding step. Across a variety of tasks, we demonstrate that SpecVocab can achieve a higher acceptance length than state-of-the-art speculative decoding approach, EAGLE-3. Notably, this yields up to an 8.1% increase in average throughput over EAGLE-3.
Key Techniques

Speculative decoding
Draft model optimization
Vocabulary speculation
SpecVocab method

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.46SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration0.46PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models0.45Variational Speculative Decoding: Rethinking Draft Training from Token Likelihood to Sequence Acceptance0.42DART: Diffusion-Inspired Speculative Decoding for Fast LLM Inference0.41
SpecVocab (Speculative Vocabulary)
Core Idea: Instead of using a fixed reduced vocabulary for draft model inference, dynamically select a per-step vocabulary subset that is most likely to contain the target model’s next token, eliminating out-of-vocabulary speculation failures while retaining the throughput gains of vocabulary reduction.
Problem It Solves: Existing reduced-vocabulary speculative decoding methods (e.g., EAGLE-3 variants) shrink the draft model’s output vocabulary statically to cut embedding matrix computation costs, but this causes speculation to fail entirely when the target token falls outside the reduced set — degrading acceptance length on out-of-distribution tokens.
How It Works:

Vocabulary Selection: At each decoding step, a lightweight predictor uses the current hidden state to score all vocabulary tokens and selects a top-k subset (the “speculative vocabulary”) most likely to match the target model’s output.
Restricted Draft Decoding: The single-layer draft model performs its forward pass with the output embedding matrix sliced to only the selected subset, reducing computation proportional to subset size.
Standard Speculative Verification: The target model verifies the drafted token as in standard speculative decoding; if the drafted token is in the speculative vocabulary and accepted, the step succeeds — the method remains lossless (identical outputs to greedy/sampling).

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    H[Hidden State] --> VP[Vocabulary Predictor]
    VP --> VS[Vocab Subset k tokens]
    VS --> EM[Sliced Embedding Matrix]
    H --> DL[Draft Layer single decoder]
    DL --> EM
    EM --> DT[Draft Token]
    DT --> TM[Target Model Verification]
    TM --> OUT[Accepted Token / Reject]

Where It’s Useful:

Inference acceleration for large autoregressive LMs where the output embedding matrix is a dominant cost (e.g., models with large vocabularies: 100k+ tokens)
Any speculative decoding pipeline using a single-layer draft head (EAGLE-style architectures)
Latency-sensitive serving where acceptance length is critical to throughput

Limitations / Caveats:

The vocabulary predictor itself adds overhead; gains depend on its accuracy and efficiency relative to the embedding matrix savings
Subset size k is a tunable hyperparameter — too small risks excluding the target token, too large erodes compute savings
Benefit scales with vocabulary size; smaller-vocabulary models see diminishing returns over static reduction baselines

Internal Use Cases:

LLM serving with ranking heads: If a ranking or scoring head over a large item vocabulary is the inference bottleneck, per-step candidate set prediction mirrors SpecVocab’s logic — dynamically restrict the scored set based on context
Draft model in retrieval-augmented generation: Combine with DReSD-style dense retrieval to bias the speculative vocabulary toward tokens semantically relevant to retrieved context, potentially increasing acceptance length further
Batch verification pipelines: SpecVocab’s per-step vocab selection is orthogonal to batch verification (see Batch Verification in Speculative Decoding) and could be composed with it — reduce both embedding cost and verification passes simultaneously

literature/2026-02-17-The-Potential-of-C.title: The Potential of CoT for Reasoning: A Closer Look at Trace Dynamics
literature/2026-02-17-The-Potential-of-C.content:
The Potential of CoT for Reasoning: A Closer Look at Trace Dynamics
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #3 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~30 min
Tags: LLM
Abstract
Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting is a de-facto standard technique to elicit reasoning-like responses from large language models (LLMs), allowing them to spell out individual steps before giving a final answer. While the resemblance to human-like reasoning is undeniable, the driving forces underpinning the success of CoT reasoning still remain largely unclear. In this work, we perform an in-depth analysis of CoT traces originating from competition-level mathematics questions, with the aim of better understanding how, and which parts of CoT actually contribute to the final answer. To this end, we introduce the notion of a potential, quantifying how much a given part of CoT increases the likelihood of a correct completion. Upon examination of reasoning traces through the lens of the potential, we identify surprising patterns including (1) its often strong non-monotonicity (due to reasoning tangents), (2) very sharp but sometimes tough to interpret spikes (reasoning insights and jumps) as well as (3) at times lucky guesses, where the model arrives at the correct answer without providing any relevant justifications before. While some of the behaviours of the potential are readily interpretable and align with human intuition (such as insights and tangents), others remain difficult to understand from a human perspective. To further quantify the reliance of LLMs on reasoning insights, we investigate the notion of CoT transferability, where we measure the potential of a weaker model under the partial CoT from another, stronger model. Indeed aligning with our previous results, we find that as little as 20% of partial CoT can “unlock” the performance of the weaker model on problems that were previously unsolvable for it, highlighting that a large part of the mechanics underpinning CoT are transferable.
Key Techniques

Chain-of-thought prompting
potential analysis
reasoning transferability

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityImgCoT: Compressing Long Chain of Thought into Compact Visual Tokens for Efficient Reasoning of Large Language Model0.31Chain-of-thought obfuscation learned from output supervision can generalise to unseen tasks0.27Divide-and-Conquer CoT: RL for Reducing Latency via Parallel Reasoning0.26Long Chain-of-Thought Compression via Fine-Grained Group Policy Optimization0.24Why Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models0.23
CoT Potential Analysis
Core Idea: Assign a scalar potential score to each prefix of a chain-of-thought trace by measuring how much that prefix increases the probability of a correct final answer, enabling fine-grained attribution of which reasoning steps actually drive performance.
Problem It Solves: CoT prompting is treated as a monolithic technique, but it is unknown which parts of a reasoning trace contribute to correctness vs. which are irrelevant, misleading tangents, or lucky guesses — blocking principled improvement of reasoning systems.
How It Works:

Define potential: For a partial CoT trace prefix p_k (tokens 1..k), compute \Phi(p_k) = P(\text{correct answer} \mid \text{question}, p_k) - P(\text{correct answer} \mid \text{question}), i.e., the lift in correct-answer probability attributable to that prefix.
Sample completions: Estimate P(\text{correct} \mid \text{question}, p_k) by generating many completions from the model conditioned on the prefix and scoring final answers.
Plot potential over trace length: Produce a potential curve across the full trace to identify where potential rises (insights), drops (tangents), or spikes abruptly (jumps/lucky guesses).
CoT transferability test: Feed partial prefixes from a stronger model into a weaker model and measure the weaker model’s potential, quantifying how much reasoning “bootstrap” transfers across models.

Architecture (if applicable):
flowchart LR
    Q[Question] --> SM[Strong Model\nfull CoT trace]
    SM --> P["Prefix p_k\n(0%...100% of trace)"]
    P --> WM[Target Model\nsampled completions]
    WM --> S[Answer scoring]
    S --> PHI["Potential Φ(p_k)\nvs. trace position"]
    PHI --> C{Pattern\nClassification}
    C --> T[Tangent\nnon-monotone drop]
    C --> I[Insight\nsharp spike up]
    C --> LG[Lucky Guess\nspike near end]

Where It’s Useful:

Diagnosing reasoning quality in math/coding benchmarks (competition-level problems where ground-truth answers exist)
Selecting optimal CoT prefix length for speculative decoding or prompt caching
Cross-model distillation: determining how much of a strong model’s trace a weak model needs to “unlock” solvability

Limitations / Caveats:

Potential estimation requires many sampled completions per prefix, making it computationally expensive at scale
Requires access to ground-truth answers to score completions, limiting applicability to open-ended generation
“Lucky guess” and “insight” categories are identified post-hoc and resist mechanistic interpretation; the paper acknowledges some spikes remain opaque
Findings are on competition mathematics — generalization to other domains (code, science, etc.) is unverified

Internal Use Cases:

LLM reranking: Use potential curves to identify the minimal prefix length that separates good from bad reasoning paths, enabling early-exit reranking without full trace generation
Training data curation: Filter CoT training examples by removing traces with high tangent regions (potential drops) to improve SFT/RLHF data quality
Retrieval-augmented reasoning: Transferability finding (20% prefix unlocks weaker models) suggests injecting a short “insight prefix” from a retrieval system could substitute for expensive full CoT

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-17-UniWeTok-An-Unifie.title: UniWeTok: An Unified Binary Tokenizer with Codebook Size 2^{128} for Unified Multimodal Large Language Model
literature/2026-02-17-UniWeTok-An-Unifie.content:
UniWeTok: An Unified Binary Tokenizer with Codebook Size 2^{128} for Unified Multimodal Large Language Model
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-15
Rank: #7 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Embeddings, LLM
Abstract
Unified Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) require a visual representation that simultaneously supports high-fidelity reconstruction, complex semantic extraction, and generative suitability. However, existing visual tokenizers typically struggle to satisfy these conflicting objectives within a single framework. In this paper, we introduce UniWeTok, a unified discrete tokenizer designed to bridge this gap using a massive binary codebook (2^{128}). For training framework, we introduce Pre-Post Distillation and a Generative-Aware Prior to enhance the semantic extraction and generative prior of the discrete tokens. In terms of model architecture, we propose a convolution-attention hybrid architecture with the SigLu activation function. SigLu activation not only bounds the encoder output and stabilizes the semantic distillation process but also effectively addresses the optimization conflict between token entropy loss and commitment loss. We further propose a three-stage training framework designed to enhance UniWeTok’s adaptability cross various image resolutions and perception-sensitive scenarios, such as those involving human faces and textual content. On ImageNet, UniWeTok achieves state-of-the-art image generation performance (FID: UniWeTok 1.38 vs. REPA 1.42) while requiring a remarkably low training compute (Training Tokens: UniWeTok 33B vs. REPA 262B). On general-domain, UniWeTok demonstrates highly competitive capabilities across a broad range of tasks, including multimodal understanding, image generation (DPG Score: UniWeTok 86.63 vs. FLUX.1 [Dev] 83.84), and editing (GEdit Overall Score: UniWeTok 5.09 vs. OmniGen 5.06). We release code and models to facilitate community exploration of unified tokenizer and MLLM.
Key Techniques

Based on the paper text provided
here are the key techniques or methods:

**Pre-Post Distillation

Generative-Aware Prior
Convolution-Attention Hybrid Architecture with SigLu Activation
Three-Stage Training Framework**

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBitDance: Scaling Autoregressive Generative Models with Binary Tokens0.35VisionTrim: Unified Vision Token Compression for Training-Free MLLM Acceleration0.24ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.23VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.21Youtu-VL: Unleashing Visual Potential via Unified Vision-Language Supervision0.21
UniWeTok: Unified Binary Visual Tokenizer
Core Idea: A discrete visual tokenizer using a massive 2^128-entry binary codebook combined with Pre-Post Distillation and a Generative-Aware Prior, enabling a single tokenizer to simultaneously support high-fidelity reconstruction, semantic understanding, and image generation within unified multimodal LLMs.
Problem It Solves: Existing visual tokenizers face a trilemma — reconstruction fidelity, semantic richness, and generative suitability conflict with one another, forcing practitioners to use separate tokenizers or accept degraded performance on at least one axis. Additionally, standard VQ codebooks suffer from optimization conflicts between token entropy loss and commitment loss.
How It Works:

Encode: A convolution-attention hybrid encoder processes an image and produces a feature map; SigLu (sigmoid-gated linear unit) activation bounds encoder outputs, stabilizing the semantic distillation process and resolving the entropy/commitment loss conflict.
Binarize: Features are projected into a 128-bit binary code (one bit per dimension), yielding a codebook of size 2^128 without explicit storage — each token is a compact binary vector.
Pre-Post Distillation: A pretrained vision encoder (e.g., CLIP/DINOv2) provides semantic targets before and after the bottleneck, transferring rich semantic knowledge into the discrete tokens via distillation loss.
Generative-Aware Prior: A learned prior is injected into the discrete token space to bias tokens toward distributions favorable for autoregressive or diffusion-based generation.
Three-Stage Training: Stage 1 trains base tokenization; Stage 2 extends to variable resolutions; Stage 3 fine-tunes on perception-sensitive domains (faces, text/OCR) for high-fidelity detail.
Decode: A convolutional-attention hybrid decoder reconstructs the image from binary tokens; the same tokens are passed to a unified MLLM for understanding or generation tasks.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    IMG[Input Image] --> ENC["Conv-Attn Hybrid\nEncoder + SigLu"]
    ENC --> BIN["Binary Quantizer\n(128-bit codes, 2^128 codebook)"]
    BIN --> DEC["Conv-Attn Hybrid\nDecoder"]
    DEC --> RECON[Reconstructed Image]

    BIN --> MLLM["Unified MLLM\n(Understanding / Generation)"]

    PPD["Pre-Post Distillation\n(CLIP/DINO teacher)"] -.->|semantic loss| BIN
    GAP["Generative-Aware Prior"] -.->|prior loss| BIN

Where It’s Useful:

Unified multimodal LLMs that must handle image understanding, generation, and editing within one model
Scenarios requiring compact discrete visual representations for autoregressive generation (next-token prediction over image tokens)
High-resolution or domain-sensitive vision tasks (document understanding, face recognition) where tokenizer fidelity matters

Limitations / Caveats:

Binary codes are not explicitly stored but must be handled carefully in downstream LLM vocabularies — integrating 128-bit tokens into standard LLM embedding tables requires non-trivial engineering
The three-stage training curriculum adds complexity and sensitivity to stage transition hyperparameters
Generative-Aware Prior effectiveness likely depends on alignment with the specific generation architecture (AR vs. diffusion); generalizability across generation paradigms is not fully characterized

Internal Use Cases:

Multimodal retrieval/ranking: Binary token codes are naturally suited to Hamming-distance similarity search, making UniWeTok tokens directly usable as compact image fingerprints in embedding-based retrieval pipelines (complementing Universal Multimodal Embedding Alignment)
Cross-modal representation learning: The Pre-Post Distillation mechanism mirrors Alignment-Guided Representation Learning — the same distillation losses could be adapted to align image tokens with text embedding spaces for ranking or retrieval scoring
Generative recommendation: If item images are tokenized with UniWeTok, an LLM backbone can perform joint understanding (CTR/CVR features) and generation (creative/ad synthesis) from a single shared token vocabulary

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-17-Unleash-the-Potent.title: Unleash the Potential of Long Semantic IDs for Generative Recommendation
literature/2026-02-17-Unleash-the-Potent.content:
Unleash the Potential of Long Semantic IDs for Generative Recommendation
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-14
Rank: #1 (score: 0.00, max_sim: 0.89)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Semantic ID-based generative recommendation represents items as sequences of discrete tokens, but it inherently faces a trade-off between representational expressiveness and computational efficiency. Residual Quantization (RQ)-based approaches restrict semantic IDs to be short to enable tractable sequential modeling, while Optimized Product Quantization (OPQ)-based methods compress long semantic IDs through naive rigid aggregation, inevitably discarding fine-grained semantic information. To resolve this dilemma, we propose ACERec, a novel framework that decouples the granularity gap between fine-grained tokenization and efficient sequential modeling. It employs an Attentive Token Merger to distill long expressive semantic tokens into compact latents and introduces a dedicated Intent Token serving as a dynamic prediction anchor. To capture cohesive user intents, we guide the learning process via a dual-granularity objective, harmonizing fine-grained token prediction with global item-level semantic alignment. Extensive experiments on six real-world benchmarks demonstrate that ACERec consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving an average improvement of 14.40% in NDCG@10, effectively reconciling semantic expressiveness and computational efficiency.
Key Techniques

Attentive Token Merger
Intent Token
Dual-granularity objective

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityUnleash the Potential of Long Semantic IDs for Generative Recommendation0.88GLASS: A Generative Recommender for Long-sequence Modeling via SID-Tier and Semantic Search0.33DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.31R2LED: Equipping Retrieval and Refinement in Lifelong User Modeling with Semantic IDs for CTR Prediction0.30Rethinking Generative Recommender Tokenizer: Recsys-Native Encoding and Semantic Quantization Beyond LLMs0.28
ACERec (Attentive Compression for Expressive Recommendation)
Core Idea: Decouple item tokenization granularity from sequential modeling granularity by compressing long Optimized Product Quantization (OPQ) semantic ID token sequences into compact latent representations via attention-based merging, then using a dedicated intent token as a dynamic prediction anchor.
Problem It Solves: Existing generative recommendation approaches face a hard trade-off: RQ-VAE-based short semantic IDs are tractable for sequential modeling but sacrifice representational expressiveness, while OPQ-based long semantic IDs are expressive but require naive rigid aggregation that discards fine-grained semantic information. ACERec resolves this by separating the tokenization and modeling stages.
How It Works:

Tokenization: Items are encoded into long OPQ-based semantic ID sequences (multiple discrete tokens per item), capturing fine-grained semantic content from item embeddings.
Attentive Token Merger: A cross-attention mechanism compresses the long per-item token sequence into a small set of compact latent vectors, preserving semantic signal without forcing a fixed aggregation (e.g., mean pooling).
Intent Token Injection: A learnable Intent Token is prepended to the user’s interaction sequence; it aggregates cohesive user intent across the compressed item latents and serves as the dynamic anchor from which the next-item prediction is generated.
Dual-Granularity Training Objective: The model is trained jointly on (a) fine-grained token-level prediction (autoregressively predicting the OPQ token sequence of the next item) and (b) global item-level semantic alignment (contrastive or similarity loss against the item’s full embedding), harmonizing local token fidelity with global semantic consistency.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Item Embedding] --> B[OPQ Tokenizer\nlong token seq]
    B --> C[Attentive Token Merger\ncross-attention]
    C --> D[Compact Latents\nper item]
    D --> E[Sequential Encoder\n+ Intent Token]
    E --> F[Intent Token State]
    F --> G1[Fine-grained\nToken Prediction Head]
    F --> G2[Global Semantic\nAlignment Loss]
    G1 --> H[Generated Semantic ID\n→ Item Retrieval]

Where It’s Useful:

Generative retrieval recommender systems where items must be represented as discrete token sequences for autoregressive decoding
Large-scale ranking pipelines needing richer item semantics than short RQ-VAE codes provide without prohibitive sequence length costs
Cold-start and long-tail settings where content-derived OPQ embeddings carry more signal than interaction-derived codes

Limitations / Caveats:

The Attentive Token Merger adds a learned compression step that must be jointly trained; suboptimal merger training could bottleneck the whole pipeline
OPQ codebook construction is offline and static; dynamic item catalogs may require periodic retraining of the tokenizer
Dual-granularity loss introduces an additional hyperparameter (loss weighting) that may require tuning per dataset

Internal Use Cases:

YouTube-style semantic IDs: The OPQ tokenizer + Attentive Token Merger could replace naive mean-pooling used to collapse RQ-VAE codes into a single ID, preserving the multi-subspace structure of product quantization
Ranking model cold-start: Intent Token paradigm is directly applicable to session-based ranking where a single query-side vector must summarize heterogeneous interaction history with varying item granularities
