Generative retrieval beam search: Fine-grained token prediction head is compatible with constrained beam search over a semantic ID trie, enabling exact candidate retrieval without ANN index

literature/2026-02-17-VIPA-Visual-Inform.title: VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation
literature/2026-02-17-VIPA-Visual-Inform.content:
VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #3 (score: 0.24, max_sim: 0.76)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) aims to segment a target object described by a natural language expression. Existing methods have evolved by leveraging the vision information into the language tokens. To more effectively exploit visual contexts for fine-grained segmentation, we propose a novel Visual Informative Part Attention (VIPA) framework for referring image segmentation. VIPA leverages the informative parts of visual contexts, called a visual expression, which can effectively provide the structural and semantic visual target information to the network. This design reduces high-variance cross-modal projection and enhances semantic consistency in an attention mechanism of the referring image segmentation. We also design a visual expression generator (VEG) module, which retrieves informative visual tokens via local-global linguistic context cues and refines the retrieved tokens for reducing noise information and sharing informative visual attributes. This module allows the visual expression to consider comprehensive contexts and capture semantic visual contexts of informative regions. In this way, our framework enables the network’s attention to robustly align with the fine-grained regions of interest. Extensive experiments and visual analysis demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Our VIPA outperforms the existing state-of-the-art methods on four public RIS benchmarks.
Key Techniques

Visual Informative Part Attention (VIPA)
Visual Expression Generator (VEG) module
Cross-modal projection with semantic consistency
Local-global linguistic context cues

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDo MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.51Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs0.39ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.39Thinking with Drafting: Optical Decompression via Logical Reconstruction0.37Fast-Slow Efficient Training for Multimodal Large Language Models via Visual Token Pruning0.33
Visual Informative Part Attention (VIPA)
Core Idea: Instead of projecting language tokens into visual space (or vice versa) directly, VIPA constructs a “visual expression” — a distilled, noise-reduced set of visual tokens grounded by linguistic context — and uses this as the attention medium for cross-modal alignment in referring image segmentation.
Problem It Solves: Standard cross-modal attention in Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) suffers from high-variance projection when mapping between vision and language spaces, causing semantic drift and poor localization of fine-grained regions described by natural language expressions.
How It Works:

Visual Expression Generation (VEG): Given image features and a language expression, retrieve visually informative tokens by attending over local-global linguistic context cues — local cues capture phrase-level grounding, global cues capture full-expression semantics.
Noise Reduction & Attribute Sharing: The retrieved tokens are refined (e.g., via aggregation/filtering) to suppress irrelevant background tokens and propagate shared semantic attributes across informative regions, producing a compact “visual expression.”
Visual Expression Attention: The visual expression replaces raw visual or language tokens as the cross-modal attention key/value source, so the segmentation decoder attends to structurally and semantically coherent visual parts rather than the full noisy token set.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    I[Image] --> VE[Visual Encoder]
    T[Text Expression] --> TE[Text Encoder]
    VE --> VEG[Visual Expression\nGenerator]
    TE --> VEG
    VEG -->|local-global\nlinguistic cues| R[Retrieved\nVisual Tokens]
    R -->|noise reduction\n+ attribute sharing| VX[Visual Expression]
    VX --> ATTN[Cross-Modal\nAttention]
    TE --> ATTN
    ATTN --> DEC[Segmentation Decoder]
    DEC --> M[Segmentation Mask]

Where It’s Useful:

Referring image segmentation (grounding free-form language to pixel masks)
Visual grounding and phrase localization tasks
Any cross-modal alignment task where the visual token space is large and noisy relative to a sparse linguistic query

Limitations / Caveats:

The VEG module adds computational overhead for token retrieval and refinement; quality degrades if the linguistic cues are ambiguous or very short
Performance on highly occluded or compositionally complex scenes may still be limited since “informative parts” are retrieved relative to the expression, not geometric structure
Evaluated on standard RIS benchmarks (RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, RefCOCOg, ReferIt); generalization to open-vocabulary or zero-shot settings is unverified

Internal Use Cases:

Multimodal retrieval/RAG: The VEG pattern (retrieve sparse informative visual tokens via linguistic cues, then refine) directly mirrors the Multimodal Textual Knowledge Generation via Open-Vocabulary VLMs pattern — could replace dense captioning with targeted region retrieval for more precise grounding in retrieval pipelines
Ranking with visual context: In recommendation or ranking systems with product images, a VEG-style module could identify the visually discriminative parts relevant to a user query before cross-attention scoring, reducing noise from background regions
Long-context attention efficiency: The token sparsification strategy is complementary to Dilated Attention — VIPA prunes semantically irrelevant tokens; dilated attention handles positional sparsity; combining both could yield efficient, semantically precise cross-modal transformers

literature/2026-02-17-Web-Scale-Multimod.title: Web-Scale Multimodal Summarization using CLIP-Based Semantic Alignment
literature/2026-02-17-Web-Scale-Multimod.content:
Web-Scale Multimodal Summarization using CLIP-Based Semantic Alignment
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #8 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Search, Embeddings, LLM
Abstract
We introduce Web-Scale Multimodal Summarization, a lightweight framework for generating summaries by combining retrieved text and image data from web sources. Given a user-defined topic, the system performs parallel web, news, and image searches. Retrieved images are ranked using a fine-tuned CLIP model to measure semantic alignment with topic and text. Optional BLIP captioning enables image-only summaries for stronger multimodal coherence.The pipeline supports features such as adjustable fetch limits, semantic filtering, summary styling, and downloading structured outputs. We expose the system via a Gradio-based API with controllable parameters and preconfigured presets.Evaluation on 500 image-caption pairs with 20:1 contrastive negatives yields a ROC-AUC of 0.9270, an F1-score of 0.6504, and an accuracy of 96.99%, demonstrating strong multimodal alignment. This work provides a configurable, deployable tool for web-scale summarization that integrates language, retrieval, and vision models in a user-extensible pipeline.
Key Techniques

Fine-tuned CLIP model ranking
BLIP captioning
Parallel web/news/image searches

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityUniWeTok: An Unified Binary Tokenizer with Codebook Size 2^{128} for Unified Multimodal Large Language Model0.20Multimodal Generative Retrieval Model with Staged Pretraining for Food Delivery on Meituan0.18Embed-RL: Reinforcement Learning for Reasoning-Driven Multimodal Embeddings0.17ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.15MoToRec: Sparse-Regularized Multimodal Tokenization for Cold-Start Recommendation0.15
CLIP-Based Semantic Alignment for Multimodal Web Summarization
Core Idea: A retrieval-then-rank pipeline that uses a fine-tuned CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) model to score and filter web-retrieved images by semantic alignment with a topic and its associated text, enabling coherent multimodal summary generation.
Problem It Solves: Web-retrieved images are noisy and topically inconsistent—naive inclusion degrades summary quality. This addresses the lack of a lightweight, deployable mechanism for aligning heterogeneous web-scale image retrieval results with textual content without requiring large generative vision-language models.
How It Works:

Parallel retrieval: Given a user topic, concurrently fetch text (web/news search) and images from web sources with configurable fetch limits.
Semantic ranking: Pass each retrieved image and the topic/text through a fine-tuned CLIP encoder; compute cosine similarity in the shared embedding space to produce an alignment score; filter or rerank images by threshold.
Summary generation: Combine top-ranked images with retrieved text; optionally run BLIP (Bootstrapping Language-Image Pretraining) captioning on images to generate auxiliary captions for image-only or caption-augmented summary paths.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    T[User Topic] --> WS[Web/News Search]
    T --> IS[Image Search]
    WS --> TX[Retrieved Text]
    IS --> IM[Retrieved Images]
    TX --> CLIP[Fine-tuned CLIP Encoder]
    IM --> CLIP
    CLIP --> RK[Similarity Ranking & Filtering]
    IM --> BLIP[Optional BLIP Captioner]
    BLIP --> RK
    TX --> SG[Summary Generator]
    RK --> SG
    SG --> OUT[Structured Multimodal Summary]

Where It’s Useful:

Automated news briefing or topic digest generation with illustrative images
Multimodal RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipelines requiring image relevance filtering before context injection
Content curation systems that must surface visually coherent results from unstructured web crawls

Limitations / Caveats:

CLIP alignment scores reflect distributional similarity, not factual correctness—a visually similar but contextually wrong image can score highly
Fine-tuning on 500 pairs with 20:1 negatives is a narrow evaluation set; generalization to out-of-domain topics is unverified
Depends on upstream retrieval quality; garbage-in-garbage-out dynamics apply before CLIP ever runs
BLIP captioning adds latency and is optional, meaning image-text coherence is inconsistent across configurations

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking: CLIP similarity scores can serve as an additional multimodal relevance signal in image-augmented ranking pipelines alongside text-based BM25/dense scores
LLM context construction: Pre-filtering retrieved images by CLIP alignment before injecting captions into LLM context windows reduces noise and token waste
RecSys: In content recommendation, CLIP-ranked images can gate which items receive visual thumbnails, improving click-through prediction feature quality for multimodal candidate re-rankers

literature/2026-02-18-1-Bit-Wonder-Impro.title: 1-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization
literature/2026-02-18-1-Bit-Wonder-Impro.content:
1-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #4 (score: 0.62, max_sim: 0.79)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Quantization-aware training (QAT) is an effective method to drastically reduce the memory footprint of LLMs while keeping performance degradation at an acceptable level. However, the optimal choice of quantization format and bit-width presents a challenge in practice. The full design space of quantization is not fully explored in the context of QAT, and the precise trade-off between quantization and downstream performance is poorly understood, as comparisons often rely solely on perplexity-based evaluations. In this work, we address these shortcomings with an empirical study of QAT in the low-bit regime. We show that k-means based weight quantization outperforms integer formats and can be implemented efficiently on standard hardware. Furthermore, we find that, under a fixed inference memory budget, the best performance on generative downstream tasks is achieved with 1-bit quantized weights.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityOJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization0.28BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.28Unleashing Low-Bit Inference on Ascend NPUs: A Comprehensive Evaluation of HiFloat Formats0.26BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.26QuEPT: Quantized Elastic Precision Transformers with One-Shot Calibration for Multi-Bit Switching0.25
K-Means Quantization-Aware Training (KM-QAT)
Core Idea: Replace uniform integer quantization grids with k-means-learned codebooks during quantization-aware training, then show that 1-bit k-means quantization maximizes downstream generative task performance under a fixed inference memory budget.
Problem It Solves: Standard QAT uses integer (uniform) quantization formats whose fixed grid spacing is suboptimal for the non-uniform distributions of neural network weights; additionally, the QAT design space (format × bit-width) is underexplored, and evaluations rely on perplexity rather than generative task performance, obscuring the true quality/memory trade-off.
How It Works:

Codebook initialization: Run k-means clustering on the weight tensor of each layer to find 2^b centroids (e.g., 2 centroids for 1-bit), producing a learned, non-uniform quantization grid that matches the actual weight distribution.
Straight-through gradient estimation: During the forward pass, each weight is snapped to its nearest centroid; gradients are passed through unchanged (straight-through estimator) so the full-precision “latent” weights continue to be updated via backprop.
Codebook update: Centroid values are periodically re-estimated (via k-means on the current latent weights) or jointly optimized, so the grid adapts as training progresses.
Bit-width sweep under fixed memory budget: Models are trained at multiple bit-widths (1-bit, 2-bit, etc.); given a fixed inference VRAM ceiling, lower bit-widths allow larger models — the paper finds 1-bit quantized weights on a larger model outperform higher-bit quantized smaller models on generative benchmarks.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    W[Full-precision weights] --> KM[K-Means clustering\n2^b centroids]
    KM --> CB[Codebook]
    CB --> Q[Quantized weights\nnearest centroid lookup]
    Q --> FWD[Forward pass / loss]
    FWD --> STE[Straight-through\ngradient]
    STE --> W
    CB -- periodic re-fit --> KM

Where It’s Useful:

Deploying large language models on memory-constrained hardware (edge devices, consumer GPUs) where integer quantization leaves accuracy on the table
Any scenario with a hard inference-memory budget where choosing the right model-size/bit-width Pareto point is non-obvious
Post-training quantization initialization: k-means codebooks can seed PTQ pipelines before fine-tuning

Limitations / Caveats:

Codebook lookup requires scatter/gather ops; although the paper claims efficient implementation on standard hardware, latency overhead versus integer formats needs careful kernel-level optimization
Centroid re-fitting adds training complexity and a hyperparameter (re-fit frequency) not present in integer QAT
1-bit k-means is essentially a learned ternary/binary scheme — extreme compression may still hurt tasks requiring precise numerical reasoning even if generative benchmarks improve

Internal Use Cases:

LLM serving for ranking/retrieval: Compressing a reranker or query-understanding LLM to 1-bit k-means weights could fit a much larger model into the same GPU memory, improving ranking quality at the same inference cost
Embedding models at scale: Apply KM-QAT to embedding towers in two-tower RecSys architectures to reduce memory and bandwidth during candidate retrieval without reverting to perplexity-only validation — use downstream retrieval metrics (Recall@K, NDCG) as the evaluation signal, mirroring the paper’s shift to generative task benchmarks
Multi-task LLM fine-tuning: When fine-tuning a shared LLM backbone for multiple ranking signals, k-means QAT enables fitting larger backbones under GPU constraints that would otherwise force a smaller base model

literature/2026-02-18-COMPOT-Calibration.title: COMPOT: Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers Compression
literature/2026-02-18-COMPOT-Calibration.content:
COMPOT: Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers Compression
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #6 (score: 0.25, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Post-training compression of Transformer models commonly relies on truncated singular value decomposition (SVD). However, enforcing a single shared subspace can degrade accuracy even at moderate compression. Sparse dictionary learning provides a more flexible union-of-subspaces representation, but existing approaches often suffer from iterative dictionary and coefficient updates. We propose COMPOT (Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers), a training-free compression framework that uses a small calibration dataset to estimate a sparse weight factorization. COMPOT employs orthogonal dictionaries that enable closed-form Procrustes updates for the dictionary and analytical single-step sparse coding for the coefficients, eliminating iterative optimization. To handle heterogeneous layer sensitivity under a global compression budget, COMPOT further introduces a one-shot dynamic allocation strategy that adaptively redistributes layer-wise compression rates. Extensive experiments across diverse architectures and tasks show that COMPOT consistently delivers a superior quality-compression trade-off over strong low-rank and sparse baselines, while remaining fully compatible with post-training quantization for extreme compression. Code is available \href{github.com/mts-ai/COMPOT}{here}.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityCOMPOT: Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers Compression0.89Algorithmic Simplification of Neural Networks with Mosaic-of-Motifs0.17Don’t be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold0.17SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.17Data Distribution Matters: A Data-Centric Perspective on Context Compression for Large Language Model0.17
COMPOT (Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers)
Core Idea: Replace truncated SVD with sparse dictionary learning using orthogonal dictionaries that admit closed-form Procrustes solutions and single-step sparse coding, enabling training-free Transformer weight compression without iterative optimization.
Problem It Solves: Standard low-rank SVD compression forces all weight structure into a single shared subspace, degrading accuracy at moderate compression ratios. Existing sparse dictionary learning approaches require expensive alternating iterative updates (dictionary update + sparse coding) that are slow and unstable. COMPOT eliminates both limitations simultaneously.
How It Works:

Calibration-driven estimation: Pass a small calibration dataset through the model to collect layer-wise activation statistics, producing an empirical covariance estimate used to weight the importance of each weight component.
Orthogonal dictionary factorization: Decompose each weight matrix W ≈ D·A where D is an orthogonal dictionary (D^T D = I). The orthogonality constraint converts the dictionary update into a Procrustes problem — solved in closed form via SVD of a single cross-product matrix, with no iterative refinement needed.
Analytical sparse coding: Given a fixed orthogonal D, coefficients A are computed in a single step (A = D^T W projected/thresholded), exploiting the orthogonality to decouple coefficient estimation from dictionary optimization entirely.
One-shot dynamic layer allocation: A global compression budget is redistributed across layers by estimating each layer’s sensitivity (via calibration loss proxy) and solving a one-shot allocation problem — layers that are more sensitive receive higher rank/sparsity budgets, heterogeneous layer importance is handled without per-layer tuning.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Pretrained\nTransformer] --> B[Calibration\nDataset Pass]
    B --> C[Layer Sensitivity\nEstimation]
    C --> D[One-Shot Budget\nAllocation]
    D --> E[Per-Layer\nOrthogonal Dictionary\nInit]
    E --> F[Closed-Form\nProcrustes Update\nfor D]
    F --> G[Single-Step\nSparse Coding\nfor A]
    G --> H[Compressed\nWeight: W ≈ D·A]
    H --> I[Optional:\nPost-Training\nQuantization]

Where It’s Useful:

Compressing large language models (LLMs) for inference on memory-constrained hardware without fine-tuning
Stacking with post-training quantization (PTQ) for extreme compression (e.g., 4-bit weights + low-rank factorization)
Any setting where calibration data is available but retraining is infeasible (proprietary models, fast deployment cycles)
Architectures with heterogeneous layer sensitivity (encoder-decoder models, MoE layers) where uniform compression rates are suboptimal

Limitations / Caveats:

Orthogonality constraint on D restricts the representational family compared to unconstrained dictionary learning — may underperform in highly anisotropic weight distributions
Calibration data quality and domain match affect the sensitivity estimates; out-of-distribution calibration can misallocate budget
Closed-form sparse coding (hard thresholding of D^T W) may be suboptimal versus pursuit algorithms (OMP, LASSO) for highly non-uniform sparsity patterns
Compression gains are bounded by the union-of-subspaces assumption; extremely low bitwidth targets may still require fine-tuning recovery

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: Compress embedding projection layers and interaction MLP blocks in two-tower or cross-attention retrieval models for lower-latency inference without retraining on private interaction data
LLM serving: Apply to KV-cache projection matrices (K/V heads) or FFN layers in serving infrastructure to reduce memory bandwidth and fit larger batch sizes
Distillation alternative: Use as a cheaper substitute to knowledge distillation when labeled data is unavailable — calibration only requires unlabeled forward passes
Quantization pipeline integration: Stack COMPOT + PTQ as a two-stage compression pipeline to push models below 4-bit effective precision on edge deployments

literature/2026-02-18-Detecting-Overflow.title: Detecting Overflow in Compressed Token Representations for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
literature/2026-02-18-Detecting-Overflow.content:
Detecting Overflow in Compressed Token Representations for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #10 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search, RAG
Abstract
Efficient long-context processing remains a crucial challenge for contemporary large language models (LLMs), especially in resource-constrained environments. Soft compression architectures promise to extend effective context length by replacing long token sequences with smaller sets of learned compressed tokens. Yet, the limits of compressibility — and when compression begins to erase task-relevant content — remain underexplored. In this paper, we define token overflow as a regime in which compressed representations no longer contain sufficient information to answer a given query, and propose a methodology to characterize and detect it. In the xRAG soft-compression setting, we find that query-agnostic saturation statistics reliably separate compressed from uncompressed token representations, providing a practical tool for identifying compressed tokens but showing limited overflow detection capability. Lightweight probing classifiers over both query and context xRAG representations detect overflow with 0.72 AUC-ROC on average on HotpotQA, SQuADv2, and TriviaQA datasets, demonstrating that incorporating query information improves detection performance. These results advance from query-independent diagnostics to query-aware detectors, enabling low-cost pre-LLM gating to mitigate compression-induced errors.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLess Is More — Until It Breaks: Security Pitfalls of Vision Token Compression in Large Vision-Language Models0.18ATACompressor: Adaptive Task-Aware Compression for Efficient Long-Context Processing in LLMs0.17When Less is More: The LLM Scaling Paradox in Context Compression0.14Context Compression via Explicit Information Transmission0.14Attn-GS: Attention-Guided Context Compression for Efficient Personalized LLMs0.13
Query-Aware Overflow Detection for Soft-Compressed Retrieval-Augmented Generation
Core Idea: Train lightweight probing classifiers over joint query and compressed-context representations to detect when soft token compression has discarded information necessary to answer a query, enabling pre-LLM gating before inference.
Problem It Solves: Soft compression architectures (e.g., xRAG) reduce token sequences to compact learned representations, but provide no signal about whether a given query’s answer survives compression — leading to silent failures where the LLM confidently generates wrong answers from information-lossy inputs.
How It Works:

Represent: For each (query, context) pair, extract the xRAG compressed token embeddings for the context and the token embeddings for the query separately.
Label: Define overflow ground truth — a sample is “overflow” if the LLM fails to answer correctly from the compressed context but succeeds from the uncompressed context, isolating compression-induced failures.
Probe: Train a lightweight binary classifier (e.g., logistic regression or shallow MLP) over the concatenated or jointly encoded query + compressed-context representations to predict overflow likelihood.
Gate: At inference time, run the probe before the LLM; if overflow probability exceeds a threshold, fall back to uncompressed retrieval or flag the response as unreliable.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    Q[Query Tokens] --> QE[Query Encoder]
    C[Raw Context] --> XR[xRAG Compressor]
    XR --> CE[Compressed Token Repr.]
    QE --> CAT[Concat / Joint Repr.]
    CE --> CAT
    CAT --> PC[Probe Classifier]
    PC --> OV{Overflow?}
    OV -- No --> LLM[LLM Inference]
    OV -- Yes --> FB[Fallback / Uncompressed Path]

Where It’s Useful:

RAG pipelines with soft compression where latency savings must be balanced against answer reliability
Resource-constrained deployments needing selective compression: compress easy/long contexts, expand only when needed
Multi-hop QA (HotpotQA-style) where compression is most likely to drop bridging facts

Limitations / Caveats:

AUC-ROC of 0.72 is modest — meaningful overflow cases will still slip through at practical thresholds
Probe is trained on specific datasets (HotpotQA, SQuADv2, TriviaQA); generalization to domain-shifted queries is unvalidated
Requires labeled overflow examples for probe training, creating a data dependency tied to a specific compressor (xRAG) and LLM backbone
Query-agnostic saturation statistics (fallback signal) show limited overflow detection, so the query must be available at gate time — ruling out purely offline pre-compression screening

Internal Use Cases:

LLM-based ranking: Compressed passage representations in retrieval rerankers could be gated — detect when a compressed passage is too lossy to support a relevance judgment before scoring
RAG quality control: Pre-generation overflow scores could serve as a confidence signal for downstream calibration or abstention in question-answering products
Adaptive retrieval: Route queries to compressed vs. full-context retrieval dynamically based on predicted overflow risk, optimizing cost-quality tradeoff across a query stream

literature/2026-02-18-ER-MIA-Black-Box-A.title: ER-MIA: Black-Box Adversarial Memory Injection Attacks on Long-Term Memory-Augmented Large Language Models
literature/2026-02-18-ER-MIA-Black-Box-A.content:
ER-MIA: Black-Box Adversarial Memory Injection Attacks on Long-Term Memory-Augmented Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, Search, RAG, RecSys
Abstract
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly augmented with long-term memory systems to overcome finite context windows and enable persistent reasoning across interactions. However, recent research finds that LLMs become more vulnerable because memory provides extra attack surfaces. In this paper, we present the first systematic study of black-box adversarial memory injection attacks that target the similarity-based retrieval mechanism in long-term memory-augmented LLMs. We introduce ER-MIA, a unified framework that exposes this vulnerability and formalizes two realistic attack settings: content-based attacks and question-targeted attacks. In these settings, ER-MIA includes an arsenal of composable attack primitives and ensemble attacks that achieve high success rates under minimal attacker assumptions. Extensive experiments across multiple LLMs and long-term memory systems demonstrate that similarity-based retrieval constitutes a fundamental and system-level vulnerability, revealing security risks that persist across memory designs and application scenarios.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMemFly: On-the-Fly Memory Optimization via Information Bottleneck0.20MalURLBench: A Benchmark Evaluating Agents’ Vulnerabilities When Processing Web URLs0.18Scene-Aware Memory Discrimination: Deciding Which Personal Knowledge Stays0.17Language Model Memory and Memory Models for Language0.16Heterogeneous Computing: The Key to Powering the Future of AI Agent Inference0.15
ER-MIA: Ensemble Retrieval Memory Injection Attack
Core Idea: A black-box adversarial framework that exploits similarity-based retrieval mechanisms in long-term memory-augmented LLMs by crafting malicious memory entries that are preferentially retrieved over legitimate ones, hijacking model responses without access to model internals.
Problem It Solves: Long-term memory systems introduce a new attack surface beyond the model itself — the retrieval layer — which is largely undefended and exploitable purely through crafted text inputs, even without white-box access to embeddings or model weights.
How It Works:

Threat modeling: Attacker defines a goal (inject false content, redirect answers to a target topic) and chooses an attack setting — content-based (make a malicious document retrievable for broad queries) or question-targeted (make a malicious document retrievable for a specific query).
Attack primitive composition: Attacker applies composable primitives to a seed adversarial document — e.g., query mirroring (repeat likely query terms), semantic amplification (add semantically related vocabulary), or decoy wrapping (embed malicious content inside benign-looking text) — to maximize cosine/dot-product similarity with target queries under the retrieval index.
Ensemble attack: Multiple primitive-augmented variants are generated and ranked by estimated retrieval score (via black-box query probing); the highest-scoring variant is injected into the memory store, displacing legitimate documents in top-k retrieval results and causing the LLM to generate attacker-controlled responses.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Attacker Goal + Seed Doc] --> B[Attack Primitive Library\ne.g. query mirror, semantic amplify]
    B --> C[Candidate Variant Generation]
    C --> D[Black-Box Retrieval Scoring\nvia query probing]
    D --> E[Ensemble Selection\nhighest-scored variant]
    E --> F[Memory Store Injection]
    F --> G[Retrieval at Query Time\ntop-k returns poisoned doc]
    G --> H[LLM generates attacker-controlled response]

Where It’s Useful:

Red-teaming / security auditing: Evaluating robustness of RAG pipelines and memory-augmented agents before deployment.
Defensive benchmark construction: Building adversarial test suites for retrieval filters, anomaly detectors, and memory sanitization systems.
Trust modeling in multi-user memory systems: Identifying risks when multiple users or tools can write to a shared memory store (e.g., agentic workflows, persistent chat assistants).

Limitations / Caveats:

Assumes write access to the memory store — does not address scenarios where injection is itself a hard capability barrier.
Black-box retrieval scoring via probing is noisy; effectiveness degrades when the retrieval model is actively rotated or query responses are rate-limited/obfuscated.
Evaluated on similarity-based (dense/sparse) retrieval; does not address hybrid systems with re-ranking stages that use cross-encoders, which could catch semantic anomalies.
Success rates likely vary with embedding model family — primitives tuned against one encoder may not transfer cleanly to another.

Internal Use Cases:

RAG system hardening: Any internal LLM pipeline using vector DB retrieval (e.g., Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector) should be stress-tested with ER-MIA-style injections to validate top-k poisoning resistance before production.
Ranking signal integrity: In retrieval-augmented ranking or recommendation, adversarial document injection mirrors catalog poisoning attacks — ER-MIA primitives map directly to SEO-style manipulation of dense retrieval signals.
Agent memory auditing: For long-running LLM agents with persistent memory (e.g., MemGPT-style systems), this framework motivates provenance tracking and write-access controls on memory stores.

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-18-Enabling-Low-Laten.title: Enabling Low-Latency Machine learning on Radiation-Hard FPGAs with hls4ml
literature/2026-02-18-Enabling-Low-Laten.content:
Enabling Low-Latency Machine learning on Radiation-Hard FPGAs with hls4ml
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #5 (score: 0.34, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
This paper presents the first demonstration of a viable, ultra-fast, radiation-hard machine learning (ML) application on FPGAs, which could be used in future high-energy physics experiments. We present a three-fold contribution, with the PicoCal calorimeter, planned for the LHCb Upgrade II experiment, used as a test case. First, we develop a lightweight autoencoder to compress a 32-sample timing readout, representative of that of the PicoCal, into a two-dimensional latent space. Second, we introduce a systematic, hardware-aware quantization strategy and show that the model can be reduced to 10-bit weights with minimal performance loss. Third, as a barrier to the adoption of on-detector ML is the lack of support for radiation-hard FPGAs in the High-Energy Physics community’s standard ML synthesis tool, hls4ml, we develop a new backend for this library. This new back-end enables the automatic translation of ML models into High-Level Synthesis (HLS) projects for the Microchip PolarFire family of FPGAs, one of the few commercially available and radiation hard FPGAs. We present the synthesis of the autoencoder on a target PolarFire FPGA, which indicates that a latency of 25 ns can be achieved. We show that the resources utilized are low enough that the model can be placed within the inherently protected logic of the FPGA. Our extension to hls4ml is a significant contribution, paving the way for broader adoption of ML on FPGAs in high-radiation environments.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityReshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems0.131-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization0.12OJBKQ: Objective-Joint Babai-Klein Quantization0.11BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.11QuEPT: Quantized Elastic Precision Transformers with One-Shot Calibration for Multi-Bit Switching0.11
hls4ml PolarFire Backend (hls4ml-PF)
Core Idea: A new synthesis backend for the hls4ml library that automatically translates trained neural network models into High-Level Synthesis (HLS) projects targeting Microchip PolarFire radiation-hard FPGAs, enabling on-detector machine learning inference in high-radiation environments.
Problem It Solves: The hls4ml library — the standard tool in high-energy physics (HEP) for deploying ML on FPGAs — had no support for radiation-hard FPGAs (e.g., PolarFire), blocking adoption of on-detector ML in experiments where commercial Xilinx/Intel FPGAs would fail due to radiation-induced bit flips and latch-up events.
How It Works:

Model ingestion: A trained neural network (here, a lightweight autoencoder) is parsed by hls4ml, which extracts layer topology, weights, and activation functions into an intermediate representation.
Hardware-aware quantization: Weights are systematically reduced to 10-bit fixed-point precision using a quantization sweep that monitors reconstruction performance, minimizing resource usage while preserving model fidelity within acceptable bounds.
PolarFire HLS code generation: The new backend emits Libero SoC-compatible HLS C++ targeting PolarFire primitives (DSP blocks, BRAM layout, clock domains), replacing Xilinx Vivado HLS assumptions baked into the default backend.
Synthesis and placement: The generated project is synthesized for a target PolarFire device; the backend ensures the model fits within the FPGA’s inherently radiation-hardened logic regions (configuration SRAM is triple-vote protected on PolarFire).

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Trained Model\nKeras/PyTorch] --> B[hls4ml Parser\nLayer IR]
    B --> C[Quantization\n10-bit weights]
    C --> D[PolarFire Backend\nHLS C++ codegen]
    D --> E[Libero SoC\nSynthesis]
    E --> F[PolarFire FPGA\n25 ns latency]
    F --> G[Rad-hard\nOn-detector Inference]

Where It’s Useful:

High-energy physics trigger systems requiring sub-100 ns inference latency at LHC detector frontends (e.g., LHCb, ATLAS, CMS upgrade detectors)
Any safety-critical or space-grade application requiring FPGA inference in ionizing radiation environments (satellite payloads, nuclear instrumentation)
Calorimeter signal compression and anomaly detection where data must be reduced on-sensor before transmission

Limitations / Caveats:

Backend is currently validated on a single model class (autoencoder); broader layer/op support (RNNs, attention) requires additional backend development
PolarFire’s DSP and BRAM architecture differs enough from Xilinx that latency/resource estimates from existing hls4ml profiling tools do not transfer directly
Radiation hardness applies to configuration logic; user logic in fabric still accumulates single-event upsets under extreme flux without additional mitigation (TMR)
10-bit quantization was validated for this specific autoencoder task; other models may require task-specific quantization tuning

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: The hardware-aware quantization workflow (sweep bit-width vs. metric degradation) maps directly to post-training quantization of embedding lookup and scoring networks for edge inference on constrained hardware
LLM serving: The backend code-generation pattern — decoupling model IR from target-specific HLS emission — mirrors the compiler backend approach used in LLM inference engines (e.g., TVM, XLA); the same strategy could target novel accelerator ISAs
Anomaly detection: The autoencoder-in-latent-space compression approach (32 samples → 2D latent) is directly applicable to streaming anomaly detection in logging pipelines where reconstruction error serves as an outlier score

literature/2026-02-18-Learning-to-Config.title: Learning to Configure Agentic AI Systems
literature/2026-02-18-Learning-to-Config.content:
Learning to Configure Agentic AI Systems
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-12
Rank: #2 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM, RL, Search, RAG
Abstract
Configuring LLM-based agent systems involves choosing workflows, tools, token budgets, and prompts from a large combinatorial design space, and is typically handled today by fixed large templates or hand-tuned heuristics. This leads to brittle behavior and unnecessary compute, since the same cumbersome configuration is often applied to both easy and hard input queries. We formulate agent configuration as a query-wise decision problem and introduce ARC (Agentic Resource & Configuration learner), which learns a light-weight hierarchical policy using reinforcement learning to dynamically tailor these configurations. Across multiple benchmarks spanning reasoning and tool-augmented question answering, the learned policy consistently outperforms strong hand-designed and other baselines, achieving up to 25% higher task accuracy while also reducing token and runtime costs. These results demonstrate that learning per-query agent configurations is a powerful alternative to “one size fits all” designs.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLearning to Configure Agentic AI Systems0.78MATA: A Trainable Hierarchical Automaton System for Multi-Agent Visual Reasoning0.21A Picture of Agentic Search0.19Look Inward to Explore Outward: Learning Temperature Policy from LLM Internal States via Hierarchical RL0.18DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.16
Agentic Resource & Configuration Learner (ARC)
Core Idea: A hierarchical reinforcement learning policy that dynamically selects per-query agent configurations (workflows, tools, token budgets, prompts) rather than applying a single fixed template to all inputs.
Problem It Solves: Static “one-size-fits-all” agent configurations waste compute on easy queries and underperform on hard ones; hand-tuned heuristics are brittle and fail to exploit the combinatorial configuration space effectively.
How It Works:

Query intake: A lightweight policy network observes the incoming query (and optionally its difficulty signal) to produce a configuration selection.
Hierarchical decision: The policy operates in two levels — a high-level selector chooses coarse configuration categories (e.g., ReAct vs. chain-of-thought workflow, tool subset, budget tier), then a low-level selector refines within that category (specific prompt variant, exact token cap).
RL training: The policy is trained via reinforcement learning using task accuracy and token cost as reward signals, learning which configuration classes generalize to which query types without exhaustive enumeration.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    Q[Query] --> P[Lightweight Policy\nHigh-level Selector]
    P --> C1[Workflow Choice\ne.g. ReAct / CoT]
    P --> C2[Tool Subset]
    P --> C3[Token Budget Tier]
    C1 & C2 & C3 --> L[Low-level Refiner]
    L --> CFG[Final Configuration]
    CFG --> A[LLM Agent Execution]
    A --> R[Reward: Accuracy - Cost]
    R -->|RL update| P

Where It’s Useful:

Agentic reasoning systems (tool-augmented QA, multi-step planning) where query difficulty varies widely
Cost-sensitive LLM deployments requiring adaptive compute allocation
Multi-benchmark or multi-domain agent pipelines needing automatic prompt/workflow specialization

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires a reward signal at training time, which may be unavailable or noisy for open-ended generation tasks
Policy generalization to out-of-distribution query types is not guaranteed; may need retraining as task distributions shift
Adds a meta-level training loop on top of the base agent, increasing system complexity and offline data requirements

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: Dynamically configure a retrieval-augmented agent based on query intent class (navigational vs. exploratory), allocating deeper tool chains only to ambiguous or complex user requests
LLM serving infrastructure: Route queries to different prompt templates and context budgets based on predicted complexity, reducing p99 latency and token costs without sacrificing accuracy on hard queries
Pipeline orchestration: Replace fixed multi-step pipelines with a learned dispatcher that selects the minimal sufficient workflow per request, directly optimizing the accuracy/cost frontier

Discussions & References

literature/2026-02-18-NeuroSymActive-Dif.title: NeuroSymActive: Differentiable Neural-Symbolic Reasoning with Active Exploration for Knowledge Graph Question Answering
literature/2026-02-18-NeuroSymActive-Dif.content:
NeuroSymActive: Differentiable Neural-Symbolic Reasoning with Active Exploration for Knowledge Graph Question Answering
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #4 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: Embeddings, LLM, Graph, RAG, Search
Abstract
Large pretrained language models and neural reasoning systems have advanced many natural language tasks, yet they remain challenged by knowledge-intensive queries that require precise, structured multi-hop inference. Knowledge graphs provide a compact symbolic substrate for factual grounding, but integrating graph structure with neural models is nontrivial: naively embedding graph facts into prompts leads to inefficiency and fragility, while purely symbolic or search-heavy approaches can be costly in retrievals and lack gradient-based refinement. We introduce NeuroSymActive, a modular framework that combines a differentiable neural-symbolic reasoning layer with an active, value-guided exploration controller for Knowledge Graph Question Answering. The method couples soft-unification style symbolic modules with a neural path evaluator and a Monte-Carlo style exploration policy that prioritizes high-value path expansions. Empirical results on standard KGQA benchmarks show that NeuroSymActive attains strong answer accuracy while reducing the number of expensive graph lookups and model calls compared to common retrieval-augmented baselines.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityBridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning0.30Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning0.29Bridging Graph Structure and Knowledge-Guided Editing for Interpretable Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning0.25LIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs0.21Unified Multi-Domain Graph Pre-training for Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Graphs via Domain-Specific Expert Encoding0.20
NeuroSymActive
Core Idea: A modular framework that couples differentiable soft-unification symbolic reasoning with a Monte Carlo value-guided exploration controller to perform efficient multi-hop Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA).
Problem It Solves: Integrating knowledge graph structure with neural models is caught between two failure modes — embedding raw graph facts into prompts is inefficient and brittle, while purely symbolic/search-heavy traversal is expensive in graph lookups and lacks gradient-based refinement. NeuroSymActive bridges this by making symbolic reasoning differentiable and steering exploration toward high-value paths rather than exhaustive retrieval.
How It Works:

Soft-Unification Module: Given a natural language question, symbolic predicates and entities are matched to KG triples via learned soft-unification scores (differentiable fuzzy matching), producing weighted partial proofs rather than hard boolean lookups.
Neural Path Evaluator: A neural scorer evaluates candidate reasoning chains (sequences of KG edges), assigning value estimates to partial paths based on their relevance to the question — providing gradient signal to refine the unification weights end-to-end.
Active Exploration Controller: A Monte Carlo-style policy uses the path evaluator’s value estimates to prioritize which graph nodes/edges to expand next, reducing expensive graph lookups by skipping low-value branches and focusing budget on promising paths.
Answer Extraction: The highest-scoring complete paths are decoded to retrieve final answer entities from the KG.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    Q[Question] --> SU[Soft-Unification\nModule]
    KG[(Knowledge\nGraph)] --> SU
    SU --> PE[Neural Path\nEvaluator]
    PE -->|value estimates| AC[Active Exploration\nController]
    AC -->|prioritized expansions| KG
    AC --> PE
    PE --> AE[Answer\nExtraction]
    AE --> ANS[Answer Entities]

Where It’s Useful:

Multi-hop question answering over structured KGs (e.g., Freebase, Wikidata, domain ontologies)
Retrieval-augmented generation pipelines where KG lookup cost must be constrained
Neuro-symbolic AI systems requiring interpretable reasoning chains with gradient-based learning
Enterprise knowledge base QA where graph traversal over large schemas must be efficient

Limitations / Caveats:

Soft-unification quality degrades when surface-form mismatches between question language and KG predicates are severe (requires good entity/relation linking upstream)
Monte Carlo exploration introduces non-determinism; path budgets must be tuned per graph density or question complexity
Differentiable symbolic modules add architectural complexity vs. pure retrieval-augmented baselines, raising engineering overhead
Evaluation is on standard KGQA benchmarks — generalization to highly heterogeneous or rapidly-updated KGs is unverified

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: Value-guided path expansion mirrors beam search with learned heuristics — applicable to structured session graphs or user-item knowledge graphs where multi-hop reasoning explains recommendations
LLM grounding: The soft-unification layer can serve as a structured retrieval module replacing dense passage retrieval when a KG exists, providing more precise factual grounding with lower hallucination risk
Query understanding: The neural path evaluator can score candidate entity/intent chains in query rewriting pipelines, prioritizing expansions that match structured product or content taxonomies

literature/2026-02-18-On-Surprising-Effe.title: On Surprising Effectiveness of Masking Updates in Adaptive Optimizers
literature/2026-02-18-On-Surprising-Effe.content:
On Surprising Effectiveness of Masking Updates in Adaptive Optimizers
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #7 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: LLM
Abstract
Training large language models (LLMs) relies almost exclusively on dense adaptive optimizers with increasingly sophisticated preconditioners. We challenge this by showing that randomly masking parameter updates can be highly effective, with a masked variant of RMSProp consistently outperforming recent state-of-the-art optimizers. Our analysis reveals that the random masking induces a curvature-dependent geometric regularization that smooths the optimization trajectory. Motivated by this finding, we introduce Momentum-aligned gradient masking (Magma), which modulates the masked updates using momentum-gradient alignment. Extensive LLM pre-training experiments show that Magma is a simple drop-in replacement for adaptive optimizers with consistent gains and negligible computational overhead. Notably, for the 1B model size, Magma reduces perplexity by over 19% and 9% compared to Adam and Muon, respectively.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLCSB: Layer-Cyclic Selective Backpropagation for Memory-Efficient On-Device LLM Fine-Tuning0.20Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.19Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.19Quantized Evolution Strategies: High-precision Fine-tuning of Quantized LLMs at Low-precision Cost0.17Masked Diffusion Generative Recommendation0.16
Magma (Momentum-aligned Gradient Masking)
Core Idea: Selectively mask parameter updates based on alignment between current gradients and momentum, inducing curvature-aware geometric regularization that smooths optimization trajectories.
Problem It Solves: Dense adaptive optimizers (Adam, Muon) apply updates to all parameters at every step, accumulating noise along high-curvature directions and making optimization trajectories erratic; existing methods address this with increasingly complex preconditioners at significant computational cost.
How It Works:

Compute momentum-gradient alignment: At each step, measure the cosine alignment score between the current gradient g_t and the exponential moving average momentum m_t for each parameter; aligned parameters (score > threshold) are candidates for updating.
Generate momentum-aligned mask: Construct a binary mask M_t that selects the top-k% of parameters by alignment score, concentrating updates on parameters whose gradient direction is consistent with recent history.
Apply masked adaptive update: Apply the standard RMSProp/Adam preconditioned update elementwise, but zero out masked positions — only aligned parameters receive gradient steps, with the effective update being M_t ⊙ (g_t / sqrt(v_t + ε)).

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    G[Gradient g_t] --> A[EMA Momentum m_t]
    G --> V[EMA Variance v_t]
    A --> AL[Alignment Score\ncos sim g_t · m_t]
    AL --> MK[Binary Mask M_t\ntop-k% by alignment]
    V --> PC[Preconditioned Update\ng_t / sqrt v_t]
    MK --> APP[Masked Apply\nM_t ⊙ preconditioned]
    PC --> APP
    APP --> P[Parameter Update Δθ]

Where It’s Useful:

LLM pre-training at scale (demonstrated 1B+ parameter models with >19% perplexity reduction vs. Adam)
Any setting where optimizer memory/compute budget is constrained but adaptive updates are needed
Training runs sensitive to loss spikes or unstable trajectories, where gradient noise dominates

Limitations / Caveats:

Masking ratio (k%) is an additional hyperparameter requiring tuning; sensitivity across architectures is not fully characterized
Theoretical analysis is for random masking; the alignment-based masking adds inductive bias whose interaction with specific architectures (e.g., MoE, SSMs) is unexplored
Gains over Adam and Muon are reported on language modeling; generalization to vision, RL, or fine-tuning regimes is unverified

Internal Use Cases:

LLM pre-training: Drop-in Adam replacement with lower perplexity at equivalent compute; directly applicable to any internal foundation model runs
Ranking/RecSys embedding training: Sparse gradient regimes (embedding tables) may benefit from alignment-gated updates that avoid noisy updates to rarely-seen features
Fine-tuning / RLHF: Momentum-gradient alignment naturally suppresses conflicting gradient signals across tasks, potentially useful for multi-task or preference-tuning stages

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-18-OpaqueToolsBench-L.title: OpaqueToolsBench: Learning Nuances of Tool Behavior Through Interaction
literature/2026-02-18-OpaqueToolsBench-L.content:
OpaqueToolsBench: Learning Nuances of Tool Behavior Through Interaction
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #5 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search, RAG
Abstract
Tool-calling is essential for Large Language Model (LLM) agents to complete real-world tasks. While most existing benchmarks assume simple, perfectly documented tools, real-world tools (e.g., general “search” APIs) are often opaque, lacking clear best practices or failure modes. Can LLM agents improve their performance in environments with opaque tools by interacting and subsequently improving documentation? To study this, we create OpaqueToolsBench, a benchmark consisting of three distinct task-oriented environments: general function calling, interactive chess playing, and long-trajectory agentic search. Each environment provides underspecified tools that models must learn to use effectively to complete the task. Results on OpaqueToolsBench suggest existing methods for automatically documenting tools are expensive and unreliable when tools are opaque. To address this, we propose a simple framework, ToolObserver, that iteratively refines tool documentation by observing execution feedback from tool-calling trajectories. Our approach outperforms existing methods on OpaqueToolsBench across datasets, even in relatively hard settings. Furthermore, for test-time tool exploration settings, our method is also efficient, consuming 3.5-7.5x fewer total tokens than the best baseline.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityTVCACHE: A Stateful Tool-Value Cache for Post-Training LLM Agents0.29SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.26Ontology-to-tools compilation for executable semantic constraint enforcement in LLM agents0.26AgentStepper: Interactive Debugging of Software Development Agents0.26Learning to Configure Agentic AI Systems0.18
ToolObserver
Core Idea: Iteratively refines tool documentation by observing execution feedback from tool-calling trajectories, enabling LLM agents to self-improve their understanding of opaque tools without expensive human annotation.
Problem It Solves: Real-world tools are often underspecified—lacking clear best practices, edge case behavior, and failure modes—making existing LLM benchmarks (which assume perfectly documented tools) poor proxies for actual deployment. Existing auto-documentation methods are token-expensive and unreliable when tool behavior must be inferred empirically.
How It Works:

Execute: Agent attempts tasks using tools with minimal/underspecified documentation, generating interaction trajectories (tool calls + execution feedback/errors).
Observe: ToolObserver analyzes the trajectory outputs—successes, failures, and edge case responses—to extract behavioral signals about each tool (e.g., implicit constraints, failure patterns, effective call patterns).
Refine: Extracted observations are used to iteratively update the tool documentation, producing progressively more accurate and complete tool descriptions that are fed back into subsequent agent calls.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Underspecified\nTool Docs] --> B[LLM Agent]
    B --> C[Tool Calls +\nTrajectories]
    C --> D[ToolObserver\nObservation Module]
    D --> E[Documentation\nRefinement]
    E --> A
    C --> F[Task Output]

Where It’s Useful:

LLM agents operating against third-party or legacy APIs with poor documentation
Agentic systems that must adapt to proprietary search or retrieval APIs with non-obvious failure modes
Automated tool onboarding pipelines where human documentation effort is impractical
Long-horizon agentic tasks where tool misuse compounds across steps

Limitations / Caveats:

Refinement quality depends on the diversity and coverage of trajectories—rare failure modes may not surface unless the task distribution exercises them
Iterative refinement adds latency before the agent reaches peak performance; unsuitable for single-shot or extremely low-latency settings
Documentation improvements may overfit to the observed task distribution and fail to generalize to novel tool usage patterns

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: Agents calling internal retrieval or feature-serving APIs (e.g., candidate generators, feature stores) could use ToolObserver to auto-document implicit rate limits, cold-start behaviors, or field-level quirks without manual API audits
LLM pipeline tooling: When chaining LLM calls with external tools (web search, SQL engines, vector DBs), ToolObserver-style refinement could reduce prompt engineering overhead by learning effective query patterns empirically
A/B testing agents: Agents that interact with experimentation APIs could self-document exposure rules and eligibility constraints discovered through interaction

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-18-Recursive-Concept-.title: Recursive Concept Evolution for Compositional Reasoning in Large Language Models
literature/2026-02-18-Recursive-Concept-.content:
Recursive Concept Evolution for Compositional Reasoning in Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #8 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RL, Search, RAG
Abstract
Large language models achieve strong performance on many complex reasoning tasks, yet their accuracy degrades sharply on benchmarks that require compositional reasoning, including ARC-AGI-2, GPQA, MATH, BBH, and HLE. Existing methods improve reasoning by expanding token-level search through chain-of-thought prompting, self-consistency, or reinforcement learning, but they leave the model’s latent representation space fixed. When the required abstraction is not already encoded in this space, performance collapses. We propose Recursive Concept Evolution (RCE), a framework that enables pretrained language models to modify their internal representation geometry during inference. RCE introduces dynamically generated low-rank concept subspaces that are spawned when representational inadequacy is detected, selected through a minimum description length criterion, merged when synergistic, and consolidated via constrained optimization to preserve stability. This process allows the model to construct new abstractions rather than recombining existing ones. We integrate RCE with Mistral-7B and evaluate it across compositional reasoning benchmarks. RCE yields 12-18 point gains on ARC-AGI-2, 8-14 point improvements on GPQA and BBH, and consistent reductions in depth-induced error on MATH and HLE.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityReThinker: Scientific Reasoning by Rethinking with Guided Reflection and Confidence Control0.14Why Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models0.14Decomposing Query-Key Feature Interactions Using Contrastive Covariances0.14Why Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models0.13Inverse Depth Scaling From Most Layers Being Similar0.12
Recursive Concept Evolution (RCE)
Core Idea: RCE enables pretrained LLMs to dynamically construct new low-rank representational subspaces during inference when existing latent geometry is insufficient to support a required abstraction.
Problem It Solves: LLMs hit a hard ceiling on compositional reasoning tasks (ARC-AGI-2, GPQA, MATH, BBH, HLE) because their representation space is fixed at inference time — when a required abstraction isn’t already encoded, the model cannot construct it, causing sharp accuracy degradation that token-level search methods (chain-of-thought, self-consistency, RL) cannot fix.
How It Works:

Detect representational inadequacy: Monitor intermediate activations during a forward pass; flag when the current latent geometry cannot faithfully encode the concept required by the current reasoning step (e.g., via high reconstruction error or entropy in the activation distribution).
Spawn a low-rank concept subspace: Dynamically generate a new low-rank adapter (LoRA-style projection matrix) targeted at the detected gap; select it using a Minimum Description Length (MDL) criterion to avoid overfitting or redundancy.
Merge synergistic subspaces: Identify newly spawned subspaces that are geometrically compatible with existing ones and merge them to reduce overhead and encourage generalization.
Consolidate via constrained optimization: Update the merged representation using constrained optimization (e.g., projected gradient) to integrate the new abstraction while preserving stability of existing knowledge.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input Tokens] --> B[Frozen Pretrained LLM Layers]
    B --> C{Representational\nAdequacy Check}
    C -- Adequate --> F[Continue Forward Pass]
    C -- Inadequate --> D[Spawn Low-Rank\nConcept Subspace\nMDL Selection]
    D --> E[Merge Synergistic\nSubspaces]
    E --> G[Constrained Optimization\nConsolidation]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Output / Next Layer]

Where It’s Useful:

Compositional and multi-step reasoning tasks that require building new abstractions from primitives (ARC-AGI-2, math olympiad problems)
Scientific question answering requiring cross-domain concept synthesis (GPQA)
Any inference-time scenario where the required concept distribution is out-of-distribution relative to pretraining

Limitations / Caveats:

Inference-time subspace spawning adds latency and memory overhead proportional to the number of detected inadequacies — cost is unpredictable per sample
MDL-based selection and constrained consolidation introduce hyperparameters that may need tuning per domain; sensitivity analysis is not yet established
Gains are demonstrated on Mistral-7B; generalization to larger models or different architectures is unverified
Dynamically modifying activations at inference time risks instability if consolidation constraints are too loose — the stability-plasticity tradeoff is not fully characterized
No persistent learning across inference calls — each session spawns subspaces independently, so improvements do not accumulate

Internal Use Cases:

LLM reranking: When a ranker LLM encounters a query type poorly covered by its training distribution, RCE could spawn subspaces to better encode novel relevance signals without retraining
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG): For queries requiring compositional synthesis across multiple retrieved chunks, RCE could build transient abstractions bridging disparate concepts mid-inference
Cold-start feature synthesis: In ranking models using LLM-generated features, RCE-style dynamic subspace construction could handle novel item/user concept combinations not seen at train time

literature/2026-02-18-ResearchGym-Evalua.title: ResearchGym: Evaluating Language Model Agents on Real-World AI Research
literature/2026-02-18-ResearchGym-Evalua.content:
ResearchGym: Evaluating Language Model Agents on Real-World AI Research
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #9 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search, RAG
Abstract
We introduce ResearchGym, a benchmark and execution environment for evaluating AI agents on end-to-end research. To instantiate this, we repurpose five oral and spotlight papers from ICML, ICLR, and ACL. From each paper’s repository, we preserve the datasets, evaluation harness, and baseline implementations but withhold the paper’s proposed method. This results in five containerized task environments comprising 39 sub-tasks in total. Within each environment, agents must propose novel hypotheses, run experiments, and attempt to surpass strong human baselines on the paper’s metrics. In a controlled evaluation of an agent powered by GPT-5, we observe a sharp capability—reliability gap. The agent improves over the provided baselines from the repository in just 1 of 15 evaluations (6.7%) by 11.5%, and completes only 26.5% of sub-tasks on average. We identify recurring long-horizon failure modes, including impatience, poor time and resource management, overconfidence in weak hypotheses, difficulty coordinating parallel experiments, and hard limits from context length. Yet in a single run, the agent surpasses the solution of an ICML 2025 Spotlight task, indicating that frontier agents can occasionally reach state-of-the-art performance, but do so unreliably. We additionally evaluate proprietary agent scaffolds including Claude Code (Opus-4.5) and Codex (GPT-5.2) which display a similar gap. ResearchGym provides infrastructure for systematic evaluation and analysis of autonomous agents on closed-loop research.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.24AgentStepper: Interactive Debugging of Software Development Agents0.23LLMs as the new high level language0.23Hybrid-Gym: Training Coding Agents to Generalize Across Tasks0.23ASTRA: Automated Synthesis of agentic Trajectories and Reinforcement Arenas0.22
ResearchGym
Core Idea: A containerized benchmark environment that evaluates AI agents on end-to-end scientific research by providing baseline implementations from real published papers while withholding the proposed methods, requiring agents to independently discover and surpass state-of-the-art solutions.
Problem It Solves: Existing agent benchmarks test narrow, synthetic tasks rather than the full research loop (hypothesis generation → experimentation → evaluation), making it impossible to measure whether agents can perform genuine open-ended scientific discovery in realistic computational environments.
How It Works:

Environment Construction: Select high-quality published papers (oral/spotlight from ICML, ICLR, ACL); extract their repositories and preserve datasets, evaluation harnesses, and baseline code while surgically removing the paper’s proposed method
Containerization: Package each stripped repository into an isolated Docker environment with fixed compute budgets, dependency versions, and reproducible evaluation metrics — yielding 5 task environments with 39 sub-tasks
Agent Evaluation: Deploy an agent (e.g., GPT-5, Claude Code, Codex) into the container; the agent must read the codebase, propose hypotheses, implement experiments, execute them, and iteratively improve metric scores against the held-out human baseline
Scoring: Compare agent-achieved metric scores against both the provided baseline and the withheld paper’s result; sub-task completion rate and improvement percentage are tracked separately

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Published Paper + Repo] --> B[Method Stripping]
    B --> C[Containerized Environment\ndatasets + eval harness + baselines]
    C --> D[Agent Interface\nread/write/execute]
    D --> E[Agent\nGPT-5 / Claude / Codex]
    E -->|hypothesize + implement| D
    D --> F[Eval Harness]
    F --> G[Score vs Baseline\nvs Paper Method]

Where It’s Useful:

Benchmarking frontier AI agents on realistic long-horizon scientific tasks
Identifying failure modes in agentic systems (resource management, parallelism, context limits)
Measuring capability-reliability gaps in state-of-the-art models
Guiding development of agent scaffolds for autonomous research acceleration

Limitations / Caveats:

Only 5 task environments (39 sub-tasks) — narrow coverage of research domains and difficulty levels
Withholding the method from a repo may leave implicit clues in comments, variable names, or ablation configs, potentially leaking signal
Fixed compute/time budgets may disadvantage agents that could succeed given more resources, conflating resource limits with capability limits
Evaluation is expensive and slow, limiting large-scale sweeps across many agents or hyperparameter configurations
Tasks sourced exclusively from ML venues — no coverage of biology, chemistry, or other scientific domains

Internal Use Cases:

LLM ranking/evaluation pipelines: Use the containerized eval harness pattern to create closed-loop AutoML agents that propose and test ranking feature combinations against held-out offline metrics
Recommender system research: Instantiate ResearchGym-style environments around internal RecSys papers to test whether agents can rediscover retrieval or re-ranking improvements autonomously
Agent reliability analysis: Apply the capability-reliability gap framework to audit internal agent deployments — tracking what fraction of runs that “succeed once” succeed consistently, as a production readiness signal

Discussions & References

huggingface.co
github.com

literature/2026-02-18-ScrapeGraphAI-100k.title: ScrapeGraphAI-100k: A Large-Scale Dataset for LLM-Based Web Information Extraction
literature/2026-02-18-ScrapeGraphAI-100k.content:
ScrapeGraphAI-100k: A Large-Scale Dataset for LLM-Based Web Information Extraction
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #6 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search, RAG
Abstract
The use of large language models for web information extraction is becoming increasingly fundamental to modern web information retrieval pipelines. However, existing datasets tend to be small, synthetic or text-only, failing to capture the structural context of the web. We introduce ScrapeGraphAI-100k, a large-scale dataset comprising real-world LLM extraction events, collected via opt-in ScrapeGraphAI telemetry during Q2 and Q3 of 2025. Starting from 9M events, we deduplicate and balance by schema to produce 93,695 examples spanning diverse domains and languages. Each instance includes Markdown content, a prompt, a JSON schema, the LLM response, and complexity/validation metadata. We characterize the datasets structural diversity and its failure modes as schema complexity increases. We also provide a fine-tuning experiment showing that a small language model (1.7B) trained on a subset narrows the gap to larger baselines (30B), underscoring the datasets utility for efficient extraction. ScrapeGraphAI-100k enables fine-tuning small models, benchmarking structured extraction, and studying schema induction for web IR indexing, and is publicly available on HuggingFace.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLLMStructBench: Benchmarking Large Language Model Structured Data Extraction0.27A Feature Extraction Pipeline for Enhancing Lightweight Neural Networks in sEMG-based Joint Torque Estimation0.17CoDa: Constrained Generation based Data Augmentation for Low-Resource NLP0.15DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.13DynaWeb: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning of Web Agents0.12
ScrapeGraphAI-100k Dataset Construction Pipeline
Core Idea: A real-world, large-scale dataset of LLM-based structured web extraction events is constructed from opt-in telemetry, then deduplicated and schema-balanced to produce high-quality training and benchmark examples.
Problem It Solves: Existing web information extraction datasets are small, synthetic, or text-only, lacking structural context (schemas, JSON outputs, validation metadata) needed to train or benchmark LLMs on realistic structured extraction tasks.
How It Works:

Collect: Gather ~9M raw extraction events via opt-in ScrapeGraphAI telemetry (Q2–Q3 2025); each event captures Markdown page content, a natural-language prompt, a JSON output schema, the LLM response, and complexity/validation metadata.
Deduplicate: Remove near-duplicate events based on content and schema similarity to prevent training set contamination and benchmark leakage.
Schema-balance: Subsample across schema types and complexity levels to produce 93,695 diverse examples, preventing overrepresentation of simple or common schema patterns.
Annotate: Attach per-example metadata including schema complexity scores and validation pass/fail labels for use as training signal or evaluation filters.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[9M Raw Telemetry Events] --> B[Deduplication]
    B --> C[Schema Complexity Scoring]
    C --> D[Stratified Balancing]
    D --> E[93,695 Examples]
    E --> F[Fine-tuning / Benchmarking]

    subgraph Each Example
        G[Markdown Content]
        H[NL Prompt]
        I[JSON Schema]
        J[LLM Response]
        K[Validation Metadata]
    end

Where It’s Useful:

Fine-tuning small language models (1–3B) for structured extraction, narrowing the gap to much larger models (30B+)
Benchmarking LLMs on realistic structured web extraction across domains and languages
Studying schema induction for web IR indexing pipelines
Evaluating extraction robustness as output schema complexity scales

Limitations / Caveats:

Telemetry bias: distribution reflects ScrapeGraphAI user behavior, not the full diversity of web extraction use cases
Extraction quality depends on the LLMs used during collection; responses are not ground-truth-verified by humans
Schema complexity increase correlates with failure mode proliferation, limiting utility for very complex schemas
Opt-in collection may undersample niche domains or low-resource languages

Internal Use Cases:

LLM pipeline grounding: Use as a source of realistic (prompt, schema, page) triples to evaluate or fine-tune extraction steps in research digestion or content indexing pipelines
Schema-conditioned ranking: Study how schema structure affects extraction fidelity, informing schema design for structured data retrieval
Small model distillation: The fine-tuning result (1.7B approaching 30B) is directly applicable to cost-efficient extraction in high-throughput pipelines like paper digest or newsletter generation

literature/2026-02-18-Sparrow-Text-Ancho.title: Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs
literature/2026-02-18-Sparrow-Text-Ancho.content:
Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing for Speculative Decoding in Video LLMs
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #1 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RAG
Abstract
Although speculative decoding is widely used to accelerate Vision-Language Models (VLMs) inference, it faces severe performance collapse when applied to Video Large Language Models (Vid-LLMs). The draft model typically falls into the trap of attention dilution and negative visual gain due to key-value cache explosion and context window mismatches. We observe a visual semantic internalization phenomenon in Vid-LLMs, indicating that critical visual semantics are implicitly encoded into text hidden states during deep-layer interactions, which renders raw visual inputs structurally redundant during deep inference. To address this, we propose the Sparrow framework, which first utilizes visually-aware text-anchored window attention via hidden state reuse to fully offload visual computation to the target model, and leverages intermediate-layer visual state bridging to train the draft model with semantic-rich intermediate states, thereby filtering out low-level visual noise. Additionally, a multi-token prediction strategy is introduced to bridge the training-inference distribution shift. Experiments show that Sparrow achieves an average speedup of 2.82x even with 25k visual tokens, effectively resolving the performance degradation in long sequences and offering a practical solution for real-time long video tasks.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDo MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.45Thinking with Drafting: Optical Decompression via Logical Reconstruction0.40VIPA: Visual Informative Part Attention for Referring Image Segmentation0.39PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models0.38ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.37
Sparrow: Text-Anchored Window Attention with Visual-Semantic Glimpsing
Core Idea: A speculative decoding framework for Video LLMs that offloads visual computation entirely to the target model by reusing cached text hidden states in a sliding window attention mechanism, while training the draft model on semantically-enriched intermediate layer states instead of raw visual tokens.
Problem It Solves: Standard speculative decoding collapses on Video LLMs because the draft model suffers from (1) attention dilution — visual KV-cache grows proportionally with video length, drowning out relevant signal — and (2) negative visual gain — processing raw visual tokens in the draft model adds noise rather than useful signal, since by deep inference layers the visual semantics have already been absorbed into text hidden states.
How It Works:
