Identify visual semantic internalization: Empirically confirm that deep-layer text hidden states already encode critical visual semantics, making raw visual token inputs to the draft model structurally redundant.
Text-anchored window attention (TAWA): The draft model attends only over a sliding window of recent text tokens using cached hidden states (bypassing raw visual tokens entirely), effectively routing all visual computation to the target model’s full attention pass.
Intermediate-layer visual state bridging (glimpsing): During draft model training, inject semantic-rich hidden states from intermediate layers of the target model as supervision signal, filtering low-level visual noise and aligning the draft model’s internal representations.
Multi-token prediction (MTP): Train the draft model to predict multiple future tokens per step, closing the distribution gap between training (teacher-forced) and inference (autoregressive speculation) modes.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    V[Video Frames] --> T[Target Model\nFull Attention]
    T -->|Intermediate hidden states| B[Visual State Bridge\nGlimpsing]
    B -->|Semantic supervision| D[Draft Model\nTAWA Sliding Window]
    D -->|Candidate token sequences| V2[Verification\nby Target Model]
    V2 -->|Accept / reject| O[Final Output Tokens]
    T --> V2

Where It’s Useful:

Real-time long-video understanding (25k+ visual tokens) where standard speculative decoding degrades
Any Vid-LLM inference pipeline with KV-cache pressure from dense visual token sequences
Streaming video captioning, video QA, and temporal reasoning tasks requiring low-latency generation

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires access to intermediate target model activations at training time, coupling draft model training tightly to a specific target architecture
Window size in TAWA is a fixed hyperparameter — may need tuning per task or video length regime
Speedup gains are measured against a specific draft/target pairing; generalization to arbitrary model pairs is unverified

Internal Use Cases:

Long-context ranking/retrieval: The hidden-state-reuse principle applies when a lightweight scorer needs to re-rank items conditioned on a long multimodal context — offload heavy modality encoding to a single full-pass model, reuse its states for fast candidate scoring
LLM serving with multimodal inputs: Any system processing high-token-count visual inputs (product images, ad creatives, video ads) can use TAWA-style draft models to reduce per-request latency without sacrificing accuracy on the full model verification pass
Speculative decoding for dense retrieval re-rankers: The intermediate-layer bridging idea generalizes to training lightweight re-rankers supervised on deep representations from a large cross-encoder, preserving semantic richness while cutting inference cost

literature/2026-02-18-The-Equalizer-Intr.title: The Equalizer: Introducing Shape-Gain Decomposition in Neural Audio Codecs
literature/2026-02-18-The-Equalizer-Intr.content:
The Equalizer: Introducing Shape-Gain Decomposition in Neural Audio Codecs
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #7 (score: 0.05, max_sim: 0.74)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Neural audio codecs (NACs) typically encode the short-term energy (gain) and normalized structure (shape) of speech/audio signals jointly within the same latent space. As a result, they are poorly robust to a global variation of the input signal level in the sense that such variation has strong influence on the embedding vectors at the output of the encoder and their quantization. This methodology is inherently inefficient, leading to codebook redundancy and suboptimal bitrate-distortion performance. To address these limitations, we propose to introduce shape-gain decomposition, widely used in classical speech/audio coding, into the NAC framework. The principle of the proposed Equalizer methodology is to decompose the input signal — before the NAC encoder — into gain and normalized shape vector on a short-term basis. The shape vector is processed by the NAC, while the gain is quantized with scalar quantization and transmitted separately. The output (decoded) signal is reconstructed from the normalized output of the NAC and the quantized gain. Our experiments conducted on speech signals show that this general methodology, easily applicable to any NAC, enables a substantial gain in bitrate-distortion performance, as well as a massive reduction in complexity.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPisets: A Robust Speech Recognition System for Lectures and Interviews0.21Benchmarking the Energy Savings with Speculative Decoding Strategies0.13Unheard in the Digital Age: Rethinking AI Bias and Speech Diversity0.12Price of universality in vector quantization is at most 0.11 bit0.12LGQ: Learning Discretization Geometry for Scalable and Stable Image Tokenization0.10
Shape-Gain Decomposition for Neural Audio Codecs (Equalizer)
Core Idea: Decompose audio signals into a normalized shape vector and a scalar gain before encoding, process them through separate quantization paths, and reconstruct the output signal post-decoding — preventing gain variation from polluting the NAC latent space.
Problem It Solves: Neural Audio Codecs (NACs) encode gain (short-term energy) and shape (normalized structure) jointly in the same latent space, causing global amplitude variations to strongly perturb encoder embeddings and their vector quantization, wasting codebook capacity on level-dependent redundancy and degrading bitrate-distortion performance.
How It Works:

Decompose: On a short-term frame basis, extract the gain (RMS or energy envelope) from the input audio signal and divide the signal by it to obtain a normalized shape vector with unit energy.
Encode shape: Pass the normalized shape vector through the standard NAC encoder + vector quantizer; because energy is factored out, the latent space captures only structural/spectral information.
Quantize gain separately: Scalar-quantize the gain values independently (low-bitrate side channel), transmitting them alongside the NAC bitstream.
Reconstruct: At the decoder, pass the received codes through the NAC decoder to get a normalized output waveform, then multiply by the dequantized gain to restore the correct amplitude.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Input Audio] --> B[Shape-Gain Decomposer]
    B -->|Normalized Shape| C[NAC Encoder]
    B -->|Gain| D[Scalar Quantizer]
    C --> E[Vector Quantizer]
    E --> F[NAC Decoder]
    F -->|Normalized Output| G[Gain Reapplication]
    D -->|Quantized Gain| G
    G --> H[Reconstructed Audio]

Where It’s Useful:

Neural speech/audio compression at low-to-mid bitrates where codebook efficiency is critical
Any NAC-based system (EnCodec, DAC, SoundStream, etc.) as a drop-in preprocessing wrapper
Speech synthesis and voice coding pipelines sensitive to loudness variation
Streaming audio applications where complexity reduction matters for real-time inference

Limitations / Caveats:

Gain quantization introduces a separate, albeit small, distortion source; coarse scalar quantization of gain can cause audible level artifacts
Short-term gain extraction requires careful frame alignment between the gain side channel and NAC decoder output to avoid reconstruction mismatches
Evaluated primarily on speech; generalization to music or general audio (with complex dynamic envelopes) is not demonstrated

Internal Use Cases:

Audio embedding models: Factoring out loudness before computing audio embeddings improves loudness-invariance, useful for audio retrieval/ranking where loudness is not a relevance signal
LLM audio tokenizers: NACs used to tokenize audio for LLM input (e.g., speech-language models) benefit from a cleaner, more compact token vocabulary when gain is removed from the latent space
Data augmentation robustness: Training ranking or recommendation models on audio features derived from Equalizer-preprocessed codecs reduces sensitivity to volume-normalized vs. non-normalized training data

literature/2026-02-18-The-Vision-Wormhol.title: The Vision Wormhole: Latent-Space Communication in Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Systems
literature/2026-02-18-The-Vision-Wormhol.content:
The Vision Wormhole: Latent-Space Communication in Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Systems
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #3 (score: 0.85, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) powered by Large Language Models have unlocked advanced collaborative reasoning, yet they remain shackled by the inefficiency of discrete text communication, which imposes significant runtime overhead and information quantization loss. While latent state transfer offers a high-bandwidth alternative, existing approaches either assume homogeneous sender-receiver architectures or rely on pair-specific learned translators, limiting scalability and modularity across diverse model families with disjoint manifolds. In this work, we propose the Vision Wormhole, a novel framework that repurposes the visual interface of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to enable model-agnostic, text-free communication. By introducing a Universal Visual Codec, we map heterogeneous reasoning traces into a shared continuous latent space and inject them directly into the receiver’s visual pathway, effectively treating the vision encoder as a universal port for inter-agent telepathy. Our framework adopts a hub-and-spoke topology to reduce pairwise alignment complexity from O(N^2) to O(N) and leverages a label-free, teacher-student distillation objective to align the high-speed visual channel with the robust reasoning patterns of the text pathway. Extensive experiments across heterogeneous model families (e.g., Qwen-VL, Gemma) demonstrate that the Vision Wormhole reduces end-to-end wall-clock time in controlled comparisons while maintaining reasoning fidelity comparable to standard text-based MAS. Code is available at github.com/xz-liu/heterogeneous-latent-mas
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDo MLLMs Really See It: Reinforcing Visual Attention in Multimodal LLMs0.35ViCA: Efficient Multimodal LLMs with Vision-Only Cross-Attention0.32Thinking with Drafting: Optical Decompression via Logical Reconstruction0.30ClueTracer: Question-to-Vision Clue Tracing for Training-Free Hallucination Suppression in Multimodal Reasoning0.30SwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMs0.29
Vision Wormhole (VW)
Core Idea: Repurpose the visual input pathway of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as a universal inter-agent communication channel by encoding heterogeneous reasoning traces into synthetic visual tokens, enabling text-free, model-agnostic latent state transfer between agents of different architectures.
Problem It Solves: Standard multi-agent LLM systems communicate via serialized text, incurring tokenization overhead, context-length bottlenecks, and lossy information quantization. Direct latent-space transfer is faster but breaks down across heterogeneous model families whose internal representation manifolds are disjoint — existing translators require O(N²) pairwise adapters that don’t generalize.
How It Works:

Encode: The sender agent produces a reasoning trace (hidden states or intermediate activations); a Universal Visual Codec (a lightweight encoder) maps these heterogeneous latent vectors into a shared continuous visual embedding space — a “hub” representation agnostic to the source architecture.
Transmit: The hub embedding is rendered as synthetic visual tokens and injected directly into the receiver VLM’s vision encoder input pathway, bypassing text generation entirely.
Align: A teacher-student distillation objective trains the visual channel (student) to reproduce the reasoning behavior of the text pathway (teacher), label-free — keeping the visual shortcut faithful to the model’s native reasoning patterns without requiring task-specific supervision.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Sender Agent\nHidden States] --> B[Universal Visual Codec\nHub Encoder]
    B --> C[Shared Visual\nEmbedding Space]
    C --> D[Spoke Decoder\nper receiver family]
    D --> E[Receiver VLM\nVision Encoder Port]
    E --> F[Receiver Agent\nReasoning Output]

    G[Text Pathway\nTeacher] -.->|distillation loss| D

Where It’s Useful:

Heterogeneous multi-agent pipelines where different tasks are routed to specialized model families (e.g., Qwen-VL for vision-heavy subtasks, Gemma for text reasoning)
High-throughput agentic systems where text serialization latency is a bottleneck (real-time tool-use chains, streaming inference)
Cross-architecture knowledge distillation at inference time, not just training time
Any MAS topology requiring N agents to share reasoning state without a shared tokenizer or vocabulary

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires all receiver agents to be VLMs with an accessible vision encoder — pure text-only LLMs cannot serve as receivers without architectural modification
Hub-and-spoke alignment still requires training per model family (O(N) spokes), so cold-start cost exists for new architectures
Distillation fidelity depends on the visual channel’s capacity; very long or highly structured reasoning traces may exceed what visual token budgets can represent
Controlled wall-clock comparisons may not reflect real deployment variance (batching, KV-cache effects, hardware heterogeneity)

Internal Use Cases:

Multi-stage ranking pipelines: Pass dense retrieval signals from an embedding model directly into a VLM reranker’s visual pathway rather than serializing candidate features as text, reducing reranking latency
LLM cascade architectures: In a cheap-model-first / expensive-model-fallback setup, encode the small model’s partial reasoning state visually and inject it into the large model, avoiding redundant prefill computation
Ensemble reasoning: Aggregate reasoning traces from N specialized agents (e.g., one per content domain) into a hub embedding and inject into a single arbitrator VLM, replacing expensive concatenated text prompts

literature/2026-02-18-jina-embeddings-v5.title: jina-embeddings-v5-text: Task-Targeted Embedding Distillation
literature/2026-02-18-jina-embeddings-v5.content:
jina-embeddings-v5-text: Task-Targeted Embedding Distillation
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #2 (score: 0.93, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Text embedding models are widely used for semantic similarity tasks, including information retrieval, clustering, and classification. General-purpose models are typically trained with single- or multi-stage processes using contrastive loss functions. We introduce a novel training regimen that combines model distillation techniques with task-specific contrastive loss to produce compact, high-performance embedding models. Our findings suggest that this approach is more effective for training small models than purely contrastive or distillation-based training paradigms alone. Benchmark scores for the resulting models, jina-embeddings-v5-text-small and jina-embeddings-v5-text-nano, exceed or match the state-of-the-art for models of similar size. jina-embeddings-v5-text models additionally support long texts (up to 32k tokens) in many languages, and generate embeddings that remain robust under truncation and binary quantization. Model weights are publicly available, hopefully inspiring further advances in embedding model development.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityThe Case for Multi-Vector Contrastive Pre-Training, Eliminating Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search in Large-Scale Recommendation, and More!0.23Semantic Search At LinkedIn0.20Mine and Refine: Optimizing Graded Relevance in E-commerce Search Retrieval0.20Domain-Adaptive and Scalable Dense Retrieval for Content-Based Recommendation0.20Pailitao-VL: Unified Embedding and Reranker for Real-Time Multi-Modal Industrial Search0.19
Task-Targeted Embedding Distillation (TTED)
Core Idea: Combine knowledge distillation from a large teacher embedding model with task-specific contrastive loss during student training, jointly optimizing for both teacher alignment and discriminative task performance.
Problem It Solves: Small embedding models trained purely with contrastive loss lack the representational richness of larger models, while pure distillation ignores task-specific discriminative structure — TTED addresses the quality/size tradeoff by leveraging both signals simultaneously.
How It Works:

Teacher forward pass: A large, high-capacity embedding model encodes input text pairs, producing dense embedding vectors that serve as soft targets capturing fine-grained semantic relationships.
Student forward pass: A compact student model (small/nano scale) encodes the same inputs, producing its own embeddings — these are supervised by two loss signals applied jointly.
Dual-loss optimization: A distillation loss (e.g., MSE or cosine distance) aligns student embeddings toward teacher embeddings, while a task-specific contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE/NT-Xent with hard negatives) enforces discriminative structure for the target task (retrieval, clustering, classification).
Weighted combination: Final loss = α · L_distill + (1 − α) · L_contrastive, where α balances fidelity to teacher vs. task discrimination.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    T[Large Teacher Model\nfrozen] -->|soft embedding targets| DL[Distillation Loss\nMSE / cosine]
    IN[Input Text Pairs] --> T
    IN --> S[Small Student Model\ntrainable]
    S --> DL
    S --> CL[Contrastive Loss\nInfoNCE + hard negatives]
    HN[Hard Negatives\nper task] --> CL
    DL --> L[Combined Loss\nα·L_distill + 1-α·L_contrastive]
    CL --> L
    L -->|backprop| S

Where It’s Useful:

Information retrieval / dense passage retrieval where compact models are needed at query-time
On-device or edge semantic search (nano-scale models with binary quantization)
Multilingual embedding with long-context documents (up to 32k tokens) under memory constraints
Embedding APIs where latency and cost scale with model size

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires a high-quality teacher model — student quality is upper-bounded by teacher representational capacity
Introduces an additional hyperparameter (α) requiring tuning per task or dataset mix
Teacher inference cost during training increases compute budget vs. pure contrastive training
Task-specific contrastive stage may reduce zero-shot generalization if task distribution is narrow

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: Train a nano-scale item embedding model distilled from a large cross-encoder teacher, enabling real-time candidate retrieval with minimal latency
LLM RAG pipelines: Replace large retrieval encoders with distilled small models that retain teacher-level retrieval quality, reducing embedding serving cost at scale
Query understanding: Distill a multilingual teacher into a compact model for query-to-intent classification, supporting 32k-token long-form query contexts

literature/2026-02-19-Agent-Skill-Framew.title: Agent Skill Framework: Perspectives on the Potential of Small Language Models in Industrial Environments
literature/2026-02-19-Agent-Skill-Framew.content:
Agent Skill Framework: Perspectives on the Potential of Small Language Models in Industrial Environments
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #5 (score: 0.89, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Agent Skill framework, now widely and officially supported by major players such as GitHub Copilot, LangChain, and OpenAI, performs especially well with proprietary models by improving context engineering, reducing hallucinations, and boosting task accuracy. Based on these observations, an investigation is conducted to determine whether the Agent Skill paradigm provides similar benefits to small language models (SLMs). This question matters in industrial scenarios where continuous reliance on public APIs is infeasible due to data-security and budget constraints requirements, and where SLMs often show limited generalization in highly customized scenarios. This work introduces a formal mathematical definition of the Agent Skill process, followed by a systematic evaluation of language models of varying sizes across multiple use cases. The evaluation encompasses two open-source tasks and a real-world insurance claims data set. The results show that tiny models struggle with reliable skill selection, while moderately sized SLMs (approximately 12B - 30B) parameters) benefit substantially from the Agent Skill approach. Moreover, code-specialized variants at around 80B parameters achieve performance comparable to closed-source baselines while improving GPU efficiency. Collectively, these findings provide a comprehensive and nuanced characterization of the capabilities and constraints of the framework, while providing actionable insights for the effective deployment of Agent Skills in SLM-centered environments.
Key Techniques

Agent Skill framework
context engineering
formal mathematical definition of Agent Skill process
systematic evaluation across multiple model sizes

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMemSkill: Learning and Evolving Memory Skills for Self-Evolving Agents0.41GenAI for Social Work Field Education: Client Simulation with Real-Time Feedback0.18JADE: Expert-Grounded Dynamic Evaluation for Open-Ended Professional Tasks0.16Adaptive Confidence Gating in Multi-Agent Collaboration for Efficient and Optimized Code Generation0.15GenAI for Social Work Field Education: Client Simulation with Real-Time Feedback0.15
Agent Skill Framework for Small Language Models (ASF-SLM)
Core Idea: A structured paradigm that decomposes agent tasks into discrete, formally-defined “skills” (atomic tool-calling units) to determine whether this context engineering approach—proven effective for large proprietary models—translates to small language models (SLMs) in constrained industrial deployments.
Problem It Solves: Industrial environments cannot rely on public LLM APIs due to data-security and cost constraints, yet locally-deployable SLMs exhibit poor generalization in highly customized scenarios; this work characterizes whether the Agent Skill abstraction compensates for SLM capability gaps.
How It Works:

Formal skill definition: Each skill is mathematically defined as a tuple (name, description, parameters, executor) that maps a natural-language intent to a deterministic function call with typed I/O
Skill selection: The SLM receives a task prompt plus the skill registry (descriptions + schemas) and must select the correct skill and populate its arguments—this is the critical bottleneck evaluated across model sizes
Skill execution & composition: Selected skills execute deterministically; multi-step tasks chain skill outputs as inputs to subsequent skill calls, reducing the LLM’s burden to selection/routing rather than computation

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Task Prompt] --> B[SLM Skill Selector]
    S[Skill Registry\nname + schema + description] --> B
    B --> C{Skill Selected?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Skill Executor\ndeterministic fn]
    C -- No / Hallucinated --> E[Failure / Fallback]
    D --> F[Result]
    F --> B

Where It’s Useful:

On-premise industrial automation (insurance claims processing, manufacturing QA) where data cannot leave the firewall
Budget-constrained deployments needing repeatable, auditable tool use without API costs
Regulated industries requiring explainable, step-traceable agent decisions

Limitations / Caveats:

Tiny models (<12B parameters) fail at reliable skill selection, making the framework ineffective regardless of skill quality
Skill registry design is manual and domain-specific; poor descriptions degrade routing accuracy
Code-specialized ~80B models are needed to match closed-source baselines, still requiring substantial on-prem GPU hardware

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: Decompose recommendation pipelines into skills (candidate retrieval, feature lookup, re-ranking, explanation generation) so a local SLM orchestrates without needing to internalize business logic
LLM evaluation harnesses: Wrap eval metrics as skills so a moderately-sized model can self-evaluate outputs against typed schemas rather than free-form judgment
Data pipeline orchestration: Replace fragile prompt-based ETL with skill-routed steps (parse, validate, transform, load) giving deterministic fallback paths when the SLM misroutes

literature/2026-02-19-Balancing-Faithful.title: Balancing Faithfulness and Performance in Reasoning via Multi-Listener Soft Execution
literature/2026-02-19-Balancing-Faithful.content:
Balancing Faithfulness and Performance in Reasoning via Multi-Listener Soft Execution
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #3 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RL, LLM
Abstract
Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning sometimes fails to faithfully reflect the true computation of a large language model (LLM), hampering its utility in explaining how LLMs arrive at their answers. Moreover, optimizing for faithfulness and interpretability in reasoning often degrades task performance. To address this tradeoff and improve CoT faithfulness, we propose Reasoning Execution by Multiple Listeners (REMUL), a multi-party reinforcement learning approach. REMUL builds on the hypothesis that reasoning traces which other parties can follow will be more faithful. A speaker model generates a reasoning trace, which is truncated and passed to a pool of listener models who “execute” the trace, continuing the trace to an answer. Speakers are rewarded for producing reasoning that is clear to listeners, with additional correctness regularization via masked supervised finetuning to counter the tradeoff between faithfulness and performance. On multiple reasoning benchmarks (BIG-Bench Extra Hard, MuSR, ZebraLogicBench, and FOLIO), REMUL consistently and substantially improves three measures of faithfulness — hint attribution, early answering area over the curve (AOC), and mistake injection AOC — while also improving accuracy. Our analysis finds that these gains are robust across training domains, translate to legibility gains, and are associated with shorter and more direct CoTs.
Key Techniques

Multi-party reinforcement learning
Speaker-listener architecture for reasoning trace generation and execution
Masked supervised finetuning for correctness regularization
Multiple faithfulness metrics (hint attribution
AOC

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityThe Potential of CoT for Reasoning: A Closer Look at Trace Dynamics0.23End-to-End Joint ASR and Speaker Role Diarization with Child-Adult Interactions0.18Generative Reasoning Re-ranker0.18Generative Reasoning Re-ranker0.17ClueTracer: Question-to-Vision Clue Tracing for Training-Free Hallucination Suppression in Multimodal Reasoning0.17
Reasoning Execution by Multiple Listeners (REMUL)
Core Idea: A multi-party reinforcement learning framework where a speaker LLM is trained to produce chain-of-thought traces that listener LLMs can independently follow and execute to the correct answer, incentivizing faithful reasoning through communicative pressure.
Problem It Solves: Chain-of-thought reasoning is often unfaithful — the visible reasoning trace does not reflect the model’s true computation — and prior approaches to improving faithfulness typically degrade task accuracy, creating a tradeoff that REMUL breaks by using communicative legibility as a proxy for faithfulness.
How It Works:

Speaker generates trace: A speaker model produces a reasoning trace for a given problem, which is truncated at an intermediate point.
Listeners execute trace: A pool of listener models receives the truncated trace (without the original problem) and independently continues it to a final answer.
Reward signal: The speaker is rewarded when listeners arrive at the correct answer, incentivizing traces that are self-contained and executable by others.
Correctness regularization: Masked supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on correct completions is applied to the speaker to counteract any faithfulness-vs-accuracy tradeoff introduced by listener optimization alone.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    P[Problem] --> S[Speaker Model]
    S -->|Full reasoning trace| T[Truncate]
    T -->|Partial trace| L1[Listener 1]
    T -->|Partial trace| L2[Listener 2]
    T -->|Partial trace| LN[Listener N]
    L1 & L2 & LN -->|Answers| R[Reward: correct answers]
    R -->|RL gradient| S
    GT[Ground Truth] -->|Masked SFT| S

Where It’s Useful:

Reasoning tasks requiring interpretable, auditable chain-of-thought (logic puzzles, math, multi-step inference)
Settings where faithfulness matters for trust or debugging (e.g., medical reasoning, legal analysis)
Benchmarks: BIG-Bench Extra Hard, MuSR, ZebraLogicBench, FOLIO

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires maintaining a pool of listener models during training, increasing compute cost relative to standard RL fine-tuning.
Faithfulness gains are measured via proxy metrics (hint attribution, early answering AOC, mistake injection AOC) — not direct mechanistic verification.
Speaker and listener models may converge on a shared “dialect” that is legible to each other but not to humans, limiting true interpretability gains.

Internal Use Cases:

LLM reasoning pipelines: Apply REMUL-style training to reasoning models used in complex query understanding or multi-hop retrieval, improving trace legibility for debugging.
Ranking/RecSys explanation: If using LLMs to generate explanations for recommendations, REMUL’s communicative pressure could produce explanations that are more faithfully grounded in the model’s actual scoring logic.
Agent evaluation: Use the listener-execution paradigm as an offline faithfulness probe — truncate agent traces and measure how well a separate model can reproduce the agent’s conclusions.

literature/2026-02-19-Beyond-Learning-A-.title: Beyond Learning: A Training-Free Alternative to Model Adaptation
literature/2026-02-19-Beyond-Learning-A-.content:
Beyond Learning: A Training-Free Alternative to Model Adaptation
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #2 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, LLM
Abstract
Despite the continuous research and evolution of language models, they sometimes underperform previous versions. Existing approaches to overcome these challenges are resource-intensive, highlighting the need for alternatives that enable immediate action. We assume that each language model has a local module inside that is suitable for a specific function. First, this work identifies a set of modules showing consistent and local activation changes under an inference workload through activation-based analysis. Subsequently, we transplant an internal module that is properly activated for a specific task into the target model, leading to immediate and measurable functional changes without additional training or fine-tuning. To experimentally demonstrate the effectiveness of the transplant technique, we quantify the relationship between transplant strength and performance improvement under different conditions for two language models. In the cross-generation setting, we find that transplanting activation-selected modules can substantially improve the underperforming model, reaching up to twice the target baseline and achieving gap-based recovery above 100%. Moreover, in transplant experiments between a base model and its instruction-tuned counterpart, transplantation improves the underperforming model toward the stronger baseline, yielding up to about 2.33 times the target baseline with gap-based recovery reaching up to 100% in the best case. These results show that meaningful capacity transfer can be realized through the implantation of highly localized modules implied by language models. Overall, this work provides empirical evidence for task-localized modularity in language models and presents a new research area: model transplantation.
Key Techniques

Activation-based module identification
Internal module transplantation
Cross-generation transplantation
Transplant strength quantification

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityPrefillShare: A Shared Prefill Module for KV Reuse in Multi-LLM Disaggregated Serving0.14S2D: Selective Spectral Decay for Quantization-Friendly Conditioning of Neural Activations0.11ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.10LRAgent: Efficient KV Cache Sharing for Multi-LoRA LLM Agents0.10ConceptMoE: Adaptive Token-to-Concept Compression for Implicit Compute Allocation0.10
Model Transplantation
Core Idea: Selectively identify task-localized modules in a donor language model via activation analysis, then directly implant those modules into a target (underperforming) model to transfer functional capacity without any training.
Problem It Solves: Model adaptation methods (fine-tuning, LoRA, RLHF) are resource-intensive and require time before deployment; newer model versions sometimes regress on specific tasks compared to predecessors, with no lightweight remedy.
How It Works:

Activation Profiling: Run inference workloads on both donor and target models; record per-module activation patterns to identify modules exhibiting consistent, localized activation changes correlated with the target task.
Module Selection: Rank modules by activation-based criteria to select the minimal set that is reliably “lit up” for the specific function in the donor model.
Transplantation: Directly copy selected donor modules (weight tensors) into the corresponding positions in the target model at a tunable transplant strength (interpolation coefficient between donor and target weights), then evaluate immediately—no gradient updates required.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Donor Model] --> B[Activation Profiler]
    B --> C[Module Selector\ntask-localized]
    C --> D[Transplant Op\nstrength α]
    E[Target Model] --> D
    D --> F[Augmented Target\nno retraining]

Where It’s Useful:

Rapid recovery when a newer model version regresses on a specific benchmark vs. a prior version (cross-generation setting)
Transferring instruction-following capability from an instruction-tuned model into its base counterpart without fine-tuning
Low-resource or latency-constrained deployments where retraining is infeasible

Limitations / Caveats:

Assumes structural compatibility (matching architecture) between donor and target; cross-architecture transplants are not demonstrated
Transplant strength is a scalar hyperparameter requiring empirical tuning per task—wrong values can degrade performance
Activation-based module selection may not generalize cleanly to tasks with diffuse, non-localized activation patterns

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys LLMs: If a production ranking model regresses after a version update, transplant task-relevant modules from the prior version to recover performance instantly without redeployment cycles
MultiLoRA integration: Rather than training new LoRA adapters for each task, use transplanted modules as warm initialization targets to reduce adapter training cost
Instruction-following in base models: Transplant instruction-tuning modules into a base model serving as a retrieval or reranking backbone to improve prompt adherence without full RLHF

literature/2026-02-19-CLAA-Cross-Layer-A.title: CLAA: Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation for Accelerating LLM Prefill
literature/2026-02-19-CLAA-Cross-Layer-A.content:
CLAA: Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation for Accelerating LLM Prefill
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #3 (score: 0.92, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The prefill stage in long-context LLM inference remains a computational bottleneck. Recent token-ranking heuristics accelerate inference by selectively processing a subset of semantically relevant tokens. However, existing methods suffer from unstable token importance estimation, often varying between layers. Evaluating token-ranking quality independently from heuristic-specific architectures is challenging. To address this, we introduce an Answer-Informed Oracle, which defines ground-truth token importance by measuring attention from generated answers back to the prompt. This oracle reveals that existing heuristics exhibit high variance across layers: rankings can degrade sharply at specific layers, a failure mode invisible to end-to-end benchmarks. The diagnosis suggests a simple fix: aggregate scores across layers rather than relying on any single one. We implement this as Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation (CLAA), which closes the gap to the oracle upper bound and reduces Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) by up to 39% compared to the Full KV Cache baseline.
Key Techniques

Answer-Informed Oracle
Token-ranking heuristics
Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation (CLAA)
Layer-wise variance diagnosis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityCLAA: Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation for Accelerating LLM Prefill0.89HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.38HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.34FOCUS: DLLMs Know How to Tame Their Compute Bound0.24FASA: Frequency-aware Sparse Attention0.22
Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation (CLAA)
Core Idea: Aggregate token importance scores across multiple transformer layers rather than relying on any single layer’s attention signal, closing the gap to an oracle upper bound for selective token processing during LLM prefill.
Problem It Solves: Existing token-ranking heuristics for accelerating LLM prefill suffer from high variance in importance estimates across layers — rankings can degrade sharply at specific layers, causing tokens to be incorrectly pruned. This failure mode is invisible to end-to-end benchmarks because aggregate metrics mask per-layer degradation.
How It Works:

Oracle Definition: Construct an Answer-Informed Oracle by running full inference, then measuring attention weights from generated answer tokens back to prompt tokens — this defines ground-truth token importance.
Diagnosis: Use the oracle to reveal that existing heuristics (e.g., quest, InfLLM, SnapKV) have unstable rankings at specific layers, not just noisy estimates overall.
Cross-Layer Aggregation: During prefill, compute attention-based token importance scores at each layer and accumulate them across layers (e.g., via summation or weighted averaging) before making token selection decisions.
Selective Processing: Use the aggregated scores to retain only the top-K most important tokens for subsequent layers, reducing compute while preserving accuracy.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    P[Prompt Tokens] --> L1[Layer 1 Attention]
    L1 -->|importance scores| AGG[Cross-Layer Aggregator]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2 Attention]
    L2 -->|importance scores| AGG
    L2 --> LN[Layer N Attention]
    LN -->|importance scores| AGG
    AGG -->|top-K token mask| SEL[Token Selector]
    SEL --> GEN[Generation / Decode]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context LLM inference where prompt length dominates TTFT (retrieval-augmented generation, document QA, summarization)
Latency-sensitive serving where Time-to-First-Token is the key SLO metric
Any system where KV cache memory is a bottleneck and selective attention is already in use

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires accumulating scores across layers, adding memory overhead proportional to number of layers tracked
Oracle is answer-informed and thus not usable at inference time — it motivates the design but cannot be directly deployed
Evaluated primarily on prefill; decode-phase acceleration is a separate problem
Token selection is still a heuristic; aggregation reduces but does not eliminate variance from the oracle

Internal Use Cases:

LLM-based ranking/reranking: If using an LLM to score candidates over a long context (e.g., a large item pool), CLAA-style aggregation could identify which candidate tokens are actually attended to, pruning irrelevant items before expensive decode
RAG pipelines: Use cross-layer aggregated attention to filter retrieved chunks mid-prefill, reducing context length dynamically rather than at retrieval time
Inference cost monitoring: The Answer-Informed Oracle methodology is a useful evaluation tool — apply it offline to audit whether existing token-pruning heuristics degrade at specific layers in your stack

literature/2026-02-19-COMPOT-Calibration.title: COMPOT: Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers Compression
literature/2026-02-19-COMPOT-Calibration.content:
COMPOT: Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers Compression
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-16
Rank: #11 (score: 0.25, max_sim: 0.77)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Post-training compression of Transformer models commonly relies on truncated singular value decomposition (SVD). However, enforcing a single shared subspace can degrade accuracy even at moderate compression. Sparse dictionary learning provides a more flexible union-of-subspaces representation, but existing approaches often suffer from iterative dictionary and coefficient updates. We propose COMPOT (Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers), a training-free compression framework that uses a small calibration dataset to estimate a sparse weight factorization. COMPOT employs orthogonal dictionaries that enable closed-form Procrustes updates for the dictionary and analytical single-step sparse coding for the coefficients, eliminating iterative optimization. To handle heterogeneous layer sensitivity under a global compression budget, COMPOT further introduces a one-shot dynamic allocation strategy that adaptively redistributes layer-wise compression rates. Extensive experiments across diverse architectures and tasks show that COMPOT consistently delivers a superior quality-compression trade-off over strong low-rank and sparse baselines, while remaining fully compatible with post-training quantization for extreme compression. Code is available github.com/mts-ai/COMPOT{here}.
Key Techniques

Sparse dictionary learning with orthogonal dictionaries
Procrustes optimization for dictionary updates
analytical single-step sparse coding
dynamic layer-wise compression rate allocation

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityCOMPOT: Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers Compression0.89Don’t be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold0.17ATACompressor: Adaptive Task-Aware Compression for Efficient Long-Context Processing in LLMs0.15Don’t be so Stief! Learning KV Cache low-rank approximation over the Stiefel manifold0.15SONIC: Segmented Optimized Nexus for Information Compression in Key-Value Caching0.15
COMPOT (Calibration-Optimized Matrix Procrustes Orthogonalization for Transformers)
Core Idea: A training-free Transformer compression framework that factorizes weight matrices into sparse combinations of orthogonal dictionary atoms, using closed-form Procrustes updates and analytical sparse coding to eliminate iterative optimization.
Problem It Solves: Standard SVD-based low-rank compression forces a single shared subspace, which degrades accuracy at moderate compression rates. Sparse dictionary learning offers richer union-of-subspaces representations but traditionally requires slow alternating optimization between dictionary and coefficient updates.
How It Works:

Calibration-guided estimation: Pass a small calibration dataset through the model to collect layer-wise activation statistics, which inform how each weight matrix should be factorized.
Orthogonal dictionary construction: Decompose each weight matrix W ≈ D · A where D is an orthogonal dictionary (columns are orthonormal atoms); orthogonality enables a closed-form Procrustes solution for D — a single SVD step rather than iterative gradient updates.
Analytical sparse coding: Given the fixed orthogonal D, compute sparse coefficients A in a single step via orthogonal matching pursuit or a closed-form thresholding operation (orthogonality decouples atom contributions, making coefficient estimation non-iterative).
One-shot dynamic budget allocation: Estimate per-layer sensitivity (e.g., via reconstruction error or activation magnitude) and redistribute the global sparsity/rank budget across layers in one pass, assigning higher fidelity to sensitive layers without iterative search.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    W[Weight Matrix W] --> CAL[Calibration\nActivations]
    CAL --> SENS[Layer Sensitivity\nEstimation]
    SENS --> ALLOC[Dynamic Budget\nAllocation]
    ALLOC --> PROC[Procrustes Update\nfor Dictionary D\nclosed-form SVD]
    PROC --> SC[Sparse Coding\nfor Coefficients A\nanalytical step]
    SC --> OUT[Compressed W ≈ D·A]
    OUT --> PTQ[Optional: Post-training\nQuantization]

Where It’s Useful:

Compressing large Transformer LLMs for inference on memory-constrained hardware
Scenarios requiring extreme compression via stacking with post-training quantization (PTQ)
Heterogeneous architectures where layer sensitivity varies significantly (vision + language models)

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires a calibration dataset; quality of compression depends on how representative it is of the target distribution
Orthogonal dictionary assumption may not be optimal for all weight geometries — non-orthogonal dictionaries can be more expressive
One-shot allocation heuristic may not match the globally optimal per-layer budget found by exhaustive search

Internal Use Cases:

Compressing retrieval or ranking LLMs (e.g., cross-encoders) for low-latency inference without fine-tuning on proprietary data
Reducing KV-cache footprint in attention layers via sparse factorization of projection matrices
Stacking with quantization for extreme model compression in embedding models used in two-tower RecSys architectures

literature/2026-02-19-Fast-KV-Compaction.title: Fast KV Compaction via Attention Matching
literature/2026-02-19-Fast-KV-Compaction.content:
Fast KV Compaction via Attention Matching
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #2 (score: 0.95, max_sim: 0.85)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Scaling language models to long contexts is often bottlenecked by the size of the key-value (KV) cache. In deployed settings, long contexts are typically managed through compaction in token space via summarization. However, summarization can be highly lossy, substantially harming downstream performance. Recent work on Cartridges has shown that it is possible to train highly compact KV caches in latent space that closely match full-context performance, but at the cost of slow and expensive end-to-end optimization. This work describes an approach for fast context compaction in latent space through Attention Matching, which constructs compact keys and values to reproduce attention outputs and preserve attention mass at a per-KV-head level. We show that this formulation naturally decomposes into simple subproblems, some of which admit efficient closed-form solutions. Within this framework, we develop a family of methods that significantly push the Pareto frontier of compaction time versus quality, achieving up to 50x compaction in seconds on some datasets with little quality loss.
Key Techniques

Attention Matching
KV cache compaction
Latent space compaction
Closed-form solutions

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityHySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.31FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion0.27HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.27ParisKV: Fast and Drift-Robust KV-Cache Retrieval for Long-Context LLMs0.26WildCat: Near-Linear Attention in Theory and Practice0.25
Attention Matching (AM)
Core Idea: Constructs compact KV cache entries in latent space by solving for keys and values that reproduce the attention outputs of the full context, decomposed into efficient per-head subproblems with closed-form solutions.
Problem It Solves: Existing KV cache compaction via token-space summarization is lossy; latent-space alternatives (e.g., Cartridges) achieve high quality but require slow end-to-end optimization—Attention Matching closes this gap by enabling fast, high-quality latent-space compaction.
How It Works:

Target construction: For each attention head, compute the full-context attention outputs and attention mass distribution as the reconstruction targets.
Decomposition: Reformulate the compaction objective into separable subproblems—one for matching attention mass (over keys) and one for matching weighted value aggregation (over values).
Closed-form solve: The value-matching subproblem admits a closed-form least-squares solution per head; the key subproblem is solved efficiently via constrained optimization, yielding a compact KV cache in seconds rather than minutes/hours.
Inference: The resulting compact KV cache (e.g., 50x fewer tokens) is used as a drop-in replacement for the full KV cache during decoding.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Full Context] --> B[Run Prefill\nGet Full KV Cache]
    B --> C[Compute Attention\nOutputs + Mass per Head]
    C --> D[Decompose into\nKey / Value Subproblems]
    D --> E[Closed-Form /\nEfficient Solve]
    E --> F[Compact KV Cache\ne.g. 50x smaller]
    F --> G[Decoder\nInference]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context inference serving where KV cache memory is the primary bottleneck
RAG pipelines that repeatedly load large document contexts per query
Streaming or real-time applications requiring fast context compression without fine-tuning

Limitations / Caveats:

Compaction quality may degrade on tasks highly sensitive to fine-grained token-level detail not captured by attention output matching
Assumes the full prefill pass is still run once; saves memory/latency for decoding, not prefill
Per-head decomposition may not fully account for cross-head interactions in multi-head attention

Internal Use Cases:

Long-context LLM serving: Compress user session history or product catalog contexts into compact KV caches, enabling larger effective batch sizes
RAG over large corpora: Pre-compact document KV caches offline and serve them at query time, trading a one-time compaction cost for fast retrieval-augmented decoding
Ranking/RecSys with LLM rerankers: Compress lengthy item description contexts to reduce per-request memory pressure in LLM-based reranking pipelines

literature/2026-02-19-LGQ-Learning-Discr.title: LGQ: Learning Discretization Geometry for Scalable and Stable Image Tokenization
literature/2026-02-19-LGQ-Learning-Discr.content:
LGQ: Learning Discretization Geometry for Scalable and Stable Image Tokenization
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #10 (score: 0.72, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Discrete image tokenization is a key bottleneck for scalable visual generation: a tokenizer must remain compact for efficient latent-space priors while preserving semantic structure and using discrete capacity effectively. Existing quantizers face a trade-off: vector-quantized tokenizers learn flexible geometries but often suffer from biased straight-through optimization, codebook under-utilization, and representation collapse at large vocabularies. Structured scalar or implicit tokenizers ensure stable, near-complete utilization by design, yet rely on fixed discretization geometries that may allocate capacity inefficiently under heterogeneous latent statistics.   We introduce Learnable Geometric Quantization (LGQ), a discrete image tokenizer that learns discretization geometry end-to-end. LGQ replaces hard nearest-neighbor lookup with temperature-controlled soft assignments, enabling fully differentiable training while recovering hard assignments at inference. The assignments correspond to posterior responsibilities of an isotropic Gaussian mixture and minimize a variational free-energy objective, provably converging to nearest-neighbor quantization in the low-temperature limit. LGQ combines a token-level peakedness regularizer with a global usage regularizer to encourage confident yet balanced code utilization without imposing rigid grids.   Under a controlled VQGAN-style backbone on ImageNet across multiple vocabulary sizes, LGQ achieves stable optimization and balanced utilization. At 16K codebook size, LGQ improves rFID by 11.88% over FSQ while using 49.96% fewer active codes, and improves rFID by 6.06% over SimVQ with 49.45% lower effective representation rate, achieving comparable fidelity with substantially fewer active entries. Our GitHub repository is available at: github.com/KurbanIntelligenceLab/LGQ
Key Techniques

Learnable Geometric Quantization
Temperature-controlled soft assignments
Variational free-energy objective
Token-level and global usage regularizers

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityRQ-GMM: Residual Quantized Gaussian Mixture Model for Multimodal Semantic Discretization in CTR Prediction0.23Price of universality in vector quantization is at most 0.11 bit0.22ProtoQuant: Quantization of Prototypical Parts For General and Fine-Grained Image Classification0.22Differentiable Semantic ID for Generative Recommendation0.21UniWeTok: An Unified Binary Tokenizer with Codebook Size 2^{128} for Unified Multimodal Large Language Model0.20
Learnable Geometric Quantization (LGQ)
Core Idea: Replace hard nearest-neighbor codebook lookup with temperature-annealed soft assignments derived from an isotropic Gaussian mixture posterior, making the entire quantization geometry learnable end-to-end.
Problem It Solves: Existing vector-quantized tokenizers suffer from biased straight-through gradients, codebook under-utilization, and representation collapse at large vocabulary sizes; structured alternatives (FSQ, SimVQ) avoid these instabilities but fix the discretization geometry, wasting capacity when latent statistics are heterogeneous.
How It Works:

Soft assignment: For each encoder output z, compute posterior responsibilities over K codebook entries as r_k \propto \exp(-\|z - e_k\|^2 / \tau); the quantized vector is the responsibility-weighted sum of codewords — fully differentiable, no straight-through estimator needed.
Temperature annealing: \tau is decayed during training so soft assignments sharpen toward a one-hot; at inference \tau \to 0 recovers exact nearest-neighbor lookup, matching standard VQ behavior.
Dual regularization: A peakedness (token-level) regularizer maximizes assignment entropy sharpness per token to encourage confident codes; a global usage regularizer minimizes the KL divergence of the empirical code frequency from uniform to prevent codebook collapse.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Image] --> B[CNN Encoder]
    B --> C[Latent z]
    C --> D["Soft Assignment\n(Gaussian Mixture Posterior, temp τ)"]
    D --> E["Codebook E\n(learnable geometry)"]
    E --> F["Quantized z_q\n(hard at inference)"]
    F --> G[CNN Decoder]
    G --> H[Reconstructed Image]
    D --> I[Peakedness Reg.]
    D --> J[Usage Reg.]

Where It’s Useful:

Discrete image tokenization for autoregressive or masked visual generation (VQGAN-style backbones)
Any domain requiring a learned vector codebook where codebook collapse or underutilization is a concern (video, audio, 3D point clouds)

Limitations / Caveats:

Temperature schedule is an additional hyperparameter; aggressive annealing can reintroduce gradient bias near the hard-assignment limit
Soft assignments during training increase memory/compute relative to straight-through VQ (storing K responsibilities per token)
Evaluated under VQGAN backbone on ImageNet only; gains on non-image modalities or alternative generator architectures are unverified

Internal Use Cases:

Discrete bottleneck for user-behavior sequence tokenization in RecSys: learn item-cluster geometry rather than fixing a grid, potentially improving cluster purity for downstream ranking
Replacing fixed-vocab discrete autoencoders in LLM multimodal pipelines where codebook collapse is observed at >4K vocabulary sizes

literature/2026-02-19-MoE-Spec-Expert-Bu.title: MoE-Spec: Expert Budgeting for Efficient Speculative Decoding
literature/2026-02-19-MoE-Spec-Expert-Bu.content:
MoE-Spec: Expert Budgeting for Efficient Speculative Decoding
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #4 (score: 0.91, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Speculative decoding accelerates Large Language Model (LLM) inference by verifying multiple drafted tokens in parallel. However, for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, this parallelism introduces a severe bottleneck: large draft trees activate many unique experts, significantly increasing memory pressure and diminishing speedups from speculative decoding relative to autoregressive decoding. Prior methods reduce speculation depth when MoE verification becomes expensive. We propose MoE-Spec, a training-free verification-time expert budgeting method that decouples speculation depth from memory cost by enforcing a fixed expert capacity limit at each layer, loading only the experts that contribute most to verification and dropping the long tail of rarely used experts that drive bandwidth overhead. Experiments across multiple model scales and datasets show that this method yields 10—30% higher throughput than state-of-the-art speculative decoding baselines (EAGLE-3) at comparable quality, with flexibility to trade accuracy for further latency reductions through tighter budgets.
Key Techniques

Speculative decoding
expert capacity limiting
expert contribution-based prioritization
verification-time budgeting

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySpeculative Decoding with a Speculative Vocabulary0.34SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration0.32DART: Diffusion-Inspired Speculative Decoding for Fast LLM Inference0.32DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.31PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models0.29
MoE-Spec: Expert Budgeting for Speculative Decoding
Core Idea: At verification time, enforce a fixed expert capacity limit per MoE layer, loading only the highest-contribution experts and dropping the low-utilization tail, decoupling speculation depth from memory bandwidth cost.
Problem It Solves: Speculative decoding’s draft trees activate many unique experts across parallel token candidates in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, causing memory bandwidth to scale with tree size rather than generation length — bottlenecking throughput and erasing speculative decoding’s speedup over autoregressive decoding.
How It Works:

Score experts by contribution: For each MoE layer during draft-tree verification, compute aggregate routing weights across all active tokens in the tree to rank experts by utilization.
Apply capacity budget: Retain only the top-K experts (by aggregated routing weight) up to a fixed budget; drop remaining experts whose combined activation is in the long tail of bandwidth cost.
Verify with reduced expert set: Run the forward pass using only loaded experts; tokens routed to dropped experts are handled via fallback (e.g., top-1 among loaded experts), preserving output quality for high-probability paths while avoiding memory loads for rarely-used experts.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    DT[Draft Tree\nTokens] --> R[Router\nper Layer]
    R --> S[Aggregate\nRouting Scores]
    S --> B[Expert Budget\nTop-K Selection]
    B --> E[Load Selected\nExperts Only]
    E --> V[Parallel\nVerification Pass]
    V --> OUT[Accepted Tokens]
    B -- dropped experts --> FB[Fallback\nRouting]
    FB --> V

Where It’s Useful:

Inference serving of MoE LLMs (Mixtral, DeepSeek-MoE, Grok) where memory bandwidth is the binding constraint
High-throughput batch serving where multiple draft trees amplify expert fan-out
Latency-sensitive applications using EAGLE-3 or similar tree-based speculative drafters on MoE targets

Limitations / Caveats:

Accuracy degrades as budget tightens; tokens routed to dropped experts receive approximate logits, increasing the risk of speculative rejection for low-probability paths
Budget tuning is dataset/model-sensitive: optimal K varies with routing entropy of the workload
Training-free, so cannot redistribute expert capacity learned at train time; a co-trained variant could close the quality gap at tight budgets

Internal Use Cases:

Accelerating MoE-based re-rankers or generative retrievers at inference time without model changes
Reducing serving cost for LLM-generated feature extraction pipelines that use MoE models, by applying expert budgeting during speculative prefill
Applicable to Batch Verification in Speculative Decoding workflows: when verifying multiple draft sequences in one forward pass, expert fan-out is the same bottleneck MoE-Spec targets — budgeting can be layered on top of batch verification directly

literature/2026-02-19-Quecto-V1-Empirica.title: Quecto-V1: Empirical Analysis of 8-bit Quantized Small Language Models for On-Device Legal Retrieval
literature/2026-02-19-Quecto-V1-Empirica.content:
Quecto-V1: Empirical Analysis of 8-bit Quantized Small Language Models for On-Device Legal Retrieval
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #7 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RAG, LLM
Abstract
The rapid proliferation of Large Language Models (LLMs) has revolutionized Natural Language Processing (NLP) but has simultaneously created a “resource divide.” State-of-the-art legal intelligence systems typically rely on massive parameter counts (7B+) and cloud-based inference, rendering them inaccessible to practitioners in resource-constrained environments and posing significant data sovereignty risks. This paper introduces Quecto-V1, a domain-specific Small Language Model (SLM) engineered to democratize access to Indian legal intelligence. Built upon a custom configuration of the GPT-2 architecture (124 million parameters), Quecto-V1 was trained from scratch exclusively on a corpus of Indian statutes, including the Indian Penal Code (IPC), the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC), and the Constitution of India. Unlike generalist models, which prioritize broad world knowledge, our approach maximizes “lexical density” within the legal domain. Furthermore, we address the deployment bottleneck by applying post-training 8-bit quantization (GGUF format), compressing the model to a memory footprint of under 150 MB. Our empirical analysis demonstrates that Quecto-V1 achieves high fidelity in retrieving statutory definitions and penal provisions, outperforming general-purpose SLMs in domain-specific exact match tasks while running entirely offline on consumer-grade CPUs. We further present an ablation study showing that 8-bit quantization yields a 74% reduction in model size with less than 3.5% degradation in retrieval accuracy compared to full-precision baselines. These findings suggest that for specialized, high-stakes domains like law, domain-specific training coupled with aggressive quantization offers a viable, privacy-preserving alternative to monolithic cloud models.
Key Techniques

Domain-specific training on Indian legal corpus
custom GPT-2 architecture
8-bit quantization (GGUF format)
lexical density maximization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityQuecto-V1: Empirical Analysis of 8-bit Quantized Small Language Models for On-Device Legal Retrieval0.85Domain-Adaptive and Scalable Dense Retrieval for Content-Based Recommendation0.17BPDQ: Bit-Plane Decomposition Quantization on a Variable Grid for Large Language Models0.151-Bit Wonder: Improving QAT Performance in the Low-Bit Regime through K-Means Quantization0.15Reshape and Adapt for Output Quantization (RAOQ): Quantization-aware Training for In-memory Computing Systems0.14
Domain-Specific SLM Quantization Pipeline (Quecto-V1)
Core Idea: Train a compact GPT-2-scale language model from scratch on a narrow legal corpus, then apply post-training 8-bit quantization (GGUF format) to produce a sub-150 MB model capable of offline statutory retrieval on consumer CPUs.
Problem It Solves: State-of-the-art legal NLP systems require 7B+ parameter cloud-hosted models, creating a resource divide that excludes practitioners in constrained environments and introduces data sovereignty risks when sensitive legal queries must leave the device.
How It Works:

Corpus construction: Curate a high-lexical-density domain corpus (Indian Penal Code, Code of Criminal Procedure, Constitution of India) — no general web text, maximizing token budget on statutory language.
From-scratch pretraining: Train a 124M-parameter GPT-2 architecture on this corpus; no general-purpose pretraining or fine-tuning — the full parameter budget encodes only legal domain knowledge.
Post-training 8-bit quantization: Convert the full-precision checkpoint to GGUF format with 8-bit integer quantization, reducing model size by ~74% with <3.5% degradation on domain-specific exact-match retrieval tasks.
On-device inference: Deploy the quantized model (<150 MB) for offline CPU inference, serving statutory definition and penal provision retrieval without network dependency.

Architecture (if applicable):
flowchart LR
    A[Indian Legal Corpus\nIPC / CrPC / Constitution] --> B[GPT-2 124M\nFrom-Scratch Training]
    B --> C[Full-Precision Checkpoint\nFP32]
    C --> D[Post-Training Quantization\n8-bit GGUF]
    D --> E[Offline CPU Inference\n<150 MB]
    E --> F[Statutory Retrieval\nExact Match Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Privacy-sensitive legal query answering where queries cannot leave the device
Low-resource legal aid tools in jurisdictions with limited cloud infrastructure
Edge deployment of domain-specific QA in regulated industries (healthcare, finance) with similar sovereignty constraints

Limitations / Caveats:

Extremely narrow generalization: model has no world knowledge outside the training statutes, so cross-domain or common-law queries will degrade sharply
GPT-2 architecture lacks modern advances (RoPE, GQA, SwiGLU) — retrieval fidelity ceiling is lower than equivalently-sized modern SLMs
Exact-match evaluation metric may overstate practical utility; semantic correctness and hallucination are not measured
124M parameters is insufficient for multi-hop reasoning across long statutory documents

Internal Use Cases:

Retrieval pipeline compression: The quantization-first deployment pattern applies directly to on-device dense retrievers — embed a domain-fine-tuned bi-encoder, quantize to INT8/GGUF, serve candidate retrieval without cloud round-trips
Domain lexical density as a training signal: The corpus curation philosophy (maximize in-domain token density, exclude general text) is transferable to vertical RecSys embedding models where general pretraining noise hurts niche-domain ranking
Ablation methodology: The size-vs-accuracy ablation (74% size reduction, <3.5% accuracy drop) is a reusable evaluation template for quantizing any retrieval or reranking model before production deployment

literature/2026-02-19-Rethinking-ANN-bas.title: Rethinking ANN-based Retrieval: Multifaceted Learnable Index for Large-scale Recommendation System
literature/2026-02-19-Rethinking-ANN-bas.content:
Rethinking ANN-based Retrieval: Multifaceted Learnable Index for Large-scale Recommendation System
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #8 (score: 0.91, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search is widely used in the retrieval stage of large-scale recommendation systems. In this stage, candidate items are indexed using their learned embedding vectors, and ANN search is executed for each user (or item) query to retrieve a set of relevant items. However, ANN-based retrieval has two key limitations. First, item embeddings and their indices are typically learned in separate stages: indexing is often performed offline after embeddings are trained, which can yield suboptimal retrieval quality-especially for newly created items. Second, although ANN offers sublinear query time, it must still be run for every request, incurring substantial computation cost at industry scale. In this paper, we propose MultiFaceted Learnable Index (MFLI), a scalable, real-time retrieval paradigm that learns multifaceted item embeddings and indices within a unified framework and eliminates ANN search at serving time. Specifically, we construct a multifaceted hierarchical codebook via residual quantization of item embeddings and co-train the codebook with the embeddings. We further introduce an efficient multifaceted indexing structure and mechanisms that support real-time updates. At serving time, the learned hierarchical indices are used directly to identify relevant items, avoiding ANN search altogether. Extensive experiments on real-world data with billions of users show that MFLI improves recall on engagement tasks by up to 11.8%, cold-content delivery by up to 57.29%, and semantic relevance by 13.5% compared with prior state-of-the-art methods. We also deploy MFLI in the system and report online experimental results demonstrating improved engagement, less popularity bias, and higher serving efficiency.
Key Techniques

Residual quantization
Multifaceted hierarchical codebook
Co-training embeddings with codebook
Real-time indexing structure

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityRethinking ANN-based Retrieval: Multifaceted Learnable Index for Large-scale Recommendation System0.91Domain-Adaptive and Scalable Dense Retrieval for Content-Based Recommendation0.23DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.22Price of universality in vector quantization is at most 0.11 bit0.22RQ-GMM: Residual Quantized Gaussian Mixture Model for Multimodal Semantic Discretization in CTR Prediction0.21
MultiFaceted Learnable Index (MFLI)
Core Idea: Co-train item embeddings and a hierarchical residual quantization codebook end-to-end, then serve retrieval at inference time by directly looking up precomputed index codes—eliminating ANN search entirely.
Problem It Solves: ANN-based retrieval has two compounding flaws: (1) embeddings and indices are trained separately, degrading quality especially for cold/new items; (2) ANN must run per-request at serving time, creating unavoidable per-query compute cost at billion-user scale.
How It Works:

Embedding + Codebook Co-training: Item embeddings are quantized via residual quantization (RQ) into a hierarchical codebook. Each item is assigned a multi-level code (e.g., coarse → fine residuals). The codebook and embeddings are trained jointly with the recommendation objective, not post-hoc.
Multifaceted Index Construction: Each quantization level forms one “facet” of the index—a mapping from code → list of items sharing that code. New items can update their codes in real time without full retraining.
ANN-free Serving: At query time, the user embedding is quantized using the same codebook to obtain a hierarchy of codes. Matching items are retrieved by direct index lookup at each facet level, then aggregated—no vector distance computation needed.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    IE[Item Embeddings] --> RQ[Residual Quantization\nCodebook]
    RQ --> HI[Hierarchical Index\nfacet_1...facet_k]
    UE[User Query Embedding] --> CQ[Codebook Quantization]
    CQ --> LU[Direct Index Lookup\nper facet]
    HI --> LU
    LU --> RC[Retrieved Candidates]

Where It’s Useful:

Large-scale industrial recommenders with strict serving latency budgets (billions of users, high QPS)
Platforms with high item churn or cold-start pressure (e.g., short-video, news feeds) where new content must be retrievable immediately
Systems where popularity bias from ANN score calibration is a known problem

Limitations / Caveats:

Quantization introduces lossy compression—recall ceiling is bounded by codebook capacity and depth
Co-training adds complexity; instability during joint optimization of codebook + embeddings is a known failure mode in RQ-based methods
Real-time index update mechanisms add infrastructure complexity not present in static ANN indices

Internal Use Cases:

Cold-content delivery: The 57.29% cold-content improvement directly maps to new-item cold-start in two-tower retrieval systems (see Hybrid Retrieval Architecture for Content Recommendations)
Latency reduction: Replaces the ANN stage entirely, addressing the re-ranking latency bottleneck described in the hybrid retrieval architecture note—budget can be reallocated to heavier re-rankers
Popularity bias mitigation: Index lookup decouples retrieval from raw embedding similarity norms, which tend to favor high-frequency items in standard ANN; relevant to any dense retrieval system exhibiting popularity skew

literature/2026-02-19-Retrieval-Collapse.title: Retrieval Collapses When AI Pollutes the Web
literature/2026-02-19-Retrieval-Collapse.content:
Retrieval Collapses When AI Pollutes the Web
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #5 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, RAG, LLM
Abstract
The rapid proliferation of AI-generated content on the Web presents a structural risk to information retrieval, as search engines and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems increasingly consume evidence produced by the Large Language Models (LLMs). We characterize this ecosystem-level failure mode as Retrieval Collapse, a two-stage process where (1) AI-generated content dominates search results, eroding source diversity, and (2) low-quality or adversarial content infiltrates the retrieval pipeline. We analyzed this dynamic through controlled experiments involving both high-quality SEO-style content and adversarially crafted content. In the SEO scenario, a 67% pool contamination led to over 80% exposure contamination, creating a homogenized yet deceptively healthy state where answer accuracy remains stable despite the reliance on synthetic sources. Conversely, under adversarial contamination, baselines like BM25 exposed \sim19% of harmful content, whereas LLM-based rankers demonstrated stronger suppression capabilities. These findings highlight the risk of retrieval pipelines quietly shifting toward synthetic evidence and the need for retrieval-aware strategies to prevent a self-reinforcing cycle of quality decline in Web-grounded systems.
Key Techniques

Controlled experiments
BM25 ranking
LLM-based rankers
Contamination analysis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityER-MIA: Black-Box Adversarial Memory Injection Attacks on Long-Term Memory-Augmented Large Language Models0.14ScrapeGraphAI-100k: A Large-Scale Dataset for LLM-Based Web Information Extraction0.12A Picture of Agentic Search0.10Real-Time Trend Prediction via Continually-Aligned LLM Query Generation0.10Rich-Media Re-Ranker: A User Satisfaction-Driven LLM Re-ranking Framework for Rich-Media Search0.10
Retrieval Collapse
Core Idea: A two-stage ecosystem-level failure mode where AI-generated content first dominates search results (eroding source diversity), then low-quality or adversarial content infiltrates the retrieval pipeline, creating a self-reinforcing degradation cycle.
Problem It Solves: Existing retrieval systems lack a framework for understanding how AI-generated web content systematically corrupts RAG pipelines — not just reducing quality, but masking that degradation behind stable accuracy metrics.
How It Works:

Stage 1 – Pool Contamination: AI-generated documents (SEO-optimized or adversarial) are injected into the retrieval corpus at controlled ratios; at 67% pool contamination, retrieved results show >80% exposure contamination
Stage 2 – Downstream Propagation: Contaminated retrievals feed into LLM-based readers/rankers; SEO-style contamination preserves answer accuracy while silently replacing human sources with synthetic ones; adversarial contamination degrades outputs, with BM25 exposing ~19% harmful content vs. stronger suppression from LLM-based rankers
Measurement: Source diversity metrics (distinct domain/author counts) and exposure contamination rate (fraction of retrieved docs that are AI-generated) track collapse independent of accuracy

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Web Corpus] --> B[AI Content Injection\nSEO or Adversarial]
    B --> C[Retrieval System\nBM25 / Dense / LLM Ranker]
    C --> D{Collapse Stage}
    D -->|Stage 1| E[Source Homogenization\nDiversity Loss]
    D -->|Stage 2| F[Quality/Harm\nInfiltration]
    E --> G[RAG Reader\nStable Accuracy, Synthetic Sources]
    F --> H[RAG Reader\nDegraded / Harmful Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Auditing RAG pipelines for silent synthetic drift in production search systems
Benchmarking retrieval robustness against AI-content flooding and SEO spam
Designing contamination-aware retrieval evaluations for web-grounded QA systems

Limitations / Caveats:

Controlled injection ratios may not reflect real-world AI content distribution dynamics
“Deceptively healthy” SEO scenario means accuracy-only evals will fail to detect collapse
Adversarial suppression by LLM rankers may itself be vulnerable to prompt-injection or model-targeted attacks

Internal Use Cases:

RAG system monitoring: Track source diversity and synthetic-origin rates as health metrics alongside accuracy
Ranking robustness: Use adversarial contamination experiments to stress-test LLM re-rankers before deployment
Data quality pipelines: Apply pool contamination thresholds to trigger human-review flags in corpus ingestion

literature/2026-02-19-Revolutionizing-Lo.title: Revolutionizing Long-Term Memory in AI: New Horizons with High-Capacity and High-Speed Storage
literature/2026-02-19-Revolutionizing-Lo.content:
Revolutionizing Long-Term Memory in AI: New Horizons with High-Capacity and High-Speed Storage
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #4 (score: 0.98, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, RAG
Abstract
Driven by our mission of “uplifting the world with memory,” this paper explores the design concept of “memory” that is essential for achieving artificial superintelligence (ASI). Rather than proposing novel methods, we focus on several alternative approaches whose potential benefits are widely imaginable, yet have remained largely unexplored. The currently dominant paradigm, which can be termed “extract then store,” involves extracting information judged to be useful from experiences and saving only the extracted content. However, this approach inherently risks the loss of information, as some valuable knowledge particularly for different tasks may be discarded in the extraction process. In contrast, we emphasize the “store then on-demand extract” approach, which seeks to retain raw experiences and flexibly apply them to various tasks as needed, thus avoiding such information loss. In addition, we highlight two further approaches: discovering deeper insights from large collections of probabilistic experiences, and improving experience collection efficiency by sharing stored experiences. While these approaches seem intuitively effective, our simple experiments demonstrate that this is indeed the case. Finally, we discuss major challenges that have limited investigation into these promising directions and propose research topics to address them.
Key Techniques

Extract then store
Store then on-demand extract
Discovering insights from probabilistic experiences
