Improving experience collection efficiency through sharing

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityMemFly: On-the-Fly Memory Optimization via Information Bottleneck0.15ER-MIA: Black-Box Adversarial Memory Injection Attacks on Long-Term Memory-Augmented Large Language Models0.12Incorporating Eye-Tracking Signals Into Multimodal Deep Visual Models For Predicting User Aesthetic Experience In Residential Interiors0.12MeepleLM: A Virtual Playtester Simulating Diverse Subjective Experiences0.12Beyond Retention: Orchestrating Structural Safety and Plasticity in Continual Learning for LLMs0.11
Store-Then-On-Demand-Extract (STODE)
Core Idea: Retain raw AI experiences in full fidelity rather than pre-filtering them, then retrieve and extract only the relevant portions at inference time based on the current task’s specific needs.
Problem It Solves: The dominant “extract-then-store” paradigm irreversibly discards information during upfront extraction, since what is deemed useful at storage time may be critical for a different downstream task — STODE eliminates this lossy compression bottleneck.
How It Works:

Raw storage: Experiences (observations, interactions, trajectories) are stored wholesale without any pre-filtering or summarization, preserving full informational content.
Query-time retrieval: When a task arises, a retrieval mechanism indexes the raw experience store using the current task context as a query signal.
On-demand extraction: Only at query time is relevant information extracted and distilled from retrieved raw experiences, allowing the extraction logic to be tailored to the specific task rather than a generic upfront heuristic.

Architecture (if applicable):
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Experience] --> B[High-Capacity Store\nno filtering]
    Q[Task Query] --> C[Retrieval Module]
    B --> C
    C --> D[On-Demand Extractor]
    D --> E[Task-Specific Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-horizon AI agents that must handle diverse future tasks from a shared experience buffer
Continual learning systems where task distributions shift and pre-extracted summaries become stale
Multi-task LLM pipelines where the same raw experience must serve heterogeneous downstream objectives
Memory-augmented retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems needing lossless episodic recall

Limitations / Caveats:

Requires substantially higher storage capacity than extract-then-store, since raw experiences are not compressed upfront
Retrieval latency increases as the raw store grows; efficient indexing over unstructured or high-dimensional experience data remains an open challenge
On-demand extraction must be fast enough for real-time use, imposing constraints on extractor complexity
Sharing raw experiences across agents (the paper’s “experience sharing” variant) introduces privacy and security risks

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: Store full user session logs rather than pre-aggregated behavioral features; extract task-relevant signals (click, dwell, skip) at ranking time per candidate context
LLM memory: Replace summarization-based conversation memory with raw turn storage, enabling downstream prompts to retrieve verbatim context rather than lossy summaries
Agent pipelines: Accumulate all tool-call traces unfiltered; let a retrieval layer surface the precise prior experience most relevant to the current agentic subtask

literature/2026-02-19-SLA2-Sparse-Linear.title: SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT
literature/2026-02-19-SLA2-Sparse-Linear.content:
SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT
Source: HuggingFace
Published: 2026-02-13
Rank: #9 (score: 0.89, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Sparse-Linear Attention (SLA) combines sparse and linear attention to accelerate diffusion models and has shown strong performance in video generation. However, (i) SLA relies on a heuristic split that assigns computations to the sparse or linear branch based on attention-weight magnitude, which can be suboptimal. Additionally, (ii) after formally analyzing the attention error in SLA, we identify a mismatch between SLA and a direct decomposition into sparse and linear attention. We propose SLA2, which introduces (I) a learnable router that dynamically selects whether each attention computation should use sparse or linear attention, (II) a more faithful and direct sparse-linear attention formulation that uses a learnable ratio to combine the sparse and linear attention branches, and (III) a sparse + low-bit attention design, where low-bit attention is introduced via quantization-aware fine-tuning to reduce quantization error. Experiments show that on video diffusion models, SLA2 can achieve 97% attention sparsity and deliver an 18.6x attention speedup while preserving generation quality.
Key Techniques

Learnable router for sparse/linear attention selection
Faithful sparse-linear attention formulation with learnable ratio
Sparse + low-bit attention with quantization-aware fine-tuning

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.93HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.40HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.39Superlinear Multi-Step Attention0.39FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion0.39
SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and Quantization-Aware Training
Core Idea: Replaces SLA’s heuristic attention-weight-magnitude split with a learnable router that dynamically routes each attention computation to either sparse or linear attention, combined with a learnable mixing ratio and quantization-aware fine-tuning for the linear branch.
Problem It Solves: SLA’s heuristic routing (based on attention-weight magnitude) is suboptimal and introduces a formulation mismatch between the combined output and a theoretically correct sparse+linear decomposition; additionally, the linear attention branch accumulates quantization error that degrades generation quality.
How It Works:

Learnable Router: For each attention computation, a trainable gating module takes Q/K representations as input and outputs a binary (or soft) routing decision — sparse attention or linear attention — replacing the fixed magnitude-based heuristic.
Faithful Decomposition: A learnable scalar ratio α combines sparse and linear attention outputs as α · SparseAttn + (1−α) · LinearAttn, directly parameterizing the branch weighting rather than inferring it from attention scores post-hoc.
Sparse + Low-Bit Attention via QAT: The linear attention branch is quantized to low-bit precision; quantization-aware fine-tuning (QAT) updates model weights to minimize the quantization error introduced in this branch, preserving end-to-end generation fidelity.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    QKV[Q, K, V] --> Router[Learnable Router\nGating Network]
    Router -->|sparse tokens| SA[Sparse Attention\nfull precision]
    Router -->|linear tokens| LA[Linear Attention\nlow-bit / QAT]
    SA --> Mix[Weighted Combine\nlearnable α]
    LA --> Mix
    Mix --> Out[Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Video diffusion model inference, where attention over long spatiotemporal token sequences is a primary bottleneck
Any transformer architecture where attention sparsity can be exploited but heuristic routing leaves performance on the table
Deployment scenarios requiring both speedup and quantization (edge or resource-constrained inference)

Limitations / Caveats:

Router adds training complexity and requires fine-tuning on top of a pretrained model; not a drop-in replacement at init
QAT requires an additional fine-tuning stage, increasing total training cost relative to post-training quantization approaches
Evaluated primarily on video diffusion; generalization to language models or other modalities is unverified

Internal Use Cases:

LLM serving: Learnable routing could replace fixed sliding-window or top-k heuristics in long-context LLM attention, adapting sparsity patterns per input rather than per architecture design
Ranking/retrieval transformers: In cross-attention between query and large candidate sets, a learned router could dynamically allocate full attention to high-signal candidates and linear attention to the long tail
Recommendation sequence models: For user history transformers with long sequences, SLA2-style routing could adaptively compress distant history tokens via linear attention while preserving recent/high-weight interactions with sparse attention

literature/2026-02-19-The-Diversity-Para.title: The Diversity Paradox revisited: Systemic Effects of Feedback Loops in Recommender Systems
literature/2026-02-19-The-Diversity-Para.content:
The Diversity Paradox revisited: Systemic Effects of Feedback Loops in Recommender Systems
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #6 (score: 0.97, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: RecSys, LLM
Abstract
Recommender systems shape individual choices through feedback loops in which user behavior and algorithmic recommendations coevolve over time. The systemic effects of these loops remain poorly understood, in part due to unrealistic assumptions in existing simulation studies. We propose a feedback-loop model that captures implicit feedback, periodic retraining, probabilistic adoption of recommendations, and heterogeneous recommender systems. We apply the framework on online retail and music streaming data and analyze systemic effects of the feedback loop. We find that increasing recommender adoption may lead to a progressive diversification of individual consumption, while collective demand is redistributed in model- and domain-dependent ways, often amplifying popularity concentration. Temporal analyses further reveal that apparent increases in individual diversity observed in static evaluations are illusory: when adoption is fixed and time unfolds, individual diversity consistently decreases across all models. Our results highlight the need to move beyond static evaluations and explicitly account for feedback-loop dynamics when designing recommender systems.
Key Techniques

Feedback-loop model
Implicit feedback
Periodic retraining
Temporal analysis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityR-Diverse: Mitigating Diversity Illusion in Self-Play LLM Training0.14A Task-Centric Perspective on Recommendation Systems0.13SWE-Master: Unleashing the Potential of Software Engineering Agents via Post-Training0.13ORCH: many analyses, one merge-a deterministic multi-agent orchestrator for discrete-choice reasoning with EMA-guided routing0.13SCASRec: A Self-Correcting and Auto-Stopping Model for Generative Route List Recommendation0.12
Feedback-Loop Simulation Framework (FLSF)
Core Idea: A closed-loop simulation framework that jointly models implicit user feedback, periodic recommender retraining, probabilistic recommendation adoption, and heterogeneous recommender systems to study systemic temporal effects on individual and collective consumption diversity.
Problem It Solves: Existing recommender system evaluations are static (snapshot-based), ignoring how user behavior and model parameters coevolve over time — leading to illusory diversity gains that vanish or reverse when temporal dynamics are properly modeled.
How It Works:

Initialization: Seed the loop with real interaction data (e.g., retail purchases, music streams); train an initial recommender model on observed implicit feedback
Recommendation & Adoption: At each timestep, generate a ranked recommendation list per user; each user probabilistically adopts items from the list (adoption rate α controls how much behavior is recommendation-driven vs. organic)
Feedback Collection: Adopted interactions are logged as new implicit feedback signals, augmenting the historical data
Periodic Retraining: The recommender model is retrained on the accumulated interaction log at fixed intervals, updating item scores and propagating any biases introduced in prior steps
Metric Tracking: After each cycle, compute individual diversity (intra-list diversity, personal catalog spread) and collective diversity (aggregate popularity distribution, Gini coefficient) to separate temporal trends from static cross-sectional snapshots

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Historical\nImplicit Feedback] --> B[Recommender\nModel Training]
    B --> C[Ranked Rec Lists\nper User]
    C --> D{Probabilistic\nAdoption\nRate α}
    D -->|adopted items| E[New Interaction\nLog]
    D -->|organic items| E
    E --> F[Periodic\nRetraining Trigger]
    F --> B
    E --> G[Diversity Metrics\nIndividual & Collective]

Where It’s Useful:

Auditing long-term fairness and diversity effects of deployed recommender systems in e-commerce and streaming platforms
Comparing recommender architectures (MF, neural CF, etc.) under realistic closed-loop conditions rather than offline holdout benchmarks
Policy simulation: testing what adoption rates or retraining schedules minimize popularity concentration

Limitations / Caveats:

Adoption probability α is a simplifying scalar; real user adoption is context-, session-, and item-dependent
Organic (non-recommended) behavior is assumed stationary, whereas true user tastes drift independently of recommendations
Computationally expensive at scale: full periodic retraining over many simulation steps requires significant infrastructure
Results are domain- and model-dependent, limiting direct generalization across recommendation contexts

Internal Use Cases:

Feedback loop auditing: Apply FLSF to detect whether current implicit-feedback training pipelines (cf. negative sampling work in vault) progressively concentrate popularity, compounding impression bias identified in causal models
Continual RecSys integration: Use FLSF’s retraining cycle as a benchmark harness for continual learning methods (cf. Continual Recommender System note) — measuring whether plasticity gains come at the cost of diversity collapse over time
Diversity vs. relevance tradeoff analysis: Simulate how tuning adoption rate interacts with collective Gini concentration, informing whether diversity regularization should be applied at training time or re-ranking time

Discussions & References

github.com

literature/2026-02-19-Variable-Length-Se.title: Variable-Length Semantic IDs for Recommender Systems
literature/2026-02-19-Variable-Length-Se.content:
Variable-Length Semantic IDs for Recommender Systems
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #6 (score: 0.00, max_sim: 0.88)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Generative models are increasingly used in recommender systems, both for modeling user behavior as event sequences and for integrating large language models into recommendation pipelines. A key challenge in this setting is the extremely large cardinality of item spaces, which makes training generative models difficult and introduces a vocabulary gap between natural language and item identifiers. Semantic identifiers (semantic IDs), which represent items as sequences of low-cardinality tokens, have recently emerged as an effective solution to this problem.   However, existing approaches generate semantic identifiers of fixed length, assigning the same description length to all items. This is inefficient, misaligned with natural language, and ignores the highly skewed frequency structure of real-world catalogs, where popular items and rare long-tail items exhibit fundamentally different information requirements. In parallel, the emergent communication literature studies how agents develop discrete communication protocols, often producing variable-length messages in which frequent concepts receive shorter descriptions. Despite the conceptual similarity, these ideas have not been systematically adopted in recommender systems.   In this work, we bridge recommender systems and emergent communication by introducing variable-length semantic identifiers for recommendation. We propose a discrete variational autoencoder with Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization that learns item representations of adaptive length under a principled probabilistic framework, avoiding the instability of REINFORCE-based training and the fixed-length constraints of prior semantic ID methods.
Key Techniques

Semantic identifiers
Variable-length semantic identifiers
Discrete variational autoencoder with Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityVariable-Length Semantic IDs for Recommender Systems0.90DOS: Dual-Flow Orthogonal Semantic IDs for Recommendation in Meituan0.27The Case for Semantic Tokens in Modern Ranking Systems, How Embedding Size Affects Dense Retrieval, and More!0.26Unleash the Potential of Long Semantic IDs for Generative Recommendation0.25End-to-End Semantic ID Generation for Generative Advertisement Recommendation0.25
Variable-Length Semantic IDs (VL-SID)
Core Idea: A discrete variational autoencoder with Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization that learns item representations as adaptive-length token sequences, assigning shorter codes to frequent items and longer codes to rare ones.
Problem It Solves: Fixed-length semantic IDs waste representation capacity by treating all items equally, ignoring the Zipfian frequency distribution of real catalogs where popular items need less description and long-tail items need more — also creating a misalignment with natural language where common concepts have shorter encodings.
How It Works:

Encode: An item’s features (text, metadata, embeddings) are passed through an encoder network to produce a latent representation; a learned length predictor outputs a probability over sequence lengths.
Discretize: Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization samples a discrete token sequence of the predicted length from a shared low-cardinality vocabulary, enabling differentiable training without high-variance REINFORCE gradient estimators.
Decode & Optimize: A decoder reconstructs the item from its token sequence; the ELBO objective balances reconstruction fidelity against a KL penalty that encourages shorter codes for items with lower information content (proxied by frequency or entropy).

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Item Features] --> B[Encoder]
    B --> C[Length Predictor]
    B --> D[Gumbel-Softmax\nDiscretizer]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Variable-Length\nToken Sequence]
    E --> F[Decoder]
    F --> G[Reconstructed\nItem Embedding]
    E --> H[Generative\nRecommender Model]

Where It’s Useful:

Generative retrieval recommenders (e.g., TIGER, LETTER) that autoregressively decode item IDs
LLM-integrated recommendation pipelines where item tokens must coexist with natural language vocabulary
Large catalogs with heavy-tailed popularity distributions (e-commerce, streaming, search)

Limitations / Caveats:

Variable-length outputs complicate beam search and constrained decoding, requiring length-aware trie structures or masked generation
Length prediction adds a learned component that may overfit frequency signals, potentially under-representing rare items if the length prior is poorly calibrated
Gumbel-Softmax temperature annealing introduces an additional sensitive hyperparameter schedule

Internal Use Cases:

Directly applicable to the SGLang constrained decoding work (see Semantic ID Constrained Decoding note): the variable-length trie must be extended to handle codes of differing depths, but the payoff is a smaller average beam width for frequent items
Complements IDGenRec’s textual IDs: VL-SID operates in a learned discrete space rather than natural language tokens, making it more compact but less human-interpretable — the two could be compared as indexing strategies under the Item Indexing for LLM-based Recommendation framework
In a ranking pipeline, shorter IDs for popular items reduce autoregressive generation latency at inference time, directly improving p50 latency for head queries while maintaining quality on tail queries

literature/2026-02-19-Why-Thinking-Hurts.title: Why Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models
literature/2026-02-19-Why-Thinking-Hurts.content:
Why Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #1 (score: 0.96, max_sim: 0.84)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Integrating Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning into Semantic ID-based recommendation foundation models (such as OpenOneRec) often paradoxically degrades recommendation performance. We identify the root cause as textual inertia from the General Subspace, where verbose reasoning dominates inference and causes the model to neglect critical Semantic ID. To address this, we propose a training-free Inference-Time Subspace Alignment framework. By compressing reasoning chains and applying bias-subtracted contrastive decoding, our approach mitigates ungrounded textual drift. Experiments show this effectively calibrates inference, allowing foundation models to leverage reasoning without sacrificing ID-grounded accuracy.
Key Techniques

Inference-Time Subspace Alignment
Reasoning Chain Compression
Bias-subtracted Contrastive Decoding

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityWhy Thinking Hurts? Diagnosing and Rectifying the Reasoning Shift in Foundation Recommender Models0.84ImgCoT: Compressing Long Chain of Thought into Compact Visual Tokens for Efficient Reasoning of Large Language Model0.35S^2GR: Stepwise Semantic-Guided Reasoning in Latent Space for Generative Recommendation0.26SwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMs0.25The Potential of CoT for Reasoning: A Closer Look at Trace Dynamics0.23
Inference-Time Subspace Alignment (ITSA)
Core Idea: A training-free decoding framework that suppresses verbose textual drift in Chain-of-Thought (CoT)-augmented recommendation models by compressing reasoning chains and applying bias-subtracted contrastive decoding to restore Semantic ID signal dominance at inference time.
Problem It Solves: When CoT reasoning is integrated into Semantic ID-based foundation recommender models (e.g., OpenOneRec), “textual inertia” causes the model’s General Subspace (text generation) to overwhelm the Semantic ID Subspace during decoding—producing fluent but ID-ungrounded outputs that degrade recommendation accuracy despite richer reasoning.
How It Works:

Reasoning Compression: The CoT chain generated at inference time is truncated or summarized to reduce token volume, limiting the surface area over which textual inertia can propagate into the decoding distribution.
Bias Estimation: A “bias vector” is computed by running the model on compressed or null reasoning context, capturing the residual General Subspace drift introduced by the reasoning prefix alone.
Contrastive Decoding: At each decoding step, the bias vector is subtracted from the full logit distribution (model with CoT) to cancel ungrounded textual influence, sharpening probability mass toward valid Semantic ID tokens.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[User History + Query] --> B[CoT Generator]
    B --> C[Reasoning Chain]
    C --> D[Chain Compressor]
    D --> E[Compressed Reasoning Prefix]
    E --> F[Full Model Decoder]
    E --> G[Bias Model Decoder\nnull/compressed context]
    F --> H[Logits_full]
    G --> I[Logits_bias]
    H --> J["Contrastive Subtraction\nLogits_full − α·Logits_bias"]
    J --> K[Semantic ID Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Foundation recommender models that use Semantic IDs (e.g., RQ-VAE-derived hierarchical IDs) and want to incorporate reasoning without retraining
Any generative retrieval system where a language model must output structured tokens (IDs, codes) after a free-text reasoning prefix

Limitations / Caveats:

Contrastive decoding requires two forward passes per step, increasing inference compute roughly 2×
The bias subtraction coefficient α is a hyperparameter that may need tuning per model/dataset; poor calibration can over-suppress valid semantic content
Assumes a clean separation between General and Semantic ID subspaces—may be less effective if the model’s pretraining blurs this boundary

Internal Use Cases:

ID-based Representation for FM4RecSys: Directly applicable—ITSA restores ID-grounded decoding when CoT prefixes are prepended to Semantic ID generation
Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for User Preference Modeling: Use ITSA downstream to ensure CoT-enriched user profiles don’t cause textual drift when fed into a generative ranker
Chained Graph Reasoning in LLM Recommenders: ITSA could stabilize multi-hop reasoning chains that otherwise accumulate textual bias before the final item ID prediction step

literature/2026-02-22-2Mamba2Furious-Lin.title: 2Mamba2Furious: Linear in Complexity, Competitive in Accuracy
literature/2026-02-22-2Mamba2Furious-Lin.content:
2Mamba2Furious: Linear in Complexity, Competitive in Accuracy
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-19
Rank: #7 (score: 0.70, max_sim: 0.81)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Linear attention transformers have become a strong alternative to softmax attention due to their efficiency. However, linear attention tends to be less expressive and results in reduced accuracy compared to softmax attention. To bridge the accuracy gap between softmax attention and linear attention, we manipulate Mamba-2, a very strong linear attention variant. We first simplify Mamba-2 down to its most fundamental and important components, evaluating which specific choices make it most accurate. From this simplified Mamba variant (Mamba-2S), we improve the A-mask and increase the order of the hidden state, resulting in a method, which we call 2Mamba, that is nearly as accurate as softmax attention, yet much more memory efficient for long context lengths. We also investigate elements to Mamba-2 that help surpass softmax attention accuracy. Code is provided for all our experiments
Key Techniques

Mamba-2 simplification (Mamba-2S)
A-mask improvement
hidden state order increase
2Mamba method development

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.31Kalman Linear Attention: Parallel Bayesian Filtering For Efficient Language Modelling and State Tracking0.29SLA2: Sparse-Linear Attention with Learnable Routing and QAT0.29Superlinear Multi-Step Attention0.24HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.24
2Mamba
Core Idea: A simplified and enhanced linear attention architecture derived from Mamba-2 that improves the A-mask and increases hidden state order to close the accuracy gap with softmax attention while retaining linear complexity.
Problem It Solves: Linear attention variants like Mamba-2 are memory-efficient but trail softmax attention in accuracy due to limited expressivity in their state-transition masks and low-order hidden states; 2Mamba targets this accuracy gap without sacrificing linear-time scaling.
How It Works:

Simplify Mamba-2 → Mamba-2S: Strip Mamba-2 down to its most impactful components, ablating away elements that don’t contribute to accuracy, yielding a clean baseline (Mamba-2 Simplified).
Improve the A-mask: Replace the original decay/selection mask in Mamba-2S with a more expressive A-mask that better controls how information flows and decays across the recurrent hidden state.
Increase hidden state order: Expand the order of the recurrent hidden state (analogous to increasing state-space model (SSM) rank/degree), giving the model greater capacity to encode long-range dependencies within its fixed-size memory before each recurrent step.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Token Sequence] --> B[Mamba-2S\nStripped Core]
    B --> C[Enhanced A-mask\nExpressive Decay]
    C --> D[Higher-Order\nHidden State]
    D --> E[Linear Recurrence\nO-n Memory]
    E --> F[Output\nLogits]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context language modeling where quadratic softmax attention is memory-prohibitive
Deployment-constrained inference (edge, streaming) requiring fixed memory footprint per token
Sequence modeling tasks (document understanding, code, genomics) with sequences exceeding typical transformer context budgets

Limitations / Caveats:

Still trails softmax attention slightly in accuracy on some benchmarks; the gap narrows but is not fully closed
Higher hidden state order increases per-step compute/memory relative to vanilla Mamba-2, partially trading recurrent efficiency for expressivity
Architectural improvements are empirically validated but the theoretical justification for the improved A-mask form may not be fully characterized

Internal Use Cases:

Long-context ranking/retrieval: Replace softmax attention in rerankers processing long documents or session histories with 2Mamba to enable O(n) memory scaling without major accuracy loss
Sequential recommendation: Model long user interaction histories recurrently with higher-order hidden states, capturing richer temporal patterns than standard SSMs
LLM serving with extended context: Use 2Mamba layers in place of attention in production LLMs to reduce KV-cache memory, enabling larger batch sizes or longer contexts at fixed GPU memory

literature/2026-02-22-A-Picture-of-Agent.title: A Picture of Agentic Search
literature/2026-02-22-A-Picture-of-Agent.content:
A Picture of Agentic Search
Source: arXiv:cs.IR
Published: 2026-02-19
Rank: #4 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: Search, RAG
Abstract
With automated systems increasingly issuing search queries alongside humans, Information Retrieval (IR) faces a major shift. Yet IR remains human-centred, with systems, evaluation metrics, user models, and datasets designed around human queries and behaviours. Consequently, IR operates under assumptions that no longer hold in practice, with changes to workload volumes, predictability, and querying behaviours. This misalignment affects system performance and optimisation: caching may lose effectiveness, query pre-processing may add overhead without improving results, and standard metrics may mismeasure satisfaction. Without adaptation, retrieval models risk satisfying neither humans, nor the emerging user segment of agents. However, datasets capturing agent search behaviour are lacking, which is a critical gap given IR’s historical reliance on data-driven evaluation and optimisation. We develop a methodology for collecting all the data produced and consumed by agentic retrieval-augmented systems when answering queries, and we release the Agentic Search Queryset (ASQ) dataset. ASQ contains reasoning-induced queries, retrieved documents, and thoughts for queries in HotpotQA, Researchy Questions, and MS MARCO, for 3 diverse agents and 2 retrieval pipelines. The accompanying toolkit enables ASQ to be extended to new agents, retrievers, and datasets.
Key Techniques

Agentic retrieval data collection methodology
Agentic Search Queryset (ASQ) dataset
ASQ extension toolkit

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySAGE: Steerable Agentic Data Generation for Deep Search with Execution Feedback0.23Agentic-R: Learning to Retrieve for Agentic Search0.23Fat-Cat: Document-Driven Metacognitive Multi-Agent System for Complex Reasoning0.23DeepImageSearch: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Context-Aware Image Retrieval in Visual Histories0.21PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.20
Agentic Search Queryset (ASQ) Collection Methodology
Core Idea: A systematic data collection methodology that intercepts and logs all intermediate artifacts—queries, retrieved documents, and chain-of-thought reasoning traces—produced by agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems during query answering, yielding a benchmark dataset for studying non-human search behavior.
Problem It Solves: IR evaluation infrastructure (metrics, datasets, user models, caching strategies, query pre-processors) is built around human querying behavior; as automated agents increasingly issue search queries, these assumptions break down, yet no datasets exist to characterize the distinct behavioral signature of agentic search.
How It Works:

Instrument agentic RAG pipelines — wrap 3 diverse agent architectures and 2 retrieval pipelines with logging hooks that capture every retrieval call issued during multi-step reasoning (input: a source question set, e.g. HotpotQA, Researchy Questions, MS MARCO)
Capture the full agentic trace — for each query, record the reasoning-induced sub-queries the agent generates, the documents retrieved per sub-query, and the intermediate chain-of-thought thoughts that bridge retrieval steps (output: a structured tuple of ⟨query, sub-queries, retrieved docs, thoughts, final answer⟩)
Release + extend via toolkit — package the collected data as ASQ and provide a toolkit that allows researchers to slot in new agents, retrievers, or source datasets to extend coverage without rebuilding the collection pipeline

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    Q[Source Query\nHotpotQA / MS MARCO\nResearchy Qs] --> A[Agentic RAG System\n3 agent types]
    A -->|sub-query 1..n| R[Retrieval Pipeline\n2 pipeline types]
    R -->|retrieved docs| A
    A -->|chain-of-thought| T[Thought Logger]
    T --> ASQ[(ASQ Dataset\nqueries + docs\n+ thoughts)]
    A --> ANS[Final Answer]
    ASQ --> TK[Extensibility Toolkit\nnew agents / retrievers]

Where It’s Useful:

Benchmarking IR systems under realistic agentic workloads (burst query patterns, reformulated sub-queries)
Analyzing how caching, query pre-processing, and ranking models behave when the “user” is an LLM rather than a human
Training or fine-tuning retrieval models on agent-generated query distributions

Limitations / Caveats:

Dataset reflects behavior of only 3 agent architectures and 2 retrieval pipelines; may not generalize to all agentic systems
Source datasets (HotpotQA, MS MARCO) were originally human-curated, so the underlying information needs still reflect human intent even if query formulation is agentic
Static snapshot — agentic querying behavior will evolve as LLM reasoning capabilities improve, requiring periodic re-collection

Internal Use Cases:

Caching strategy re-evaluation: ASQ query distributions can stress-test whether existing cache hit-rate assumptions hold for agent-issued queries (relevant to Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) System)
Reward signal calibration: Thought traces in ASQ provide a proxy for intermediate retrieval satisfaction, usable as weak supervision for the reward signals in User-Centric Reinforcement Feedback Loop in IR
Metric audit: ASQ enables side-by-side comparison of precision/recall vs. agent task-completion, directly informing the triad audit described in Hybrid Evaluation: Precision, Recall, User Satisfaction Triad

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-22-ArXiv-to-Model-A-P.title: ArXiv-to-Model: A Practical Study of Scientific LM Training
literature/2026-02-22-ArXiv-to-Model-A-P.content:
ArXiv-to-Model: A Practical Study of Scientific LM Training
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-19
Rank: #5 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Transformer, RAG, Search
Abstract
While frontier large language models demonstrate strong reasoning and mathematical capabilities, the practical process of training domain-specialized scientific language models from raw sources remains under-documented. In this work, we present a detailed case study of training a 1.36B-parameter scientific language model directly from raw arXiv LaTeX sources spanning mathematics, computer science, and theoretical physics. We describe an end-to-end pipeline covering metadata filtering, archive validation, LaTeX extraction, text normalization, domain-aware tokenization, and dense transformer training under constrained compute (2xA100 GPUs). Through 24 experimental runs, we analyze training stability, scaling behavior, data yield losses, and infrastructure bottlenecks. Our findings highlight how preprocessing decisions significantly affect usable token volume, how tokenization impacts symbolic stability, and how storage and I/O constraints can rival compute as limiting factors. We further analyze convergence dynamics and show stable training behavior in a data-rich regime (52B pretraining tokens). Rather than proposing a novel architecture, this work provides an engineering-grounded, transparent account of training a small scientific language model from scratch. We hope these insights support researchers operating under moderate compute budgets who seek to build domain-specialized models.
Key Techniques

LaTeX extraction
domain-aware tokenization
dense transformer training
text normalization

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityImproving Data and Reward Design for Scientific Reasoning in Large Language Models0.25PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.21PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.18PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.16PaperSearchQA: Learning to Search and Reason over Scientific Papers with RLVR0.16
ArXiv-to-Model End-to-End Scientific LM Training Pipeline
Core Idea: A complete, engineering-grounded pipeline for training domain-specialized scientific language models directly from raw arXiv LaTeX sources under constrained compute, covering every stage from metadata filtering through dense transformer pretraining.
Problem It Solves: The practical process of building domain-specialized scientific LMs is under-documented — researchers lack transparent, reproducible accounts of how preprocessing decisions, tokenization choices, and I/O constraints affect usable token yield and training stability at moderate compute budgets.
How It Works:

Metadata filtering & archive validation: Filter raw arXiv dumps by domain (math, CS, theoretical physics) using metadata; validate archive integrity before extraction to avoid silent data corruption
LaTeX extraction & text normalization: Parse .tex sources to strip macros, environments, and formatting artifacts; normalize mathematical notation to preserve symbolic structure
Domain-aware tokenization: Train or adapt a tokenizer with vocabulary coverage tuned for scientific symbols, Greek letters, and LaTeX-derived tokens to maintain symbolic stability
Dense transformer pretraining: Train a 1.36B-parameter transformer on ~52B tokens across 24 experimental runs on 2×A100 GPUs, monitoring convergence dynamics and stability

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Raw arXiv\nLaTeX Archives] --> B[Metadata Filter\n& Validation]
    B --> C[LaTeX Extraction\n& Normalization]
    C --> D[Domain-Aware\nTokenizer]
    D --> E[Token Dataset\n~52B tokens]
    E --> F[Dense Transformer\n1.36B params\n2×A100]
    F --> G[Scientific LM]

Where It’s Useful:

Training specialized LMs for scientific domains (math, physics, CS) without frontier compute
Benchmarking preprocessing pipeline decisions against downstream token yield
Reproducible scientific NLP research under academic/moderate-budget constraints

Limitations / Caveats:

Storage and I/O bottlenecks can rival compute as the primary constraint — findings may not generalize to well-provisioned cloud environments
Preprocessing yield losses are significant; usable token fraction from raw arXiv can be substantially lower than expected
1.36B parameters limits capability headroom; findings on scaling behavior may not extrapolate to larger regimes

Internal Use Cases:

Domain-specialized retrieval: A scientific LM trained this way could produce higher-quality embeddings for ranking math/CS papers than general-purpose encoders
LLM pipeline grounding: The tokenization and normalization strategies are directly applicable when preprocessing scientific corpora for RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) over arXiv content
Data yield auditing: The pipeline’s stage-by-stage token accounting methodology could be adopted to diagnose data quality losses in any large-scale pretraining corpus

Discussions & References

huggingface.co
huggingface.co
github.com

literature/2026-02-22-Beyond-Message-Pas.title: Beyond Message Passing: A Symbolic Alternative for Expressive and Interpretable Graph Learning
literature/2026-02-22-Beyond-Message-Pas.content:
Beyond Message Passing: A Symbolic Alternative for Expressive and Interpretable Graph Learning
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #8 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~15 min
Tags: RecSys, Graph
Abstract
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have become essential in high-stakes domains such as drug discovery, yet their black-box nature remains a significant barrier to trustworthiness. While self-explainable GNNs attempt to bridge this gap, they often rely on standard message-passing backbones that inherit fundamental limitations, including the 1-Weisfeiler-Lehman (1-WL) expressivity barrier and a lack of fine-grained interpretability. To address these challenges, we propose SymGraph, a symbolic framework designed to transcend these constraints. By replacing continuous message passing with discrete structural hashing and topological role-based aggregation, our architecture theoretically surpasses the 1-WL barrier, achieving superior expressiveness without the overhead of differentiable optimization. Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that SymGraph achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing self-explainable GNNs. Notably, SymGraph delivers 10x to 100x speedups in training time using only CPU execution. Furthermore, SymGraph generates rules with superior semantic granularity compared to existing rule-based methods, offering great potential for scientific discovery and explainable AI.
Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySemi-Supervised Learning on Graphs using Graph Neural Networks0.23One Paradigm for All: Relational Deep Learning for Representation of Universal Behavioral Profiles0.20NeuroSymActive: Differentiable Neural-Symbolic Reasoning with Active Exploration for Knowledge Graph Question Answering0.17LIT-GRAPH: Evaluating Deep vs. Shallow Graph Embeddings for High-Quality Text Recommendation in Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs0.16Exploring the impact of adaptive rewiring in Graph Neural Networks0.15

literature/2026-02-22-CLAA-Cross-Layer-A.title: CLAA: Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation for Accelerating LLM Prefill
literature/2026-02-22-CLAA-Cross-Layer-A.content:
CLAA: Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation for Accelerating LLM Prefill
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-17
Rank: #3 (score: 0.92, max_sim: 0.87)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The prefill stage in long-context LLM inference remains a computational bottleneck. Recent token-ranking heuristics accelerate inference by selectively processing a subset of semantically relevant tokens. However, existing methods suffer from unstable token importance estimation, often varying between layers. Evaluating token-ranking quality independently from heuristic-specific architectures is challenging. To address this, we introduce an Answer-Informed Oracle, which defines ground-truth token importance by measuring attention from generated answers back to the prompt. This oracle reveals that existing heuristics exhibit high variance across layers: rankings can degrade sharply at specific layers, a failure mode invisible to end-to-end benchmarks. The diagnosis suggests a simple fix: aggregate scores across layers rather than relying on any single one. We implement this as Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation (CLAA), which closes the gap to the oracle upper bound and reduces Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) by up to 39% compared to the Full KV Cache baseline.
Key Techniques

Answer-Informed Oracle
Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation (CLAA)
Token-ranking heuristics

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityCLAA: Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation for Accelerating LLM Prefill0.89HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.35HySparse: A Hybrid Sparse Attention Architecture with Oracle Token Selection and KV Cache Sharing0.31PrefillShare: A Shared Prefill Module for KV Reuse in Multi-LLM Disaggregated Serving0.25FASA: Frequency-aware Sparse Attention0.25
Cross-Layer Attention Aggregation (CLAA)
Core Idea: Aggregate token importance scores across multiple transformer layers rather than relying on any single layer’s attention pattern, closing the gap to an oracle upper bound for selective token processing during LLM prefill.
Problem It Solves: Existing token-ranking heuristics for accelerating LLM prefill suffer from high per-layer variance — rankings can degrade sharply at specific layers, causing important tokens to be dropped, a failure mode invisible to end-to-end benchmarks.
How It Works:

Oracle Construction: Compute ground-truth token importance via an Answer-Informed Oracle — run full inference, then measure attention weights from generated answer tokens back to prompt tokens, defining which prompt tokens were truly critical.
Diagnosis: Use the oracle to audit existing heuristics (e.g., quest, SnapKV) layer-by-layer, revealing that single-layer token rankings exhibit high variance and catastrophic degradation at specific layers.
Aggregation: During prefill, compute token importance scores at each layer (or a subset), then aggregate (e.g., sum or average) scores across layers before making the token selection decision, smoothing out per-layer noise.
Selective Processing: Retain only top-ranked tokens in the KV cache / attention computation for subsequent layers, reducing FLOPs while preserving answer quality.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    P[Prompt Tokens] --> L1[Layer 1 Attention]
    P --> L2[Layer 2 Attention]
    P --> LN[Layer N Attention]
    L1 -->|scores_1| AGG[Cross-Layer Aggregator]
    L2 -->|scores_2| AGG
    LN -->|scores_n| AGG
    AGG -->|stable ranking| SEL[Token Selector]
    SEL -->|top-k tokens| REST[Remaining Layers]
    REST --> OUT[First Token Output]

Where It’s Useful:

Long-context LLM inference where prefill is the dominant latency bottleneck (RAG pipelines, document Q&A, long-form summarization)
Any setting with strict Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) SLA requirements
Multi-document or needle-in-a-haystack retrieval tasks where only a fraction of the prompt is semantically relevant

Limitations / Caveats:

Aggregating across layers adds bookkeeping overhead; benefits diminish if the prompt is short (prefill cost is already low)
The Answer-Informed Oracle requires ground-truth answers at evaluation time — it is a diagnostic/analysis tool, not a deployment component
Performance depends on what fraction of tokens are retained (the sparsity budget); aggressive pruning can still hurt accuracy on tasks requiring broad context coverage

Internal Use Cases:

LLM-based ranking/reranking: When using an LLM to score candidate items against a long query context, CLAA-style prefill acceleration reduces latency for the prompt encoding step without sacrificing which query tokens drive the scoring
RAG retrieval augmentation: Passages fed to an LLM generator can be selectively compressed at prefill time, reducing TTFT while preserving the tokens the model would actually attend to when generating the answer
Experiment evaluation tooling: The Answer-Informed Oracle methodology can be adapted as an offline diagnostic to audit any attention-based token pruning heuristic your team is evaluating, providing layer-wise variance analysis rather than just end-to-end accuracy

literature/2026-02-22-Conditional-Flow-M.title: Conditional Flow Matching for Continuous Anomaly Detection in Autonomous Driving on a Manifold-Aware Spectral Space
literature/2026-02-22-Conditional-Flow-M.content:
Conditional Flow Matching for Continuous Anomaly Detection in Autonomous Driving on a Manifold-Aware Spectral Space
Source: arXiv:cs.AI
Published: 2026-02-19
Rank: #8 (score: 0.14, max_sim: 0.76)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Safety validation for Level 4 autonomous vehicles (AVs) is currently bottlenecked by the inability to scale the detection of rare, high-risk long-tail scenarios using traditional rule-based heuristics. We present Deep-Flow, an unsupervised framework for safety-critical anomaly detection that utilizes Optimal Transport Conditional Flow Matching (OT-CFM) to characterize the continuous probability density of expert human driving behavior. Unlike standard generative approaches that operate in unstable, high-dimensional coordinate spaces, Deep-Flow constrains the generative process to a low-rank spectral manifold via a Principal Component Analysis (PCA) bottleneck. This ensures kinematic smoothness by design and enables the computation of the exact Jacobian trace for numerically stable, deterministic log-likelihood estimation. To resolve multi-modal ambiguity at complex junctions, we utilize an Early Fusion Transformer encoder with lane-aware goal conditioning, featuring a direct skip-connection to the flow head to maintain intent-integrity throughout the network. We introduce a kinematic complexity weighting scheme that prioritizes high-energy maneuvers (quantified via path tortuosity and jerk) during the simulation-free training process. Evaluated on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset (WOMD), our framework achieves an AUC-ROC of 0.766 against a heuristic golden set of safety-critical events. More significantly, our analysis reveals a fundamental distinction between kinematic danger and semantic non-compliance. Deep-Flow identifies a critical predictability gap by surfacing out-of-distribution behaviors, such as lane-boundary violations and non-normative junction maneuvers, that traditional safety filters overlook. This work provides a mathematically rigorous foundation for defining statistical safety gates, enabling objective, data-driven validation for the safe deployment of autonomous fleets.
Key Techniques

Optimal Transport Conditional Flow Matching (OT-CFM)
PCA bottleneck for low-rank spectral manifold
Early Fusion Transformer with lane-aware goal conditioning
kinematic complexity weighting scheme

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityLearned harmonic mean estimation of the marginal likelihood for multimodal posteriors with flow matching0.13PANC: Prior-Aware Normalized Cut for Object Segmentation0.12SToRM: Supervised Token Reduction for Multi-modal LLMs toward efficient end-to-end autonomous driving0.12GOSV: Global Optimization for Safety Vector Extraction in LLMs0.12OSCAR: Optimization-Steered Agentic Planning for Composed Image Retrieval0.10
Deep-Flow: Manifold-Constrained Optimal Transport Conditional Flow Matching (OT-CFM)
Core Idea: Model the continuous probability density of expert driving behavior by training a flow-matching generative model constrained to a low-rank PCA spectral manifold, then use exact log-likelihood scores as anomaly signals for safety-critical event detection.
Problem It Solves: Traditional rule-based heuristics cannot scale to rare long-tail driving scenarios; standard generative models operating in raw high-dimensional coordinate space suffer from kinematic instability, intractable Jacobian computation, and multi-modal ambiguity at complex junctions.
How It Works:

PCA Spectral Bottleneck: Raw trajectory coordinates are projected onto a low-rank spectral manifold via PCA, enforcing kinematic smoothness by construction and collapsing dimensionality to a space where the Jacobian trace is analytically exact (no stochastic estimation needed).
OT-CFM Training: A simulation-free flow-matching objective learns a deterministic vector field transporting a source Gaussian to the manifold-projected expert trajectory distribution; Optimal Transport couplings straighten flow paths, reducing training variance. A kinematic complexity weighting scheme up-weights high-jerk, high-tortuosity samples to prioritize rare energetic maneuvers.
Conditioned Flow Inference: At inference, an Early Fusion Transformer encoder ingests scene context (agent history + lane graph) and produces a goal-conditioned latent with a direct skip-connection into the flow head, preserving intent signal. Log-likelihood under the learned density is computed exactly via the manifold Jacobian and used as the anomaly score — low likelihood = out-of-distribution = safety-critical flag.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Agent History\n+ Lane Graph] --> B[Early Fusion\nTransformer Encoder]
    B --> C[Goal-Conditioned\nLatent]
    C -- skip-connection --> F
    A --> D[PCA Spectral\nBottleneck]
    D --> E[Manifold\nCoordinates]
    E --> F[OT-CFM\nFlow Head]
    F --> G[Vector Field\n/ Log-Likelihood]
    G --> H[Anomaly Score]

Where It’s Useful:

Safety validation and statistical gating for Level 4 autonomous vehicle fleets
Unsupervised out-of-distribution detection in any sequential, trajectory-structured domain (robotics, UAV path planning)
Replacing hand-crafted heuristic filters with learned density thresholds in simulation pipelines

Limitations / Caveats:

PCA bottleneck rank is a fixed hyperparameter; too low a rank discards semantically meaningful variance, too high reintroduces instability — sensitive to dataset characteristics
AUC-ROC of 0.766 against a heuristic golden set means the method is not yet a drop-in replacement for curated safety oracles; golden set quality directly caps measured performance
Kinematic complexity weighting requires defining tortuosity/jerk thresholds, reintroducing domain priors the framework aims to avoid
Evaluated on a single dataset (WOMD); generalization to other sensor modalities or geographic domains is unverified

Internal Use Cases:

Ranking/RecSys: The OT-CFM density estimation pattern is directly applicable to session-sequence anomaly detection — model normal user engagement trajectories on a spectral manifold and flag low-likelihood sessions as fraud, bot traffic, or distribution shift without labeled anomaly data
LLM evaluation: Apply manifold-constrained flow matching to token-probability trajectories across decoding steps to detect out-of-distribution generations (hallucinations, jailbreaks) as low-likelihood sequences under a learned “safe behavior” density
Data quality gating: Use exact log-likelihood scores as a simulation-free filter to automatically surface and up-weight rare but high-signal training examples in fine-tuning pipelines, analogous to the kinematic complexity weighting scheme

literature/2026-02-22-Dynamic-Delayed-Tr.title: Dynamic Delayed Tree Expansion For Improved Multi-Path Speculative Decoding
literature/2026-02-22-Dynamic-Delayed-Tr.content:
Dynamic Delayed Tree Expansion For Improved Multi-Path Speculative Decoding
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-19
Rank: #4 (score: 0.78, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
Multi-path speculative decoding accelerates lossless sampling from a target model by using a cheaper draft model to generate a draft tree of tokens, and then applies a verification algorithm that accepts a subset of these. While prior work has proposed various verification algorithms for i.i.d rollouts, their relative performance under matched settings remains unclear. In this work, we firstly present a systematic evaluation of verification strategies across model families, tasks, and sampling regimes, and find that Traversal Verification dominates consistently, with OT-based methods lagging far behind. Our analysis uncovers that this occurs because OT-based methods achieve high multi-token acceptance near the root of the draft tree, while multi-token gains are most impactful deeper in the draft tree, where draft and target distributions diverge. Based on this insight, we propose delayed tree expansion, which drafts a partial single path, delaying the i.i.d. branching point. We show that delayed tree expansion preserves the target distribution and improves on root-node i.i.d rollouts. Further, we develop a dynamic neural selector that estimates the expected block efficiency of optimal-transport-based verification methods from draft and target features, enabling context-dependent expansion decisions. Our neural selector allows OT-based methods like SpecInfer to outperform Traversal Verification for the first time, achieving 5% higher average throughput across a wide range of models, datasets, and sampling settings.
Key Techniques

Delayed tree expansion
Dynamic neural selector
Traversal Verification
OT-based methods

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityGreedy Multi-Path Block Verification for Faster Decoding in Speculative Sampling0.49DART: Diffusion-Inspired Speculative Decoding for Fast LLM Inference0.46PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models0.42SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration0.39DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.38
Dynamic Delayed Tree Expansion (DDTE)
Core Idea: Rather than branching a draft tree immediately at the root via i.i.d. rollouts, DDTE delays the branching point by first drafting a single linear path, then applies a learned neural selector to decide context-dependently where along that path to begin i.i.d. expansion — enabling optimal-transport (OT)-based verification methods to reclaim their theoretical advantage.
Problem It Solves: OT-based multi-path speculative decoding methods (e.g., SpecInfer) achieve high multi-token acceptance near the root but waste their budget where it matters least; the largest acceptance gains occur deeper in the draft tree where draft and target distributions diverge, yet root-branching i.i.d. rollouts never reach those depths efficiently. This causes OT methods to consistently underperform simpler Traversal Verification under matched compute budgets.
How It Works:

Single-path prefix drafting: Instead of immediately fanning out into an i.i.d. tree from the root, the draft model generates a single linear token sequence for d steps, delaying the branching point to depth d.
Neural selector decision: A lightweight neural selector takes as input features from both the draft model and target model (e.g., token probabilities, entropy, hidden-state statistics along the drafted prefix) and predicts the expected block efficiency (accepted tokens per step) for OT-based verification at each candidate branching depth, selecting the depth d^* that maximizes throughput.
Delayed i.i.d. tree expansion: From depth d^*, the draft model fans out into the standard i.i.d. branching tree; the full draft tree (prefix path + branched subtree) is then verified in a single target-model forward pass using an OT-based verification algorithm, preserving the target distribution exactly.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Context Tokens] --> B[Draft Model\nLinear Rollout]
    B --> C[Draft + Target\nFeature Extraction]
    C --> D[Neural Selector\nPredicts d*]
    D --> E[i.i.d. Branch\nat depth d*]
    B --> E
    E --> F[Draft Tree\nPrefix + Subtree]
    F --> G[Target Model\nSingle Forward Pass]
    G --> H[OT Verification\ne.g. SpecInfer]
    H --> I[Accepted Tokens]

Where It’s Useful:

Any autoregressive LLM inference pipeline using multi-path speculative decoding with a cheap draft model
High-throughput serving where draft and target distributions diverge significantly (e.g., long generation, out-of-domain inputs)
Settings where OT-based draft tree construction is already in use and Traversal Verification has been the only competitive alternative

Limitations / Caveats:

The neural selector introduces a learned component that must be trained and may not generalize perfectly across all model families or tasks without retraining
The single-path prefix phase reduces parallelism in the drafting step compared to immediate root-branching; benefit depends on selector accuracy at identifying the right depth
Gains are predicated on OT-based verification budget — in very low-budget or greedy-decoding regimes the advantage may shrink

Internal Use Cases:

LLM inference acceleration: Directly applicable to any serving stack (e.g., vLLM, TGI) using speculative decoding; the neural selector could be adapted to predict branching depth per request type (e.g., retrieval-augmented generation vs. open-ended generation)
Draft shape optimization: Complements work like DReSD and Dense Retrieval for Speculative Decoding by providing a principled way to decide when to branch retrieved draft sequences rather than branching uniformly
Batch verification integration: The delayed prefix path aligns naturally with batch verification frameworks — the linear prefix can be verified cheaply, and the subtree batch-verified in one pass, reducing total verification overhead

literature/2026-02-22-Fail-Closed-Alignm.title: Fail-Closed Alignment for Large Language Models
literature/2026-02-22-Fail-Closed-Alignm.content:
Fail-Closed Alignment for Large Language Models
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-19
Rank: #1 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, RecSys
Abstract
We identify a structural weakness in current large language model (LLM) alignment: modern refusal mechanisms are fail-open. While existing approaches encode refusal behaviors across multiple latent features, suppressing a single dominant feature-via prompt-based jailbreaks-can cause alignment to collapse, leading to unsafe generation. Motivated by this, we propose fail-closed alignment as a design principle for robust LLM safety: refusal mechanisms should remain effective even under partial failures via redundant, independent causal pathways. We present a concrete instantiation of this principle: a progressive alignment framework that iteratively identifies and ablates previously learned refusal directions, forcing the model to reconstruct safety along new, independent subspaces. Across four jailbreak attacks, we achieve the strongest overall robustness while mitigating over-refusal and preserving generation quality, with small computational overhead. Our mechanistic analyses confirm that models trained with our method encode multiple, causally independent refusal directions that prompt-based jailbreaks cannot suppress simultaneously, providing empirical support for fail-closed alignment as a principled foundation for robust LLM safety.
Key Techniques

Fail-closed alignment
progressive alignment framework
iterative ablation of refusal directions
mechanistic analysis

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityA Behavioral Fingerprint for Large Language Models: Provenance Tracking via Refusal Vectors0.34Risk Awareness Injection: Calibrating Vision-Language Models for Safety without Compromising Utility0.20GOSV: Global Optimization for Safety Vector Extraction in LLMs0.19The Side Effects of Being Smart: Safety Risks in MLLMs’ Multi-Image Reasoning0.11CSR-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating the Cross-modal Safety and Reliability of MLLMs0.11
Progressive Fail-Closed Alignment (PFCA)
Core Idea: Iteratively identify and ablate learned refusal directions during safety fine-tuning, forcing the model to reconstruct alignment along orthogonal, causally independent subspaces, so that no single prompt-based suppression can collapse refusal behavior.
Problem It Solves: Current refusal mechanisms are fail-open: a single dominant latent feature encodes most refusal behavior, so suppressing it via prompt-based jailbreaks (e.g., role-play, prefix injection, adversarial suffixes) causes full alignment collapse. There is no redundancy — one broken pathway = unsafe generation.
How It Works:

Initial alignment pass: Fine-tune the base LLM on safety data as normal, producing a first refusal direction r₁ in the model’s representation space.
Direction identification: Extract the dominant refusal direction rₖ from the current model’s activations (e.g., via probing classifiers or contrastive feature extraction on safe vs. unsafe prompts).
Ablation + re-alignment: Project out rₖ from the model’s representational subspace (nullify it), then re-run safety fine-tuning. The model can no longer rely on the ablated pathway and must learn a new refusal direction rₖ₊₁ in an orthogonal subspace.
Iteration: Repeat steps 2–3 for K rounds, producing K independent refusal directions {r₁, …, rₖ} that are causally redundant — a jailbreak suppressing any one direction leaves the others intact.
Deployment: The final model retains all K refusal directions; an attacker must simultaneously suppress all K to achieve unsafe generation.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    A[Base LLM] --> B[Safety Fine-Tune Round 1]
    B --> C[Extract Refusal Direction r1]
    C --> D[Ablate r1 from Representation Space]
    D --> E[Safety Fine-Tune Round 2]
    E --> F[Extract Refusal Direction r2]
    F --> G[Ablate r2 ...]
    G --> H[... Round K]
    H --> I[Final Model with K Independent Refusal Pathways]

Where It’s Useful:

Hardening production LLMs against prompt-based jailbreaks (role-play, prefix attacks, adversarial suffixes, many-shot prompting)
Safety-critical deployments where over-refusal is costly but unsafe generation is unacceptable
Red-teaming evaluation: measuring how many independent suppression steps are required to break a model

Limitations / Caveats:

Assumes refusal directions are extractable and ablatable cleanly — entangled representations may cause helpfulness degradation during ablation
K rounds of fine-tuning adds computational overhead, though the paper reports it is small
Does not address weight-space attacks (e.g., fine-tune-based jailbreaks) that bypass representation-level defenses entirely
The independence of learned directions is empirically validated but not theoretically guaranteed to scale indefinitely

Internal Use Cases:

Safety in ranking/RecSys LLMs: If a recommendation LLM has refusal logic for harmful queries (e.g., illegal product requests), this framework could harden that refusal against prompt-injected retailer instructions attempting to override it
Multi-pathway refusal for content moderation: Applying progressive ablation to content-filter heads in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines to prevent single-feature suppression via adversarial documents in the retrieved context
Probing refusal geometry: The direction-extraction step (step 2) is independently useful as a diagnostic — run it on existing models to measure how concentrated (fail-open) their refusal representations are before committing to retraining

Discussions & References

huggingface.co

literature/2026-02-22-Greedy-Multi-Path-.title: Greedy Multi-Path Block Verification for Faster Decoding in Speculative Sampling
literature/2026-02-22-Greedy-Multi-Path-.content:
Greedy Multi-Path Block Verification for Faster Decoding in Speculative Sampling
Source: arXiv:cs.LG
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #6 (score: 0.74, max_sim: 0.86)
Reading time: ~0 min
Tags: Uncategorized
Abstract
The goal of L-step speculative decoding is to accelerate autoregressive decoding of a target model by using a cheaper draft model to generate a candidate path of L tokens. Based on a verification algorithm involving target and draft model probabilities, a prefix of the candidate sequence is accepted, and an additional correction token is sampled from a residual distribution to ensure that the final output adheres to the target distribution. While standard speculative decoding uses a verification algorithm which is independent at each token on the path, a recent extension called block verification uses a joint condition involving all sampled on-path probabilities. Block verification (BV) was shown to be optimal over all verification algorithms which use only on-path probabilities, improving on standard speculative decoding. In this work, we first show that block verification is optimal even over verification algorithms that use off-path probabilities, by constructing an information-agnostic linear program (LP). Further, we can extend our LP to the setting where the draft model samples multiple candidate paths, and use it to construct a natural class of multi-path block verification generalizations. While computing the optimal algorithm in this class is not tractable, by considering a stricter class of greedy algorithms, we can formulate an efficient method called greedy multi-path block verification (GBV). Empirically, GBV can improve block efficiency by over 30% and reduce decoding walltimes by over 15% relative to BV. On Llama-3 70B, GBV can improve the end-to-end decoding throughput over SOTA multi-path verification methods by more than 15%.
Key Techniques

Speculative decoding
Block verification
Greedy multi-path block verification
Information-agnostic linear program

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilarityDynamic Delayed Tree Expansion For Improved Multi-Path Speculative Decoding0.49PRISM: Parametrically Refactoring Inference for Speculative Sampling Draft Models0.45Variational Speculative Decoding: Rethinking Draft Training from Token Likelihood to Sequence Acceptance0.44DART: Diffusion-Inspired Speculative Decoding for Fast LLM Inference0.44DFlash: Block Diffusion for Flash Speculative Decoding0.44
Greedy Multi-Path Block Verification (GBV)
Core Idea: A tractable multi-path speculative decoding verification algorithm that greedily maximizes block efficiency by jointly verifying multiple draft token sequences using an information-agnostic linear program framework.
Problem It Solves: Standard block verification (BV) is optimal for single-path speculative decoding but cannot exploit multiple draft candidates; naive multi-path extensions require solving an intractable optimization over all possible verification algorithms, leaving significant throughput gains on the table.
How It Works:

Draft: A cheap draft model proposes multiple candidate token paths (each of length L), expanding the search space beyond a single sequence.
LP Construction: For each verification decision, construct an information-agnostic linear program over joint acceptance probabilities that upper-bounds block efficiency—this LP is valid even when using off-path token probabilities, proving BV-class optimality is not an artifact of restricting to on-path information.
Greedy Selection: Instead of solving the full (intractable) multi-path LP globally, GBV greedily selects the acceptance decision at each step that locally maximizes expected accepted tokens, yielding an efficient closed-form algorithm.
Correction Sampling: After accepting a prefix across paths, sample a correction token from the residual distribution to maintain exact adherence to the target model’s distribution.

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    D[Draft Model] -->|K candidate paths, length L| VP[Multi-Path\nVerification Pool]
    T[Target Model] -->|Single forward pass\nover all paths| VP
    VP -->|Greedy LP solve\nper-step| GA[Greedy Acceptance\nDecision]
    GA -->|Accepted prefix + correction token| O[Output Tokens]
    GA -->|Residual distribution| CS[Correction Sampler]
    CS --> O

Where It’s Useful:

LLM inference serving where latency reduction is critical (e.g., Llama-3 70B serving)
Settings where multiple cheap draft sources exist (e.g., smaller models, retrieval-based drafts, speculative edits)
High-throughput generation pipelines where walltime per token matters more than raw FLOPs

Limitations / Caveats:

Greedy local decisions may miss globally optimal acceptance strategies that the full LP would find
Benefit depends on draft model quality and path diversity; low-diversity paths collapse to single-path BV performance
Adds coordination overhead for managing and verifying multiple draft paths simultaneously

Internal Use Cases:

LLM inference acceleration: Directly applicable when serving large ranking or generative models with a smaller draft model in the loop—GBV’s 15%+ walltime reduction compounds over high-QPS workloads
Retrieval-augmented drafting (cf. DReSD/Batch Verification): When draft sequences come from retrieved candidates rather than a draft model, GBV’s multi-path framework slots in naturally as the verification layer, complementing batch verification’s parallelism
Draft shape tuning: GBV’s LP framing gives a principled signal for how many paths vs. path length to allocate, potentially replacing heuristic ablations used in draft shape optimization

literature/2026-02-22-How-I-use-Claude-C.title: How I use Claude Code: Separation of planning and execution
literature/2026-02-22-How-I-use-Claude-C.content:
How I use Claude Code: Separation of planning and execution
Source: HackerNews
Published: 2026-02-22
Rank: #6 (score: 0.99, max_sim: 0.83)
Reading time: ~30 min
Tags: Search
Abstract
HN: 716 pts, 454 comments
How I Use Claude Code | Boris Tane How I Use Claude Code | Boris Tane boris tane
blog talks projects
How I Use Claude Code
Feb 10, 2026
·
[ agents
sdlc
]
Table of Contents
Phase 1: Research
Phase 2: Planning
The Annotation Cycle
Phase 3: Implementation
Feedback During Implementation
Staying in the Driver’s Seat
Single Long Sessions
The Workflow in One Sentence
I’ve been using Claude Code as my primary development tool for approx 9 months, and the workflow I’ve settled into is radically different from what most people do with AI coding tools. Most developers type a prompt, sometimes use plan mode, fix the errors, repeat. The more terminally online are stitching together ralph loops, mcps, gas towns (remember those?), etc. The results in both cases are a mess that completely falls apart for anything non-trivial.
The workflow I’m going to describe has one core principle: never let Claude write code until you’ve reviewed and approved a written plan . This separation of planning and execution is the single most important thing I do. It prevents wasted effort, keeps me in control of architecture decisions, and produces significantly better results with minimal token usage than jumping straight to code.
flowchart LR
R[Research] ⇒ P[Plan]
P ⇒ A[Annotate]
A ⇒|repeat 1-6x| A
A ⇒ T[Todo List]
T ⇒ I[Implement]
I ⇒ F[Feedback & Iterate] Phase 1: Research
Every meaningful task starts with a deep-read directive. I ask Claude to thoroughly understand the relevant part of the codebase before doing anything else. And I always require the findings to be written into a persistent markdown file, never just a verbal summary in the chat.
read this folder in depth, understand how it works deeply, what it does and all its specificities. when that’s done, write a detailed report of your learnings and findings in research.md
study the notification system in great details, understand the intricacies of it and write a detailed research.md document with everything there is to know about how notifications work
go through the task scheduling flow, understand it deeply and look for potential bugs. there definitely are bugs in the system as it sometimes runs tasks that should have been cancelled. keep researching the flow until you find all the bugs, don’t stop until all the bugs are found. when you’re done, write a detailed report of your findings in research.md
Notice the language: “deeply” , “in gr
Key Techniques

Research
Plan review before coding
Annotation cycle
Single long sessions

Related Litnotes





























NoteSimilaritySoftware factories and the agentic moment0.21Idea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives0.16Idea2Story: An Automated Pipeline for Transforming Research Concepts into Complete Scientific Narratives0.16LLMs as the new high level language0.14ResearchGym: Evaluating Language Model Agents on Real-World AI Research0.14
Plan-Before-Code (PBC) Workflow
Core Idea: Enforce strict phase separation between research, planning, and code generation — Claude never writes code until a human-reviewed written plan exists.
Problem It Solves: LLM coding assistants produce architectural drift, wasted tokens, and hard-to-reverse decisions when prompted to generate code directly, especially on non-trivial tasks where early structural choices cascade through the implementation.
How It Works:

Research Phase: Prompt Claude to deeply read the relevant codebase section and write findings to a persistent research.md file (never a verbal summary) — this grounds the model in actual code rather than hallucinated assumptions
Planning Phase: Claude drafts an implementation plan; the human iterates via annotation comments (the “Annotation Cycle”, repeated 1–6x) until the plan accurately reflects intended architecture, edge cases, and constraints
Implementation Phase: Claude converts the approved plan into a TodoWrite task list and executes sequentially, with the human providing feedback only at natural breakpoints — no mid-task interruptions that cause context drift

Architecture:
flowchart LR
    R[Research\nresearch.md] --> P[Plan Draft]
    P --> A[Annotate]
    A -->|repeat 1-6x| A
    A --> T[TodoWrite\nTask List]
    T --> I[Implement]
    I --> F[Feedback\n& Iterate]

Where It’s Useful:

Any non-trivial feature implementation spanning multiple files or subsystems
Refactoring work where architectural decisions need human sign-off before destructive changes
Onboarding Claude to an unfamiliar codebase before delegating autonomous work

Limitations / Caveats:

Front-loads human effort into the planning phase — faster for simple tasks to skip and prompt directly
Annotation cycle quality depends on human domain knowledge; weak annotations produce weak plans
Persistent research.md files become stale if the codebase changes significantly between sessions

Internal Use Cases:

Before implementing a new ranking feature, have Claude write research.md covering the existing feature pipeline, signal ingestion, and serving path — then annotate the plan before touching scorer code
For LLM fine-tuning infrastructure changes (data loaders, training loops), use the annotation cycle to lock down data flow contracts before any code is written
When adding a new retrieval stage to a RecSys pipeline, force a research pass over the existing candidate generation code to surface implicit assumptions before introducing new components

literature/2026-02-22-Hybrid-Gym-Trainin.title: Hybrid-Gym: Training Coding Agents to Generalize Across Tasks
literature/2026-02-22-Hybrid-Gym-Trainin.content:
Hybrid-Gym: Training Coding Agents to Generalize Across Tasks
Source: arXiv:cs.CL
Published: 2026-02-18
Rank: #2 (score: 1.00, max_sim: 0.82)
Reading time: ~20 min
Tags: LLM, Search
Code: github.com/yiqingxyq/Hybrid-Gym
Abstract
When assessing the quality of coding agents, predominant benchmarks focus on solving single issues on GitHub, such as SWE-Bench. In contrast, in real use, these agents solve more various and complex tasks that involve other skills such as exploring codebases, testing software, and designing architecture. In this paper, we first characterize some transferable skills that are shared across diverse tasks by decomposing trajectories into fine-grained components, and derive a set of principles for designing auxiliary training tasks to teach language models these skills. Guided by these principles, we propose a training environment, Hybrid-Gym, consisting of a set of scalable synthetic tasks, such as function localization and dependency search. Experiments show that agents trained on our synthetic tasks effectively generalize to diverse real-world tasks that are not present in training, improving a base model by 25.4% absolute gain on SWE-Bench Verified, 7.9% on SWT-Bench Verified, and 5.1% on Commit-0 Lite.
